o Cambridge Assessment English test available as an add-on (~£80), in addition to EXSPORTISE testing/certificates. 

**4. Coaching and activities highlights** 

• **Football.** 

o EXSPORTISE described being the exclusive/official partner of Arsenal Football Club for residential language & football camps in the UK. 

o Coaching described as built around Arsenal’s “Strong Young Gunners” approach (as referenced on the call). 

• **Multi-Activity (Clayesmore).** 

o Includes activities like climbing, kayaking, paddleboarding, and archery; many sessions involve off-site venues by minibus. 

o Supplement referenced on the call (~£325). 

• **Horse riding.** 

o Off-site stables (often ~20–30 minutes); supplement referenced (~£325). o Blend of riding and stable time (examples mentioned: dressage, show jumping, hacking). o Typical riding ratios referenced around ~1:6. 

• **Rugby (Worth).** 

o Described as boys-only and typically offered during the first ~4 weeks; coaching described as coming from experienced/ex-pro staff. 

o Ratio referenced around ~1:10. 

• **Excursions.** Students staying 2+ weeks typically have weekend excursions; football weeks may include an Arsenal Stadium tour. 

**5. Supervision, welfare, and accommodation** 

• **Dates and changeover.** Standard pattern described as arrive Sunday and depart Saturday, across a ~6-week summer season. 

• **Staffing ratio.** Overall supervision described at about 1 staff to 4 students. • **Welfare structure.** 

o Centre Manager + Welfare Manager on each site. 

o House Parents in each boarding house; coaches and teachers live on campus.

o Medical officer/medical centre referenced. 

• **Accommodation.** 

o Typically 2–3 students per room with shared bathrooms; younger students may be in dorm-style rooms (e.g., 4–6). 

o Common/social spaces in each house for evenings. 

**6. Meals, phones, and pricing notes** 

• **Meals.** Three meals daily (cafeteria/boarding-school style); dietary needs can typically be supported (discussed generally on the call). 

• **Phones.** Phones are not used during lessons/coaching or in the dining hall; EXSPORTISE described not confiscating phones but actively managing usage to keep students engaged. 

• **Pricing notes (as quoted).** 

o Base price referenced around ~£1,775 per week. 

o Deposit referenced at ~£350 (with refund policy tied to a May 1 date on the call). o Supplements referenced for Multi-Activity and Horse Riding (~£325 each).

title: Camp_Twin_Creeks_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Twin Creeks – Program Write-Up** 

**1. Overview and Youth Development Positioning** 

• **Milestone year:** The camp described Summer 2025 as its 25th summer under the current leadership (operating since 2001). 

• **Youth development focus:** Leadership emphasized refining and naming the camp’s youth development philosophy—ensuring staff training and materials reflect learning outcomes, not just activities. 

• **Year-round resource for parents:** The camp rolled out a newsletter (“parents, partners in parenting”) intended to support families beyond the summer season. 

**2. Session Model and Classic Camp Arc** 

• **Primary format:** Two-week sessions designed with a clear beginning/middle/end—modeled on the classic New England camp arc. 

o Traditions mentioned included opening campfire, color war, and a closing banquet. o A strong CIT program and multi-year camper “journey” were emphasized. 

• **Extended option:** Some campers attend for ~4 weeks by doing two consecutive 2-week sessions, enabling deeper immersion and additional electives/trips between sessions. 

**3. One-Week “Dream Week” Pathway for Younger Campers** 

• **Intro experience:** The camp offers a one-week Dream Week option aimed at younger/new campers as a first step toward longer sessions. 

• **2025 expansion:** The camp doubled its Dream Week availability, offering four Dream Week sessions early in the summer to meet demand for shorter first-time experiences. 

**4. 2025 Program Updates and Facility Enhancements** 

• **Arts emphasis:** The camp is investing more in arts programming to match the “do a bit of everything” camper profile (sports, arts, adventure, waterfront, and riding were all mentioned). 

• **Capital projects:**

o A new outdoor theater was described as breaking ground imminently (weather permitting). 

o The wood shop is being renovated into a more flexible craft/creative space. 

**5. Logistics and Transportation Options** 

• **Chaperoned travel:** The camp can offer chaperoned flights for Florida families for the first two sessions (aligned with Florida school calendars). 

• **Regional transport:** A Pennsylvania transportation option was also referenced.

title: Camp_Tevya_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Tevya — Program Write-Up** 

**1. Camp Snapshot** 

• **Location**: Brookline, New Hampshire (about 1 hour 20 minutes north of Boston). • **Program type**: Co-ed Jewish overnight camp, described as a “home away from home” community. 

• **Ages/grades**: Approximately ages 8–15/16 (rising 3rd grade through rising 10th grade). • **Camper population**: ~300 campers across the summer. 

• **Session options**: Two 3.5-week sessions or the full 7 weeks (older campers more commonly full-summer, though some younger campers choose 7 weeks as well). 

**2. Campus and Waterfront** 

• **Lakefront setting**: Camp sits on a lake (referenced on the call as Lake Potanipo). • **No pool**: All aquatics take place in the lake, including boating/canoeing, paddleboarding, waterskiing, wakeboarding, and instructional swim. 

• **Layout**: Girls area is closer to the lake; boys area is set farther back in a wooded section of campus. 

**3. Daily Schedule and Activity Design** 

• **Morning start**: Wake-up around 7:30 a.m.; flag at ~8:00 a.m. with U.S. and Israeli flags raised daily. 

• **Activity blocks**: Three 45-minute activity periods in the morning, lunch + rest hour, then three activity periods in the afternoon. 

• **Bunk activities + electives**: Younger campers rotate through offerings with their bunk; elective choice expands with age (7th/8th graders ~4 elective periods; 9th/10th graders ~6). • **Goal over time**: Expose younger campers broadly, then allow older campers to increasingly personalize their schedules and deepen skills. 

**4. Cabins, Dining, and Daily Life** 

• **Cabins**: Mostly bunk beds for campers; counselors in single beds. Most bunks have indoor bathrooms (2 toilets, 2 showers, 3 sinks). 

• **Meals**: Family-style dining with assigned tables that mix ages and genders; table counselors support conversation, community, and camper wellbeing at meals. 

• **Allergies and food policy**: Dedicated special-diets team (including gluten-free/celiac support). Camp asks families not to send food/candy to bunks; campers receive three meals + two snacks daily, plus weekly canteen. 

**5. Staffing, Support, and Inclusion** 

• **Staff composition**: Roughly half alumni “homegrown” staff and half international staff (examples mentioned: Israel, UK, Australia, New Zealand, France, Spain, Hungary). • **Leadership team**: Director/assistant director plus additional adult leadership, including head counselors and a MESH (mental, emotional, social health) coordinator.

• **Head counselors**: Experienced adults (described as parents, teachers, social workers, psychologists) who provide camper support and guidance. 

• **Inclusion**: The call noted experience successfully including campers on the autism spectrum; fit is evaluated thoughtfully with family partnership and the camp’s support structure. 

• **Emergency services**: Fire department and police/ambulance access were described as very close to camp (minutes away). 

**6. Jewish Life and Values** 

• **Tarboot**: Weekly Jewish-values programming (interactive, developmentally appropriate, often tied to a “value of the week” such as kindness, respect, integrity, leadership). • **Shabbat**: Friday evening and Saturday morning services as a full-camp community—camp-style with music, singing, and a strong sense of togetherness. • **Values in action**: Weekly values are reinforced across camp culture and highlighted during Shabbat programming. 

**7. Teen Arc Across the Cohen Camps** 

• **Travel program**: Rising juniors in high school participate in a shared travel experience (typically Israel; Spain/Portugal was cited for a recent summer). 

• **Kadima CIT**: After travel, teens return for the final stretch of camp to close out their camper experience and begin the Kadima counselor-in-training trajectory. 

• **Staff pathway**: Kadima graduates can apply to return as staff in subsequent summers.

title: Camp_Tel_Noar_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Tel Noar — Program Write-Up** 

**1. Camp Snapshot** 

• **Program format**: Two-session Jewish overnight camp (part of the Cohen Camps network). • **Session options**: Two 3.5-week sessions (7 weeks total). Camp shared that ~60–70% of campers attend both sessions. 

• **Current size**: Recently operating at ~150–200 campers per session, with a longer-term goal of returning to ~250–275. 

• **Overall feel**: Intentionally small and intimate—described as a place where community connection is easy and familiar faces are everywhere. 

**2. Daily Schedule and Program Progression** 

• **Activity blocks**: Three activity periods in the morning and three in the afternoon (a mix of bunk rotations and electives). 

• **Aspirational arc**: Younger campers try a wide range of activities with their bunk; elective choice increases with age so older campers can design more of their own schedules (10th graders largely elective-based). 

• **Daily rhythm**: Flag raising, post-breakfast cleanup, afternoon snack, rest hour after lunch, evening flag lowering, supervised free play before dinner, and nightly programs (bunk/unit/campwide traditions). 

**3. Cabins and Community Design** 

• **Fugim housing**: Many camper areas are “fugim” complexes—three cabins connected by a common room with a shared, recently remodeled bathroom. 

• **Multi-age connection**: Campers rotate through shared spaces with adjacent age groups over time, creating built-in mentorship and a strong “small-camp” feel. 

• **Dining hall culture**: Mixed tables help strengthen cross-age relationships and broaden the sense of community. 

**4. Facilities and Highlights** 

• **Waterfront**: Full waterfront program including an inflatable course, waterskiing, kayaking, canoeing, stand-up paddleboarding, and open swim. 

• **Pool**: On-site pool used for swim lessons and large community moments (the call referenced a signature Israeli-dancing tradition in/around the pool on the final Shabbat). • **Indoor spaces**: Gym, rec hall, and multiple multi-purpose program areas; dining hall described as newer and well-suited to camp needs. 

• **Recent build**: A new outdoor chapel was unveiled recently and is used for Shabbat and community gathering. 

**5. Jewish Life, Values, and Shabbat** 

• **Shabbat prayer**: Prayer held on Shabbat only (Friday night, Saturday morning, Saturday evening).

• **Jewish Life & Learning**: Rotating JLL programming for younger campers 2–3x/week (weekly values, Israel, Torah, big questions through experiential learning). • **Joelectives**: Older campers (9th/10th) choose their Jewish-learning topics in small groups with educators from camp leadership and visiting teachers. 

• **Values in culture**: Values are reinforced through camp traditions and recognition systems (the call referenced collectible “value gibbets” as a recent example). 

• **Shabbat experience**: Community walk to chapel, Israeli folk dancing, Shabbat dinner traditions, song session (Shira), and special Saturday programming with a more relaxed pace. 

**6. Culture and Differentiators Within Cohen Camps** 

• **Positioning**: Compared to sister camps, Tel Noar was described as generally more relaxed with a strong emphasis on inclusivity, welcome, and kindness. 

• **Who attends**: Primarily families from the Northeast, with a high percentage from the greater Boston area; the call noted a small number of international campers (and that the camp does not actively recruit internationally). 

• **Where the camp is headed**: Leadership described an “upward trajectory” post-COVID and a focus on solidifying Tel Noar’s identity as an intimate, community-centered option. 

**7. Facilities Planning and Future Growth** 

• **Capital planning**: Camp is in a visioning process to determine next major facility priorities. • **Nature initiative**: A new nature/camping program is being planned to better use camp-owned woodland acreage and give campers a regular camping experience.

title: Camp_Pembroke_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Pembroke — Program Write-Up** 

**1. Camp Snapshot** 

• **Program type**: All-girls, pluralistic Jewish overnight camp (campers and staff include both Jewish and non-Jewish community members). 

• **Jewish life**: “Jewish-light” and values-based—woven into daily life through experiential education and community traditions. 

• **Session options**: Two 3.5-week sessions (7 weeks total). About half of campers attend the full 7 weeks. 

• **Additional entry option**: A 2-week introductory session (first two weeks of second session) for first-time campers, rising 2nd–5th grade. 

**2. Program Structure and Daily Schedule** 

• **Activities**: Mix of core bunk/age-group programming (arts & crafts, athletics, swim, waterfront, nature, drama, dance, etc.) plus a robust elective system. 

• **Electives**: Campers select electives before arriving (with flexibility to change). ~70 elective offerings; 7-week campers re-select for the second half of summer. 

• **Typical day**: Three activity periods in the morning, lunch, one early-afternoon period, a short break (with optional snack), then two more afternoon periods. 

• **Evening flow**: Shower hour after the final period, end-of-day flag, dinner, supervised free-play/down time, then an all-camp evening program; bedtime prep includes an optional cabin snack. 

**3. Community, Staffing, and Culture** 

• **Relationship-first philosophy**: Skill-building matters, but camp prioritizes friendships, role models, and “soft skills” as core outcomes. 

• **Retention**: Reported camper retention runs ~92–94% year-over-year, with many campers returning longer and later joining staff. 

• **Staff profile**: ~40% of counselors are “homegrown” alumni; additional staff come internationally (examples mentioned: Israel, UK, Spain, Mexico, Australia, New Zealand). • **Bunks**: Target 12–14 campers per bunk with 3–4 counselors; counselors live in the cabins. Camp flexes bunk groupings year-to-year based on age-group sizes. 

**4. Jewish Life and Traditions** 

• **Shabbat services**: Friday night and Saturday morning services (~35–40 minutes), designed to be interactive and accessible; blend of English and Hebrew. 

• **Saturday night**: Short Havdalah followed by a large community circle experience led by older campers. 

• **Values focus**: Jewish learning is built around community values and experiential practice rather than formal denominational alignment. 

**5. Health, Food, and Support Systems**

• **Health center**: Rotating nursing team (10 nurses referenced), with an overnight nurse on-call; overseen by a nurse practitioner. Nearby hospital access (~18 minutes noted). • **Medical support**: Experience supporting campers with specific medical needs (type 1 diabetes was discussed), including overnight coverage plans. 

• **Dietary accommodations**: Dedicated dietary support for allergies and preferences (gluten/dairy referenced). Camp described a nut-aware approach (no nut products; careful sourcing). 

• **Inter-camp connections**: Opportunities for inter-camp sports and events; structured visits with nearby boys camps were described as part of the social experience. 

**6. Teen Pathway and Cohen Camps Connection** 

• **Camper-to-staff pathway**: Teen progression includes leadership development and preparation for staff roles. 

• **Cohen camps programming**: Cross-camp experiences with sister camps (Tevya and Tel Noar) including pre-trip community-building for older campers. 

• **Israel travel**: A multi-week Israel experience for campers heading into 11th grade was described as part of the broader Cohen camps teen arc.

title: Camp_Runoia_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Runoia – Program Write-Up** 

**1. Camp Overview and Community Style** 

• **Relationship-driven culture:** The camp described itself as highly communicative and supportive—particularly strong with families who want frequent touchpoints and reassurance. o The director highlighted the camp’s ability to support “quirky” kids who need a little extra and parents who are anxious or new to sleepaway camp. 

• **Consultative admissions style:** The team emphasized being available to talk with families about camp even if they ultimately choose a different program. 

**2. Newer / Younger Camper Programming** 

• **Harmony Lane:** A younger-camper program (described as serving kids as young as ~6–9) that is more hands-on and managed; multiple sessions were described as filling quickly. 

• **Harmony Heights:** A new “next step” option for slightly older young campers—more like traditional camp, but designed as a shorter “taste” experience. 

o A ~10-day format was highlighted as working well for kids who are still rebuilding comfort with time-away (post-COVID patterns were mentioned). 

• **Sibling flexibility:** The director noted the ability to accommodate sisters in different age/program groups. 

**3. Leadership and Older Camper Options** 

• **CIT adjustments:** The camp lowered the CIT commitment to allow a half-summer option, while still supporting a full-season pathway. 

• **Expanded eligibility:** The CIT/leadership track is now open to teens who may not have previously attended the camp (a ~3-week leadership training program for ~16-year-olds was described). 

**4. Parent Engagement and Community Events** 

• **“Mega Weekend”:** A late-summer on-campus weekend designed for mothers/daughters (and other female family members) to experience camp together; demand was described as parent-driven.

title: Camp_Jeanne_dArc_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Jeanne d’Arc – Program Write-Up** 

**1. Camp Identity, Setting, and Mission** 

• **What it is:** A traditional all-girls sleepaway camp in the Adirondacks of upstate New York, with a mission centered on helping families build strong girls. 

o The directors described the camp as ~103 years old with a long-standing international history and culture. 

• **Waterfront setting:** Located on a lake with beach and waterfront access (sailing, canoeing, stand-up paddleboarding, and swim instruction were highlighted). 

**2. Session Options and Core Camp Program** 

• **Session lengths:** Offers a range from ~2 weeks up to ~7 weeks. 

o The camp emphasized creating a full “classic camp” arc—opening traditions, skill progression, and end-of-session milestones. 

• **Traditional activity mix:** 

o Highlighted examples included horseback riding and water skiing, alongside standard waterfront programming. 

• **Progressive camper journey:** The program is designed to bring campers in young and build skills year-over-year toward leadership and older-camper experiences. 

**3. International Community and Cultural Traditions** 

• **Global representation:** The camp positions itself as a cultural experience where campers and staff encounter many nationalities. 

o A “flag-raising” tradition was described: the camp raises a new flag when it welcomes a camper or counselor from a new country. 

o Recent examples mentioned included adding flags for Argentina and Azerbaijan. 

**4. Older Camper Focus: The “Owl” Experience and Trips** 

• **Owl program:** A capstone “oldest camper” experience that includes an Adirondacks adventure trip (described as ~3 days / 2 nights) with a guide.

o The intent is to let campers demonstrate learned outdoor and independence skills in a supported setting. 

o The program was described as a major focus area for growth and refinement. • **Adventure destinations discussed:** 

o An example trip mentioned was to Ausable Chasm (referred to as a “Grand Canyon of the East/New York”), with activities like rafting/tubing and climbing. 

**5. Best-Fit Campers and Families** 

• **Best for:** Families seeking a classic, tradition-rich all-girls camp with strong waterfront and riding options, and a progressive skill-building pathway as campers return year after year.

title: Camp_Hidden_Meadows_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Hidden Meadows – Program Write-Up** 

**1. Leadership Context and Background** 

• **Director transition:** The camp’s director shared he was in his 8th–9th month leading Camp Hidden Meadows, bringing ~30 years of camping leadership experience. o He previously directed Emma Kauffman Camp in Morgantown, West Virginia for ~25 years before returning to residential camping after a period in day camps. 

**2. Session Model and “Choose Your Own Adventure” Structure** 

• **Short-session strength:** The camp emphasized the value of the 1-week and 2-week formats as a true “complete” camp experience (not just a trial). 

• **Daily choice:** Programming was described as a “choose your own adventure” model supported by workshops and varied daily activities. 

o Examples mentioned included fly fishing and overnight backpacking opportunities. 

**3. Earth Expeditions and Older Camper Adventure Options** 

• **Earth Expeditions:** A travel/adventure component described as adventures around West Virginia for roughly 7th–10th graders. 

• **Regional adventure framing:** The director referenced families combining a one-week camp experience with West Virginia travel (e.g., Snowshoe) as part of how some families think about summer plans. 

**4. Location and Climate as a Differentiator** 

• **Mountain climate:** The camp highlighted West Virginia’s cooler summer temperatures as a selling point. 

o An example shared: the hottest day of the summer was ~85°F at camp while nearby Mid-Atlantic areas were in the 90s–100s.

**5. Market Footprint and Recruitment Focus** 

• **Primary feeder markets:** Recruitment emphasis was described around Maryland, Virginia, and Washington, DC, with additional campers coming from farther afield (examples like Florida and Texas were mentioned). 

• **Community-building:** The director discussed strengthening a parent-ambassador approach (small gatherings/home-based outreach) to build a strong core camp community.

title: Camp Watitoh Write Up
text:
**Camp Watitoh** 

**Quick facts** 

● **Type:** Small, traditional coed sleepaway camp (≈**220 campers** total) 

● **Location:** Berkshires (Center Lake; clear, spring-fed, sand bottom) 

● **Session model: Full-season 7 weeks**; **4-week option** for **entering 2nd–5th**. Case-by-case 4-week **exceptions for 6th–7th** (and occasionally older) when fit is right. 

○ Families commonly start at 4 weeks and **extend in-season**; last summer **only 6 campers** left at 4 weeks. 

● **Vibe:** Classic, throwback, **tight-knit “everyone knows everyone”** community; emphasis on tradition, warmth, and down-to-earth culture (not a bells-and-whistles camp). 

**Who thrives here** 

● Kids who want **small, known** community and **high staff attention**. 

● Families seeking **traditional structure** + **some choice** (especially on the waterfront and via “hobby” period), growing to more choice with age. 

● Strong fits for **waterfront lovers**, **tennis/swim/sailing**, and **drama/arts** kids. 

**Potential misfit** 

● Campers who want **full-day free-choice** across all periods from day one. 

● Teens who only want highly competitive, travel-team style sports all day (sports are solid, but the ethos is **balanced/all-camp**). 

**Program & schedule**

● **Progressive programming:** More structure for younger campers; **increasing electives** as grades rise. 

● **Daily “Hobby” (elective) period** chosen weekly for **all ages**. 

● **Waterfront twice daily:** 

○ **AM:** Instruction (Red Cross swim, sailing, canoe/kayak, water-ski). 

○ **PM: Free choice** waterfront. 

● **Upper Seniors (entering 9th):** Heavily **choice-based** (reduced group structure). ● **Trips:** Different trip each year by age; **entire camp to Six Flags** once per summer. ● **Overnights:** Every age; younger on camp property across the street; older off-camp. 

● **Evenings:** Mix of all-camp/coed/single-campus events (glow/silent disco, hoedown, luau, campfires, etc.). 

● **Color War:** Green vs. Tan; includes **sports + arts/chess/climbing/cooking**; Apache relay; rope burn. 

**Activities & facilities (highlights)** 

● **Waterfront:** Clear, warm **Center Lake**; sailing (strong regatta tradition), water-ski (boom → rope), SUP, trampoline/blob, kayaking/canoeing, fishing. 

● **Athletics: Tennis** (largest land sport) + **Tennis Plus** advanced add-on (6th+; paid), **new hockey rink** (2026), pickleball, basketball, soccer, baseball/softball. 

● **Adventure:** High ropes & climbing with new elements added in recent years. 

● **Arts/Enrichment:** Ceramics (two kilns), woodworking, cooking (huge favorite), nature/STEM (seasonal animals—e.g., baby goats), two **musicals** each summer, fitness/weight training for older campers. 

● **Bunks & comforts:** All cabins **renovated**; **showers now in every cabin** (boys previously used a shower house). Nugget-ice **hydration stations** on boys’ & girls’ sides (surprisingly impactful).

**Food & dining** 

● **All meals together** as one camp in the mess hall (campers sit by bunk). ● **Stations:** Breakfast yogurt/granola; lunch & dinner **salad bars**; dinner **pasta bar**. 

● **Allergies:** No nuts or “may contain” served; accommodate **celiac/gluten-free** and other needs (one kitchen chef is a **dietary specialist**). 

● **Baker on staff** (fresh challah Fridays; house-made breads & desserts). 

**Jewish life** 

● **Cultural/traditional, not religious**: short **Friday Shabbat** before campfire; “more kumbaya than brachot.” Friday dinner feels like family Shabbat. Works for Jewish and non-Jewish families. 

**Staffing, health & safety** 

● **Gate attendant & 24/7 roving security**. 

● **Health center: 4 RNs + 2 student nurses**; **telehealth pediatric concierge** 24/7 with connected vitals device; strong local hospital/urgent care relationships. 

● Directors are **highly present** with campers; **proactive new-family onboarding** (home visits, regional pizza parties, pen-pals, June open house, 48-hour adjustment calls). 

**Intercamps & competition** 

● Participates broadly in **Berkshires intercamps** (tennis, swim, sailing, archery, dance, etc.). 

● Particularly strong in **tennis, swim, sailing, dance**; solid participation emphasis and sportsmanship. 

**Geography & transportation**

● **Tri-state core**: Westchester (largest), NYC, NJ (Maplewood/Montclair/South Orange/Ridgewood, etc.). 

● **Boston & South Florida** each ~15–20 families; growing **LA/CO** presence; some one-offs (e.g., WV). 

● **Bus:** Single **Westchester** pickup (families prefer all arriving together). 

● **Flights: Chaperoned Florida flight** (FLL or PBI; to/from). Others may fly to NYC for bus. **UM pickups** at NYC airports supported. 

**Enrollment notes (2026)** 

● **Openings remain** across many age groups; active touring; families 

comparison-shopping. 

● **Older new-camper entries possible** (even **entering 8th**), with deliberate pre-work and integration. 

**What’s new / recent upgrades** 

● **Expanded electives** (especially as kids age). 

● **All cabins now with showers**; **renovated interiors**. 

● **New hockey rink** underway (announced). 

● **Nugget-ice “watering poles”** to boost hydration. 

**Talk tracks for families** 

● “If you want a **small, classic camp** where your child is **known** and still gets **real choice**—especially on the **waterfront**—Watito fits beautifully.” 

● “The **4-week start** is a great safety net; historically **most extend** once they’re there.” 

● “Waterfront is a **signature**: clear lake, serious instruction, tons of fun toys—and **free-choice afternoons** daily.”

● “**All-camp meals** and frequent **all-camp events** reinforce the **family feel** you sensed on tour.” 

**Possible concerns to pre-empt** 

● **“Is there enough choice?”** → Yes: daily hobby period (weekly selection) + **daily free-choice waterfront**; **much more choice** by 9th grade. 

● **“Big-time sports?”** → Competitive outlets exist (intercamps, Tennis Plus), but ethos is **balanced** rather than hyper-elite. 

● **“Food allergies?”** → Robust systems: nut-free operation, dietary-specialist chef, strong accommodations. 

**Follow-ups / Assets to request** 

● Ask for the **age-group mailer** they just produced (Drew offered to send to advisors). ● Social post/details on the **new hockey rink**. 

● Current **2026 availability by grade/gender** and **Florida flight specifics** once set.

title: Camp KenMont & KenWood Write-Up
text:
**Camp KenMont & KenWood** 

**TL;DR** 

● **Enrollment:** ~**70%** enrolled now. **2-week, 2nd session = waitlisted** (very tight). **1st session = more space**, esp. **boys** (girls pacing slightly ahead). 

● **Model:** Traditional **brother–sister** camp on one property; **two 3.5-week sessions** with a **2-week starter** option at the start of each. ~**10–15%** of 2-weekers extend (case-by-case based on bunk dynamics). 

● **Positioning:** Uniform, **electronics-free** (basic music players ok), **down-to-earth** culture; **equal emphasis** on **sports, waterfront, adventure, arts** (45+ activities). Single-gender days with **appropriate co-ed social** (dances, quad meetups, family time). 

● **Differentiators:** Single-sex experience + shared campus; **optional Friday night kiddush** (open-air); **non-denominational** community; renovated **cabins & bathrooms**, **towel service**; no bunk A/C (keeps classic feel). 

**Capacity & Dates** 

● **Now:** ~70% full. **2-week (Session 2)**: largely **waitlist**. 

● **Movement:** Expect changes around **Feb 6** (full-refund **drop deadline**). ● **Priority:** If waitlist moves, **3.5-week** gets priority over 2-week for openings. 

**Advisor note:** Starting **Oct 1** you’ll get **weekly space updates**; use “**this was the last known availability**; spots are moving” language. Early-returning-family incentive ends **Sept 30**. 

**Who Thrives Here** 

● Families seeking **uniform**, no-frills camp with strong **values/relationships** over flash.

● Kids who want **breadth** (not just “sports camp” or “arts camp”). 

● **Diversity:** Roughly **60/40 Jewish** in Session 1, **~50/50** Session 2; many “Jewish-adjacent”; **8–10% international** (UK/Europe/LatAm) and growing. 

**Program & Daily Life** 

● **Six-period day** retained (short sessions = can’t lose periods; ensures all areas hit). ● Big finish: **Trip Day → Carnival → Color War → Burning of the Numbers → Banquet**. ● **Bunking:** Buildings split into two bunks (≈9 kids + 3 counselors per side). 

○ 2-weekers either grouped together **or** blended with full-session (majority full-session). 

○ After 2-week departure: make beds with **camp linen**; schedule **Trip Day next morning** to smooth emotions. 

**Facilities & Comfort** 

● **Cabins:** Nearly fully **renovated**; **bathrooms redone** (parent-pleaser). ● **Towel service** (hygiene/packing ease). 

● **No A/C in bunks** (intentional, classic camp). 

**Activities (select highlights)** 

● **Waterfront:** Ski/wake, sailing, aqua-park, etc. 

● **Adventure/Ropes:** Long-tenured leadership (e.g., Head of Ropes 33 yrs).

● **Arts/Culinary/Photo** funded on par with sports. 

● **Horseback riding:** Off-site with **Brook Run Farms**; targeting **~30 campers/session**. ○ Groups of 10; each camper **3 x 90-min** visits. 

○ **2-weekers can ride** (all rides scheduled in first two weeks). **High demand** → waitlist common. 

**Travel & Chaperones** 

● **Chaperoned flights: Fort Lauderdale (to/from)**; **Los Angeles (to camp)** via red-eye. ● **Unaccompanied minor meet-ups** at **JFK/LGA** available. 

● **Atlanta:** Open to adding a chaperone route if numbers justify. 

**Sales/Process Tips** 

● **Direct intro email = 70%+ conversion.** Please **CC/intro** Brad & Hylton (not just a lead form). 

● Use the **virtual tour** & video—families engage and convert higher. 

● **Two-week program** capped at ~**25%** overall (skews younger). It’s a **great on-ramp**; many convert to 3.5-week after seeing what they’d miss at the end. 

**Waitlist Reality Check** 

● **2-week S2** movement is **unlikely** before Feb unless families change plans. 

● When openings appear, **3.5-week** gets priority for program/community continuity (and yes, viability).

**What They Want From Us** 

● Keep the candid **feedback** coming (program, comms, dining, bunks). They’ve implemented many advisor-driven tweaks. 

● **Tour invites** open; they’ll send **monthly “your enrollments”** tallies and **weekly capacity** (from Oct 1).

title: Camp Barney Zoom Write-Up
text:
**Camp Barney Medintz** 

**Program Snapshot** 

Camp Barney Medintz is a large, Jewish overnight camp located on 540 acres in Cleveland, Georgia, serving campers through high school. Known for its balance of community care, Jewish values, and exciting activities, the program emphasizes connection through a **cabin-based structure** where campers travel with their bunk and three dedicated counselors. Campers experience a progression-based curriculum in sports, arts, ropes, and aquatics, with signature activities like tubing, waterskiing, and a zip line that ends in the lake. The camp integrates joyful, participatory Shabbat services and core values—Joy, Jewish Identity, Making a Positive Difference, Integrity, Community, and Self-Discovery—into every aspect of programming. 

**Camper, Staff & Director Profile** 

Camp Barney enrolls approximately **1,338 unique campers per summer** across two sessions, with **620–630 campers on site at a time**. Roughly **65% are from Atlanta**, with others from Florida, Alabama, the Carolinas, Tennessee, and beyond; international campers (recently from Israel and France) are welcome. The staff totals over **420 members**, including specialists, unit heads, community care professionals, and kitchen staff. Director **Danny Herz** brings an educational background as an assistant principal and athletic director, emphasizing structure, safety, and personal growth. His leadership has introduced initiatives such as three counselors per cabin and a stronger focus on community care and security. 

**Living Quarters** 

Campers live in **cabins of 10–12** with **three counselors**, creating a high staff-to-camper ratio. Unlike some camps, cabin counselors stay with their groups full-time and do not serve as activity specialists. Cabin groupings are intentionally crafted to reflect campers’ interests and requests, balancing familiarity with opportunities to build new connections. Bathrooms are shared, and laundry is managed by the camp. A strong emphasis is placed on **cabin identity and group cohesion**, fostering close friendships and support networks. 

**Waterfront & Aquatics** 

Camp Barney has **two on-site lakes**, one dedicated to motorized sports like tubing and waterskiing—two of the camp’s most popular activities. All campers complete a swim assessment, and waterfront programs are integrated into cabin schedules to ensure broad participation. Older campers may take off-site ski trips as an additional highlight. The waterfront is a centerpiece of camp life, combining skill development with recreational fun.

**Daily Timetable & Choice** 

Campers’ schedules are built primarily around their cabin groups, which research shows fosters stronger connections. **Younger campers** participate in a full day of core activities together. **Middle campers** select one focused elective to pursue for four consecutive days, allowing for progression and depth. **Older campers** enjoy structured free time in designated zones (lake dock, athletic fields, pickleball/tennis courts), each staffed and supervised with “zone coverage.” Cabin counselors craft weekly schedules based on camper interests, ensuring exposure to both familiar and new activities. 

**Program Highlights** 

● **Waterfront:** Tubing, waterskiing, lake zip line (7th grade+), kayaking 

● **Sports:** Basketball, soccer, tennis, pickleball (growing in popularity) 

● **Adventure:** Ropes course progression, trail riding, climbing 

● **Arts & Drama:** Tie-dye → wire jewelry → pottery wheels; camp-wide theater productions for 1,000+ attendees 

● **Jewish Life:** Daily services optional, spirited **Friday (55 min) and Saturday (65 min) Shabbat** **services** led by camper units 

● **Music & Community:** Song leaders, DJ nights, spirited dining hall traditions ● **Trips:** 6-day trip for oldest campers, 3-day trip for rising 10th graders—**no phones permitted** 

**Health, Safety & Food** 

The **health center**, built in 2012, is staffed by **four nurses, three assistants, and a doctor**, with eight rooms for overnight care. The camp maintains strong relationships with local emergency services and has **24/7 armed security, cameras, license-plate readers, and a single gated entrance**. Dining is **family-style** by cabin and age section, with a hot option plus **two salad/sandwich bars** at every meal. Breakfasts feature a **yogurt bar, bagel bar, and cereal bar**. A **dedicated chef for special diets** ensures every camper can be accommodated, and evening milk-and-cookies is a beloved tradition. 

**Vibe & Culture** 

Barney blends **big-camp energy with small-group intimacy**. The three-counselor cabin model allows for close staff attention, while large all-camp meals and spirited Shabbat create a sense of unity. Jewish identity is presented as joyful, inclusive, and participatory, often the highlight for campers with little prior 

exposure. Campers are **phone-free** throughout, even on trips, reinforcing independence and relationship-building. Core values are woven into programming and carried home, contributing to strong year-round community connections across the Southeast.

**Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra** **Costs** 

Most campers arrive by **bus from Atlanta suburbs**, where opening day includes the festive movement of 3,000+ bags and 15 coach buses. Buses also run from regional hubs like Birmingham, Savannah, Nashville, and Charlotte. The **Atlanta airport (1h–1h20 away)** is serviced directly for campers traveling alone. Luggage shipping services are supported. Barney maintains a **screen-free culture**, with no cell phones for campers; communication is primarily through parent updates and staff support. Tuition includes all core activities; specialty trips may incur additional cost. 

**Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary** 

Camp Barney Medintz is ideal for campers seeking a **structured, values-based Jewish camp** **experience** with a wide variety of activities and strong attention to safety and wellbeing. Its **cabin-based** **schedule** fosters unusually strong camper connections, while the scale of the property and community offers abundant opportunities for growth. Barney combines progressive skill-building in sports, arts, and aquatics with **joyful Jewish living and spirited traditions**. It’s a great fit for families who want both **big-camp excitement and small-group care** in an inclusive, secure environment. 

**Internal Insights (Highlights & Lowlights)** 

**Highlights:** Three-counselor cabins set a high bar for supervision and connection; robust community care infrastructure (MSW-led team, Zen Den, first-time camper specialist); spirited dining hall and Jewish programming; strong safety posture with 24/7 security; seamless transportation logistics; wide age-progressive curriculum. 

**Lowlights:** Less individual choice for younger campers may not appeal to families seeking fully elective programs; large size (600+ per session) may feel overwhelming for some; rustic cabins and no-phone policy may be challenging for certain campers.

title: Berkshire Trails Write-Up
text:
**Quick snapshot** 

● **Type:** Coed overnight camp + separate day camp, strong **sports** program with growing **traditional camp** vibe 

● **Location:** Great Barrington, Massachusetts (Berkshires) 

● **Capacity:** ~250 overnight campers when full 

● **Sessions:** 2-week blocks; campers can stack to **2, 4, 6 or 8 weeks** 

● **Ownership:** 

○ New owner/director: **David Fried** (longtime camp pro) 

(413-644-0077/david@berkshiretrailscamp.com) 

○ Partners: **Jill & Jason Kleinman (Taconic), Jeff Gould (Independent Lake), Mark Transport (Southwoods)** 

**Director background & “feel”** 

● David has been in camp since age 5; now 48 and still in it. 

● 25 years as director of **Camp Shalom of Central Florida** (small, middle-class Jewish camp, ~150 campers). 

● Then **Camp Barney** (GA, JCC overnight) → promoted to **Chief Program Officer** for the whole JCC (overnight + day camps). 

● Left the “corporate/non-profit” world to return to **private camping**; has two kids (7 & 10) now camp-age. 

● Personal connection: went to **Kutcher’s Sports Academy as a camper**; knows former director Mike Gilberg. 

● Very warm, hands-on, “front-lines with kids and staff” style. His self-described superpower: **creating camp community / spirit**, not just running sports.

**Program identity & vision** 

Historically: 

● Kutcher’s = **hardcore sports camp** (sports morning/afternoon/evening, limited “campy” programming). 

Now under Berkshire Trails / David: 

● **Keep**: high-level coaching and serious sports for kids who want it. 

● **Add**: much stronger **traditional camp layer** – all-camp evening activities, special events, camp spirit, cross-age camaraderie. 

● Clear goal: fix what families felt was “missing” – a **whole-camp community feel**, not only sport-by-sport silos. 

Good way to explain to families: 

“It’s still a great option for sports-driven kids, but now with much more of that classic camp feeling – evening programs, special events, and a real sense of whole-camp community.” 

**Program structure – Overnight Camp** 

**Ages:** Standard sleepaway range (elementary through high school); exact rookie-week ages TBD (David is confirming). 

**Upper Camp (older campers)** 

● **Fully choice-based schedule**: 

○ **6 activity periods/day – all elective.** 

○ If a teen wants *basketball 3x/day and soccer 3x/day*, that’s possible. 

● Designed to keep the **old Kutcher’s sports kids happy** while adding camp spirit via evenings and specials. 

**Lower Camp (younger campers)**

● **Hybrid model**: 

○ **4 periods** with their cabin (structured, well-rounded exposure). 

○ **2 periods** of individual choice each day. 

● After dinner: more typical **free-choice time** / camp-wide activities for all ages. 

**Evenings & specials** 

● Big focus on **non-sport evening activities** and special events: 

○ All-camp programs, not just more games/practices. 

○ Designed to build **camp identity, traditions and spirit**. 

**Sports & activities** 

● **Sports remain a core pillar** – strong for athletic kids and ex-Kutcher’s families: ○ Head coaches returning for **golf, basketball, tennis**, etc. 

○ Optional **off-campus golf course play**. 

● **Four-week campers**: 

○ Special **Six Flags trip** as a highlight. 

● **Horseback riding:** 

○ Not currently on site; David is exploring off-site options (no promise yet). 

**Day Camp (on the same property)** 

● **Program:** Staying essentially **the same** as last year (same hours & activities). ● Staffing tweak: David aims to add more **domestic staff** vs. being mostly international.

● **Drop-off/pick-up:** 

○ Parents drive onto property but use a **separate carpool loop** on the outskirts. 

○ Day campers are then walked into the main gym; **no real mixing** with overnights during those transitions. 

● **Transportation (day camp):** 

○ Bus service from **Hudson, Lenox, Pittsfield, Stockbridge**. 

● **Care options:** Early drop-off and late pick-up available for an additional fee. 

**Sessions, size & enrollment** 

● **Structure now:** 

○ All **2-week sessions**, stackable to 4/6/8 weeks. 

○ There is a **Rookie Week** option – details/ages to be confirmed (he’s sending more info). 

● **Last summer (old structure):** 

○ 2 x **3-week** sessions + 1 x **1-week** session. 

○ ~**75–100 campers per session**; felt “light” on site (full is ~250). 

● **Current summer goals:** 

○ Already ~**40 campers per session** pre-January. 

○ Target: **100 campers per session** (still room to grow but healthier energy). 

○ David expects a **big enrollment bump after Jan 1**, and momentum once families experience the “new” Berkshire Trails. 

**Pricing, discounts & flexibility**

● **Standard:** 

○ Sibling discount (shown on website). 

● **Early enrollment:** 

○ 10% off until Oct 31 (past). 

○ **5% off** through Dec 31. 

● **Black Friday promo (internal info for you):** 

○ **15% off, pay-in-full**, through Dec 15. 

● David is very open about wanting to **“make deals” in year one** to fill beds – likely flexible with Camp Experts families, especially on longer stays or multiple kids. 

**Transportation & logistics** 

**Overnight camp:** 

● **Airport pickups:** 

○ **Yes.** Prefer **JFK**, but can arrange **LaGuardia, Newark, or Boston Logan** if needed. 

● **Linens for internationals:** 

○ **Yes, provided** (no need to schlep bedding from abroad). 

● **Bus to camp (overnight):** 

○ Regular coach from **Westchester, NY**. 

○ David is open to **organizing other group routes** (e.g., Boston) if enough kids. ● **Chaperoned flights:** 

○ Planning **chaperoned flights** from **South Florida** and **Atlanta**, where he has strong personal networks (his wife may be the primary chaperone).

**Who it’s best for** 

**Great fits:** 

● Kids who love **sports** and want **serious instruction** but: 

○ Don’t want a “sports academy” feel only; 

○ Want **color wars, evening programs, camp friends and traditions**. ● Families loyal to **Kutcher’s** who: 

○ Still care about sports, 

○ Are open to/looking for a more **traditional camp community** on top. ● **First-time overnight families** who like the idea of: 

○ **2-week entry** with the option to extend to 4 weeks if the child thrives. 

**Watch-outs / clarify with families:** 

● It is **not** purely a relaxed, artsy camp – **sports are still central**, especially for older campers who may choose sports all six periods. 

● **Rookie week** details (ages, exact structure) are still being finalized; double-check current info before promising specifics. 

**Key talking points you can use with families** 

● “Berkshire Trails used to be Kutcher’s Sports Academy – now it keeps the strong sports, but **layers in classic camp spirit**: evening activities, special events, and a real sense of whole-camp community.” 

● “For older campers it’s **full choice** – they can live on the courts/fields if they want. For younger ones, it’s a **blend** of cabin-based activities and their own electives, so they try lots of things.”

● “It’s run by a **career camp professional** with 25+ years as a director, plus backing from the teams behind Taconic, Independent Lake and Southwoods.” 

● “They’re intentionally **smallish right now** and really focused on growing the right way, which means lots of attention on each camper and a director who is very reachable.” 

● “Airport pickup, linens, and even **chaperoned flights** from key hubs make it logistically doable for international and long-distance families.”

title: Beber Zoom Write-Up
text:
**Bieber Camp** 

**Program Snapshot** 

Bieber Camp is a values-driven, independent Jewish overnight camp in **Wisconsin**, about **35 minutes from Milwaukee Airport** and **~1h15 from Chicago’s North Shore**. The program is **choice-based** via a robust **Hobby** system—campers pick **3 hobbies/day** in **5-day blocks**—paired with daily **cabin activities** for bonding and exposure across camp. Set on a lake (plus a pool), Bieber offers deep aquatics, athletics, arts, challenge course, outdoor adventure/farm, fine arts, and a standout **music/recording** program. Jewish life centers on spirited, guitar-led **Shabbat**, Israeli dancing, song sessions, and weekly values. 

**Camper, Staff & Director Profile** 

~**330 campers per session** across **four divisions** (roughly 2–5th, 6–7th, 8–10th, and teen leadership). Geographic mix is broad: ~**55% Chicago area**, strong cohorts from **Colorado (Denver/Boulder)**, plus **Cleveland, Louisville, Memphis**, and beyond. Director **Michael Wex** (36th summer) leads a supervision model that includes **four counselors per cabin** and **division-based social workers** (to coach staff and support campers; **not** a therapeutic camp). **Camper retention ~91%**. 

**Living Quarters** 

Cabins house **12–15 campers with 4 counselors** (CIT joins second session). **Plumbing in every cabin** (toilets/sinks); **~75%** have **in-cabin showers** (all girls’ cabins do). A modern **shower house** offers private stalls; **well-water filtration** and **on-demand hot water** prevent outages. **All cabins are air-conditioned**, and counselors live in-cabin; **night/rest-hour coverage** is continuous. 

**Waterfront & Aquatics** 

Lakefront + pool. **Water skiing (11 ski boats)**, **wakeboarding**, **sailing**, **windsurfing** (~8 boards), canoeing, kayaking, paddleboarding, fishing, swim, synchronized swim, snorkel, lifeguard training, water aerobics, plus a large **Aqua Park** (with trampoline). **No tubing**—Bieber prefers **skill-building** at the waterfront. 

**Daily Timetable & Choice**

● **Wake 8:00 AM** → cabin cleanup → five activity periods anchored by **3 Hobbies** (choice; **~91% first choices**) and **2 Cabin Activities** (bonding/rotation across areas). 

● **Rest hour** after lunch; **shower time**, flag-lowering, dinner. 

● **General Activity (7:30)** = open rec across fields/courts (water closed except fishing). 

● **Evening programs** (casino night, scavenger hunts, talent show, musical), then **lights-out** by age. 

Hobbies run **15 days** of the ~27-day session; the balance includes field trips, trips-out, Olympic Day, and Shabbat. 

**Program Highlights** 

**Aquatics:** Water skiing (guaranteed hobby when selected), wakeboarding, sailing, windsurfing, swim, Aqua Park, pool. 

**Athletics:** Archery; **baseball** (batting cage, all-star game); basketball; mountain biking; flag football; gaga; **gymnastics** (beam/tumble track/uneven bars); **horseback riding** (off-site, ~5 min); martial arts; soccer (**field lights** coming online); tennis; **pickleball**; ultimate; volleyball; 

**BMX pump track** under construction (part of an **extreme sports park** targeted for 2027). **Arts & Crafts:** Beading, ceramics, copper enameling, pewter casting, pottery, silver jewelry, mosaics, painting, tie-dye, woodworking, more. 

**Fine Arts:** Chess, circus arts, dance, drama games, **indoor cooking**, mahjong, movie-making, **musical** (e.g., *Wizard of Oz*), newspaper, podcasting; **theater renovation** with **25-ft rear projection** backdrop. 

**Challenge Course:** Classic high ropes (climbing walls, zip line, leap of faith) **plus** a **double-decker self-belay** course (~26 elements). 

**Outdoor Adventure & Farm:** Greenhouse, outdoor cooking, survival skills, raft-building; animals (**cows, sheep, goats, rabbits, chickens**). 

**Music:** Two **sound-proof recording studios** + three rehearsal rooms (new in **Crown Hall**); visiting studio pros; bands, guitar lessons; archive at **beaverrocks.com**. 

**Health, Safety & Food** 

**Supervision:** 4 counselors/cabin; **division-based social workers** to coach staff and coordinate care (not ongoing therapy). 

**Food: Buffet-style** with **choice at every meal**. 

● Breakfast: hot options (e.g., eggs or pancakes/waffles) + **cereal bar** + **yogurt/oatmeal/fruit** bar; **acai bowls weekly**.

● Lunch/Dinner: rotating mains; robust **salad bar**; **pasta always available**; **sunflower-butter & jelly** station; **BBQ weekly**; **poke bowls weekly**. 

**Allergies/Special Diets:** Dedicated **allergy window** (staff in red aprons) supports **dairy-free, gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian**; **nut-aware** (no nuts). 

**Medical:** Standard camp infirmary coverage (noted via supervision section); not a therapeutic setting. 

**Vibe & Culture** 

**Traveler-friendly location**, big-camp resources with **small-group feel**. Jewish practice is **inclusive and spirited** (not Orthodox/Shomer Shabbat aligned), with Friday night dress-up, **~45-min guitar-led service**, Israeli dancing, and high-energy song sessions (Jewish/pop medleys). **Mishpachah** (“family”) big-/little program meets weekly, knitting divisions together. 

**Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs** 

**Airports: MKE** (~35 min) and **ORD** (~1h15 from North Shore). 

**Flights:** Mix of **group flights** (e.g., Colorado) and **chaperoned routes** when **~3+ campers** align; in some cases camp **covers a parent chaperone**. Older campers may route independently, case-by-case. 

**Sessions:** Two **four-week** sessions with **similar programming**; Southern families often lean **Session 1**, NY/Northeast often **Session 2**. 

**Extras:** Horseback riding is off-site (standard offering); no “pay-for-more” private lessons model. 

**Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary** 

Bieber is ideal for campers who want **true choice** every day, **serious aquatics**, strong **sports and arts**, and an upbeat, **values-forward** Jewish community. The **Hobby** system rewards curiosity and progression while cabin blocks keep relationships tight and inclusive. Campers who love **skill-building** (e.g., water-skiing, windsurfing, ropes, music/recording) thrive here; families seeking Orthodox/Shomer Shabbat practice or purely competitive, sport-only programs may prefer other options. Bieber blends **accessibility (MKE/ORD)** with **big offerings and warm supervision**. 

**Internal Insights (Highlights & Lowlights)** 

**Highlights:** Choice-based **Hobby** structure (3/day in 5-day blocks; **~91% first choices**); **11 ski boats** + deep waterfront; double-decker self-belay **challenge course**; expanding **music**

**studios** and **theater**; strong **arts/athletics** (BMX track in progress); **4 counselors per cabin** + social-worker support; **AC cabins**; consistent **Shabbat culture**; flexible **flight chaperoning**; **~$200k** annual need-based aid; **One Happy Camper** eligible. 

**Lowlights:** Not Orthodox/Shomer-Shabbat aligned; **no tubing** (philosophy: skills over rides); highly competitive, single-sport specialists seeking intensive private coaching may find the model less targeted; limited Israel component recently (paused two years, planned to resume).

title: Adirondack Write-Up
text:
**Adirondack Camp – Overview & Key Highlights** 

**Who They Are** 

Adirondack Camp is a **120-year-old, co-ed, traditional sleepaway camp** located on a stunning **1.5-mile private peninsula on Lake George (NY)**. One of the oldest continually operating camps in the US, Adirondack is known for: 

● Deep multi-generational traditions 

● A loyal, long-tenured leadership team (over **300 combined years** of experience) ● A strong wilderness + waterfront identity 

● A deeply relational, values-driven culture 

● Contact: shawn@adirondackcamp.com 

It is a **nature-immersive**, **screen-free**, deliberately **unpretentious** camp focused on confidence-building, character, community, and authentic camp magic. 

**Leadership & Staffing** 

The core leadership team has decades-long tenures: 

● **Sean Carraher, Owner** (family legacy since the 1950s) 

● **Matt Basinet, Director** (camper since age 15; 30+ years in education & coaching) ● **Chris, Assistant Director** (19 summers; oversees staff hiring & training, kitchen, trips) ● **Ricky Galusha, VP Operations** (23 summers; works closely with families & enrollment) ● **Rachel, Program Director** (15+ years; elementary school counselor, SEL expert) 

Additional notes:

● Staff return rate hovers **55–60%**. 

● Staff come from US + international partners; all international staff are personally interviewed. 

● Staff are hired **by skill**, not as general counselors — each teaches a specialty area. ● Staff training includes **10+ days onsite**, plus early arrival for specialized certifications. 

**Health, Safety & Wellbeing** 

Adirondack’s health center is unusually strong: 

● **3 full-time Registered Nurses** (all RNs, not mixed-level staff) 

● **Camp Doctor:** Dr. Allison Toth (ER Pediatric Chief, Chicago Children’s Hospital; co-author of AAP camping guidelines) 

● **MeSH Team:** Two mental health professionals onsite, including licensed counselors ● **Telehealth access** via UVM Medical 

● **Wilderness certifications:** WFA/WFR for trip leaders, Red Cross lifeguards, advanced training in all risk areas 

The ethos: emotional safety is as important as physical safety. 

**Philosophy & Camper Profile** 

ADK is a **traditional, non-competitive, child-centered camp** ideal for families seeking: ● A **nature-first**, unplugged experience 

● A warm, **inclusive** co-ed community (gender-inclusive policies; single-use facilities added; case-by-case family partnership on cabin placement)

● Character development (“plaques” like *integrity*, *community*, *empathy* are woven into daily life) 

● Depth of skill-building in specific activities 

● A camp that emphasizes **individual growth**, not athletics or achievement metrics 

No “type” of camper — introverts, extroverts, sporty, artsy, adventurous, and hesitant campers all thrive here. 

**Facilities & Waterfront Strength** 

A major differentiator: 

● **Exclusive, road-free, mile-and-a-half waterfront** on Lake George 

● Clear, spring-fed lake; safe and expansive 

● Open-air cabins loved by campers (breezy, nature-connected) 

● Newly updated health center 

Families consistently cite the **waterfront** as a top reason they choose ADK. 

**Program Structure & Sessions** 

Ages **7–17** (CIT at 17; Ranger program at 16) 

**Session Options:** 

● **Full Summer:** ~7 weeks 

● **First Session:** 4 weeks 

○ Within this: **Taste of ADK** = two separate 2-week sessions (open to *any* age; not limited to one year; priced differently for older campers)

● **Second Session:** Now **3 weeks** (recently reduced from 4 to make late-summer staffing and family schedules easier) 

● **Adventure Camp:** 5-week option (focused on trips + wilderness progression) 

Pricing includes all core programs; add-ons priced separately (camp store, glassblowing, magic, whitewater trips, etc.). 

Enrollment trends: 

● First session historically fuller than second 

● More kids than ever staying 6–7 weeks due to schedule redesign 

● Ideal session sizes ~200–220 per period 

**Activity Program** 

ADK divides programming into five pillars: 

● **Waterfront** (waterskiing, sailing, kayaking, paddleboard yoga, canoeing, swim instruction) 

● **Land Sports** 

● **Arts** (ceramics, fine arts, culinary, drama, music; enhanced by guest artists weekly) ● **Wilderness** (outdoor skills, nature, backcountry skills) 

● **Trips** (extensive Adirondack trips program) 

Key notes: 

● **Swimming is mandatory** (Red Cross progression; safety-first given waterfront-heavy life) 

● **Preference-based scheduling:** 

○ Campers submit top 10 activities before arrival

○ Meet-the-staff “course fair” 

○ Formal rescheduling period for adjustments 

● **Staff are specialists**, not generalists 

● **Adventure Period** (1×/day) adds novelty + counselor creativity 

● **Guest Artist Program** includes: magic, glassblowing, hip-hop, cheesemaking, and more 

**Skill Progression Model:** 

Activities have long-term pathways — e.g., kayaking progresses from strokes → rolling → mini whitewater → Hudson River day trip → **4-day Ottawa Class III/IV capstone**. 

Families wanting depth of instruction in outdoor skills will appreciate this. 

**Trips Program** 

One of the strongest wilderness programs in the Northeast: 

● Day hikes 

● Canoe trips 

● Multi-day treks across Adirondack High Peaks 

● Sailing trips 

● Kayaking progression including whitewater 

● Fire tower quests & LG5 peak challenges 

Trips are **age-appropriate**, interest-based, and optional — but widely loved. 

**Traditions & Culture** 

ADK is rich in tradition, but not resistant to evolution.

Highlights include: 

● **Live bugler** (Reveille, Taps, activity transitions) 

● **Blue-White Games** — not a 1–2 day color war, but a *summer-long* friendly competition ● **ADK Cella** — arts showcase + culinary festival each session 

● **Wish Boards** — hand-painted candle boards gifted to every camper; cherished keepsake 

● **Carnevale** — CIT-run end-of-summer camp-wide carnival 

● **Candlelight** — emotional closing ceremony continuing a decades-old tradition 

**Diversity of Enrollment** 

Last three years include campers from: 

● **33 US states** 

● Highest concentrations: 

○ NY (40%+ post-COVID; growing) 

○ NJ (~10%) 

○ CT/FL/CA/MA/PA (~3–7% each) 

Healthy mix of domestic + international campers, though international representation dipped slightly post-COVID. 

**Ideal Adirondack Camper** 

This camp is a top fit for: 

● Families wanting a **traditional, warm, inclusive**, *unplugged* camp

● Kids who love **waterfront + wilderness** or want to *learn* those skills ● Families who value **stability, longevity, and professionalism** in leadership 

● Kids who thrive in mixed-age, mixed-gender communities (ADK is truly co-ed in programming) 

● Families seeking **2-week on-ramping** with the option to return for longer ● Kids who benefit from strong SEL-focused support 

This is **not** the camp for families wanting: 

● Highly competitive athletics 

● Luxurious or “polished” facilities 

● A tech-enabled or indoor-focused experience 

ADK is rustic, intentional, relational, and proudly outdoorsy. 

**Referral Process Notes** 

● When advisors include parent email, ADK will CC the advisor and indicate “referred by Camp Experts.” 

● For leads marked **Do Not Contact**, ADK prefers permission to email (not call) so Campminder automations don’t break the communication chain. 

● Advisors can request **warm three-way introductions** when families are ready. 

ADK is eager to boost Camp Experts partnership numbers back to prior levels and welcomes advisor input.

title: Alpengirl_Write_Up
text:
**Alpengirl – Program Write-Up** 

**1. Program Overview and Identity** 

• **What it is:** An overnight summer adventure travel program for teen girls (ages 11–17) built around small-group, instructor-led expeditions (no fixed campus; trips start/end at airports). o Groups are typically ~10–12 campers with 2–3 guides. 

o Trip lengths run roughly 8–14 days. 

• **Core mission:** Spark a lifelong love of the outdoors by making wilderness experiences approachable, confidence-building, and fun. 

o Alpengirl explicitly positions itself as not a wilderness therapy program; outdoor skills are taught in a supportive, upbeat environment. 

**2. Trip Structure, Activities, and Destinations** 

• **Multi-adventure format:** Each expedition blends a “backcountry” element with additional outdoor experiences. 

o Examples discussed include backpacking, sea kayaking, surfing, yoga, hot springs, and horsepacking (including a popular multi-day horsepacking trip in Montana). 

• **2025 destination highlights:** 

o Maine: an ~8-day expedition with separate middle school and high school groupings; trips start/end in Portland, Maine. 

o Iceland: a high-school program (up to rising seniors) that was described as nearing capacity / almost sold out at the time of the call. 

o Specialty offerings mentioned include a mother–daughter adventure trip and a glacier/llama-packing style expedition (noted as filling quickly). 

**3. Staffing, Safety, and Culture** 

• **Guide requirements:** Guides are at least 21; each trip includes a guide with Wilderness First Responder (WFR) credentials. 

o Small group sizes are a deliberate safety and community feature. 

• **Camp culture:** Social, high-energy, and bonding-focused—described with examples like “roadside dance parties” and friendship-bracelet moments alongside real outdoor challenge. 

• **Accreditation:** The program referenced ACA accreditation.

**4. Best-Fit Campers and Practical Notes** 

• **Best for:** Adventurous, open-minded girls who want an outdoor experience (including those new to camping, as the program teaches core skills). 

• **Capacity:** Several trips were described as filling quickly (with some already full), so early planning matters for families who want specific destinations/dates.

title: Corredor Counselors (1)
text:
**Overview** 

Felipe Corredor joined Camp Experts to share a college admissions–focused framework for evaluating summer programs and how different types of experiences actually land with admissions officers. As a college and graduate school admissions consultant based in Miami, Felipe works with students as early as middle school through the full college application process, helping families build coherent, competitive profiles over time. 

**How Colleges View Summer Experiences** 

Felipe emphasized that colleges value depth over novelty. The strongest summer experiences show progression, responsibility, skill development, tangible outcomes, and coherence with a student’s broader story. Programs that are purely recreational, disconnected from a student’s interests, or “pay-to-play” without substance tend to be far less impactful unless framed strategically. 

**Framework for Evaluating Summer Programs** 

Felipe shared a five-part lens he uses to assess summer experiences from an admissions perspective: 

– Responsibility and ownership 

– Progression over time 

– Concrete skill development (hard or soft skills) 

– Tangible deliverables or impact 

– Coherence with the student’s overall narrative 

Programs that hit three to five of these criteria are generally strong; those hitting one or none require careful reconsideration or reframing. 

**Traditional Sleepaway Camps** 

Traditional camps were rated as a medium-impact option for college admissions. They can be valuable when students demonstrate leadership progression, increased responsibility (such as CIT or junior counselor roles), and multi-year growth. Camps become less effective when experiences remain static year after year without clear development or outcomes. 

**Pre-College Academic Programs** 

These programs ranged from low to medium impact depending on rigor and output. Programs that are lecture-based or brand-driven without meaningful work or deliverables tend to carry little weight. In contrast, academically rigorous programs with real projects, mentorship, and tangible outcomes can be highly valuable, regardless of institutional prestige. 

**Teen Travel and Service Trips** 

Felipe generally rated teen travel programs and volunteer trips abroad as low impact unless they include clear responsibility, meaningful output, or a strong personal connection. Colleges prefer sustained, local service over short-term international volunteering without depth. Travel 

experiences can be strengthened through post-program projects, documentation, or long-term follow-through.

**Research, Internships, and Independent Projects** 

These experiences were rated as high impact when executed well. Strong examples include research with clear questions and outcomes, internships with real accountability, or independent projects showing initiative and measurable results. Weak versions include shadowing, superficial roles, or family-connected work without substance. 

**When Camp Still Makes Sense** 

Felipe advised continuing camp when students are on a leadership track, showing increasing responsibility, and deeply connected to the camp community. Camp becomes less strategic when it prevents exploration, skill-building, or differentiation during key high school years. Hybrid models—splitting summer or pairing camp with projects—can be effective compromises. 

**Essay Strategy and Long-Term Planning** 

Strong college essays rely on experiences where students took responsibility, grew through challenge, and produced real outcomes. Felipe stressed the importance of planning summer experiences early so students are not forced to “manufacture meaning” later in the application process. 

**How Camp Experts Can Use This Framework** 

Felipe encouraged Camp Experts to begin conversations by clarifying a family’s goals for the summer—fun, growth, exploration, or performance—and then guiding them toward best-fit options using an admissions-aware lens. Families seeking a multi-year strategy, targeting highly selective schools, or feeling uncertain about direction may benefit from a referral. 

**Working With Felipe** 

Felipe works with students from middle school through senior year, offering comprehensive admissions consulting, academic tutoring, standardized test prep, and executive functioning support. Engagements range from limited services to full admissions retainers, with pricing depending on level of involvement. He welcomes direct outreach from Camp Experts and families alike.

title: ACA Zoom Recap
text:
**Core Differentiators and Philosophy** 

• **Personalized Attention**: 

o ACA emphasizes boutique, personalized services, including direct  communication with program leadership. 

o 24/7 availability ensures immediate parent-staff interaction in emergencies  or for routine updates. 

• **Safety as a Priority**: 

o Safety is the cornerstone of ACA programs. The staff-student ratio is  carefully maintained: 

▪ **10:1 in the US** (often closer to 8:1). 

▪ **8:1 internationally**. 

▪ **6.5:1 for middle school programs**. 

o Programs avoid public transportation, favoring private options or central  accommodations to reduce safety risks. 

• **Unmatched Accommodations**: 

o Programs are based in premium hotels or residence halls with air  conditioning, daily linen changes, and in-suite bathrooms. 

o Locations are chosen for their proximity to city centers and major landmarks. • **Freedom of Choice**: 

o Students select activities daily, based on personal interests. Options are  curated to ensure engagement, including cultural experiences, outdoor  adventures, and recreational activities. 

o While flexibility is encouraged, students are not allowed to opt out entirely to  ensure consistent participation. 

• **Specialized Staff**: 

o Staff members are handpicked by leadership and undergo rigorous vetting. o Average age: 30 years (21 minimum). 

o Staff include teachers, coaches, and professionals who act as mentors. • **Program Scope and Accessibility**: 

o ACA’s programs are accredited by the **American Camp Association (ACA)**,  ensuring adherence to the highest industry standards.

**Daily Schedule and Activities** 

• **Morning**: 

o Breakfast (8:30 AM–9:15 AM). 

o Elective classes (9:30 AM–1:00 PM) with two blocks. Students select  electives before arrival but can switch in the first few days. 

• **Afternoon**: 

o Lunch (1:00 PM–2:00 PM) is included in US programs but not in Europe,  where students can choose grab-and-go or sit-down meals. 

o Freedom of choice activities (2:00 PM–5:00 PM): Examples include museum  visits, workouts, or beach trips, tailored to the program location. 

• **Evening**: 

o Dinner on the “Dining on the Town” plan includes local restaurants,  encouraging cultural immersion. 

o Evening activities (8:30 PM–11:00 PM) provide additional options for  engagement. 

• **Night**: 

o Students socialize before curfew (10:00 PM for middle school, 11:00 PM for  high school). 

o Final check-ins at midnight ensure all students are accounted for. 

**Program Offerings** 

***Study Abroad Programs*** 

1\. **Locations**: 

a. Florence, Barcelona, Nice, Seville (Spanish immersion), and Canada. 2. **Features**: 

a. 2- or 3-week options, with extensions to Greece or Croatia. 

b. Programs include cultural experiences, culinary classes, and weekend trips  (e.g., South of France from Barcelona). 

c. Seville’s immersion program avoids homestays, ensuring consistent ACA  standards. 

3\. **Class Size**: 

a. Average class size is 10 students, with specialized instructors.

***Pre-College Programs*** 

1\. **Locations**: 

a. UCLA, Pepperdine, Wisconsin, Georgetown. 

2\. **Features**: 

a. Students explore college-level subjects and tour prestigious universities. b. Optional Boston extension includes visits to 9 major universities. 

***Teen Tours*** 

1\. **Programs**: 

a. European Adventure and similar options. 

2\. **Features**: 

a. Participants spend at least 3–4 days in each location to reduce travel fatigue. b. Greece or Croatia extensions connect with students from other programs. 

***Career Exploration and Mentorship*** 

1\. **Focus Areas**: 

a. Film and Acting (Hollywood), Fashion Design, Entrepreneurship. 

2\. **Location**: 

a. Pepperdine University in Los Angeles. 

3\. **Features**: 

a. Hands-on projects, guest speakers, and immersive activities. 

***Service Programs*** 

1\. **Locations**: 

a. Costa Rica, Panama, Hawaii, and Southern California. 

2\. **Activities**: 

a. Conservation projects, community service, and environmental restoration. 3. **Additional Options**: 

a. Service electives are also available in other programs. 

***Sports Camps*** 

1\. **Focus**: 

a. Surfing, soccer, tennis, and basketball.

2\. **Location**: 

a. Pepperdine University. 

3\. **Features**: 

a. Partnerships with organizations like Nike ensure top-notch coaching. ***Middle School Programs*** 

1\. **Options**: 

a. On-campus programs at Pepperdine and the University of Vermont. 2. **Features**: 

a. Catered specifically for younger students, offering unique experiences. 

**Costs and Additional Expenses** 

• **Included**: 

o Most meals, accommodation, elective classes, weekend trips. 

• **Additional Costs**: 

o Lunch in Europe, optional specialized activities, and personal shopping. o Certain electives (e.g., advanced culinary courses) may require  supplemental fees. 

**Safety and Logistics** 

• **Travel**: 

o Chaperoned group flights are offered internationally to manage delays and  complications. 

• **Insurance**: 

o Risk-free enrollment allows refunds until February 1. 

o Cancel-for-any-reason and trip interruption insurance are available. • **Monitoring**: 

o ACA stays updated with global events through local embassies and the US  State Department.

**Audience Questions and Responses** 

1\. **Staff Age and Ratio**: 

a. Staff average age: 30 years. 

b. Ratios: 10:1 (US), 8:1 (international), 6.5:1 (middle school). 

2\. **Class and Program Sizes**: 

a. Class size: Average 10 students. 

b. Program size: 40–125 students. 

3\. **International Students**: 

a. Welcomed in US programs but balanced to maintain cultural integration. 4. **Gender Balance**: 

a. Families are notified if gender imbalances are observed. 

5\. **Special Requests**: 

a. ACA accommodates unique needs (e.g., kosher meals, Zoom auditions). 

**Closing Remarks** 

• **Leadership Accessibility**: 

o Jason is available across US programs, and Christy oversees European  programs. 

o Christy leads the Greece extension and monitors all European locations  within a two-hour flight radius. 

• **Encouragement to Reach Out**: 

o Families and advisors are encouraged to contact ACA with questions or  special requests. 

• **Enrollment Update**: 

o Early enrollment for 2025 is strong, with programs expanding to meet  demand.

title: DB Sports
text:
**1. Introduction & Overview** 

• **Recording & Participants:** 

o The meeting is recorded and introduced by Sam Goldberg. 

o Key speakers include Darren S (Head of Operations), Cynthia (International  Liaison Officer), Paul O’Brien (Academy Director), and others such as Riley  McDnonough. 

• **Organization Background:** 

o The academy, known as dB Sports (also referred to as Dbs International  Academy), is based in Dublin, Ireland. 

o Established in 2010, it was Ireland’s first full-time soccer academy and has  been running both full-time programs and seasonal camps since then. 

**2. Camp Structure & Schedule** 

• **Duration & Timing:** 

o The summer camp runs from the first week of June to the last week of  August. 

o Daily soccer sessions are held Monday through Friday from 10 AM to 1 PM. • **Program Components:** 

o **Pitch Sessions:** 

▪ Focus on position-specific training for goalkeepers, defenders,  

midfielders, and forwards. 

▪ Includes game scenario training and technical drills such as futsal to  enhance quick decision-making. 

o **Classroom Modules:** 

▪ Sessions feature game analysis, tactical breakdowns, and the study  of modern trends in soccer. 

▪ Utilizes UEFA Youth League match footage and includes guest  

speakers to give players a holistic understanding of the game. 

o **Gym Sessions:** 

▪ Tailored to different age groups, focusing on injury prevention, body  mechanics, and conditioning. 

▪ Younger players (e.g., 11–12-year-olds) work on body weight  

exercises rather than heavy lifting. 

• **English Language Option:**

o An additional English as a Second Language (ESL) program is offered for  players interested in improving their language skills. 

o This component is delivered in partnership with the Emerald Cultural  Institute and includes cultural workshops and excursions. 

**3. Facilities & Equipment** 

• **National Sports Campus:** 

o The camp is hosted at a high-quality facility where professional athletes  (e.g., national teams in football, rugby, hockey, and even Olympians) train. • **Playing Fields:** 

o **Indoor:** A fully covered, FIFA-approved full-size field ensures play continues  regardless of weather. 

o **Outdoor:** Two natural grass soccer fields and a multipurpose 5-a-side field. o **Small-Sided Games:** Eight artificial 6-a-side fields designed for technical  drills. 

• **Additional Infrastructure:** 

o Indoor photo arena, classrooms, meeting rooms, video analysis rooms, and  a fully equipped gym. 

o Dedicated spaces for physiotherapy and sports therapy, ensuring  comprehensive athlete support. 

**4. Coaching & Staff** 

• **Qualified Coaching Team:** 

o The team includes high-level UEFA licensed coaches: 

▪ Paul O’Brien, an elite UEFA licensed coach with a rich background  (experience with clubs like Shamrock Rovers). 

▪ Kevin Becky, who has coached with RCD Espanyol and St. Pat's  

Athletic, among others. 

o **Supporting Staff:** 

▪ Auxiliary coaches, including UEFA A and B licensed professionals,  

strength and conditioning coaches, and physiotherapists. 

▪ Some school teachers with coaching qualifications are also involved  to ensure robust support for every age group. 

• **Coaching Model:**

o Every player group is led by a dedicated head coach. 

o The structure allows for additional coaches if the numbers require splitting  players into smaller, more focused groups. 

o Player ability, age, and physical attributes are carefully considered to place  them in the most suitable bracket. 

**5. Player Demographics & Grouping** 

• **Age Range & Integration:** 

o The camp serves a broad age range—from around 10 years old up to under 19. 

o It uniquely integrates international players with local Irish players to foster  diverse learning and competition. 

• **Grouping Methodology:**
