• Two focus areas: 

o **Interior Design:** Reimagining spaces and working with AI tools. o **Product Design:** Developing creative concepts for everyday objects.

***Daily Structure*** 

• Morning lectures and afternoon labs in Raffles Milan’s facilities. • Evening activities include karaoke, movie nights, and socializing. 

***Cultural Immersion*** 

• Excursions to historical landmarks and cultural sites, similar to the Fashion  Program. 

**4. Culinary Arts Program with Congusto Gourmet Institute** 

• **Age Range:** High school students, ages **14–18**, and gap-year participants (18–19). • **Program Duration:** Four weeks in July (one or two-week modules). 

***Two Modules Offered*** 

**1. Italian Cooking:** 

a. Focus on pasta, regional dishes, and pizza-making. 

b. Includes a pizza and focaccia master class. 

**2. Italian Pastry:** 

a. Learn to make desserts, cookies, and pastries. 

***Additional Features*** 

• **Food Photography:** Learn to market culinary creations visually. • **Restaurant Business Basics:** Sessions on hospitality, customer service, and  managing a restaurant. 

• **Culinary Competition:** A Master Chef-style cooking contest. 

***Cultural Immersion*** 

• Visits to local markets for sourcing ingredients. 

***Cost*** 

• **€1,950 for one week** (residential).

• **€3,700 for two weeks** (residential). 

**Accommodation for Fashion, Design, and Culinary Programs** 

• **Location:** A Parto Dormitory, Milan. 

• **Features:** 

o Single rooms with private bathrooms. 

o Amenities include a gym, basketball court, cinema room, and 24/7 security. o Meals served in a cafeteria; lunch provided at respective program sites. 

**Future Plans** 

• Expanding year-round options, including: 

o Spring Break programs featuring soccer and culinary experiences. o Gap-year options for participants aged 18–19. 

• New locations planned for Lake Como and Rimini. 

**Key Questions and Answers** 

**1. Supervision:** 

a. 24/7 monitoring at accommodations and training centers. 

b. House parents and staff ensure safety and well-being. 

**2. Language Requirements:** 

a. Programs are conducted in English; no prior Italian knowledge is needed. **3. Cultural Integration:** 

a. Excursions to iconic Italian landmarks enhance the educational experience. **4. Air Conditioning:** 

a. All accommodations and program sites are fully air-conditioned.

title: SILC
text:
**Snapshot** 

SILC (often pronounced “Silk”) is a leadership-focused summer provider operating five boutique camps across Switzerland and France. The organization has run leadership programs for 14+ years, originally delivering 21st-century skills workshops in top international schools worldwide. 

Their camps center on real-life skill development — communication, teamwork, resilience, creativity, entrepreneurship — taught through adventure activities, corporate experiences, and daily leadership themes. SILC runs small, highly personalized programs (16–70 students max) with very diverse student bodies and a family-style culture. 

**Locations & Camp Portfolio** 

SILC operates **five camps**: 

**Switzerland (3 camps)** 

1\. Gstaad (ages 10–17) – classic Swiss summer camp with accessible adventure 2. Montreux (ages 14–17) – pre-university focus + high adventure 

3\. Leysin (ages 13–17) – outdoor survival + adventure challenges 

**France (2 camps)** 

4\. Corsica “Sea & Island Adventure” (ages 11–17) – catamaran-based island expedition 5. Cannes “Creative & Entrepreneurial Camp” (ages 13–17) – innovation, idea-building & coastal activities 

Each camp has its own theme and strengths, but all follow SILC’s leadership-skills DNA. 

**Group Size & Culture** 

SILC camps are intentionally small: 

● Largest camp = 70 students (Gstaad) 

● Montreux = ~40 students

● Leysin = ~30 students 

● Cannes = ~30 students 

● Corsica = 16–18 students (8 boys + 8 girls on two catamarans) 

Their boutique scale ensures strong supervision, low homesickness risk, and a supportive “family atmosphere.” Older students naturally mentor younger ones, reinforcing the values of care, contribution, and community. 

**Educational Philosophy & Daily Structure** 

The SILC model is built on **21st-century skills**, developed through **activities, not classrooms**. Skills include: 

● Goal-setting 

● Teamwork & communication 

● Decision-making 

● Conflict management 

● Creativity & entrepreneurship 

● Resilience & responsibility 

Each day includes: 

● A **leadership topic of the day** (e.g., goal-setting, communication) 

● A 10–15 minute leadership briefing 

● A full day of **outdoor activities and workshops** where students apply that skill ● Evening activities that reinforce reflection and community 

Their programs are extremely active — morning, afternoon, and evening are all scheduled, with many days fully off-campus in mountains, forests, oceans, or corporate environments.

**Camp-by-Camp Overview** 

**Gstaad (Switzerland)** 

● Ages 10–17 

● 60–70 students per session 

● Only camp offering 1-, 2-, 3-, or 4-week options 

● Most accessible entry point for first-time campers 

● High adventure but at a gentler level compared to other Swiss programs 

● Focus: interpersonal & intrapersonal skills (goal-setting, confidence, choices, communication) 

**Montreux — Swiss Summer Academy** 

● Ages 14–17 

● ~40 students 

● Located at the former Monte Rosa boarding school campus 

● Mixes pre-university content with adventure 

● Corporate visits: Nestlé, Hublot/Omega, UBS 

● Workshops: innovation, personal finance, branding, photography 

● Outdoor activities: glacier expedition with mountain-hut overnight, via ferrata, rock climbing, canyoning, boat building 

● Students even run their own gala evening 

● Very dynamic, academically enriching, and physically challenging **Leysin (Switzerland)**

● Ages 13–17 

● ~30 students 

● Strong outdoor and survival focus 

● More challenge-based and expedition-style than Gstaad 

● Blends the creativity of Montreux with the adventure of Swiss outdoor programs 

**Corsica — Sea & Island Adventure** 

● Ages 11–17 (sometimes exceptions for age 10) 

● 16–18 students total (two catamarans) 

● One session already sold out (high demand) 

● Island-circling expedition where boats are for travel, sleeping, and eating 

● Activities done on beaches, cliffs, forests, and ocean: snorkeling, hiking, paddling, exploration, island-based challenges 

● Leadership, resilience, and teamwork at the core 

● Includes an overnight camping experience on land 

● Very high adventure, extremely immersive 

**Cannes — Creative & Entrepreneurial Camp** 

● Ages 13–17 

● Located in a historic palace on the French Riviera 

● More leisure-oriented activities: beach days, biking, coastal excursions ● Core theme is creativity and entrepreneurship 

● Workshops: web design, photography, social media, brand creation, idea development

● Not an arts camp or a business bootcamp — more of a relaxed, exploratory environment for discovering talents 

**Safety, Staffing & Supervision** 

● High staff-to-student ratios across all camps 

● Gender-separated floors and same-gender staff assigned 

● Night watch staff on duty overnight 

● All technical activities run with professional instructors: 

○ Swiss mountain guides in Switzerland 

○ Certified French guides in France 

● SILC “trainers” (counselors) specialize in leadership development and personal coaching ● Strong pastoral culture built around belonging and personal growth 

**Languages & Nationalities** 

● Program language is **English**, across all camps (Switzerland + France) ● Students come from all over the world: 

○ USA, Latin America, Europe, Middle East 

○ Some Africa and Asia, but less dominant 

● Nationality cap of **~6% per country** to maintain diversity 

● Camps typically run full each summer, allowing them to control cohort balance

**Airports & Transfers** 

Airport transfers are **included** in the program fees. 

**Switzerland Camps:** 

● Geneva (preferred) 

● Zurich (possible but less common) 

**France Camps:** 

● Cannes: Nice Airport 

● Corsica: Figari Airport 

Families only need to arrange flights; everything else is covered. 

**Fees & What’s Included** 

SILC uses all-inclusive pricing. Example: 

● Cannes = €6,700 

● Tuition, lodging, insurance, transfers, all activities, guides, and supervision included ● No add-on activity fees 

All programs are 2-week sessions except Gstaad, which offers flexible durations. 

**Parent Consultation & Matching Process** 

SILC encourages advisors to introduce interested families directly. SILC will: ● Hold a 30–45 minute consultation

● Ask about interests, experience level, comfort with adventure, and goals ● Match the student to the most appropriate camp 

● Advisors may join these meetings or hand them off entirely 

They also offer post-camp follow-up and personal development plans for students who want continued growth. 

**Best-Fit Families** 

SILC is ideal for: 

● Families seeking a **small, international, boutique** camp experience ● Students who prefer structured, active days with constant engagement ● Kids who want to build leadership, confidence, and life skills 

● Teens interested in creativity, entrepreneurship, adventure, or pre-university insight ● Parents who value supervision, safety, and strong personal attention 

Less ideal for: 

● Students wanting a large, traditional, social American-style camp 

● Teens not comfortable with outdoor activities or active itineraries

title: Sangamon
text:
**Camp Sangamon** 

**Program Overview** 

Sangamon is a rustic, unplugged boys’ camp in Vermont built around outdoor living, free choice, and community. Cabins are intentionally simple, with no electricity or running water, encouraging kids to spend nearly all of their time outside and fully engaged with one another and the natural environment. The camp is completely screen-free, with no phones, video games, or media, which parents consistently see as a major benefit. 

**Philosophy & Camper Experience** 

Sangamon is rooted in the belief that there are many paths to success and that every camper belongs. The program emphasizes empathy, kindness, communication, and collaboration, with a strong focus on social-emotional growth. Campers are encouraged to practice conflict resolution, nonviolent communication, and community responsibility in a supportive, inclusive environment that celebrates individuality rather than competition. 

**Free Choice Activity Model** 

Campers operate within a true free choice system: they do not have to do any one activity, but they do need to be engaged in something. Activities are announced daily, and campers move fluidly between options based on interest and energy. A camper may spend an entire day immersed in woodshop or choose to rotate between sports, swimming, animal care, and creative pursuits. The emphasis is on agency, decision-making, and intrinsic motivation rather than schedules or required rotations. 

**Activities & Skill Development** 

Sangamon offers a wide range of hands-on activities, including woodworking, farming, horseback riding, archery, swimming, arts, crafts, sports, outdoor cooking, and nature exploration. While campers gain practical outdoor and creative skills, the primary goal is building independence, confidence, communication skills, and emotional awareness. Staff encourage campers to try new experiences when they are ready, rather than forcing participation. 

**Camper Fit** 

Sangamon is particularly well suited for boys who benefit from autonomy, variety, and movement. It works extremely well for campers with diverse interests, shorter attention spans, creative minds, or those who may struggle in rigid or highly structured environments. It is not an ideal fit for campers who require highly scheduled days, constant structure, or one-on-one support to function successfully. 

**Cabins, Supervision & Ratios** 

Camp operates with an exceptionally high staff-to-camper ratio of approximately 2:1. Cabins serve as each camper’s home base, where they sleep, eat meals, and check in daily with

counselors about plans and experiences. Staff members are both cabin counselors and activity leaders, ensuring consistent supervision and strong relationships throughout the day. 

**Community & Relationships** 

Strong relationships are central to the Sangamon experience. Campers form friendships across ages through shared interests rather than grade or age grouping. Cabin staff actively foster meaningful conversations, shared experiences, and reflection, helping campers develop deeper interpersonal skills. Nearly every camper forms a close connection with at least one adult mentor during their time at camp. 

**Sessions & Ages** 

Most new campers begin with a two-week session, which offers a manageable introduction and leaves campers eager to return. As campers grow older and return, many extend to longer sessions of three to six weeks. Younger campers typically attend shorter sessions, while returning and older campers often stay longer. 

**Health, Safety & Emotional Support** 

Sangamon maintains a well-staffed health center with RNs, EMTs, and access to local medical professionals, including pediatric and emergency care oversight. Medications are administered daily, and the camp regularly supports campers with allergies, medical needs, and emotional support requirements. Emotional well-being is treated proactively, with staff trained to identify and support homesickness, anxiety, and social challenges. 

**Food & Dietary Needs** 

The camp accommodates a range of dietary needs, including celiac and dairy-free diets, on a case-by-case basis. While Sangamon is not nut-free, families work closely with staff to ensure safe and appropriate meal planning. 

**Weekend Programming** 

Weekends offer a slightly different pace, including special breakfasts, staff-led seminars, themed all-camp games, and community events. Sundays include cabin cookouts and a full camp gathering focused on gratitude, reflection, and shared values. 

**Sister Camp Connection** 

Sangamon has a sister camp for girls, Betsy Cox, located next door. Campers connect weekly through socials, shared waterfront access, and occasional joint activities. Siblings have regular opportunities to see one another while still maintaining largely independent camp experiences. 

**Values & Culture** 

Sangamon emphasizes acceptance, empathy, and respect without framing these values politically or religiously. The camp acknowledges spirituality broadly through gratitude and reflection but does not offer religious instruction or services. Diversity, inclusion, and kindness are treated as core human values rather than ideological positions.

title: Rustic_Pathways_Recap_fixed
text:
Rustic Pathways — Team Recap (Student Travel & 2026 Preview) 

Team & Scope 

Patrick Ziemnik (school partnerships; 13 years; HQ Cleveland / Seoul), Emily Green (Admissions; Costa Rica/Hawaii), Scott Ingram (Admissions; Japan Manager). Focus for us: Student Travel; Rustic also builds custom School/Group travel year‑round. 

2025 Performance 

Top destinations: Costa Rica (#1), Greece (fast riser), then Thailand, Italy, Japan; mid: Germany/US/Fiji; niche: Portugal/Belize/South Korea/DR. 

High NPS; especially strong on Safety and Pre‑Program Communication. Parents value daily photos/videos; students’ #1 outcome: close friendships. 

2026 Updates 

Website tools: 4–5 question matching quiz; browse by destination or age band. 

Pre‑built 2‑week combos (single cohort, leaders escort between countries): Japan+Korea; Italy+Greece; Spain+Portugal; plus 4‑week Europe Grand (Portugal→Spain→Italy→Greece). Spring Break: Belize live; Costa Rica Surf+Service coming. Winter: Germany Ski. Costa Rica/DR 2026 dates coming soon. 

Fit & Experience 

First‑timers to seasoned travelers all supported. Most programs use 

hotels/guesthouses/hostels with modern amenities; exceptions exist by design (e.g., Thailand “Come With Nothing” is rustic). All meals included; leaders nudge local cuisine while meeting comfort needs. 

Differentiators 

• 1) Depth and safety (robust risk management and care coordination). 2) Individual attention (cohorts ~12–20; tailored growth goals). 3) Cohort continuity (leaders escort across borders on combo programs). 

Advisor Quick Hits 

First‑timer fits: Costa Rica, Greece, Japan MS cohorts. Longer asks: 2‑week combos / Europe Grand. School/Group custom to Patrick. Spring Break planners: Belize now; Costa Rica imminent. Contacts: scott@rusticpathways.com; admissions for Emily when Scott is in‑field.

title: Robin Hood (NH) Zoom Recap
text:
**Camp Overview & Unique Features** 

**General Structure** 

• **One-session camp model** (No mid-season enrollments). 

• **Full summer experience:** 

o Majority stay for **full summer**, but ~25% leave after **four weeks**. 

o Those who leave do **not disrupt camp dynamics** (no second-session  arrivals). 

• **Long-standing traditions:** 

o Many staff members **grew up at camp**, including second-generation  campers. 

o A tight-knit, **family-like atmosphere**. 

**Camper Demographics & Environment** 

• **Midwestern influence:** The camp attracts many families from **Chicago and the  Midwest**, offering a **less status-driven, more inclusive environment** compared to  some East Coast camps. 

• **Diverse Population:** Campers from all over the **U.S. and internationally**, including  **London, Spain, Israel, Kuwait, Qatar**, and other countries. 

o Approximately **30-40 international campers** (around 10% of enrollment). o No more than **two non-English speakers per group** to encourage language  immersion. 

**Facilities & Investments** 

• **Significant facility upgrades** since COVID (multi-million-dollar investment). • **70-acre waterfront property** with expanded facilities. 

• **New structures & renovations:** 

o Dining hall **expansion** (increased capacity by 100). 

o New **two-story infirmary** on the lake (separate sick bays for boys & girls). o **Arts & crafts center relocation** and conversion of old space into boys’ bunk. o **Venice Beach-style weightlifting area**. 

o **6 tennis courts & 6 pickleball courts**.

**Program Structure & Approach** 

• **Focus on personal growth:** 

o Emphasis on **pleases and thank-yous, respect, and helping others**. o Campers return home as **better siblings, children, and citizens**. • **Structured Activity Selection:** 

o Activities are **chosen daily**, rather than signing up in advance. 

o Groups rotate together, **ensuring all campers try multiple activities**. o Campers **must try all activities** to encourage exposure and respect for  different experiences. 

• **No child walks alone:** 

o **Three or four counselors per group** accompany campers at all times. 

**Activities & Specialty Programs** 

**Sports & Instructional Improvements** 

• **New professional coaches** for major sports like **baseball, basketball, volleyball,  and tennis**. 

• **More experienced specialists** rather than former campers teaching major sports. • **Strong but balanced competition:** 

o **Not a “D1 athlete” training camp** but **competitive in regional**  

**tournaments**. 

o Girls’ teams are **undefeated**, and boys’ teams have won multiple  tournaments. 

**Arts, Music, & Theater** 

• **Expanded creative offerings:** 

o Theater & musicals introduced last year. 

o Increased staff specialization in **music and performing arts**. 

**Tripping & Outdoor Adventures** 

• **Excursions & Adventure Trips:** 

o Every camper **climbs a mountain** within the first three days.

o Multiple **optional hikes** throughout the summer. 

• **Changes to overnight excursions:** 

o **Older campers (9th-11th grade) have chaperoned trips with security**. o Younger campers go on **day-long excursions** but **return to camp at night** to  avoid safety risks. 

**Color War (5-day Event)** 

• **Highly immersive, all-camp competition.** 

• **Unique events:** 

o **Opening “break” is a major event** (last year’s wasn’t well received, aiming  to improve). 

o **Rubik’s Cube & chess tournaments** kick off the competition. 

o **Night 4 features a massive talent show**, including **dances from junior and  senior campers**. 

o Ends with a **sing-off and a tug-of-war**. 

• **Judging:** 

o Former **color war captains from past decades return to judge** (~90 judges  last year). 

**Enrollment & Marketing Challenges** 

**Current Enrollment** 

• **Capacity:** 400 campers. 

• **Remaining spots:** 50 open spots left. 

• **Strong word-of-mouth growth:** 

o **75% of new campers from referrals**. 

o Only **25% from camp consultants or brokers** (opposite of industry norms). **Outreach & Recruitment Issues** 

**1. Lack of Conversions from Referrals:** 

a. Last year, **only one camper enrolled from camp consultants** despite **60-70  leads**. 

b. Many **initial inquiries disappear without follow-up**.

**2. Aggressive Follow-Up Approach May Be Detrimental:** 

a. Current strategy: 

i. Immediate email after lead submission. 

ii. Second email **within 2-3 days**. 

iii. Phone call follow-ups. 

b. Consultant feedback: 

i. **Recommended waiting at least 24 hours** before initial contact. 

ii. **NYC & PA families prefer email** over unsolicited calls. 

iii. Personal introductions via **warm email intros** from camp consultants  work better. 

**Feedback & Adjustments** 

• **Less immediate outreach; more strategic follow-ups.** 

• **Stronger emphasis on softer, personal touches.** 

• **Leverage in-person visits & tours** (Woody willing to fly for family meetings). • **More visibility & engagement with camp consultants.** 

**Additional Camp Opportunities** 

**Rookie Day (Trial Experience)** 

• **One-day immersion for prospective campers & parents.** 

• Parents **stay on-site** while kids participate in activities with counselors. • **Key selling point:** Parents experience the **high-energy, communal dining hall  experience**. 

• **Date: July 13th, 2024**. 

**Gymnastics Expansion** 

• Current gymnastics program **is a weak point**. 

• **Plans to improve for 2024 & 2026.** 

• Potential **construction of a dedicated gymnastics pavilion** by 2026.

**Final Takeaways & Next Steps** 

**Strengths of Robin Hood Camp** 

• **Long-standing traditions & tight-knit alumni network.** • **Balanced focus on sports, arts, and outdoor adventure.** • **Single-session structure (no mid-summer disruption).** • **Diverse population from across the U.S. & internationally.** 

**Areas for Improvement** 

**1. Adjust recruitment follow-up strategy.** 

**a. More personalized, less aggressive outreach.** 

b. Focus on **email introductions over cold calls**. 

**2. Increase visibility & outreach in Tri-State area.** 

a. Attend more **camp fairs & consultant events**. 

b. Conduct more **private family tours & home visits**. 

**3. Continue improving specialty programs.** 

a. Strengthen **gymnastics & performing arts**. 

b. Maintain **upgraded professional coaching staff**. 

**4. Leverage Rookie Day & camp tours more effectively.** 

a. Encourage more families to **visit & experience camp firsthand**.

title: Private_Prep_Recap
text:
Private Prep — Team Recap (Services, Strategy & How to Refer) 

Presenter & Partnership Notes 

Speaker: Steve Feldman (Founder). Longstanding partner to Camp Experts; known for proactive usage updates and transparent quarterly reporting for referred families. 

Footprint: Offices across NY Metro (NYC, Scarsdale, Port Washington, Ridgewood, Summit) and Bethesda, MD. ~70% of hours delivered online → serves students nationwide and internationally. 

What Private Prep Offers (4 Core Buckets) 

1) Test Prep — SAT/ACT for college; SSAT/ISEE for secondary admissions; SHSAT (NYC specialized HS), HSPT/Catholic HS tests. 2) College Admissions Guidance — Strategy from 8th–11th grade; school list building, app strategy, essays, ED/EA/RD plans. 3) Academic Support — Middle school through AP/advanced courses; writing, math, science, history. 4) Executive Functioning (EF) — Study skills, time management, organization, accountability. 

Typical on-ramp: Initial strategy consult → bespoke plan (align around camp, sports, theater calendars). 

Admissions Landscape & Strategy Signals for Families 

Selectivity is up. Most competitive schools use holistic review. Baseline bar = strong GPA in rigorous courses + competitive test scores; differentiators = activities, essays, recommendations, character. 

Course planning matters starting 8th–9th grade. PP balances student well‑being with rigor—no one-size-fits-all AP loading. 

Testing (SAT/ACT) — Current Reality & Timing 

Post‑COVID pendulum is swinging back toward testing at many competitive schools; families in PP/Camp Experts’ demographic typically benefit from submitting scores when strong. 

When to start: connect in late 10th grade; build plan over summer into junior year. Most students sit 2x (sometimes 3x; rarely 4x due to diminishing returns). PP runs full diagnostics for SAT & ACT to select one path. 

SAT is now digital and adaptive; ACT changes are in flux. PP guides policies on score choice/superscore per college list. 

Private Prep’s Test‑Prep Approach 

Three pillars: (1) Content mastery refresh (esp. grammar); (2) Strategy (timing, backsolving, elimination); (3) Performance prep (test‑day mindset). PP partners with

sports‑psych resources; emphasizes confidence and mental health as academic performance drivers. 

Executive Functioning (EF) — How It Works 

Goal: build durable habits for organization, planning and initiation; break long‑term work into weekly/daily action steps. 

Format: frequent, short touchpoints (e.g., 2–3x/week for 15–30 minutes). Coach = accountability partner (not Mom/Dad). Common targets: procrastination, planning backward from due dates, study schedules, emotional regulation. 

Outcome: lower family friction, higher follow‑through; skills translate to HS/college and early career. 

Operations, Communication & Quality Control 

Each lesson generates a written summary in PP’s platform → approved by a full‑time Director of Client Relations (DCR) and sent to parents. DCRs quarterback the relationship (student/parent ↔ tutor/coach), handle placement, and ensure alignment. 

Billing: summaries tie to payroll/billing; families billed bi‑weekly via card/ACH. 

Geography & Delivery 

Local presence in NY metro & DC; online delivery means PP supports students across the U.S. and abroad. International admissions experience: yes (e.g., Europe, Latin America). 

Pricing & Packages (for family expectation‑setting) 

Academic tutoring & Executive Functioning: ~$150–$250/hr (most experienced higher). Test Prep: ~$175–$400/hr. 

College Admissions: higher rates, typically packages (e.g., 20 or 40 hrs) to reserve counselor bandwidth: ~${7,000} for 20 hrs at $350/hr; ~${13,300}+ for 40 hrs (with ~5% package discount). Actual rates vary by coach. 

Many families begin with a lower‑commitment **Strategy Session**: roughly **$600–$1,500** to map a plan. 

Referral Tips for Camp Experts 

• Best initial touchpoint: Strategy Session (grades 8–10) or diagnostics (late 10th) for SAT/ACT plan. • Share calendar constraints (camp/sports), target colleges/majors, and specific struggles (e.g., procrastination) in your referral form—more context = better placement. • One family often becomes multi‑service, multi‑sibling → recurring referral value. 

Contacts

Primary: **Steve Feldman — steve@privateprep.com** Main line: **212‑244‑7190** (calls returned quickly). PP will route to the right Director based on location and bandwidth.

title: Point O' Pines (Zoom - Nov. 4, 2025)
text:
**Point O' Pines Camp for Girls — Recap**  Prepared from the conversation with directors Nikki Zenkel Grossman and Max Grossman. 

**Snapshot** 

All-girls, uniform, traditional sleepaway camp with a 7-week season and a 4-week option for first-time campers. POP emphasizes a warm, family feel: leadership meets every new family in person before summer. Ownership transitions to Nikki and Max after this summer, becoming the third set of owners in the camp's 70-year history. Core spirit, traditions, and sisterhood stay central while programming is refreshed. 

**Leadership and Philosophy** 

Directors and next owners: Nikki Zenkel Grossman and Max Grossman. Goal: preserve POP traditions and community while adding energy and modern program upgrades. Culture: highly personal partnership with families; leaders know every camper and family. 

**Sessions and Structure** 

\- 7-week full season; 4-week option for first-time campers (extensions commonly offered; many elect to stay). 

\- Younger bunks are intentionally small: typically 6 to 7 campers with 3 counselors for extra support. 

\- Bunking evolves as campers age; sizes grow to roughly 8 to 10. Friendships are nurtured and respected; modest mixing year to year. 

\- Peak summer: the final-year cohort lives together in a single house called the Peak. **Activities and Facilities: What is New** 

\- Six pickleball courts added; fast-growing, highly popular activity. 

\- New woodworking studio. 

\- Gaga added to campus. 

\- Archery range redesigned and refreshed. 

\- Spin bikes added to the fitness studio. 

\- Ongoing quality upgrades to equipment and program areas. 

**Activities and Facilities: What is Next** 

\- Onsite climbing and ropes course under active exploration (currently offered offsite, 15 minutes away). - Security enhancements: entrance gate and increased screening from the road. 

\- Capital plan: new gymnastics and fitness studio, new dance center, upgraded cooking studio, plus infrastructure items such as roofing and windows. 

**Health, Wellness, and Food**

\- Health center staffed 24/7 with 4 to 5 nurses on property; camp doctor typically a camp parent. 

\- Dietary accommodations: vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, and kosher-style (note: not a fully kosher kitchen). 

\- Optional Friday night Shabbat and optional Sunday church transportation for those who wish to attend. **Traditions and Community** 

Weekly Sunday team competition (color-war style) is a core POP tradition. Oldest campers draft teams each year, and events mirror those enjoyed by generations of alumnae. Dining tables mix across age groups after Week 1; frequent all-camp evening activities build cross-age friendships. 

**Brother-Sister Partnership with Brant Lake Camp** 

Close collaboration with Brant Lake Camp (about 4 minutes away). Two brother-sister or cousin visits most weeks; cross-camp lunches added last summer. Frequent socials and age-group activities, from scavenger hunts to July 4th celebrations; interactions increase with age. Shared alignment on dates, rates, transportation, and camp ethos. 

**Touring and Admissions Touchpoints** 

\- Rookie Day: Saturday, July 18 (4 hours). Campers try activities while parents receive an expanded tour. - Little Sister Sleepover before Visiting Day for POP and Brant Lake siblings/cousins (overnight sampler). 

\- In-season tours most Thu, Fri, Sat at 11:00am and 3:00pm; winter tours available since POP operates a winter events business and staff are on site year-round. 

\- About 30 to 40 percent of families tour before enrolling; directors also conduct in-home visits and virtual tours. 

**Enrollment and Availability Notes** 

\- Space available across most grades; younger cohorts (finishing Grades 2 to 3) are tighter but open as of the conversation. 

\- New camper cutoff: age 13 (finishing Grade 7 is the last entry year). 

\- Families from roughly 20 U.S. states and several countries; about half of campers live outside the New York area. 

**Families With Early School Starts** 

POP can be flexible for southern and international school calendars in the early years (for example, leaving a week early). From Grades 6 to 7 onward, many culminating traditions occur late in the season; the camp encourages full-session participation whenever possible. 

**Ownership and Transition** 

Nikki and Max assume ownership after the upcoming summer, with prior owners Brandon and Carissa (Hemoff family) present this year as part of a legacy-honoring transition. Day-to-day camper and parent communications are led by Nikki and Max. 

**How to Position POP to Families** 

\- Tradition-rich, all-girls experience with a modern program refresh.

\- Exceptional attention to first-time campers via small young bunks (6 to 7 campers with 3 counselors). - Strong health-and-safety posture with robust nursing coverage and a camp-parent physician. - Flexible first-summer entry (4-week option) with high extension rates to the full season. - Frequent, structured brother-sister touchpoints with Brant Lake while preserving single-gender benefits. 

Note: Details reflect the live conversation and may change. Verify dates and options directly with Point O' Pines when advising families.

title: Pine Forest
text:
**Pine Forest Camp, Timber Tops & Lake Owego — Partner Call Recap** 

**Legacy & ownership** 

● **Founded in 1931**, approaching **95 years** and nearing 100, with **five generations** of the same family still owning and operating the camps. 

● Founded by **Huey Black**, a professional basketball player whose team is honored in the Basketball Hall of Fame. 

● The family remains deeply hands-on across all areas of camp life, from leadership to food service to camper relationships. 

● Leadership includes **Mickey Black**, **Barbara Black**, **Anna Black Morin**, **Eric Morin**, **Lisa Black**, and now the **fifth generation** as campers. 

**The three-camp model** 

● All three camps are located side-by-side in **Greeley, Pennsylvania** (Pike County), about **1.5 hours from NYC** and **2 hours from Philadelphia**. 

● The camps operate as a **connected ecosystem**, but each has a distinct structure: 

**Pine Forest Camp** 

● **Traditional co-ed**, 7-week full summer. 

● Functionally operates like a **brother–sister camp**: 

○ Separate boys’ and girls’ pools. 

○ Separate lineups. 

○ Staggered meals; boys and girls never eat together. 

○ Activities are single-gender. 

● First-year campers may try **half summer**, but expectation is full summer long-term.

**Camp Timber Tops** 

● **All-girls** camp. 

● Offers **half-season and full-season** options. 

● Majority of campers ultimately attend full season (approx. **240 of 320 girls**). 

● Designed so half-season campers still experience a complete arc (beginning, middle, end). 

**Lake Owego** 

● **All-boys** camp. 

● Same half-season/full-season philosophy as Timber Tops. 

● Bunking grouped by **age + length of stay**, with first-time campers together. 

**Camp philosophy & family fit** 

● Strong emphasis on a **softer, gentler, down-to-earth** traditional camp experience. ● Appeals to families prioritizing: 

○ friendship 

○ community 

○ emotional safety 

○ childhood pacing (keeping kids “young” longer) 

● Not a flashy or trend-driven camp; intentionally avoids “bells and whistles.” ● Families are often **not choosing the same camp as neighbors or classmates**. 

● Many families are Jewish (not exclusively), and families are typically comparing camps nationally, not regionally. 

**Camper demographics**

● Core regions: **NY, NJ, PA**, followed by **DC/Maryland**. 

● Meaningful outliers from **California, Colorado, Utah**, and beyond. 

● Families frequently cross-shop: 

○ Adirondack camps 

○ Maine camps 

○ North Carolina camps 

● Pine Forest often wins as a **closer but philosophically similar alternative**. 

**Leadership & staffing culture** 

● Leadership team is heavily **education-based**: 

○ former teachers 

○ principals 

○ school counselors 

○ college administrators 

● Explicit distinction: leadership roles are held by **educators and child-development professionals**, not just “camp parents.” 

● **Sarah & Hal Schwartz** now lead Boys’ and Girls’ Camp at Pine Forest: ○ Both are educators. 

○ Met at camp. 

○ Parents of four campers. 

○ Philosophy centers on dignity, respect, experiential learning, and meeting each child where they are. 

**Cabin life & supervision**

● Approximately **10 campers per cabin**. 

● **3–4 counselors per bunk** (4 for younger campers). 

● High-touch, relationship-driven supervision model. 

**Programming structure** 

● **6-period day**, intentionally redesigned to reduce stress and rushing. ● Campers wake between **7:45–8:00 am**. 

● Staggered mornings: 

○ One camp eats breakfast while the other does cleanup, lineup, and announcements. 

● Periods are ~**55 minutes**, with **10-minute transitions**. 

● **3 morning periods**, lunch + rest, **3 afternoon periods**. 

● Every day ends with **open swim**, ensuring daily water access. 

**Activity design (by age)** 

● Camp offers **40+ activities**. 

● Younger campers: 

○ More **pre-programmed schedules**. 

○ Guaranteed exposure to core activities. 

○ Through 6th grade: **3 swim lessons + 3 tennis lessons per week**. ● Athletics: 

○ Internal leagues (not opt-in). 

○ Skill assessments at the start of summer.

○ Campers placed on teams by age group, not bunk. 

○ Full seasons with standings, playoffs, and champions. 

○ Emphasis on team belonging and social integration, not elite competition. ● Non-athletic core experiences: 

○ ropes course 

○ arts & crafts 

○ lake programming (twice weekly per age group) 

● Lake is a **10-minute wooded walk**, allowing for extended, immersive sessions. 

**Choice & progression** 

● Not an all-choice camp, but **choice increases with age**. 

● Older campers: 

○ More elective periods. 

○ Broader choice menus. 

○ By the oldest ages, **~50% of schedule is elective-based**. 

● Goal is to help campers discover interests first, then choose more intentionally. 

**Outdoor & tripping culture** 

● All three camps maintain strong **overnight tripping programs**, which many camps no longer offer: 

○ Appalachian Trail 

○ Delaware Water Gap 

● Trips are framed as developmental, not extreme.

● Builds resilience, independence, and confidence over time. 

**Admissions & family engagement** 

● **Lisa Black** serves as primary relationship-builder with prospective families. ● Families may engage via: 

○ personal tours 

○ play days (10–4) 

○ **explorer overnights** (2 per summer) 

● Camp team will travel extensively to meet families in person. ● Camp Experts families are explicitly valued and seen as well-vetted. 

**Advisor positioning notes** 

● Strong fit for families seeking: 

○ multi-generational stability 

○ traditional values 

○ educator-led environments 

○ emotionally safe, inclusive community 

● Especially compelling for: 

○ younger campers 

○ families wary of overly competitive or hyper-commercial camps ○ families cross-shopping national traditional camps

title: Pine Forest (1)
text:
**Historical Roots and Family Legacy** 

• **Multi-Generational Involvement:** 

The camp, originally established in 1931, is steeped in family history. One of the  speakers, who grew up at the camp and later served in various roles for over three  decades, emphasizes that the camp’s traditions are passed down through  generations. 

• **Expansion and Identity:** 

Over the years, the camp system expanded to include gender-specific branches (for  example, separate camps for boys and girls). This expansion was done thoughtfully,  ensuring that the core values and family-oriented nature of the camp were  preserved even as the institution grew. 

**Camp Philosophy and Cultural Ethos** 

• **A Sense of Belonging and Authenticity:** 

The directors stress that camp is much more than a recreational facility—it is about  creating an enduring feeling of community and belonging. The camp environment is  designed to foster personal growth, deep relationships, and a sense of authenticity. • **Balancing Tradition with Modern Amenities:** 

While modern amenities like sports courts and pools are available, the camp  deliberately maintains a rustic, “campy” atmosphere. The emphasis is on outdoor  adventure, simplicity, and genuine human connection rather than high-tech or  overly commercialized experiences. 

• **Values Over Competition:** 

There is a strong emphasis on non-competitiveness and on creating a supportive  environment. The camp intentionally targets families and campers who value a  down-to-earth, genuine experience over one driven by intense competition. 

**Staffing, Leadership, and Operational Approach** 

• **Staff Recruitment and Development:** 

The camp leadership places a premium on hiring staff who not only have  professional credentials—such as experience in teaching, coaching, or  counseling—but who also resonate deeply with the camp’s values and culture. • **Internal Growth and Fresh Perspectives:** 

A dual strategy is in place: promoting staff from within to preserve tradition, while 

also inviting new perspectives from those outside the established system. This  blend aims to enhance both the legacy and the evolving needs of the camp. • **Multi-Tiered Leadership Structure:** 

The organization features a clear hierarchy with diverse roles ranging from cabin  counselors to directors. This structure ensures that every camper's interests and  talents are recognized and that leadership is well distributed across different  operational levels. 

**Recruitment Strategies and Community Outreach** 

• **Expanding the Camp’s Reach:** 

Although the camp traditionally attracts families from nearby urban areas like  Westchester and Manhattan, there is a conscious effort to reach out to untapped  markets such as Long Island and beyond. 

• **Engagement Initiatives:** 

The directors discuss practical strategies such as hosting open houses and  personalized events aimed at demonstrating the camp’s authentic experience.  These initiatives are designed to attract families who might be unfamiliar with the  camp’s unique approach. 

• **Emphasizing Unique Values:** 

By highlighting the contrast between their genuine, family-focused environment and  more conventional, commercialized camps, the camp leadership works to position  the camp as a distinctive alternative that appeals to families seeking more than just  recreational activities. 

**Personal Narratives and Future Vision** 

• **Personal Journeys Informing Leadership:** 

The conversation includes personal anecdotes from the camp’s leadership— stories of childhood experiences, personal growth, and the transition from camper  to leader. These narratives underscore the deep personal investment that each  director has in the camp’s success. 

• **Vision for the Future:** 

Looking ahead, the speakers are committed to maintaining the integrity and  authenticity of the camp experience while adapting to new challenges and  opportunities. They stress that a loyal and well-nurtured staff is critical for  sustaining the camp’s unique environment.

• **Sustaining a Legacy Through Adaptation:** 

The directors highlight that while preserving tradition is essential, the camp must  also remain flexible and open to innovation. This balance is seen as key to ensuring  that the camp continues to thrive and resonate with new generations of campers.

title: Peak Tennis
text:
**Snapshot** 

Peak Tennis International runs high-performance, sports-focused residential camps for ages 10–17 across **Austria**, **Switzerland**, and (new for 2026) **Eton College, UK**. 

Programs combine elite-level tennis (and golf in Austria) with a strong emphasis on physical conditioning, mindset training, and cultural immersion. The camps are intentionally small, highly personalized, and structured around long, active days focused on movement, challenge, and personal growth. 

A signature part of their model is a **strict no-phone policy**, which parents and students have responded to exceptionally well. 

**Locations & Distinctives** 

**Austria** 

● Setting: Beautiful, non-touristic lakeside region in the Austrian Alps 

● Ages: 10–17 

● Sports: Tennis **or** golf (new addition) 

● Housing: Double rooms with bathroom 

● Sessions: Two **two-week** sessions 

● Max cohort: 30 campers 

● Notes: Golf partnership with a top regional academy (18-hole course, professional instructors) 

**Switzerland** 

● Setting: Swiss Alps (cool, crisp summer weather) 

● Ages: 10–17

● Sports: Tennis only 

● Housing: Double rooms with bathroom 

● Sessions: Two **one-week** sessions 

● Students may combine weeks; curriculum differs each week 

● Max cohort: 30 campers 

**Eton College, UK (New 2026 Launch)** 

● Setting: Historic Eton College campus, Windsor 

● Ages: 12–17 

● Sports: Tennis only 

● Housing: Private single rooms, private bathrooms (boys and girls housed separately) ● Sessions: One **two-week** session only (pilot program) 

● Max cohort: 80 campers 

● Highlights: Wimbledon visit, Windsor horse racing outing, London cultural excursions ● Ideal for advanced/elite players 

**Program Philosophy & Structure** 

The camps are **intense, athletic, and mindset-driven**, built around the idea that young people learn best when active, challenged, and fully immersed. 

**Daily rhythm** 

● Mornings: Dedicated tennis or golf training 

● Afternoons:

○ Recreational sports 

○ Lake visits, city trips 

○ Cultural excursions 

○ Climbing, aqua sports, mini-golf 

● Evenings: 

○ Activities, cinema outings, camping nights 

○ Mindset sessions and breathwork 

○ Cold exposure / ice bath challenges (optional but popular) 

Total tennis hours per two-week program: 

● Standard: 30–35 hours 

● Elite pathway (Eton): Up to 45 hours + private 1:1 coaching 

Elite-track players also participate in an **end-of-camp tournament** (internal to the camp group). 

**Coaching Model & Staffing** 

● Ratio: **1 coach per 6–8 students** 

● Coaches know each student individually and customize training 

● Physiotherapists on-site to support body awareness and injury prevention ● Dedicated mindset coach providing daily sessions on breathing, resilience, focus ● Nurses on-site at each location 

● Hospitals located 2–15 minutes away depending on camp

Before arrival, families complete a detailed health + allergy form due to the athletic nature of the program. 

**No-Phone Policy** 

One of the strongest elements of Peak Tennis. 

● Students may bring one device 

● Phones are collected and stored 

● Students receive **30 minutes/day** of access in the evenings 

● Dramatically improves focus, bonding, social interaction, and wellbeing 

Parents receive a **Daily WhatsApp Broadcast** (photos, videos, updates) and direct contact access to the Camp Director if needed. 

**Who These Camps Are Best For** 

Peak Tennis is ideal for students who: 

● Want serious, structured sports programming 

● Enjoy an active, physically demanding schedule 

● Thrive under high supervision and small-group coaching 

● Are new to tennis *or* deeply advanced (both levels supported) 

● Want an English-language program without needing perfect fluency (B1 is fine) ● Prefer international friendships and diverse cohorts 

Best match for strong players: **Eton** 

Best match for well-rounded multi-sport athletes: **Austria** (tennis + golf) 

Best match for intense alpine training weeks: **Switzerland**

Not ideal for students who want significant downtime, relaxed camp pacing, or light recreational tennis. 

**Group Size & Cohort Composition** 

● Austria: max 30 campers/session 

● Switzerland: max 30 campers/session 

● Eton: max 80 campers/session (split into smaller teams) 

International mix varies, but current concentrations include: 

● Europe 

● Latin America 

● USA 

● Expanding into Asia (China, Japan, Thailand) 

Priority is placed on balanced nationality distribution where possible. 

**Dates & Duration** 

● **Eton College (UK):** July 2–16 

● **Switzerland:** August 

● **Austria:** mid-July and mid-August 

Each week or session has a distinct curriculum, allowing students to combine sessions without repetition.

**Pricing** 

● **Eton College:** 5,990 CHF 

● Austria & Switzerland prices in brochures (shared separately) ● Everything included except: 

○ Flights 

○ Airport transfers (available at an extra fee) 

**Safety & Medical Access** 

● Nurses on-site 

● Physiotherapists 

● Mindset coaching staff 

● Nearby hospitals: 

○ Switzerland: 2 minutes 

○ Austria: 5–10 minutes 

○ Eton: 15 minutes 

● 24-hour security at all camps 

**Summary for Advisors** 

Peak Tennis International is a strong fit for families seeking: ● A **serious sports camp** with tennis (and golf in Austria) at its core

● Structured days, fully programmed schedules, and minimal downtime ● A **small, personal, highly supervised** environment 

● English-language programming with an internationally mixed cohort ● A meaningful digital detox 

● Professional coaching, mindset training, and wellness support 

Eton is the standout elite option; Austria is the best all-rounder; Switzerland offers an intimate alpine sports experience.

title: Patagonia Education Partners (Zoom Nov. 4th)
text:
**Patagonia Education Partners — Zoom  Notes (Gonzalo López Jordán)** 

Two week summer program in **Duino, Italy (UWC Adriatic / Duino Castle)** for high-schoolers  (ages **15–19**). Framed as a **holistic, debate-driven** experience with global peers; positioned  closer to an academic enrichment + leadership institute than a traditional camp. 

**Who they are & why this exists** 

• **Patagonia Education Partners** sits under **Patagonia Financial Holdings** (Miami). **Gonzalo López Jordán** is both **chairman** of the PE firm and **program director** here;  he’s a **UWC Adriatic alum** and said this is a “give-back to education” effort post retirement. 

• **Formal tie-in:** Program is a **joint venture with UWC Adriatic** (the school hosts it;  staff/trainings follow UWC protocols). 

• **Pedigree context:** UWC movement → roots in **Kurt Hahn/Lord Mountbatten** era;  IB’s origins were cited in this lineage. 

**What the program is (structure, daily flow)** 

• **Two sessions:** first two weeks of **July**, and second two weeks of **July** (this coming  summer). 

• **Cohort size:** target **~50 students** per session. Last summer: **~40 students from 29  countries / 5 continents**; **~4 from the U.S.** All instruction in **English**. 

• **Daily rhythm:** 

o **Mornings (≈ 4 hrs):** short **subject-leader “spark”** (~15 min over Zoom) →  **facilitated debate** (Harkness/roundtable style) led by **Program Advisors**. o **Afternoons:** alternate **Art** and **Social Service** blocks. 

o **Two full day trips: Trieste** and **Venice**. 

o **One free day** in the two-week span (beach/relax, Duino is seaside). 

• **Six integrated components (holistic, not siloed): Leadership, Sustainability,  International Relations, Financial Literacy, Art, Social Service.** 

They emphasized “real-world readiness”: character foundations, global citizenship,  leadership practice. 

**People students meet (how “experts” interact)**

• **Subject leaders** (examples named): **Chrystia Freeland** (Canada), **Emma Tucker** (WSJ), **Andrés Franco** (UNICEF), **Marco Becht** (finance), **Emilio González** (former  USCIS), **Juan Pablo Di Pace** (film), plus corporate/academic leaders in HR,  sustainability, finance, biotech, EU institutions, energy, cybersecurity. 

Format: **virtual mini-lectures** then **student debate**; **two in-person keynotes** (one per  week). 

• **Program Advisors (PAs):** university students (recent examples from **Yale, Brown,  Duke, Middlebury, Univ. of Pisa**, etc.). They live with/coach small groups and  **facilitate the discussions**. 

**Community design, housing, and safety** 

• **Houses/tutorial groups**: ~**10–12 students** per PA; used for projects, games, and  cohesion. 

• **Rooming**: intentionally **mixed across schools/cities/countries** (to force cross-cultural  interaction). 

• **Dorms**: UWC residences spread around Duino; **15–40 beds** each, male/female **by floor**;  **one PA per dorm**. Each floor has its own **bathrooms/showers + laundry**; common **day  room**; **curfew** enforced. 

• **Training/compliance**: staff follow UWC standards (**CPR, first aid, fire safety,  emotional well-being** protocols). 

**What students actually** ***do*** **(examples from last summer)** 

• **Debate blocks** tied to the six strands (PAs push the discussion beyond “check-the-box”  extracurriculars). 

• **House projects/competitions:** e.g., **dragon-boat teamwork** (Venice canals) tied to  leadership; 

**short-film production** mentored by a film director; **poster design** via visual-arts tutor. • **Social Service with a local footprint:** last year they **built a Sustainability Center** for  the school (clean-out, paint, organize, launch). 

**IB/CAS + service hours** 

• Program is **IB CAS–certified** (via UWC). 

• They can **certify community service hours** for non-IB schools as well (guidance given  on the call: **~50 hours per session**).

**How to apply (and how they track referrals)** 

• **Application** requires basic info **+ a short video** introducing the student and their “why.” They are explicit: not pay-to-attend—**they want contributors to the discourse.** • **Fee: $50** application fee; **Camp Experts gets waiver codes** (waives fee and **tracks  referral attribution**/finder fees). 

• **Selectivity:** last year was **competitive** partly due to facility maintenance limits; capacity  is normal this year (target 50). 

**Logistics / what’s included** 

• **Included in tuition:** campus **pickups**, **day trips**, program **materials**. 

• **Exception: Venice pickup** carries an extra **€/$200** charge (≈ 2.5-hour transfer). • **Group chaperone**: possible **for travel only** (not a parent, not embedded in program life);  they arrange **local lodging** in Duino for the chaperone. 

**Notes we’ll keep handy with families** 

• **Location: Duino / UWC Adriatic** (Trieste–Venice corridor). Seaside setting, historic  castle campus. 

• **Profile fit (from the way they talked):** globally curious teens, strong English, eager to  **argue ideas**, comfortable mixing with peers from **many countries**; students who want  **structured intellectual challenge** plus tangible **service output**; **IB** and **non-IB** welcome. 

• **Vibe:** more “**mini-UWC**” than summer school; **debate-heavy**, project-based,  intentionally cross-cultural. 

**Quick Q&A captured** 

• **How many years running?** Entering **year 2** (UWC site itself has ~40+ years of  operations). 

• **U.S. representation?** Last year **~4** U.S. students; aim remains **internationally diverse** cohorts. 

• **Language? English** throughout. 

• **Affiliation status? Joint venture** with **UWC Adriatic**; uses school facilities, staff  oversight, and standards. 

• **Service hours?** They can **document ~50 hrs/session** for schools that require them.

• **Dorm setup?** UWC residences, co-ed buildings with **single-sex floors**, PA on each;  bathrooms/laundry on each floor. 

• **Competitiveness/decisions?** Not automatic admits; video matters; decisions follow  application, with normal capacity this year. 

• **Session dates? Two July sessions** (weeks 1–2 and weeks 3–4). 

• **Costs covered?** Campus pickups/day trips/materials included; **Venice pickup +€/$200**;  **waiver codes** remove the **$50** application fee for our referrals.

title: Oxford Scholastica
text:
**Oxford Scholastica — Program Overview (Oxford, UK)** **Quick Snapshot** 

● **What it is:** A **2-week residential academic summer program** in Oxford for ages **12–18**, plus **online courses** and **online research internships**. 

● **Mission:** Help students **“seize the future”** through inspiring academics, real-world application, and strong student support. 

● **Track record:** Running since **2013**; has welcomed **8,000+ students**. 

● **Accreditation & recognition: BAC-accredited (British Accreditation Council)** and recipient of the **King’s Award for Enterprise**. 

● **Reputation:** Cited as having **1,028 reviews** and a **4.9/5 rating** (positioned as top-rated among European summer programs). 

**Ages & Academics** 

● **Age bands** 

○ **12–14:** Business, Medicine, Technology 

○ **15–18:** Wider subject menu (students choose a specialty based on interest) ● **Class size:** Small-group teaching — **~10 students average**, **15 max**. 

● **Teaching style:** Oxford/Cambridge-influenced instruction with highly qualified tutors (often with Oxford/Cambridge backgrounds and real-world expertise). 

● **Curriculum transparency:** Curriculum details are explicitly outlined (positioned as a differentiator vs. providers where tutors build highly idiosyncratic syllabi). 

**Signature Learning Model** 

Oxford Scholastica describes a **three-part philosophy**:

1\. **Inspire** through top-tier teaching in small classes 

2\. **Meet “heroes”** — young professionals/guest speakers who share career insight and Q&A 

3\. **Apply learning** via real-world challenges and simulations 

**Examples of “practical challenge” experiences:** 

● **Medicine:** Hospital visit + medical history-taking and diagnostic reasoning exercises ● **Law:** Mock trial with assigned roles 

● **Politics:** UN Security Council simulation 

● **Engineering:** Rocket Ship Challenge 

● **Business:** “Shark Tank”-style pitch challenge 

**Location & Living in Oxford** 

● **Where students live:** Oxford colleges used include **St. Anne’s**, **St. Peter’s**, and **Corpus Christi** (central Oxford locations). 

● **Rooms:** Mainly **single rooms**, with **twin-share available** for friends. 

● **Bathrooms:** Standard is shared (often **4–6 students**); **ensuite/private bathroom upgrades** are available. 

● **Security:** Colleges are enclosed with a **porter** and controlled entry (e.g., key fob access). 

● **On-site pastoral model:** “Heads of House” live on-site; students are separated by **age group** and **gender** in accommodation. 

● **Staff ratio:** Approx. **1 staff member per 15 students**. 

**Schedule & Student Life**

● **Program length: 2 weeks** 

● **General daily flow:** 

○ **Mornings:** Classes (e.g., **9–11am**) 

○ **Late morning:** Structured **study/project time** (e.g., **11am–1pm**) ○ **Afternoons:** Cultural activities / additional learning / excursions 

○ **Evenings:** Supervised social programming 

● **Homework:** Projects are set, primarily completed during the built-in **study block** (though some students may work beyond that by choice). 

**Food** 

● **Included: Breakfast + dinner** (at the college). 

● **Lunch: Not included** — students typically buy lunch around Oxford during a daily free-time window. 

**Supervision & Rules** 

● **Free time:** Students have **unsupervised free time over lunch** (typically ~2 hours daily). 

● **Movement expectations by age:** 

○ **Ages 12–14:** Must be **with at least one other student** when out. ○ **Ages 15–18:** Encouraged to go with friends; **may go solo** if they choose. ● **Strict conduct expectations (including for 18-year-olds):** 

○ **No alcohol** (zero-tolerance) 

○ **No leaving accommodation at night**

● **Participation expectations:** Most activities and excursions are described as **mandatory** (designed to push students to try new things, even if they’re skeptical at first). 

**Excursions & Highlights** 

Examples mentioned: 

● **Windsor Castle** 

● **Blenheim Palace** (Churchill birthplace) 

● **Punting on the river** 

● **International Ball** (boat outing with music) 

● **Evening sports & fitness options:** football, volleyball, informal running clubs, parks; some students independently join a local gym. 

**Outcomes & Takeaways** 

● **Graduation certificate** 

● **Personal letter of recommendation** from tutor (detailed and individualized) 

● **Online research internship option:** students **co-author a research paper** that is published on the program’s journal site (positioned as another “portfolio” asset). 

**Admissions & English Requirement** 

● **Application emphasis:** Personal statement is the key piece — they prioritize **motivation and intellectual curiosity**, not transcripts/grades. 

● **Decision timeline:** Applications reviewed frequently; decisions typically returned within **~24 hours**.

● **English level:** Students should have **C1-level English**; the team may **informally assess** via a call if needed. 

**Dates & Tuition** 

● **Residential: Three sessions** in July/August, each **2 weeks** 

● **Residential tuition: £7,495** (all-inclusive for academics, housing, breakfast/dinner, activities/excursions, graduation, etc.; lunch excluded) 

● **Payment timing:** Deposit due to secure place; remaining balance due **March 31** (as referenced). 

● **Online options:** One- or two-week online courses + virtual internships at different price points. 

**Travel & Transfers** 

● **Airport transfers:** Available as **door-to-door** pickup/drop-off service between airport and college (optional add-on). 

● **Visa:** Described as straightforward; some students may use a low-cost app-based authorization, and the program can provide **visa support letters** where required. 

**Student Mix** 

● **International cohort:** Students from **85+ nationalities**. 

● **Largest share: US (~20%)**, then **UK (~10%)**, with the remainder spread widely. **Notes for Referrals**

● Best fit for students who: 

○ Want **serious academics** in a **small-class** environment 

○ Thrive in an **international** peer group 

○ Can handle a program with **clear rules** and **structured expectations** ● Key positioning lines from the call: 

○ “Seize the future” (mission language) 

○ High review volume + **4.9/5 rating** as a credibility point 

○ Real-world challenges (hospital/UN/mock trial/rocket challenge/Shark Tank)

title: Oak Crest Recap
text:
**Camp Overview & Philosophy** 

**General Information** 

• **Traditional day camp model** designed for children **ages 3-14**. 

• **Session Length:** Camp runs for **8 weeks**. 

• **Enrollment Options:** Families can choose **4, 5, 6, 7, or 8 weeks**. • **Flexible Scheduling:** 

o Weeks do **not have to be consecutive**. 

o Campers can enroll for specific weeks based on personal schedules. **Camp Philosophy** 

• **Balanced Approach:** Oak Crest aims to **combine elements of sleepaway camp  with the accessibility of a day camp**. 

• **Progressive Skill Development:** Campers **learn and grow through hands-on  activities, personal challenges, and structured play**. 

• **Social & Emotional Growth:** The camp **fosters independence, leadership,  teamwork, and resilience**. 

• **Inclusivity:** Serves a **diverse camper population** with varying interests and skill  levels. 

**Transportation & Logistics** 

**Pickup & Drop-Off Options** 

• **Two options for transportation:** 

o **Central Pickup Stops:** (Used by 60%+ of families) 

▪ Located at **schools, shopping centers, and community hubs**. ▪ Designed for **efficiency and shorter bus rides**. 

o **Door-to-Door Service:** Available for **an extra fee**. 

**Bus Rides & Supervision** 

• **Travel Time:** Typically **under one hour**.

• **Supervision:** 

o Each bus has a **professional driver + a bus counselor**. 

o Bus counselors **take attendance, engage kids with games, and maintain  safety**. 

• **Flexibility for International Families:** 

o **If families are visiting the U.S., transportation can be arranged**. o **Custom pickup options** can be discussed. 

**Daily Camp Schedule & Structure** 

**Daily Routine** 

• **8 activity periods per day**, which include: 

o **Instructional Swim (morning)** 

o **Free Swim (afternoon)** 

o **Athletics & Adventure** 

o **Creative & Performing Arts** 

o **Choice Periods (2nd grade and up)** 

• **Lunch Break:** Midday meal (provided, no extra cost). 

• **Snack Break:** Afternoon snack (ice cream or Italian ices). 

**Swim Program** 

• **Two swim sessions per day:** 

o **Instructional Swim:** Focus on **technique, water safety, and skill-building**. o **Free Swim:** Opportunity for **relaxation and socialization**. 

• **Private Swim Lessons:** Available **after camp for a separate fee**. 

**Activities & Program Offerings** 

**Core Activity Areas** 

**1. Sports & Athletics** 

a. **Traditional Sports:** Basketball, soccer, softball, pickleball, volleyball.

b. **Scaled-Down Sports for Younger Kids:** Mini soccer field, mini basketball  court, and beginner-friendly play spaces. 

c. **Gaga Ball:** Highly popular among all age groups. 

d. **Tri-Camp Gaga Tournament:** A **competition between Oak Crest, Pine  Grove, and Tamarack**. 

**2. Adventure & Outdoor Activities** 

**a. Archery** 

**b. Skateboarding** 

**c. Biking (Instructional & Free-Ride)** 

i. **Specialized program to teach kids how to ride bikes**. 

ii. Addresses **parental concerns about limited time for bike lessons  at home**. 

**d. Obstacle Courses & Ropes Courses** 

**e. Hiking & Outdoor Exploration** 

**3. Creative & Performing Arts** 

**a. Jewelry Making** 

**b. Pottery & Ceramics** 

**c. Fine Arts & Cartooning** 

**d. Yoga & Dance** 

**e. Drama & Theater** 

**i. “Show Crest” (Week 6):** 

**1. Dance performances (girls only).** 

**2. Theater production (boys & girls).** 

**3. Held in an off-site theater with parent attendance.** 

**4. Teen Travel Program (7th-9th Grade)** 

**a. Two-Day Excursions Weekly:** 

i. Trampoline parks, Six Flags, Dorney Park, Jersey Shore. 

**b. 3-Day Overnight Trips:** 

i. **7th Graders:** Club Getaway (Kent, CT). 

ii. **8th & 9th Graders:** Destinations rotate (e.g., Boston, Virginia,  Poconos).

**Special Events & Themed Weeks** 

**Signature Events** 

• **Color War (Week 7)**: 

o Traditional 3-day event with **four teams competing**. 

o Events include **sports, relay races, trivia, and team spirit challenges**. • **Carnival (Week 8):** 

o Features **inflatables, dunk tanks, games, and food stations**. • **Pool Trash (Week 5):** 

o **Floating inflatables in the pool for play sessions**. 

• **Visiting Day (Week 3):** 

o **Parents visit camp to engage in activities with their children.** o Enhances **family involvement and camper confidence**. 

**Meals & Dietary Accommodations** 

• **Lunch & Snacks Included (No Extra Cost)** 

• **Daily Lunch Offerings:** 

o Hot options: **Burgers, chicken patties, pizza, meatball sandwiches.** o **Bagels, sandwiches (ham, turkey, PB&J), full salad bar available daily.** • **Dietary Restrictions:** 

o Accommodations for **gluten-free, vegetarian, and allergen-sensitive  campers**. 

o **Peanut butter is served** but carefully managed. 

• **Dining Environment:** 

o **Open-air tented dining space** (covered but outdoors). 

**Staffing & Supervision** 

**Staff Qualifications** 

• **Junior & Senior Counselors:** 

o **General counselors are juniors in high school or older.**

• **Specialists Lead All Activities:** 

o **Dedicated experts for sports, arts, biking, and adventure programs.** o **General counselors assist but do not run activities.** 

• **CIT/LIT Program:** 

o **Freshman & sophomore high school students serve as junior counselors**. **Division Breakdown & Camper Supervision** 

• **Age Groups:** 

o **Munchkins (3-5 years old, co-ed).** 

o **Explorers (1st-2nd grade, separate boys/girls).** 

o **Juniors (3rd-4th grade, separate boys/girls).** 

o **Seniors (5th-6th grade, separate boys/girls).** 

o **Teens (7th-9th grade, separate boys/girls).** 

• **Camp Groups:** 

o Each age division consists of **4-5 groups of 12-15 campers**. 

**Enrollment, Open Houses & Registration** 

• **4-week minimum enrollment (non-consecutive weeks allowed).** • **Next Open House Dates:** 

o **March 9th & April 6th.** 

o **Private tours available upon request.** 

• **Early Enrollment:** 

o Registration for **next summer opens in early August**. 

o **Early Bird discounts available.**

title: NJY_Camps_Recap_fixed
text:
NJY Camps — Team Recap (Overview, Inclusion, 2025 Dates) 

Presenters & Camps 

Carrie Youngs (Senior Director; Director of Camp Nah-Jee-Wah), Aryn Barer (Director, Round Lake Inclusion), Kelsey Kilduff (Assistant Director, Cedar Lake). Jason (CLC Director) was at jury duty. 

Four programs in Milford, PA: Nah-Jee-Wah (ages 6–11), Cedar Lake (12–14), Teen Camp (15–17; in-camp + travel), and Round Lake (inclusion, ages 7–17). Separate facilities and private lakes for NJW and CLC; cabins have bathrooms; camps are screen-free. 

Who Attends & Sessions 

Core markets: NY metro (NY/NJ/CT/PA) with strong Florida plus VA/MD/TX; interest in New England growth. 

Sessions: Short-Stay (~2 wks), 3 wks, 4 wks, 6 wks (athlete-friendly), 7 wks (full summer). Teen Camp combines 3 weeks in-camp with West Coast travel; Israel travel historically offered when demand/safety align. 

Program Highlights 

Electives: ~25 (NJW) and 40+ (CLC) including theater (new facility), ceramics/jewelry, STEM (robotics, coding, sports science), media (cameras & drones), ropes/zip lines. 

Total Specialty Camp (1 week/session, added fee): Soccer (NY Red Bulls), Baseball (Team Israel), Basketball (ex-NBA), Tennis (USPTA Pro). Unique add-ons: horseback riding, skate park. 

Round Lake Inclusion (Ages 7–17) 

≈150 campers each summer (≈35–40 at NJW; 80–90 at CLC). Full inclusion in daily program; two structural differences: staffing depth and housing (smaller bunks of ~6–7 for more support). Profile: autistic/ADHD/anxiety/learning differences—independent with executive-function/social support. Speech/OT/academic supports per 12‑month IEP offered when demanded. Inclusive travel options (e.g., West Coast; past: Israel, Alaska/Hawaii). Post‑grad pathway: Seva B’Yachad (job coaching & life skills). 

Jewish Life & Values 

Pluralistic, unaffiliated; ~20–25% interfaith; ~10% keep kosher. Joyful Shabbat (white attire, singing/dancing), Israel Day with 30–40 Israeli staff. Roots Values program (“building mensches one at a time”) with weekly value bracelets. 

Health, Safety, Food

Medical: three doctors + six nurses on site; electronic med administration (E‑MAR). Security: full‑time onsite with armed guards. Kosher kitchen; breakfast/lunch dairy, dinner meat; separate celiac kitchen; veg options; snacks built in. 

Positioning Notes for Advisors 

Down‑to‑earth community versus peers (Echo, Canadensis, Silver Lake). Great first‑timer fit at NJW; CLC leans into independence and large-scale spirit. Round Lake is a standout for inclusion; refer broadly beyond the Northeast.

title: NEGATC
text:
**New England Golf and Tennis** 

**Location**: Maine 

**Session Length**: Two-week minimum, 6-week season (rookie week option available) **Camp Size**: 45–65 campers in 2025; aiming for 75–85/week in 2026 

**Ideal For**: Golf, tennis, and hockey-focused campers or siblings with mixed interests **Gender**: Coed (currently leans slightly male and older teen) 

**Religious Affiliation**: None 

**Ages**: 8–16 

**Program Overview** 

New England Golf and Tennis is a **sports specialty camp** with a **resident camp vibe**, offering **structured instruction** in golf, tennis, and (new for 2026) **ice hockey**, plus a wide range of electives. The camp caters to both **passionate athletes and casual participants**, with **customizable schedules**. 

**Sports Instruction** 

● **Morning focus**: Golf, Tennis, or Ice Hockey (2 periods/day) 

● Optional combo programming (e.g., Golf + Tennis) 

● Coaches assigned based on camper skill level and goals 

● **Elective schedule** available for siblings or those less sport-focused 

**Camp Activities** 

● Waterfront: wakeboarding, trick skiing, canoeing, kayaking, sailing 

● High adventure, arts & crafts, science, music 

● Weekly trip days (beach, amusement park, etc.) 

● Biweekly: Ryder Cup, whitewater rafting 

**Food**

● Buffet-style meals, in-house chef (no 3rd party vendor) 

● Major food overhaul for 2026: healthier options, salad bar, better variety ● One seating only to ensure all campers receive sufficient portions 

**Transportation** 

● Pickups from Darien, CT 

● Airport transportation from Portland and Boston

title: Moorland Hall
text:
**Moorland Hall – Overview & Highlights** 

**Location & Setting** 

Moorland Hall is a small, family-run camp located in **Devon, England**, on the edge of the **Dartmoor National Park**—a stunning, rural region ideal for outdoor adventure. The camp is run directly by the Farrington family, who **live on-site** in the center of the property and are personally involved in every aspect of the children’s experience: airport pickup, activities, meals, supervision, and bedtime. 

Transfers are done **by the camp team themselves**, not by outside drivers. They meet students at Heathrow, Bristol, or local train stations and manage all transport with their own minibuses. Travel from Heathrow averages about **4.5 hours**, with breaks in Exeter along the way. 

**Campus & Facilities** 

The property is a classic English manor-style estate with multiple buildings: ● **Main House** – dining room, kitchen, sitting room, dormitories. 

● **Coach House** – dorm rooms (2-bed, 4-bed, 10-bed, and 12-bed setups), all with ensuite bathrooms. 

● **Newly renovated building** – classroom, games room, and additional accommodation. 

● **Outdoor space** – extensive fields, gardens, high ropes course, archery range, and direct access to Dartmoor. 

The camp also has: 

● A heated **outdoor swimming pool** 

● A large common room for relaxation, presentations, or watching major sporting events ● On-site equipment for every activity (wetsuits, helmets, harnesses, bikes, etc.)

**Program Structure** 

Moorland Hall offers a **highly structured and well-supervised daily schedule** developed specifically for younger campers and first-time travelers. 

**Daily routine:** 

● **Breakfast** 

● **Morning English & Journaling (30 minutes)** 

A practical approach focused on speaking, vocabulary, and reflection based on the previous day’s activities. 

● **Morning Activity Block (3 hours)** 

● **Lunch + Supervised Free Time** 

● **Afternoon Activity Block (3 hours)** 

● **Dinner + Free Time** 

● **Evening Program (1.5 hours)** – games, sports, crafts, hikes, or campfire-style activities. 

Activities rotate so campers are not constantly off-site; usually one “on-site” day alternates with one “excursion” day. 

**Activities & Excursions** 

**On-Site Adventure** 

● High ropes course 

● Leap of Faith 

● Crate stack challenge 

● Survival skills 

● Mountain biking

● Kayaking & raft building (nearby lake) 

● Archery 

● Swimming 

● Team games and sports 

● Tie-dye shirts, baking, crafts 

● Dogs on-site for comfort & fun 

**Off-Site Excursions** 

● **Dartmoor National Park** – guided nature walks, legends, history, geography ● **Tavistock** – local town visit, museum tour, mock trial experience with costumes ● **South Coast (Looe)** – RNLI lifeboat station tour 

● **North Coast (Port Isaac/Padstow)** – coastal path walk, ferry ride, crabbing, sandcastle competition 

● **Surfing & bodyboarding** at Widemouth Bay, with a sunset beach BBQ ● **Eden Project** – environmental & sustainability workshops 

● **National Marine Aquarium (Plymouth)** – dissection or stop-motion animation workshops 

● Indoor climbing for rainy days 

**Ages & Ideal Students** 

Best suited for **ages 9–14**, though some mature 15–16 year olds attend. A particularly strong option for: 

● First-time away-from-home campers

● Families seeking a **warm, personal, family-style environment** 

● Parents wanting a **small, highly supervised, homelike setting** rather than a large boarding-school campus 

● Students who may be younger, shy, nervous, or need a gentler transition to international sleepaway camps 

**Session Sizes & Structure** 

Two **two-week sessions**, with some campers choosing to stay for a third week by bridging between sessions. 

● Typical size: **30–35 campers** 

● Maximum capacity: 65 beds, though the summer intentionally stays far below capacity to maintain individual attention. 

**Staff & Safety** 

● Full-time professional instructors (not seasonal university hires) 

● Staff begin training in January/February and stay through late October ● Housemothers + the Farrington parents present across all areas of camp life ● Certifications: AALA, BAPA, LOTC, Adventure Mark 

Medical access: 

● Tavistock (7 minutes): doctors + minor injuries unit 

● Plymouth (30 minutes): major hospital

**Meals & Accommodation** 

All meals are home-cooked on site with fresh ingredients, including British cultural staples: ● Fish & chips 

● Roast dinner 

● Cornish pasty 

● Cream tea 

Dietary needs can be fully accommodated. 

Dorm rooms vary by size; summer campers are usually placed 2–4 per room. Younger children stay closer to the main house for added supervision. 

**English Language Program** 

Not all students take English, but for those who do, the program is: 

● Highly practical 

● Short daily sessions 

● Focused on speaking confidence, vocabulary building, and written journaling Students are grouped by ability after assessment on arrival. 

**Who This Camp Is Best For** 

● Families seeking **a nurturing, home-style British camp** 

● Students ages 9–14 

● First-time campers or anxious travelers 

● Parents wanting **true 24/7 owner involvement** and hands-on supervision

● Students who want outdoor adventure but not a huge, intense, fast-paced camp environment

title: Modin Recap
text:
***General Structure*** 

• **Session Options:** Offers two sessions or a full-season option (6.5 weeks). o About two-thirds of campers attend the full season. 

o The oldest campers (10th grade) must attend the full season and must be  returning campers. 

• **Camper Capacity:** 375–400 campers per session. 

• **Bunk Organization:** 

o Campers are grouped by grade in double-sided cabins with bathrooms in the  center. 

o Each cabin side houses 12–14 campers and counselors, with 24–28 campers  in total per cabin. 

***Campers' Geographic Distribution*** 

• Majority of campers (50%) come from the Greater New York area (Princeton, NJ to  Westport, CT). 

• Significant growth in campers from South Florida, Dallas, California, and  international locations (e.g., Israel, UK, France, and South America). 

**Jewish Pluralistic Identity** 

• Camp Modin is a **pluralistic Jewish community**: 

o Welcomes families from diverse levels of observance, excluding those  requiring Orthodox-level practices. 

o Provides a balance between Jewish traditions and classic camp  

experiences. 

• **Religious Practices:** 

o Kosher camp with labeled dining areas. Vegetarian and dairy options are  allowed on off-camp trips. 

o Weekday prayers are abbreviated and camp-style, with more elaborate  Shabbat practices: 

▪ **Friday Nights:** A short service, communal Shabbat dinner, and  

spirited sing-alongs.

▪ **Saturdays:** Extended wake-up time, an hour-long service with  

English/Hebrew discussion, and camp-wide activities (e.g., carnivals,  scavenger hunts). 

▪ **Havdalah:** Concludes Shabbat with a brief service after evening  

activities. 

o Camp avoids travel and monetary transactions on Shabbat but adjusts for  practical reasons, like scheduling evening activities for younger children. 

**Programming and Activities** 

***Daily Schedule*** 

• **Morning:** Bunk activities in rotation, such as tennis, gymnastics, trapeze, water  skiing, sailing, etc. 

• **Afternoon:** Electives chosen daily via iPads, allowing campers to explore interests  or focus on specific areas like sports, arts, or waterfront activities.
