o Campers design their day based on their interests. 

o Offers a balance between competitive sports, individual activities, and  creative arts. 

**Programs and Activities** 

***1. Culinary Arts*** 

• Facilities: 

o Three fully equipped teaching kitchens. 

• Activities: 

o Campers prepare dishes like sushi, cookies, bread, and granola bars from  scratch. 

o Annual cookbooks compile recipes for families to try at home. 

***2. Fitness and Individual Activities*** 

• **Martial Arts**: 

o Classes in fitness boxing, Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu, wrestling, and Krav Maga. o Dedicated martial arts gym. 

• **Strength Training**: 

o Fully equipped gym with personal trainers. 

o "Fitness Goals" program: Customized plans for campers to improve strength  and agility, tailored to personal interests (e.g., soccer training). 

• **Outdoor Adventure**: 

o Hiking, camping, outdoor cooking, and navigation. 

• **Gymnastics and Dance**: 

o Dance performances at the end of each three-week session.

o Floor routines, yoga, and dance classes. 

• **Circus Arts**: 

o Popular program including aerial silks, hoops, and straps. 

o Performances recorded and shared with families. 

***3. Outdoor Adventures*** 

• **Zip Line**: Runs across the main campus. 

• **Climbing and Bouldering**: Includes a new outdoor ninja ropes course. • **Horseback Riding**: 

o Lessons are 1 minute from camp (transport provided). 

o 2–3 lessons per week, pre-registration required. 

o Extra cost. 

***4. Water Activities*** 

• Location: Private 60-acre Hickory Lake. 

• Activities: Water skiing, tubing, canoeing, kayaking, sailing, and paddleboarding. • Heated Pool: 

o Swim instruction for campers below 6th grade is mandatory. 

o Optional swim team for older campers. 

o Additional swim lessons for campers failing the swim test. 

***5. Team Sports*** 

• Competitive programs in: 

o Soccer, volleyball, basketball, flag football, baseball, and tennis. • Participation in the Wayne County Camp Alliance for inter-camp competitions. • Recent Upgrades: 

o New tennis and basketball courts. 

o Enhanced coaching staff for sports programs. 

***6. Creative Arts*** 

• **Music**: 

o Music performances at the end of each session. 

• **Fine Arts**: 

o Painting, ceramics, crafts, tie-dye, and more.

• **Theater**: 

o Full musicals performed at the end of each session. 

o Professional theater director oversees the program. 

***7. Evening Programs*** 

• All-camp activities include: 

o Dance parties, movie nights, campfires with s’mores, Iron Chef  competitions, and July 4th fireworks. 

**Facilities** 

• **Location**: Wayne County, Pennsylvania. 

• **Bunks**: 

o Heated and air-conditioned. 

o Hotel-quality mattresses and private bathrooms. 

o 12–14 campers and 3–4 staff per bunk. 

o Organized into units with dedicated unit heads. 

• **Campus Map**: 

o Interactive map on the website, showing facilities and program areas. 

**Special Programs** 

***Taste of Zeke*** 

• **Duration**: One week. 

• **Target Audience**: Families hesitant to commit to three weeks for first-time  campers. 

• **Structure**: 

o Taste of Zeke campers have dedicated bunks based on age. 

o They rotate activities with corresponding age groups from the full program. • **Goal**: Provide a positive experience to encourage return for full sessions. • **Extensions**: 

o Campers can extend if there is space available, but this is not guaranteed.

**Jewish Life and Inclusivity** 

• **Kosher Kitchen**: 

o Fully supervised, ensuring all families feel welcome. 

• **Shabbat**: 

o Public areas maintain a “Shomer Shabbat” atmosphere. 

o Campers can follow personal practices in their bunks. 

o Activities are adjusted subtly on Saturdays to respect observant campers. • **Diversity**: 

o Camp welcomes families across the Jewish spectrum and non-Jewish  families. 

**Tuition and Financial Assistance** 

• **Transparency**: 

o Rates and Tuition Assistance prominently featured on the website. • **Scholarships**: 

o Robust scholarship program for families, including those with six-figure  incomes. 

o Designed to make camp accessible, reflecting the rising costs in the  industry. 

**Strategic Growth and Enrollment Goals** 

• **Core Sessions**: 

o 3-week and 6-week options, with increasing demand for full-season  enrollment. 

o Recent summers have reached capacity; the goal is to fill spots earlier in the  year. 

• **Expansion**: 

o Plans to increase capacity and improve facilities.

title: Camp Emerson
text:
**Key Updates and Improvements at Camp Emerson:** 

1\. **Program Development**: 

a. A strong focus on developing sports curriculum with structured progression  plans, moving away from unstructured play. 

b. Each sports session will focus on practicing specific skills, drills, and ending  with fun, ensuring campers feel they’ve learned something new. 

c. An emphasis on creating a safe environment for positive experiences,  including learning from failure. 

2\. **Activity Expansion**: 

a. Introduction of a culinary arts program in a newly constructed space with  professional-grade equipment. 

b. The return of inter-camp competitions with other Berkshire camps for variety  and challenge (not daily but periodic). 

3\. **Facilities Upgrades**: 

a. New **staff housing**: A structure called "The Quad" has replaced an outdated  facility with upgraded amenities. 

b. **New cabins on the girls' side**: These feature toilets and sinks, with similar  renovations planned across camp over time. 

c. **Zip line renovation**: A revamped zip line into Lake Emerson. 

d. **Dance studio renovation**: The studio has been gutted and rebuilt for an  enhanced arts experience. 

e. Plans to continue adding toilets and sinks in camper bunks while  maintaining existing high-quality bathhouses. 

4\. **Holistic Approach**: 

a. Activities span across sports, arts, and cabin life, ensuring a well-rounded  experience. 

b. Focus on shorter two-week sessions, with a fast-paced, engaging “sprint”  mentality to maximize camper enjoyment. 

c. Older campers (CITs) will take on leadership roles to inspire younger  campers. 

**Operational Details:** 

1\. **Elective Model**:

a. Campers pre-select activities and can adjust their schedules after arrival to  align with their interests or new friendships. 

b. Each elective session lasts 1 hour, with two sessions in the morning, two in  the afternoon, and a free time period before lunch. 

c. A structured “A/B schedule” allows for variety. Every 5th day is an “S Day”  (Special Day) with camp-wide activities and extra rest. 

2\. **Camper Demographics**: 

a. Last year, 260 total campers attended across sessions, with a mix of two week (65%) and four/six-week (35%) campers. Most campers opt for two week stays. 

b. Campers come from a broad geographic range, including international  locations (e.g., Paris, England, Ghana, Mexico, Canada). 

3\. **Activity Structure**: 

a. Activities span sports, arts, STEM (e.g., woodworking, rocketry, and science  experiments), culinary arts, water skiing, and wakeboarding. 

b. Water activities are held at a nearby lake, accessible via van transport. 4. **Special Trips for Older Campers**: 

a. CITs (age 16): A three-day trip to Vermont, including activities like hiking,  farm tours, and visiting the Ben & Jerry’s factory. 

b. Oldest campers (age 15): An overnight camping trip with activities like  mountain coasters and zip-lining. 

**Camper and Staff Support:** 

1\. **Dining and Dietary Accommodations**: 

a. Nut-free and gluten-free menus with vegetarian and vegan options. b. A dedicated dietary chef to handle allergen-friendly meals. 

c. A dietetic intern will assist with ensuring high-quality food service. d. Flexible meal options to ensure campers always find something to enjoy. 2. **Supervision and Safety**: 

a. Staff are stationed across camp to monitor transitions between activities. b. Attendance is taken at the start of every activity, with clear protocols if a  camper is late or absent. 

3\. **Staff Training**: 

a. A robust orientation includes mock scenarios for activities, meals, and  bedtime to ensure counselors are well-prepared.

b. Emphasis on building relationships among staff and ensuring they feel  confident and connected before campers arrive. 

4\. **Health and Medical Support**: 

a. Multiple nurses on-site and a doctor available for consultations. b. Urgent care is 10 minutes away, and a hospital is 18 minutes away. 5. **Inclusivity**: 

a. Case-by-case plans for transgender or gender-diverse campers, ensuring  personalized support and a safe environment. 

b. Private bathrooms and showers contribute to the camp’s inclusivity efforts. 

**Parent and Camp Expert Engagement:** 

1\. **Marketing and Communication**: 

a. Plans for a new promotional video and social media shorts to showcase  camp highlights. 

b. The co-directors are willing to travel to meet families, including visits to  South Florida, New York, and New Jersey. 

2\. **Camp Experience for Specific Groups**: 

a. The camp provides a welcoming environment for children with diverse  needs, such as dyslexia, by training staff to adapt communication and  teaching methods. 

3\. **Transportation Options**: 

a. Closest airports: Boston (preferred), Albany, and Hartford. 

b. Camp offers bus transportation from Newton, MA, and White Plains, NY, for  an additional fee.

title: Camp Arrow Wood
text:
**Camp Arrow Wood** 

**Location**: New York 

**Session Length**: Two 3-week sessions (6 weeks total) 

**Camp Size**: ~130 campers/session, with plans to grow to 250 

**Ideal For**: Motivated athletes who thrive in high-energy, sports-first environments **Gender**: Coed 

**Religious Affiliation**: None explicit; ~40–50% Jewish 

**Ages**: Middle school through rising 11th grade 

**Program Overview** 

Camp Arrow Wood is a **modern, sports-focused sleepaway camp** combining elite athletic training with traditional camp spirit. It offers both **intensive instruction** and **fun, structured gameplay**, with an emphasis on **competition with compassion**. Ideal for kids who are **active, competitive, and community-oriented**. 

**Core Sports** 

● Basketball, Soccer, Baseball, Softball, Football (Tackle & Flag), Tennis, Lacrosse, Golf ● Each led by a **college- or pro-level coach** 

● **Media & Sports Marketing elective** (e.g., highlight reels, sports broadcasting) 

**Electives & Activities** 

● Beach volleyball, field hockey, sports media, outdoor adventure 

● Full waterfront program: wakeboarding, trick skiing, sailing, kayaking ● Traditional camp events: color war, campfires, evening activities 

**Living & Structure** 

● 8 campers per bunk, 2 counselors 

● Bunked by age and gender

● Daily schedule includes: Intensive → Explore → Camper Choice → Game Time → Lake Time 

**Transportation** 

● Bus pickup from Westchester (Tarrytown/White Plains) 

● Airport pickups for national/international campers

title: Bryn Mawr Recap
text:
***Traditional Camp:*** 

**1. Structure and Offerings:** 

a. Operates as a full-season camp with a 5-week option for first-time campers,  most of whom extend their stay. 

b. Caters to campers entering **2nd grade (Mini Manor House)** up to **10th grade  (Bunk One)**. 

c. Features an **LIT program (Leaders-in-Training)** for 11th graders, including  leadership roles, program assistance, and a 10-day trip to Hawaii. 

**2. Programming and Schedules:** 

a. Younger campers (2nd–5th grade) follow structured schedules, while older  campers (6th grade and up) have more elective-based options. 

b. By 9th and 10th grades, schedules are nearly fully elective, allowing  customization and inclusion of group activities tailored to camper  

preferences. 

**3. Pullout Programs:** 

a. Specialized pullout instruction offered for activities like gymnastics, dance,  horseback riding, soccer, tennis, and basketball. 

b. These cater to campers with specific interests for more focused, small group instruction. 

**4. Retention and Recruitment:** 

a. Boasts a high **90% retention rate** year-over-year. 

b. Recruitment strategies include **tours** and a **Rookie Explorers Weekend**, a 2- night program to introduce potential campers to the camp experience. 

***Riding Camp:*** 

**1. Facilities and Capacity:** 

a. Operates at a dedicated **50-acre farm** with 30 horses and an **indoor riding  arena**, supplemented by 10 horses stabled at the main camp and 2  

additional arenas. 

b. Offers four 2-week sessions with a capacity of **88 campers per session**. **2. Program Highlights:** 

a. Focuses on high-quality instruction, advanced riding techniques (up to 3- foot jumps), and tailored lessons for riders of various skill levels.

b. Strong focus on safety and partnerships with secondary institutions like  Findlay University for high-caliber horses and training. 

**3. Camper Integration:** 

a. Riding campers dine with traditional campers but are housed and participate  in separate programs, maintaining autonomy. 

***Dance Camp:*** 

**1. Programming and Disciplines:** 

a. Offers instruction in various genres: **hip hop, ballet, jazz, tap, lyrical, and  musical theater**. 

b. Hip hop is the most popular genre among campers. 

**2. Session Goals and Performance:** 

a. The goal is to host **three sessions of 25 campers**. Last year, two sessions  were fully enrolled. 

b. Culminates in an end-of-session **performance for parents**, showcasing  camper progress. 

**3. Camper Dynamics:** 

a. Dance campers integrate with traditional campers in shared spaces like  dining but maintain program-specific activities and housing. 

***Gymnastics Program:*** 

**1. Facilities and Expertise:** 

a. Features a **40x40 spring vaulted floor**, tumble track, in-ground trampoline,  and foam pit. 

b. Hosts the **Wayne County Gymnastics Meet**, consistently winning titles for  over 40 years. 

c. Gymnastics director **Bill**, a veteran of 44 years, is lauded for his expertise  and rapport with campers. 

**2. Camper Fit:** 

a. Serves gymnasts up to **Level 5–7**. Advanced gymnasts aiming for elite-level  training typically transition to specialty programs like IGA camps.

**Camper and Staff Development** 

***Camper Support:*** 

• Emphasis on individualized attention, fostering resilience, communication skills,  and social responsibility. 

• Collaboration with parents and therapists for tailored support plans. • Dedicated programs like a **Friendship Coach** for relationship-building and one-on one social activities. 

***Staff Support:*** 

**1. Hiring and Training:** 

a. Staff are selected for qualities like warmth, empathy, adaptability, and  dedication to camper care. 

b. Comprehensive orientation and continuous training throughout the summer. **2. Amenities and Support:** 

a. Amenities like a **staff lounge, coffee bar, lockers**, and outdoor relaxation  spaces. 

b. Organized **staff trips** and social events to promote morale. 

c. A **staff coach** facilitates weekly meetings, check-ins, and workshops to  address challenges. 

**3. Behavioral Standards:** 

a. Strict policies to ensure safety and professionalism (e.g., no one-on-one  camper-staff interactions). 

b. Staff residing with campers are not permitted alcohol, while others may  enjoy limited drinks off duty under supervision. 

**Operational and Facility Updates** 

***Capital Improvements:*** 

• **Fieldhouse Revamp:** Converted storage into activity spaces (e.g., camper lounge,  cold cooking, sewing room). 

• **Dining Room:** $400,000 roof replacement for the 22,000 sq. ft. facility. • **New Additions:** Pickleball courts, new gym floor, and plans to air-condition the  gymnastics building.

• **Cold Cooking Program:** Added as a separate activity from hot cooking to meet  demand. 

***Camp Group Ownership:*** 

• Recently acquired by Camp Group, which provides back-end support like payroll,  insurance, and staffing resources. 

• Operational decisions remain with the Kagan family, ensuring continuity of Bryn  Mawr’s culture and standards. 

**Cultural and Strategic Emphasis** 

• **Single-Sex Environment:** Unique positioning as the only single-sex camp in Wayne  County. 

• **Community Integration:** Efforts to integrate specialty and traditional camp  communities while maintaining distinct programming. 

• **Staff-Camper Culture:** Strong focus on fostering a **sisterhood** and creating an  inclusive, supportive atmosphere. 

**Food and Dietary Accommodations** 

• **Dietary Needs:** Separate kitchen for gluten-free meals; accommodates dairy, soy,  and sesame allergies. 

• **Snack Program:** Introduced morning and evening snacks to address hunger outside  traditional meal times. 

• **Cooking Activities:** Cold cooking includes smoothies, acai bowls, and vegetable  sushi.

title: Brown Ledge Recap
text:
**Brown Ledge Camp — at a glance** 

**Type:** Traditionally all-girls overnight camp (inclusive of anyone who can feel safe and thrive in that program) 

**Location:** Mallets Bay on Lake Champlain (Colchester, VT), near Burlington **Big headline:** 100% freedom-of-choice programming + standout equestrian, theater, and waterfront 

**What makes Brown Ledge different** 

**“Freedom of choice” — truly 100%.** 

This isn’t “pick your schedule.” Campers decide how they spend activity time: they can be at an activity area, relaxing on a bench reading, showering, or hanging in their cabin. Staff keep an eye out for campers who are disengaging too much, but the core premise is independence and self-direction. 

**Two activity blocks per day.** 

● Morning: ~9:30–12:00 

● Afternoon: ~2:30–5:00 

Riding lessons (for campers in equestrian) are the main “fixed-time” daily commitment; theater rehearsals happen afternoons/evenings for campers who choose theater. 

**Counselors don’t sleep in camper cabins.** 

Most camper cabins are small (typically 4–5 campers, same age). Counselors are assigned to cabins and check in at night, but don’t live inside the cabin—another intentional independence-builder. 

**The setting + facilities vibe** 

**Rustic, classic, and intentionally simple.** 

Cabins have electricity, but **no running water**. Camp uses **central bathrooms** for campers and separate bathrooms for staff (a few staff cabins have private bathrooms, but those are limited).

**“Feels remote” while still close to town.** 

Camp is about **8 miles from Burlington** and near local services (including a police station), which helps with both parent peace-of-mind and staff quality of life. 

**Size:** Max ~180 campers; recent summers ~155; target ~160 per session. 

**Sessions + ages** 

**Ages:** 10–17 

**Sessions:** Two **4-week** sessions, or **full 8 weeks**. 

**Typical mix:** 

● About **50 full-summer** (includes Junior Counselors) 

● About **100 changeover** each session 

**No short “intro session.”** 

It’s 4 or 8 weeks. 

**Signature philosophy: the achievement system** Brown Ledge has an **achievement progression** designed to reward depth and mastery: ● **Basic:** attainable after a few visits 

● **Intermediate:** typically ~3 weeks of focused effort 

● **Vanguard:** highest level (often full-summer older campers / junior counselors), includes **mastery + teaching the activity**; not everyone earns it 

The camp values trying, learning, and even “productive failure,” supported by **activity specialist staff** (staff are trained/experienced in the areas they teach). 

**Camper care + support**

**Campers are expected to handle a lot of independence.** 

Brown Ledge can be a great fit for campers who want autonomy, but it’s **not ideal for campers who need constant supervision and prompting**. 

**Dedicated Camper Care team (created post-COVID).** 

There are **three Camper Care adults** (often educators / similar backgrounds) whose role is supporting campers—homesickness, conflict resolution, general wellbeing, and cabin inspections/cleanliness support. 

**Activities families ask about most** 

**Equestrian (included — no extra fee)** 

● No additional cost 

● About **60–70 campers per session** participate 

● Riding is the key scheduled element each day for riders 

**Theater (a major marquee)** 

● High-level productions: 

○ First two weeks: **three one-act plays** 

○ Third week of session: **full-length play or musical** 

● Campers can participate, but **acting roles require auditions** 

**Waterfront on Lake Champlain** 

● **Water skiing + wakeboarding** 

● **Sailing** (including an overnight sailing trip on the larger lake) 

● **Diving instruction** (learning real dives—swan dives, backflips, etc.) + springboard ● Canoeing, kayaking, paddleboarding

● Sundays: more “fun waterfront” (tubing, hang/swim) rather than instruction 

● Swim area is deep-water dock entry (not a beach), and the camp owns an island that helps protect the swim zone from boat traffic 

● They noted fewer algae issues in their swim area than other parts of the lake 

**Swim instruction:** Yes, offered (and they do in-camp swim meets / “Aqua Cade” end-of-summer show featuring skiing/sailing/boating + synchronized swimming). 

**Other popular areas** 

● **Riflery** (.22s, live ammo) + **archery** 

● **Tennis** (4 clay courts; rebuilding program with a new tennis director) 

● **Arts & crafts** (includes deeper instruction—example: ceramics taught by a professional potter alum; also sewing) 

**Food + allergies** 

**Allergies and dietary needs:** They said they can accommodate “almost all / essentially all” food allergies. 

**Kitchen:** In-house staffed/managed, giving flexibility in meal prep. 

**Religion / culture (what to tell families)** 

**Non-denominational.** 

● Meals include singing and a “grace”-style moment that’s framed more around the earth/nature than God 

● Sunday “Chapel” is not religious—campers/staff share reflections and personal experiences

**Transportation options** 

● Fly into **Burlington airport** (camp can pick up) 

● **Amtrak to Burlington** + drive/pickup 

● **Montreal pickups** available (common for international/Canada families) 

● Historically had a **NYC-area bus**, but it hasn’t run recently due to low utilization; could be revisited if demand returns 

● They noted broader challenges finding charter bus drivers lately 

**Who tends to thrive here** 

A strong fit for campers who: 

● Want **real independence** and freedom to self-direct 

● Enjoy going deep in an activity (especially equestrian, theater, or waterfront) ● Are comfortable in a **rustic** setting (central bathrooms, no running water in cabins) ● Can handle a physically “walky” campus (it’s on a ledge; mobility matters) 

Not the best fit for campers who: 

● Need constant structure, frequent check-ins, or high-supervision support 

● Have mobility limitations that make navigating camp difficult (they’ve even had ankle injuries that required going home because the terrain makes it hard) 

**Enrollment notes (helpful context for positioning)** ● Biggest feeder areas: **NYC metro**, then **Boston metro**

● Also draws from across the US + Canada (and some Europe: France/England/Spain) ● **Retention:** shared as ~67% last year 

● Second session is currently lower due to **school calendar timing** (camp ending around Aug 19 was mentioned) 

**Best point of contact** 

**Brooks Johnson (Camp Director)** currently handles **enrollment and family communication directly** (email + text are both fine).

title: Birchmont (Zoom - Nov. 4th)
text:
**Camp Birchmont — Recap (Will Pierce)** Prepared from the Zoom with Director/Owner Will Pierce. 

**Snapshot** 

Co-ed, family-owned, ACA-accredited sleepaway camp on Lake Wentworth in Wolfeboro, New  Hampshire. Target size ~275 campers per session (capacity ~300). Seven-week full season, with  4-week (first) and 3-week (second) options; a tightly managed two-week “Rookie” on-ramp for  first-timers (two per bunk; many elect to extend). Community is intentionally diverse by  geography and faith; culture emphasizes first-principles character development and confidence. 

**Leadership & Sales Approach** 

• **Director/Owner:** Will Pierce (taking primary lead on family sales/relations), with  Director Greg Howes supporting overflow and program work. 

• **Access:** Will intends to be “in the room or on the Zoom” with prospective families; open  to direct coordination with referral partners. 

• **Why now:** Birchmont has long been a “hidden gem”; the team wants to keep the  intimacy while removing the “too hidden” part. 

**Mission & Culture** 

• **Three C’s:** *Community. Confidence. Camp.* 

• **Welcome ethos:** “There are no strangers within—only friends waiting to be met.” This  greets every camper on arrival and sets the tone for cross-regional bunk communities. • **Confidence challenge:** First-night campfire prompt—“What would you attempt to do if  you knew you could not fail?” Campers state a summer goal; staff coach toward  achievement. Weekly “quiet hour” gatherings reinforce reflection and growth. 

**Location & Facilities** 

• **Wolfeboro, NH (America’s oldest summer resort):** 7 miles from Maine; ~50 miles to  Portland (PWM); ~90 miles to Boston (BOS); full-service hospital 7 miles from camp. • **Lake Wentworth waterfront:** ~½-mile private shoreline on a clean, warm (≈78–82°F)  natural-sand lake with restricted public access—quiet and safe for waterfront instruction. • **Campus scale:** 300+ acres including **Mount Delight** on property (hiking, orienteering,  lean-tos, blackberry picking), 100-tree orchard, organic garden. 

• **Program differentiators (existing):** Equestrian center (6 horses; trails + ring), 12 lit  tennis courts, 250-yard driving range, large indoor lodge for performing arts/community. • **2026 capital projects (contracts in progress):** 

o **Large indoor sports complex** with full **gymnastics** center (addressing past  demand; designed for rainy-day coverage).

o **Outdoor covered dining pavilion** with pizza ovens and grills (supports evolving,  more fresh/local food service). 

o **Food vision (multi-year):** Grow farm-to-camp sourcing partnerships; “grilled  proteins + vegetables” emphasis while keeping mainstream options. 

o **Campfire amphitheater (orchard overlook):** Planned; **may slip to 2027** pending forestry/permitting cadence. 

**Program & Daily Life** 

• **Structured choice (not a free-elective model):** At every period (except required  morning swim), campers choose among several options; deeper electives (“Senior  Select”) begin at age ~13 (elective mornings + structured-choice afternoons). • **Advanced tracks available:** 

o **Riding:** 0–5 sessions/week by sign-up. 

o **Tennis:** HPI (High Performance Instruction) up to ~1 extra hour/day with a  USPTA pro. 

o **Golf:** On-site range plus weekly play on local courses. 

o **Aquatics:** Robust progression; lifeguard certification available (25 grads last  season). Teens who pass the swim test may opt out of instruction—but most still  participate. 

• **Evening “Clubs” (2x/week):** 20 staff-led micro-electives (e.g., rocketry, fishing,  gardening, ukulele, dodgeball), chosen on the night—varies with staff passions. • **Mobility & resilience:** Birchmont **owns its bus company**, enabling ready intercamp  playdates, rainy-day trips (movies/bowling), and defined emergency relocation  (Kingswood Regional HS shelter, ~5 miles). 

**Sessions, Admissions & Community** 

• **Season structure:** 7-week full; 4-week (first) and 3-week (second) sessions. • **Rookie Camp (2 weeks, first-timers only):** Two per bunk to protect dynamics; rookies  arrive with second-session campers and can **extend to stay the final week**. o **Case-by-case** early-session two-week starts are possible when calendars demand,  but departures mid-bonding are avoided when feasible. 

• **Camper profile:** Families seeking independence and cross-regional friendships more  than “arrive with a crowd.” Birchmont is **non-denominational** and intentionally mixed  by faith and background. 

• **Scale:** ~275 campers/session, aiming toward a warm, know-every-child environment; no  plans to “go big.” 

**Transportation** 

• **Included buses:** Long Island, NYC, and Darien, CT. 

• **Air:** Staffed pickups at BOS and PWM; **chartered Florida flight** with chaperone.

**How to Position Birchmont to Families** 

• **Distinctive place + program:** A “New Hampshire classic” on a private, warm lake with  a mountain on property—paired with a structured-choice program that ensures breadth  early and autonomy as kids age. 

• **Confidence with substance:** Goal setting, reflection rituals, and visible growth arcs— beyond slogans. 

• **Right size, right mix:** ~275 campers/session fosters personal attention; cabins blend  regions/faiths; kids make *new* friends. 

• **Waterfront credibility:** Clean, warm lake; daily instruction; strong aquatics outcomes  (including lifeguard cert). 

• **2026 upgrades:** Indoor complex with gymnastics and a covered outdoor dining venue  modernize rainy-day and food experiences without losing camp feel. 

**Notes We’re Keeping Handy with Families** 

• **Structure:** Not a “do anything anytime” elective camp; it’s *guided choice* with  increasing autonomy in the teens. 

• **Rookie nuance:** Two per bunk; designed to encourage extension rather than “two-weeks forever.” 

• **Travel:** Buses from LI/NYC/Darien; BOS/PWM airport service; chartered Florida  option. 

• **Diversity:** Co-ed, non-denominational, geographically broad; kids typically arrive with  few (or no) hometown friends—by design. 

**Open Items to Track (Internal)** 

• **CapEx timeline:** Indoor complex + gymnastics + covered dining **promised for Summer  2026**; amphitheater likely **2027** if permits slow. Confirm milestones in spring  photos/updates. 

• **Rookie intake guardrails:** Keep clarity on the “two per bunk” limit and extension  strategy; document any case-by-case early-session exceptions. 

• **Structured-choice messaging:** Ensure families expecting free-elective models  understand how Birchmont’s approach works (and why it benefits younger campers). • **Florida flight:** Verify annual viability (carrier/chaperone logistics) as demand fluctuates.

title: Berridge Programs Zoom Summary
text:
**Location and Unique Features** 

**1. Chateau Mont a Pengue:** 

a. Full-time home of Andrea Mardon and her partner Chris Gaja, who are also  program directors and mentors. 

b. Served as the central hub for all Berridge programs. 

c. Students are housed in independent cottages nearby, fostering life skills and  peer bonding. 

**2. Normandy, France:** 

a. Rich in culture, history, and natural beauty with landmarks like: 

**i. Mont Saint-Michel.** 

**ii. Bayeux Tapestry.** 

**iii. D-Day Landing Beaches.** 

b. Offers 400 miles of pristine coastline and a micro-culture centered on food,  art, and history. 

c. Described as where the French vacation, giving students a unique immersion  into authentic French culture. 

**Ethos of Berridge Programs** 

• Programs focus on **creativity, community, cultural immersion, and personal  growth.** 

• Emphasis on **connection and belonging** for students in the "Covid generation" who  often missed key socialization opportunities. 

• Programs offer a **"soft landing"** for first-time travelers abroad, with a nurturing  environment and high student-to-staff ratio (4:1). 

***Accreditation and Standards:*** 

• Fully accredited by the **Gap Year Association** in 2022. 

• Accreditation ensures: 

o Rigorous health and safety standards. 

o Risk management systems in place (Cornerstone Safety Group as a partner). o Transparency for families. 

o Ability for students to apply **529 College Savings Funds** and earn **college  credit** (optional).

**Programs Overview** 

***1. Summer Program: Cooking and Culture*** 

• **Target Audience:** High school students, ages 16–19 (mature 15-year-olds  considered). 

• **Focus:** 

o Joyful cooking experiences emphasizing creativity and community. o Exploration of French food culture and traditions. 

o A blend of culinary education, cultural immersion, and creative reflection. • **Program Highlights:** 

o **Gastronomic Adventures:** Includes visits to bakeries, goat cheese farms,  and oyster tastings. Students dine at a Michelin-starred restaurant in  Cherbourg. 

o **Hands-On Cooking Classes:** Covers French classics (e.g., beef  Bourguignon, crème brûlée) and life skills (e.g., making an omelet or hosting  a party). 

o **Creative Component:** Students create a **food anthology** featuring recipes,  photographs, and reflective stories, which can serve as a portfolio or  keepsake. 

o **Excursions:** Visits to French markets, coastal hikes, and Normandy  landmarks. 

• **Daily Schedule:** 

o Morning: Community Hour (students contribute by gardening, kitchen prep,  etc.). 

o Afternoon: Cooking classes, outings, or dinner preparation. 

o Evening: Meals and sunset activities (long summer days with 10:30 PM  sunsets). 

• **Logistics:** 

o **Dates:** 

▪ Session 1: June 29 – July 19. 

▪ Session 2: July 20 – August 9. 

▪ Students may opt for both sessions (6 weeks). 

o **Cost:** $5,600 (inclusive of housing, meals, excursions, and transport within  France, excluding airfare).

***2. Gap Year Programs*** 

**1. Fall: Creative Explorer (10 Weeks)** 

a. **Focus:** Visual arts (writing, photography, filmmaking, painting, and drawing). **b. Activities:** 

i. Immersive workshops inspired by the birthplace of Impressionism. ii. Visits to Rouen, Giverny (Monet’s gardens), and local French towns. iii. Designed for students who want to explore multiple creative  

mediums. 

c. **Target Audience:** Creative, adventurous students passionate about cultural  exploration. 

**2. Spring: Creative Pathways (10 Weeks)** 

a. **Focus:** Artistic pursuits for students interested in acting, filmmaking, and  portfolio building. 

**b. Activities:** 

i. Workshops tailored to career goals in creative fields. 

ii. A week-long trip to Paris exploring museums, landmarks, and hidden  gems. 

c. **Target Audience:** Students with strong interests in creative arts or  considering careers in artistic fields. 

**3. Special Program: 30 Days of Wonder (May 5 – June 5)** 

a. **Focus:** Meditation, mindfulness, and personal growth. 

**b. Activities:** 

i. Bird sanctuary visits, coastal hikes, and philosophy workshops. 

ii. Designed to address post-high school burnout and mental health  challenges. 

c. **Target Audience:** Students seeking clarity, connection, and reflection before  transitioning to college or careers. 

• **Logistics for Gap Year Programs:** 

o **Ages:** 17–22. 

o **Cost:** $17,000 per semester. 

o Scholarships available for families with financial need. 

**Housing and Health Considerations** 

• **Student Housing:**

o Students live in cottages near the chateau, allowing for semi-independent  living. 

o Over-18 and under-18 students are housed separately to provide age appropriate environments. 

• **Health and Safety:** 

o Accommodates dietary restrictions (gluten-free, vegetarian, etc.), though  vegan and kosher diets are not supported. 

o Strong relationships with local doctors and hospitals. 

o No in-house medical staff but access to 24/7 urgent care. 

**Q&A Highlights** 

**1.** ***Dietary Needs:*** 

a. Gluten-free, nut-free, and vegetarian diets are accommodated. 

b. Vegan and kosher diets cannot be supported. 

**2. Transportation:** 

a. Summer program: Staff escorts students from Paris airport to Normandy via  private coach. 

b. Gap Year: Students travel via a staff-accompanied train from Paris. **3. Group Size:** 

a. Summer program: 8–16 students per session. 

b. Gap Year: Typically 12–18 students per cohort. 

**4. Mental Health Support:** 

a. Partnered with Cornerstone Safety Group for risk management and mental  health resources.

title: Bede's Recap
text:
**Bede’s Summer School — Overview** 

Bede’s isn’t a pop-up summer program — it’s a **year-round school** that runs a **well-established summer school (about 34 years)** with the benefit of full-time infrastructure behind it. That means **strong safeguarding, health/safety, and clinical leadership** carried over from the academic year into the summer operation. 

**Program Snapshot** 

● **Program type:** Residential programs across sites, with **day programs now available** (notably at Eastbourne) 

● **Ages: 6–20** overall, split by campus to keep age bands tight 

● **Locations (summer): 5 sites**, all in the **southeast of England** 

● **Nationalities: 58+ nationalities** represented (last year) 

● **Language quota: Max 20% per spoken language** at each center to keep an international mix 

**The Bede’s Model** 

Bede’s runs **age-specific campuses** (rather than mixing very young and older teens together). Each site has: 

● A defined **age range** 

● A program built for that age group — **academics + activities + excursions** 

● Close geographic proximity between sites, which allows **easy quality control** from head office

**Locations and Ages** 

● **Eastbourne (Prep site): 6–11** 

● **Windlesham: 8–13** 

● **Upper Dicker (Senior site / “Dicker”): 12–17** 

● **Lancing: 13–17** 

● **Brighton (University campus): 15–20** 

**Eastbourne — “Little Explorers” (Ages 6–11)** 

Designed for Bede’s youngest students, with structured mornings and fun, age-appropriate afternoons. 

● **Schedule: 17.5 hours of English/week** 

○ English in the **morning** 

○ **Activities** in the afternoon (ball games, crafts, swimming, etc.) 

● **Excursions: Wednesday + Saturday + Sunday** 

○ Wednesday: local trip (theme/adventure parks, etc.) 

○ Saturday: **London** (kid-friendly attractions like Madame Tussauds, etc.) ○ Sunday: local/coastal options (Brighton or Eastbourne-area outings) ● **Care & supervision:** Strong ratios and **individual attention** for homesickness/support 

● **New add-on: Student Ambassadors** (year-round Bede’s prep students) assisting in classes 

● **Local integration:** A **holiday club** brings local students into activities/breaks to encourage more English practice 

Students can attend as:

● **Residential**, or 

● **Day program (Mon–Fri)** with a **parent program option off-site** (parents don’t stay on campus but can stay nearby in private accommodation) 

**Windlesham (Ages 8–13)** 

A “mid-sized” center (roughly **100–120** students at peak weeks), in a countryside setting with strong facilities. Students can book **1–5 weeks**. 

**Future Explorers** 

A progression pathway from Little Explorers: 

● **English Skills** (reading/writing/speaking/listening) 

● **Explorations** (subject-based learning via themes like science/tech/geography) ● **New for 2026: Mission Possible** 

○ A weekly class challenge designed so **every student has a role**, helping quieter students participate and showing the group that “impossible” becomes possible through teamwork 

**Bear Grylls Adventure Camp** 

A Bede’s partnership program at Windlesham: 

● Students still do core morning English components (incl. Mission Possible), but the rest of the day is **adventure-focused** 

● Includes: **orienteering, camp building, firelighting, raft survival, first aid** ● Delivered by **Bear Grylls’ team** (not just brand licensing) 

**Upper Dicker (Ages 12–17) — Bede’s Main Specialist Hub**

This is the flagship senior-school campus and the home base for several specialist programs. It’s a green, spread-out countryside campus and one of the busiest sites (around **200 students/week** capacity). 

**Core Program: English Plus** 

● **English Skills + Enrichment + Research Project** 

● Projects have included things like **Dragon’s Den** and **podcasts** 

**Specialist Programs (run from Dicker)** 

● **Pro Tennis** (led by Francesca Byrne, former GB representative) — 

strength/conditioning, tactics, resilience; flexible 1–3 week participation 

● **Pro Dance** (2-week program; small cohort ~20) led by **Paul Liburd, MBE**, West End background; ends with a filmed showcase (parents may attend if in the UK) 

● **Pro Music** (2-week) — guitar/piano/singing; performance + improvisation/performance workshops 

● **Zoology & Animal Management** — built around the **on-site zoo** (about **70 species**, including lemurs, squirrel monkeys, reptiles, etc.) 

● **La Liga Football** — Spanish La Liga coaches + elite-level strength/conditioning support ○ Can be **football-only** or **football + language** 

○ Requires at least **A2 English** to participate 

○ High-performance focus (analytics, conditioning, skills), with at least one past example of a student being identified for a trial opportunity 

**Notable Academy Option** 

● **Flying Academy** (not a simulator): students go up in a **real aircraft** with an instructor (example shared: flight to Isle of Wight, tea, and return) 

**Lancing (Ages 13–17)**

Often described as: **slightly older average age** than Dicker, and **more academic**. ● **Minimum English level: A2 required** (Dicker can take beginners) 

● Programs include: 

○ **English Plus** 

○ **English for the Future** (theme-based future-focused English development) ○ **Business Leadership (2-week)** 

■ Covers: entrepreneurship, operations, finance, HR, marketing 

■ Includes visits to local businesses and planned external speakers 

(example mentioned: a Bede’s governor who was CEO of Eurostar) 

**Brighton University (Ages 15–20)** 

For older students who want a university-style experience while still under Bede’s supervision. ● Students live in **university accommodation** and eat in **university dining halls** ● Still structured: students are expected to **attend lessons and activities** (not optional) ● Courses: 

○ **English for the Future** 

○ **Pathways (16–20)** including **IELTS prep + exam option** 

**Who It’s Best For** 

Bede’s is a strong fit for students who want: 

● A **structured, well-supervised UK summer school** backed by a real year-round institution

● A **true age-banded** environment (no 7-year-olds mixed with 17-year-olds) ● **International diversity** with a real effort to avoid single-language clustering (20% quota) 

● Options ranging from **classic English + activities** to **highly specialized** pathways (La Liga football, dance, music, zoology, flying, business leadership) 

● A pathway that can start young (**age 6**) and continue through older teen/young adult (**up to age 20**)

title: Atlas Tennis Recap
text:
**Introduction to Atlas Tennis** 

• **What is Atlas Tennis?** 

o A summer tennis program for teenagers aged 13–18, combining competitive  play, immersive training, and cultural exploration in Europe. 

o Focused on providing unique tennis experiences, including tournaments,  training on red clay courts, and engaging with local communities. 

• **Objective**: 

o Go beyond conventional tennis tours to create transformative personal  growth experiences by blending high-level tennis with cultural immersion. 

**Atlas Tennis Program Breakdown** 

***Program Structure and Itineraries*** 

1\. **Trips Offered**: 

a. **Atlas Tennis Signature Trip**: 

i. Focused on Spain (north of Barcelona), Holland (near Amsterdam),  and Switzerland (Swiss Alps, Geneva). 

ii. Activities: Tournaments, training, and participation in local tennis  camps. 

iii. Cultural highlights include sightseeing in Geneva and the Swiss Alps. b. **Bucket List Trip**: 

i. Combines visits to iconic locations like Wimbledon and Milan. 

ii. Activities: Playing on grass courts, attending Wimbledon during its  first week, and playing tournaments in Amsterdam. 

iii. Incorporates pro interactions (e.g., clinics with professional players  or coaches). 

c. **Explore European Tennis Trip**: 

i. Geared toward beginner and intermediate players. 

ii. Covers London, France (Annecy and Paris), and optionally  

Amsterdam. 

iii. Offers training and tournaments but at a less intense pace. 

d. **Clay Court Prep Trip**: 

i. Designed for advanced players targeting collegiate tennis. 

ii. Early-summer focus with intense training and competitions.

2\. **Flexibility in Participation**: 

a. Participants can join trips late or leave early, with itineraries adjusted  accordingly. 

b. Trips run for 2–3 weeks, with group departures starting the last Saturday in  June. 

3\. **Group Dynamics**: 

a. Each group comprises 10–15 players. 

b. Supervised by 2–3 coaches, maintaining a high player-to-coach ratio. c. Coaches are current or former college coaches with extensive experience. 

***Daily Life and Activities*** 

1\. **Tennis Training**: 

a. Practice 6 days a week, tailored to the group’s needs and skill levels. b. Training evolves based on group energy—intense early on, with lighter  sessions as fatigue builds. 

c. Focus on adapting to red clay courts, which emphasize strategy and  endurance. 

2\. **Tournaments and Matches**: 

a. Participants compete against local players in European tournaments. b. Matches count toward Universal Tennis Rating (UTR), appealing to  competitive players. 

c. Matches help participants grow by exposing them to new playing styles and  strategies. 

3\. **Cultural Exploration**: 

a. Includes sightseeing in major cities (Paris, London, Amsterdam) and smaller,  scenic towns (Annecy, Swiss Alps). 

b. Immersive local experiences: staying in residential areas, visiting historic  sites, and exploring regional cuisine. 

4\. **Fitness and Mental Conditioning**: 

a. Off-court fitness sessions like cross-training and warm-ups. 

b. Discussions on mental toughness and sports psychology to handle  challenges, improve focus, and manage stress. 

5\. **Accommodation and Logistics**: 

a. Accommodations include clean, safe hotels and occasional university  dorms.

b. Weekly laundry stops are organized, with coaches assisting first-timers. 

6\. **Meals**: 

a. Breakfast and dinner included daily. 

b. Lunch is flexible, based on match schedules and personal preferences. c. Coaches encourage healthy eating but allow personal choices. 

***Program Highlights*** 

1\. **Professional Exposure**: 

a. Opportunities to interact with top players (e.g., Matteo Berrettini) and attend  professional tournaments. 

b. Behind-the-scenes tours, clinics, and networking with college and  professional coaches. 

2\. **Cultural Immersion**: 

a. Integration into local tennis communities, fostering international friendships. b. Experience life outside tourist hubs by staying and playing in residential  areas. 

3\. **Growth-Oriented Environment**: 

a. Focus on personal development: handling adversity, managing discomfort,  and embracing new perspectives. 

b. Group discussions foster reflection on personal growth and learning. 4. **Advanced Technology**: 

a. Incorporates AI-driven apps to analyze match performance, providing  players with detailed feedback. 

**Program Logistics** 

1\. **Travel Coordination**: 

a. Most participants travel from New York on the same flight, chaperoned by  coaches. 

b. Flexible options for participants traveling from other locations or countries. 2. **Safety and Supervision**:

a. Coaches ensure players are well-supervised, including in accommodations  and during travel. 

b. Health and safety measures include managing injuries, prioritizing rest, and  ensuring adequate nutrition. 

3\. **Accommodations**: 

a. Mid-range hotels or dorms, chosen for safety, cleanliness, and convenience. b. Coaches ensure weekly laundry facilities are accessible. 

4\. **Cost Flexibility**: 

a. Trips are prorated for participants joining late or leaving early. 

**Future Expansion** 

1\. **Custom Trips**: 

a. Atlas Tennis plans to offer customized trips for families, schools, or adult  groups. 

b. These may include shorter options, like spring break programs. 

2\. **Year-Round Opportunities**: 

a. Expanding the scope beyond summer trips to include travel during other  parts of the year. 

3\. **Adult and Family Trips**: 

a. Developing plans for trips tailored to adults or multi-generational families,  building on the program’s current success.

title: Amigos Recap
text:
**Mission and Vision** 

• **Mission:** To inspire lifelong leadership and global citizenship through service and  immersion experiences. 

• **Vision:** A world where all individuals are lifelong leaders sharing responsibility for  the global community. 

**Program Structure and Components** 

Amigos provides several programs for youth aged 13–19. These programs are divided into  three categories, designed to meet participants’ levels of experience, maturity, and  interests. 

***1. Introductory Programs (2 Weeks)*** 

• **Target Audience:** Youth aged 13–14 or first-time international travelers. • **Spanish Requirement:** None. 

• **Length:** 12–14 days. 

• **Host Family Stay:** Included. 

• **Focus:** Group-based activities during the day and individual time with host families  in the evening. 

• **Key Themes:** 

o **Costa Rica (Ecotourism and Environmental Protection):** 

▪ Work with a women’s association on river and biodiversity  

conservation. 

o **Panama (Education and Community Service):** 

▪ Engage with schools, lead extracurricular activities for children, and  contribute to community improvement projects. 

• **Support Structure:** Daily supervision by group staff (minimum ratio of 1 staff to 8  participants).

***2. Intermediate Programs (3–3.5 Weeks)*** 

• **Target Audience:** Youth aged 15–19 with at least two years of Spanish experience. • **Spanish Requirement:** Two years or equivalent; no fluency required. • **Length:** 21–24 days. 

• **Host Family Stay:** Included, with staff and group supervision during daytime  activities. 

• **Focus:** A mix of group and individual experiences, combining service projects with  cultural immersion. 

• **Key Themes and Locations:** 

o **Costa Rica (Biodiversity, Marine Conservation):** Focus on farming,  community organizing, and wildlife conservation. 

o **Panama (Sustainability and Agriculture):** Focus on rural food systems and  sea turtle conservation. 

o **Paraguay (Cultural Preservation):** Engage with local communities and learn  Guarani, an indigenous language. 

o **Dominican Republic (Community Development):** Collaborate with  women’s associations and lead youth activities. 

• **Support Structure:** Small to medium-sized groups, with at least one staff member  per group. 

***3. Intensive Programs (4–6 Weeks)*** 

• **Target Audience:** Youth aged 15–19 seeking greater independence and cultural  immersion. 

• **Spanish Requirement:** Advanced beginner to intermediate proficiency; fluency not  required. 

• **Length:** 4–6 weeks. 

• **Host Family Stay:** Included. 

• **Focus:** High independence, with volunteers placed in individual communities (with  1–2 other volunteers) to develop and implement service projects. 

• **Key Themes and Locations:** 

o **Paraguay:** Deep cultural immersion with community-led initiatives. o **Dominican Republic:** Emphasis on local leadership and service project  planning. 

• **Support Structure:**

o Volunteers set their own daily schedules and integrate fully into the  community. 

o Weekly check-ins with staff to monitor progress, well-being, and host family  dynamics. 

**Core Program Features** 

**1. Host Family Stays:** 

a. A defining feature for programs involving ages 15–19. 

b. Families are thoroughly vetted through background checks, training, and  ongoing relationships. 

c. Typically, volunteers stay with one or two peers in a family setting. d. Volunteers report transformative experiences in building relationships with  their host families. 

**2. Service Learning:** 

a. Projects are **community-led**, addressing local needs with a focus on  collaboration, not charity. 

b. Hands-on activities like trail building, farming, teaching, and biodiversity  conservation. 

**3. Disconnect to Reconnect Policy:** 

a. Volunteers are prohibited from using internet-connected devices during the  program. 

b. Designed to foster connection with the community and group members. c. Staff share updates via social media (e.g., Instagram) and email with  families. 

**4. Cultural Immersion and Leadership:** 

a. Volunteers develop critical cross-cultural communication skills. b. Exposure to global challenges like climate change, indigenous rights, and  community organizing. 

**5. Post-Program Opportunities:** 

a. Alumni can return as project staff or mentors and participate in the **Social  Impact Accelerator**, which offers mentorship and funding for local service  projects.

**Health and Safety Protocols** 

**1. Pre-Departure:** 

a. Thorough mental and physical health screening. 

b. Students must be stable on any psychotropic medications for six months  before departure. 

**2. In-Country Support:** 

a. Staff-to-volunteer ratio of 1:8. 

b. Weekly check-ins ensure volunteers' physical and emotional well-being. c. 24/7 emergency response system, including on-call support and vetted  medical facilities. 

**3. Host Family Policies:** 

a. Volunteers are immediately reassigned if issues arise. 

b. Families are selected based on strong relationships with local partner  organizations. 

**Program Costs** 

**1. Tuition:** 

a. Costs range from $3,275–$5,425 depending on program length and location. b. Includes meals, accommodations, and activities but excludes airfare. **2. Fundraising:** 

a. National chapter: Fundraising optional but encouraged. 

b. Local chapters: Fundraising required, reducing costs. 

**3. Airfare:** 

a. Booked by Amigos for consistency; billed separately. 

b. Flights typically connect through gateway hubs like Houston or Miami. **4. College Credit Option:** 

a. Participants can earn 2–3 credits through Western Colorado University for a  fee ($170/credit). 

b. Tuition qualifies for payment via 529 College Savings Plans. 

**Eligibility and Admissions** 

**1. Basic Requirements:** 

a. Age: 13–19, depending on program.

b. Spanish proficiency: Varies by program (none to intermediate). 

c. Health: Stable physical and mental health. 

**2. Application Process:** 

a. Online form with $75 fee. 

b. Essay responses demonstrating readiness and enthusiasm. 

c. Rolling deadlines: April–May for summer programs. 

**3. Selection Criteria:** 

a. Non-competitive, but applicants must show maturity, independence, and  awareness of program demands. 

**Impact and Outcomes** 

**1. Measured Benefits:** 

a. Improved leadership skills, problem-solving, and self-confidence. b. Increased cultural awareness and respect for diversity. 

c. Inspiration for lifelong service and global engagement. 

**2. Alumni Network:** 

a. Participants join a large community of passionate leaders, with opportunities  for continued involvement in Amigos programs. 

**Key Questions from Q&A** 

**1. Host Family Pairing:** 

a. Most volunteers are placed in pairs or trios; solo placements depend on  program logistics. 

b. Concerns about placements can be flagged for accommodations. **2. Group Sizes:** 

a. Introductory programs: 8–25 participants. 

b. Intermediate programs: 10–25 participants, divided into smaller groups if  necessary. 

c. Intensive programs: Volunteers placed individually or in pairs. 

**3. Language Preparation:** 

a. While programs require varying levels of Spanish, the immersive experience  improves fluency regardless of prior proficiency.

title: Active Travel
text:
**Snapshot** 

Active Travel Camps is a small, owner-led adventure travel program in southern France run by Phil Gasson (a qualified schoolteacher with about 40 years’ experience in school and family trips). 

The program is built as a three-part journey across France: 

● Week 1: Mountains (Alps – Morzine area) 

● Week 2: River (Ardèche Gorge) 

● Week 3: Sea (Mediterranean coast, near the Spanish border) 

Families can book 1, 2, or 3 weeks, but more than 80% choose the full three-week experience. Maximum group size is 24 students, which keeps it intimate and highly supervised. 

**Ages, Group Size & Profile** 

● Ages: 10–18 (mean age ~13) 

● Most common: 13–14-year-olds, roughly UK Year 8–9 

● Group cap: 24 students total 

● Students are split into age-appropriate groups for activities 

● Nationalities: very mixed (“like the UN”) 

○ South America (notably Colombia) 

○ Europe (Germany, Iceland, France, UK, Israel, others) 

○ No single nationality dominates 

**Program Structure: 1–3 Weeks**

Families may book: 

● Any 1 of the 3 weeks 

● Any 2-week combination 

● All 3 weeks (the most common option) 

Some families start with 1 week and then send their child back the following year to complete the “missing” weeks. Active Travel is flexible on joining and departure points, and coordinates closely with parents on logistics. 

**Week 1 – Mountains (Morzine, Alps)** 

Location and feel: 

● Based in Morzine, an alpine resort town in the French Alps 

● Cooler temperatures (around 17°C), good acclimatization week 

Core activities: 

● Rock climbing 

● Introductory whitewater rafting 

● Kayaking on a glacier-fed lake (distinctive milky-blue color) 

● Archery and other land-based games by the lakeside 

● A guided trek from France into Switzerland with Alpine guides 

Accommodation: 

● Ski chalet / mountain center in Morzine 

● Purpose-built for youth groups 

● Warm, solid base for the more “mountain” style activities

All technical activities are delivered by qualified French instructors, with the Active Travel team overseeing and accompanying. 

**Week 2 – River (Ardèche Gorge)** 

Location and climate: 

● Ardèche region in southern France 

● Much warmer: mid-to-high 30°C (≈90°F) at times 

Signature river expedition: 

● Two-day kayak trip down the Ardèche Gorge 

● Overnight “bivvy” on official government bivouac sites 

● Students carry their personal kit (sleeping bag, mat, clothes, toiletries) in barrels in their kayaks 

● Active Travel carries the food and oversees logistics 

● Very clear, starry skies, with no light pollution (“million-star hotel”) 

Canyoning (private canyon): 

● Full three-quarter-day canyoning experience 

● Privately owned canyon where natural obstacles have been made safe ○ Aluminum ladders, slides, and toboggans added where needed 

● High-energy, but carefully controlled; a big highlight for campers 

High ropes and zipline day: 

● Full-day high ropes course in the trees 

● Multiple routes and levels, including a zipline crossing the river

● Unlimited “laps” – kids typically don’t get bored 

Cultural and recovery day: 

● Visit to Chauvet Cave (Grotte Chauvet) replica 

● Prehistoric art and cave paintings (around 37,000 years old) ● Acts as a lighter, cultural day to rest bodies mid-week 

Evenings: 

● Structured evening program with: 

○ Team games 

○ Reflection / plenary at the end of each day 

○ Mindfulness woven into the daily wrap-up 

**Week 3 – Sea (Mediterranean Coast, near Spanish Border)** 

Location: 

● Mediterranean coast in southern France, inland from the beach ● House is within about 25 minutes of all activity sites 

Core sea and beach activities: 

● Catamaran sailing 

● Single-sail dinghies (Picos) 

● Stand-up paddleboarding

● Windsurfing 

● Kayaking close to shore (no long open-sea crossings) 

Mountain and river day in the Pyrenees: 

● Day trip up into the Pyrenees 

● Whitewater rafting on more serious rapids (up to Class IV) 

● Afternoon session in “duckies” (inflatable kayaks) on sections of the river 

Warm canyon and lake day: 

● Man-made canyon, warmer water, different feel than Week 2 canyon ● Half-day canyoning session 

● Half-day at a lake on the Spanish border 

○ Wakeboarding 

○ Water-skiing on a cable-driven system (not towed by boat), which makes it easier for students to stand and succeed 

Cultural excursion (weather dependent): 

● Visit to the Salvador Dalí Museum in Figueres, Spain 

● Half-day cultural trip across the border 

Accommodation: 

● Large, private house sleeping all 24 students 

● Swimming pool 

● Close to the beach 

● Commercial-grade kitchen and safety features (fire alarms, etc.)

**Language & Nationalities** 

Program language: 

● Day-to-day camp language is English 

● All external activity providers speak English to a good level 

● Active Travel staff are bilingual (at minimum English/French), and several also speak Spanish, German, etc. 

English level: 

● Many non-native students attend international or American schools and already use English daily 

● English proficiency is not treated as a hard barrier 

● Students with very little English can attend, but a basic level is helpful for comfort and group integration 

English courses: 

● English lessons can be offered, but historically almost no one takes them 

● Language learning mostly happens informally “by osmosis” through living and playing in English 

**Travel, Airports & Chaperone Options** 

Primary airport for Week 1 / full 3-week arrival: 

● Lyon (main hub, flights from North America, South America, Iceland, etc.) Other options:

● Geneva also works for Week 1 (close to Morzine) 

● For Week 3-only or late joiners: 

○ Carcassonne or Montpellier commonly used 

● Active Travel coordinates individually with parents to find the best arrival point and time 

Transfers: 

● Pick-up and drop-off organized by Active Travel for the specified airports/locations ● Additional “chaperone service” available at extra cost: 

○ Example: sending a staff member to meet a younger child in Paris and accompany them by train to camp 

● In some cases, parents meet the group at a motorway service area for handover 

**Accommodation & Transport** 

Accommodation across the three weeks: 

● Week 1 (Morzine): ski chalet / youth mountain center 

● Week 2 (Ardèche): small cabins, 2–4 per cabin 

● Week 3 (Mediterranean): large house for the whole group with pool and commercial kitchen, fully equipped for youth groups 

Transport within France: 

● Two (sometimes three) commercial minibuses (≈17–20 seats each) 

● Smaller vehicles allow access to narrow roads and activity areas better than full coaches ● All drivers hold commercial licenses (PCV or D4)

**Staffing, Ratios & Safety** 

Staffing: 

● Supervision ratio: 1 staff member to 8 campers 

○ Better than typical UK ratio of 1:10 

● Mixed-gender staff team 

● Phil and other leaders are experienced school professionals 

Safety approach: 

● All high-risk activities (rafting, canyoning, climbing) led by fully qualified local French providers 

● Active Travel staff attend all activities and maintain oversight 

● Full written risk assessments exist for every activity and can be shared on request 

● Canyons and courses are carefully engineered for safety, with man-made improvements where necessary 

**Dates & Pricing** 

Dates: 

● Full 3-week program: June 27 – July 18 

● Timed to follow UK independent schools breaking up (around June 25–26), leaving August free for family travel 

Pricing: 

● 2,000 euros per week 

● 6,000 euros for all three weeks

● Price has intentionally not been raised; seen as fair value for the level of activities and travel involved 

● Families must arrange their own travel insurance 

● Flights are not included 

**Who Active Travel Is a Good Fit For** 

Ideal for: 

● Adventurous, outdoorsy kids who love water, mountains, and trying new sports 

● Families seeking a genuine travel program (moving through regions), not a single-site camp 

● Students comfortable with bivvying under the stars, canyoning, rafting, and full active days 

● Families who value small group size and direct contact with the owner/operator ● International students wanting a diverse group without one nationality dominating ● Kids who might benefit from informal English immersion while doing activities 

Less ideal for: 

● Students who prefer a campus-based, classroom-heavy program 

● Teens who are not comfortable with physical challenge, moving around, or being outdoors most of the time 

**How to Present Active Travel to Parents** 

Key talking points for advisors:

● Three-part “mountain – river – sea” journey across France, with distinct weekly themes ● Small, tightly run group (max 24) with high staffing ratios and owner involvement ● Long-standing operator with 40 years’ experience in school and youth travel 

● Very strong activity mix: canyoning, rafting, sailing, wakeboarding, bivvy expedition, climbing, etc. 

● Program conducted in English, with bilingual staff and a wide international mix 

● Flexible entry: 1-, 2-, or 3-week options; multiple airport choices; possibility of chaperoned travel 

● Excellent option in France for families who want a high-energy, multi-sport travel experience rather than a traditional camp

title: ActionQuest _ Global Expedition Group
text:
**Global Expeditions Group** 

**(ActionQuest / Seamester / GoBeyond) — Zoom Notes** Snapshot 

• Presenters: Blair Williamson (GXG), Jake Steward (Ops Director), Mike Meighan  (Executive Director, ActionQuest). 

• GXG is the umbrella for three programs: ActionQuest (teen sailing/scuba), Seamester  (gap + college credit at sea), GoBeyond (service/adventure, mostly land). Origins trace to  the 1960s–70s “Flint School” year-at-sea model. 

• Tone: expedition mindset, close-quarters community, skill progression (sailing, scuba,  marine biology), and a big emphasis on risk management with senior leaders on the  water. 

ActionQuest (teen) — what families need to know 

• Ages: typically 13–19 (can take 12-year-olds; most are teens). 

• Format: live aboard 50-ft sailboats around the BVI. Each boat ≈ 12 students + 3 staff  (skipper, dive instructor, and a medically advanced first mate—nurse/EMT/WFR). Boats  are co-ed; cabins divided by gender; age/grade peer grouping. 

• Durations: 21-day has been the mainstay; 17–21 day options are most common. 14-day  launched last year; first 10-day option launches this year (they noted 10 days effectively  becomes ~8 on-program due to travel). Some students stack two sessions (≈6 weeks). 

• Daily life: flotillas of ~4 boats move together/apart; new anchorages most days. Schedule  flexes for weather/opportunity (“expedition mentality”). 

• Skills & certs: sailing from absolute beginner to advanced; PADI scuba from Open Water  through Divemaster; variable-depth marine biology; watersports throughout. Community  service possible (up to ~100 hours, depending on track). 

• Sleeping & cabins: many students choose to sleep on deck under the stars; cabins often  used for gear/clothes. Cabins typically double-berth style with a private head; expect  “glamping” more than hotel furnishings. 

• Travel & arrivals: students commonly fly to St. Thomas (USVI). Staff meet them in the  U.S., then ferry to the BVI together to clear customs. 

• Seasickness & “pirates”: waters are generally calm; some take OTC meds for the first 2– 3 days. Pirate lore = fun history hikes (e.g., Blackbeard lookout), not a present-day  concern. 

• Weather & safety: itinerary tweaks are routine (sail around squalls or swap in academics).  In a rare tropical storm year, they docked all boats and moved students to shoreside villas  for a night; back sailing next day. Mike and Jake lead on-water during the season, not  from a desk; risk management is a core pillar. 

New/unique items that stood out 

• 2026 ocean voyages for teens using a Seamester schooner: 

• Leg 1 (3 weeks): BVI → Bermuda → New York City timed with the U.S. 250th (July 

4). 

• Leg 2 (≈40 days): New York → Boston (250th events) → transatlantic to Barcelona;  includes University of South Florida credit. 

• Longer track students can combine sessions; GXG leaned into the “expedition” identity  and leadership development that happen over time. 

Seamester (gap + college credit) — quick context for older siblings/counsel 

• High-school grads (18–23 typical). Three big schooners operate year-round; voyages 20– 90 days worldwide. 

• In-person academics onboard (no online modules); University of South Florida credit  (≈3–12 credits depending on length). 

• Sailing/scuba from beginner to professional levels; “live, study, crew, and cross oceans”  framing. 

GoBeyond (service/adventure) — quick context 

• Ages trend a bit older within high school (15–19). 

• 2- and 3-week programs. Land-based options this year in Australia and Iceland; one BVI  option overlaps with AQ but emphasizes meaningful service hours over certs. 

Admissions & fit 

• EQ + readiness: emotionally capable of close-quarters living; comfortable in/around  water; physically able to crew on a moving boat. 

• Families often ask, “Is my student a fit if they’re new to sailing?” Most are first-timers;  GXG teaches from zero to advanced. 

• Dietary needs (veg/vegan/GF) are accommodated with reasonable flexibility given  provisioning constraints. 

Notes we’ll keep handy with families 

• “Boat as cabin” is the big mental shift: shared living, teamwork, rotating roles, and  leadership moments daily. 

• Expect a mix of tight-knit small-group days (your flotilla) and big social moments when  the fleet converges. 

• First-time sailors are normal; strong staff-to-student ratios and clear eligibility criteria  help set expectations. 

• Weather plan is practical, not heroic: change the plan early; dock and go shoreside if  needed.

title: Academy Camps
text:
**Academy Camps** 

**Location**: Suffield Academy, Connecticut 

**Session Length**: 6 one-week sessions (mix and match); campers can attend 1–6 weeks **Camp Size**: ~100–150 campers/session 

**Ideal For**: Well-rounded student-athletes and rising 5th–10th graders 

**Gender**: Coed 

**Religious Affiliation**: Secular (no religious programming) 

**Program Overview** 

Academy Camps offers a **modern, high-performance summer experience** blending **elite-level coaching** in 8 sports with a classic sleepaway camp lifestyle. Perfect for campers who want a **structured, fun, and emotionally supportive environment** without the pressure of a pure elite-sport pipeline. 

**Sports Offered** 

● Basketball, Soccer, Tennis, Football, Volleyball, Swimming, Baseball, Squash ● Campers choose one sport per week (can switch weekly) 

● 3 hours of daily training with top-tier coaches 

**Specialty Offerings** 

● **Varsity Class**: Leadership & mentorship program for rising 10th graders ● **Bridge Weekends**: Off-campus trips for multi-week campers 

**Camp Life** 

● Afternoon electives, AC Cup (color war-style competition) 

● Evening programs: trivia, movie nights, adventure elements 

● Fully residential in **boarding school dorms**

**Concerns to Know** 

● Dorm rooms **not air-conditioned**, but common areas are (fans encouraged) ● Small session sizes can feel quiet compared to large traditional camps ● Not a fit for high-intensity elite-only sport families

title: Agawak — Ashley — Unknown Date
text:
# Agawak



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Agawak, established in 1921, is an all-girls camp located on a 450 acre, private lake in Minocqua, Wisconsin. Agawak serves rising 1st-10th grade campers and offers 4 different session options (4-day rookie camp, 2-week rookie camp, (2) 3.5 week sessions, or the full 7 weeks)--Priced from $2,700 to $14,000. The camp is ACA accredited, and has been owned and operated by Mary Fried for over 30 years.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Summer 2025 hosted upwards of 230 campers. Campers come mostly from the Chicagoland area, but there are many campers from California, Ohio, Wisconsin, New York, Florida, Missouri, Texas. There are international campers as well from Mexico, Taiwan, and Israel. Approximately 25% of campers are daughters or granddaughters of alumni. 80% of campers stay for the full 7 weeks. Camper: staff ratio is 3:1. The staff is mostly former campers. There are also staff from Australia, UK, New Zealand, and Scotland.

Director (Mary) is incredibly hands on and present throughout the camp day. The assistant director, Chris, is wonderfully kind and knowledgeable. Mary's son, Jacob, is the associate director and is planning to take over camp in the next few years. They all know every camper by name and are the heart and soul of camp.



## 3 · Living Quarters

There are 26 bunks in camp--all have toilets and sinks, and most have showers inside the cabins. Campers sleep in wooden bunk beds. Counselors live in a "counselor room" separate from campers. All cabins have heat, as well as screened windows and fans for air flow. Cabins that do not have showers utilize shower houses with private shower stalls and toilets. Cabins are cleaned by campers. Common areas (shower houses, dining hall, etc.) are cleaned professionally.

Laundry is sent out once a week and is returned the next day, folded.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The 450-acre, sand-bottom lake is the centerpiece of camp. The lake front is expansive and breathtaking. There is a swim area with a water trampoline and inflatable slide. There is an expansive boating area with kayaks, canoes, stand up paddle boards, and sailboats. There is a separate area for pontoon boats and fishing. Agawak has an impressive ski program with a fleet of top of the line boats.

Swim instruction and free swim are done in the lake. There is a beach area that spans the length of the lakefront. Campers spend a lot of time at the lake for activities and special events. There is no pool.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Each activity day runs 6 periods. All activities are chosen by the camper. There is activity sign up one time per week and campers can choose their own schedules. Swim is mandatory based on age and the results of their swim test. After breakfast and cabin clean up, campers go to 3 activities. Then lunch and rest hour. After rest hour there are 3 activities, a free period and then dinner. After dinner is evening program. Evening programs are usually all-camp activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Agawak is known for their competitive Blue and White color war program. Color war lasts all summer, and campers are on teams for life. Families are always kept on the same team. Signature Blue and White programs include Lip Sync, Capture the Flag, and Treasure Hunt.

Olympic games (first session) and Final Four (second session) competitions last for approx. 3 days each session. Camp is split up into 4 teams. These teams and themes change each year. These are an additional color war type program.

Counselor In Training (CIT) campers are required to attend all summer and go on a required, week-long camping and canoe trip to the Boundary Waters.

Agawak's activity and camp facilities are top of the line. They just built a brand new, state of the art dance studio. Other offerings to note are a designated indoor cooking building, large climbing wall, radio station, ceramics building, and newly refinished basketball and tennis courts. All outdoor courts have lights for night-time play.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The infirmary is staffed by nurses and a visiting doctor each session. The nearest hospital is approx. 10 minutes away from camp. There is a guard house near one camp entrance that is manned from 11pm-6am daily. CCTV cover main access points.

Camp is NOT nut-free. Campers eat in the newly built dining hall at tables by cabin. There is a mix of round tables and cafeteria type long tables. All meals are served family style. There is a salad bar and alternative food options at each meal. Sunday mornings are lazy breakfast--made to order eggs/omelets or pancakes. Several times per week, there will be cookout meals--campers eat at picnic tables and can choose to sit with whoever they want. These meals are served buffet style.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp Agawak is a very high-energy camp--lots of singing, cheering, dancing. Campers of all ages spend time together on a regular basis. Staff are very present, caring, and eager to spend time with their campers. Agawak campers and staff are passionate about the camp traditions and competitions. The blue and white competition is the cornerstone of the camp program. Agawak is definitely for the more Type-A, competitive, high-energy camper.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Offers bus service from the Northshore suburbs of Chicago and Milwaukee. Campers flying to camp are required to book travel through camp's travel agent and camp will pick campers up.

Campers communicate via letter writing only. No packages of any kind are allowed and will be returned to sender.

Photos are uploaded daily and camp utilizes facial recognition.

Deposits are refundable up until December 1st with a $500 fee. After December 1 there are no tuition refunds.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Agawak is a primer all-girls camp located on 200 acres in Minocqua, Wisconsin. Their dynamic, fun and fast-paced program is a mix of elective activity periods and special events. Top of the line facilities, expansive waterfront, quality instruction, and a true family feeling make Agawak the ideal choice for families seeking an elevated, yet traditional camp experience. Agawak is definitely for the more Type-A, competitive, high-energy camper.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - The facilities are spotless and impeccably manicured.
  - The waterfront and lake is second to none--best in the midwest by far.
  - Camp is definitely for the more type-A camper, but campers do not have to be the best athlete, dancer, etc.
  - Tons of activities and quality instruction.
  - Camp is expansive, but feels intimate.
  - The new dining hall and dance studio are great additions.
  - Agawak is a great option for east coast families that want to do something different.



#### Lowlights

  - nothing noteworthy



#### Similar Camps

  - Chippewa Ranch Camp
  - Marimeta
  - Lake of the Woods
  - Could give with any all-girls, elective camp that is highly spirited with great facilities.
