title: Timber Lake West — Emily Salant — 9-4-2025
text:
# Timber Lake West



## 1 · Program Snapshot

325 first session; 280 second session. Would like to grow 2nd session. Good number of international kids 2nd session - 10% or so. Under 10 kids stay full season. First session is 28 days and then 2nd is 22 days. A few kids do 6 weeks. 2 week option 2nd session for rising 3 and 4th. Visiting day each session at the 2 week mark. Color war both session. Doing the same things both sessions. Do trips and intercamps.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Justin and Cassie. 35% ex-campers as staff; mostly international otherwise. Saw some diversity. Will get a lot of rising 6th or 7th.



## 3 · Living Quarters

8-10 kids in a cabin - 4/5 counselors. Cabins connected. 2 per age group. Laundry once a week - individual bag. Unpack up through 6th grade. Switch bunks each year.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

2 heated pools. Instructional swim to fifth grade. Lake is motorized. Waterski and banana boat. Private lake. One piece bathing suit only. Offer fishing\!\!\!\! Towel service everywhere.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 activities a day; 2 AM - 3 PM. Structured program with 2 electives but choosing within. Instruction at every activity - even electives. Only electronic elective is Dj. Some kids do bring roller skates but there is no instruction; it is for hockey.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Color war both session. Do trips and intercamps. Full on camper up to rising 11th - do overnight to Hershey and dorney - included in price\! And then counselor at rising 12. One dance a session.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

No religion. Traditional Friday night dinner. 9 kids who eat kosher, but do serve pork. Can do all allergies. All camp eats together; boys one side, girls on other. Buffet style. Nut free. Sit with division - but open seating. Fruit out all day. 3 pm snack. Health center air conditioned. 5 nurses and Dr. Only pediatricians and ER docs.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Whole camp gathers before breakfast and dinner. Staff always in TLW shirt. Music playing in camp\!\! Mix of evening activities - all camp. Are bringing in evening activities.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bus from Paramus and LI. Most kids bus. Chaperoned flight from FL. New camper day before camp. No extra charge for anything but baggage and canteen money for trips. Even private instruction is included. 1 call each session. One way email. Post 2x a day on social media. Lots of pics.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Timber Lake West runs two sessions (28 days first session, 22 days second session), with a structured program of 5 activities a day (2 AM - 3 PM) and 2 electives, with instruction at every activity. Most kids bus (with a chaperoned flight from FL), and there is a visiting day each session at the 2 week mark. The program includes heated pools, a motorized private lake with waterski and banana boat, trips and intercamps, and full on camper up to rising 11th do an overnight to Hershey and dorney included in price. Ideal for families seeking a traditional, nut-free, structured camp with lots of photos, one dance a session, mix of evening activities, and all-camp gatherings before breakfast and dinner.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Instruction at every activity - even electives
  - No extra charge for anything but baggage and canteen money for trips; even private instruction is included
  - 2 heated pools; instructional swim to fifth grade
  - Lake is motorized; waterski and banana boat on a private lake
  - Towel service everywhere
  - Whole camp gathers before breakfast and dinner; all camp eats together
  - Health center air conditioned; 5 nurses and Dr.; only pediatricians and ER docs
  - Post 2x a day on social media; lots of pics
  - Staff always in TLW shirt; music playing in camp\!\!



#### Lowlights

  - Would like to grow 2nd session
  - One piece bathing suit only
  - Only electronic elective is Dj
  - Some kids do bring roller skates but there is no instruction; it is for hockey
  - 1 call each session and one way email
  - Do serve pork (while 9 kids eat kosher)



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Pontiac — Michelle — Unknown Date
text:
# Pontiac



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Traditional all boys camp located in the Berkshires. Since 1916. Full summer at $16,450. 5 weeks at $13,350 and 4 weeks at $11,100.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

“Not a big Long Island contingent crowd. NYC, Westchester, NJ, CT, Florida, small TX. 300 total campers with a very high retention rate. Socialize with girls from Romaca, Belvoir Terrace and Bryn Mawr.” 3 male and 1 female with 8 kids per bunk.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Showers not located in bunks. Toilets located in bunks. 3 shower houses. Someone can always see in; done with intention all around camp so that kids are never alone with an adult.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

No pool. Lakefront impressive with inflatables, canoes, kayaks, stand up paddles, knee boards, water skiing, wakeboarding, tubing, sailing (on a separate side.). Share lake with Watitoh. Swim instruction mandatory for kids 12 and under.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

“Kids need structure but monotonous days are bad.” Highly instructive camp- all about teaching and campers making progress. “Teaching not entertaining.” More choice as kids get older. 5th grade and below get 2 choice periods.



## 6 · Program Highlights

“Intercamp competition in everything; even chess.” Activities: wood, drawing, painting, ceramics, robotics, hiking, nature, fitness for 12 and up, fencing, basketball, archery, roller hockey, fishing, lacrosse, pickleball, ropes course, soccer, flag football, outdoor cooking, golf (5 holes, driving range, sand trap. Go off grounds for more.) tennis. 2 color wars bc competition for younger kids is not promoted. Under 12 is Olympics so it’s more countries not just 2 teams and more chances to medal. 12 and up is traditional grey v red. Private lessons in sports offered at no extra cost. Fields drain- under grass is gravel. Clay tennis courts.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Cafeteria style. Staggered seating. Built to be age appropriate. Half the camp at once. They feed younger kids earlier as is their natural instinct. Guard at gate.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Big brother program. Not a high gloss clientele. Everything has purpose and value. “A boy with obnoxious parents would not do well here.” “Entitled” and “Nasty” families are not welcomed. Very in tune with growth and child development. Very focused on how kids treat each other. Kindness.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Pictures posted. Don’t require any purchases for camp clothing. Private lessons in sports offered at no extra cost. Travel logistics and tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Traditional all boys camp in the Berkshires that is highly instructive—“teaching not entertaining”—with strong structure and increasing choice as boys get older. Lakefront is impressive (no pool) with extensive aquatics and mandatory swim instruction for kids 12 and under. Culture prioritizes kindness and growth, with a big brother program and a not high gloss clientele; “entitled” and “nasty” families are not welcomed. Sessions include full summer ($16,450), 5 weeks ($13,350), or 4 weeks ($11,100), serving about 300 campers with very high retention.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Highly instructive camp—teaching not entertaining.
  - Impressive lakefront with extensive aquatics (sailing, water skiing, wakeboarding, tubing).
  - Intercamp competition in everything; even chess.
  - Two color wars; Olympics for under 12, traditional grey v red for 12 and up.
  - Private lessons in sports offered at no extra cost.
  - Fields drain—under grass is gravel; clay tennis courts.
  - Big brother program.
  - Guard at gate.



#### Lowlights

  - Showers not located in bunks (3 shower houses).
  - No pool.
  - Share lake with Watitoh.
  - Not a high gloss clientele (may not fit some families).



#### Similar Camps

  - Winadu
  - Cedar
  - Brant Lake



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Traditional all boys camp located in the Berkshires.  Full summer at $16,450.  5 weeks at $13,350 and 4 weeks at $11,100.  Guard at gate.  Since 1916.      "Not a big Long Island contingent crowd.

title: Emma Kaufmann — Ashley — Unknown Date
text:
# Emma Kaufmann



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Emma Kaufmann Camp, established in 1908, is a co-ed Jewish camp located 70 miles south of Pittsburgh, in Morgantown, West Virginia. It is a program of the Jewish Community Center of Greater Pittsburgh. The camp serves rising 2nd-11th graders and offers a wide variety of session lengths--from 1 to 7 weeks--priced $1,795 to $11,395, with additional discounts for members of the Pittsburgh JCC. The camp is ACA accredited and accepts the One Happy Camper grant. Summer 2025 hosted approximately 375 campers at any given time--719 unique campers throughout the summer. Approximately 10% stay all summer. Their oldest campers are required to stay all 7 weeks.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers hail mostly from Pittsburgh, Philadelphia, Cleveland, Columbus, and Cincinnati. Director (Aaron) has been at camp for 7 years and is very outgoing, friendly, and passionate about camp. He knows campers and staff by name and was very tuned in to what was going on at all times throughout camp.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Campers live by village--girls and boys cabins of the same age group are next to each other. There are approx. 8-12 campers per cabin with 2 counselors in each cabin. All cabins have showers and toilets inside. There was a new girls building built in 2025 (large building with multiple cabin groups). The oldest campers (Teen Village) live in raised tents with flaps that are opened during the day. Campers are bunked by gender identity. Laundry is sent out 1x in a 3 week session and 2x in a 3.5 week session.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

EKC's lakefront area is for boating and water sports only. All swimming is done in their pool. Lake is approx. 7 minute walk down a path from main camp, or 2 minute walk down very steep stairs. It is not a private lake--there are houses very close to camp. Their boating area is tucked away from the main part of the lake. Overall, small but nice lakefront area. EKC's pool is large, heated, and in the center of camp. Their pool supports daily swim instruction and free swim. It also has a spray ground/zero depth area.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers participate in activities with their cabin groups. They have one elective period every other day. Activities are approximately 50 minutes. There is rest hour after lunch, a snack period in the afternoon, and an evening snack. Lights out is around 11pm each day.



## 6 · Program Highlights

EKC observes Shabbat on Friday nights with a service and traditional dinner. They also have Saturday morning services and Havdalah on Saturday evening. They have color war one time per 3.5 week session.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The health center has nurses and a new visiting doctor each week. Camp utilizes urgent care, as it is closest to camp (approx. 10 min). The children's hospital is approx. 25 minutes away. There is a 24-hour gated, manned entrance. Visitors need to be announced and are not let in without prior authorization. The guard on duty checks IDs. All guests need to sign a code of contact before entering camp grounds. There are emergency buttons in several places around camp--when activated, the silent alarm will notify the local 911 that there is an emergency. There are comprehensive emergency procedures in place that are not shared with individuals outside of the organization. The kosher kitchen is able to handle all special diets. Camp is NOT nut free. The dining hall has air conditioning. Dining hall, infirmary, and camp office have A/C.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp feels small and tight-knit. Campers appeared happy and having fun. The dining hall was high energy during their afternoon snack time--music playing, campers dancing on chairs, etc. There is not a heavy emphasis placed on athletics or activity instruction. All campers are welcome and included in camp activities. Seems like most kids would do well here--could recommend to anyone looking for a down-to-earth, Jewish overnight camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

There is no transportation to and from camp--all campers are dropped off and picked up by their parents. Campers are allowed 1 package and unlimited flat envelopes, any size. Parents can use one-way email through camp minder--all emails are included in tuition (parents do not need to "buy tokens"). Camp store purchases are all included in tuition. Camp is proud of their "all-inclusive" tuition model--there is nothing extra to pay for. They have a generous refund policy.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Emma Kaufmann Camp is a smaller, tight-knit, co-ed, Jewish overnight camp located 70 miles south of Pittsburgh, in Morgantown, WV. EKC is a program of the Jewish Community Center of Greater Pittsburgh. Their large option of session and program lengths offer a more flexible camping experience for families, and all campers are welcome, included, and made to feel successful--with a full medical team, gated and manned security, and allergy-friendly dining hall. Ideal for families looking for a down to earth, traditional, Jewish camping experience, with all of the activities a camper could want.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - EKC was clean and kept up very nicely.
  - Cabins all have toilets and showers, and dining hall, infirmary, and camp office have A/C.
  - Overall, it is a nice, no-frills camp.
  - Camp is inclusive and willing to work with campers with all types of needs.
  - Campers seemed happy to be there.
  - The dining hall was high-energy and fun--lots of music and stuff going on.



#### Lowlights

  - During my 4 hour visit, I did not observe campers to be doing many activities.
  - Seemed low energy around camp.
  - Lake was underwhelming.
  - Their registration is VERY competitive--they fill up most age groups within minutes of registration opening.



#### Similar Camps

  - Camp Interlaken
  - Beber Camp
  - Camp Wise
  - Camp Chi
  - Other URJ or JCC camps



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Camp Canadensis — Jennifer — Unknown Date
text:
# Camp Canadensis



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Canadensis is a coed camp in the Poconos with approximately 600 campers. It is in its 85th year in operation. Summer 2025 had approximately 600 campers. They take campers from finishing 1st to finishing 10th grades. They have about an 85-90 percent camper return rate. Campers come primarily from the Northeast. This is a 7 week camp but will allow 4 weeks for first summer only. This year they had 18 campers sign up for the 4 week intro option and 15 of the 18 ended up extending. Camp Canadensis is a traditional 7 week coed camp located in the Poconos that hosts approximately 600 campers per summer. It offers a wide range of activities and is a good fit for a camper looking for an all around camp experience.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Brian Krug gave us the tour and has been the camp director at Canadensis for the past 20 years. They have about an 85-90 percent camper return rate. Campers come primarily from the Northeast.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Average bunk size is 14 campers with 4 staff, mostly use single beds but will put in bunk beds if needed. Both pool and lake towels are provided but campers need to bring own shower towels. Bunks all have bathrooms and showers inside, 2 toilets, sinks and showers. Bunks are kept the same year to year. Laundry is done on site and returned the same day.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Canadensis has a large lovely private lake in the center of camp, they have 2 motorboats and about 50-60 campers waterski daily. They also have 2 pools with water slides on campus. Lake is a highlight of camp and big part of programming.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The day has 6 periods plus a free play. Younger campers get 1 elective, middle camp (5th and 6th grade) get 2 electives and senior camp (grades 7 and up) choose 75-90% of their schedule. Electives can be kept the same for whole summer or changed weekly.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Lake is a highlight of camp and big part of programming. Offers a specialty horseback riding program for an additional fee, 12 tennis courts. They just put in a new climbing ropes program where campers self clip in. They have a big arts center with specialty rooms and a 4 week dedicated theater intensive. They also have 2 pools with water slides on campus. Highlights of the camp include the large lake, nice arts and STEM program.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The health center has a doctor on a weekly rotation who is not allowed to leave campus for that week. It is also staffed by 6 rotating RNs. Nearest urgent care is 20 minutes and closest hospital is 25 minutes. Kitchen is nut free. They built a separate outdoor dining pavilion that is covered by a roof for grades 7-10. Staggered seating in the dining hall. They do have a special dietary line staffed by a camp mom and specialty chef.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Canadensis is a very spirited camp and tends to attract sportier, social well rounded campers. Camp is predominantly Jewish and they do a 10 minute Shabbat on Friday nights in the amphitheater. Campers wear white to Shabbat.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bus service from Long Island, Westchester, North Jersey, Cherry Hill, NYC, Philadelphia (Willow Grove). Brian does visit with every new camp family prior to camp. Tours are done as a family and child stays with their parents. Campers get 3 phone calls home for the summer, 2 before visiting day and 1 after. No electronics allowed. Extra costs would be for horseback riding, tennis lessons.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Canadensis is a traditional 7 week coed camp located in the Poconos that hosts approximately 600 campers per summer. The large lovely private lake is a highlight and the camp offers a wide range of activities, with nice arts and STEM alongside strong sports. It tends to attract sportier, social, well rounded campers and is predominantly Jewish with a 10 minute Shabbat on Friday nights where campers wear white. It is a good fit for a camper looking for an all around camp experience.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Highlights of the camp include the large lake, nice arts and STEM program.
  - Large lovely private lake in the center of camp with 2 motorboats; about 50-60 campers waterski daily.
  - Big arts center with specialty rooms and a 4 week dedicated theater intensive.
  - 12 tennis courts and a new climbing ropes program where campers self clip in.
  - On-site laundry is done and returned the same day.
  - Kitchen is nut free with a special dietary line staffed by a camp mom and specialty chef.
  - Strong camper return rate (about 85-90 percent).



#### Lowlights

  - Lowlights- nothing specific but I think it could be camp that would be more challenging to start at an older age.



#### Similar Camps

  - Pine Forest



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Canadensis is a coed camp in the Poconos with approximately 600 campers. It is in its 85th year in operation. Brian Krug who gave us the tour and has been the camp director at Canadensis for the past 20 years.  Summer 2025 had approximately 600 campers . They take campers from finishing 1st to finishing 10th grades. They have about an 85-90 percent camper return rate. Campers come primarily from the Northeast. This is a 7 week camp but will allow 4 weeks for first summer only.  This year they had 18 campers sign up for the 4 week intro option and 15 of the 18 ended up extending.    Average bunk size is 14 campers with 4 staff, mostly use single beds but will put in bunk beds if needed. Both pool and lake towels are provided but campers need to bring own shower towels.  Bunks all have bathrooms and showers inside, 2 toilets, sinks and showers. Bunks are kept the same year to year. Laundry is done on site and returned the same day.      Canadensis has a large lovely private lake in the center of camp, they have 2 motorboats and about 50-60 campers waterski daily.  They also have 2 pools with water slides on campus.     The day has 6 periods plus a free play.  Younger campers get 1 elective, middle camp ( 5th and 6th grade) get 2 electives and senior camp ( grades 7 and up) choose 75-90% of their schedule. Electives can be kept the same for whole summer or changed weekly   Lake is a highlight of camp and big part of programming.  Offers a specialty horseback riding program for an additional fee, 12 tennis courts. They just put in a new climbing ropes program where campers self clip in. They have a big arts center with specialty rooms and a 4 week dedicated theater intensive      The health center has a doctor on a weekly rotation whom is not allowed to leave campus for that week. It is also staffed by 6 rotating RNS. Nearest urgent care is 20 minutes and closest hospital is 25 minutes .Kitchen is nut free.  They built a separate outdoor dining pavillion that is covered by a roof for grades 7-10.  Staggered seating in the dining hall. They do have a special dietary line staffed by a camp mom  and specialty chef     Canadensis is a very spirited camp and tends to attract sportier, social well rounded campers. Camp is predominantly Jewish and they do a 10 minute Shabbat on Friday nights in the amphitheater. Campers wear white to Shabbat     Bus service from Long Island, Westchester, North jersey, Cherry Hill, NYC, Philadelphia ( Willow Grove).  Brian does visit with every new camp family prior to camp.  Tours are done as a family and child stays with their parents. Campers get 3 phone calls home for the summer, 2 before visiting day and 1 after.  No electronics allowed.  Extra costs would be for horseback riding, tennis lessons  Camp Canadensis is a traditional 7 week coed camp located in the Poconos that hosts approximately 600 campers per summer. It offers a wide range of activities and is a good fit for a camper looking for an all around camp experience.  "Highlights of the camp include the large lake, nice arts and STEM program.

Lowlights- nothing specific but I think it could be camp that would be more challenging to start at an older age."      Other 7 week traditional camps in the Poconos - maybe Pine Forest ( although PF more rustic)

title: Kennybrook — Emily Salant — 9-4-2025
text:
# Kennybrook



## 1 · Program Snapshot

130 kids stay full summer. 70 two week campers went home today. Day 1 - 310 kids here. July 22 - 240 will be here (60 go home after 2 week). July 22 new kids come in. Most people like half session. Try to book 2 weekers separately.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Andrew Grossman - assistant director. Long Island, NYC, NJ, westChester. Some Florida. 30 % of campers and staff are international. Do take finishing 1st. Rising 9th grade are campers. Then waiter/waitress summer- rising 10th. Counselor Assistant - rising 11th. All the group leaders are educators. 85% return rate. New campers at any age.



## 3 · Living Quarters

8-10 in a bunk and 2 per grade. Unpacking up to 8th grade.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Boys and girls swim differently. Waterskiing and banana boating about 3 minutes away. Pool - instruction up to 6th grade. Offer fishing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 periods - 2 elective rest assigned. Combo of instruction and recreational. Every area does have a curriculum.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Pickleball, Gaga, archery. New music electives including rock band. Robotics is new elective. Ceramics. Offer cooking. Rock climbing and a zip line. Fitness and dance. Olympics - first session, 4 teams. Color war - 2nd session 2 days. Offer private tennis, basketball, baseball.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

3 nurses and Dr on camp at all times. Canteen - afternoon snack -chips etc, one day a week is candy. Older than 6th grade come at night. All of Camp eats at once. Whole camp eats at once. Sit by bunk.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Doesnt seem very buttoned up at all.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Mix of busses and parent drop off. Only extra really other than the senior trip. Parent communication and tech policies: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kennybrook runs a combo of instruction and recreational programming across 6 periods (2 elective, the rest assigned), and every area does have a curriculum. Day 1 - 310 kids here, with 130 kids staying full summer; most people like half session, and July 22 new kids come in (July 22 - 240 will be here; 60 go home after 2 week). Activities span waterskiing and banana boating about 3 minutes away, rock climbing and a zip line, pickleball, gaga, archery, ceramics, fitness and dance, new music electives including rock band, and a robotics elective. All the group leaders are educators, 3 nurses and a Dr are on camp at all times, and the return rate is 85%.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All the group leaders are educators
  - 30 % of campers and staff are international
  - 85% return rate
  - Waterskiing and banana boating about 3 minutes away
  - New music electives including rock band
  - Robotics is new elective
  - Every area does have a curriculum
  - Combo of instruction and recreational



#### Lowlights

  - Doesnt seem very buttoned up at all.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Chipinaw - Silver Lake — Emily Salant — 9-4-2025
text:
# Chipinaw / Silver Lake



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Two-camp program: Chip 250 g - 250 b; Silver Lake 180 b / 180 g. Shares ATVs, horseback riding, gymnastics, circus and volleyball.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

30-40 kids in division. Coaches all in sneakers and socks. Group leaders carry iPads. All staff wears shirts.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Towel service for pool and lake. All new docks on lake.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

David schedule - 2/2/2 by division: 2 water, 2 team sports, elective chosen for day before. Not elective program. 1 week rotation. Electives by division; as you get older more electives. Cap programs at 8. Evening activities. 14 leagues.



## 6 · Program Highlights

2 full golf simulators - golf range. ATV / spin. Colorwar for Silver Lake 2 days first session. 4 day College Bowl. Animal Kingdom - nature. New weight room etc. Horse back riding - all included basic program. Rock shop.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Family style / kosher. Sit by cabin. Jack and Jill dining room - girls / boys. Waiters and waitresses 10th and 11th. 4 camp moms. E\&R laundry.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Friday night service - all white. Not for learning - more for playing.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Send welcome letter after 4 days. Zoom calls for phone calls. Group leaders carry iPads.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Chipinaw / Silver Lake is a two-camp program serving Chip 250 g - 250 b and Silver Lake 180 b / 180 g. The daily David schedule runs 2/2/2 by division (2 water, 2 team sports, and an elective chosen the day before) on a 1-week rotation; it is not an elective program, though electives by division increase as you get older and programs cap at 8. Highlights include shared ATVs, horseback riding (all included basic program), gymnastics, circus, volleyball, 2 full golf simulators with a golf range, ATV/spin, 14 leagues, Colorwar for Silver Lake (2 days first session), a 4-day College Bowl, Animal Kingdom (nature), a new weight room, and a Rock Shop, plus evening activities. Dining is family style/kosher with waiters/waitresses (10th and 11th) and sitting by cabin; there is towel service for pool and lake and all new docks on the lake, E\&R laundry, 4 camp moms, Friday night service in all white, and communication via a welcome letter after 4 days and Zoom calls for phone calls.

#### Lowlights

Pool. Provide pool towels.

Majors / minors - 6 periods; pick when they get there. Majors are by level. 15 min between each period. Lazy day - one each session. All camp evening activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Radio is a big program. Magic is a new program. DJing and recording. Circus / visual arts - 15 circus staff from Club Med most popular activity. Basketball head sport - Olympic coach. English and western dressage. Inter camps. Programming and coding game design. Pioneering / mountain bikes etc. More pickle ball courts 2026.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

3 nurses - share doc with FW Festival. Nut free. All eat together - sit by bunk. Younger kids downstairs. Dinner can sit anywhere. Laundry is every day. Candy in canteen. Vending machines - store with French Woods stuff.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Falcon is mascot. Inter camps. This is definitely for the older kids. A lot of indoor activities. Not too much going on.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

5 visiting days - before change over. Kids have phones here. Vending machines - store with French Woods stuff. Candy in canteen.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

FW Sports and Arts serves 200 kids across 5 2 week sessions with majors / minors over 6 periods that you pick when you get there. Entering 6th - rising seniors can dive into radio (a big program), magic (a new program), DJing and recording, circus / visual arts with 15 circus staff from Club Med, and a head sport in basketball with an Olympic coach. Facilities include a pool (towels provided), A/C and WiFi in every bunk, nut free dining where all eat together and sit by bunk, and 3 nurses who share a doc with FW Festival. title: Independent Lake — Emily Salant — 9-4-2025
text:
# Independent Lake



## 1 · Program Snapshot

3 primes - majors. 1st session - 3 weeks starts June 22; 2nd - 3 weeks; 3rd - 2 weeks. 500 + kids. Rookie week is week 9. 35% mid session. 2 yrs younger per kid than 10 years ago. Performance day the last Saturday of each session / go home. Sunday new new session. Activities Co - ed. Will work with you on dates.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lots of kids around the age 7/8/9th grade. Mixed Private and public school. 15-18% international. 2 yrs younger per kid than 10 years ago. Jeff knows everyone. Matt Stone's daughter.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Keep bunks same each year. Bunks 10-12 kids; 2-4 counselors depending on age. Bunks are in a line. Porches. Share backroom. Crowded bunks. Lakeside 8 and up. Elkview is 1-7 grade. Trolley comes up to the lower camps. New work out room.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Shares lake with Westmont. Fishing is big now.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Primes they pick at camp. 3 primes. 2 dailies. Shift around things to make every prime work. Check it out day first day of camp / try out day. Activities Co - ed. Elk view experience - some activities with bunk 1 per day. Take attendance after every switch. 3 week session - 2 trip days. 2 week 1 trip. Evening activities - upper camp has one lower camps. Coed.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Circus. Flying trapeze. Intense program. Kids go to cirdesoli. Indoor circus. Gymnastics program. Theater - musical for older and younger each session. Digital arts building was redone 2024. Magic. Watching kids film project. Skate park is very big. Redid canteen - new porch video games. Redoing indoor basketball court. New work out room. 9 horses - pre sign up. Riding lessons every day 61 kids in this session. Nature program with chicks. Kids love djing and recording. Have own instruments but kids can bring stuff.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining - 2 seatings upper / lower; sit with bunk. Screens in dining hall - everyone engaged during meals. All buffet - all dietary / no nuts. 2 doctors on camp.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Jeff knows everyone. Kids walk around with Backpacks. Mixed Private and public school. Redid canteen - new porch video games.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Parents drop off. Will work with you on dates. Sunday new new session. Performance day the last Saturday of each session / go home. 1 x a week phone calls. First time camper picnic beginning of June. Kids bringing towels. Have own instruments but kids can bring stuff.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Independent Lake is a co-ed camp with 500+ kids and a majors-based program with 3 primes and 2 dailies. Sessions include a 3-week first session starting June 22, a second 3-week session, and a 2-week third session, with Rookie week in week 9 and performance day the last Saturday of each session before going home. Highlights include an intense circus with flying trapeze and indoor circus, theater, digital arts (redone 2024), a very big skate park, horses with daily riding lessons, magic, DJing/recording, and more. Parents can expect weekly phone calls, buffet dining with all dietary needs accommodated (no nuts), and two doctors on camp.

title: Berkshire Lake Arts — Natasha — 9-4-2025
text:
# Berkshire Lake Arts



## 1 · Program Snapshot

BLA is part of Camp Group. It’s down the road from Danbee; there is a short cut thru on their property to get to Danbee. The BLA girls do not overlap with Danbee girls—they tried and it was awkward; they go 2x a session for a pool party and tubing. 2025 is the 2nd summer the camp has run. Sessions: 2 weeks, 1 week, 2 weeks—kids have to leave in between sessions if staying more than one. 2026-$4830 -2 weeks, $2850 – 1 week. 2025- 28 campers 1st, 43 campers 2nd, 24 campers 3rd. Max number per session is 80. Campers range from 7-13 years old.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Natasha, Risa, Lana and Michelle B toured Berkshire Lake Arts Camp on 7/21/2025. Toured by Mandy Miller—used to be the Director of a Girl Scout camp—7 years prior to BLA. All staff are either working in their area of expertise or studying it in school; we met a dance instructor who was a former Rockette. Campers are from the greater NY area, Boston, DC, Florida. Skill level ranges from beginner to intermediate.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The property used to be a Catholic camp. The old bunks have been converted to activity spaces and new bunks have been built for housing. The new bunks are two story buildings and beautiful; look like log cabins. 8-10 girls /3 counselor per bunk, showers and bathrooms per floor, wooden bunk beds and cubby storage.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

No pool – swim in the lake – free time before dinner, many swim then. Waterfront activities include Swimming, Fishing, Paddling, Tubing. They go 2x a session for a pool party and tubing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 Periods a day – fully elective. Fine Arts; Jewelry-Making & Metalworking; Digital Arts; Printmaking; Traditional Camp Arts & Crafts; Ballet; Tap; Hip Hop; Jazz; Contemporary; Lyrical; Yoga; Acting Fundamentals; Vocal Coaching; Acting Scenework; Musical Theater Dance; Swimming; Fishing; Paddling; Tubing; Gaga Ball; Archery; Pickleball; Volleyball; Basketball; Outdoor Living Skills.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Evening Activities—try to keep classic camp: Campfires, Masked Singer, Casino Night, Spa Night, Movie Night, Square Dancing. For the 2 week session—2nd week they work on a cabaret piece and then performance. Trips in the 2 week session – Performing Arts or Arts based.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

RN on staff and Danbee Doctor up the road. Dining Hall can handle all allergies. Nut aware - peanut butter okay here. Sit by bunk – buffet style.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

No Socials. Try to keep classic camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Most are dropped off by parents 99%—but do have a van from NYC; about 10 girls used 2025. Daily Pictures – thru Campanion. Given one camp shirt – not required to buy anything. No canteen. They offer privates for an extra cost – i.e., work on ballet point – transportation is also extra.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Berkshire Lake Arts Camp is part of Camp Group, down the road from Danbee, with sessions of 1 or 2 weeks for campers ages 7–13 (max 80 per session); kids have to leave in between sessions if staying more than one. The program is 6 periods a day—fully elective—across arts, dance, camp sports, and outdoor living skills, with trips in the 2 week session and a cabaret piece and performance in the 2nd week. New two story bunks that look like log cabins and lake swimming (no pool) support a classic camp feel, with evening Campfires, Masked Singer, Casino Night, Spa Night, Movie Night, and Square Dancing. Dining Hall can handle all allergies (nut aware—peanut butter okay here), RN on staff and the Danbee Doctor up the road, daily pictures thru Campanion, and straightforward logistics (mostly parent drop-off with a NYC van option); 2026 is $4830 for 2 weeks and $2850 for 1 week.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - BLA is part of Camp Group; down the road from Danbee.
  - 6 Periods a day – fully elective.
  - All staff are either working in their area of expertise or studying it in school; met a dance instructor who was a former Rockette.
  - New two story bunks that look like log cabins; showers and bathrooms per floor.
  - Trips in the 2 week session – Performing Arts or Arts based.
  - For the 2 week session—the 2nd week they work on a cabaret piece and then performance.
  - Dining Hall can handle all allergies; nut aware.
  - RN on staff and Danbee Doctor up the road.
  - Daily Pictures – thru Campanion.
  - Most are dropped off by parents; van from NYC available.
  - Max number per session is 80.



#### Lowlights

  - Kids have to leave in between sessions if staying more than one.
  - No pool – swim in the lake.
  - No Socials.
  - No canteen.
  - Privates for an extra cost – transportation is also extra.
  - 2025 is the 2nd summer the camp has run.
  - 2025- 28 campers 1st, 43 campers 2nd, 24 campers 3rd.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Tranquillity — Natasha — 9-4-2025
text:
# Tranquillity



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Tranquility Camp. Camp was founded in 1819, 107 years old. Sessions are 4 to 7 weeks. They currently have 125 campers; three-quarter stay for full session (75% of campers stay full). 2025 is a year with small enrollment - lots of kids come on scholarship - use One Happy Camper. There are three divisions: juniors, inters, seniors. Tranquility is a privately owned camp and there is a board.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Natasha, Risa and Michelle B visited Tranquility Camp on 7/20/2025. We were toured by Director Stacy Budkofsky, who went to camp from ages 6 to 21. She was the girls head, on the board and now director. Stacy worked 19 years at the JCC in Tenafly b/f camp. 85% Jewish. This year, there are 4 Israeli counselors—weren’t sure they’re going to make it after October 7, but they did. It’s a huge legacy camp.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Kids clean the bunks—mop, clean toilets, showers—everything—only professionally cleaned bf camp. We saw a dirty mop in a bucket in one of the bunks. The TY house is also one of the oldest buildings on camp—used to be the old dining hall. It’s now a hangout spot with books; her goal is to turn it into staff housing. There’s a building that’s called the barn. It’s the oldest building on camp; it’s the logo for their camp T-shirts.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They have a lake—no motorsports—and two pools (one deep, one shallow). Two docks at the lake: one for fishing, one for boating—no motor sports. Fourth is usually water.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule - They travel by bunk: breakfast; cleanup; first period / second period; everyone gathers at the blacktop followed by lunch / rest hour; third period; fourth period (fourth is usually water); shower; dinner; Free Play—they can pick one activity; they can’t be moving from activity to activity; Night activity. Monday night is club night (Israeli dancing, Mahjong lessons, camp-wide challah making). Tuesday nights and Wednesday nights are game nights (i.e., bingo). Thursday nights are talent shows or sing contests by bunk. Friday night—services outside or in the multipurpose room if it’s raining. Every Saturday there’s a new show. Sunday is a fun day—They had two dances that day and a color run the day we visited.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Popular activities: Pickleball, Soccer, Beach volleyball, Challenge course, Zumba. When we first got to camp, we saw mini golf, old school swings, gaga. There is a farm with cows and chickens—just use the farm for hayrides—no activities there. They do have a Olympics.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

No security greeted us—just walked right on. They have no true security on camp. It’s just two men—one guy (Justin) lives and grew up there. He maintains the farm and the other "security" is their handyman. Justin has a sick sense of what is going on at camp at all times. No gate—and on a road (not private—runs thru the middle of the camp—but very quiet). Two RNs at all the times; telehealth; the RNs are qualified to test for strep, Covid and flu. Doctor 6 miles away at urgent care; hospital in Albany 15 miles. Air Conditioning in the infirmary and the office. Beautiful dining hall—an alumni project in 1970—with a working fireplace and a piano; there’s lots of singing on Saturdays. They can handle all gluten-free, dairy free, celiac, but they can’t handle airborne allergies, airborne nuts. They do serve peanut butter and jelly at lunch. They are kosher, but not supervised—separate silverware, plates—not Glatt Kosher. Waiters (14yo) serve family style, and the oldest girls are the bread-and-butter girls. They all love it having this responsibility.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

TC is a Jewish camp with a Friday night Service—the boys wear suits and ties, girls wear white dresses. Do the Motze and prayer after every meal. Services are about 45 minutes—led by the same camper the last 7 years—in both Hebrew and English. Everything is kid powered—that’s their Camp motto. Lots of singing on Saturdays. It’s a huge legacy camp. While the kids all seemed happy, it felt very run down and “loose”. VERY bare bones.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

One bus from Queens or campers are dropped off by car. They have a few from Ohio, Florida, and Connecticut. No trips. Pictures every day with companion and Campminder. No phone calls, snail mail only unless it’s your birthday. Some kids have bar mitzvah lessons or therapist appointment appointments on Zoom. One Visiting Day in the 2nd session. No canteen, but might change that. Tranquility $8,650 for full summer. 75% of campers stay full. Sessions are 4 to 7 weeks. 2025 is a year with small enrollment—lots of kids come on scholarship—use One Happy Camper.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

TC is a Jewish camp with a Friday night Service—the boys wear suits and ties, girls wear white dresses. Sessions are 4 to 7 weeks; Tranquility $8,650 for full summer, and 75% of campers stay full. They have a lake—no motorsports—and two pools, a beautiful dining hall with lots of singing on Saturdays, and popular activities like pickleball, soccer, beach volleyball, challenge course, and Zumba. While the kids all seemed happy, it felt very run down and “loose”—very bare bones.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Jewish traditions (Friday night services; Motze and prayer after every meal; services in Hebrew and English).
  - Beautiful dining hall (alumni project in 1970) with lots of singing on Saturdays.
  - Popular activities and challenge course; lake and two pools (two docks; boating and fishing).
  - Club night, game nights, talent/sing contests; weekly Saturday show; Sunday fun day.
  - Everything is kid powered; campers take on responsibilities (waiters; bread-and-butter girls).
  - Legacy camp with strong alumni; director with deep history at camp.
  - Scholarships and One Happy Camper support.



#### Lowlights

  - Felt very run down and “loose”; VERY bare bones.
  - No true security; no gate; public road runs through the middle of camp.
  - No security greeted us—just walked right on.
  - Can’t handle airborne allergies (airborne nuts); PB\&J served at lunch.
  - No trips.
  - No canteen (might change).
  - 2025 is a year with small enrollment.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

She was the girls head, on the board and now director. Stacey worked 19 years at the JCC in Tenafly b/f camp. Camp was founded in 1819 , 107 years old. They currently have 125 campers three-quarter stay for full session.
Sessions are 4 to 7 weeks. 2025 is a year with small enrollment - lots of kids come on scholarship - use One Happy Camper.
When we first got to camp, we saw mini golf, old school swings , gaga. No security greeted us - just walked right on. All the kids were on a big field getting ready for rest-hour after lunch.

shower
dinner
Free Play- they can pick one activity they can’t be moving from activity to activity.
Night activity

Monday night is club night. They do things like Israeli dancing, Mahjong lessons, camp-wide challah making
Tuesday nights and Wednesday nights are game nights i.e bingo
Thursday nights are talent shows or sing contests by bunk
Friday night-services outside or in the multipurpose room if it’s raining

Every Saturday there’s a new show.

Sunday is a fun day - They had two dances that day and a color run the day we visited.

Kids clean the bunks- mop, clean toilets, showers - everything - only professionally cleaned bf camp - we saw a dirty mop in a bucket in one of the bunks.
Tranquility is a privately owned camp and there is a board
Two docks at the Lake one for fishing one for boating- no motor sports
Beautiful dining hall. It was an alumni project in 1970 - has a working fireplace and a piano in the dining hall -  there’s lots of singing on Saturdays.They can handle all gluten-free, dairy free, celiac but  they can’t handle airborne allergies, airborne nuts. They do serve peanut butter and jelly at lunch.
They are kosher, but not supervised -separate silverware, plates -not Glatt Kosher
No trips
Pictures every day with companion and Campminder
No phone calls, snail mail only unless it’s your birthday
Air Conditioning in the infirmary on the office
Some kids have bar mitzvah lessons or therapist appointment appointments on zoom
They do have a Olympics
One Visiting Day in the 2nd session
There’s a building that’s called the barn. It’s the oldest building on Camp. It’s the logo for their camp T-shirts.
Two RNs at all the times, telehealth, the RNs are qualified to test for strep, Covid and flu
Doctor 6 miles away at urgent care, hospital in Albany 15 miles
No canteen, but might change that
Waiters (14yo) serve family style, and the oldest girls are the bread-and-butter girls. They all love it having this responsibility
The TY house is also one of the oldest buildings on camp used to be the old dining hall. It’s now a hangout spot with books, her goal is to turn it into staff housing.

Tranquility $8,650 for full summer. 75% of campers stay full.
While the kids all seemed happy, it felt very run down and “loose”.  VERY bare bones.




### Author: Natasha

### Updated: 2025-09-04

title: Micah — Lisa — 9-4-2025
text:
# Micah



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Founded in 2001.
Current capacity: 285 campers (equal boys/girls).
Location: Private lake with no public access.
Sessions offered: Two-week taste (first 2 weeks); First session (3.5 weeks); Second session (3.5 weeks); Full summer option.
Most campers grades 6+ attend full summer.
Grades 3-5: Approximately 1/3 attend full session, increases by grade.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

85-90 staff working directly with children; about 1/3 international (Israel, Ireland, England, Australia, New Zealand).
Director: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Separate boys and girls sides.
Bunks house 7-9 campers with 2-3 counselors.
Air-conditioned facilities include: Theater, Art building, Dining hall.
Cabin bathrooms, laundry, and bunk A/C: Unknown.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Private lake with no public access.
Waterfront includes: Multiple boats, Paddle boards, Canoes/kayaks, Swimming area with inflatable obstacles.
Waterfront activities are among the most popular.
Waterfront bracelet achievement system.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Daily schedule: 6 periods per day.
Two “goal days” (Sun/Thurs), Two “blue days” (Tues/Fri), One “maroon day”.
Activity choice specifics: Unknown.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Most popular activities: Ninja course, Waterfront activities, Basketball, Volleyball.
Special programs: Theater/musical productions; Hiking program (2 vans capacity); Color War (1.5 days first session, 3 days second session).
New construction: Large dome being built; Plans for indoor basketball court; Potential golf simulator.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical team: Head nurse (22-year tenure); Rotating doctors (2-week shifts, all camp parents); Camp therapist/support specialist.
Security: Two armed guards on premises.
Nearest hospital: Bridgeton (basic care), Portland (45 mins, major emergencies).
Dining: Single seating meals; Family-style service; Comprehensive allergy protocols; Multiple alternative options at each meal.
Supervision: Structured supervision ratios in bunks; One counselor always present in bunk.
Medical screening for specific conditions (e.g., diabetes management).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Social relationship policies: Hand-holding permitted; Clear consequences for rule violations.
Gender inclusion: Accommodates non-binary campers; Successfully integrated trans counselors.
Overall culture descriptors beyond the above: Unknown.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation: 5 buses from Boston area; 1 full bus from NY/CT (55 passengers); Chaperoned flights.
Transportation fee: $150 first session, $300 full session.
Communication: One phone call per session; Letters required twice weekly; Photos posted 3 days per week.
Technology policy and other extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Founded in 2001 on a private lake with no public access, Camp Micah serves about 285 campers with equal boys/girls. Sessions include a two-week taste, 3.5-week first or second session, or a full summer option, with most campers grades 6+ attending full summer and grades 3-5 seeing approximately 1/3 attend full session (increases by grade). Daily life features 6 periods per day across “goal,” “blue,” and “maroon” days, with popular activities like the ninja course, waterfront, basketball, volleyball, plus theater/musical productions, a hiking program (2 vans capacity), Color War, and a waterfront bracelet achievement system. Health, safety, and food are emphasized with a head nurse (22-year tenure), rotating parent doctors, a camp therapist/support specialist, two armed guards, single seating family-style meals with comprehensive allergy protocols, and structured supervision ratios.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Private lake with no public access
  - Founded in 2001
  - Current capacity: 285 campers (equal boys/girls)
  - 85-90 staff working directly with children; about 1/3 international (Israel, Ireland, England, Australia, New Zealand)
  - Head nurse (22-year tenure), rotating doctors (2-week shifts, all camp parents), and a camp therapist/support specialist
  - Two armed guards on premises
  - Waterfront includes multiple boats, paddle boards, canoes/kayaks, and a swimming area with inflatable obstacles
  - Waterfront bracelet achievement system
  - Air-conditioned theater, art building, and dining hall
  - Color War (1.5 days first session, 3 days second session)
  - Chaperoned flights; 5 buses from Boston; 1 full bus from NY/CT
  - New construction: large dome being built; plans for indoor basketball court; potential golf simulator



#### Lowlights

  - Only one phone call per session
  - Letters required twice weekly
  - Photos posted 3 days per week
  - Transportation fee: $150 first session, $300 full session
  - Nearest major hospital in Portland (45 mins; Bridgeton for basic care)
  - Hiking program capacity limited to 2 vans
  - Director: Unknown
  - Tech policy: Unknown
  - Bunk amenities (bathrooms, A/C) in cabins: Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Tapawingo — Lisa — 9-4-2025
text:
# Tapawingo



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Traditional Maine girls camp on private pond. Current enrollment: 135 campers, building to cap of 150 campers. 60-65% are 7-week campers (goal is 80%). Age range: Rising 3rd grade through rising 10th grade. 10% international campers (Mexico, Spain).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Staff Structure: 85 total staff members; 65 program staff (excluding kitchen/maintenance); 50% international staff. Family Liaison (Marianne) handles first-year family communication. Strong returning leadership team, working to increase bunk counselor retention. Enrollment Trends: Strong growth in 7-9 year olds. 25% legacy campers. Geographic mix: Strong Maryland/DC presence; California increasing; New York/Connecticut base. $300,000 in campership funds awarded this season. Maximum age for new camper entry: 14 years old.



## 3 · Living Quarters

One shower house for entire camp. Unknown bunk details.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Daily swim instruction mandatory. Waterfront activities: skiing, sailing, swimming, canoeing, paddleboarding. No wake surfing or wakeboarding (occasional exceptions). 2 ski boats (one runs at a time).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 activity periods daily (3 morning, 2 afternoon).



## 6 · Program Highlights

Ropes course: 5 high elements, 3 low elements. Traditional arts programs including ceramics, stained glass (6th grade+), woodworking. Weekly theater performances by age group. Trip Program (Mandatory): Rising 7th: 2-night camping near Sunday River; Rising 8th: 4-day backpacking or canoeing; Rising 9th: 6-day backpacking or canoeing; Rising 10th: Mount Washington overnight + Ogunquit trip.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical team: 2 RNs + camp doctor (Dr. Lisa). Full-time medical staff. 15 minutes from Bridgeton Hospital. Can accommodate: Gluten-free, Vegetarian, Vegan, Most allergies. Allergy shots administered off-site.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Friday night services (non-religious, values-based). Mixed-age dining tables, rotated between lunch/dinner.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation & Logistics: Bus service from Portland; Boston (Logan and Lexington); New York (City and Westchester); West Hartford. Chaperoned flight from DC. Laundry service weekly. Required uniform purchases (\~$150). Extra charges for: Horseback riding ($650/summer); Senior trips ($200-450); Transportation; Baggage shipping. Parent Communication & Tech: No phone calls except birthdays. Photos posted twice weekly (250 photos). Newsletters Tuesdays/Fridays. Required letter writing twice weekly. No electronics/devices policy.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Traditional Maine girls camp on private pond; current enrollment: 135 campers, building to cap of 150 campers. Age range: Rising 3rd grade through rising 10th grade, with 60-65% 7-week campers (goal is 80%) and 10% international campers (Mexico, Spain). Program features 5 activity periods daily (3 morning, 2 afternoon), daily swim instruction, robust waterfront (skiing, sailing, swimming, canoeing, paddleboarding), ropes course, traditional arts, weekly theater, and a mandatory Trip Program by age group. Families will find clear logistics (bus service from Portland, Boston, New York, and West Hartford; chaperoned flight from DC), a no electronics/devices policy with structured parent communication, and full-time medical support 15 minutes from Bridgeton Hospital.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong returning leadership team, working to increase bunk counselor retention.
  - Family Liaison (Marianne) handles first-year family communication.
  - Daily swim instruction mandatory.
  - Ropes course: 5 high elements, 3 low elements.
  - Traditional arts programs including ceramics, stained glass (6th grade+), woodworking.
  - Weekly theater performances by age group.
  - Trip Program (Mandatory) by age: Rising 7th: 2-night camping near Sunday River; Rising 8th: 4-day backpacking or canoeing; Rising 9th: 6-day backpacking or canoeing; Rising 10th: Mount Washington overnight + Ogunquit trip.
  - Robust transportation options (bus service from Portland, Boston, New York, West Hartford; chaperoned flight from DC).
  - $300,000 in campership funds awarded this season.



#### Lowlights

  - One shower house for entire camp.
  - No phone calls except birthdays.
  - No electronics/devices policy.
  - No wake surfing or wakeboarding (occasional exceptions).
  - 2 ski boats (one runs at a time).
  - Working to increase bunk counselor retention.
  - Maximum age for new camper entry: 14 years old.
  - Required uniform purchases (\~$150) and extra charges for horseback riding ($650/summer) and senior trips ($200-450).



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

ARCADIA:

Better than what I expected since it had been a while since I was last there.
Highlights:  Younger leadership, felt more organized, down-to-earth girls, social worker on staff, 50-60% alumni, a Jewish camper asked for Friday night candles and they accommodated her and others joined in.  They are a diverse camp.
Lowlights:  Rustic but for the right family, dragonflies down the hill by the lake were annoying.

title: Med-O-Lark- Monica — Sam — 9-3-25
text:
# Med-O-Lark- Monica



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Size: \~180 campers. Sessions: Multiple two-week sessions throughout the summer. Transportation: Buses from Westchester and Newton; flights into Portland with airport pick-up provided. Return Rate: High.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camper Profile Age Range: Average age 12-13; many join later after attending other camps. Gender Ratio: \~70% girls, 30% boys. Inclusivity: Very warm, artsy, and inclusive culture; high percentage of LGBTQ+ and non-binary campers. International Community: \~25% from Spain, France, China, Japan, Costa Rica. Ideal For: Quirky, artistic children; campers on the spectrum; non-athletic boys; those who may not fit in traditional camp environments. Counselors: Ages 18-24. Director: Sadie (new; prior experience running a gym program). Specialty Staff: Brazilian circus team with 15 years' history at camp.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabin Structure: Campers bunk by age. Staffing Ratio: 4 counselors, 1 junior counselor, and 6 campers per bunk.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Kayaking, waterskiing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Choice-based schedule. 3 majors chosen for each two-week session. 3 electives rotated every four days.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Arts & Creativity: Extensive and serious program—culinary arts ("dacha" with brick oven, from bao buns to cake decorating), visual arts in every medium, music, dance, yoga, pilates, enameling, 3D modeling/printing, programming. Performing Arts: Large circus program (led by long-standing Brazilian staff, 15 years returning). Sports & Outdoors: Tennis, kayaking, waterskiing; horseback riding off premises.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Style: "Pick your own adventure" seating. Food Policies: Nut-free, accommodates all allergies, can cater to kosher. Health/Safety: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very warm, artsy, and inclusive culture. High percentage of LGBTQ+ and non-binary campers. International community: \~25% from Spain, France, China, Japan, Costa Rica.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation: Buses from Westchester and Newton; flights into Portland with airport pick-up provided. Parent Communication: Unknown. Tech Policy: Unknown. Extra Costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Med-O-Lark- Monica is a \~180-camper, choice-based program with multiple two-week sessions and transportation via buses from Westchester and Newton or flights into Portland with airport pick-up provided. The culture is very warm, artsy, and inclusive, with \~70% girls/30% boys, a high percentage of LGBTQ+ and non-binary campers, and \~25% international (Spain, France, China, Japan, Costa Rica). Campers choose 3 majors per session plus rotating electives, with extensive and serious arts (including culinary arts with a brick oven), a large circus program led by long-standing Brazilian staff (15 years), and waterfront options like kayaking and waterskiing. Campers bunk by age with 4 counselors, 1 junior counselor, and 6 campers per bunk; the camp is nut-free, accommodates all allergies, can cater to kosher, and is ideal for quirky, artistic children, campers on the spectrum, non-athletic boys, and those who may not fit in traditional camp environments.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Extensive and serious arts program, including culinary arts ("dacha" with brick oven, from bao buns to cake decorating)
  - Large circus program led by long-standing Brazilian staff (15 years returning)
  - Very warm, artsy, and inclusive culture with a high percentage of LGBTQ+ and non-binary campers
  - Choice-based schedule with 3 majors and rotating electives
  - International community (\~25% from Spain, France, China, Japan, Costa Rica)
  - Nut-free and accommodates all allergies; can cater to kosher



#### Lowlights

  - Director is new (Sadie)
  - Horseback riding off premises



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Camp Wise — Ashley — 9-3-25
text:
# Camp Wise



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Wise, founded in 1907, is a program of the Mandel JCC of Cleveland. The co-ed, Jewish camp serves rising 2nd-10th graders and offers a variety of sessions and programs ranging from 1-week to 7-weeks. Tuition ranges from $1,760 to $14,300 with discounts for members of the Mandel JCC. The camp is ACA-accredited and accepts the One Happy Camper grant and PJ Library grant.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Summer 2025 hosted approximately 270 campers first session and 300 campers second session (including campers on a trip to Europe). Campers come primarily from Ohio (90%--75% Cleveland, with others hailing from Toledo, Akron, Columbus, and Dayton). There are campers from LA and Florida (Jupiter and Palm Beach Gardens). The staff is comprised of 50% former campers/alumni, and the remaining 50% are International (Israel, Ireland, Scotland, UK, Australia). All campers are welcome at Camp Wise. There is a large inclusion program that has grown significantly in the past few years. Campers with ASD, ADHD, etc. can all attend and enjoy an integrated experience. There is a big focus on social skills, friendship, and community. Director (Dan) is a Rabbi, but not super hands-on or high energy. Their assistant directors and Jewish educators mostly run camp. They have a large mental and emotional support team comprised of 3 social workers and 3 camper care specialists.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The youngest campers live in traditional cabins with bathrooms and showers attached. They sleep on wood bunk beds. There are 2 bunk counselors and 2 specialists in each cabin. They are small, but clean. Middle school campers live in tents on a raised surface with soft sides that are open during the day. These are incredibly small, close quarters, hot, and dirty. They need to be renovated. Counselors live in the same room as the campers. Campers sleep in metal bunk beds and facilities are all in shower houses centrally located. Older campers live in yurts in their own village. There is a centrally located shower house with showers and toilets. These are larger, but hot, old and dirty. Campers sleep in single, metal beds. Staff in training live in larger tents. Bathrooms and shared spaces are professionally cleaned on a daily basis. Overall, the living conditions are lackluster at best. They are planning a renovation for 2026-2027, but they have not yet secured the funding. Not sure if the renovations will occur in their desired timeframe.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Camp Wise sits on a man-made lake with a small lakefront area. It is about a 5 minute walk from the main area of camp, down a rocky path. The lake is only used for boating, tubing, knee boarding, and water ski (pulled behind jet skis). They also have stand up paddle boarding and SUP yoga. The lake is sand-bottom and professionally maintained. Campers spend time at the lake during activity times only. They have a pool in the center of camp, but it is small and old. Needs to be updated. Has two small tube slides that campers can use. Swim lessons and free swim are done in the pool. Campers are required to take swim lessons until they are entering 7th grade.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Typical activity days are 4 periods--2x/day cabin activities, 2x/day elective activities. Elective activities are co-ed, cabin activities are mostly single gender. Activity schedules are changed every 5 days. As campers get older, they have more time in elective activities. There is a daily rest period following lunch. Evening programs are mostly done by age-group and are special programs or more time to try activities offered during the day. Camp Wise is a Jewish camp, so Friday nights include a special meal and 45 minute Shabbat services. There is a Shabbat walk that "picks up" each cabin on their way to services. Campers dress up for Shabbat--they can wear anything they want, there is no dress code. Saturday is late wake up, followed by a morning service, and open activity areas in the afternoon. They have Havdallah on Saturday evening to end Shabbat.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities are run by specialists and include basketball, pickle ball, tennis, soccer, yoga, volleyball, mountain biking, lacrosse, baseball. There is outdoor living skills/nature, fitness, swim, lake activities, archery, etc. They have a new, very impressive ropes course and giant swing that has become a popular activity. There is horseback riding at camp, and horses are frequently seen walking through camp. They do not offer any tournaments or intercamp competitions. They are proud of their library that campers can take books from or spend quiet time there reading. Color war (Maccabiah) takes place once a session for approx. 2 days and is a highlight of the summer. There are always 4 teams, but the theme is different each year/session. Siblings are always together on Maccabiah teams. There is a camp play and talent show that they say is very popular with campers. They do skits during meals as well. They take day trips frequently to amusement parks and neighboring cities. Each group is required to do an overnight camping trip. They get more intense as they get older. Bar/Bat Mitzvah tutoring is available and included in tuition. They offer opportunities to complete mitzvah projects and volunteer hours. They offer an Israel trip to rising 11th grade campers. This trip has been a Europe trip the past two years. It is a 4 week trip that is open to all rising 11th graders regardless of their previous participation at Camp Wise.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The health center is staffed by a doctor and nurses. There is air conditioning in the health center. Camp is 5 minutes from a major hospital. Camp can accommodate campers with diabetes. They allow campers to leave "medical food" in the health center to access as needed. There is a gated entrance and 24/7 Sheriff on duty. Visitors must be announced and on a list before entering. Camp Wise is a Kosher facility. Their dining hall is large, but needs some TLC. Campers and staff sit with their cabin groups. Meals are served family style with food on the tables. There is also a salad bar available at each meal. They are a total NUT FREE camp. They have an impressive allergen board that indicates which allergens are present in each aspect of the meal. There is a separate window for "allergen-free" food. They can accommodate vegetarian, vegan, gluten free and dairy free diets. They are willing to work with families to make sure their camper is satisfied at camp. Their kitchen is run by a catering company, but food seemed just ok when I was there. They say prayers before and after every meal. Friday nights, they have a special chicken, Shabbat meal with challah. Campers are allowed to sit wherever they want and are encouraged to sit with their siblings or cousins. Saturday is also free seating at meals.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Generally, camp felt a little low energy. The campers all seemed happy and busy at activities. It is a smaller camp, and it felt small. Staff wear lanyards to identify them as staff, and they seemed to be present at every area. Staff was observed to be on their phones A LOT throughout my visit. They do not place a heavy emphasis on competition or athletics, campers looked to be having fun but there was no real instruction taking place. The dining hall had the most energy--skits and songs were being performed during lunch. A type-A camper would not do well at Camp Wise. The quirkier, more reserved camper could thrive in this environment.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Offers bus service from Cleveland and airport pick up as needed. Communication with parents is limited to letters. Photos are posted in CampMinder daily with facial recognition. Laundry is sent 2x/session in a camp-provided colored bag. There is a $150 registration fee (non-refundable) due with application. All tuition and the security fee is refundable for any reason before close of business on 12/31/2025. From 1/1/2026-3/1/2026, there is a $500 processing fee taken. After 3/1/2026, all tuition and fees are non-refundable. There is a security fee of $180 added to each camper’s tuition. This is in addition to the listed tuition and fees on their website.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Wise is a smaller sized, co-ed, Jewish overnight camp through the JCC of Cleveland. Their large option of session and program lengths offer a more flexible camping experience for families. All campers are welcome, included, and made to feel successful--with a full medical team, gated and manned security, and allergy-friendly dining hall. Ideal for families looking for a no-frills, traditional, Jewish camping experience, with all of the activities a camper could want.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong Jewish programming
  - traditional Jewish camp feel
  - Their commitment to safety in the dining hall with regard to allergies was impressive.



#### Lowlights

  - Overall, Camp Wise left a lot to be desired.
  - Their facilities are run down and need a LOT of TLC.
  - They claim to be renovating next year, but I am skeptical.
  - Almost all of their campers are from Ohio, with the majority being from their JCC community.
  - The lake and pool are both eh.
  - Their director lacked all enthusiasm, and quite frankly, seemed miserable at camp.
  - Staff was observed to be on their phones A LOT throughout my visit.
  - There was no real instruction taking place.
  - Overall, the living conditions are lackluster at best.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Wyonegonic — Shari — 9-3-25
text:
# Wyonegonic



## 1 · Program Snapshot

All-girls camp ages 8-15 (3rd-10th grade). 190 total campers per session. Three units: Junior (8-10), Intermediate (11-12), Senior (13-15). 51% of campers stay full 6-week season. 58 campers have siblings at brother camp (Winona). 25% of campers are children of alumnae.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp Wyonegonic Director Dave Suddouth toured Shari Levine, Lisa Borg and Beth Goldstein Thu, Jul 3, 2025. 110 total staff members. 60-65% returning staff rate. \~50% of counselors are former campers. Two-year leadership training program for 16-17 year olds (CITs/ACs).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins house 5 campers + 1 counselor (some variations). Centralized bathrooms/showers (no in-cabin plumbing). Dining hall operates in two shifts: Junior/Intermediate (150 people); Senior (100 people). Professional laundry service (48-hour turnaround). Separate activity areas for different age groups.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Mile and a half of lakefront. Daily swimming for all ages is required. First-week canoeing for new campers. Sailing program.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 daily activity periods (3 morning, 2 afternoon). Required activities: daily swimming for all ages; first-week canoeing and outdoor living skills for new campers.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Specialized programs: English horseback riding (11 horses, 90% of younger campers participate); extensive trip program (67 trips per summer); theater productions each session; tennis tournament participation; sailing program. No extra charges for activities or trips.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health center staffed by: resident pediatrician; rotating pediatrician from Mass General; team of RNs (including nurse practitioner); two rotating mental health professionals (LCSWs). Hospital 8 miles away (10-minute drive). Accommodate dietary restrictions (separate prep areas). Nut-aware facility (adjusts based on session needs). Accept diabetic campers with prior relationship. Inclusive gender identity policy: welcome campers who identify as female at enrollment; successfully integrated transitioning campers; experience with gender-neutral/transitioning staff.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

25% of campers are children of alumnae. 58 campers have siblings at brother camp (Winona).



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No phone calls except: birthday calls; mid-season calls for full-season campers if parents can't visit. Weekly letter writing required (minimum 3 per session). Photo updates at least weekly. No extra charges for activities or trips.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

All-girls camp ages 8-15 (3rd-10th grade) with 190 total campers per session. Three units: Junior (8-10), Intermediate (11-12), Senior (13-15), and 5 daily activity periods (3 morning, 2 afternoon) with required daily swimming and first-week canoeing/outdoor living skills for new campers. Specialized programs include English horseback riding (11 horses, 90% of younger campers participate), an extensive trip program (67 trips per summer), theater productions each session, tennis tournament participation, and a sailing program, with no extra charges for activities or trips. Health, safety, and inclusion are strong (resident and rotating pediatricians, team of RNs including a nurse practitioner, two rotating mental health professionals (LCSWs), hospital 8 miles away) and an inclusive gender identity policy, alongside structured parent communication (weekly letter writing; limited phone calls; photo updates at least weekly).



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Extensive trip program (67 trips per summer)
  - English horseback riding (11 horses, 90% of younger campers participate)
  - Health center staffed by: Resident pediatrician; Rotating pediatrician from Mass General; Team of RNs (including nurse practitioner); Two rotating mental health professionals (LCSWs)
  - Inclusive gender identity policy: Welcome campers who identify as female at enrollment; Successfully integrated transitioning campers; Experience with gender-neutral/transitioning staff
  - Mile and a half of lakefront
  - No extra charges for activities or trips
  - 60-65% returning staff rate; \~50% of counselors are former campers
  - 51% of campers stay full 6-week season



#### Lowlights

  - Centralized bathrooms/showers (no in-cabin plumbing)
  - No phone calls except: Birthday calls; Mid-season calls for full-season campers if parents can’t visit
  - Accept diabetic campers with prior relationship



#### Similar Camps

  - Winona

Camp Wyonegonic Director Dave Suddouth t oured Shari Levine, Lisa Borg and Beth Goldstein Thu, Jul 3 , 2025 Camp Overview ? All-girls camp ages 8-15 (3rd-10th grade) ? 190 total campers per session ?
