title: Maine Teen — Shari — 9-3-25
text:
# Maine Teen



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Maine Teen Camp Matt Pines toured Shari Levine, Lisa Borg and Beth Goldstein Thu, Jul 3, 2025. 41 years of operation. Current capacity: \~200 campers. Current enrollment: First session: 165 campers; Second session: 145 campers. Age range: 12-17 years old. Property size: 30-35 acres with access to hundreds of acres of trails. Session Structure: Two main sessions currently; Two-week option available at start of each session; Planning to transition to all two-week sessions next summer (options will be 2 weeks, 4 weeks, or 6 weeks). Full summer campers (8 weeks): \~10 children. Visiting days: No formal visiting days, but parents can visit if desired.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Demographics & International Program: International campers: Currently 20-25% (traditionally 30-35% pre-pandemic). Geographic diversity: Campers from Florida, international locations. Director: Unknown. Staff profile beyond medical: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Water activities: Water skiing, wakeboarding, kayaking, canoeing, paddle boarding, sailing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Activity Structure: 5 periods per day. Fully elective system. Weekly activity selection. Popular activities (cooking, water skiing) may have waitlists.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports: Tennis (private lessons available: 3-5-7 lesson packages, \~$55/lesson). Arts: Music (7 camper bands), stained glass, jewelry making, video production. Specialty: Blacksmithing, cooking, mountain biking, axe throwing. Special Programs: Scholarship programs available (up to 50% financial aid); Partnerships with foundations (Wildflower Foundation, Summer Search); International scholarship programs (Old Road Fund - Antigua); Local school district arrangements.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical Facilities: Current staff: 1 nurse practitioner, 2 RNs, 2 EMTs; 8 rotating medical staff through summer. New health center under construction. Nearest hospital: Bridgeton (35 min) or Maine Med in Portland (55 min). Safety Protocols: Device-free camp (exceptions for medical devices). Required swim tests before waterfront activities. Clear behavioral contracts and boundaries. Storm safety procedures in place. Food Service: Buffet-style dining. Accommodates dietary restrictions. Not nut-free but manages allergies. Multiple snack times throughout day.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Unknown



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation options: Airport pickups: Portland and Boston. No bus service from NY/NJ (discontinued post-pandemic). Unaccompanied minor service available for under-15s. Parent Communication & Packages: Two landline phones available for camper calls (1-2 times/week). Email system for campers. Care packages allowed (2 per session). Regular parent updates and evaluations. Extra Costs: Tennis private lessons (3-5-7 lesson packages, \~$55/lesson).



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Maine Teen Camp has 41 years of operation, serves \~200 campers ages 12–17, and sits on 30–35 acres with access to hundreds of acres of trails. The program is fully elective with 5 periods per day and major programs across water activities, sports, arts, and specialty areas like blacksmithing and cooking. Two main sessions are offered with a two-week option at the start of each session, and they are planning to transition to all two-week sessions next summer (2, 4, or 6 weeks). Health and safety include an expanded medical team, required swim tests, device-free policies, a new health center under construction, and buffet-style dining that accommodates dietary restrictions.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Fully elective system with 5 periods per day and weekly activity selection.
  - Strong waterfront: Water skiing, wakeboarding, kayaking, canoeing, paddle boarding, sailing.
  - Arts and specialty breadth: Music (7 camper bands), stained glass, jewelry making, video production; blacksmithing, cooking, mountain biking, axe throwing.
  - International campers 20-25% (traditionally 30-35% pre-pandemic); geographic diversity including Florida and international locations.
  - Scholarship programs up to 50% financial aid; partnerships with Wildflower Foundation, Summer Search; Old Road Fund (Antigua).
  - Medical coverage: 1 NP, 2 RNs, 2 EMTs with 8 rotating medical staff; new health center under construction.
  - Device-free camp (exceptions for medical devices).
  - Property size 30-35 acres with access to hundreds of acres of trails.



#### Lowlights

  - No bus service from NY/NJ (discontinued post-pandemic).
  - Popular activities (cooking, water skiing) may have waitlists.
  - Not nut-free (manages allergies).
  - Nearest hospitals are 35–55 minutes away.
  - No formal visiting days (parents can visit if desired).



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Crescent Cove — Shari — 9-3-25
text:
# Crescent Cove



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Crescent Cove (previously Nashoba North). They bought camp in Nov 2022. In 2024 they had about 70 campers per session. 250 will be their max. Two 3.5 wk sessions. Can also do 2 wks at any age for any number of summers (website had indicated 1st summer only but they changed it while I was on the phone with them) though they don't encourage multiple summers, they are not a 2wk camping experience long term generally speaking. May be able to do both 3.5 wk sessions but must be picked up in-between for them to give counselors their full day off and prepare camp for the 2nd session overall. They did have about 8 kids who did it in 2024. Currently their return rate is high but they find that session camp sign-ups come later than full summer sign-ups (their daughters go to Mataponi and families rush to get spots).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Shari Levine call with Nicole and Jason, 15Jan2025. They bought camp in Nov 2022. About 12 alumni kids and they feel in next 1-3 years they will have more. Staff both domestic and international. Campers from many different states (CA, AZ, FL, MD, tri-state, TX, IL, NC, New England, etc) and a few internationals. Historically NN was mostly non-Jewish families and though they are a nondenominational camp, the new families are trending a little more Jewish. Their daughters go to Mataponi.



## 3 · Living Quarters

No towel service yet (they had mentioned on our zoom in 2023) but hopefully by summer or definitely for 2026. Otherwise, Unknown.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Hoping for changing rooms down at beach, working on permits, and long term with bathrooms.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown



## 6 · Program Highlights

Capital improvements: dining hall all redone (photo on website, beautiful). Golf greens were re-done. Also want to convert upper barn to gymnastics studio for 2026.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Capital improvements: dining hall all redone (photo on website, beautiful). Otherwise, Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Historically NN was mostly non-Jewish families and though they are a nondenominational camp, the new families are trending a little more Jewish. They get lots of word-of-mouth referrals and campers who found via google searches.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation: in '24 they did vans from Westchester and Boston, fee was $150 each way. 25 min from Portland airport, pick ups there too. NN was 98% drop-off by parents so they’re trying to move away from that. They reassured me that they visit every family before camp. Tech and extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Crescent Cove (previously Nashoba North) is owned by Nicole and Jason (they bought camp in Nov 2022) and runs two 3.5 wk sessions with a flexible 2 wk option at any age. In 2024 they had about 70 campers per session (max 250), with campers from many different states and a few internationals, and staff both domestic and international. Capital improvements include a redone dining hall and re-done golf greens, with plans for beach changing rooms/bathrooms and an upper barn conversion to a gymnastics studio for 2026. Transportation options include vans from Westchester and Boston ($150 each way) and Portland airport pick-ups; they visit every family before camp.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Two 3.5 wk sessions; 2 wk option at any age for any number of summers.
  - In 2024 they had about 70 campers per session; 250 will be their max.
  - Campers from many different states and a few internationals; staff both domestic and international.
  - Capital improvements: dining hall all redone; golf greens re-done.
  - Plans for changing rooms at the beach (permits in process) and long-term bathrooms; upper barn to gymnastics studio for 2026.
  - High return rate; lots of word-of-mouth referrals.
  - Transportation vans from Westchester and Boston ($150 each way); 25 min from Portland airport with pick-ups.
  - They visit every family before camp.



#### Lowlights

  - No towel service yet (hopefully by summer or definitely for 2026).
  - May be able to do both 3.5 wk sessions but campers must be picked up in-between.
  - NN was 98% parent drop-off; they are trying to move away from that.
  - Session camp sign-ups come later than full summer sign-ups.
  - Hoping for changing rooms down at beach (permits pending).



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Skylemar — Shari — 9-3-25
text:
# Skylemar



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Mon, Jun 30, 2025 Camp Overview. Location: Maine on Tricky Pond (private waterfront). Capacity: 200-225 boys; current enrollment \~200 boys. Age range: 7-15 years old. Session options: 3 weeks or 6 weeks. Return rate: 93%. Demographics: \~65% Jewish, geographically diverse.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Skylemar Shari Levine, Lisa Borg, Laurie Karol, Jennifer Markizon and Beth Goldstein met with Arleen Shepard and toured with son Nick. Siblings Blake, Adam and Lucy involved with camp. Staffing & Structure. Staff ratio: 1:2 (140 staff to 200 campers). Counselors: All domestic college athletes (except 1 international sailing instructor). In-bunk staffing: 3 counselors per cabin with 5-8 campers. Key staff: Arlene & Jeff: Owners/Directors; Nick Shepard: Assistant Director (recruitment, media); Adam Shepard: Culinary Program Director; Lucy Shepard: New Camper Integration; 2 full-time doctors; Multiple RNs; 2 dietitians.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Single beds in most cabins. Newer cabins have in-bunk bathrooms. Separate shower houses for some bunks.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront on Tricky Pond (private waterfront). Daily waterfront period (3 rotations): Swim instruction (required through age 13); Water skiing/wakeboarding; Non-motorized boats. 4 ski boats. Rated one of top 3 cleanest lakes in Maine. Secondary waterfront for fishing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

7 activity periods per day: 5 assigned periods by age group and 2 choice periods (mixed ages).



## 6 · Program Highlights

Unique Programs. PAL (Person of Wisdom) Program: Life skills curriculum; Gentlemen’s Academy for youngest; Includes tie-tying, table setting, parallel parking. Band of Brothers: Mixed-age mentorship groups. Culinary program with dedicated kitchen. Theater: 2 productions per summer. Yellowhorn STEM program. Power tools/woodworking education. Sports facilities include tennis/pickleball courts, ropes course, and multiple athletic fields.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Meals: One seating, buffet style. Separate allergy kitchen/protocols. Morning snack at 11am. Health: 2 full-time doctors; Multiple RNs; 2 dietitians. Outdoor dining pavilion.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

High return rate (93%). Geographically diverse with \~65% Jewish. Family-run feel: Arlene & Jeff (Owners/Directors) with Nick, Adam and Lucy involved; siblings Blake, Adam and Lucy involved with camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation provided from BWI (50-60 kids, 5 escorts), New York (35-40 kids), and Boston (20 kids). Communication: FaceTime calls once per session; blog updates nightly; photo galleries every 3-4 days (\~1000 photos). Trips: 2 off-camp trips per week; age-group overnight trips (2-5 days); Quebec trip for oldest campers; on-property overnight camping experiences. Extra costs: Unknown. Tech/device policy beyond FaceTime calls: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Skylemar is a boys camp in Maine on Tricky Pond (private waterfront) for ages 7-15 with a capacity of 200-225 boys and current enrollment \~200 boys. Sessions are 3 weeks or 6 weeks, with 7 activity periods per day (5 assigned by age group and 2 choice), and a daily waterfront period with swim instruction required through age 13, water skiing/wakeboarding, and non-motorized boats. The program features PAL (Person of Wisdom), Band of Brothers, a culinary program with dedicated kitchen, theater (2 productions per summer), Yellowhorn STEM, and power tools/woodworking, with sports facilities including tennis/pickleball courts, a ropes course, and multiple athletic fields. Families will appreciate the high return rate (93%), family leadership (Arlene & Jeff with Nick, Adam and Lucy), robust health supports (2 full-time doctors, multiple RNs, 2 dietitians), separate allergy kitchen, and clear communication (FaceTime once per session, nightly blog, photo galleries), plus transportation from BWI, New York and Boston.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Private waterfront on Tricky Pond; rated one of top 3 cleanest lakes in Maine.
  - Staff ratio: 1:2 (140 staff to 200 campers).
  - Counselors: All domestic college athletes (except 1 international sailing instructor).
  - Return rate: 93%.
  - PAL (Person of Wisdom) Program and Band of Brothers.
  - Culinary program with dedicated kitchen.
  - 4 ski boats and robust water skiing/wakeboarding.
  - 2 full-time doctors, multiple RNs, and 2 dietitians.
  - Separate allergy kitchen/protocols.
  - Transportation provided from BWI, New York, and Boston.
  - Photo galleries every 3-4 days (\~1000 photos) and blog updates nightly.



#### Lowlights

  - Not all cabins have in-bunk bathrooms; separate shower houses for some bunks.
  - Tech/device policy beyond FaceTime calls is unknown.
  - Extra costs: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Kingsley Pines — Shari — 9-3-25
text:
# Kingsley Pines



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Becky Alford toured Shari Levine, Lisa Borg, Beth Goldstein and Laurie Karol Tue, Jul 1 2025. Location: Maine. Family-run by 3 brothers. 9th summer for tour guide Becky Alford. Sessions: Two 2-week sessions; One 3-week session. Maximum stay: 5 weeks. About 25-30 campers stay multiple sessions.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Capacity & demographics: Total summer enrollment \~600 campers. Per session: 170-225 campers (typically 185-200). Age range: 7 years (3rd grade) to 16 years. Geographic mix: New England; New York City; Florida; International (65 campers from Japan, Germany, Chile). Scholarship/tuition assistance: \~1/3 of campers receive some form. Staffing & supervision: Cabin staff 68 counselors. Ratio: 3:1 camper to staff. International staff: 25-33% (primarily UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabin arrangements: 2-3 counselors per cabin; 8-12 campers per cabin. Younger cabins: Private bathrooms. Older cabins: Separate bathhouse facilities.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Popular aquatics: Wakesurfing/waterskiing (4 boats).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

4 activity periods daily: 3 morning activities (pre-selected weekly), 1 afternoon activity (pre-selected), 1 daily choice period. Age restrictions apply for certain activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Popular activities: High ropes course; Stained glass (age 10+); Woodworking; Theater; Arts (ceramics, jewelry, photography); Sports (soccer, frisbee, baseball, basketball). Weekly out-of-camp trips for older campers.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical staff: 2 full-time RNs + rotating nurses. Can accommodate various dietary restrictions. Gender identity accommodations available through individual family discussions. Weekly laundry service (individual bags).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Non-competitive philosophy.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No phones/electronics (basic music players allowed). No packages allowed. Communication with home: Daily printed emails from parents; Weekly scanned postcards to home; First-week updates (calls for new campers, emails for returning). Additional fees: Airport transportation; Older camper activities; Adventure trips.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kingsley Pines is a family-run camp in Maine operated by 3 brothers with a non-competitive philosophy. Sessions include two 2-week sessions and one 3-week session, with a maximum stay of 5 weeks. Campers are ages 7 (3rd grade) to 16, with \~600 total summer enrollment and a 3:1 camper to staff ratio (68 counselors; 25-33% international staff). Popular activities include wakesurfing/waterskiing (4 boats), a high ropes course, arts, sports, and weekly out-of-camp trips for older campers, with medical staff on site and clear parent communication systems.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Family-run by 3 brothers in Maine.
  - 3:1 camper to staff ratio; 68 counselors.
  - International staff: 25-33% (primarily UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia).
  - Diverse camper mix, including 65 international campers from Japan, Germany, Chile.
  - Strong aquatics: Wakesurfing/waterskiing (4 boats).
  - High ropes course and robust arts (stained glass age 10+, ceramics, jewelry, photography) and sports.
  - Non-competitive philosophy.
  - Medical staff: 2 full-time RNs + rotating nurses; dietary accommodations.
  - Clear communication with home (daily printed emails; weekly scanned postcards; first-week updates).

  - No packages allowed.
  - Age restrictions apply for certain activities.
  - Older cabins use separate bathhouse facilities.
  - Additional fees for airport transportation, older camper activities, and adventure trips.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Robin Hood — Carolina — 9-3-25
text:
# Robin Hood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Robin Hood New Hampshire is a large coed camp with about 400 campers, serving ages 7–16. Located in New Hampshire, it primarily draws campers from the Tri-State area, though they come from different schools and neighborhoods rather than one concentrated group. The camp offers 4-week and full-season sessions and boasts a strong \~90% return rate, with 97 new campers this year. Emphasizing tradition and community rituals, Robin Hood fosters a sense of continuity and belonging among its campers.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: Mix of athletic and arts-inclined kids. Good for homesick children due to supportive environment. 60% Jewish, \~10% international. Staff: Strong college-aged counselors for sports and activities. Director/Leadership: Run by Woody and family. Very involved leadership.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins \~30 kids each, grouped by age (all age group in the same bunk). Bathrooms and shower houses shared. Laundry every 4–5 days. Towel and sheet service provided. In-house laundry.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

5 ski boats and 5 pontoon boats. Strong water program with ski, swim, sailing. Competes with other camps in aquatics and sports.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 periods per day; 3 are structured and 3 are electives. Campers experience both structure and choice, with half their schedule assigned and half electives, creating balance for all ages. Strong sports focus (tennis, soccer, basketball, etc.).



## 6 · Program Highlights

Wide range of land and water sports. Arts: theater, music, and dance shows. Dance shows and performances are part of camp culture. All age groups do a dance show to all camp. Friday rituals include challah and grape juice service. Leadership program for older campers. Strong and energetic leadership. Large camp traditions.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Full-season physician + 8 nurses on site. Can accommodate allergies; peanut-free and kosher-style meals available. Questionnaires to all campers at days 5, 15, 25 to check on food, counselors, bullying, etc.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Large, energetic, and social. Competitive (but not excessively), but also has strong creative and performing arts outlets. Camp has a proud, traditional identity and strong loyalty among returning families. Very social environment; large camp traditions. Good for kids who enjoy being part of a large, spirited environment.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from NY (\~90 kids), chaperoned flights from LA (\~40 kids) and Miami (\~20 kids). Closest airports: Boston and Portland. Communication: One parent phone call permitted per session; international families can use email, outgoing mail service for all campers. No electronics. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Robin Hood NH is a large, high-energy camp that blends strong sports, waterfront, and creative arts with a deeply traditional culture. Campers experience both structure and choice, with half their schedule assigned and half electives, creating balance for all ages. The camp is vibrant and spirited, offering both competitive athletics and a robust arts program, all within a supportive community. With extensive resources, a proud identity, and strong leadership, Robin Hood NH is best for confident kids who thrive in a big, competitive, and tradition-rich environment.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong waterfront
  - Large traditions
  - Competitive sports
  - Strong and energetic leadership



#### Lowlights

  - Very large and social



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Maine Teen — Carolina — 9-3-25
text:
# Maine Teen



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Maine Teen Camp is a fully elective coed program in Maine for teens ages 13–17. It runs two main sessions, each 3.5 weeks long, with some 2-week options, and has a capacity of about 200 campers (currently averaging around 165 per session). 2026 will most likely do 3 sessions of 2 weeks. Known for its participative and energetic culture, Maine Teen Camp brings the community together through lively morning meetings where everyone laughs, engages, and connects. Has strong tennis and music programs (with privates available).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: Social, open-minded teens from the US and abroad (Brazil, Peru, Spain, Mexico). US base largely Northeast (NY, NJ, NH) with some from FL, TX, CA. Campers are diverse, creative, and less traditional “camp types.” The camp has a strong return rate of 85–90%, with most campers in the 14–15 age range, and many experiencing camp for the first time at this age. Staff: Counselors (\~20%) international; many from performing arts, sports, or outdoor backgrounds. Strong supervision model with multiple layers of accountability (attendance taken at every activity). Director: Matt (since 1997). Strong focus on independence, inclusivity, and creating a device-free, pressure-free community for teens. He is doing his PhD on the Impacts of a device-free environment for Teenagers, and is very into the subject.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Boys: 3 cabins in one building (averaging 10–18 kids per cabin). Older boys in a separate “cottage.” Girls: multiple cabins by age. Bedtime \~10:30–11pm.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Ski/wakeboarding. Popular: whitewater rafting (3x per session), canoe, kayak, swim. Outdoor trips highly popular, especially whitewater rafting.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Fully elective program: \~5 activities chosen weekly. Strong community rituals include participatory morning meetings. End-of-week trips: Boston, beach, Freeport shopping, local excursions.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports: volleyball, soccer, basketball, mountain biking. Compete in chess and kickball with other camps. Performance arts: band, dance, theater (with "School of Rock" style program). Unique programs: Shark Tank, Tween Day, blacksmithing, chess, jewelry-making, stained glass. Music + tennis are the most popular activities. Performance opportunities: band shows, dance, theater. Outdoor adventure and arts are heavily emphasized. Has strong tennis and music programs (with privates available). Safe but less competitive than traditional camps.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Accommodates allergies (gluten-free, vegan, diabetic kids). Wellness support: therapist on staff. Kids on the spectrum are welcome – Counselors present/supervision for quiet times. Strong supervision model with multiple layers of accountability (attendance taken at every activity). No pressure, no social hierarchy – anxiety-reducing environment.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Known for being inclusive, pressure-free, and welcoming to “different” teens (quirky, creative, socially non-traditional). No social pressure, no devices; teens engage more naturally. Campers describe it as a space where they can be themselves without judgment. Strong arts/music identity; less focus on traditional camp competition. Strong community rituals: participatory morning meetings, inclusive vibe. Strong focus on independence, inclusivity, and creating a device-free, pressure-free community for teens. Maine Teen is more woodsy and outdoorsy compared to French Woods Sports & Arts, which draws a heavier “artsy/alternative” crowd.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Closest airports: Boston, Portland. Bus service from NY. Communication: landline call home 1x/week; emails possible. No electronics for campers.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Maine Teen Camp is a teen-only program with a fully elective schedule, offering a mix of sports, outdoor adventure, and arts in a supportive and balanced environment. Campers enjoy music, tennis, whitewater rafting, creative electives, and trips, all within a warm, inclusive, and device-free culture. While it welcomes quirky, creative, and independent kids, it is equally a fit for mainstream teens who want choice, freedom, and a strong sense of community. Many campers have never been to camp before.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Inclusive vibe
  - excellent music/arts
  - strong international mix
  - unique programs (Shark Tank, blacksmithing)
  - device-free culture
  - strong tennis structure



#### Lowlights

  - less traditional sports
  - not ideal for highly competitive athletes



#### Similar Camps

  - French Woods Sports & Arts

title: Waziyatah — Carolina — 9-3-25
text:
# Waziyatah



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Waziyatah in Maine serves campers ages 6–16 and runs two 4-week sessions each summer. With about 250 campers, including many internationals from countries like Canada, Brazil, Spain, and Norway, it offers a diverse community. The program is elective-based, giving campers four daily periods of choice plus one required swim period, along with off-camp trips twice per session and overnight trips by cabin. This structure combines flexibility, adventure, and community bonding. It is rustic and basic programming, with Maine-level pricing.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: inclusive community, mix of U.S. and international (notably many from New England, Florida, Canada, Brazil, Spain, Norway). Staff: 2–4 counselors per cabin, strong supervision, counselors certified in areas like waterfront. Directors: Amanda & Brandon (owners/directors).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Standard cabins: 8–10 campers + 2–4 counselors. One larger cabin houses 20 campers + 5 counselors.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lakefront setting, swimming daily. Waterskiing, sailing, canoeing (popular choices). Swim instruction included. Counselors certified in waterfront.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Fully elective schedule: 4 periods of choice + 1 swim required + 1 "silly" period (skipping rocks, frisbee on the lake, etc). Intramural camp sports (non-competitive). Dance program particularly strong (up to 4–5 periods/day for enthusiasts). Strong arts & dance program.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Outdoor adventure (waterski, sailing, climbing). Weekly campfire with performances. Cabin overnight trips + off-camp excursions (older campers will go to Acadia for 3 days). Off-camp trips included for all cabins, with two off-camp trips per session.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Buffet-style dining, allergy accommodations available. Nut-free kitchen. Counselors trained and certified in key program areas. Strong supervision.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Inclusive: experience with deaf and trans campers. Strong emphasis on inclusivity and "everyone belongs here." Community-oriented; not a competitive or sports-driven culture, but a fun culture. No religious services (though owners are Jewish). Marketed heavily as "Best Maine Camp" — feels somewhat commercialized. Camp Waziyatah feels like a "Guákinima all the way in Maine" experience — but with Maine-level pricing. The camp is very basic, with nothing particularly special to differentiate it from others in the region.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Parents receive communication updates (weekly letters). No phones/devices for campers. Off-camp trips included in tuition. Maine-level pricing. We don’t believe it’s a justifiable option for families from South Florida or Latin America.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Waziyatah in Maine serves campers ages 6–16 and runs two 4-week sessions each summer with about 250 campers, including many internationals. The program is fully elective with four periods of choice plus one required swim and a "silly" period, and the lakefront setting offers swimming daily along with waterskiing, sailing, canoeing, and a strong arts & dance program. It is rustic and basic programming with Maine-level pricing and is marketed heavily as the "Best Maine Camp," which feels somewhat commercialized. We don’t believe it’s a justifiable option for families from South Florida or Latin America.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Lakefront setting, swimming daily
  - Fully elective schedule: 4 periods of choice + 1 swim required + 1 'silly' period (skipping rocks, frisbee on the lake, etc)
  - Dance program particularly strong (up to 4–5 periods/day for enthusiasts)
  - Inclusive community; experience with deaf and trans campers
  - Cabin overnight trips + off-camp excursions (older campers will go to Acadia for 3 days)
  - Buffet-style dining; allergy accommodations; nut-free kitchen
  - Parents receive communication updates (weekly letters)
  - Intramural camp sports (non-competitive)



#### Lowlights

  - It is rustic and basic programming, with Maine-level pricing
  - Marketed heavily as 'Best Maine Camp' — feels somewhat commercialized
  - The camp is very basic, with nothing particularly special to differentiate it from others in the region
  - We don’t believe it’s a justifiable option for families from South Florida or Latin America



#### Similar Camps

  - Guákinima

title: Robin Hood — Carolina — 9-3-25
text:
# Robin Hood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Robin Hood Maine is a fully choice, uniform camp located in Maine, serving campers ages 6-18 with around 286 campers at a time. The camp has a very international community, with approximately 80% of campers coming from abroad, giving it a strong global feel. Campers can start any week, though those beginning in the first week must stay through week three, and the program includes about 38 activities. Known for its challenging and adventurous culture, Robin Hood Maine offers both ocean and lakeside access.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: Highly international mix; lots of camper rotation each week (constant arrivals/departures on Sundays), which can be harder for homesick kids. Directors: Owner is older, no clear succession plan. Staff: Mix of internationals, including high-caliber instructors (e.g., Colombian champion Olympic waterski teacher).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: 6-18 campers per cabin. Bathrooms: Inside bunk. Shower house also available.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Strongest program: Waterski (best offering, top instructors, 10-boat fleet). Both ocean and lake access. Sailing available. Mile swim challenge.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

AM: 2 weeklong chosen activities. PM: 2 daily chosen activities. All-choice program with rotation. Campers can start any week, though those beginning in the first week must stay through week three.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Challenges are emphasized (mile swim, mountain hikes, camping trips). Tennis: 6 courts. Horses (extra cost). Golf, squash, fencing. "Academies" advertised (e.g., squash), but facilities/structure are old and basic. Every day up to 50 campers out of camp on optional day/overnight trips. Each camper has multiple opportunities weekly for excursions & camping trips. Less strong in arts and creative programs.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Buffet style meals. Allergies okay.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very spirited camp. Strong international presence (global community feel). Culture of challenges and outdoor adventure is central. Lots of camper turnover weekly (rotation). In and out every Sunday (frequent arrivals/departures). Weekly camper turnover may unsettle homesick kids.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Hard to access: nearest airport is Bangor (\~1 hour) with no direct flights from most cities; long drive from Portland. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Horses = extra cost.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Robin Hood in Maine is a full choice, spirited, uniform camp with a very international community (about 80% of campers) and its strongest program in waterski, supported by Olympic-level coaching. The culture emphasizes challenges, exploration, and constant opportunities for day and overnight trips, with access to both the ocean and a lake. While it promotes a wide range of "academies," the facilities and infrastructure are old and basic, arts and drama programs are weak, and the frequent weekly camper turnover can be difficult for homesick children. Best suited for adventurous, outdoorsy campers who thrive on variety, global friendships, and high-energy activities.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Robust waterski program with Olympic-level coaching
  - Access to ocean and lake
  - Highly international community (\~80% of campers)
  - Focus on challenges, outdoor adventures



#### Lowlights

  - Hard to access (Bangor airport, no direct flights, Long drive from Portland)
  - Weekly camper turnover may unsettle homesick kids
  - Leadership succession uncertain



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

? Robin Hood Maine is a fully choice, uniform camp located in Maine, serving campers ages 6?18 with around 286 campers at a time. The camp has a very international community, with approximately 80% of campers coming from abroad, giving it a strong global feel. Campers can start any week, though those beginning in the first week must stay through week three, and the program includes about 38 activities. Known for its challenging and adventurous culture, Robin Hood Maine offers both ocean and lakeside access, but is very hard to reach, as the nearest airport is Bangor (about an hour away) with no direct flights from most cities. ? Campers: Highly international mix; lots of camper rotation each week (constant arrivals/departures on Sundays), which can be harder for homesick kids ? Directors: Owner is older, no clear succession plan ? Staff: Mix of internationals, including high-caliber instructors (e.g., Colombian champion Olympic waterski teacher) ? Cabins: 6?18 campers per cabin ? Bathrooms: Inside bunk ? Shower house also available ? Strongest program: Waterski (best offering, top instructors, 10-boat fleet) ? Both ocean and lake access ? Sailing available ? AM: 2 weeklong chosen activities ? PM: 2 daily chosen activities ? All-choice program with rotation ? Challenges are emphasized (mile swim, mountain hikes, camping trips) ? Tennis: 6 courts ? Horses (extra cost) ? Golf, squash, fencing ? ?Academies? advertised (e.g., squash), but facilities/structure are old and basic ? Every day up to 50 campers out of camp on optional day/overnight trips ? Each camper has multiple opportunities weekly for excursions & camping trips ? Buffet style meals ? Allergies okay ? Very spirited camp ? Strong international presence (global community feel) ? Culture of challenges and outdoor adventure is central ? Less strong in arts and creative programs ? Lots of camper turnover weekly (rotation) ? In and out every Sunday (frequent arrivals/departures) ? Horses = extra cost ? Small, hard-to-reach airport (Bangor \~1 hr; no direct flights from most cities) Robin Hood in Maine is a full choice, spirited, uniform camp with a very international community (about 80% global campers) and its strongest program in waterski, supported by Olympic-level coaching. The culture emphasizes challenges, exploration, and constant opportunities for day and overnight trips, with access to both the ocean and a lake. While it promotes a wide range of ?academies,? the facilities and infrastructure are old and basic, arts and drama programs are weak, and the frequent weekly camper turnover can be difficult for homesick children. Best suited for adventurous, outdoorsy campers who thrive on variety, global friendships, and high-energy activities,. ? Highlights: - Robust waterski program with Olympic-level coaching - Access to ocean and lake - Highly international community (\~80% of campers) - Focus on challenges, outdoor adventures Lowlights - Hard to access (Bangor airport, no direct flights, Long drive from Portland) - Weekly camper turnover may unsettle homesick kids - Leadership succession uncertain

title: Birchmont — Carolina — 8-25-25
text:
# Birchmont



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Birchmont is a coed camp that hosts about 300 campers per session, serving ages 7–15, with younger campers often starting at ages 7–8, a strong middle-aged group around 10–11, and older campers continuing up to 15. Sessions are offered for 3 or 4 weeks, with the option to combine for a full season. Known for its warm, family-centered, and values-driven culture, the camp maintains a welcoming atmosphere that encourages strong connections. It also has a very high return rate of around 90%, reflecting the loyalty and satisfaction of its families.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: Mix of enthusiastic, well-rounded kids who like to try everything. Strong social environment with high acceptance of newcomers. Not overly competitive, though sports are played daily. Can welcome campers for the 1st time at any age. Staff: Skilled specialists for activities (sports, arts, waterfront). Many counselors return, with \~10% former campers on staff. Strong adult presence. CITs at 16, JCs at 17. Director: Greg leads with a focus on family values, warmth, and inclusion. Known for welcoming anxious or first-time campers with extra care.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins with \~10 campers and counselors. Bathrooms inside cabins. Larger cabins have a lounge/common space. Won't take more than 1 Spanish-speaking camper per cabin.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Beautiful and very complete waterfront offering everything: swimming, sailing, watersports, lifeguard training. It is down the hill but they offer a bus to return. Beach/water time twice a day. Intercamp competitions in water sports. Safe, structured aquatics program.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Mix of structure and electives: younger campers = \~⅔ assigned, ⅓ choice; older campers = 50% electives. Activities run morning + 2-hour afternoon blocks. Daily tennis, even if later rescheduled due to weather. 12 courts with strong instruction program. Electives chosen by week – pick one in each area (ex Nature, A\&C, ceramics, woodshop). No special Friday service.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Wide range of land sports (soccer, baseball, basketball, hockey, pickleball, futsal, dodgeball). Arts: ceramics, nature, woodshop, photography (with darkroom), Harry Potter–themed creative programs. Great Riding program in-house (horse/pony care; riding lessons billed separately, up to 5x per week). Offers special/extra riding at 6am for experienced kids. Outdoor adventure trips 2x per week. Special Derby program (design/build race cars, then compete). Life skills component woven into daily activities. ARG – Archery, riflery, golf (with outside golf trips 2x/week). Zipline, ropes course, Apple, Pear and Peach Orchids that kids can volunteer to take care. Campers choose a lifetime sport to focus on (such as tennis), supported by very experienced directors and well-run programming.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Healthcare staff: 4 RNs, nurse practitioner, and doctor (pediatric). Allergy-friendly kitchen; can accommodate special diets (gluten-free, kosher meat, etc.). Has a separate refrigerator for specific items. Nut-aware (not completely nut-free). Very supportive with mild special needs (ADHD, spectrum).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very warm, family-centric, and values-based. Vibrant and inclusive — great for first-time campers. Strong group-bonding emphasis (campfire, team competitions, evening events). Balanced environment: competitive sports exist, but not high-pressure. Enthusiastic, positive culture with lots of variety. The campus sits on a tabletop hill with the lake below — a bit of a walk (though buses are available) — giving it the feel of a real camp: not manicured, but also not overly rustic.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Many families from FL, NY, CA, TX, MS; some international (Spain, France, UK). Chaperoned flight from Miami. Letters home standard; email available for international families. Horseback lessons billed separately.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Birchmont is a warm, family-centric camp with a welcoming environment perfect for first-time campers. Its program blends semi-structure with choice, offering strong sports instruction, a vibrant waterfront, and creative outlets like ceramics, woodshop, and photography. Campers choose a lifetime sport to focus on (such as tennis), supported by very experienced directors and well-run programming. The campus sits on a tabletop hill with the lake below — a bit of a walk (though buses are available) — giving it the feel of a real camp: not manicured, but also not overly rustic.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Family warmth
  - Excellent tennis and waterfront
  - Variety of creative options
  - Strong first-time camper support



#### Lowlights

  - Not for boys who need heavy sports competition
  - Cabins intentionally limit Spanish speakers
  - Campus sits on a tabletop hill with the lake below



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Kabeyun — Carolina — 8-25-25
text:
# Kabeyun



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Kabeyun is a small, non-profit boys’ camp in New Hampshire, operating for over 100 years. The camp hosts a max of 114 campers per session, along with counselors and CITs, and offers 4-week sessions, with a 2-week introductory option for younger boys (11 and under). Serving ages 7–15, Kabeyun has a strong 70–80% return rate, welcoming around 30 new campers each session. Its entirely elective program features 14–20 activities chosen daily, with trips or overnights chosen every Sunday, allowing campers to shape their own experiences.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: Independent, “quirkier” boys than average, creative, outdoorsy, and often drawn to arts, nature, and sailing. Not primarily sports-oriented. Best for boys who thrive on independence and daily choice. 50% come from DC/New England. Not many international campers. They don't enroll by age, so there is no limit. Staff: many are former campers. 5–6 CITs per session; must be former campers. Counselors often stay multiple years. Staff quality varies, with some activities depending heavily on counselor expertise. Director: Ken Robins leads with a philosophy centered on independence, self-discovery, and non-competitive accomplishment.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins of 6–12 boys, grouped by age. Bathrooms and showers outside cabins (1 shower per week required; other days swim or optional shower). Rustic, simple facilities.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Strong sailing program with a large fleet of sailboats; they earn ratings and can evolve each year. Canoeing, kayaking, swimming, water trips. Separate lake for ski, wakeboarding, etc. No inflatables, just a slide.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Fully elective schedule (campers choose activities daily from a list of 14–20 options). Trips (hiking, kayaking, climbing) chosen weekly; trips or overnights chosen every Sunday. Sundays are different: late wake-up, group camp activity, campfire.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Sailing is a signature strength. Outdoor trips and overnights (canoeing, rock climbing, hiking). Woodshop, photography (including darkroom, real camera, wet photography), leatherwork room is great. Pottery room also distinctive for a boys camp. Indoor badminton. Drama program depends on the counselor's talent and interest.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health center staffed with 4 nurses; physician on board; hospital 8 miles away. Meals communal, allergy-aware but not nut-free. Emphasis on daily choice and independence, even at mealtimes. Can accommodate special needs (in the spectrum, ADHD) case by case, but if dedicated aid is needed at school, then it is not a good fit because of the daily choice model.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Independent, non-competitive, outdoorsy, creative atmosphere. Campers encouraged to take ownership of their experience and set their own goals. Positive for boys seeking independence, less structured than mainstream camps. Boys described as “nicer” and more individualistic compared to average camp peers. Not highly social with other camps (no dances, limited interaction).



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Pick-up/drop-off offered in Boston; bus service available. Communication: Letters home; international families can use email. No electronics allowed. Scholarships available due to non-profit status. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kabeyun offers a unique, fully elective boys’ camp where independence and self-discovery are central. Campers choose their daily schedule and weekly trips, fostering responsibility and ownership of their experience. With a strong sailing fleet, extensive outdoor adventure program, and a culture that values creativity and individuality over competition, Kabeyun attracts boys who thrive outside of a rigid structure. Its rustic environment and non-profit mission create a thoughtful, community-based camp experience ideal for families seeking an independent, outdoorsy, creative camp where boys chart their own course rather than follow a traditional structure.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong sailing and outdoor trips
  - deeply independent culture
  - leather, photography and pottery programs



#### Lowlights

  - Rustic facilities
  - limited sports
  - program quality can depend on counselors
  - not a fit for boys needing structure



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Moosilauke — Carolina — 8-25-25
text:
# Moosilauke



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Moosilauke is a boys’ camp in New Hampshire with a history of over 100 years, welcoming about 140 campers from 19 states and several countries, including Singapore, Hong Kong, and Spain. Set on 100+ acres, the camp offers sessions of 2.5 weeks, 4 weeks, or 6 weeks, with most campers choosing the full summer. Moosilauke provides a spirited, traditional boys’ camp experience with a balance of structure and independence, blending mandatory sports, waterfront adventure, and backcountry leadership trips.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: Mainstream boys, active and eager to participate. Athletic but not overly competitive; mix of very sporty kids and some less sporty. Diverse racially, socioeconomically, and geographically. Culture emphasizes resilience, teamwork, and independence. Staff: Close camper/staff relationships. 10 female counselors support younger boys (but don’t sleep in cabins). Many staff return year after year; strong admin team and on-call physician. Known for their BTCOD (Beyond the Call of Duty) commitment. Director: Bill has been director for 35 years. Deeply experienced, personally recruits families (including in Miami). Values positive role models, culture of resilience, and the belief that it’s OK to fail.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins grouped by age. Creek and bridge naturally separate cabin areas. Shower houses located outside cabins.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterskiing, kneeboard, wakesurf, sailing, canoe, kayak. No trampoline or inflatable water toys. Classic, traditional lakefront aquatics focus.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Morning: 2 assigned periods (structured). Afternoon: full choice of electives. Mandatory sports each morning ensure all boys get athletic exposure. Land sports: basketball, baseball, tennis, soccer, lacrosse, etc. Outdoor adventure: hiking, climbing, mountain biking. Backcountry Leadership Program: 2 night trips (camping, canoeing, outdoor skills). Creative arts and electives available. Occasional socials with sister girls’ camp (1 per session).



## 6 · Program Highlights

Backcountry Leadership Program and waterfront adventure are standouts. With a strong role model emphasis, and a culture that embraces resilience, boys return home more confident, independent, and connected. Strong director, excellent leadership/outdoor trips, positive culture of resilience.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

On-call physician, strong admin team with school experience and advanced degrees. Peanut-free tables (not camp); gluten-free and vegan options available. Family-style dining at mixed-age tables. Family-style meals encourage bonding. Reflection moments after each meal build community.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Spirited, positive, rooted in tradition. Encourages independence, trying new things, and learning through failure. Older boys model behavior for younger ones, creating a strong mentorship culture. Staff and peers reinforce resilience, authenticity, and teamwork.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Airport pick-ups: Boston, Westchester, and New York. 2 hrs 20 min from Boston. No electronics allowed. Communication via traditional letters. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Moosilauke offers a spirited, traditional boys’ camp experience with a balance of structure and independence. Program blends mandatory sports, waterfront adventure, and backcountry leadership trips. With a strong role model emphasis, and a culture that embraces resilience, boys return home more confident, independent, and connected. Good for kids who are good in one single sport and want to try others and are ok to not be the best at all.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong director
  - Excellent leadership/outdoor trips
  - Positive culture of resilience



#### Lowlights

  - Outside shower houses
  - Limited socials
  - Not peanut free (only a few tables are)



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Windsor Mountain — Carolina — 8-25-25
text:
# Windsor Mountain



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Windsor Mountain is a co-ed, alternative overnight camp in New Hampshire for children ages 7–16. With an enrollment of about 275 campers per session, the camp offers both 2-week and 3.5-week sessions, with roughly 25% of campers choosing to stay for the full summer. Known for being unique and creative rather than traditional, Windsor Mountain provides a distinctive camp experience that emphasizes individuality and self-expression.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Director: Jake. Staff: Diverse, many with arts/music backgrounds; several non-binary counselors. Staff culture less formal, sometimes felt inappropriate (e.g., male counselor in a bikini during cooking class). Camper base: 40% New York metro area; 30% New England; smaller groups from California and Texas. Attracts kids who are more artistic, eclectic, and less traditional/sporty. Artsy, eclectic kids — not sporty.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Rustic cabins and a treehouse lounge overlooking the lake. Cabin life less structured/formal than traditional camps. Animals on site: goats, dogs, mini horse.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Large natural lake with no motorized watercraft. Zipline over the water. Canoeing, kayaking, and fishing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

4 activity periods daily (mostly electives) + 1 “fun” block. Heavy emphasis on counselor-led specialty activities (quality depends on staff). Unique / random offerings (e.g., “tractor supply private tour”). Campfire once a week. 3-day trips open to any age.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Strong arts focus: music, stained glass, ceramics, and drumming (same Rastafarian instructor for \~10 years). Culinary program (hands-on, knife skills), ceramics, stained glass, archery, music studio, drum program. No competition or leagues — highly collaborative and creative.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Nurses and doctor on site. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very alternative/hippie feel. Very relaxed, free-spirited, highly dependent on counselor energy and initiative. Less structured and more experimental than mainstream camps. Highly collaborative and creative. Artsy, eclectic kids — not sporty.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Windsor Mountain is an eclectic, arts-driven camp designed for free-spirited, creative kids who thrive in alternative settings. With unique programs in music, arts, and culinary, campers explore self-expression in a collaborative and non-competitive environment. The culture is highly relaxed, counselor-driven, and far less structured than mainstream camps. Best suited for families seeking an alternative, artsy experience who are also comfortable with a diverse camper and counselor community, including non-binary and non-traditional staff.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Known for being unique and creative rather than traditional; provides a distinctive camp experience that emphasizes individuality and self-expression.
  - Program uniqueness is a strength.
  - Strong arts/music focus.
  - Highly collaborative and creative; no competition or leagues.
  - Unique / random offerings (e.g., “tractor supply private tour”) can be fun.



#### Lowlights

  - Overall, we did not feel comfortable recommending Windsor Mountain broadly.
  - The camp is very alternative, with staff culture and boundaries that did not always feel age-appropriate.
  - Quality depends heavily on the specific counselor — some activities felt random.
  - Staff culture less formal, sometimes felt inappropriate (e.g., male counselor in a bikini during cooking class).
  - Not a fit for families expecting a traditional or structured environment.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Tevya — Carolina — 8-22-25
text:
# Tevya



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Tevya is a co-ed Jewish overnight camp in New Hampshire, founded in 1940. It is culturally Jewish but not overly religious, welcoming campers who are rising 3rd–10th graders, with CITs being rising seniors and an Israel trip offered for rising 11th graders. Sessions run for 3.5 weeks, with a full-summer option available, and the camp enrolls about 300 campers per session, making it larger than its sister camp, Tel Noar. With a strong retention rate of 93%, Camp Tevya has a long-standing reputation for tradition and community.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Director: Lauren. Campers: Mostly from Massachusetts, some from NY/NJ, plus a handful of Israeli campers. Staff: Mostly 18–21 years old; \~100 total; 6–8 per bunk. Head Counselors: Adults overseeing learning areas. CITs: Rising seniors with their own living area (with A/C) on a strong leadership track. Campers bunked by gender identity (but historically only a few nonbinary campers). Boys and girls dine together and mix for many activities. CITs live separately with upgraded housing. Bunk ratio: 6–8 staff per bunk.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Campers bunked by gender identity (but historically only a few nonbinary campers). CITs live separately with upgraded housing; CITs have their own living area (with A/C). 6–8 per bunk.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Strong waterfront program, big lake – skiing most popular (for riding 5th graders and up). No pool. Lake-based aquatics (swim, canoe, kayak, etc.).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Electives and bunk-based: Younger campers: 3 electives; Middle: 4 electives; Older: 6 electives (greater variety, including sports). Israeli dance offered after Shabbat and as an elective. CITs and Israel trip programs available for older campers. Bunks and electives chosen before arrival.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Spirited Shabbat with guitar/keyboard music. Arts & crafts, photography, videography. Israeli dance. High & low ropes course. Overnight trips on request. Israel trip for rising 11th graders. CIT leadership training (solid, structured track).



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health Center: 3 nurses + 1 doctor on site. Can support allergies, medications, diabetes, asthma, and mild autism (depending on social skills). Does not accept kids with more complex special needs. Kosher-style, family style meals; spirited mealtime environment.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Culturally Jewish, spirited but not heavily religious. Shabbat services filled with music and song. Boys and girls are integrated in dining and activities. Generally sportier and more active vibe than sibling camp Tel Noar. Camp Tevya offers a spirited, sporty Jewish camp experience. It is larger than its sibling camp Tel Noar, with a structured program and an energetic feel.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Location: New Hampshire (transportation from Boston Logan). CITs and Israel trip require additional cost/logistics. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Tevya offers a spirited, sporty Jewish camp experience. It is larger than its sibling camp Tel Noar, with a structured program and an energetic feel. Shabbat services are strong and spirited, filled with music and singing, and the broad range of electives allows campers to balance both structure and independence. Ideal for families seeking a co-ed, athletic, Jewish camp that is structured and traditional, Judaically similar to Eisner but more campy than Eisner, which feels more institutional.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - A well-run, spirited co-ed Jewish camp with strong traditions.
  - More sporty and active vibe than Tel Noar, which may appeal to athletic campers.
  - A reliable recommendation, though better suited for kids who thrive in a busier, sportier environment rather than those seeking a laid-back pace.
  - Strong waterfront program, big lake – skiing most popular (for riding 5th graders and up).
  - With a strong retention rate of 93%, Camp Tevya has a long-standing reputation for tradition and community.



#### Lowlights

  - Does not accept kids with more complex special needs.
  - No pool.
  - CITs and Israel trip require additional cost/logistics.



#### Similar Camps

  - Tel Noar
  - Eisner



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Tevya is a co-ed Jewish overnight camp in New Hampshire, founded in 1940. It is culturally Jewish but not overly religious, welcoming campers who are rising 3rd–10th graders, with CITs being rising seniors and an Israel trip offered for rising 11th graders. Sessions run for 3.5 weeks, with a full-summer option available, and the camp enrolls about 300 campers per session, making it larger than its sister camp, Tel Noar. With a strong retention rate of 93%, Camp Tevya has a long-standing reputation for tradition and community. • Director: Lauren • Campers: Mostly from Massachusetts, some from NY/NJ, plus a handful of Israeli campers • Staff: Mostly 18–21 years old; It is larger than its sibling camp Tel Noar, with a structured program and an energetic feel. title: Winnaukee — Carolina — 8-25-25
text:
# Winnaukee



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Winaukee is a boys’ camp in New Hampshire with about 125 campers on the main campus and an additional 15 boys in the Island program for grades 7–9, accessible only by boat and run as a separate program with shared community-wide events. Campers choose weekly electives from a mix of land and water sports, arts, and activities. Sessions of 3, 4, or 7 weeks, though most stay 3–4 weeks or the full summer. Offer intro 2 weeks for up to 6th graders, only first summer. The camp draws heavily from CT, NJ, and NY (around 30%) as well as international campers from Asia, Germany, Spain, and France. Winaukee is owned by Camp Group.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: Mix of sporty and mainstream boys; Not a fit for boys uninterested in sports. Learn teamwork and resilience, especially in areas they are not naturally strong at. Diverse socioeconomically and religiously (approx. 20% Jewish with Friday/Saturday services). Staff: 20% female staff (often support younger boys). Mostly supportive staff; many are international. Cabin groups of 5–7 boys with strong counselor presence. Some concern about lack of strong male role models in leadership (main campus). Director/Leadership: Matt, Ashley, and Puff lead the camp. Leadership appears to be in transition, and the camp could benefit from stronger male role models and more consistent oversight.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins of max 7 boys (goal is 5) in Main Campus, overlooking the lake (very close). Island program cabins larger - 10 kids + 3 counselors. Shower houses and centralized facilities.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Very complete waterfront: swimming, sailing, canoeing, waterski, wakeboard, even snorkeling. Island program has a strong water sports focus.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Main Island: Morning: 3 assigned/structured activity periods, Afternoon: 2 elective choice periods. The island is full choice, more sports-centric with 7 periods per day in total. AM for practice, PM for playing/competing. Electives chosen weekly. Sports (land and water) dominate daily schedule. Land sports: basketball, baseball, soccer, tennis, lacrosse. Arts: woodshop, arts & crafts, animation, ceramics. Intensive programs (e.g., golf, tennis) available for an additional fee. Ice hockey and squash offered once a week for an additional fee. No camp out.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Island seen as “rite of passage” — milestone for older campers. Intercamp tournaments and competitions with other camps. Socials with Robindel and Wicosuta. Island has pickleball courts. Gym with indoor basketball court. Community events bring together Island and Main Camp.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Healthcare center on-site: 2 RNs, 1 Nurse, virtual ped (one in main campus and another at the Island). Hospital 15 mins away. Nut-aware (not specified as fully peanut-free). Meals served family-style.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Competitive, sporty, energetic atmosphere. Resiliency and teamwork emphasized, but can feel very sports-driven. Jewish presence (20%) but religiously diverse. Friday night service and campfire; offer mass service as option too. Leadership: older boys run services on Friday (15 min prayers, challah and grape juice). Community events bring together Island and Main Camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Pick-up/drop-off: Boston, Manchester, and nearby NY/CT/NJ areas. Parents can communicate by email/letters. Extra fees for intensive instruction in golf, tennis, hockey; ice hockey and squash offered once a week for an additional fee. Tech: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Winaukee offers sports-driven boys’ camp experience with a unique Island program designed as a rite of passage for older campers. While the Island concept is strong in theory, in practice it currently feels underdeveloped, lacking structure and modernization. Leadership appears to be in transition, and the camp could benefit from stronger male role models and more consistent oversight. At this stage, it may be a camp to revisit in the future rather than a top recommendation today.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Island program as rite of passage
  - very strong sports and waterfront
  - resilient culture
  - community-building events



#### Lowlights

  - Not ideal for boys uninterested in sports
  - concern about lack of strong male role models
  - low number of campers



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Campers choose weekly electives from a mix of land and water sports, arts, and activities, with sessions of 3, 4, or 7 weeks, though most stay 3–4 weeks or the full summer. Offer intro 2 weeks for up to 6th graders, only first summer. The camp draws heavily from CT, NJ, and NY (around 30%) as well as international campers from Asia, Germany, Spain, and France. Cabin groups of 5–7 boys with strong counselor presence. title: Pembroke — Carolina — 8-22-25
text:
# Pembroke



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Pembroke is a Jewish all-girls overnight camp in Massachusetts, located about 45 minutes to an hour from Logan Airport. It welcomes rising 2nd–10th graders and offers sessions of 3.5 weeks, 5.5 weeks (full summer), or a shorter 2-week option during the second session for younger campers (rising 2nd–5th grade).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp Pembroke is led by Director Amy and has a staffing model that includes not only 18–22-year-old counselors but also “real adults” such as psychologists, teachers, and social workers. The staff team is diverse, with counselors from the U.S. as well as abroad, including Israel, England, Spain, and Mexico. About 70% of the campers come from the Boston area, though the camp also welcomes a few non-Jewish campers, adding to its inclusivity. The camp hosts around 312 campers each summer, with 247 enrolled in the first session of 2025 and 227 in the second, and maintains an impressive 94% retention rate.



## 3 · Living Quarters

• Bunks house 10–16 campers with 3–4 counselors
• Campers are bunked by grade (rising 2nd–10th)
• Showers and bathrooms located inside the bunks



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Nice waterfront with all water sports. Swimming lessons for grades 2–10. Pool on site. 4 lifeguards.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

• 7 activity periods/day
• Mix of assigned and elective activities; electives run 3x per week
• Fridays: 5 activities, day ends early for Shabbat
• Elective time increases with age:
  o Lower campers: 1/3 electives
  o Middle campers: 50% electives
  o Upper campers: 75% electives



## 6 · Program Highlights

• Arts & crafts
• Animal care (ducks, goats)
• Musical theater, yoga, gymnastics, sports
• Ropes and ninja course (expanding)
• Weekly video & blog updates created by campers in media elective
• Israeli dance and Jewish values classes
• Trips: 2x per session (including overnights)
• Older campers: Israel trip with other Cohen Camps
• Social opportunities with other camps provide variety



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

• Health center: 4 nurses, 20 minutes to hospital
• Can accommodate Type 1 diabetes (with limitations/monitoring)
• 24/7 on-site security
• Kosher food (no mashgiach)
• Gluten-free, dairy-free, and other special diets accommodated
• Separate food prep areas
• Canteen twice a week (snacks/candy)



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Down-to-earth, inclusive, and non-cliquey. Girls focus on themselves — no pressure about boys, makeup, or appearance. Encouraged to be the “best version” of themselves. Jewish identity present but accessible (Shabbat, Israel culture, Jewish values). Strong Jewish values and spirited Shabbat services create meaningful connections. Balance of tradition and choice.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

• No chaperone from Florida
• Airport pickup from Boston Logan (45 mins–1 hr)
• Parent communication: Mail 3x/week; can receive emails but not send
• Weekly laundry service
• Blog & videos for updates
• Tech policy: Unknown
• Extra costs: Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Pembroke is an all-girls Jewish camp with a balance of tradition and choice, where campers enjoy a wide range of activities — from animal care to Israeli dance — while shaping their own schedules through electives. Strong Jewish values and spirited Shabbat services create meaningful connections, while social opportunities with other camps provide variety. Ideal for families seeking a nurturing, down-to-earth camp where girls can focus on themselves, build resilience, and return home more confident and self-assured. Cute camp with a strong girls-only identity — excellent alternative for families specifically seeking an all-girls environment.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Cute camp with a strong girls-only identity — excellent alternative for families specifically seeking an all-girls environment.
  - This is the only all girls Jewish camp we have.
  - A very nice camp, AND excellent Judaics.
  - Good installations, rustic but solid.
  - Great Jewish values integration without being overly rigid.
  - Confident we could comfortably recommend and send families there.
  - Particularly good for well-rounded girls. Most of campers tend to be in competitive sports elsewhere, and here they can relax, enjoy time with their friends, and experience camp without pressure.



#### Lowlights

  - Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

It welcomes rising 2nd–10th graders and offers sessions of 3.5 weeks, 5.5 weeks (full summer), or a shorter 2-week option during the second session for younger campers (rising 2nd–5th grade). Camp Pembroke is led by Director Amy and has a staffing model that includes not only 18–22-year-old counselors but also “real adults” such as psychologists, teachers, and social workers. The staff team is diverse, with counselors from the U.S. as well as abroad, including Israel, England, Spain, and Mexico. About 70% of the campers come from the Boston area, though the camp also welcomes a few non-Jewish campers, adding to its inclusivity. The camp hosts around 312 campers each summer, with 247 enrolled in the first session of 2025 and 227 in the second, and maintains an impressive 94% retention rate. • Bunks house 10–16 campers with 3–4 counselors • Campers are bunked by grade (rising 2nd–10th) • Showers and bathrooms located inside the bunks Nice waterfront with all water sports • Swimming lessons for grades 2–10 • Pool on site • 4 lifeguards • 7 activity periods/day • Mix of assigned and elective activities; electives run 3x per week • Fridays: 5 activities, day ends early for Shabbat • Elective time increases with age: o Lower campers: 1/3 electives o Middle campers: 50% electives o Upper campers: 75% electives • Arts & crafts • Animal care (ducks, goats) • Musical theater, yoga, gymnastics, sports • Ropes and ninja course (expanding) - Weekly video & blog updates created by campers in media elective • Israeli dance and Jewish values classes • Trips: 2x per session (including overnights) • Older campers: Israel trip with other Cohen Camps • Health center: 4 nurses, 20 minutes to hospital • Can accommodate Type 1 diabetes (with limitations/monitoring) • 24/7 on-site security • Kosher food (no mashgiach) • Gluten-free, dairy-free, and other special diets accommodated • Separate food prep areas • Canteen twice a week (snacks/candy) • Down-to-earth, inclusive, and non-cliquey • Girls focus on themselves — no pressure about boys, makeup, or appearance • Encouraged to be the “best version” of themselves • Jewish identity present but accessible (Shabbat, Israel culture, Jewish values) • No chaperone from Florida • Airport pickup from Boston Logan (45 mins–1 hr) • Parent communication: Mail 3x/week, can receive emails but not send • Weekly laundry service • Blog & videos for updates Camp Pembroke is an all-girls Jewish camp with a balance of tradition and choice, campers enjoy a wide range of activities — from animal care to Israeli dance — while shaping their own schedules through electives. Strong Jewish values and spirited Shabbat services create meaningful connections, while social opportunities with other camps provide variety. Ideal for families seeking a nurturing, down-to-earth camp where girls can focus on themselves, build resilience, and return home more confident and self-assured. • Cute camp with a strong girls-only identity — excellent alternative for families specifically seeking an all-girls environment. this is the only all girls Jewish camp we have. A very nice camp, AND excellent Judaics • Good installations, rustic but solid. • Great Jewish values integration without being overly rigid. • Confident we could comfortably recommend and send families there. • Particularly good for well-rounded girls. Most of campers tend to be in competitive sports elsewhere, and here they can relax, enjoy time with their friends, and experience camp without pressure.
