title: Robindel — Monica — 8-25-25
text:
# Robindel



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Robindel is a traditional all-girls camp located on Lake Winnipesaukee, New Hampshire, offering a full summer program only (6.5 weeks). Kindergarten and 1st graders may attend a two-week “taster” session. Enrollment is currently around 185 campers, with plans to grow to 250. Founded in 1951, the camp was taken over in 2021 by Cory and Jolly, two longtime friends whose partnership and female leadership are deeply felt throughout camp. Robindel’s campus is manicured and filled with flowers, with a uniformed look that contributes to its organized, polished aesthetic.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Most campers come from the Tri-State area (NY, NJ, CT), with strong representation from Florida, DC, California, Oregon, Washington, and Texas. International campers join from Mexico, Spain, and Peru, staying for the full summer. Robindel is known for starting girls young, with 21 campers finishing 1st grade enrolled in 2024. The camp accepts new campers up through age 13, with occasional spots for older girls (11+). Cory and Jolly bring different strengths: Cory is approachable and warm, while Jolly, an experienced educator with time living in France, infuses camp culture with thoughtful teaching and global perspective. The average counselor age is 24, and many are “homegrown” alumni. Staff undergo two full weeks of training, with 8 staff for every 12 campers in younger cabins. Some male staff are present. Camper return rate is exceptionally high (99.2%).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins are age-grouped, with two cabins per age group. Cabins feature clean, modern bathrooms, and towel service is provided. Beds are assigned before arrival, supporting structure and community. The dining hall—round, cheery, and air-conditioned, with assigned tables by age. The physical campus is manicured and beautiful, filled with flowers.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Robindel’s waterfront features three ski boats and a pontoon boat, plus a zipline into the lake. All campers participate in waterfront programming, including structured swim, waterskiing, sailing, and canoeing. A weekly Sunday campfire takes place by the lake, where the entire camp sings and dances together.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Younger campers follow a dictated schedule designed so that everyone tries everything. As girls grow older, choice expands, empowering them to focus on activities they love. Activities include sports, arts, aquatics, outdoor adventure, and community service. Special weekly traditions include two cookouts, gardening, and blueberry picking.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports: everyone tries everything. Waterfront: Ski boats, pontoon boating, sailing, canoeing, swimming, zipline into the lake. Arts & Culture: Theater with two shows each summer, dance, visual arts. Nature & Community: Gardening, blueberry picking, Sunday campfires. Extras: Horseback riding (2–4 times weekly, extra fee). Traditions: Girls make challah for Friday Shabbat; daily gardening; singing the “Johnny Appleseed” song in the dining hall.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Robindel maintains a good health program with one doctor and three nurses on site, and the nearest hospital 30 minutes away. Allergies are accommodated, with protocols in place for safety and inclusion. The kitchen is overseen by a Cordon Bleu–trained chef, offering diverse menus and in-house bakery items. Meals are served family-style with lots of options, and food is a point of pride, with campers actively engaged in discussions around meals. Peanut butter is served, but allergy protocols are strictly enforced.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Robindel’s culture is deeply values-driven and emphasizes action: campers are encouraged to focus on their “to be” list rather than their “to do” list. The camp explicitly teaches kindness, courage, and reflective living through intentional conversations and programming. Friday night Shabbat is celebrated by all campers, regardless of background, with singing, and dancing. The camp’s female leadership is powerful and visible, setting the tone for empowerment and connection. With structured traditions, a polished environment, and intentional educator-led guidance, Robindel fosters growth, character, and confidence.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Chaperoned flights are offered from Miami, Baltimore, Philadelphia, and San Diego. Families have three scheduled phone calls per summer and can use a one-way email system to send notes to campers. Laundry and towel service are included in tuition. Horseback riding is offered at additional cost. No CIT or JC program. Sent from my iPhone



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Robindel is ideal for girls seeking a structured, nurturing, and values-driven community led by powerful female role models. The polished campus, thoughtful programming, and emphasis on character development make it especially appealing to families looking for more than just activities. Campers benefit from a mix of tradition, fun, and growth—whether through Shabbat challah baking, blueberry picking, or theater productions. Robindel is a strong choice for families who value both excellence and intentionality in a full-summer camp experience.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong female leadership
  - polished, manicured campus
  - exceptional food program with in-house bakery
  - values-based curriculum
  - outstanding leadership
  - chaperoned flights from multiple cities
  - vibrant Shabbat and Sunday campfire traditions



#### Lowlights

  - No CIT/JC program
  - limited sports emphasis for younger campers
  - full-summer requirement may not work for all families



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Kenwood Evergreen — Monica — 8-25-25
text:
# Kenwood Evergreen



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Kenwood & Evergreen are brother-sister camps founded in 1930, located in Wilmot, New Hampshire. With an “invisible line” separating the two campuses, the programs offer single-gender living and instruction with coed events and shared traditions. Each camp serves approximately 150 campers, ages 8–15, with session lengths of 3 weeks, 3.5 weeks, and full-summer. Tuition ranges from $9,500 to $17,500 depending on session. The camps are ACA-accredited. Longtime director Scott Brody is stepping down, and leadership is transitioning to Jack Schott and Ali Brockman, who are now the faces of the next chapter of Kenwood & Evergreen.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

In 2024, Kenwood & Evergreen hosted roughly 300 campers total (evenly split by gender), with a 91% return rate. Most campers come from the Northeast (NY, NJ, MA, CT), with international campers from England, Spain, Australia, and China. Staff retention is strong (85%), and many counselors are alumni. The Junior Counselor (JC) program is for rising 12th graders who have skipped a summer after 10th grade; all are returning campers. The camp community is intentionally low-key and supportive, and the incoming directors—Jack Schott and Ali Brockman—are committed to maintaining that philosophy while leading the camp forward.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins at both Kenwood and Evergreen are well-maintained and organized into clusters of 4 cabins, each with 6–8 campers and 2–3 counselors. Clusters include shared spaces such as lounges and a dedicated dance room, encouraging group bonding. Bathrooms are clean and shared, and laundry is done weekly. Cabins are mixed by full- and half-summer campers, supporting new friendships. Recent upgrades across the campus have enhanced comfort and community life.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The camps share a spring-fed private lake used for tubing, water skiing, canoeing, and swim instruction. Campers complete a swim assessment on arrival. Swim lessons are mandatory 2–3 times a week, and all waterfront activities are staffed by certified lifeguards. Older campers participate in progressively challenging trips, including canoeing and whitewater rafting. The lake is clean and warm in mid-summer, and waterfront access is built into the regular schedule.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers follow a structured six-period day, with an even balance between assigned cabin activities and electives (approx. 50/50). Mornings typically include swim, sports, or arts; afternoons focus on camper choice. Evenings feature campfires, socials, talent shows, and theatrical productions. Evergreen traditions include milk and cookies after rest hour and blueberry picking. The program offers a steady rhythm with some flexibility as campers age.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Kenwood & Evergreen offer a well-rounded program with: Sports: Soccer, tennis, basketball, archery, and intercamp games with Walt Whitman, Wicosuta, and Robindel. Arts: Professional-level ceramics studio (longtime instructor of 30+ years), RISD-led woodshop, theater productions, music festivals. Outdoor Adventure: Day trips to Dartmouth and local beaches, hikes, canoe trips, whitewater rafting, island campouts, and amusement park visits. Traditions: Friday Shabbat services, all-camp events, and coed evening programs. Extras: Firetruck spray day (a camper favorite) adds whimsy and fun. The program is intentionally low-key and not designed for ultra-competitive athletes. It is best for campers who enjoy a mix of physical and creative pursuits. Campers who want only sports or no sports at all will not find the right fit here.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The camp has 4 full-time nurses and 1 on-site doctor. The nearest hospital (Dartmouth Medical Center) is about 45 minutes away. A separate allergy kitchen handles serious dietary needs with care and attention. Meals are served family-style and include pork; vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free options are available. The dining hall is loud, lively, and full of singing. Security includes a monitored entrance and camera system.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Kenwood & Evergreen are welcoming, relationship-focused camps with a non-competitive spirit and strong community bonds. The environment supports quirky, introspective, and kind-hearted campers who may not thrive in louder, high-octane programs. There’s a high sense of belonging, and most 13–15-year-olds are returning campers, making the upper division feel tight-knit. Jewish traditions are woven in—like Friday night Shabbat—but handled in a casual and inclusive way. Counselors are visible, engaged, and form strong connections with their campers. While the community is strongly gendered, the camp has stated that it will welcome transgender campers who seek that structure, though they note it may not be the right fit for every family.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation is provided via bus from Boston, Westchester, and Manhattan. A chaperoned flight is available from Florida. Campers send handwritten letters home weekly. Emails and phone calls are reserved for emergencies. Photos are posted daily. Cellphones are not permitted. Laundry is included.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kenwood & Evergreen are best suited for families seeking a nurturing, well-balanced program that blends tradition, creativity, and adventure in a warm, low-pressure environment. The camps’ structure fosters meaningful friendships and steady personal growth across a wide range of activities. Not ideal for “alpha” personalities or campers looking for intense specialization, but a great fit for quirky, reflective kids who enjoy community and variety. A perfect choice for families who value relationship-based camps that are structured but not rigid.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Friendly environment
  - Camp traditions like firetruck day, blueberry picking, and cookies & milk give warmth and charm.
  - Thoughtful JC model and strong leadership transition underway.



#### Lowlights

  - Expensive.



#### Similar Camps

  - Walt Whitman
  - Wicosuta
  - Robindel

title: Wicosuta — Monica — 8-25-25
text:
# Wicosuta



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Wicosuta is a traditional all-girls sleepaway camp located in Hebron, New Hampshire, with a 105-year legacy of empowering girls through structure, spirit, and community. The camp serves rising 2nd through 9th graders and operates two 3.5-week sessions with no full-summer option. Each session hosts around 295 campers. Wicosuta is ACA-accredited, non-denominational, and owned and directed by Corey and Justin Dockswell, who bring decades of combined camp leadership and are actively involved in daily operations.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Each session welcomes 295 girls, with a strong return rate and continuity through 8th grade. Campers come primarily from NY, NJ, CT, Boston, Philadelphia, DC, Florida, and California, with smaller international cohorts from countries such as China and Spain. Staff retention is strong at 82%, with many specialists returning year after year. Campers are grouped by age into younger, middle, and older divisions, each with increasing levels of choice. Corey and Justin Dockswell are hands-on, approachable, and deeply invested in camper development.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins house 6–15 girls with 2–4 counselors, grouped by age division. All bunks have bathrooms attended to daily by cleaning staff, and laundry is provided weekly. New campers are thoughtfully welcomed into the community through symbolic rituals like candle-lighting, helping them feel embraced from day one.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Wicosuta sits on a private lake with four ski boats and a variety of non-motorized craft. Activities include sailing, tubing, kayaking, paddleboarding, and structured swim lessons in both the lake and a heated pool. Lifejackets are mandatory, and swim assessments are conducted on arrival. A negative aspect is that campers walk past another camp to reach the waterfront.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Wicosuta runs on a highly structured schedule designed to maximize participation. Younger campers have one elective per day, middle campers enjoy 50% choice, and older campers select their full schedules. Six daily activity periods plus evening programs keep the pace high. Meals are served buffet-style in an energetic dining hall with assigned seating by age, fostering both structure and spirit. Evening activities include talent shows, flash mobs, and campfires.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Land Sports: Soccer, volleyball, gymnastics (privates available), tennis. Waterfront: Waterskiing, tubing, sailing, canoeing, structured swim. Creative Arts: Ceramics, jewelry-making, photography, art barn, dance, theater productions. Adventure: Ropes course, hiking, camping overnights, trampolining, outdoor survival skills. Trips & Events: Weekly off-camp trips to water parks and attractions; "try-it" sessions for younger campers; spirited all-camp events such as Color War and themed days.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Wicosuta is well-staffed with nurses and has on-call physicians nearby. Allergies and dietary restrictions are well-supported, including vegetarian, dairy-free, gluten-free, and kosher-style options. Meals are inclusive and varied, balancing kid-friendly with healthy. Social-emotional specialists provide additional camper support, and individualized health protocols are in place for conditions like diabetes.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Wico’s culture is high-energy, structured, and spirited—perfect for girls who thrive on activity and engagement. While not officially a Jewish camp, many campers are Jewish, and optional Friday/Saturday Shabbat services attract 30–40 girls weekly. Counselors are enthusiastic and warm, and new campers are quickly integrated into the community through beloved rituals. The dining hall is famously loud and joyful, setting the tone for the camp’s vibrant personality.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation: Coach buses from the New York metro area, Boston, and surrounding regions. Flights: check if they offer escorted flight. Communication: Families receive weekly emails and scheduled calls; campers do not bring phones or electronics. A secure photo feed is updated regularly. Extras: Laundry is included; optional private lessons (e.g., gymnastics, horseback) may have additional costs. Tips are not required.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Wicosuta is best suited for girls who enjoy a structured, high-energy environment with a wide variety of choices. It offers robust programming across sports, arts, and adventure, supported by strong camper care and enthusiastic staff. Welcoming traditions and spirited rituals help new campers adjust quickly and feel at home. Wicosuta is an excellent fit for active, social girls who thrive in a lively all-girls community.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Excellent staff retention and camper care
  - strong variety of programming
  - enthusiastic counselor culture
  - welcoming rituals for new campers
  - energetic dining hall and evening programs



#### Lowlights

  - Limited camper choice at younger ages
  - loud dining hall may overwhelm shy or noise-sensitive girls



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Wicosuta Program Snapshot Camp Wicosuta is a traditional all-girls sleepaway camp located in Hebron, New Hampshire, with a 105-year legacy of empowering girls through structure, spirit, and community. The camp serves rising 2nd through 9th graders and operates two 3.5-week sessions with no full-summer option. Each session hosts around 295 campers. Wicosuta is ACA-accredited, non-denominational, and owned and directed by Corey and Justin Dockswell, who bring decades of combined camp leadership and are actively involved in daily operations. Camper, Staff & Director Profile Each session welcomes 295 girls, with a strong return rate and continuity through 8th grade. Campers come primarily from NY, NJ, CT, Boston, Philadelphia, DC, Florida, and California, with smaller international cohorts from countries such as China and Spain. Staff retention is strong at 82%, with many specialists returning year after year. Campers are grouped by age into younger, middle, and older divisions, each with increasing levels of choice. Corey and Justin Dockswel l are hands-on, approachable, and deeply invested in camper development. Living Quarters Cabins house 6–15 girls with 2–4 counselors, grouped by age division. All bunks have bathrooms attended to daily by cleaning staff, and laundry is provided weekly. New campers are thoughtfully welcomed into the community through symbolic rituals like candle-lighting, helping them feel embraced from day one. Waterfront & Aquatics Wicosuta sits on a private lake with four ski boats and a variety of non-motorized craft. Activities include sailing, tubing, kayaking, paddleboarding, and structured swim lessons in both the lake and a heated pool. Lifejackets are mandatory, and swim assessments are conducted on arrival. A negative aspect is that campers walk past another camp to reach the waterfront. Daily Timetable & Choice Wicosuta runs on a highly structured schedule designed to maximize participation. Younger campers have one elective per day, middle campers enjoy 50% choice, and older campers select their full schedules. Six daily activity periods plus evening programs keep the pace high. Meals are served buffet-style in an energetic dining hall with assigned seating by age, fostering both structure and spirit. Evening activities include talent shows, flash mobs, and campfires. Program Highlights Land Sports: Soccer, volleyball, gymnastics (privates available), tennis Waterfront: Waterskiing, tubing, sailing, canoeing, structured swim Creative Arts: Ceramics, jewelry-making, photography, art barn, dance, theater productions Adventure: Ropes course, hiking, camping overnights, trampolining, outdoor survival skills Trips & Events: Weekly off-camp trips to water parks and attractions; “try-it” sessions for younger campers; spirited all-camp events such as Color War and themed days Health, Safety & Food Wicosuta is well-staffed with nurses and has on-call physicians nearby. Allergies and dietary restrictions are well-supported, including vegetarian, dairy-free, gluten-free, and kosher-style options. Meals are inclusive and varied, balancing kid-friendly with healthy. Social-emotional specialists provide additional camper support, and individualized health protocols are in place for conditions like diabetes. Vibe & Culture Wico’s culture is high-energy, structured, and spirited—perfect for girls who thrive on activity and engagement. While not officially a Jewish camp, many campers are Jewish, and optional Friday/Saturday Shabbat services attract 30–40 girls weekly. Counselors are enthusiastic and warm, and new campers are quickly integrated into the community through beloved rituals. The dining hall is famously loud and joyful, setting the tone for the camp’s vibrant personality. Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs Transportation: Coach buses from the New York metro area, Boston, and surrounding regions. Flights: check if they offer escorted flight. Communication: Families receive weekly emails and scheduled calls; campers do not bring phones or electronics. A secure photo feed is updated regularly. Extras: Laundry is included; optional private lessons (e.g., gymnastics, horseback) may have additional costs. Tips are not required. Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary Camp Wicosuta is best suited for girls who enjoy a structured, high-energy environment with a wide variety of choices. It offers robust programming across sports, arts, and adventure, supported by strong camper care and enthusiastic staff. Welcoming traditions and spirited rituals help new campers adjust quickly and feel at home. Wicosuta is an excellent fit for active, social girls who thrive in a lively all-girls community. Internal Insights (Highlights & Lowlights) Highlights: Excellent staff retention and camper care; strong variety of programming; enthusiastic counselor culture; welcoming rituals for new campers; energetic dining hall and evening programs. Lowlights: Limited camper choice at younger ages; loud dining hall may overwhelm shy or noise-sensitive girls.

title: Tel Noar — Monica — 8-25-25
text:
# Tel Noar



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Tel Noar is a warm, Jewish values-driven sleepaway camp with over 80 years of tradition. Located in New Hampshire, it is intentionally smaller and more intimate than many larger camps, fostering a close-knit community. The camp runs two 3.5-week sessions (typically filled to capacity with around 160 campers each) and also offers a 2-week introductory option for younger campers up to 7th grade. Tel Noar emphasizes Jewish identity, learning, and traditions while balancing fun, growth, and meaningful community experiences.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp Tel Noar is directed by Aaron, who is known for his approachable leadership and personal attention to each camper. Many counselors are alumni, creating a multigenerational family feel, and the camp benefits from a strong alumni base that sustains its culture. Campers come from across the U.S.—including New Hampshire, DC, Florida, California, and Texas—as well as internationally, with a notable presence from Israel. Leadership progression is built into the program: rising 11th graders travel to Israel, and rising 12th graders serve as Junior Counselors (JCs). The youngest counselors are entering college, providing relatable role models for campers.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins are simple and age-appropriate, with newly renovated bathrooms providing comfort. Each cabin houses 8–15 campers with counselors living alongside them. Weekly laundry is provided, and the smaller camp size ensures individualized attention and stronger bonds within age groups.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Tel Noar offers a full aquatics program, including motorized water sports as well as sailing, canoeing, and swimming. Campers undergo swim checks and safety is emphasized. The waterfront is a central feature of the camp’s activity schedule and helps anchor the program’s balance of tradition and fun.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Camp Tel Noar runs a structured schedule with gradually increasing choice as campers grow older. Younger campers are guided through a balanced program, while older campers gain more autonomy in shaping their days. Activities span land sports, arts, outdoor adventure, and aquatics. Evening programs, campfires, and theme days add variety and energy to camp life.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Aquatics: Swimming, sailing, canoeing, motorized water sports. Arts: Drama, music, photography (including darkroom). Adventure: Rafting, hiking, camping trips, ropes courses (with expansion plans). Traditions: Weekly BBQ Thursdays, omelette day, Shabbat singing and dancing (even in the pool). Leadership: Rising 11th graders travel to Israel; rising 12th graders become JCs. Jewish life is central to the Tel Noar experience. Campers participate in morning Hatuga (flag-raising with reflection) and Shira (music) daily. Services are instrumental and inclusive of both Reform and Conservative practices. Shabbat is celebrated joyfully, with spirited singing, dancing, and special traditions. Jewish learning is interactive and values-based, ensuring that it feels relevant and engaging to campers.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Camp Tel Noar offers a dedicated wellness space and Camper Care Specialists who support emotional wellbeing. The small size of the camp ensures that first-time campers are quickly integrated and supported. Health staff accommodate allergies and dietary needs, reinforcing the camp’s inclusive and supportive culture. The camp features a beautiful dining hall where family-style meals are shared, with 8–9 campers per table. All food is kosher, and Hamotzi and Birkat Hamazon are recited at every meal. Meals are camper-friendly, with accommodations for allergies and picky eaters. Camp traditions such as BBQ Thursdays and omelette day add to the sense of routine and joy in dining.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp Tel Noar is known for its family-like community, Jewish traditions, and strong sense of belonging. The culture emphasizes fun, spirit, and growth, while grounding campers in Jewish values and identity. Alumni, staff, and families reinforce continuity, making the camp multigenerational. With its traditions, song sessions, holiday observances, and interactive programming, Tel Noar fosters both a joyful summer and a lasting Jewish connection.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Camp provides airport pickups for international campers and coordinated travel for U.S. families. Parent communication includes updates and a structured photo-sharing system. Laundry is included in tuition, and costs are transparent, with no significant hidden fees. Optional extras may include specialized trips or lessons.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Tel Noar is ideal for families seeking a smaller, intimate camp community grounded in Jewish traditions. It balances structure and fun with meaningful Jewish learning and spirited celebrations. Campers benefit from a wide range of activities, supportive staff, and strong leadership pathways through Israel and JC programs. Tel Noar is especially suited for families who value Jewish identity, multigenerational tradition, and a warm, close-knit summer experience.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong Jewish life integrated into daily camp
  - Intimate size fostering community
  - Newly renovated bathrooms
  - Multigenerational alumni connections
  - Meaningful leadership progression (Israel and JC programs)



#### Lowlights

  - Smaller size may limit breadth of offerings compared to larger camps
  - Families seeking less Jewish emphasis or a more competitive program may find it less aligned with their priorities



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Cardigan Mountain — Monica — 8-25-25
text:
# Cardigan Mountain



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Cardigan Mountain School, founded in 1945, is an all-boys junior boarding school located in Canaan, New Hampshire on a scenic former Dartmouth College estate. The school serves boys in grades 6–9 during the academic year and welcomes both boys and girls (ages 8–15) to its coed summer program. The summer experience combines academic enrichment with outdoor adventure in a culturally rich, international setting. Boarding-only. Tuition for the academic year and summer program varies; financial aid is available, with 35% of students receiving aid. The school is known for its elite prep-school placement and high standards.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

During the academic year, Cardigan enrolls approximately 235 boys from over 20 countries, with a 65/35 domestic-international split. Students benefit from individual academic coaching and structured dorm life. Faculty includes university-affiliated instructors. The summer camp hosts around 155 boys and girls, with a sweet spot of ages 12–13, and draws from a similarly diverse population. The school and camp leadership emphasize mentorship, global citizenship, and personal development. Staff includes licensed teachers and trained outdoor leaders. Directors are deeply involved and uphold a structured, values-based culture year-round.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Dormitories are primarily two-person rooms with shared common spaces and access to campus lounges. 80% of dorms have been renovated in recent years. Bathrooms are shared by floor, cleaned daily. On-campus health services are strong, with a resident nurse and doctor available. Campers live without technology during summer, encouraging bonding and full participation. Laundry service is provided weekly for both school-year and summer students.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The summer program features a comprehensive lakefront with swimming, sailing, kayaking, and canoeing. Swim instruction is required until proficiency is demonstrated. Outdoor staff are certified in lifeguarding and first aid. Whitewater rafting and canoe trips are built into the adventure curriculum. Waterfront activities are well-supervised and take place daily, often paired with outdoor skills or group challenges.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Both the academic and summer programs are highly structured. Summer days have academic electives and physical activities. Students can choose from language classes (Spanish, French, Chinese, Japanese), ESL, and enrichment options (STEM, photography, ceramics, math, and other academic courses). Afternoons and evenings include sports, creative programming, and special events. Weekend trips and theme days (e.g., Halloween in July, Color War) provide variety and fun. Campers are unplugged and follow a daily rhythm that fosters independence and connection.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Academic Year: Over 70 academic offerings with personalized learning plans; academic coaches for students with dyslexia, dysgraphia, ADHD; flexible add/drop week; preparation for elite boarding schools such as Exeter, Andover, Deerfield. Athletics: Competitive soccer, ice hockey, basketball, wrestling, fitness, and tennis. Strong support for serious student-athletes. Summer Camp: Cardboard Boat Regatta, Color War, overnight wilderness trips, and global friendships. Balanced mix of academics, sports, and adventure in a coed format.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

On-campus nurse and resident doctor ensure round-the-clock care during both academic and summer terms. The dining hall provides nutritious meals family-style during the year and buffet-style in summer. Dietary accommodations are available upon request. The campus is gated with staff on duty 24/7. Cardigan maintains a low camper-to-staff ratio and emphasizes safety in both academic and wilderness environments.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Cardigan’s academic-year environment is structured, supportive, and values-driven, with an emphasis on responsibility, honor, and effort. The summer camp retains that structure but shifts the tone to fun, exploration, and connection. Chapel services and shared traditions build a strong sense of community, while the unplugged setting promotes focus and friendships. Families report meaningful personal growth in both academic and summer sessions.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

The campus is easily accessible from Boston, with shuttle and airport service available. Parents receive weekly communication during summer via email, with landline calls allowed after the first five days. Personal technology is not permitted at camp. All communication is through designated channels. Laundry is included. Academic-year tuition is based on grade level and boarding, and the summer program may include modest fees for wilderness trips or special electives. Financial aid is robust, with $270,000 awarded annually.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Cardigan Mountain School is ideal for families seeking a nurturing, academically focused all-boys boarding program that lays the foundation for top secondary school admissions. Its summer camp blends academic enrichment with outdoor adventure in a globally diverse setting. Campers grow socially, intellectually, and emotionally in a structured, low-tech environment. The program is a strong fit for motivated middle school boys during the year and for curious, globally-minded campers ages 8–15 in the summer.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Exceptional academic coaching and teacher quality.
  - Elite sports offerings with state-of-the-art facilities.
  - Thoughtful international mix creates a global peer group.
  - Summer culture is vibrant, with unique camp events and reflection-based community building.



#### Lowlights

  - During the school year, middle school-only boarding may limit fit for families seeking longer-term or coed placements.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Wa-Klo- Monica — Sam — 8-25-25
text:
# Wa-Klo- Monica



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Waklo is a warm, values-driven all-girls camp located in Kingswood, New Hampshire, welcoming girls through high school and inclusive of transgender campers. Founded over 75 years ago, Waklo emphasizes personal growth, community, and character over competition. The camp runs a fully elective program aside from required swim and features rustic living, a strong nature component, and a values-based culture. Waklo is ACA-accredited and uniform-required. Tuition is tiered by age and session length, with over $250,000 in scholarships awarded annually to promote accessibility.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Waklo enrolls approximately 150 girls each summer, with a strong 79% return rate. Most campers come from the Northeast (NY, NJ, CT, MA), with about 10% international, including many from Latin America. The camp is led by a values-oriented director known for being present and connected to camp life. Staff highlight includes "Sunshine," an outdoor/nature leader whose earthy, passionate style embodies Waklo’s culture. Leadership opportunities include the LIT program for 16-year-olds (with limited phone use) and a leadership training program for 17-year-olds, open even to first-time campers for a single session.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Campers live in rustic cabins organized by age group, fostering close bunk communities. Bathrooms are shared and cleaned regularly, and laundry is done weekly. A unique tradition is the "handwashing canoe" outside the dining hall—introduced during COVID and now part of the daily routine. The uniform policy fosters equality and minimizes social pressure around clothing.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The camp has a private lake with waterskiing, windsurfing, kayaking, and canoeing. Swim lessons are required until proficiency is demonstrated, and all campers complete a swim assessment on arrival. Lifejackets are mandatory for all water activities. Waterfront choices are seamlessly integrated into the elective program, so all campers can participate at their own level.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Waklo operates on a fully elective schedule aside from swim requirements, allowing campers to design their day across creative arts, land sports, waterfront, and animal care. The camp is intentionally reflective: each meal begins with a quote of the day, and Sunday nights feature all-camp reflections on values and character growth. Halloween is celebrated with homemade costumes by cabin, emphasizing creativity, collaboration, and community.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Waterfront: Waterskiing, windsurfing, swimming, canoeing, kayaking
Sports: Pickleball, tennis, archery (field & target), basic gymnastics
Arts & Drama: Visual arts, crafts, dance, theater, bead necklace traditions tied to character milestones
Leadership: LIT program (16); leadership training (17, including first-timers)
Special Traditions: Pioneer Project for older campers, 9th-grade trip to Martha’s Vineyard, Halloween in cabins, daily dining hall quotes, Sunday reflections
Animals: Horse and pet care included in tuition; campers help feed and care for animals as electives



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

An on-site wellness team includes nurses with access to local doctors. Meals are served family-style in a cozy dining hall, beginning with a daily quote. Food is balanced and kid-friendly, with accommodations for allergies and picky eaters. Counselors are trained in restorative justice and conflict resolution, reinforcing Waklo’s emphasis on wellbeing.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Waklo is unplugged, non-competitive, and deeply values-driven. The camp explicitly teaches restorative justice as a life skill, helping campers resolve conflicts constructively and respectfully. This fosters a safe community where girls feel free to be themselves without social pressure. Gratitude, inclusion, and connection are prioritized over awards or performance.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation is offered from Boston and New York. Leadership campers (LITs only) may use cell phones one hour per day. Families can send weekly emails, and campers can call home after the first five days. Daily photos are posted on a secure platform. Laundry and all electives—including animal care—are included in tuition, with no hidden fees.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Waklo is ideal for girls who thrive in nurturing, reflective, and non-competitive environments. Its unplugged setting and emphasis on restorative justice foster deep friendships and personal growth. Campers build confidence through elective-driven programming, unique traditions, and inclusive leadership opportunities. Waklo is a perfect match for families seeking a summer community where values matter as much as activities.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Bead tradition fosters self-esteem
  - Thoughtful nature and animal programs
  - Creative Halloween and Sunday reflection rituals
  - Inclusive leadership opportunities
  - Strong scholarship commitment
  - Highly present director



#### Lowlights

  - Rustic living and slower-paced culture may not appeal to campers who prefer high-energy, competitive environments.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

The camp runs a fully elective program aside from required swim and features rustic living, a strong nature component, and a values-based culture. Waklo is ACA-accredited and uniform-required. Tuition is tiered by age and session length, with over $250,000 in scholarships awarded annually to promote accessibility. The camp is intentionally reflective: each meal begins with a quote of the day, and Sunday nights feature all-camp reflections on values and character growth. title: Pemigewassett — Emily — 8-21-25
text:
# Pemigewassett



## 1 · Program Snapshot

About 175 boys at camp. 2nd session is more popular. About 60 boys stay for 7 weeks. Session choices are 3, 4 or 7. They will do an Intro 2-week session for 8–10-year-olds with option to extend. Limited availability.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ken, the Director toured Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin. Pemi was founded in 1908. Family owned. Has a board that Kenny reports to. Have some international campers as well as campers from the Midwest especially Cleveland where Kenny is from. 75% of counselors are Pemi alumni. There are division heads for each age group and coaches for each activity. Coaches also live in the bunks.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks are very rustic along the lake. About 8 kids per bunk. Not enclosed. Showers and bathrooms outside of bunks in shower house.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Pemi owns the lake and 650 acres around the camp. Have sailing, canoeing, paddle boarding and motorized water sports in a different section.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Kids choose their weekly activities. Free period in the afternoon. There are 20 activity choices per slot. They can switch choices after a week.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities include outdoor adventure, hiking, all sports (basketball, tennis, soccer, lacrosse), music, archery, fishing, woodworking, etc. Have a science center to study rocks and insects. Also have a quieter library building where kids can play games, read, chess etc. Play intercamps against Moosilauke, Kingswood etc. Don’t have to try out. Camp offers many hiking and overnight trips. Some required, some optional. 16-year-old boys go out west for backpacking and canoeing trip to Montana. At 17 they can be assistant counselors. They have Pemi week which is their version of color war.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

On a private road, no gates or security but there is a camera on the bridge to get into camp. Dining hall is nice and whole camp eats together and boys sit by cabin group and sometimes mix it up. There are flags from all the countries counselors and campers come from. They can handle vegan, gluten free and food allergy. Camp is nut free. Pemi has a songbook and piano in dining hall to sing camp songs. Older boys are waiters who get paid.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall, Pemi seems to be both a sporty and outdoorsy type of camp. The boys all looked happy and busy. Ken was very engaged with the kids. The bunks are VERY rustic which may not be okay for every family. The dining hall is nice and the grounds looked well kept. The counselors were playing with the kids and engaged.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses come from New York and CT at beginning and end of camp, not mid-session. Pick up from Logan and Manchester airports. If a family doesn’t get to tour camp, Ken does a once-a-month Open House webinar. Shows a video of camp and answers questions. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Overall, Pemi seems to be both a sporty and outdoorsy type of camp. The boys all looked happy and busy, and the counselors were playing with the kids and engaged. Ken was very engaged with the kids. The bunks are VERY rustic which may not be okay for every family; the dining hall is nice and the grounds looked well kept.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - About 175 boys at camp.
  - 75% of counselors are Pemi alumni.
  - Kids choose their weekly activities (20 activity choices per slot; free period).
  - Pemi owns the lake and 650 acres around the camp.
  - Activities include outdoor adventure, hiking, all sports, music, archery, fishing, woodworking, etc.
  - 16-year-old boys go out west for backpacking and canoeing trip to Montana; at 17 they can be assistant counselors.
  - Pemi week (their version of color war).
  - Dining hall is nice; whole camp eats together; can handle vegan, gluten free and food allergy; camp is nut free.



#### Lowlights

  - Bunks are VERY rustic, not enclosed; showers and bathrooms outside of bunks in shower house.
  - On a private road, no gates or security (camera on the bridge).
  - Limited availability for Intro 2-week session.
  - 2nd session is more popular.
  - Buses come only at beginning and end of camp (not mid-session).



#### Similar Camps

  - Moosilauke
  - Kingswood



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Also have a quieter library building where kids can play games, read, chess etc. There are division heads for each age group and coaches for each activity. Coaches also live in the bunks. Bunks are very rustic along the lake. About 8 kids per bunk. Not enclosed. Showers and bathrooms outside of bunks in shower house. Play intercamps against Moosilauke, Kingswood etc. Don’t have to try out. Dining hall is nice and whole camp eats together and boys sit by cabin group and sometimes mix it up. title: Moosilauke — Emily — 8-21-25
text:
# Moosilauke



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp has been owned all the years by Sabina’s family. About 140 boys at camp both sessions. Campers are from 19 states and 4 countries (China, Singapore). About 140 is Bill’s goal, doesn’t want it to get larger. Session choices are 4 weeks, 2 weeks and 2.5 weeks. See themselves as primarily a 4-week camp. Camp owns 100 acres of land including the lake and a campsite. Activities focus around sports, water sports and outdoor adventure. Overall, Moosilauke is a pretty camp with a nice lake and sports fields.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Bill, the Director toured Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin. Bill and Sabina are very experienced with children. Worked at boarding school in California (Thatcher). Activity counselors also live in the bunks. For younger kids, there are "camp moms" assigned to helping out. There are 4 Head Counselors who are older. There is a CIT program and then Junior Counselor. To be a Counselor, have to have done 1 year of college.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks are located up the hill. Bathrooms are in most bunks. Showers are in separate house. Near the bunks. Activity counselors also live in the bunks. Bunks have about 12 kids. Bunks are simple and have electricity. All the kids have their own wood locker to keep equipment in.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Many activities on the lake including motorized waterskiing, wakeboarding, sailing, rowing, canoeing, kayaks, paddle boards and fishing. Evening activities include waterskiing, tubing, fishing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Kids have assigned activities and 2 elective periods in the afternoon. Sundays are fun events like Carnival, Capture the Flag. Evening activities include waterskiing, tubing, fishing, team building activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports include tennis, basketball, lacrosse, archery, soccer. Offers high quality instruction. Play intercamps against Pemi, Kingswood and Lana Kila. Offers many backcountry trips, some are optional, kayaking, canoeing, hiking and camping. Also have mountain biking. Also offers woodshop and arts and crafts. Boys do socials with Merriwood, Wicosuta and Aloha.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Infirmary has 2 Nurses and a Doctor on Call. Dining Hall has a new kitchen that was rebuilt during covid. Kids have assigned seats for 1 week that are not with their cabin. Then they can switch. Always a breakfast bar, salad bar. Camp is not nut free but have nut free tables for kids with allergies. Can handle gluten and vegetarian, just not airborne allergies. Kids cheer and sing in dining hall. Pictures of camp history on the walls. Camp is located on a private road. There is no gate.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp philosophy is to specialize in boys’ development. Be unplugged, role models, be able to take healthy risks. Bill had an article in Boston Globe about it. Have a strong philosophy. Kids cheer and sing in dining hall. It is simple and a bit rustic but very well kept. Kids were between sessions, so we toured without the kids there.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Run buses from New York and Greenwich, CT. Visiting Day is leave day where parents can spend the day doing activities with kids. Tech: Be unplugged. Parent communication: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Overall, Moosilauke is a pretty camp with a nice lake and sports fields. Activities focus around sports, water sports and outdoor adventure. It would work well for a boy who focuses on sports and outdoor activities. It is simple and a bit rustic but very well kept.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Bill and Sabina have a wonderful philosophy with the boys.
  - Offers high quality instruction.
  - About 140 is Bill’s goal, doesn’t want it to get larger.
  - Camp owns 100 acres of land including the lake and a campsite.
  - See themselves as primarily a 4-week camp.
  - Run buses from New York and Greenwich, CT.



#### Lowlights

  - Do not offer music or theater.
  - Camp is not nut free but have nut free tables for kids with allergies; can handle gluten and vegetarian, just not airborne allergies.
  - There is no gate.



#### Similar Camps

  - Pemi
  - Kingswood
  - Mah-kee-nac
  - Skylemar



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

CAMP MOOSILAUKE 07/21/25 Bill, the Director toured Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin Bill and Sabina are very experienced with children. Worked at boarding school in California (Thatcher). Camp has been owned all the years by Sabina’s family. About 140 boys at camp both sessions. Campers are from 19 states and 4 countries (China, Singapore). About 140 is Bill’s goal, doesn’t want it to get larger. Run buses from New York and Greenwich, CT. Session choices are 4 weeks, 2 weeks and 2.5 weeks. See themselves as primarily a 4-week camp. Camp owns 100 acres of land including the lake and a campsite. Camp is located on a private road. There is no gate. Kids were between sessions, so we toured without the kids there. Camp philosophy is to specialize in boys’ development. Be unplugged, role models, be able to take healthy risks. Bill had an article in Boston Globe about it. Have a strong philosophy. Boys do socials with Merriwood, Wicosuta and Aloha. Visiting Day is leave day where parents can spend the day doing activities with kids. Activities focus around sports, water sports and outdoor adventure. Do not offer music or theater. Kids have assigned activities and 2 elective periods in the afternoon. Sundays are fun events like Carnival, Capture the Flag. Evening activities include waterskiing, tubing, fishing, team building activities, Many activities on the lake including motorized waterskiing, wakeboarding, sailing, rowing, canoeing, kayaks, paddle boards and fishing Sports include tennis, basketball, lacrosse, archery, soccer. Offers high quality instruction. Play intercamps against Pemi, Kingswood and Lana Kila. Offers many backcountry trips, some are optional, kayaking, canoeing, hiking and camping. Also have mountain biking. All the kids have their own wood locker to keep equipment in. Also offers woodshop and arts and crafts. Bunks are located up the hill. Bathrooms are in most bunks. Showers are in separate house. Near the bunks. Activity counselors also live in the bunks. Bunks have about 12 kids. Bunks are simple and have electricity. For younger kids, there are “camp moms” assigned to helping out. There are 4 Head Counselors who are older. There is a CIT program and then Junior Counselor. To be a Counselor, have to have done 1 year of college. Infirmary has 2 Nurses and a Doctor on Call Dining Hall has a new kitchen that was rebuilt during covid. Kids have assigned seats for 1 week that are not with their cabin. Then they can switch. Always a breakfast bar, salad bar. Camp is not nut free but have nut free tables for kids with allergies. Can handle gluten and vegetarian, just not airborne allergies. Kids cheer and sing in dining hall. Pictures of camp history on the walls. Overall, Moosilauke is a pretty camp with a nice lake and sports fields. It would work well for a boy who focuses on sports and outdoor activities. It is simple and a bit rustic but very well kept. title: Wolfeboro — Emily — 8-21-25
text:
# Wolfeboro



## 1 · Program Snapshot

WOLFEBORO 07/22/25 Toured by Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin. Not a boarding school, just summer school in New Hampshire that is a non profit (501C). About 85 kids. Can fit more. Price runs $17,000 for the 5 weeks. Offer some financial aid. Paying for very hands on and small group and individual academic instruction. Offer 5 academic programs. Each student takes 3. Can get high school credit if approved. 3 week program is for kids up to age 14. 5-week program is for high school students. Oldest kid is 17. Faculty is mostly from boarding schools and independent schools.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Kids attending are mostly from private or boarding schools. They may go to help with admission to school or it can be provisional for the kid to be allowed to stay at the school. Admission process is involved to figure out what kind of kid and student and where they need to be, what their academic goals are. They fill out a very involved goals document with parents and kids about what they want to achieve by end of session. Need buy in from student that they are willing to put the work in. Kids who attend tend to struggle in a particular academic area. Some have ADHD/learning issues. Tend to need help with routine and organization. Faculty is mostly from boarding schools and independent schools.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Kids live in tents with electricity. Residential life staff run the afternoon activities and live in tents with the kids. There is a student center with weight room, ping pong, snacks.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront is one of the afternoon activities. Can pay extra for a Rowing (crew) program which runs in the mornings before class. Costs $1500.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The whole morning is spent on academics, the afternoon is elective more fun activities. Academics run until 12:30 PM, then lunch and then elective activities they sign up for. Include basketball, soccer, volleyball, waterfront, studio art etc. Kids can also choose a quieter activity in the afternoon such as reading, board games, in Old Day Hall. Weekends include trip days for fun activities. Academics start Sunday night. Classes are small 3-5 kids. Kids have homework every night and tests. They have study hall every night with assigned seats. Need to be able to do the night homework independently without asking for help.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Academics include math, writing, science reading, language, history. Also have SAT/ACT prep. Paying for very hands on and small group and individual academic instruction. Kids don’t use computers. Use paper and white boards. Want them to learn the basic process first. Also teach process, organization, transitions etc. Kids have an academic and daily planner to write the days assignments and stay organized. Bulletin board in the dining hall has the day organized by schedule, list of waiters, leagues for Wolfeboro Cup and afternoon schedule. Kids get a report with grades and overall writeup of their progress. Also get midterm report. Wolfeboro is really a program unto itself. Much more academic than Cardigan Mountain and other boarding school summer programs.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There are Nurses on staff. Medical center down the road. Dining Hall meals, everyone eats together with the teachers. There is an adult at every table. It’s a time to slow down the day. Can handle allergies. Kids take turns being waiters and set the table and clear the table. Lunch is intentionally run in an organized way and a specific sequence to teach the kids routine and organization. (Sit down, announcements, Food)



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Also teach process, organization, transitions etc. Lunch is intentionally run in an organized way and a specific sequence to teach the kids routine and organization. Bulletin board in the dining hall has the day organized by schedule, list of waiters, leagues for Wolfeboro Cup and afternoon schedule. Wolfeboro is really a program unto itself. Much more academic than Cardigan Mountain and other boarding school summer programs.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Cell phones are allowed 40 mins each day. Rest of the time they are in a locker. Kids don’t use computers. There is a parent education piece as well. The transition back home can be difficult for kids to sustain the routine and organization. Many parents aren’t around or don’t know how to help the kids. Counsel the parents to ask, how can I help you succeed at school? Do you need a desk, a quiet place to work? Etc. Extra costs: Can pay extra for a Rowing (crew) program which runs in the mornings before class. Costs $1500.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Not a boarding school, just summer school in New Hampshire that is a non profit (501C). Much more academic than Cardigan Mountain and other boarding school summer programs, with very hands on and small group and individual academic instruction (classes are 3-5 kids). For a kid who needs intense academic time during the summer and help with routine, organization, process; kids attending are mostly from private or boarding schools, and some have ADHD/learning issues. Price runs $17,000 for the 5 weeks (offer some financial aid), with mornings on academics and afternoons for elective more fun activities.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Offer 5 academic programs; each student takes 3; can get high school credit if approved.
  - Classes are small 3-5 kids.
  - Kids don’t use computers. Use paper and white boards.
  - They have study hall every night with assigned seats; kids have homework every night and tests.
  - Kids get a report with grades and overall writeup of their progress; also get midterm report.
  - Faculty is mostly from boarding schools and independent schools.
  - Kids live in tents with electricity; there is a student center with weight room, ping pong, snacks.
  - There are Nurses on staff; medical center down the road.
  - Bulletin board in the dining hall has the day organized by schedule, list of waiters, leagues for Wolfeboro Cup and afternoon schedule.
  - Can pay extra for a Rowing (crew) program which runs in the mornings before class. Costs $1500.



#### Lowlights

  - Admission process is involved.
  - Need to be able to do the night homework independently without asking for help.
  - Cell phones are allowed 40 mins each day; rest of the time they are in a locker.
  - The transition back home can be difficult for kids to sustain the routine and organization.
  - Travel logistics: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Cardigan Mountain



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

WOLFEBORO 07/22/25 Toured by Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin Not a boarding school, just summer school in New Hampshire that is a non profit(501C). About 85 kids. Can fit more. Price runs $17,000 for the 5 weeks. Offer some financial aid. Paying for very hands on and small group and individual academic instruction. Offer 5 academic programs. Each student takes 3. Can get high school credit if approved. 3 week program is for kids up to age 14. 5-week program is for high school students.Oldest kids is 17. The whole morning is spent on academics, the afternoon is elective more fun activities. Faculty is mostly from boarding schools and independent schools. Admission process is involved to figure out what kind of kid and student and where they need to be, what their academic goals are. They fill out a very involved goals document with parents and kids about what they want to achieve by end of session. Need buy in from student that they are willing to put the work in. Kids attending are mostly from private or boarding schools. They may go to help with admission to school or it can be provisional for the kid to be allowed to stay at the school. Academics include math, writing, science reading, language, history. Also have SAT/ACT prep. Classes are small 3-5 kids. Kids have homework every night and tests. They have study hall every night with assigned seats. Need to be able to do the night homework independently without asking for help. Kids don’t use computers. Use paper and white boards. Want them to learn the basic process first. Also teach process, organization, transitions etc. Kids have an academic and daily planner to write the days assignments and stay organized. Bulletin board in the dining hall has the day organized by schedule, list of waiters, leagues for Wolfeboro Cup and afternoon schedule. Kids get a report with grades and overall writeup of their progress. Also get midterm report. Kids live in tents with electricity. Cell phones are allowed 40 mins each day. Rest of the time they are in a locker. Residential life staff run the afternoon activities and live in tents with the kids. There are Nurses on staff. Medical center down the road. Academics run until 12:30 PM, then lunch and then elective activities they sign up for. Include basketball, soccer, volleyball, waterfront, studio art etc. There is a student center with weight room, ping pong, snacks. Weekends include trip days for fun activities. Academics start Sunday night. Kids can also choose a quieter activity in the afternoon such as reading, board games, in Old Day Hall. Can pay extra for a Rowing (crew) program which runs in the mornings before class. Costs $1500. Kids who attend tend to struggle in a particular academic area. Some have ADHD/learning issues. Tend to need help with routine and organization. Dining Hall meals, everyone eats together with the teachers. There is an adult at every table. It’s a time to slow down the day. Can handle allergies. Kids take turns being waiters and set the table and clear the table. Lunch is intentionally run in an organized way and a specific sequence to teach the kids routine and organization. (Sit down, announcements, Food) There is a parent education piece as well. The transition back home can be difficult for kids to sustain the routine and organization. Many parents aren’t around or don’t know how to help the kids. Counsel the parents to ask, how can I help you succeed at school? Do you need a desk, a quiet place to work? Etc. Wolfeboro is really a program unto itself. Much more academic than Cardigan Mountain and other boarding school summer programs. For a kid who needs intense academic time during the summer and help with routine, organization, process.

title: Kenwood Evergreen — Michele — 8-7-25
text:
# Kenwood Evergreen



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Kenwood Evergreen is a brother/sister camp in New Hampshire; 90% of the kids stay 7 weeks. 2 sessions of full summer - 90% stay full. They said you can send 2nd session kids here with no problem, the campers are very welcoming; they never have issues with kids entering later. They do some activities together like theater. There are two waterfronts (they mentioned they are not shared boys and girls).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We met with Ali and Josh (not married, Ali has kids at the camp and Josh is young and British?). Scott is the current Owner but he is phasing out as a new owner/director Jack will be starting next summer. Ali and Josh are great, lots of energy, fun tour. They have very nice staff, very friendly. The American counselors we met were very cute and friendly\! Ex-campers who want to work there year after year. They love to get returning campers but said it’s harder now to attract them because of the limited technology usage.



## 3 · Living Quarters

We saw one bunk which was very clean and neat (and kind of new). The bunks are more like buildings so the entire group can live together. 4 bunks combine into one building so it’s more by age group which they said the girls love. They have a stairs in the middle that goes down to a rec room and can go outside. Oldest boys do not have showers in the bunks - they have offered to put it in but they refuse. It’s like a rite of passage to be the oldest and they like the bunks the way they are (according to Ali).



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There are two waterfronts - they are not shared boys and girls. Two very pretty lakefronts, no pool. They go on two overnight canoeing trips which sounded very fun.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They have a lot of choice in their day - activities are more choice but they call it "structured choice" so they don’t have a ton of options. Makes it easier for kids to decide what to do. We saw a meeting after breakfast, the girls all gathered around as the counselors read the choices and then they picked on the spot where to go for 1st period.



## 6 · Program Highlights

We then saw the girls at softball, having a lot of fun. Competitive but not intense. More skills-based. They said the boys have been "winning" more lately and not to underestimate their competitiveness. Very New Hampshire/woodsy/outdoorsy/rustic-ish/pretty; lots of people think of them as being more outdoorsy but they hold their own in sports too. They play Birchmont, Robinhood and Walt Whitman. We saw some very cool art studio (clay making). Digital photography looked very fun, very nice gym inside a field house. They have giant rocks on campus for kids to practice rock climbing, extensive ropes course and outdoor adventure is definitely popular. They have 200 acres of trails so lots to do "off campus" too. They do some activities together like theater.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

3 Nurses on staff, 15 mins from a hospital. The dining hall was nice and the food looked good. Fresh. healthy options, etc. They are nut-free and have specialty chefs for allergies. Seemed very organized. I would send a kid with allergies there and feel ok with the food and supervision.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They pride themselves on being very "down to earth" kids. The vibe was low key but fun\! Very much a NH camp, focus on outdoorsy and sports. Nice kids from down to earth families (very friendly toward us). Good leadership. Oldest boys like to BBQ at night, They also BBQ for the younger kids on the "hill". Seems like a lot of camaraderie. We had a great tour and Ali is very funny and engages with the kids so you get a real sense of what it’s like. They do offer a Friday night service (Jewish) and a non-denominational service for those who would prefer it.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown travel logistics. They get one call before V day and one after (intentionally, no need to talk more than that and parents like it). Limited technology usage. She didn't mention extra costs so not sure about that (maybe see website) we also didn’t see a canteen?



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kenwood Evergreen is a brother/sister camp in New Hampshire; 90% of the kids stay 7 weeks. Very New Hampshire/woodsy/outdoorsy/rustic-ish/pretty and they hold their own in sports too, with two very pretty lakefronts (no pool), extensive ropes course/outdoor adventure, and "structured choice" that makes it easier for kids to decide what to do. The vibe was low key but fun, nice kids from down to earth families, and staff felt very friendly and organized (nut-free with specialty chefs for allergies; 3 Nurses on staff, 15 mins from a hospital). For a family that wants a fun, well-run camp in NH this is a great brother/sister choice; for a family that needs a pool or lots of phone calls this is not for them.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Ali and Josh are great, lots of energy, fun tour.
  - They have very nice staff, very friendly.
  - Two waterfronts (not shared boys and girls) and two very pretty lakefronts.
  - "Structured choice" makes it easier for kids to decide what to do.
  - Nut-free and have specialty chefs for allergies; 3 Nurses on staff; 15 mins from a hospital.
  - Digital photography looked very fun, very nice gym inside a field house.
  - They have giant rocks on campus for kids to practice rock climbing, extensive ropes course and outdoor adventure is definitely popular.
  - They have 200 acres of trails so lots to do "off campus" too.



#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - Oldest boys do not have showers in the bunks (rite of passage).
  - Limited technology usage (harder now to attract them).
  - They get one call before V day and one after.
  - She didn't mention extra costs so not sure about that (maybe see website); we also didn’t see a canteen.
  - They didn’t talk much about intercamp games.



#### Similar Camps

  - Birchmont
  - Robinhood
  - Walt Whitman

title: Tevya — Michele — 8-7-25
text:
# Tevya



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Tevya is a Jewish co-ed sleepaway camp with Jewish customs and traditions like Fri night Shabbat, Israeli dance classes, challah making, etc. They stress Jewish values: kindness, inclusion, community, etc. This is a non profit camp that has a board who makes decisions and they are not affiliated with temples. She said kids do 3.5, 3.5 or 7 weeks; 8 years thru 16 year olds. They do a final trip to Israel (this year they went somewhere else). This is a very CO-ED camp; boys and girls seem to do almost everything together.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We met a few head staff members in the office and we toured with Lauren Fishman. She is nice, very matter of fact, didn't stop to talk to any campers, not super warm or engaging with the other staff or kids. Staff is 50% ex-campers and 50% international. Kids seemed very sweet, happy, kinda quirky and not very sports-oriented; more there for the social/fun aspects.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Little kids bunks were decent but older kids live in yurts which we found to be HOT and smelly. She said the kids love it, probably because they are in a separate area which they aspire to get to in their last year. Older kids bunks (yurts) very close together too. Metal bunk beds, nothing over the top, kinda simple. Bunks were messy inside - outside nicer, white with green trim. No bathrooms in CIT yurts.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Very nice lakefront. More of a hang out spot/cool off area.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 activities a day/co-ed/electives. Boys and girls do almost everything together.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Jewish values & culture woven into activities. The kids seemed very close within their groups and also told us about their little sisters so that part of the program is strong (big bro/big sis). They do some trips, doesn't seem to be a big focus: 1 hiking trip and 1 rafting trip; not very outdoorsy. They do a final trip to Israel (this year they went somewhere else). More play-based focus; nothing is highly advanced for instruction.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

They are a KOSHER camp with Kosher food. Decent dining room (linoleum floors - kind of old school feeling) with pictures of Israeli flags on walls. They do blessings before and after dinner. 20 mins to hospital; doctors and nurses on staff.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This is a very CO-ED camp. Very social, co-ed, fun and not over the top in anything. More low key, Jewish values-based, not very sporty. Kids seemed very sweet, happy, kinda quirky. Nurturing atmosphere. It has a family-feel.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

She didn't mention costs but did show us canteen and mentioned some trips - look on website to see if anything is additional since it's a non profit camp (maybe). Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

This would be a good fit for a social, Jewish kid who is looking to play but isn't intense with sports. More of a hang out, meet nice kids and play for fun atmosphere. Nurturing atmosphere with Jewish values & culture woven into activities. Very social, co-ed, fun and not over the top in anything.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - jewish values & culture woven into activities
  - sweet, loving, low pressure environment



#### Lowlights

  - bunks were kind of old on inside and kinda messy
  - no sports-specific counselors - they all teach sports and live with the kids
  - nothing is highly advanced for instruction
  - more play-based focus



#### Similar Camps

  - Kinder Ring
  - Micah

We met a few head staff memebers in the office and we toured with Lauren Fishman. She is nice, very matter of fact didnt stop to talk to any campers, not super warm or engageing with the other staff or kids. She said kids do 3.5, 3.5 or 7 weeks. 8 years thru 16 year olds. title: Wa-Klo- Emily R — Sam — 7-23-25
text:
# Wa-Klo- Emily R



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Tami, the Director, toured Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin. It is her 10th year as Director, 30th year at camp. About 165 girls at camp; Session 1 there were about 110; 165 is the max number they want. Most kids are from MA, CT and NY but other states as well. 10 percent of campers are International. Sessions are 4, 3 or 7 weeks. There is a 2-week option that campers can extend. Wa-Klo has a strong culture of conflict resolution and promoting an inclusive community. There is diversity at camp.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Tami is in her 10th year as Director and 30th year at camp. Most kids are from MA, CT and NY but other states as well; 10 percent of campers are International. The Division Heads are older and tend to have teaching degrees.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks – there are about 3 cabins of each age group. Girls bring hard trunks. Each bunk has about 9 kids and 3 counselors. Bathroom and showers are in bunk. Bunks are renovated and insulated. Older girls have bathrooms in the bunk, but share a shower house.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The lake is pretty and has all the water sports including motorized. Girls take swim tests designed by American Red Cross.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming is elective. Each week the girls change their elective and meet with their counselors to ensure variety. Take attendance at each activity.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities include pet care (rabbits, goats, sheep), dance, watersports, land sports, archery, all kinds of art (sketching, ceramics, stained glass, fashion design), theater, horseback riding, cheerleading, dance, fencing, gymnastics, outdoor adventure (mountain biking, hiking, canoeing). There are 14 horses. Do trail rides and instructional rides. Bring their own equipment. Riding is an extra fee. Have an outdoor amphitheater. Do Sunday reflections which is not religious but values based. Go on trips once a session to things like whale watching, rafting, rock climbing. End of summer has color war (green and white). There is a CIT Program for the older girls. Kingswood is considered the boys camp where most of the brothers go; has a similar community and philosophy. Get together for dance and pool party.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Camp is nut free and there is an allergy chart for each meal. Food is family style with salad and breakfast bar. Kids get 2 snacks a day, 1 being a healthy snack. Nurses on camp at all times and Doctor on call.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Wa-Klo has a strong culture of conflict resolution and promoting an inclusive community. An example is "restorative justice"—making amends when you hurt others and how to re-integrate into your friendships. There is diversity at camp. There is a lot of cheering and spirit. In dining hall, the 1st week you eat with your cabin, then they mix up age groups. Whole camp eats together. There is a Big/Little Sister program which is assigned before camp and they meet up before camp begins; this way the Little Sister feels like she knows someone already. Camp is uniform. One day a week the girls can wear other clothes. The camp is woodsy but not super rustic. The girls seem very kind. The camp seems organized and the girls looked happy and busy. Tami has a strong philosophy that really teaches the girls life lessons.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

There is Visiting Day for 7-week campers. Parents can send emails to campers. There are NO phone calls. Photos are posted each day. Kids write letters to parents. Tami likes to meet every family and loves to do home visits during the year. There are zoom get togethers for current campers and ice cream socials. New campers do a visit to camp in May so they feel comfortable on first day. No screens. MP3 players allowed with music. Riding is an extra fee. Wa-klo offers $250,000 in scholarships. Recruit from an all-Black girl's school in MA.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Overall, Wa-klo is a wonderful option for kids that want session or full. The camp seems organized and the girls looked happy and busy. Tami has a strong philosophy that really teaches the girls life lessons. The camp is woodsy but not super rustic.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong culture of conflict resolution and promoting an inclusive community ("restorative justice").
  - Elective programming with weekly choice and attendance at each activity.
  - Robust activities including horseback riding (14 horses), fencing, stained glass, outdoor adventure, and pet care.
  - Trips once a session (whale watching, rafting, rock climbing).
  - End-of-summer color war (green and white) and a CIT Program for older girls.
  - Kingswood partnership for dance and pool party; similar community and philosophy.
  - Nut free with allergy chart; family-style meals with salad and breakfast bar; nurses on camp at all times and Doctor on call.
  - Big/Little Sister program assigned before camp to help new campers feel known.
  - Small camp feel (about 165 girls; 165 is the max) with 10% international representation.
  - Tami is in her 10th year as Director and 30th year at camp; Division Heads tend to have teaching degrees.
  - Photos posted daily; emails and letters home; Visiting Day for 7-week campers.
  - $250,000 in scholarships; recruit from an all-Black girl's school in MA.



#### Lowlights

  - There are NO phone calls.
  - No screens (MP3 players with music only).
  - Riding is an extra fee and riders bring their own equipment.
  - Older girls share a shower house.
  - Uniform required (only one day a week can wear other clothes).



#### Similar Camps

  - Merriwood
  - Somerset

Nurses on camp at all times and Doctor on call. Camp is uniform. One day a week the girls can wear other clothes. Go on trips once a session to things like whale watching, rafting, rock climbing. End of summer has color war (green and white) There is a CIT Program for the older girls. Wa-klo offers $250,000 in scholarships. Recruit from an all-Black girl’s school in MA. Overall, Wa-klo is a wonderful option for kids that want session or full. The camp seems organized and the girls looked happy and busy. Tami has a strong philosophy that really teaches the girls life lessons. The camp is woodsy but not super rustic. The girls seem very kind. I would give this camp with Merriwood and Somerset.
