title: Wildwood — Beth — 8-3-25
text:
# Wildwood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Wildwood, Maine, all boys, finishing 2nd-finishing 9th, 7 weeks, 4 weeks for more than 1 summer but 90% stay full summer; also 2 week option for finishing K & 1st grades. 168 campers (max 185-195). 73 staff / 40% international; 20-25 staff are former campers.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

All boys; finishing 2nd-finishing 9th. 168 campers (max 185-195). 73 staff / 40% international; 20-25 staff are former campers. Director: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Main bunk area has cabins with 6 campers and 2 staff; teen village has cabins with 14 campers and 4 staff. Sink and toilet in cabin; shower house for additional toilets, sinks, and showers. Plenty of cubby / shelf space. Individual laundry cleaned weekly off-site.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Woods Pond - beautiful lake, swim instruction 5x week through 6th grade. 2 motorboats for on-site waterski; fishing on-site and pontoon boat for fishing out on the lake. All types of boating; aquapark with lots of elements; Water volleyball court.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 period day; 2 morning and 3 afternoon; 2 assigned / 3 choice; waterfront and tennis assigned daily.



## 6 · Program Highlights

All sports are top notch. For golf: on-site putting green and take to local course (Bridgton Highlands) 2x week. 15 tennis courts plus pickle ball. Separately sized fields for smaller boys and older boys. Outdoor adventure course has lots of elements both high and low plus climbing wall, archery, and mountain biking with track and trails.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Traditional looking dining hall in Main lodge with extended rectangular tables for whole division to eat together family style; counselors serve the food. Options at all meals with fruit/yogurt/cereal bar / salad bar / pasta bar; can handle all allergies. Also serve 1 am and 1 afternoon snack, 1 seating. Medical = 4 nurses and 1 doctor for full session; nearest hospital in Bridgton is 5-minute drive.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Engaged, happy boys who love sports as well as waterfront and adventure; minimal hobbies program. Nurturing positive environment. Big brother assigned in June in advance of camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bus from Port Chester, NY; chaperoned flights from Philly / D.C. / FL; pick-ups at Logan and Portland airports. Phone calls scheduled 2 before visiting day and 1 after. No screens / music only. Post pictures daily with facial recognition. Extra charge for tennis / golf / ice hockey.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

  - Nurturing positive environment
  - Big brother assigned in June in advance of camp
  - Medical = 4 nurses and 1 doctor; nearest hospital 5-minute drive



#### Lowlights

  - Minimal hobbies program
  - Waterfront and tennis assigned daily (less choice)
  - No screens / music only
  - Extra charge for tennis / golf / ice hockey



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Fernwood — Jennifer — 7-31-25
text:
# Fernwood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Fernwood is an all girls camp located in Poland, Maine on Lake Thompson. It is a traditional uniform 7 week only camp that can take up to 230 girls. For summer 2025, Fernwood had 225 girls, capacity is 230; 25% are alumni children. They take girls finishing 1st through finishing 9th grade. Campers come from all over.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

In summer 2025, Fernwood had 225 girls (capacity 230); 25% are alumni children. They take girls finishing 1st through finishing 9th grade. 50% of staff were international this summer. They do have some male staff which the campers refer to as "uncles." A typical bunk is 10–12 campers and 4 counselors. Director: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

A typical bunk is 10–12 campers and 4 counselors. There was 1 sink and 2 toilets in bunk we saw; girls shower in separate shower house. Girls all have the same uniform Camp Fernwood Cove blanket on their beds. Campers unpack for themselves at all ages as they feel it is a good life skill. Shower towels and lake towels are provided. Laundry is done 2x a week on site.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Motorized lake on site. Campers take swim lessons 2–4 times a week until they hit the highest level. Girls can bring their own bathing suits. Swimming and canoe is required for all ages except the oldest group. They do have a big waterski program and have 6 motor boats. They recently added fishing. They also offer wake surfing, wakeboarding, sailing, kayaking and paddleboarding.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The day has 6 periods; 5 scheduled and 1 free choice; it works on a credit system and campers take a mix of 24 credits. On Wednesdays, there are trip days.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Horseback riding and archery are very big at camp. Large riding program; horses are on site and approximately 60–80 girls ride each summer. Riding is an additional fee. For arts, they offer glass fusion in addition to crochet/knitting and needlepoint. They have 3 pottery wheels.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

They have 2 doctors on site and 6 RNs. The nearest hospital is 15 minutes away. The dining hall can handle most allergies. They do serve peanut butter but kept at a separate table. The dining hall is old fashioned in appearance and 4 campers sit with 2 counselors and they rotate every 3 days. Food is served family style. Snacks are available throughout the day and are mostly fruit. They have a canteen for toiletry and clothing but not candy.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp felt very old fashioned and traditional. Camp is 50% Jewish but no formal religious services; they do a nondenominational service on Friday nights where they discuss meaningful topics. Fernwood markets themselves as having the same look and feel as it did in 1921 when they opened. To me, it felt outdated and rustic and in need of some updating.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Campers come from all over and they offer bussing from NYC, Westchester, MA. Chaperoned flights from Miami and Philadelphia. Campers get 3 10 minute FaceTime calls during the summer. Flat packages only and only if a camper forgot something. Fernwood does not post pictures during the session but does a big photo dump at the end of the summer. Extra costs are for horseback riding, bussing, canteen, private tennis lessons and the senior trip.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Fernwood is a traditional 7-week all girls uniform camp in Poland, Maine on Lake Thompson, serving girls finishing 1st through 9th grade (225 campers in 2025; capacity 230, with 25% alumni children). The program runs a 6-period day (5 scheduled and 1 free-choice on a credit system), offers Wednesday trip days, and features a strong waterfront (swim lessons 2–4x/week until the highest level, canoeing, six motorboats for a big waterski program, plus wake surfing/wakeboarding, sailing, kayaking, paddleboarding, and newly added fishing). Signature land/arts options include a large on-site riding program (additional fee; 60–80 riders), archery, and arts like glass fusion, crochet/knitting, needlepoint, and three pottery wheels. Culture is old-fashioned and uniformed with family-style dining and limited parent communication (three 10-minute FaceTime calls; flat packages; end-of-summer photo dump), with 50% Jewish campers and a nondenominational Friday night service.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong waterfront: swim lessons 2–4x/week until highest level; canoeing required (except oldest); 6 motor boats; big waterski program; wake surfing/wakeboarding, sailing, kayaking, paddleboarding; newly added fishing.
  - Large riding program; horses on site; approximately 60–80 girls ride each summer (additional fee).
  - Traditional, uniform camp with family-style meals and rotating table assignments.
  - Two doctors on site and 6 RNs; nearest hospital 15 minutes away.
  - International staff presence (\~50% this summer); some male staff ("uncles").
  - Laundry 2x a week on site; towels provided; campers unpack for themselves.



#### Lowlights

  - Camp felt very old fashioned and traditional; to me, it felt outdated and rustic and in need of some updating.
  - Fernwood does not post pictures during the session (big photo dump only at end of summer).
  - Limited parent communication (3 ten-minute FaceTime calls; flat packages only).
  - Peanut butter is served (kept at a separate table).



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

For summer 2025, Fernwood had 225 girls, capacity is 230; 25% are alumni children. They take girls finishing 1st through finishing 9th grade. Campers come from all over and they offer bussing from NYC, Westchester, MA. Chaperoned flights from Miami and Philadelphia. 50% of staff were international this summer. They do have some male staff which the campers refer to as "uncles" A typical bunk is 10-12 campers and 4 counselors. There was 1 sink and 2 toilets in bunk we saw;girls shower in separate shower house. Girls all have the same uniform Camp Fernwood Cove blanket on their beds.Campers unpack for themselves at all ages as feel it is a good life skill. Shower towels and lake towels are provided. Laundry is done 2x week on site. Motorized lake on site. Campers take swim lessons 2-4 times a week until they hit the highest level. Girls can bring their own bathing suits. Snacks are available throughout the day and are mostly fruit. They have a canteen for toiletry and clothing but not candy. Camp felt very old fashioned and traditional. title: Taconic — Lana — 7-24-25
text:
# Taconic



## 1 · Program Snapshot

7 week program with 4 week first summer only option. Coed. 2026 full summer tuition is $16,600. Campers range from rising 2nd to rising 10th. Rising 11th graders are LIT’s (leaders in training). Rising 12th graders can return as counselors but only if they were an LIT; otherwise counselors are rising college freshman and up. About 550 campers in 2025. Even mix of boys and girls. They aim for around 40 campers per division.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp Taconic toured by Jason Kleinman-owner/director. Jason’s mom, Jill Kleinman (owner/director) bought camp in 2004. Jason Kleinman and Chris Ternouth are directors for boys side and Jill Kleinman and daughter Carrie are directors on girls side. Jill’s husband LK runs the waterfront. Majority of campers are from tristate area with biggest areas being Westchester, NYC, NJ, CT and also have 50 campers from Florida who arrive on a chaperoned flight. They have a nice return rate on counselors (both American and international) and will keep counselors with the same bunk year after year if it was a good fit. 50% staff is home grown. They know their campers and are well liked and attentive.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Boys side bunks and girls side bunks are separated by central campus. The newest bunks have basements under the bunk with climbing wall or gaga pits where kids can go before wake-up, free play, rainy days. There are 6 bunks total on camp with the basements. They aim for about 10 kids/bunk and 4 counselors for kids under 12 and 3 counselors for kids over 12. Full bathrooms inside bunks. Kids will be in single beds or bottom of bunk bed. Only counselors on top bunk if there are any bunk beds in the bunk.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Beautiful lake. One side of entrance has beach volleyball, sandy beach, water toys, and non motorized boating like standup paddleboards, kayaks, canoes, sailboats. Another dock around the bend has motorboats where they waterski, kneeboard, wakeboard. 3 pools at camp. One has a waterslide that is used for evening activities and free swim. The other 2 are used for instructional swim through 11 years old or free swim. Girls have to wear 1 piece bathing suits.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 periods a day. Lower camp (up to rising 5th grade) has 1 elective a day, and then kids have more choice as they get older. Wake-up is 8:00. Everyday after breakfast, the whole camp goes to morning Sing in the playhouse for an hour at 9:15 (no flagpole raising). It is an energetic and spirited event where the whole camp does karaoke to fun songs and a live band is playing. They then have 3 hour-long periods and then lunch, rest hour, milk and cookies, and 3 more hour-long periods in the afternoon. Then is dinner, evening activity, and evening snack around 8:45.



## 6 · Program Highlights

All around program with nice sports and arts. They have a circus program with a trapeze and silks. An adult director runs each program. The kids travel by division when they are going somewhere other than an arts studio or smaller space. They have a wide variety of activities including stem. They have Taconic Games at end of the summer. 2 teams which change each year. There is option for competition with intercamp competitions in many different sport areas including soccer basketball flag football dance outdoor adventure and also non-sports like chess. They try to keep intercamp competitions to camps within an hour drive. Once a week is a trip day for campers to places like bowling, movies, mini golf, Great Adventure. Campers aged 11 and up go on overnight trips to places like Vermont, Maine, and California and Utah for oldest campers and LIT’s.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The health center staff has a doctor (specialty being pediatrician most if not all weeks) 7 nurses and 2 nursing assistants. They are nut aware and do not serve nut products. They can accomodate most allergies and celiac. The whole camp eats at once in the dining hall with older campers graduating to the outdoor patio. Food is served family style. They offer an optional Friday night service after dinner where they serve challah and grape juice and say 3 short prayers led by Jill, Jason and Dana. There is a gate at the entrance and someone there to take visitors names.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

A fun camp with lots of activity and sports at camp. Dressing-up and makeup are not priorities.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Tristate campers all take the bus to and from White Plains, NY with a stop in CT. Chaperoned FL flight. Photos posted several times a day on Waldo app and also on Campminder. No packages allowed. 3 phone calls a summer, one before visiting day and two after. No screens allowed; they will allow devices to play music as long as no screens. They suggest the Mighty. No food allowed in bunks. Extra fees for horseback riding (on camp) or private tennis lessons. Camp does fill early in summer and they are only touring families with rising 2nd graders and younger. They do reserve spots for siblings.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Taconic is coed with a 7 week program and a 4 week first summer only option for rising 2nd to rising 10th graders (rising 11th are LIT’s). About 550 campers in 2025 with an even mix of boys and girls; they aim for around 40 campers per division, with most from the tristate area and a chaperoned FL flight. A beautiful lake with non motorized boating and motorboats for waterskiing plus 3 pools, a circus program with a trapeze and silks, and weekly trip days and intercamp competitions support an all around program with nice sports and arts. A camper who enjoys sports and arts and who is willing to participate will do well here.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Beautiful campus and well maintained.
  - Updated facilities.
  - The owners and staff are well liked and respected.
  - Camp does fill early in summer and they are only touring families with rising 2nd graders and younger.
  - They do reserve spots for siblings.



#### Lowlights

  - Nothing noteworthy.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They can accomodate most allergies and celiac. The whole camp eats at once in the dining hall with older campers graduating to the outdoor patio. Food is served family style. They offer an optional Friday night service after dinner where they serve challah and grape juice and say 3 short prayers led by Jill, Jason and Dana. There is a gate at the entrance and someone there to take visitors names. Vibe and Culture Prompt – general vibe of the camp, observations of camper and staff interactions, what is important to these campers and are they invested in the culture and traditions of the camp Example Frequent interactions throughout the day between older and younger campers, as it is a smaller camp. Staff are very present, as they are checking up on campers between instructional periods. There is heavy emphasis put on athletics, but they found ways to get less athletically-inclined campers involved by having them help referee the soccer game that I watched. Campers were all very excited for Color War and had a lot of chatter about it, showing that the campers are both passionate about the traditions of the camp as well as the competitive atmosphere. Seems as though most kids would do well here, would recommend to campers who are comfortable socially and have a bit of an athletic inclination. A fun camp with lots of activity and sports at camp. Dressing-up and makeup are not priorities. Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs Prompt – opening and drop off logistics, call/email schedule, tour policy, package policy, laundry policy, electronics, photo feed, paid electives, refund policy, tip policy. Example Offers bus service from NYC, Boston, Westchester, Long Island. Accompanied minor flights from FL and LA. Campers Zoom home once every three weeks; printed BunkNotes arrive daily. One package (no food) is permitted per session. Personal phones stay home, though GoPros are allowed on trips; Waldo pushes \~200 photos nightly. Optional extras: horseback $350/week, golf $95/round, CIT leadership $750. Tuition is refundable minus $500 until 1?May. No tips allowed. Tristate campers all take the bus to and from White Plains, NY with a stop in CT. Chaperoned FL flight. Photos posted several times a day on Waldo app and also on Campminder. No packages allowed. 3 phone calls a summer, one before visiting day and two after. No screens allowed, they will allow devices to play music as long as no screens. They suggest the Mighty. No food allowed in bunks. Extra fees for horseback riding (on camp) or private tennis lessons. Ideal Fit & Four?Sentence Summary Prompt – who excels here, common concerns, and a final four?sentence parent blurb. Parent Summary: Camp?Weequahic hosts 500 co?ed campers in Pennsylvania’s Endless Mountains. Three session lengths blend cabin structure with 60+ electives ranging from disc?golf to culinary arts. A full medical team, gated security and nut?restricted kitchen meet most safety expectations. Ideal for return?oriented families seeking classic traditions alongside a high-quality waterfront, athletics program, and adventure course. A camper who is enjoys sports and arts and who is willing to participate will do well here. Internal Insights (Highlights and Lowlights). Highlights include the quality of facilities, many freshly painted, as well as a standout creativity center and a dynamic waterfront. The teen lounge (""The Cove"") and marksmanship program are newer hits. The camp appears to appeal to more athletic kids than other choice-based camps, adding to its versatility. The campers appeared mainstream, happy, and fully engaged in a wide array of activities. The grounds are thoughtfully laid out, easy to navigate, and reflect significant investment. Lowlights: nothing noteworthy. Beautiful campus and well maintained. Updated facilities. The owners and staff are well liked and respected. Camp does fill early in summer and they are only touring families with rising 2nd graders and younger. They do reserve spots for siblings.

title: Bridgton Sports — Jennifer — 7-24-25
text:
# Bridgton Sports



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Bridgton Sports is an all boys sports camp owned and directed by Brian Kooperman on the campus of a boarding school in Maine. They run two 3 week sessions (do not allow any boys to stay full summer). Camp is for boys finishing 2nd through finishing 8th grade. They can take 130 boys per session and are currently at capacity. For summer 2025, camp is at capacity with 130 boys per session and has a return rate of about 65%. Their sister camp is Kents Hill, all girls sports camp about 1.5 hours away in Maine. Camper/counselor ratio is 4:1. Bridgton Sports Camp hosts 120 boys per session in Maine.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp has a 4:1 camper/counselor ratio. Many boys come from the Northeast. They have very few international counselors and most of the counselors are high level athletes or coaches. Brian is a very friendly and personable director.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Boys live in the dorms on campus. There are 2-3 boys per room and they are roomed by age. There are 3-4 counselors per floor. Bathrooms are down the hall. Laundry is done 1 x per week in individual bags, done twice per session (as a 3 week session).



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is no pool at camp. The boys use a lake that is across the street (about a 10 min walk) but they are driven there by van to save time. We did not have time to visit the lake but it is motorized; they have 2 motor boats plus a pontoon boat. Boys should be able to waterski 2 times per week if they want. They also offer fishing and have kayaks and canoes. NO sailboats.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming is elective. Campers choose 1 major sport which they do 2 hours per day plus minors. Prior to camp, campers will choose their major sport and rank their minors. After the first week, campers have the opportunity to change their minor. One of the largest and most popular programs is their ice hockey program which tends to fill early for a major. They can take 65 boys in their ice hockey program. The other major sports they offer are baseball, basketball, lacrosse and soccer. Golf is available as a minor. There is a driving range at camp and boys are taken off camp to play.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Nice fields and high level of instruction for campers. Camp is a sports camp so obviously big emphasis on sports. Highlights include the sports facilities especially the ice hockey program, high level of instruction and great camp director.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The health center is staffed by 2 RN and an ATC. The closest hospital is 10 minutes away. The kitchen is nut free and can handle most allergies. The boys eat cafeteria style and they have 3 seatings for meals based on age. The boys are assigned seats in the cafeteria and the seats are changed weekly. Snacks are available throughout the day including a fruit break under a tent. Snacks are also brought to the dorms at night.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

We saw the younger boys getting ready to leave for an activity and all seemed very happy and engaged. Brian said they focus on sportsmanship, teamwork and loyalty.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses run from North Jersey, MA and NY. Pickup from Boston and Portland Airport. Buses available from NY, NJ and MA. Campers bring their bags with them to camp and unpack themselves. He is doing Zooms with all camp families prior to the start of the summer. Campers are given access to their phones 1 hour per week and are instructed to call home. NO packages allowed. They do post pictures (I believe daily) through camp minder.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Bridgton Sports Camp hosts 120 boys per session in Maine. Two 3 week sessions are available and there is no option to stay full summer. Nice facilities, big ice hockey program and high level of instruction. This camp is good for a boy who really loves sports but also wants to experience some of the traditional camp activities and have access to a beautiful motorized lake in Maine.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Highlights include the sports facilities especially the ice hockey program, high level of instruction and great camp director.
  - Nice fields and high level of instruction for campers.
  - Big ice hockey program which tends to fill early for a major; they can take 65 boys in their ice hockey program.



#### Lowlights

  - There is no pool at camp.
  - NO sailboats.
  - Do not allow any boys to stay full summer.



#### Similar Camps

  - Academy Camps
  - Berkshire United



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Cedar — Jennifer — 7-24-25
text:
# Cedar



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Cedar is an all boys camp in Maine, family owned and run. They offer 4, 5, or 7 week sessions with 90% staying full summer. Camp has 250 boys in summer 2025; capacity is 275. Campers are finishing 1st to finishing 9th, CIT program for finishing 10th. Camp is about 95% Jewish but no religious services. Camp Cedar attracts sportier campers. Camp has a nice philosophy of 5 Cs - community, courage, character, challenge and compassion. Camp Cedar is an all boys camp in Maine with warm, nurturing directors and a good fit for a boy who likes to play sports.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We were toured by Sammy Wolf Mcleod who is the assistant director (and daughter) of owners and hinted she is ready to take over running the camp. 65% of counselors are international. Up until 6th grade, boys do have 1 female counselor assigned to them. Camp director was very warm and welcoming. She feels campers that do well here are those that want to play sports and also like nature. Also, Sammi's father is a child psychologist.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks have 10-12 boys per cabin plus 3-4 counselors with electricity and fans, toilets and sinks in the bunk. Towel service provided for younger campers only.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Nice lake with motorized water sports including wake boarding and wake surfing, also offers fishing at the lake. Swim instruction is mandatory until 6th grade. No pool.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

There are 6 periods in a day and waterfront and tennis are daily. For younger campers, activities are assigned and get more electives as they get older. For golf, they have a 4-hole on campus and are taken off site for a golf course. They are now renting ice time at Bridgton Sports for those that want ice hockey which is extra $150 per session. For younger campers there are no cuts to make the teams but as campers get older, some may not get equal playing time.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Camp Cedar attracts sportier campers and offers a high level of instruction. Camp also does some community service, younger boys is done at camp and older boys go off site for service. There is a father/son overnight for prospective families. Camp has a nice philosophy of 5 Cs - community, courage, character, challenge and compassion.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There is 1 doctor on site who changes every other week and they also have 4 RNs. Nearest hospital is 15 minutes away in Bridgton. Also, Sammi's father is a child psychologist. The kitchen is nut free and can handle all allergies. Campers sit by bunk, food is served family style. Snacks such as cheese sticks, fruit, yogurt are available throughout the day.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp director was very warm and welcoming. She feels campers that do well here are those that want to play sports and also like nature. Camp has a nice philosophy of 5 Cs - community, courage, character, challenge and compassion. Camp is about 95% Jewish but no religious services. Campers appeared happy and engaged.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from NY and MA, flights from Philly and NY. Offers bus service from Westchester and Boston area; chaperoned flights available from Philly and Laguardia. Extra costs include private tennis lessons, trips and ice hockey. They are now renting ice time at Bridgton Sports for those that want ice hockey which is extra $150 per session. For phone calls - 1 before and 1 after visiting day but can be flexible. Older boys are given their phones for 1 hour per week.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Cedar is an all boys camp in Maine, family owned and run, with warm, nurturing directors. They offer 4, 5, or 7 week sessions with 90% staying full summer, serving boys finishing 1st through 9th with a CIT program for finishing 10th. Programs attract sportier campers and offer a high level of instruction, with a nice lake for motorized water sports (no pool) and mandatory swim instruction until 6th grade. It is a good fit for boys who want to play sports and also like nature.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Family owned and run; warm, nurturing directors.
  - Sportier campers; high level of instruction.
  - Nice lake with motorized water sports including wake boarding and wake surfing; fishing at the lake.
  - Mandatory swim instruction until 6th grade; waterfront and tennis are daily.
  - Kitchen is nut free and can handle all allergies; food served family style; snacks available throughout the day.
  - Buses from NY and MA; bus service from Westchester and Boston; chaperoned flights from Philly and Laguardia.
  - Community service for younger boys at camp and older boys off site.
  - Campers appeared happy and engaged.
  - 65% of counselors are international.
  - 5 Cs - community, courage, character, challenge and compassion.



#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - Ice hockey is extra $150 per session.
  - Extra costs include private tennis lessons, trips and ice hockey.
  - As campers get older, some may not get equal playing time.
  - Towel service provided for younger campers only.
  - Phone calls limited to 1 before and 1 after visiting day (can be flexible).
  - Older boys are given their phones for 1 hour per week.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

We were toured by Sammy Wolf Mcleod who is the assistant director ( and daughter) of owners and hinted she is ready to take over running the camp. title: Walden — Jennifer — 7-24-25
text:
# Walden



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Walden is a traditional all girls camp in Maine founded in 1969. It is a full summer (7 week) uniform camp that will allow girls to do 4 weeks for the first summer at any age. Camp is for girls ages 8-15. Capacity is 140.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

For 2025, camp had 124 girls. This summer they had 18 girls enroll for the first time 4 week option. Approximately 30% of their campers are alumni. This summer, they had approximately 70 counselors, 40% international. Brothers often attend Winnebago, Wildwood, Wigwam, Skylemar, Androscoggin. Marni is the director; she does home visits with all campers prior to camp.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins and shower house on the rustic side. Cabins hold 8-12 campers and 3-4 counselors. Toilets and sinks in the bunk but separate shower house. Campers unpack for themselves with the counselors help. They do change the cabins every year to avoid cliques.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Camp Walden has a semi private lake, no pool. Swim instructions are in the lake and are required 3 x per week until age 15. One piece bathing suits only but no uniform for bathing suits. They have 2 motor boats for water skiing and water sports are on a separate part of the lake as the swimming. They also offer sailing, kayaking and canoeing. They did not have inflatables or wibits in their lake.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

There are 5 periods in a day and are a combo of assigned and electives. After dinner, there is evening activity and milk line (milk and cookies). Older campers -camp sisters and second mates help the younger campers with their evening routine.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Walden offers stables on site with 8 horses; for 2025 - has 30 intensive riders. Has all the traditional sports and considers themselves to be a noncompetitive camp. Gymnastics was outdoors on a covered pavilion and on the rustic side.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The dining hall is on the rustic side. Campers are assigned tables (all age ranges mixed) and tables are switched weekly. Food is served family style and they are nut free and can handle all allergies. On Saturday nights, they have pizza and cookouts every Sunday night. Milk and cookie line after dinner. Snacks available throughout the day and candy line available twice a week. The health center has 3 RNs on site. They have a Dr. on call for telemedicine. The closest hospital is 8 miles away.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Walden is a very nurturing camp and promotes a culture of kindness. We saw many campers come up and give Marni, the director a hug. She does home visits with all campers prior to camp. They also assign big sisters and second mates to the younger campers to promote a family like atmosphere. Campers seemed very happy.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses are available from NYC, Westchester and MA. Chaperoned flights from Baltimore and CA. Bus service available as listed above. They will also do airport pickups at Logan and Portland. Very few campers drive. Campers get 2 phone calls, 1 before and 1 after visiting day. They do allow packages but no food. Pictures are posted 3x per week. Extra costs are for bussing, trips for older girls and intensive horseback riding.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Walden is a traditional all girls camp in Maine with a very warm nurturing environment and rustic facilities. It is a full summer (7 week) uniform camp that will allow girls to do 4 weeks for the first summer at any age. Highlights of Camp Walden are definitely the director and staff; we stayed for lunch and sat with some lovely young campers, and many campers came up to hug the director, Marni while we were there. Campers seemed very happy and would be a good fit for a low key family, but the lowlights are that some of the facilities look like they could use some renovations.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Walden is a very nurturing camp and promotes a culture of kindness.
  - Highlights of Camp Walden are definitely the director and staff (Marni).
  - Considers themselves to be a noncompetitive camp with all the traditional sports.
  - Stables on site with 8 horses; for 2025 - has 30 intensive riders.
  - Semi private lake with water skiing, sailing, kayaking and canoeing.



#### Lowlights

  - Some of the facilities look like they could use some renovations.



#### Similar Camps

  - Winnebago
  - Wildwood
  - Wigwam
  - Skylemar
  - Androscoggin



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They had 124 girls for summer and their capacity is 140. Camp is for girls ages 8-15. For 2025, camp had 124 girls. This summer they had 18 girls enroll for the first time 4 week option. Approximately 30% of their campers are alumni. Brothers often attend Winnebago, Wildwood, Wigwam, Skylemar, Androscoggin. Buses are available from NYC, Westchester and MA. title: Harlam — Michelle — 7-24-25
text:
# Harlam



## 1 · Program Snapshot

URJ Camp Harlam is a coed overnight camp part of the United Reform Judaism family of camps that opened in 1958. It’s located in the Pocono Mountains and offers 2 sessions that are 3.5 weeks, with about 25% of campers staying for all 7. Tuition is $7600 per session if you’re a member of a reform synagogue and $7900 per session if you are not. First time camper tuition is $6600. Full 7 weeks is $13,800 if you are a member of a reform synagogue and $14,100 if you are not; again a discount for first time campers brings tuition to $12,800. Part of One Happy Camper and scholarship is available to help get families to camp. Jewish Life and values are at the core of camp Harlam. 70% of campers come from a reform Jewish background. The day is very structured and traditional.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Director is Lisa David. 30 counselors from Israel and a small percent of campers come from Israel. There is DEI and a strong inclusion program so that every child can be successful even if additional support is needed. This is able to be achieved by staff of "camper care" comprised of 6 social workers/special education teachers and facilities like a sensory room. Campers come from "all over."



## 3 · Living Quarters

Renovated bunks. No girls side or boys side, it’s wood side or hillside in case there are non binary campers or trans. Bunk beds, trough sinks, showers. Towels brought to camp only for the bunk; there is towel service at lake and pool. Large porches to hang out on. Jobs for each kid to do to help keep bunk clean. Private changing area for those who feel shy about their body. Max 16 per bunk and always 4 counselors.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Elementary aged kids do swim instruction. Older kids do water fun- water polo or water Zumba, something like that. 2 Olympic sized non heated pools. Lake Joshua is on premise and campers go boating and stand up paddle boarding or go on inflatables.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Typical day has 5 periods. All programming is age based. All arts and sports are electives; electives are with entire age group co Ed. Campers get 2 choice periods a day. Shabbat: one less activity on Friday so campers can get ready. Fri night 1 hour service. Kabbalat is where highlights of the week are spoken about. Saturday is a fully elective day. 1 hour 15 min Torah service. All services are led by campers and cantors and rabbis on staff only serve supporting roles. Havdalah observed at end of Shabbat.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Variety of sports offered as well as "teva" which are outdoor nature based activities such as garden, outdoor Israeli cooking and hiking. There are also "chai light" sports with emphasis on group play rather than instruction such as ga ga and 9 square. Participate in inter camp play and everyone makes the team. Activities include: hiking, mountain bikes, low and high ropes, ninja warrior, archery, boy and girl scouting, flag football, baseball, dance, drama, music (recording studio and guitar lessons), fine arts (painting drawing ceramics jewelry), digital arts (photography and videography), 1 play per session, pickleball, tennis, roller hockey, basketball, gymnastics, soccer, volleyball and yoga. Arts center was great.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

1 Dr and 6 nurses on premise at all times. Dining hall is staggered seating, kosher style. Traditional Shabbat meal served on Shabbat (tablecloth and flowers from garden). Family style meals. Equipped for all allergies. Blessings done at every meal but birkot hamazon is abridged. Peanut butter served in packets so they cannot accommodate an airborne nut allergy.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Jewish Life and values are at the core. Community very important at Harlam. They celebrate and honor character traits in Hebrew called midot. Encourage kids to be their best selves. Build Jewish identity. Harlam is not overly competitive, with lots of intermingling boy and girl and a good amount of structure in the day. Outdoor chapels were beautiful. Camp was well maintained and kids seemed super happy.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Campers come from "all over," but busses are from NJ (Edison and Livingston), Philly, Baltimore, Southern Maryland and Richmond, VA. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Harlam is a great choice for a family looking for a Jewish camp with Jewish life incorporated into their day, or someone who needs a good inclusion option where additional support is provided. Harlam is a solid choice for people looking for 3.5 week sessions, not overly competitive, lots of intermingling boy and girl, varied offerings and good amount of structure in the day. Jewish Life and values are at the core of camp Harlam. Camp was well maintained and kids seemed super happy.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Arts center was great.
  - Outdoor chapels were beautiful.
  - Camp was well maintained and kids seemed super happy.
  - Renovated bunks.
  - Strong inclusion program (DEI) with camper care staff (6 social workers/special education teachers) and a sensory room.



#### Lowlights

  - Gymnastics facility was underwhelming.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

URJ Camp Harlam is a coed overnight camp part of the United Reform Judaism family of camps that opened in 1958. It’s located in the pocono mountains and offers 2 sessions that are 3.5 weeks, with about 25% of campers staying for all 7. Director is Lisa David and tuition is $7600 per session if you’re a member of a reform synagogue and $7900 per session if you are not. First time camper tuition is $6600. Full 7 weeks is $13,800 if you are a member of a reform synagogue and $14,100 if you are not; again a discount for first time campers brings tuition to $12,800. Part of One Happy Camper and scholarship is available to help get families to camp. Jewish Life and values are at the core of camp Harlam. 70% of campers come from a reform Jewish background. Campers come from “all over” but busses are from NJ (Edison and Livingston) Philly, Baltimore, Southern Maryland and Richmond, VA. The day is very structured and traditional. Variety of sports offered as well As “teva” which are outdoor nature based activities such as garden, outdoor Israeli cooking and hiking. There are also “chai light” sports with emphasis on group play rather than instruction such as ga ga and 9 square. There are 30 counselors from Israel and a small percent of campers come from Israel. There is DEI and a strong inclusion program so that every child can be successful even if additional support is needed. This is able to be achieved by staff of “camper care” comprised of 6 social workers/special education teachers and facilities like a sensory room. Renovated bunks. No girls side or boys side, it’s wood side or hillside in case there are non binary campers or trans. Bunk beds, trough sinks, showers. Towels brought to camp only for the bunk there is towel service at lake and pool. Large porches to hang out on. Jobs for each kid to do to help keep bunk clean. Private changing area for those who feel shy about their body. Max 16 per bunk and always 4 counselors. Elementary aged kids do swim instruction. Older kids do water fun- water polo or water Zumba, something like that. 2 Olympic sized non heated pools. Lake Joshua is on premise and campers go boating and stand up paddle boarding or go on inflatables. Typical day has 5 periods. All programming is age based. All arts and sports are electives, electives are with entire age group co Ed. Campers get 2 choice periods a day. Shabbat: one less activity On Friday so campers can get ready. Fri night 1 hour service. Kabbalat is where highlights of the week are spoken about. Saturday is a fully elective day. 1 hour 15 min Torah service. All services are led by campers and cantors and rabbis on staff only serve supporting roles. Havdalah observed at end of Shabbat. Encourage kids to be their best selves. Build Jewish identity. Participate in inter camp play and everyone makes the team. Activities include: hiking, mountain bikes, low and high ropes, ninja warrior, archery, boy and girl scouting, flag football, baseball, dance, drama, music (recording studio and guitar lessons,) fine arts (painting drawing ceramics jewelry) digital arts (photography and videography) 1 play per session, pickleball, tennis, roller hockey, basketball, gymnastics, soccer, volleyball and yoga. 1 Dr and 6 nurses on premise at all times. Dining hall is staggered seating, kosher style. Traditional Shabbat meal served on Shabbat. (Tablecloth and flowers from garden.) Family style meals. Equipped for all allergies. Blessings done at every meal but birkot hamazon is abridged. Peanut butter served in packets so they cannot accommodate an air born but allergy. Community very important at Harlam. They celebrate and honor character traits in Hebrew called midot. Camp Harlam is a great choice for a family looking for a Jewish camp with Jewish life incorporated into their day, or someone who needs a good inclusion option where additional support is provided. Harlam is a solid choice for people looking for 3.5 week sessions, not overly competitive, lots of intermingling boy and girl, varied offerings and good amount of structure in the day. ? Gymnastics facility was underwhelming. Arts center was great. Camp was well maintained and kids seemed super happy. Outdoor chapels were beautiful.

title: Takajo — Jennifer — 7-24-25
text:
# Takajo



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Takajo is an all boys 7 week camp located in Maine owned by Jeff Konisberg who is also the director. For summer 2025, Takajo has 385 boys which is their capacity. They do offer a 1 time intro 4 week session but only 5 kids were doing this summer and most typically extend. Campers come from all over. Camp has a strong alumni presence and 15-20% of boys are children of alumni.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Owned by Jeff Konisberg who is also the director. A typical cabin has 8 campers and 2 staff. For younger campers (finishing 2nd through 5th) they also are assigned a female counselor. Counselors unpack for the boys at all ages. Jeff said a boy up until 5th or 6th grade could join camp and still fit in. New campers are assigned to a camp family whom they can meet before camp and have get togethers prior to camp.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The bunk we saw had 1 toilet and 1 sink and they use a shower house. Both shower and pool towels are provided. Bunks are kept the same each year.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Camp has a beautiful lake and swim instruction is done in the lake 5 days a week up until 5th grade. They have 2 motorboats and offer wake boarding, wake surfing in addition to water skiing. No pool.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

There are 6 periods in a day. Programming consists of 2 swim periods, 2 organized team sports (play by grade) as well as 2 choice (1 skill/1 hobby). For younger campers, the periods are shorter in length. The skills and hobbies are done on a 4 day rotation. As boys get older, they get more choices in their schedules.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Program highlights would be their sports and sports instruction. In addition to their regular sports counselors, they bring in outside coaches/college coaches for a week. Smaller size fields for younger campers. They have 17 tennis courts and a golf driving range on campus. There is also the option to go golfing offsite weekly at a local golf course for no additional fee. They do have a music program with drums, keyboard, piano, ukulele and teach the basics. Arts and crafts is on the basic side (were making sports themed magnets while we were there) but they do have 3 pottery wheels, woodworking and offer an outdoor cooking program (making simple things like smores and grilled cheese). Highlights of Takajo include great director and staff, strong sports instruction, beautiful waterfront and field sized for each age group.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The health center has 2 doctors at all times (usually camper parents) and 9-10 RNS. Nearest hospital is 15 minutes. The dining hall is lovely and nut free. They can handle all allergies. Campers eat in staggered seatings and food is served buffet style. Tables are assigned and are changed weekly. There is a snack shack and campers are given snack 3 times a day.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp Takajo is for sporty boys. We saw some very happy sporty boys there. They have a lot of camp spirit and a high alumni presence.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from Westchester, Newark and Newton, MA. Will provide a chaperoned flight from any location, currently has chaperoned flights from Florida and Philadelphia. Extra costs are for canteen and trips. Campers do a Facetime call every 2 weeks with their families. No care packages allowed. Takajo does post pictures daily and uses facial recognition.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Takajo is an all boys camp located on a beautiful lake in Maine that is home to approximately 385 boys per summer. They offer high level of instruction in both land and water sports and are a great fit for a sporty boy. Highlights of Takajo include great director and staff, strong sports instruction, beautiful waterfront and field sized for each age group. We saw some very happy sporty boys there.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong sports and sports instruction.
  - In addition to their regular sports counselors, they bring in outside coaches/college coaches for a week.
  - Beautiful waterfront.
  - Field sized for each age group.
  - 17 tennis courts and a golf driving range on campus.
  - Option to go golfing offsite weekly at a local golf course for no additional fee.
  - Strong alumni presence (15-20% of boys are children of alumni).
  - Health center has 2 doctors at all times (usually camper parents) and 9-10 RNS.
  - Dining hall is lovely and nut free and can handle all allergies.
  - Daily photos with facial recognition and FaceTime calls every 2 weeks.
  - New campers are assigned to a camp family.



#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - Arts and crafts is on the basic side.
  - No care packages allowed.
  - Extra costs are for canteen and trips.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Beber — Alli — 7-24-25
text:
# Beber



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Located on 350 acres in Mukwonago, WI, Beber Camp is 90 minutes from Chicago and 35 minutes from Milwaukee. The camp was founded in 1976 and formerly named B'nai B'rith Beber Camp and has since become an independent camp with ACA. The co-ed camp serves rising 2nd–10th graders and offers the following sessions: first session, Rookie (2 weeks, first time campers only, up to rising 5th graders), Second Session, Pioneer Program, Israel Trip (for Pio and CIT)\* CIT Program (4 or 8 week option) and Taste of Beber (3 days). Full sessions are priced at $7,100, Rookie sessions are $3,690. The camp is ACA-accredited and has a robust leadership and staff.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp hosts approximately 350 campers with 60 kids in the teen leadership section (Pio and CIT) and 120 staff. Campers come mostly from Chicago and the surrounding suburbs (55%). The other 45% are from Cleveland, Denver, Louisville and New York. They are looking to recruit more campers from the south. Staff has a high return rate and is a nice balance of domestic and international. About 70% of their staff return each summer. Head staff is robust with 20 full time, including a Camp Mom. Directors (Michael and Harrell) are very high energy, hands on, outgoing, and friendly. They both appeared to know most campers by name and they are involved in all aspects of the day-to-day operations. This is Michael's 35th summer at Beber. Michael Wax, Camp Director, is a former camper and supervises the full-time summer staff and all day-to-day operations. The Inclusion program (Yad B’Yad) is for campers ages 7-18 with learning, emotional, and developmental disabilities. These campers live in a bunk with all the other campers in their age group and receive one on one staff support.



## 3 · Living Quarters

As of this summer all cabins include air conditioning, ceiling fans and blackout shades. We toured a middle school aged girls cabin. Cabins house 12-16 campers with 4-5 counselors. All cabins have bunk beds, shelves with rods to hang clothes. Every cabin has toilets and sinks, all girls cabins have showers. All cabins are cleaned twice daily. Camp is gender inclusive and cabins are assigned by gender choice. There are non-gender specific bathroom facilities on site.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Camp is on a non-private lake, includes a sand beach, mid-sized AquaPark, sailing, water skiing, tubing, windsurfing and stand up paddleboard. Camp owns 12 ski boats. Campers cannot swim in the lake and it is mandatory to wear a lifevest when in the water. All campers are required to take a swim test in the pool, and the youngest campers have instructional swim in the pool. Watersports are the most popular activities at camp.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown.



## 6 · Program Highlights

More than 30 activities are offered, including athletics (tennis, basketball, beach volleyball, trail riding - they have 20 mountain bikes, yoga, baseball, archery, soccer, Gaga and more.) Next year they are planning to build a BMX track and add Pickleball. An impressive ropes course includes a zip line, climbing areas and state of the art safety. A dance studio, a performance stage and sound studio for Beber Rock Band, wood working, mosaic design, jewelry making are also offered, as well as cooking, gardening and farm with sheep, bunnies, and a new cow named Alexis who arrived during our tour\! Off-site horseback riding is also offered. Also of note is their "top notch" baseball program that includes a batting cage for the co-ed team. Campers will go on 3 field trips, including a water park, the Bristol Renaissance Faire (first session) and the Wisconsin State Fair (second session.) The annual Maccabiah Games is a big favorite and campers are given a team color for the duration of their time at Camp. The Pioneer program is the first year of a two-year acceptance based program. The first four weeks at camp focus on leadership skill development, teambuilding, community service and further exploration of their Jewish identity. At the second part of the program Pioneers have the option of participating in a 3-week trip to Israel. Note that this trip has been cancelled for two consecutive years due to ongoing violence in Israel. The CIT Program is a 8-week program and the second summer of the two-year acceptance based Teen Leadership Program. Participation in the Pioneer program is not required. In addition to having an internship in the cabins during the second session, the CITs volunteer at an Easter Seals camp with campers with special needs in Colorado during the first session.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There are security gates at each entrance and 24/7 security guards on-site while campers are present. Head staff is robust with 20 full time, including a Camp Mom and 24/7 security (10 armed guards and two buildings with bullet proof, secure doors that can be safe houses.) Campers and staff practice fire, aquatic, and other emergency skills and drills on a routine basis. The health center is available with nurses on staff 24/7, a doctor present during clinic call each day, as well as a Clinician staff to support all campers in need of additional support. All senior staff are certified in American Red Cross first aid and CPR. Camp is on the local 911 Emergency Medical System with a hospital 5 miles away in Mukwonago. Camp can accommodate many Special Needs campers as well as those with Diabetes if they are "self sufficient" and need medical oversight. The kitchen is Kosher and nut free and offers gluten free and vegan options for all meals. The Dining Hall is air conditioned and food is very well received and highly rated by campers at the end of summer questionnaire. Every meal is accompanied with a fresh salad bar, sandwiches and fruit. Snack is also provided twice a day and canteen is available twice a week.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp had a very kind, friendly, fun vibe. There is not a heavy emphasis put on athletics, but instead on traditional, outdoor team and individual camp activities. Staff are very present, as they are checking up on campers between instructional periods. Beber has 5 core Jewish values that are the foundation of their programming. Intentional programming and Jewish traditions are built into activities, including music, art, Shabbat services, Israeli dancing and Havdalah. Prayers are said at meals and flag raising includes Israel's flag. Camp is gender inclusive and cabins are assigned by gender choice. There are non-gender specific bathroom facilities on site.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Camp offers bus service from the Chicagoland area and they offer transportation to pick up from Chicago O’Hare airport and Milwaukee airport. Beber Camp provides chaperone-escorted flights from a handful of communities, as well as for Pioneer Israel Trips. 2025 Chaperone Flights: First Session Denver; First Session Louisville; First Session Atlanta; First Session Memphis; First Session Cleveland; Second Session Denver. To allow their staff the opportunity to prepare for Second Session, First Session campers must be picked up. Eight-weekers are not permitted to stay at camp between sessions. Campers attending both sessions can spend the intersession weekend at home or with a Chicagoland family. Laundry services are provided for campers attending both sessions, and they are encouraged to leave the bulk of their luggage at camp. Campers and parents are encouraged to write letters. Parents may email or fax as well. No electronics except for MP3 players, e-readers without access to games and FitBits are welcome. Campers receive canteen twice weekly and have an opportunity to select candy, soda, snacks, and toiletries. Laundry is sent out every eight days and comes back two days later. Beber Camp provides opportunities for campers to meet with their B’nai Mitzvah tutor for any camper celebrating their B’nai Mitzvah within a few months following their time at camp. Campers write a postcard home weekly and will be given four pre-printed email cover sheets that can be scanned and sent home as another way to communicate. Parents can send one-way emails via the Campanion app. Parents can also view the daily photo stream here. Small packages are accepted, including magazines, Mad Libs, photos, newspaper clippings, gum and individual drink powder packets, but they must fit into a flat envelope. Phone calls to or from campers are not permitted unless there is an emergency or it is a camper's birthday.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Beber Camp hosts 350 co-ed campers on 340 acres of lakefront property in Mukwonago, Wisconsin. Three session lengths, as well as two leadership programs for high schoolers, blend wonderfully upgraded cabin structure with more than 30 activities ranging from beach volleyball, woodworking, dance, to cooking, paddleboarding and zip lining. A full medical team, 24/7 gated and on-site security, allergy friendly and conscious, gender and special needs inclusive with strong Jewish values and programming. Ideal for return-oriented families looking to expand and strengthen their Judaism, and for campers looking to take a break from intense year round athletics or for those who enjoy individual sports or classic outdoor camp activities and traditions.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Quality and cleanliness of facilities.
  - Air conditioning and ceiling fans in all cabins and throughout camp.
  - Happy feeling, cheerfulness and friendly nature of campers and staff.
  - The waterfront is beautiful.
  - The sprawling grounds are well maintained and spotless.
  - The ropes course is impressive.
  - Director's enthusiasm and passion for camp and the campers is infectious.
  - Upcoming upgrades to Crown Hall.
  - Addition of a BMX course.
  - Additional security measures.
  - Full medical team and 24/7 security.
  - Allergy friendly and conscious, gender and special needs inclusive.
  - Strong Jewish values and intentional programming.



#### Lowlights

  - Camp is on a non-private lake.
  - Campers cannot swim in the lake and it is mandatory to wear a lifevest when in the water.
  - Israel Trip has been cancelled for two consecutive years due to ongoing violence in Israel.
  - First Session campers must be picked up; eight-weekers are not permitted to stay at camp between sessions.
  - Phone calls to or from campers are not permitted unless there is an emergency or it is a camper's birthday.
  - No electronics except for MP3 players, e-readers without access to games and FitBits are welcome.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

There is not a heavy emphasis put on athletics, but instead on traditional, outdoor team and individual camp activities. Staff are very present, as they are checking up on campers between instructional periods. The annual Maccabiah Games is a big favorite and campers are given a team color for the duration of their time at Camp. Beber has 5 core Jewish values that are the foundation of their programming. Intentional programming and Jewish traditions are built into activities, including music, art, Shabbat services, Israeli dancing and Havdalah. Prayers are said at meals and flag raising includes Israel's flag. Camp is gender inclusive and cabins are assigned by gender choice. There are non-gender specific bathroom facilities on site. Camp offers bus service from the Chicagoland area and they offer transportation to pick up from Chicago O’Hare airport and Milwaukee airport. Beber Camp provides chaperone-escorted flights from a handful of communities, as well as for Pioneer Israel Trips. 2025 Chaperone Flights: - First Session Denver - First Session Louisville - First Session Atlanta - First Session Memphis - First Session Cleveland - Second Session Denver To allow their staff the opportunity to prepare for Second Session, First Session campers must be picked up. Eight-weekers are not permitted to stay at camp between sessions. Campers attending both sessions can spend the intersession weekend at home or with a Chicagoland family. Laundry services are provided for campers attending both sessions, and they are encouraged to leave the bulk of their luggage at camp. Campers and parents are encouraged to write letters. Parents may email or fax as well. No electronics except for MP3 players, e-readers without access to games and FitBit's are welcome. Campers receive canteen twice weekly and have an opportunity to select candy, soda, snacks, and toiletries. Laundry is sent out every eight days and comes back two days later. Beber Camp provides opportunities for campers to meet with their B’nai Mitzvah tutor for any camper celebrating their B’nai Mitzvah within a few months following their time at camp. Campers write a postcard home weekly and will be given four pre-printed email cover sheets that can be scanned and sent home as another way to communicate. Parents can send one-way emails via the Campanion app. Parents can also view the daily photo stream here. Small packages are accepted, including magazines, Mad Libs, photos, newspaper clippings, gum and individual drink powder packets, but they must fit into a flat envelope. Phone calls to or from campers are not permitted unless there is an emergency or it is a camper's birthday. Beber Camp hosts 350 co?ed campers on 340 acres of lakefront property in Mukwonago, Wisconsin. Three session lengths, as well as two leadership programs for high schoolers, blend wonderfully upgraded cabin structure with more than 30 activities ranging from beach volleyball, woodworking, dance, to cooking, paddleboarding and zip lining. A full medical team, 24/7 gated and on-site security, allergy friendly and conscious, gender and special needs inclusive with strong Jewish values and programming. Ideal for return?oriented families looking to expand and strengthen their Judaism. Ideal for campers looking to take a break from intense year round athletics or for those who enjoy individual sports or classic outdoor camp activities and traditions. Highlights include the quality and cleanliness of facilities, air conditioning and ceiling fans in all cabins and throughout camp, as well as the happy feeling, cheerfulness and friendly nature of campers and staff. The waterfront is beautiful, the sprawling grounds are well maintained and spotless and the ropes course is impressive. The Director's enthusiasm and passion for camp and the campers is infectious and upcoming upgrades to Crown Hall and the addition of a BMX course and additional security measures are exciting.




### Author: Alli

### Updated: 2025-08-27

title: Laurel South — Laurie — 7-18-25
text:
# Laurel South



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Laurel South - Co-ed camp founded in 1993. Current directors are Roger & Dagni Christian and Drew & Crimson Gallagher. 3.5 week sessions. 420 current campers at capacity with 300 on waitlist. Geographic diversity: 23+ states, 6+ countries. Filled by end of September.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Age range: 2nd-9th grade (some 1st grade siblings). Gender split: approximately 220 girls, 200 boys. Florida represents \~15% of camper population. Staffing Structure: 175 camp counselors; 300 total staff; 20% international staff. Cabin ratios: 10-12 campers with 4-5 counselors. Limited former campers as counselors (must wait until after first year of college). Specialized expertise required for tennis, gymnastics. Directors: Roger & Dagni Christian and Drew & Crimson Gallagher. Sun, 29 Jun 25 Crimson & Tyler (boys head director) toured Shari, Beth, Lisa, Jennifer & Laurie.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabin ratios: 10-12 campers with 4-5 counselors. Other living quarters details: Unknown.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront: 2 ski boats, 2 jet skis. 3 lakes - fishing frogs & turtles, swimming & waterskiing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

8 A/B day schedule with S-days. 5 scheduled activities + 1 free period daily. Required through 6th grade: Tennis, Swimming, Two team sports. 7th grade and up: complete choice. Individual scheduling system. IC (Individual Choice) period daily.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Facilities include: Tennis (10-12 courts), Theater, Culinary studio, Arts & crafts center, Gymnastics facility, Discovery zone, Riding stable (10 horses). Special Programs & Traditions: Spirit Days (mid-session), Moose Bowl, Theater productions, Banquet night (lobster/steak options), Big Brother/Sister program. Inter-camp activities resumed post-COVID.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical & Safety: 24/7 health center staffing with a full-time pediatrician, full-time nurse practitioner, 10 RNs, and 2 nurse aides. Hospital access: Bridgeton (20 minutes), Portland Maine Med (40 minutes). Can accommodate diabetic campers. OIT therapy available. Virtual therapy sessions available. Food & Facilities: Dining hall (air-conditioned). Banquet night (lobster/steak options).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Co-ed community with broad geographic diversity. Big Brother/Sister program. Type of camper that does best: "soft, gentle, not competitive but wants to try everything".



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation & Logistics: Chaperoned flights from Fort Lauderdale, JFK, LA, DC. Bus service from Westchester and Boston. Communication & Policies: Two phone calls allowed (middle week only). Daily photos posted (400-500 per day). Letter writing required every other day. No package policy (except birthdays). No electronics except: Kindle Paperwhite and music players. Weekly laundry service. Visiting day on departure day only. Additional cost programs: Riding, Fly fishing, Trip expenses.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Laurel South is a co-ed camp founded in 1993 with 3.5 week sessions and a capacity of 420 campers. The program blends structure and choice with an 8 A/B day schedule, required core activities through 6th grade, and an IC (Individual Choice) period daily. Robust facilities and traditions span tennis (10–12 courts), theater, culinary, gymnastics, riding, Spirit Days, Moose Bowl, and a banquet night with lobster/steak options. Type of camper that does best is "soft, gentle, not competitive but wants to try everything."



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Filled by end of September
  - Geographic diversity: 23+ states, 6+ countries
  - 3.5 week sessions
  - 420 current campers at capacity
  - Big Brother/Sister program
  - 8 A/B day schedule with S-days
  - IC (Individual Choice) period daily
  - Required through 6th grade: Tennis, Swimming, Two team sports; 7th grade and up: complete choice
  - Waterfront: 2 ski boats, 2 jet skis; 3 lakes for fishing, swimming & waterskiing
  - Facilities: Tennis (10-12 courts), Theater, Culinary studio, Arts & crafts, Gymnastics, Discovery zone, Riding stable (10 horses)
  - Spirit Days, Moose Bowl, Theater productions, Banquet night (lobster/steak options)
  - 24/7 health center with pediatrician, NP, 10 RNs, 2 nurse aides
  - Hospital access: Bridgeton (20 minutes), Portland Maine Med (40 minutes)
  - Can accommodate diabetic campers; OIT therapy available; Virtual therapy sessions available
  - Dining hall (air-conditioned)
  - Daily photos posted (400-500 per day)
  - Chaperoned flights from Fort Lauderdale, JFK, LA, DC; Bus service from Westchester and Boston



#### Lowlights

  - 300 on waitlist
  - Limited former campers as counselors (must wait until after first year of college)
  - Two phone calls allowed (middle week only)
  - No package policy (except birthdays)
  - No electronics except: Kindle Paperwhite and music players
  - Visiting day on departure day only
  - Additional cost programs: Riding, Fly fishing, Trip expenses
  - Living quarters details beyond cabin ratios: Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Robin Hood New Hampshire
  - Birchmont
  - Kenmont/Kenwood
  - Timber Lake West
  - Silver Lake

title: Mataponi — Laurie — 7-18-25
text:
# Mataponi



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Mataponi - founded in 1910. Directors since 1994 Marcy and Dan Isdaner; daughter Nikki recently became a director. 350 girls; non-uniform. Brothers at Cedar, Takajo, Skylemar. 47-day session (6.5 weeks). One 4 week option grades 2-7. Session runs through August 8th. Cost: $17,400 tuition + $400 trip/activity fee. 94% re-enrollment rate.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Demographics & Geographic Distribution: Age range: Rising 2nd grade through 10th grade. Geographic breakdown: Florida: 27-28 campers; Philadelphia: 20 campers; California: 25 campers; International: 2 campers (Brazil, Spain). Strong enrollment in younger divisions: Junior A (2nd-3rd grade): \~60 campers; Junior B (4th grade): \~60 campers. Staffing Structure: 105 bunk counselors; 135 program staff; 220 total staff including support services. Strong Australian counselor contingent. 70& 18 former campers as counselors. Mon, 30 Jun 25 Dan toured Beth, Lisa, Jennifer, Shari and Laurie. His daughter Nikki is taking on more responsibilities and seemed great. Girls seem to really like her.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Staff housing integrated throughout campus. Laundry service with individual barcoded bags. Daily towel service. Camper cabin/bathroom details: Unknown.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront with multiple docks; separate ski dock and tubing area. 5 motorboats. Multiple sailing vessels. Beautiful setting, nice lake. Early morning water skiing option. Structured morning activities include a 2-hour waterfront block for juniors.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Daily programming: 5 activity periods per day. Structured morning activities: 2-hour waterfront block for juniors. Early morning water skiing option. Afternoon electives increase with age: 7 year olds, 1-2 electives; 8-11, 2-3 electives; 12-15, up to 5. Evening activities and special events. Daily snacks at 3:15 PM.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Athletic Facilities: 12 tennis courts; 8 pickle ball courts; indoor gymnasium ("Matadome"); multiple athletic fields. Arts Center: Ceramics studio; drawing/painting areas; arts & crafts; sewing stations; fashion design; digital photography. Tennis academy (subcontracted): 3,000 hours of private lessons. Horseback riding: 10 horses (63 riders); extra fee program; multiple riding packages available. Ropes course: Required through 6th grade; elective for older campers. Inter-camp sports program. Big sister program. Community service initiatives: World of Change program; Camp Sunshine partnership; foster care backpack project. Nice culinary program.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health & Safety: On-site medical staff; comprehensive allergy protocols; daily bunk sanitization. Dining Hall: Single seating capacity; 4 buffet stations; dedicated allergy kitchen; outdoor seating options. Nice culinary program.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp looks great. Beautiful setting, nice lake. Non-uniform. Girls that do well "friendly, grounded girls that want to try things". Dan was a bit arrogant when we arrived and said he didn't know why we were there because he has very few spots for next summer except for older girls.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation & Logistics: Multiple transportation options: 5 buses from Westchester/Massachusetts; flights from Newark, JFK, LaGuardia; dedicated Florida contingent travel; early arrival for West Coast campers. Parent communication/tech: Structured parent communication with regular photo updates, scheduled video calls, and birthday calls. Extra costs: $400 trip/activity fee. Horseback riding is an extra fee program (multiple riding packages available). Tech policy specifics: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Mataponi is a 350-girl, non-uniform camp with a beautiful setting and a nice lake. Founded in 1910 and directed by Marcy and Dan Isdaner (with their daughter Nikki recently becoming a director), it offers a 47-day session (6.5 weeks) with one 4-week option for grades 2-7 that runs through August 8th. Daily life features five activity periods, a structured 2-hour waterfront block for juniors, early morning water skiing, and age-appropriate electives plus evening activities and special events. Families should expect $17,400 tuition plus a $400 trip/activity fee and a community that favors "friendly, grounded girls that want to try things," with a reported 94% re-enrollment rate.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 94% re-enrollment rate.
  - Beautiful setting, nice lake with multiple docks, separate ski dock/tubing, and 5 motorboats.
  - Strong Australian counselor contingent and robust staffing (220 total).
  - Facility depth: 12 tennis courts, 8 pickle ball courts, indoor gym ("Matadome"), multiple athletic fields, and a well-equipped arts center.
  - Tennis academy with 3,000 hours of private lessons; riding program with 10 horses (extra fee).
  - Comprehensive allergy protocols and a dedicated allergy kitchen; single seating dining with 4 buffet stations and outdoor seating.
  - Structured parent communication with regular photo updates and scheduled video calls.
  - Big sister program and community service initiatives (World of Change, Camp Sunshine, foster care backpack project).



#### Lowlights

  - Dan was a bit arrogant when we arrived and said he didn't know why we were there because he has very few spots for next summer except for older girls.
  - Limited spots for next summer except for older girls.



#### Similar Camps

  - Vega
  - Pinecliffe
  - Matoaka (although they offer sessions)
  - Tripp
