title: Pinecliffe — Laurie — 7-18-25
text:
# Pinecliffe



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Pinecliffe was founded in 1917. Current director is Patty Lifter, associate director Maja Toncic. Capacity: 200 girls. Uniform including camp bedding. Age range: 7-15 years (1st-9th grade). Session length: 7 weeks. First-time camper option: First 4-weeks one time only any age (15 2025). Brothers attend Androscoggin, Cedar, Wildwood, Winnebago.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Sat, 28 Jun 25 Maja Toncic toured Beth, Shari, Lisa, Jennifer and Laurie. Staff ratio: 75 counselors for 200 campers. International presence: 14 international campers. Alumni connection: 28% of campers are alumni children. Girls who do well want to try everything, like to do activities, are kind to each other and supportive of one another.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Housing: bunks have 2 bathrooms/ 2 sinks and there are 2 showerhouses. Junior bunks: 6-8 campers with 2-3 counselors. Senior bunks: 8-12 campers. Oldest groups: Up to 40 campers per cabin.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

3 water ski boats. Swim lessons (Monday-Friday). Specific swim attire for lessons.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Daily Program Structure: 7 periods per day; 4 morning periods and 3 afternoon periods, with an additional choice period at day’s end. Schedule variation by age: Juniors (6th grade and below) have more assigned activities; Seniors (7th-9th grade) have more elective choices. Mandatory activities for all: Tennis; Swim lessons (Monday-Friday). Age-group plays.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Activity spaces: 6 tennis courts (2 hard, 4 clay); air-conditioned gymnastics facility; multiple art studios (jewelry, ceramics, crafts); dance studio; nature center. No culinary program. Special Programs & Traditions: Color War with two teams (Brown Teddies vs Blue Ellies) runs entire summer; captains elected from 15-year-olds. HORSEBACK RIDING - off premises 7 minutes away; maximum of 14 kids do a full week of riding every day; offered two weeks within summer (7 each week). Trip Program by age: Juniors: day trips and on-campus overnights; 12-year-olds: Franconia Notch hiking; 13-year-olds: Kennebec River rafting, optional Mt. Washington trek; 14-year-olds: Bar Harbor trip, optional Baxter State Park; 15-year-olds: Quebec trip. Weekly traditions: Friday non-denominational services by the water (campers wear white), Wednesday cookouts, Sunday outdoor pasta dinners.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical support: 6 nurses on staff. Pediatrician available. Access to Stevens Memorial Hospital. Dining hall (all-camp seated meals).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall Impression - Patty Lifter, director was present while we were there but sat at another table and made little effort to speak to us. She is definitely not warm and fuzzy. It was raining so it was hard to get a feeling of what campers are like - very little improvements have been made to the camp over the years.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation options: Buses from Boston, Westchester, New York. Chaperoned flights from DC, Philadelphia, Chicago. Communication: 3 phone calls per summer (2 pre-visiting day, 1 post). Photos posted Wednesdays and Saturdays. No care packages allowed. No cell phones permitted. Uniform requirements: Standard camp uniform. Specific swim attire for lessons. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Pinecliffe was founded in 1917 and serves 200 girls ages 7–15 for a 7-week session (first-time campers can do four weeks one time only). The daily structure is 7 periods (4 morning, 3 afternoon) with an additional choice period, mandatory tennis and Monday–Friday swim lessons, age-group plays, and more elective choice for Seniors. Facilities include bunks with bathrooms and showerhouses, 6 tennis courts (2 hard, 4 clay), 3 water ski boats, an air-conditioned gymnastics facility, multiple art studios, a dance studio, and a nature center. Signature traditions and programs include Color War (Brown Teddies vs Blue Ellies) running the entire summer, a tiered trip program culminating in Quebec, off-site riding 7 minutes away, weekly services/cookouts/pasta dinners, and strong medical support (6 nurses, pediatrician, and access to Stevens Memorial Hospital).



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Founded in 1917.
  - Capacity: 200 girls with 75 counselors for 200 campers.
  - Age range: 7-15 years (1st-9th grade); 7-week session; one-time 4-week option for first-time campers.
  - Daily structure: 7 periods per day plus an additional choice period; more choice for Seniors.
  - Mandatory tennis and Monday–Friday swim lessons.
  - Color War (Brown Teddies vs Blue Ellies) runs entire summer; captains from 15-year-olds.
  - Trip Program by age culminating in a Quebec trip for 15-year-olds.
  - HORSEBACK RIDING off premises 7 minutes away; max 14 kids per week (two weeks offered).
  - 3 water ski boats; air-conditioned gymnastics; multiple art studios; dance studio; nature center.
  - International presence: 14 international campers; Alumni connection: 28% of campers are alumni children.
  - Weekly traditions: Friday non-denominational services by the water (white attire), Wednesday cookouts, Sunday outdoor pasta dinners.
  - Transportation from Boston/Westchester/NY by bus and chaperoned flights from DC/Philadelphia/Chicago.
  - Medical support: 6 nurses, pediatrician available, access to Stevens Memorial Hospital.



#### Lowlights

  - Overall impression: director made little effort to speak to us and is definitely not warm and fuzzy.
  - It was raining so it was hard to get a feeling of what campers are like.
  - Very little improvements have been made to the camp over the years.
  - No culinary program.
  - Oldest groups: up to 40 campers per cabin.
  - Limited communication: 3 phone calls per summer; photos posted only Wednesdays and Saturdays; no care packages; no cell phones.



#### Similar Camps

  - Fernwood
  - Tripp Lake
  - Mataponi
  - Tapawingo
  - Walden
  - Vega



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

She is definitly not warm and fuzzy. It was raining so it was hard to get a feeling of what campers are like - very little improvements have been made to the camp over the years Girls who do well” want to try everything, like to do activities, are kind to each other and supportive of one another” Similar programs - Fernwood, Tripp Lake, Mataponi, Tapawingo, Walden, Vega

title: Tripp Lake — Laurie — 7-17-25
text:
# Tripp Lake



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Overview - Tripp Lake has been in operation since 1911. Since 2024 directors are Katie Milne & Liz Kahn. 250 current campers with capacity for 300. 95% Jewish demographic. Do two short Jewish prayers but consider to be non-denominational, more about friendship. Age range: Finishing 1st grade ("junior zeros") through finishing 10th grade. Girls. Uniform. 7 weeks; 4 weeks one time only for girls finishing 5th and younger. Geographic distribution: 30–35% from New York, remaining from Florida, California, Connecticut. International campers: 8 total (6 from Spain, 2 from Canada).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Directors are Katie Milne & Liz Kahn (since 2024). 250 current campers (capacity 300); girls finishing 1st through 10th grade. Staff ratio: 2:1 staff to camper ratio (approximately 125–130 program staff). 50% international staff. 95% Jewish demographic; do two short Jewish prayers but consider to be non-denominational, more about friendship. Geographic distribution: 30–35% from New York, remaining from Florida, California, Connecticut. International campers: 8 total (6 from Spain, 2 from Canada).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Circular campus layout progressing by age. 6–8 campers per bunk with 2 counselors. Only oldest campers (1AB) in bunk beds. Dedicated shower houses by age group. 3 shower houses; bathrooms in all bunks with exception of oldest campers.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront activities: sailing, kayaking, paddleboarding, water skiing. 4 ski boats. Swimming & waterfront activities are 4–5 times a week. Beautiful lake.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 periods daily with choice periods built in (1 elective for youngest; 2–3 as they get older). Daily schedule varies (not fixed weekly). Age group-based activities vs. bunk activities. Swimming, tennis & waterfront activities are 4–5 times a week. Choice & Chill period daily for free activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports include basketball, soccer, softball, field hockey, lacrosse, volleyball, gaga. Facilities & Activities: 18 tennis courts; great art studios - fine arts, arts & crafts, pottery & ceramics, silver, copper, photography, weaving, culinary, fashion design; filmmaking, creative writing. Gymnastics facility. Theater. Ropes course with 7 high elements. 8 horses with 30 riders annually. Air-conditioned facilities: health center, art center. Team structure: 4 big teams: Tigers (black/orange), Giants (blue/white), Cubs (red/white), Climbers (red/blue). Special little teams for 5th grade and under. Brother-sister activities with Takajo 4 times per summer. Unique Programming Elements: Daily team activities and competitions; age-specific theater productions; special traditions (birthday celebrations, team announcements); evening activities and talent shows; mini-camp intensive sports training post-visiting day. Plans for new shower houses & beach volleyball.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

8 nurses and rotating weekly doctor. Nearest hospital 20 minutes away. Wellness coordinator for mental health support. Special accommodations for therapy continuity. Weekly lice checks. Special diet accommodations with dedicated chef and facilities.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Considered non-denominational and more about friendship. Girls who go to Tripp Lake are "girls that want to try everything (there is choice but need to try activities; encourage kindness & courage)". Camp looked great. Fabulous arts, beautiful lake, Liz & Katie are great and a lot more positive energy.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation & Logistics: Bus departures: Needham and Westchester (3 buses from Westchester). Chaperone flights from: Florida (20 campers on flight), Los Angeles, Chicago, Philadelphia, JFK, Newark, DC. Visiting Day: Girls on Friday, Boys (Takajo) on Saturday. Parent accommodations for divorced families or multiple children. Communication & Technology: Two video calls home per summer (10–12 days in, 10 days before end). Additional calls for birthdays and special circumstances. Tech-free environment (Campfire players for music). One package allowed for 6th grade and older (team-related items). Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Tripp Lake has been in operation since 1911 and is a seven-week girls’ camp (with a one-time four-week option for girls finishing 5th and younger) with 250 campers and capacity for 300. Since 2024 the directors are Katie Milne and Liz Kahn; the camp is considered non-denominational and more about friendship, with a 95% Jewish demographic and two short Jewish prayers. Campers finishing 1st through 10th grade choose from robust arts, athletics, and waterfront on a six-period day with Choice & Chill and daily team activities on a beautiful lake. Camp looked great—fabulous arts, strong facilities, and Liz & Katie are great and a lot more positive energy.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Fabulous arts.
  - Beautiful lake.
  - Liz & Katie are great and a lot more positive energy.
  - 18 tennis courts; 4 ski boats.
  - Great art studios - fine arts, arts & crafts, pottery & ceramics, silver, copper, photography, weaving, culinary, fashion design; filmmaking, creative writing.
  - Team structure: 4 big teams and special little teams for 5th grade and under.



#### Lowlights

  - Two video calls home per summer.
  - Tech-free environment (Campfire players for music).
  - Bathrooms in all bunks with exception of oldest campers.
  - 7 weeks, 4 weeks one time only for girls finishing 5th and younger.



#### Similar Camps

  - Pinecliffe
  - Vega
  - Robindel
  - Mataponi

title: Med-O-Lark- Laurie — David Highfill — 7-17-25
text:
# Med-O-Lark- Laurie



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Med-O-Lark has been operating as an arts-focused summer camp since 1967, giving it a legacy of nearly six decades. While its roots trace back even further through earlier camps on the same site, its current creative and inclusive incarnation began with its re-founding in 1967 as a non-traditional, coed arts camp. Current Directors: Sadie Kurzban & David Weinstein. Creative arts camp focused on self-expression rather than competition. 2025 current enrollment: \~125 campers (300+ capacity). Peak session reaches slightly over 200 campers. Gender ratio: 70% female, 30% male. Age range: 7-16 years old (majority 11-13). International campers comprise \~20% of population.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lisa, Shari, Jennifer, Beth and I met with Sadie Kurzban, owner with David Weinstein. We met David briefly. Current Directors: Sadie Kurzban & David Weinstein. 125 total staff members; 65 cabin counselors; 40% international staff. Cabin ratios: 3-4 counselors per 12-20 campers. Gender identity-affirming housing policies. Sadie is very energetic and a great addition.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Mixed-age cabins with bathroom facilities. Cabin ratios: 3-4 counselors per 12-20 campers. Weekly laundry service. Facilities are a bit run down and some buildings in need of repair. Future plans: New cabin construction; dining hall improvements; classroom upgrades.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront activities. New sailboat.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming Structure: 6 activity periods daily: 3 primary activities (2-week duration) and 3 elective activities (4-day rotation). Choice-based program - campers select their activities. Sampler days allow testing \~20 activities over two weeks. No mandatory activities except participation requirement.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Specialized Activities. Art Programs: Glass blowing (12+ years), pottery, metalworking, enameling, fashion design, digital arts/3D printing, stained glass. Performance Programs: Theater/musical theater; dance (hip-hop, jazz, ballet, modern); rock band/music recording; stage makeup (new 2025). Other Activities: Culinary arts; woodworking; sports/tennis; waterfront activities; horseback riding (off-site, additional fee). 2025 Improvements: New welcome center; improved culinary center; new Gaga pit; enhanced music program inventory; new sailboat; stage makeup program added. Future plans: Program expansion; potential addition of social worker positioning.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

4 full-time RNs. Partnership with clinic (11 miles away). Hospital: Augusta General (22 miles/25 minutes). Nut-free facility. Can accommodate most dietary restrictions.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Creative arts camp focused on self-expression rather than competition. Very inclusive environment. Non-traditional, coed arts camp. Down to earth and doesn’t want a competitive environment.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Communication & Technology: No personal phones allowed. Supervised 5-minute phone calls after first 3 days. Daily photos uploaded with facial recognition. Strong social media presence. Email updates to new parents within first days. Packages allowed (screened for food). Extra costs: Horseback riding (off-site, additional fee).



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Creative arts camp focused on self-expression rather than competition, with a non-traditional, coed, inclusive vibe. Down to earth campers who don’t want a competitive environment will find great arts and lots of choice. Facilities are a bit run down and some buildings in need of repair, though 2025 improvements include a new welcome center, improved culinary center, a new Gaga pit, enhanced music program inventory, and a new sailboat. 4 full-time RNs, a nearby clinic and hospital, nut-free dining, and clear parent communication and tech policies support a safe, well-run experience.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Creative arts camp focused on self-expression rather than competition.
  - Very inclusive environment.
  - Choice-based program with 6 activity periods daily and sampler days.
  - Specialized art programs: Glass blowing (12+ years), pottery, metalworking, enameling, fashion design, digital arts/3D printing, stained glass.
  - Performance programs: Theater/musical theater, dance (hip-hop, jazz, ballet, modern), rock band/music recording; stage makeup (new 2025).
  - 4 full-time RNs; nut-free facility; can accommodate most dietary restrictions.
  - 2025 improvements: New welcome center, improved culinary center, new Gaga pit, enhanced music program inventory, new sailboat.



#### Lowlights

  - Facilities are a bit run down and some buildings in need of repair.
  - No personal phones allowed (supervised 5-minute phone calls after first 3 days).
  - Horseback riding (off-site, additional fee).



#### Similar Camps

  - Hidden Valley
  - French Woods (but more competitive)



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Lisa, Shari, Jennifer, Beth and I met with Sadie Kurzban owner with David Weinstein Med-O-Lark has been operating as an arts-focused summer camp since 1967, giving it a legacy of nearly six decades. While its roots trace back even further through earlier camps on the same site, its current creative and inclusive incarnation began with its re-founding in 1967 as a non-traditional, coed arts camp. Current Directors: • Sadie Kurzban & David Weinstein Creative arts camp focused on self-expression rather than competition 2025 current enrollment: \~125 campers (300+ capacity) Peak session reaches slightly over 200 campers Gender ratio: 70% female, 30% male Age range: 7-16 years old (majority 11-13) International campers comprise \~20% of population Programming Structure 6 activity periods daily: 3 primary activities (2-week duration) 3 elective activities (4-day rotation) Choice-based program - campers select their activities Sampler days allow testing \~20 activities over two weeks No mandatory activities except participation requirement Specialized Activities Art Programs: Glass blowing (12+ years) Pottery Metalworking Enameling Fashion design Digital arts/3D printing Stained glass Performance Programs: Theater/Musical theater Dance (hip-hop, jazz, ballet, modern) Rock band/Music recording Stage makeup (new 2025) Other Activities: Culinary arts Woodworking Sports/Tennis Waterfront activities Horseback riding (off-site, additional fee) Staff & Housing 125 total staff members 65 cabin counselors 40% international staff Cabin ratios: 3-4 counselors per 12-20 campers Mixed-age cabins with bathroom facilities Gender identity-affirming housing policies Health & Safety 4 full-time RNs Partnership with clinic (11 miles away) Hospital: Augusta General (22 miles/25 minutes) Nut-free facility Can accommodate most dietary restrictions Weekly laundry service Communication & Technology No personal phones allowed Supervised 5-minute phone calls after first 3 days Daily photos uploaded with facial recognition Strong social media presence Email updates to new parents within first days Packages allowed (screened for food) 2025 Improvements Recent upgrades: New welcome center Improved culinary center New Gaga pit Enhanced music program inventory New sailboat Stage makeup program added Future plans: New cabin construction Dining hall improvements Classroom upgrades Program expansion Potential addition of social worker positioning Type of camper Down to earth doesn’t want want competitive environment. Camp offers great arts. Very inclusive environment. Facilities are a bit run down and some buildings in need or repair. Sadie is very energetic and a great edition. We me David briefly. title: Fernwood Cove — Laurie — 7-16-25
text:
# Fernwood Cove



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Fernwood Cove has been in operation since 1999. The directors are Jim and Beigette Gill, who purchased the camp in 2004 and oversee operations year-round. Total capacity: 200 campers. Current enrollment first session 170 campers down from covid; second session 200 campers. 3.5 week sessions. Girls uniform. Age range: 8-15 years (some 6-year-olds accepted in rookie program). 80-85% retention rate. Non-denominational.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

10% international campers per session. More Europeans first session, more South Americans second session. 98% international staff. Geographic distribution: Strong in New England; need growth in Southeast. Big sister program. 80-85% retention rate. Second session currently full (207 campers). The directors are Jim and Beigette Gill, who purchased the camp in 2004 and oversee operations year-round.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks have no electricity. Have sinks and toilets and several shower houses. No mirrors in facilities. New 2023-24 improvements: All shower houses renovated; new staff housing; culinary arts facility. Future plans: Insulated gymnasium, indoor riding arena, new maintenance building, additional tennis courts planned.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront on private lake (own 3/4 of shorefront). Required activities: one week swimming and one week canoeing. Hydrofoil program (7th grade and up).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 periods daily (3 morning, 3 afternoon). All activities elective but must choose from every area: Waterfront, Athletics, Arts. Schedule changes weekly. Required activities: one week swimming; one week canoeing. Weekly trips every Thursday (nature-based only).



## 6 · Program Highlights

Natural horsemanship program (75 of 200 kids participate). Hydrofoil program (7th grade and up). Three-week commitments required for: Musical theater, dance production, rock band. Extensive arts program including: Silver smithing, stained glass, digital photography, woodworking, fabric arts. Beautiful 16 horses owned by camp.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

4 full-time RNs plus 2-3 local nurses. Partnership with Camp Docs for telehealth. Two hospitals within 15 minutes. Can accommodate most medical needs except severe diabetes. Social worker on staff. Therapy continuation available during camp.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The vibe at Fernwood Cove is focused on community, personal growth, simple living, and inclusivity. The camp creates an atmosphere that is welcoming and supportive, emphasizing the core values of loyalty, personal growth, tradition, respect for the outdoors, and friendship. Same color war team for life. Fully solar powered (110% capacity). Extensive sustainability program. Jim & Beigette are excellent directors. Facilities are beautiful and campers were very engaged. Nice girls very engaged in the activities.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bus service from: Lincoln Center, Port Chester, Enfield CT, Boston suburbs. No visiting day - informal self-guided tours on pickup day. No phones/devices. No photos during session (posted after). Daily director podcast instead of photos. Tuition: \~$10,000 per session. Foundation provides \~10 scholarships annually. $125-150K in tuition forgiveness annually. All equipment provided. Weekly laundry service included. Uniform required 100% of time. No private lessons except for maintaining elite training programs. Not for a parent that needs to see daily photos.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

The vibe at Fernwood Cove is focused on community, personal growth, simple living, and inclusivity. It is a 3.5 week girls program for ages 8-15 (some 6-year-olds accepted in the rookie program) with six daily elective periods and nature-based weekly trips. Families should be comfortable with no phones/devices and no photos during session (daily director podcast instead), a girls uniform required 100% of the time, and simple living including bunks with no electricity. Standout offerings include the natural horsemanship program, hydrofoil (7th grade and up), and an extensive arts program, all led by excellent directors Jim and Beigette Gill.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Fully solar powered (110% capacity)
  - Natural horsemanship program (75 of 200 kids participate)
  - Hydrofoil program (7th grade and up)
  - Extensive arts program including silver smithing, stained glass, digital photography, woodworking, and fabric arts
  - All shower houses renovated; new staff housing; culinary arts facility
  - Daily director podcast instead of photos
  - Facilities are beautiful and campers were very engaged



#### Lowlights

  - Not for a parent that needs to see daily photos
  - Bunks have no electricity
  - No visiting day - informal self-guided tours on pickup day
  - Can accommodate most medical needs except severe diabetes
  - No private lessons except for maintaining elite training programs
  - Second session currently full (207 campers)



#### Similar Camps

  - Somerset
  - Kippewa
  - Matoaka

title: Growing Routes — Laurie — 7-16-25
text:
# Growing Routes



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Met with Chloe Rowse. Growing Routes has been in operation since 2018. The program was founded to provide small-group explorations focusing on confidence, creativity, and compassion. Its roots trace back to 2006, when founder Chloë Rowse started a summer camp known as Camp Chloë, which later evolved into Growing Routes and the current camp. First year at new permanent location after 20 years of renting facilities. All-girls and gender-expansive youth camp. 4 sessions total: one 1-week session, three 2-week sessions. Age groups: 8-10, 10-12, 11-13, and 14-16. Current capacity: 20-22 campers maximum. Current session: 7 campers (11-13 age group). Geographic reach: Portland, Maine, New Jersey, California, Boston.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

All-girls and gender-expansive youth camp with age groups 8-10, 10-12, 11-13, and 14-16. Current session: 7 campers (11-13 age group) with geographic reach from Portland, Maine, New Jersey, California, and Boston. Staff Structure: 2 counselors per cabin when at full capacity. Current staff: 5 staff members for 7 campers. Minimum 2 wilderness first responders on staff. Doctor on call.



## 3 · Living Quarters

New bathhouse and cabins. Two main cabins with 8-bed capacity each. Amenities include: Vegetable garden and flower gardens; Mobile chicken coop; Apple orchard (65 trees); Greenhouse; Yoga/dance studio; 3 miles of trails.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Access to lake for swimming/boating. Swimming with designated test areas. Kayaking and canoeing available.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Daily Schedule: 7:45 AM wake-up; Morning contributions (cabin cleaning, personal care); Structured activities (crafts, swimming); Rest time (letter writing, reading); Free time (showers, activities). Small group size allows flexibility in scheduling.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Cooking projects (pasta making, baking). Gardening activities. Creative projects. Small-group explorations focusing on confidence, creativity, and compassion.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Minimum 2 wilderness first responders on staff. Doctor on call. 20 minutes from hospital/urgent care. Swimming with designated test areas. Food: cooking projects (pasta making, baking).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very sweet girls. Most are from Maine. Nice program for down to earth girl that wants to be in nature and experience sleepaway camp. No religious programming.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Portland airport pickup offered. Parent communication allowed based on individual needs. Full and partial scholarships available. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown. Additional travel logistics: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Growing Routes is an all-girls and gender-expansive youth camp focused on small-group explorations that build confidence, creativity, and compassion. First year at a new permanent location after 20 years of renting facilities, with access to a lake, gardens, and a cozy cabin setup. Daily rhythms include morning contributions, structured activities like crafts and swimming, rest time, and free time, with small group size allowing flexibility. Very sweet girls, mostly from Maine; a nice program for a down to earth girl that wants to be in nature and experience sleepaway camp.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Small group size allows flexibility in scheduling.
  - Access to lake for swimming/boating.
  - New bathhouse and cabins; two main cabins with 8-bed capacity each.
  - Amenities include: Vegetable garden and flower gardens; Mobile chicken coop; Apple orchard (65 trees); Greenhouse; Yoga/dance studio; 3 miles of trails.
  - Minimum 2 wilderness first responders on staff; doctor on call; 20 minutes from hospital/urgent care.
  - Full and partial scholarships available.
  - Portland airport pickup offered.
  - All-girls and gender-expansive youth camp.
  - Founded to provide small-group explorations focusing on confidence, creativity, and compassion.



#### Lowlights

  - Tech policy: Unknown.
  - Extra costs: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Growing Routes Fri, 27 Jun 25 Camp Overview - Met with Chloe Rowse - Growing Routes has been in operation since 2018. The program was founded to provide small-group explorations focusing on confidence, creativity, and compassion. Its roots trace back to 2006, when founder Chloë Rowse started a summer camp known as Camp Chloë, which later evolved into Growing Routes and the current camp. First year at new permanent location after 20 years of renting facilities All-girls and gender-expansive youth camp 4 sessions total: one 1-week session, three 2-week sessions Age groups: 8-10, 10-12, 11-13, and 14-16 Current capacity: 20-22 campers maximum Current session: 7 campers (11-13 age group) Geographic reach: Portland, Maine, New Jersey, California, Boston Facility & Operations New bathhouse and cabins Two main cabins with 8-bed capacity each Amenities include: Vegetable garden and flower gardens Mobile chicken coop Apple orchard (65 trees) Greenhouse Yoga/dance studio 3 miles of trails Access to lake for swimming/boating Staff Structure: 2 counselors per cabin when at full capacity Current staff: 5 staff members for 7 campers Minimum 2 wilderness first responders on staff Doctor on call 20 minutes from hospital/urgent care Program Details Daily Schedule: 7:45 AM wake-up Morning contributions (cabin cleaning, personal care) Structured activities (crafts, swimming) Rest time (letter writing, reading) Free time (showers, activities) Program Features: Swimming with designated test areas Kayaking and canoeing available Cooking projects (pasta making, baking) Gardening activities Creative projects Small group size allows flexibility in scheduling Parent communication allowed based on individual needs Full and partial scholarships available Very sweet girls. Most are from Maine Nice program for down to earth girl that wants to be in nature and experience sleepaway camp Portland airport pickup offered No religious programming Very sweet girls. Most are from Maine Nice program for down to earth girl that wants to be in nature and experience sleepaway camp

title: Interlaken — Brooke — 7-16-25
text:
# Interlaken



## 1 · Program Snapshot

I visited Camp Interlaken on Sunday 7/13/25. Camp Interlaken JCC, formally The Steve and Shari Sadek Family Camp Interlaken JCC, is a Jewish summer camp located in Eagle River, Wisconsin, United States. It has incoming 3rd through 10th grade Jewish children. The camp has been affiliated with the Milwaukee Jewish Community Center. The camp has two sessions with a three-day intersession from Monday to Thursday where all campers leave the camp. Most of the campers attend one of the two sessions. There is also a two-week mini session for 3rd through 5th grade campers. The majority of the campers come from Milwaukee and Madison. Others come from Chicago, Florida, Arizona, and California. In 2025 there were 245 campers first session and 290 campers second session. This year there were 100 campers from Chicago. This was the largest season they have had.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

The 10th graders live in a teen village which has yurts for bunks and a separate rec hall and dining area used for breakfast. Campers going into 11th grade typically go to Israel for the summer—this year some went to Europe. The staff consists of foreign staff (many returning) and returning campers. Cabins are typically split boys and girls with some overlap depending on numbers. The tour was given by Toni Davidson Levenberg, Executive Director.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins contain 14 campers on average. There is a separate area in the cabin for counselors. There are no bathrooms and showers in the cabins. Instead there is a separate wash house. The 10th graders live in a teen village which has yurts for bunks and a separate rec hall and dining area used for breakfast. There is extra land with sports fields across the road.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The camp has a heated pool and a beautiful lakefront. Waterfront Activities include: Waterskiing, Wake-boarding, Knee-boarding, Windsurfing, Sailing, Small Crafts, Swimming, Life Guard Training. On the tour we met two girls who passed their life guard training first session and would be life guards second session.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Swim lessons occur daily in the pool. Friday they have free play in the lake instead of swim lessons. Saturday is an open day.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities at camp include Tennis, Gymnastics, Golf, Disc golf, Tushball, Gaga, Archery, Cycling, Fitness, Rock Climbing Wall, Softball, Soccer, Basketball, Volleyball, Floor (Court) Hockey, High/Low Ropes with Zip Line. Ultimate Frisbee and Maccabiah. Interlaken also has Ceramics, Fine Arts, YOGA, Aerobics, Modern Dance, Theater, Theater Tech, Israeli Dance, Woodworking, Crafts, Guitar, Photography, Video, Nature, Fishing and Shabbat. Campers can also prepare for their Bar/Bat Mitzvah while at camp.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The kitchen is unsupervised kosher. Lunch is dairy and dinner is a meat meal. Shabbat is celebrated. The camp can handle all dietary issues and allergies. They have a detailed plan for transgender campers, which is agreed to by the camp and the camper’s family as long as discussed before camp starts. There is always a doctor on staff in the health center. Campers can only cross with security. Security at the camp is full time and there is a locked gate. At the moment there is no camp sign, but they are working on a more discrete sign option.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The camp is small and quaint. It has a beautiful feel. The grounds are small but very nice. It can be compared to Beber, a much smaller Chi, and the Canadian Jewish camps like White Pine and Wahanowin (much smaller).



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

There is a three-day intersession from Monday to Thursday where all campers leave the camp. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Interlaken JCC, formally The Steve and Shari Sadek Family Camp Interlaken JCC, is a Jewish summer camp located in Eagle River, Wisconsin, serving incoming 3rd through 10th grade Jewish children. The camp has two sessions with a three-day intersession from Monday to Thursday where all campers leave the camp, plus a two-week mini session for 3rd through 5th grade campers. It has a heated pool and a beautiful lakefront, with Waterfront Activities including Waterskiing, Wake-boarding, Knee-boarding, Windsurfing, Sailing, Small Crafts, Swimming, and Life Guard Training, plus extensive land sports and arts. The camp is small and quaint with very nice grounds and full-time security, and cabins average 14 campers with a separate counselor area and a wash house for bathrooms and showers.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - The camp is small and quaint; it has a beautiful feel; the grounds are small but very nice.
  - Heated pool and beautiful lakefront; Waterfront Activities include Life Guard Training.
  - Teen village (10th grade) in yurts with a separate rec hall and breakfast dining.
  - Full-time security with a locked gate; campers can only cross to extra fields with security.

  - Detailed plan for transgender campers agreed to with the camper’s family before camp starts.
  - There is always a doctor on staff in the health center.
  - Affiliated with the Milwaukee Jewish Community Center.
  - Staff consists of foreign staff (many returning) and returning campers.



#### Lowlights

  - There are no bathrooms and showers in the cabins (separate wash house).
  - The grounds are small.
  - All campers leave for a three-day intersession Monday to Thursday.
  - At the moment there is no camp sign (working on a more discrete sign option).



#### Similar Camps

  - Beber
  - Chi
  - White Pine
  - Wahanowin

title: SUMMER SPRINGBOARD — DENISE — 7-2-18
text:
# SUMMER SPRINGBOARD



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Summer Springboard is a small pre-college program for HS students. They offer residential and commuter programs on the beautiful campus of UC Berkeley (& Yale). Cal’s campus is located about 15 miles from the Oakland Airport and about 25 miles from SFO. SSB has 3 2-week sessions. The session is about 80 students total (½ male & ½ female). 50 of the students are residential and 30 are commuters. Students can stay for multiple sessions - just need to take a different class.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

The students are from all over. Some are from Northern California, but a good number are from other parts of the US (NY, Chicago, Wisconsin, Michigan, LA) and international (Tokyo, Hong Kong).

There are 10 staff members this summer. Megan Hsu (Campus Director), Kevin (SSB Program Asst) and 8 residential advisors. The Residential Advisors go through a vigorous interview process and must have completed their sophomore year in college. This year is different because they are all college graduates. They are picked for their experience with youth, undergo background checks and training to work specifically with teens. They have a staff to student ratio of 1:8 during the weekdays and 1:6 on the weekends (not all commuter students opt to attend weekend activities, but they can if they want to). The RAs live in single rooms on the same floors as the students.

Megan received degrees from Stanford University for both her undergraduate and graduate work. She is in special education during the school year.



## 3 · Living Quarters

When I arrived on campus, I called Megan Hsu (SSB Cal Director) and she had to come to the courtyard to let me in the building. All of the main buildings (except the dining hall and Medical program) used for SSB are in one complex that is locked at all times. You need a key and a fab in order to get in to the buildings and then into each specific room.

Students live in 2 different dorm buildings in Unit 1 Residence Hall. They are placed in doubles or triples (randomly) and each floor is single gender with RAs. The bathroom is on the floor and has 4 shower stalls, 3 toilets and 3 sinks. The buildings and elevators are locked at all times. You need a key or fab to enter the building, to ride the elevator and to open the door to the rooms. Very secure. However, the students do have a lot of freedom since they are on a college campus but there are boundaries they need to stay in.

The dining hall where they have their meal plan is a short walk from the dorms. They can also take a short walk to town where there are lots of restaurants and shops. It is a college town.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Typical Day – 7am – 9am - Breakfast – up to them to eat before classes start; 9am – 12pm - Class (Business & Entrepreneurship, Computer Science, Medical & Health, Engineering, Digital Journalism, Community Service & College Tours – has a little different program); 12-3pm – lunch and rest on their own; 3-5pm – afternoon activity; 5pm – commuters need to check out and go home; 5-8pm – dinner and rest; 8-9pm – evening activity; 10:30pm – floor check and in for the night.

5/10 days – True You Curriculum (this is what makes SSB unique and different from other programs).



## 6 · Program Highlights

True You Curriculum (this is what makes SSB unique and different from other programs) is a personal journey of self-discovery that allows students to better understand their strengths, weaknesses and opportunities for growth. We do this through a series of personality assessments, interest inventories, and reflection sessions that help students discover the best path for themselves.

Weekend trips – Santa Cruz Boardwalk/beach & downtown San Francisco.

Curriculum – all classes have at least 1 field trip and complete a project to be presented at the end of the program. Students bring their own laptops and all classes are hands-on. They want the students to experience how it would be to actually work in the field. Various levels of experience in all classes, just need to be interested.

1\. Medical & Health (30 kids) – 2 doctors from UCSF emergency room are teaching this class. They went to the hospital as their field trip and got to work on test dummies (learned how to suture through the skin and intubate). Elodin (10th grader from Wisconsin) said “She loves being in Ca and absolutely loves this class\! I would not have been able to learn to suture or intubate hands-on at home\!” They also had an activity that was hands on called “Mass casualties and triage” where the students were broken up in to groups of some casualties with different traumas and then ER doctors to figure out what to do. Great hands-on learning. I sat through a bit of the class and listened to a discussion about teaching the kids about global health along with medicine (not just being in health profession ex. like a doctor or nurse) but learning about the people you are treating and how to be a good person to truly help them. Talked about refugees – what it is like to be a refugee or treat/help them. Discussed how some people need to choose between food and medicine because they don’t have enough money for both – this doesn’t just happen in other countries, it happens right here in Berkeley/SF too. VERY IMPRESSED with this part of the program. This is the largest and most popular of the major classes at SSB.

2\. Business & Entrepreneurship (16-18 kids) – field trip to Facebook headquarters. They are broken up in to about 4 groups and they are creating a business plan. They are working interactively and collaboratively to build the plan. They will present the plan on the last day.

3\. Digital Journalism (9 kids) – field trips to Bloomberg & SF Chronicle. They went in to SF and around campus to take lots of pictures which they will incorporate in to a final project that will be presented on the last day of class.

4\. Computer Science (12 kids) – field trip to Google. In small groups and are creating an App. Using Java to write code. 3 girls working together (from LA, Michigan & San Jose) on an App to find Protests going on in your area. Once App is created, they will make a website with slides to present on the last day.

5\. Engineering (13 kids) – field trip to Stantec and were able to see the building site of a future hospital and see simulations. Lots of hands-on using different types of tools and aerodynamics. I witnessed the kids designing a shed. Their final project is to design a house that meets all codes.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health – The office has emergency kits with standard items. 2-minute drive to health center and urgent care. On-call medical advice and on-call mental health professional. They take all kinds of kids (ADHD, Anxiety Disorder, Panic Attacks, High functioning Asperger’s, visually impaired). Plenty of students are on medication and they are responsible to have that medication with them and to take it on their own.

All food allergies and food preferences can be accommodated as they eat in the dining hall of part of the UC.

Zero tolerance policy – same as UC campus very strict. Very secure (buildings and elevators are locked at all times; keys/fabs required). 10:30pm – floor check and in for the night.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very secure, but the students do have a lot of freedom since they are on a college campus, with boundaries they need to stay in. It is a college town.

All in all, I was extremely impressed with this small program. This is only their second year, so they are hoping to grow. Andrew was extremely easy to work with on getting me up to Berkeley to see the program and Megan was a great tour guide. I would definitely recommend this program, but it is for a bit of a different kid. It is VERY small and very different from a bigger program like Summer Discovery on UCLA campus.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Cal’s campus is located about 15 miles from the Oakland Airport and about 25 miles from SFO. SSB provides an optional airport pickup service for students. They must arrive at SFO between 1-4pm or OAK between 10-1pm. This optional service costs $95 each way. Families can purchase this service once they have been accepted into the program. Once they buy their plane tickets, they send in their flight information and a member of the staff will meet them at the airport (near baggage claim area) and accompany them back to campus. Similar option for Yale students at Newark.

They have an App on their phones specifically for SSB to stay in touch and to know when and where they should be. Extra costs beyond the airport pickup: Unknown. Parent communication beyond the app: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Summer Springboard is a small pre-college program for HS students that offers residential and commuter programs on the beautiful campus of UC Berkeley (& Yale). All in all, I was extremely impressed with this small program. I would definitely recommend this program, but it is for a bit of a different kid. It is VERY small and very different from a bigger program like Summer Discovery on UCLA campus.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - True You Curriculum (this is what makes SSB unique and different from other programs) is a personal journey of self-discovery that allows students to better understand their strengths, weaknesses and opportunities for growth.
  - VERY IMPRESSED with this part of the program.
  - All of the main buildings used for SSB are in one complex that is locked at all times; you need a key and a fab to get in to the buildings and then into each specific room.
  - Staff to student ratio of 1:8 during the weekdays and 1:6 on the weekends.
  - Optional airport pickup service ($95 each way) with specific arrival windows at SFO and OAK (similar option for Yale students at Newark).
  - Field trips and hands-on projects across all classes (Facebook, Google, Bloomberg, SF Chronicle, UCSF ER, Stantec).
  - Students are from all over the US and international (Tokyo, Hong Kong).



#### Lowlights

  - This is only their second year, so they are hoping to grow.
  - It is VERY small and very different from a bigger program like Summer Discovery on UCLA campus.
  - Not all commuter students opt to attend weekend activities.
  - Plenty of students are on medication and they are responsible to have that medication with them and to take it on their own.
  - Zero tolerance policy – same as UC campus very strict.



#### Similar Camps

  - Summer Discovery on UCLA campus

title: SILC GSTAAD SUMMER LEADERSHIP CAMP — David Highfill — 1-30-24
text:
# SILC GSTAAD SUMMER LEADERSHIP CAMP



## 1 · Program Snapshot

We’d be delighted to welcome your clients to our unique and exciting Gstaad Summer Leadership Camp in Switzerland. Our camp is a fun, active, and unique experience, blending in amazing outdoor adventures with fun leadership challenges and expeditions in the very heart of the Swiss Alps. Two-week Unbeatable Leadership Programme exploring Switzerland’s beauty from the Gstaad Valley to the glaciers. Dates, Fees, and Age: Seniors (11 to 17): July 21st - August 3rd 2024, 5,400 Swiss francs, Mandatory insurance fee: 200 Swiss Francs. Juniors (7 to 10): July 14th - July 20th 2024, 2,900 Swiss francs, Mandatory insurance fee: 200 Swiss Francs. All fees are all-inclusive and also include pick up, drop off and transfers from and to Geneva Airport.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ages 11 to 17 (Seniors) and 7 to 10 (Juniors). Students enjoy a variety of outdoor activities while developing leadership skills such as communication, goal setting, time management, and collaboration. Staff and director profile: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Our Authentic Swiss Campus: Our traditional Swiss chalet with a modern twist, nestled in the Swiss Alps. Spacious and cosy, the rooms feature an en-suite bathroom shared only amongst roommates. Our welcoming and tranquil ambiance is designed to ensure a safe space for growth and learning.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Students enjoy outdoor activities such as canyoning and river rafting.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Two-week Unbeatable Leadership Programme. Students enjoy a variety of outdoor activities such as canyoning, river rafting, rock climbing or via ferrata. Daily timetable: Unknown.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Exploring Switzerland’s beauty from the Gstaad Valley to the glaciers. Students enjoy a variety of outdoor activities such as canyoning, river rafting, rock climbing or via ferrata while developing leadership skills such as communication, goal setting, time management, and collaboration.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Our welcoming and tranquil ambiance is designed to ensure a safe space for growth and learning. Mandatory insurance fee: 200 Swiss Francs. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Unique and exciting; a fun, active, and unique experience in the very heart of the Swiss Alps. Authentic Swiss campus with a welcoming and tranquil ambiance designed to ensure a safe space for growth and learning.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

All fees are all-inclusive and also include pick up, drop off and transfers from and to Geneva Airport. Free Online Private Consult: If preferred, we may schedule a Zoom call to go through the programme in more detail. Discover our website. Check the camp brochure. View the camp programme for 11-17 y.o. View the camp programme for 7-10 y.o. Virtual campus tour. Camp videos. Full refunds: Secure your place now without any obligation; we have a refund policy in place to safeguard families that secure their child’s place early on (for more information see here). Application: You can register your clients through our website.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Set in the very heart of the Swiss Alps, SILC Gstaad Summer Leadership Camp blends amazing outdoor adventures with fun leadership challenges and expeditions. Students enjoy canyoning, river rafting, rock climbing or via ferrata while developing leadership skills such as communication, goal setting, time management, and collaboration. Our traditional Swiss chalet is spacious and cosy, with en-suite bathrooms shared only amongst roommates, and a welcoming and tranquil ambiance designed to ensure a safe space for growth and learning. All fees are all-inclusive and also include pick up, drop off and transfers from and to Geneva Airport.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Two-week Unbeatable Leadership Programme.
  - Exploring Switzerland’s beauty from the Gstaad Valley to the glaciers.
  - All fees are all-inclusive and also include pick up, drop off and transfers from and to Geneva Airport.
  - Free Online Private Consult (Zoom) available.
  - Traditional Swiss chalet with en-suite bathrooms shared only amongst roommates.
  - Refund policy in place to safeguard families that secure their child’s place early on.



#### Lowlights

  - LIMITED PLACES.
  - Mandatory insurance fee: 200 Swiss Francs.
  - Dates listed are 2024.
  - Food details: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

We’d be delighted to welcome your clients to our unique and exciting Gstaad Summer Leadership Camp in Switzerland. Our camp is a fun, active, and unique experience, blending in amazing outdoor adventures with fun leadership challenges and expeditions in the very heart of the Swiss Alps. Free Online Private Consult If preferred, we may schedule a Zoom call to go through the programme in more detail. You can directly do so via this link. Two-week Unbeatable Leadership Programme Exploring Switzerland’s beauty from the Gstaad Valley to the glaciers. Students enjoy a variety of outdoor activities such as canyoning, river rafting, rock climbing or via ferrata while developing leadership skills such as communication, goal setting, time management, and collaboration. Accommodation: Our Authentic Swiss Campus Our traditional Swiss chalet with a modern twist, nestled in the Swiss Alps. Spacious and cosy, the rooms feature an en-suite bathroom shared only amongst roommates. Our welcoming and tranquil ambiance is designed to ensure a safe space for growth and learning. Discover our website Check the camp brochure View the camp programme for 11-17 y.o. View the camp programme for 7-10 y.o. Virtual campus tour Camp videos Dates, Fees, and Age ? Seniors: July 21st - August 3rd 2024: LIMITED PLACES o 5,400.- Swiss francs o Ages 11 to 17 o Mandatory insurance fee: 200,- Swiss Francs ? Juniors: July 14th - July 20th 2024: LIMITED PLACES o 2,900.- Swiss francs o Ages 7 to 10 o Mandatory insurance fee: 200,- Swiss Francs All fees are all-inclusive and also include pick up, drop off and transfers from and to Geneva Airport. Full refunds: Secure your place now without any obligation We understand that sometimes life just gets in the way, therefore, we have a refund policy in place to safeguard families that secure their child’s place early on. title: STEP IT UP BASKETBALL — David Highfill — 6-9-24
text:
# STEP IT UP BASKETBALL



## 1 · Program Snapshot

My name is Yogev Berdugo, camp director of Camp Step It Up, located in upstate New York at Hobart William & Smith Colleges in the Finger Lake Region. Think of us as a sleep away camp for basketball players and kids who love sports. The premier basketball sleep away camp in the world. Summer 2024 Camp Dates: June 26 - August 7. We accept campers anywhere between 2-6 weeks. Most campers come for 4-6 weeks. We take an on court/off court approach and focus on the person as a whole. Our curriculum is proven, engaging and well thought out. Check out our new camp videos to learn more about us and feel free to share with friends: Summer 2024 Camp Video, The Step It Up Method, Girls Camp. You can read all about Summer 2024 including daily schedule, dates and rates here.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Summer 2024 we have campers attending from all over the world. Over 20 states in the US as well as Israel, Canada, Croatia, England, France, Germany, Korea, Panama, Serbia, Sweden and Switzerland. Our campers come from all walks of life. Jewish for the most part but we do accept kids from different backgrounds as well. Within the Jewish faith we have Reformed, Conservative and Modern Orthodox. A dedicated staff including more than 20 returning staff to go along with our new team members. Devoted, skilled, engaged, professional, funny, fun, responsible, inspirational people who are ready to leave a long-lasting impact on all of our campers. Founder / Camp Director: Yogev Berdugo. Video address for the parents leading up to camp (3 video slides).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Fantastic housing with air-conditioned bedrooms for all. Gorgeous outdoor and indoor areas on campus.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

An unparalleled campus which includes a lakefront nestled on Lake Seneca where campers enjoy an array of water sports (boating and tubing) as well as a swimming pool three times per week.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

You can read all about Summer 2024 including daily schedule, dates and rates here. Afternoon electives and evening activities will be at the forefront when we are not honing our skills on the court. On field trip days we wake up early, get a light workout in and off we go for a full day of fun.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Basketball brings us together. An experience unlike any other will bond us and life-long memories will be made. Camp Step It Up helps produce confident, cerebral, well-rounded young men and women who can be contributing members of society. We aim to continue and expand on the excellent upbringing and education they are already receiving at home. An unparalleled campus: an indoor turf field, soccer field, football field, tennis courts, a state of the art weight-room, plenty of indoor basketball courts and more. Off-court activities when we are not honing our skills on the court. Our 6 week program at Hobart College features three full day field trips: amusement parks, ropes course, beach trips and more. Visiting Day: Sunday, July 14th, 12:00 pm - 6:00 pm. This camp has helped produce no less than 60 collegiate basketball players, Division 1,2 and 3. Over a dozen players who have made the Israeli National Teams, dozens of professional basketball players and 1 NBA Draft Pick who currently has his NBA rights owned by the Boston Celtics.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

All of our camp food is Kosher. Great Food: Two Italian days, 2 BBQ days, an Israeli themed day, Oriental, Mexican, Salmon and Mash Potatoes. Salad bar at every meal along with fruit or dessert after every single meal. Nutrition is key. It fuels our minds and our bodies which act as our vehicles for the summer. Vehicles of change. Nut-Free Camp: We are completely nut-free. Please be mindful of this when packing your camper's snacks and shipping food.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Basketball brings us together. An experience unlike any other will bond us and life-long memories will be made. 'In order to achieve unusual results, one must take unusual measures.' THE mantra Camp Step It Up was founded upon. Unusual results are something we hold near and dear to our hearts. This is a lens into my heart, into my soul and into the heart and soul of this camp. To the summer basketball experience of a lifetime\!



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Located in upstate New York at Hobart William & Smith Colleges in the Finger Lake Region. Visiting Day Sunday, July 14th 12:00 pm - 6:00 pm. Directions. In addition to our camp in the US we have a sleep away camp in Israel the last two weeks of each summer. Israel Sleepaway Camp Dates: August 11-16 and August 18-23, 2024. We have several Americans flying with us from the US as well as American campers spending the summer in Israel on a program or traveling with their families. Email us for more info. Video address for parents leading up to camp (watch all 3 video slides). Check out our new camp videos to learn more about us and feel free to share with friends. Parent Contact: P. 888.600.0908, timetostepitup.com, yogev@campstepitup.com. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Step It Up is a premier basketball sleep away camp at Hobart William & Smith Colleges in upstate New York, with an on-court/off-court approach and a curriculum that is proven, engaging and well thought out. It serves basketball players and kids who love sports from all over the world, with kosher, nut-free food and fantastic housing with air-conditioned bedrooms. Afternoon electives, evening activities, lakefront water sports on Lake Seneca, a swimming pool three times per week, and three full-day field trips round out the program. Founded and directed by Yogev Berdugo, the camp has helped produce no less than 60 collegiate players, dozens of professional players and 1 NBA Draft Pick, and aims to empower and provide your child with the greatest summer camp experience of his/her life.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Premier basketball sleep away camp in the world.
  - On court/off court approach; focus on the person as a whole.
  - Lakefront on Lake Seneca with boating and tubing; swimming pool three times per week.
  - Kosher, nut-free food with salad bar at every meal; robust themed menu.
  - Fantastic housing with air-conditioned bedrooms for all.
  - Dedicated staff including more than 20 returning staff.
  - Three full day field trips in the 6 week program (amusement parks, ropes course, beach trips).
  - International camper base; Jewish for the most part but inclusive of different backgrounds.
  - Proven outcomes: 60 collegiate players, Israeli National Team athletes, dozens of pros, and 1 NBA Draft Pick (rights owned by the Boston Celtics).
  - Extensive facilities: indoor turf, soccer and football fields, tennis courts, state of the art weight-room, plenty of indoor basketball courts.
  - Visiting Day on Sunday, July 14th, 12:00 pm - 6:00 pm.
  - Additional Israel sleepaway camp sessions with Americans flying from the US.



#### Lowlights

  - Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown
