title: TASIS SWITZERLAND — SHARI — 2-8-24
text:
# TASIS SWITZERLAND



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Located in Italian speaking Southern part of Switzerland-1hr from Milan. 40-45 nationalities represented at the program each year. Two sessions offered. Boarding and day offered at all age levels except Minos (described below). Why TASIS Switzerland? -All students take academics. Language and Enrichment -Activities resemble summer camp: water activities, hikes, evening activities, games. -Travel woven in on weekends. -Performing arts woven in for all (ie: singing, learn ukulele, musical production performed on last night for parents). 4 Programs: High School (14-17y/o) (about 180 students total but some years 165-170)– boarding & day available. Middle School (11-13 y/o) (about 130 students) - boarding & day available. Elementary School Chateau Des Enfants (7-10 y/o) (about 100 students, 70% leave @ the end of the day so around 30 boarders)- boarding & day available. Minnows (4-6 y/o) – (about 30-40 students) - day only.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Marc-Pierre Jansen. 40-45 nationalities represented at the program each year. Teachers are mostly American and Canadian. Some from Italy and France as well. 12-14 students per class. Minor chosen and all taught by teachers who are passionate about subject area but not as intense as the morning classes. They must have proficiency in English language unless they’re coming specifically for ESL. They used to offer 6 levels of English, now they only offer 3. Les Tapies teachers are professionals or practicing artists and teachers. 8 staff to 27 students.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Dorm and lights out 11pm (HS). Live in 6 separate houses w/ 4-5 students per house and 2 students per room (Les Tapies). Other TASIS Switzerland housing specifics: Unknown.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Activities resemble summer camp: water activities. PM activities (choice of swim) for Elementary. Weekend/younger ages include beach. Sundays are relaxed on campus w/ activities available (paddleboarding, hb riding, hanging out, etc).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

High School (14-17): Morning all assemble for orientation and announcements for the day, choose a course (ESL, writing, French, shark tank-like class, fine arts, musical theatre, digital photography, filmmaking, architecture, fashion cooking, intro to investing STEAM technology), do for 3-3.5 hrs (12-14 students per class). Lunch. Minor chosen and all taught by teachers who are passionate about subject area but not as intense as the morning classes. Sports in afternoon 2x/week. Dinner. Report to mentors at 7:30, evening activity, cultural night 1x/wk, dorm and lights out 11pm. Weekends – day trips in Switzerland and Northern Italy, groups of 20 or so students to each location ie: tour of old city in Milan, see The Last Supper. Or train to Zurich, attend museum, chocolate factory, or FIFA Museum and then back for dinner. Free time is limited and lots of check-ins with staff. Optional trips offered – extra cost – Italy (Venice & Rome, Florence) & Switzerland (Interlaken & Bernese). All offered both sessions. Middle School (11-13): Same structure as HS program but fewer offerings, all on website. Runs completely separate from HS program. May only be some overlap w/ talented MS athletes to play w/ HSers but very few. Family style dinner, sit together & facilitate conversation & community. Lights out 10pm. Trips offered but more curtailed than HS trips. Elementary (Chateau Des Enfants, 7-10): Nurturing, all choose a language (English, French, Italian). Morning meeting, language classes, sports, lunch family style by language (sit w/ teacher), rest time, singing class, STEAM hour class in language they’re studying, art courses, PM activities (choice of swim, arts, STEAM, etc). Older kids in musical theatre class. Bedtime circle and goodnight in own language, chocolate and milk, lights out 9pm. Weekends no trips but travel curtailed for age group on Saturdays (Luzerne transport museum, beach, etc) and Sundays are relaxed on campus w/ activities available (paddleboarding, hb riding, hanging out, etc). Minnows (4-6): Day only. Operates about 100 yards from main campus. Kids enjoy music, languages (English French Italian), outdoor activities, rest time, lunch, story telling, most leave at 3pm, some stay and attend elementary program until 5pm. Courses/credit: Students take 1 major and 1 minor per session; they can change courses within the 1st week if they’re really unhappy if there’s still space in other classes. Classes are pretty academic but don’t get credit b/c they’re not there long enough. 1/2 credit at some schools available but very rare. Les Tapies (12–18, mostly 14–16; can do 18 y/o with structure): 3 wks only. 8-10 hrs/day 3x/wk in major (they equate 3wks of work to a whole year of school work) — 4 hrs in the morning and 3 hrs in the afternoon and some evenings. Minors are 3hrs 3x/wk and give students the opportunity to try other things outside their major. Some evenings they’ll watch films in their subject area or attend lectures. They arrive on Saturday and go right to the open market to immerse themselves in the community.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Performing arts woven in for all (ie: singing, learn ukulele, musical production performed on last night for parents). The students feel accomplished and excited about their subject areas upon completion. Optional trips offered – extra cost – Italy (Venice & Rome, Florence) & Switzerland (Interlaken & Bernese). Lots of time spent outdoors with natural landscapes. In drawing and painting they use many mediums (oil, watercolor, etc) and work on landscapes, still life etc. In architecture he breaks up the 3wks into restoration, new building, and teamwork. Hand on, serious students learning in-depth in their subject area. Excursions devoted to augmenting what they learn in the classroom. Not tourists, they’re students.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Free time is limited and lots of check-ins with staff. Family style dinner (MS), sit together & facilitate conversation & community. Lunch family style by language (Elementary). Bedtime circle and goodnight in own language, chocolate and milk (Elementary). Les Tapies: Food is outstanding, home cooked, local ingredients, allergies and specific food needs accommodated easily. Medical-meds given out by staff member. Dr is 5 min away in town. Hospital 45 min away.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Activities resemble summer camp: water activities, hikes, evening activities, games. Travel woven in on weekends. Performing arts woven in for all. Family style dinner, sit together & facilitate conversation & community. Nurturing (Elementary). Cultural night 1x/wk. Bedtime circle and goodnight in own language, chocolate and milk. The students pick Les Tapies b/c they want to be there. Passionate students of the arts, improve ability while there. They arrive on Saturday and go right to the open market to immerse themselves in the community. Not tourists, they’re students.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Airport mostly used is Milan Malpensa (50mins away). Zurich train about 2hrs away. Parents mostly fly w/ students and bring them to the grounds, but the program can provide transportation. Fees include everything upon arrival EXCEPT spending money, travel to & from the program and some optional sports if chosen. Optional trips offered – extra cost – Italy (Venice & Rome, Florence) & Switzerland (Interlaken & Bernese). All offered both sessions. Les Tapies: Les Tapies is south of Lyon, France. Valence is the closest city (45 min away) and train stops there for pick ups. Airport is in Lyon 2hrs away, some parents drop off at program. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Located in Italian speaking Southern part of Switzerland-1hr from Milan, with two sessions and 40-45 nationalities represented at the program each year. All students take academics (Language and Enrichment), with activities that resemble summer camp and travel woven in on weekends; performing arts are woven in for all. High School, Middle School, and Elementary (Chateau Des Enfants) offer boarding & day (Minnows are day only), with structured days, caring supervision, and limited free time with lots of check-ins with staff. Les Tapies in France is a unique, small, serious arts program (27 students max) focused ONLY on painting & drawing, architecture, and photography, chosen by passionate students who want to be there.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Travel woven in on weekends.
  - Performing arts woven in for all (ie: singing, learn ukulele, musical production performed on last night for parents).
  - 12-14 students per class.
  - Free time is limited and lots of check-ins with staff.
  - Les Tapies: unique, small program focused ONLY on painting & drawing, architecture, and photography (27 students max).
  - Food is outstanding, home cooked, local ingredients, allergies and specific food needs accommodated easily.



#### Lowlights

  - Classes are pretty academic but don’t get credit b/c they’re not there long enough.
  - They must have proficiency in English language unless they’re coming specifically for ESL.
  - The only criticism the students and parents have is that it’s not long enough.
  - Trips offered but more curtailed than HS trips (Middle School).
  - Minnows – day only.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Marc-Pierre Jansen Located in Italian speaking Southern part of Switzerland-1hr from Milan. Airport mostly used is Milan Malpensa (50mins away). Zurich train about 2hrs away. Parents mostly fly w/ students and bring them to the grounds, but the program can provide transportation. 40-45 nationalities represented at the program each year. Two sessions offered. Fees include everything upon arrival EXCEPT spending money, travel to & from the program and some optional sports if chosen. Boarding and day offered at all age levels except Minos (described below). Why TASIS Switzerland? -All students take academics. Language and Enrichment -Activities resemble summer camp: water activities, hikes, evening activities, games. -Travel woven in on weekends. -Performing arts woven in for all (ie: singing, learn ukulele, musical production performed on last night for parents). 4 Programs: High School (14-17y/o) (about 180 students total but some years 165-170)– boarding & day available. Morning all assemble for orientation and announcements for the day, choose a course (ESL, writing, French, shark tank-like class, fine arts, musical theatre, digital photography, filmmaking, architecture, fashion cooking, intro to investing STEAM technology), do for 3-3.5 hrs. 12-14 students per class. Lunch. Minor chosen and all taught by teachers who are passionate about subject area but not as intense as the morning classes. Sports in afternoon 2x/week. Dinner. Report to mentors at 7:30, evening activity, cultural night 1x/wk, dorm and lights out 11pm. Weekends – day trips in Switzerland and Northern Italy, groups of 20 or so students to each location ie: tour of old city in Milan, see The Last Supper. Or train to Zurich, attend museum, chocolate factory, or FIFA Museum and then back for dinner. Free time is limited and lots of check-ins with staff. Optional trips offered – extra cost – Italy (Venice & Rome, Florence) & Switzerland (Interlaken & Bernese). All offered both sessions. Middle School (11-13 y/o) (about 130 students) - boarding & day available. Same structure as HS program but fewer offerings, all on website. Runs completely separate from HS program. May only be some overlap w/ talented MS athletes to play w/ HSers but very few. Family style dinner, sit together & facilitate conversation & community. Lights out 10pm. Trips offered but more curtailed than HS trips. Elementary School Chateau Des Enfants (7-10 y/o) (about 100 students, 70% leave @ the end of the day so around 30 boarders)- boarding & day available. Nurturing, all choose a language (English, French, Italian). Morning meeting, language classes, sports, lunch family style by language (sit w/ teacher), rest time, singing class, STEAM hour class in language they’re studying, art courses, PM activities (choice of swim, arts, STEAM, etc). Older kids in musical theatre class. Bedtime circle and goodnight in own language, chocolate and milk, lights out 9pm. Weekends no trips but travel curtailed for age group on Saturdays (Luzerne transport museum, beach, etc) and Sundays are relaxed on campus w/ activities available (paddleboarding, hb riding, hanging out, etc) Minnows (4-6 y/o) – (about 30-40 students) - day only. Operates about 100yards from main campus. Kids enjoy music, languages (English French Italian), outdoor activities, rest time, lunch, story telling, most leave at 3pm, some stay and attend elementary program until 5pm. Teachers are mostly American and Canadian. Some from Italy and France as well. Students take 1 major and 1 minor per session. They can change courses within the 1 st week if they’re really unhappy if there’s still space in other classes. Classes are pretty academic but don’t get credit b/c they’re not there long enough. ½ credit at some schools available but very rare. Just focused on enrichment, cultural experience, and grammatical improvement. The students feel accomplished and excited about their subject areas upon completion. They must have proficiency in English language unless they’re coming specifically for ESL. They used to offer 6 levels of English, now they only offer 3. Les Tapies – Fernando Gonzales – 12–18-y/o (mostly 14-16 and can do 18 year old as long as they understand there is a lot of structure). Les Tapies is south of Lyon, France. Valence is the closest city (45 min away) and train stops there for pick ups. Airport is in Lyon 2hrs away, some parents drop off at program. Unique, small program focused ONLY on painting & drawing (most popular about 50% of students), architecture (Fernando is the teacher), and photography. 27 students max, fills in May usually, 3 wks only, 8-10 hrs/day 3x/wk in major. Lots of time spent outdoors with natural landscapes. In drawing and painting they use many mediums (oil, watercolor, etc) and work on landscapes, still life etc. It is a full-fledged art program, and they equate 3wks of work to a whole year of school work, 4 hrs in the morning and 3hrs in the afternoon and some evenings. Minors are 3hrs 3x/wk and give students the opportunity to try other things outside their major. In architecture he breaks up the 3wks into restoration, new building, and teamwork. Hand on, serious students learning in-depth in their subject area. Some evenings they’ll watch films in their subject area or attend lectures. The students pick Les Tapies b/c they want to be there. Excursions devoted to augmenting what they learn in the classroom. Not tourists, they’re students. Passionate students of the arts, improve ability while there. Teachers are professionals or practicing artists and teachers. 8 staff to 27 students. Food is outstanding, home cooked, local ingredients, allergies and specific food needs accommodated easily. Live in 6 separate houses w/ 4-5 students per house and 2 students per room. Nine nationalities represented but 2/3 American, the rest from several other countries. Medical-meds given out by staff member. Dr is 5 min away in town. Hospital 45 min away. They arrive on Saturday and go right to the open market to immerse themselves in the community. The only criticism the students and parents have is that it’s not long enough.

title: SURPRISE LAKE CAMP — EMILY — 7-27-18
text:
# SURPRISE LAKE CAMP



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Surprise Lake Camp is a Jewish, co-ed camp in Cold Spring, NY. Essentially two separate camps: Main & Teen. Main side: 7–12 // Teen side: 12–15 (13 before June 30th). Sessions: Two 4-week sessions with a 2-day trip break in between. Mini Camp (ages 7–9) is 1.5 weeks. Get Your Feet Wet (ages 6–10) runs Aug. 22–26 and is $195.00. 1/3 of the campers stay for 8 weeks. After the 1st session everyone packs up and is re-bunked for 2nd session.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Dena Levine toured Amanda and me. She’s the Program Director and grew up going to SLC. Her 4 daughters attend camp there. We also spoke with the director, Bradley Solmsen, who has been with SLC for 3 summers. Kids come from a 2 hour radius around NYC. 25% international counselors. Campers are bunked by age not grade – cut off is June 30. They travel in a group of 12. For the little kids (ages 7 – younger 10s), they are also in a bunk of 12, so the group and bunk are the same, but changes as you get older.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks were interesting. 7, 8, & 9’s are in a bunk with 12 campers and there is a room within the bunk that the counselors live in. The youngest girls have facilities in the bunks. 10, 11 & 12’s are in a bunk with 6 campers. There are two 6 camper cabins with a counselor cabin in the middle of it all attached. Facilities are in the shower house. 1 night toilet attached to bunk. Teen side: same as 10–12 year olds with no night toilet attached to bunk.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Each side has a separate waterfront. 1 pool on Main side, heated with a slide. Lake is spring fed. No motor-sports. Canoes/kayaks/paddleboard/sailboat. Toys in the lake.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

7 period day – 3 AM / 3 PM / 1 after dinner. Structured – based on what the kids like. The first week they are given a taste of all activities. Every week following, Dena discusses with the division heads what the group likes and she creates the schedule for the following week. Main: Specialty 3x a week for an hour; changes every week. Teen: 4x a week for 2 hours; changes every week.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Main side: climbing wall, ropes, gym, pool. Teen side: small golf program. Teen camp: high ropes, low ropes, zip line, archery. Sports is limited: Soccer, Basketball, Softball, Tennis, Roller hockey, Gaga, 9 square, Volleyball. Each side has separate sports fields. Intercamps with: Kinder Ring, Scatico, Louemma, KMKW. Art was very basic: painting, air-dried clay, beading, shrinky dinks, beads. Both sides have their own art shack. Nature program with goats, rabbits, snacks. Gardening program – full garden with chickens. Olympics is 1x a session for 1 day.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining: 4 different dining halls – 2 on Main side / 2 on Teen Side – everyone eats by group at the same time. Fans in the dining halls. Family style. Peanut butter served in separate containers. Can handle every allergy. Canteen: Main side 1x a week – 2 items; no products with nuts; do serve candy. Teen side: open all day – campers have a $15 account that parents can add to. Kosher. Blessings are said at every meal.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religion: Friday night: 45 minute service. Saturday morning: service followed by Shabbat walk which is free time for siblings to hang out and spend time with people from the other camp. More traditional services are available for people who want that. There is an eruv surrounding the camp. Lasting impressions: This camp is a little bit all over the place. Rustic in feel. A child who is looking for a lot of sports would not do well here. It’s for a family looking for a Jewish experience that doesn’t need bells and whistles. Seems to be a lot of alumni.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Kids come from a 2 hour radius around NYC. Buses from Brooklyn, Queens, NYC, Westchester. Visiting Day is only for full summer campers. Extra costs: Transportation $60.00. No electronics except music player. Packages are allowed. No food on Mainside; food is allowed on Teenside. Post pictures 1x a day.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Surprise Lake Camp is a Jewish, co-ed camp in Cold Spring, NY with Main and Teen sides and two 4-week sessions (plus shorter intro options). It’s rustic in feel and seems to be a lot of alumni. A child who is looking for a lot of sports would not do well here. It’s for a family looking for a Jewish experience that doesn’t need bells and whistles.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Jewish, co-ed camp in Cold Spring, NY
  - Two 4-week sessions with a 2-day trip break; 1/3 stay 8 weeks
  - Main & Teen sides; separate waterfronts and dining halls
  - Kosher; blessings at every meal; eruv
  - Spring-fed lake; heated pool with a slide
  - Nature program with goats, rabbits, snacks; gardening with chickens
  - Seems to be a lot of alumni



#### Lowlights

  - Sports is limited
  - Art was very basic
  - This camp is a little bit all over the place
  - A child who is looking for a lot of sports would not do well here



#### Similar Camps

  - Laurelwood
  - Pinemere
  - Berkshire Hills
  - Kinder Ring (though not Kosher)
  - Tevya
  - Tel Noar



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Get Your Feet Wet / Ages 6 – 10 / Aug. 22-26 / $195.00 ? 1/3 of the campers stay for 8 weeks \*\*\*After the 1 st sessions EVERYONE packs up and is re-bunked for 2 nd session - Kids come from a 2 hour radius around NYC - Buses from Brooklyn, Queens, NYC, Westchester - Visiting Day is only for full summer campers - Extra Costs: Transportation / $60.00 - NO electronics except music player - Packages are allowed. No food on Mainside, Food IS allowed on Teenside - Post pictures 1 x a day Essentially two separate camps: MAIN & TEEN Main side: 7 – 12 // Teen side: 12 -15 (13 before June 30 th ) Each side has separate Dining Hall, Waterfront, Sports Fields Main side: climbing wall, ropes, gym, pool Teen side: small golf program \*\*Campers are bunked by AGE not grade – Cut off is June 30. They travel in a “GROUP” of 12. More traditional services are available for people who want that. Gardening program – full garden with chickens 1 Pool on Main side. Heated with a slide. Lake is spring fed. No motor-sports. Canoes/kayaks/Paddeboard/Sailboat Toys in the lake Lasting impressions: This camp is a little bit all over the place. Rustic in feel. A child who is looking for a lot of sports would not do well here. It’s for a family looking for a Jewish experience that doesn’t need bells and whistles. Seems to be a lot of alumni. Could give with Laurelwood, Pinemere, Berkshire Hills, Kinder Ring (though not Kosher) – maybe Tevya and Tel Noar.

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

ENROLLMENT PROCESS
1\. Complete Online Application
2\. Complete paper Application Forms
3\. Mail Application Forms and deposit check to Wellesley office. We only accept checks for deposit.
• Upon completion of enrollment process, returning campers and new siblings of returning campers will be enrolled. New campers will be held in the order of receipt until Tuesday, October 16th, when we begin enrolling new campers.

CAMP TEVYA 2019 DATES, RATES & PAYMENT INFORMATION

NEW CAMPER TUITION OPTION
Standard Tuition:
• Season: $9,500 ($800 deposit check)\*
• First Session: $5,700 ($600 deposit check)\*
• Second Session: $5,400 ($600 deposit check)\*
\*First Time Camper Grant will be automatically applied when enrolled

RETURNING CAMPER TUITION OPTIONS
Early Bird Tuition (Available Until October 15th): Last year’s lower tuition rates with larger deposit. Must complete enrollment process by Monday, October 15th.
• Season: $9,200 ($1,200 deposit check)
• First Session: $5,500 ($900 deposit check)
• Second; Session: $5,200 ($900 deposit check)

Standard Tuition: Available for all campers regardless of enrollment date.
• Season: $9,500 ($800 deposit check)
• First Session: $5,700 ($600 deposit check)
• Second Session: $5,400 ($600 deposit check)

PAYMENT DATES
Remaining payments after deposits are due in two installments and can be paid by check, Visa, MasterCard, or Discover.
• 1st Payment: Friday, February 1, 2019
• 2nd Payment: Monday, April 1, 2019

TUITION DISCOUNT OPTIONS
First Time Camper Grants: New campers will automatically receive this grant
• Season: $500 for each new Season camper
• One Session: $250 for each new One Session camper

Multi-Child Discount: Available for families with at least two RETURNING campers at any Cohen Camp.
• Season: $350 towards 1 camper’s tuition
• One Session: $200 towards 1 camper’s tuition
• Dor L’Dor: $350 towards one CIT’s tuition

Cash Discount: Available for families who have completed the enrollment process and paid in full by check by Thursday, January 31
• Season: $200 for each Season camper
• One Session: $125 for each One Session camper
• Dor L’Dor: $250 for each CIT

Additional Information:
• If 2 campers are returning, the discount will be applied to the 1 camper with the highest tuition rate.
• If 1 camper is new and 1 is returning, only the First Time Camper Grant will be applied.
• If 3 campers are returning, the Multi-Child Discount will be applied to the 2 campers with the highest tuition rates.
• If 2 campers are returning and 1 is new, 1 Multi-Child Discount and 1 First Time Camper Grant will be applied.

Financial Aid: The Cohen Camps offer partial scholarships to families based on financial need. Please contact the Cohen Camps office at 781.489.2070 for information, forms, requirements, and deadlines.

Family Referral: Refer a family to any Cohen Camp and if they enroll, you will receive a $150 reimbursement at the end of the summer. Download the form here, then mail it to the Cohen Camps office.

One Happy Camper Grant: Campers spending their first summer at a Jewish camp might be eligible for an additional grant from FJC. Visit www.onehappycamper.org for additional information. It is the family’s responsibility to apply for this. Please make sure you receive two confirmation emails: 1.) To confirm your application was received; 2.) To confirm your camper will be receiving the grant.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

This write-up for Camp TEVYA centers on enrollment steps, tuition, discounts, and payment logistics. Enrollment requires completing an online application, paper forms, and mailing the forms and a deposit check to the Wellesley office; deposits are accepted by check only. New camper standard tuition is Season: $9,500 ($800 deposit), First Session: $5,700 ($600), Second Session: $5,400 ($600), with a First Time Camper Grant automatically applied, and returning campers have Early Bird (until October 15) and Standard options. Additional options include payment dates (Feb 1, 2019 and Apr 1, 2019), multi-child and cash discounts, financial aid through the Cohen Camps, a family referral reimbursement, and potential One Happy Camper grants.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - First Time Camper Grant will be automatically applied when enrolled.
  - Early Bird Tuition (Available Until October 15th): Last year’s lower tuition rates with larger deposit.
  - Remaining payments after deposits are due in two installments and can be paid by check, Visa, MasterCard, or Discover.
  - Tuition discount options include Multi-Child Discount, Cash Discount, Financial Aid, Family Referral, and One Happy Camper Grant.
  - Clear, enumerated enrollment steps are provided.



#### Lowlights

  - We only accept checks for deposit.
  - Manual enrollment requires online application, paper application forms, and mailing forms and deposit check to the Wellesley office.
  - New campers will be held in the order of receipt until Tuesday, October 16th, when we begin enrolling new campers.
  - Payment dates provided are Friday, February 1, 2019 and Monday, April 1, 2019.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Taconic — Lara — 7-12-23
text:
# Taconic



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Taconic can accommodate between 550 and 600 campers. They are offering a 4-week one time option for campers of any age.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lara and Emily S. were toured by Jason. Taconic has a high percentage of returning staff, with 50% of the staff members coming back each year. Additionally, 65 senior staff members return each summer.



## 3 · Living Quarters

They have 4 bunks in each age division consisting of 10 campers with 3-4 counselors. 2 groups share a bathroom in each bunk. If a bunk dynamic works they will keep it together summer to summer. Underneath all the bunks are extra rainy, early morning or hot day spaces with climbing walls and games. Campers bring towels to shower.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Campers have towel service at waterfronts. Recent additions include a slide in the party pool (2022).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They have a 6 period a day schedule where the youngest campers have a set schedule the first 5 periods and the last period is an elective. Campers in 8th, 9th, and 10th grades are choice and have the opportunity to choose their schedule from home.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Oldest campers travel to California. Recent additions include a ninja warrior course (2021); a pavilion type covering over the hockey rink (2023) and new pickle ball courts (2023); a slide in the party pool and an outdoor covered spin studio (2022). They have also built up a STEM program that includes a coding elective.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

All camp eats together at one family style seating. There is a full time rotating dr. and 7 nurses on site.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

As always Taconic looks great and campers all looked engaged and happy. I do feel they are at capacity though and appreciated him letting us know what age groups they could not tour.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation to camp is provided from Westchester, New Jersey - Essex County, Manhattan, and Florida. They are currently only accepting tours for rising 1st, 2nd, and 3rd graders. Parent communication, tech, and extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Taconic can accommodate between 550 and 600 campers and is offering a 4-week one time option for campers of any age. They have a 6 period a day schedule where the youngest campers have a set schedule the first 5 periods and the last period is an elective, while campers in 8th, 9th, and 10th grades are choice and have the opportunity to choose their schedule from home. Recent additions include a ninja warrior course, a pavilion type covering over the hockey rink and new pickle ball courts, a slide in the party pool, and an outdoor covered spin studio, and they have also built up a STEM program that includes a coding elective. Taconic has a high percentage of returning staff, with 50% of the staff members coming back each year; additionally, 65 senior staff members return each summer, and there is a full time rotating dr. and 7 nurses on site.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - As always Taconic looks great and campers all looked engaged and happy.
  - Taconic has a high percentage of returning staff, with 50% of the staff members coming back each year.
  - Additionally, 65 senior staff members return each summer.
  - Recent additions include a ninja warrior course (2021); a pavilion type covering over the hockey rink (2023) and new pickle ball courts (2023); a slide in the party pool and an outdoor covered spin studio (2022).
  - They have also built up a STEM program that includes a coding elective.
  - Transportation to camp is provided from Westchester, New Jersey - Essex County, Manhattan, and Florida.
  - They are offering a 4-week one time option for campers of any age.



#### Lowlights

  - I do feel they are at capacity though and appreciated him letting us know what age groups they could not tour.
  - They are currently only accepting tours for rising 1st, 2nd, and 3rd graders.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Taconic – Lara and Emily S. were toured by Jason Taconic can accommodate between 550 and 600 campers. They are offering a 4-week one time option for campers of any age. They are currently only accepting tours for rising 1st, 2nd, and 3rd graders. They have 4 bunks in each age division consisting of 10 campers with 3-4 counselors. 2 groups share a bathroom in each bunk. If a bunk dynamic works they will keep it together summer to summer. Underneath all the bunks are extra rainy, early morning or hot day spaces with climbing walls and games. They have a 6 period a day schedule where the youngest campers have a set schedule the first 5 periods and the last period is an elective. Campers in 8th, 9th, and 10th grades is choice and have the opportunity to choose their schedule from home. Oldest campers travel to California. Campers bring towels to shower but have towel service at waterfronts. All camp eats together at one family style seating. There is a full time rotating dr. and 7 nurses on site. Recent additions include a ninja warrior course (2021); a pavilion type covering over the hockey rink (2023) and new pickle ball courts (2023); a slide in the party pool and an outdoor covered spin studio (2022). They have also built up a STEM program that includes a coding elective. Transportation to camp is provided from Westchester, New Jersey - Essex County, Manhattan, and Florida. Taconic has a high percentage of returning staff, with 50% of the staff members coming back each year. Additionally, 65 senior staff members return each summer. As always Taconic looks great and campers all looked engaged and happy. I do feel they are at capacity though and appreciated him letting us know what age groups they could not tour.

title: TIMBER LAKE — LAURA — 6-28-17
text:
# TIMBER LAKE



## 1 · Program Snapshot

7-week co-ed camp located in the Catskills, NY. 460 Campers (half girls half boys). 500 acres of privately owned beautiful, immaculate grounds. Toured by Owners/Directors Mindy and Jay Jacobs. Starfish Ethics program is the “backbone of camp”.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Former campers going into grade 12 can be JCs and are the 3rd counselor in the bunk. Other counselors are college age or older. Growing in campers from Manhattan and Westchester. Tend to attract athletes.



## 3 · Living Quarters

BUNKS – Hooks outside (not lines); log cabin style; spacious and bright; 2 sided bunk with shared bath; A/C.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

2 small lakes in the center of camp. They do water ski on one of them. Spectacular Heated Pool. Indoor pool has been closed and that building now houses a spin studio, digital design studio/3D printing; radio station.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 Periods a day (3AM – 3PM). Every morning and afternoon includes: 1 elective; 1 athletic (instruction, league, dance, gymnastics); swim.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Semi-private tennis included (strong tennis program; instructors from Spain). Gymnastics (can work with mid-range gymnast). GOLF – 4 hole driving range; clubs provided; instructional program; 2-3 outings on local courses. Host large inter-camp tournaments (Gordon Cup, Jacob Cup, Owners Cup); dance competitions and local inter-camp games. High level of instruction for each sport; described as “COMPETITIVE WITH BEST SPORTSMANSHIP”. Covered arena for basketball and hockey. Fitness Center with high end equipment. Pristine athletic courts. QUEST outdoor adventure program for team building activities, high and low ropes. Tee Pee Village for overnights. Brand new state of the art kitchen facility for cooking (noted emergency allergy kit there). Art Studio with Fabric art, crafts, print making, stitchery, painting and beading. Spin studio, digital design studio/3D printing, radio station.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall is “Nut Aware”. They DO SERVE PEANUT BUTTER PACKETS. They have a food specialist to handle food allergies. Brand new state of the art kitchen facility for cooking (noted emergency allergy kit there). Health Center has A/C and pediatrician 2x a week. Meals served family style.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Described as “COMPETITIVE WITH BEST SPORTSMANSHIP”. High level of instruction for each sport. At night activities are separated boys/girls. Sometimes activities co-ed by division and sometimes full camp activities. It operates more like a brother sister camp. Brothers and sisters can get together after free play.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

ELECTRONICS – At night only PSP (hand-held games) NO CELL PHONES. Additional money for trips for older campers: Hershey, Boston, Montreal, rafting, DC, VA Beach. Timber Lake is not near any other camps. Parent communication: Unknown. Travel logistics: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Timber Lake is a 7-week co-ed camp located in the Catskills, NY with 460 campers (half girls, half boys) on 500 acres of privately owned beautiful, immaculate grounds. The Starfish Ethics program is the backbone of camp, and the program is described as “COMPETITIVE WITH BEST SPORTSMANSHIP” with a high level of instruction for each sport. Facilities include a spectacular heated pool, two small lakes with water skiing, pristine athletic courts, a covered arena, fitness center, and robust specialty areas (tennis, golf, gymnastics, QUEST ropes, arts, spin, digital design/3D printing, radio). Daily structure includes 6 periods with electives, athletics, and swim, meals are served family style, and tech is limited (no cell phones).



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong tennis program (semi-private; instructors from Spain).
  - Spectacular Heated Pool and 2 small lakes with water skiing.
  - GOLF – 4 hole driving range; clubs provided; instructional program; 2-3 outings on local courses.
  - Host large inter-camp tournaments (Gordon Cup, Jacob Cup, Owners Cup); dance competitions and local inter-camp games.
  - High level of instruction for each sport; described as “COMPETITIVE WITH BEST SPORTSMANSHIP”.
  - QUEST outdoor adventure program (team building, high and low ropes).
  - Covered arena for basketball and hockey; Fitness Center with high end equipment.
  - Art Studio with Fabric art, crafts, print making, stitchery, painting and beading.
  - Spin studio, digital design studio/3D printing, radio station.
  - BUNKS – log cabin style; spacious and bright; 2 sided bunk with shared bath; A/C.
  - Starfish Ethics program is the “backbone of camp”.



#### Lowlights

  - Dining Hall is “Nut Aware” but they DO SERVE PEANUT BUTTER PACKETS.
  - Additional money for trips for older campers.
  - Indoor pool has been closed.
  - ELECTRONICS – At night only PSP (hand-held games) NO CELL PHONES.
  - Timber Lake is not near any other camps.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: TRAILS END — 7-14-22
text:
# TRAILS END



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Coed Full Summer Camp that prioritizes taking a Detailed Approach to Summer Camp, and encouraging campers to explore new activities. Sessions: Full Summer Camp (7 weeks). Offers a 2 Day Discovery Camp for 60 kids entering 1st to 3rd grade.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: 560 campers (Full capacity), and even split of boys & girls; Ages 7 - 16. LIT Program is for campers going into 11th grade and focuses on travel, leadership & community service. Former campers can be counselors going into 12th grade. For new, older campers, if there is room, camp would suggest meeting camper before having them sign up to make sure they will fit in with current campers. Majority of campers come from the Tristate Area & Florida. 4 buses come from Long Island. Counselors/Staff: 40% of the counselors are international. I was very impressed with Marc & Kylie.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

After 7th grade, the program does allow for more choice in activities. Activities during the day are not co-ed, however, all evening activities are. There are no socials. Play is Co-ed, but otherwise activities during the day are single-sex.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Offer Academy Camp for Tennis, Golf, Soccer & Gymnastics, as well as 3 day mini-camps (workshops) for football, etc…. For Basketball, work with coaches from Level-Up. They are no longer offering horseback riding.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Uniform: No uniform, but it seems strongly enforced that all kids (specifically younger ones) wear hats, sunscreen & carry water bottles. Food: Unknown. Medical/health services: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

I was very impressed with Marc & Kylie. While the camp is big, it seems very well-run, organized, warm, energetic and that they do a good job of breaking the camp into smaller groups (by age), so that it doesn’t feel so overwhelming. The facilities are nice and it seems like there is an activity for every type of camper, and I think a majority of kids would do very well in their traditional, structured camp model. This would not be the camp, for a child who doesn’t love organized activities, wants a lot of choice in their schedule and needs some down time.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

NOTE ABOUT SUMMER 2023 - Camp is FULL with the exception of rising 5th graders. Focus on placing for Summer 2024. Tuition is expected to be $15,150. Majority of campers come from the Tristate Area & Florida. 4 buses come from Long Island. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown. Additional travel logistics: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

I was very impressed with Marc & Kylie. While the camp is big, it seems very well-run, organized, warm, energetic and that they do a good job of breaking the camp into smaller groups (by age), so that it doesn’t feel so overwhelming. The facilities are nice and it seems like there is an activity for every type of camper, and I think a majority of kids would do very well in their traditional, structured camp model. This would not be the camp, for a child who doesn’t love organized activities, wants a lot of choice in their schedule and needs some down time.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Coed Full Summer Camp that prioritizes taking a Detailed Approach to Summer Camp, and encouraging campers to explore new activities.
  - Very well-run, organized, warm, energetic.
  - They do a good job of breaking the camp into smaller groups (by age), so that it doesn’t feel so overwhelming.
  - The facilities are nice and it seems like there is an activity for every type of camper.
  - 560 campers (Full capacity), and even split of boys & girls; Ages 7 - 16.
  - LIT Program is for campers going into 11th grade and focuses on travel, leadership & community service.
  - After 7th grade, the program does allow for more choice in activities.
  - Offer Academy Camp for Tennis, Golf, Soccer & Gymnastics, as well as 3 day mini-camps (workshops) for football, etc….
  - For Basketball, work with coaches from Level-Up.
  - 40% of the counselors are international.



#### Lowlights

  - There are no socials.
  - This would not be the camp, for a child who doesn’t love organized activities, wants a lot of choice in their schedule and needs some down time.
  - They are no longer offering horseback riding.
  - Activities during the day are not co-ed.
  - Camp is big (560 campers).
  - NOTE ABOUT SUMMER 2023 - Camp is FULL with the exception of rising 5th graders.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: TIMBER TOPS — MICHELE — 7-4-24
text:
# TIMBER TOPS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

She describes it as a "campy, earthy camp with electives." They are in Wayne county. Many of the girls stay 7 but ok to do 3 1/2 and only stay one session. Brothers are mostly at Owego but also at Pine Forest.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Anna Black Morin (4th gen) toured us, we met many of the CTT girls while seeing camp (she stops to ask them what they love about camp, they’re very friendly & sweet). Anna is very nice, smart and funny - gives a really great tour\! Her father Micky is the owner of Pine Forrest. We met her daughter along the way, very nice, friendly family. We met her husband Eric at Pine Forrest too.



## 3 · Living Quarters

11th grade girls live in cute yurts (raised tent) for the summer. New 10th grade bunks with a lounge. 7th grade and up: live on senior hill. No towel service though she’s thinking of adding it since it’s going well at pine Forrest with that change.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Small lake with toys (no motorized). Pool is heated.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They get 2 choice periods a day; they decide on the spot so no need to pick ahead of time and then change. 7th grade and up then have to do certain activities that they weren’t doing when on lower camp.



## 6 · Program Highlights

They do play the other Wayne county camps in intercamp games. 7 tennis courts. Gymnastics is very nice facility and very popular. Dance is popular too. Great ropes course and outdoor adventure; they go to shohola falls for a rock climbing trip. 7th & 8th grade socials once a week and "teen canteen" get togethers as they get older. 11th grade girls go to Costa Rica on a community service trip with lake Owego boys & Pine Forrest.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

A/C in dining room, buffet style (food is good).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

She stressed that they have a "low key vibe"; when making teams they just want the girls to participate and try, it’s not all about winning. They sit by bunks and have a rule the first few days you can’t sit next to someone you know. They want them to make new friends\! Fri night they do wine and challah and no full service but do acknowledge Shabbat. Then have a special all camp campfire after to end the week. We met many of the CTT girls; they’re very friendly & sweet.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

MICHELE GERSHWIN The Camp Experts & Teen Summers In partnership with Joanne Paltrowitz 917-929-9906 cell. Travel logistics, parent communication, tech policy, and extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

TIMBER TOPS is a "campy, earthy camp with electives" in Wayne county with a low key vibe; many of the girls stay 7 but ok to do 3 1/2 and only stay one session. Campers get 2 choice periods a day and decide on the spot, with popular gymnastics and dance, 7 tennis courts, a small lake with toys (no motorized), a heated pool, and great ropes/outdoor adventure including a shohola falls rock climbing trip and intercamp games. Older campers live on senior hill, 10th grade bunks are new with a lounge, and 11th grade girls live in cute yurts and go to Costa Rica on a community service trip with lake Owego boys & Pine Forrest. Dining is A/C and buffet style (food is good), they sit by bunks with a first-days seating rule to make new friends, and Friday night they do wine and challah to acknowledge Shabbat then a special all camp campfire.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - "Low key vibe"; participation over winning on teams
  - 2 choice periods a day decided on the spot
  - Gymnastics facility is very nice and very popular; dance is popular too
  - 7 tennis courts
  - Heated pool
  - Great ropes course and outdoor adventure; shohola falls rock climbing trip
  - Intercamp games with other Wayne county camps
  - 7th & 8th grade weekly socials and "teen canteen"
  - 11th grade cute yurts; Costa Rica community service trip with lake Owego boys & Pine Forrest

  - A/C in dining room; buffet style (food is good)

#### Lowlights

  - Small lake (no motorized toys)
  - No towel service (she’s thinking of adding it)
  - 7th grade and up have to do certain activities they weren’t doing on lower camp



#### Similar Camps

  - Wayne for girls
  - Bryn Mawr (the only other all girls camps in Pa)
  - Mataponi
  - Wicosutta
  - Robindel
  - Matoaka

title: TRIPP LAKE — AMANDA — 1-17-24
text:
# TRIPP LAKE



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Philosophy/Mission: The camp’s mission is to provide a nurturing environment that enhances campers’ value & self-worth. Objectives: 1. To provide a well-rounded program that encourages campers to try new things & take on new challenges. 2. To provide a sense of community and to instill in all campers a sense of pride and belonging (illustrated from the bunk circle where all campers from youngest to oldest live in the same area; the camp community is really a sisterhood). 3. To have a camp full of rich traditions (the camp is almost 120 years old; ex. Sing). 4. To develop an appreciation for nature (they swim in the lake and take advantage of their beautiful surroundings). Camp Size: last summer, Tripp Lake had 265 campers; the goal for Summer 2024 is 275 campers. Dates/Length of Camp: Saturday, June 22 to Thursday, August 8. Program Length: this camp runs a 7-week program. First-Year Camper Option: first-year campers in grades 1st to 4th are allowed to come to camp for 4 weeks their first summer (one-time occurrence). Some exceptions to the 7 weeks will be made for international campers.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Presenters & Directors: Katie Milne, Co-Director of the camp (2 years at the camp as the Associate Director of Camp). Liz Kahn, Co-Director of the camp; a former camper and counselor; this will be her first summer as Director at camp. Leslie Konigsberg Levy, Director Emerita; a former camper, counselor, and director; she will be around for support this summer as the camp transitions to new directors. Camper Demographic Overview: Geographic—campers come from all over; the top 5 states are: New York, California, Florida, Illinois (i.e. Chicago), and New Jersey. NYC: most private schools and a lot of public schools are represented in the camp population, but no one school has a lot of kids from the same grade who attends. Age Range—the youngest campers are finishing 1st grade, and the oldest campers are going into 11th grade; most campers start after completing 2nd or 3rd grade. Senior campers are the rising 10th & 11th graders; for Summer 2024, they have 27 10th graders coming back; these girls are the leaders of the camp and responsible for creating/continuing the culture & spirit of camp (i.e. Color War Captains). International—they have 5 campers from Spain. New Campers Age Limit—the oldest new campers they will allow must be rising 9th graders (this past summer, they had a rising 9th grader from Spain who did great; GROUP MOMs play a large role in the success of older new campers transitioning). Alumni Kids: account for about 35% of the campers. Alumni: the camp prioritizes helping former campers maintain a lifelong connection to camp; they hired a Director of Alumni Relations (Georgia Sussman) and help former campers network for college, jobs, etc.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: all bunks are laid out in ONE circle to create community. Bunk Size: each bunk has about 6 campers (the age group is about 40 to 50 campers). Request Form/Friends: all campers can make requests; the camp will take into consideration but prioritize creating a good dynamic among the girls (though by middle school the bunks are pretty set). Amenities: bunks have sink & toilet but NO showers. Shower House: behind every age group is a shower house; there are 3 shower houses: lower, mid, and upper camp. Counselors: beginning in Summer 2024 all bunks will have 2 counselors.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They swim in the lake and take advantage of their beautiful surroundings. Courts are down by the lake. Specific waterfront activity offerings: Unknown.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Traveling around camp: lower campers move around as an age group. Tuesday is trip day for the entire camp. In addition, the older girls have additional age group nights out where they do fun outings (i.e. go to a Portland Sea Dogs baseball game).



## 6 · Program Highlights

Age Requirements for Activities: very few activities have age requirements since they can do most (i.e. ropes course) in an age-appropriate way; ONLY black & white photography and jewelry making have requirements (MUST be a rising 7th grader). Tennis: this is a BIG focus for the camp (they also have pickleball). They have 18 courts with lights. Girls play every day, and they work in groups of 4 girls to 1 coach. Courts are down by the lake. Extra Fee Programming: only horseback riding requires an extra fee; horseback riding is a small program with about 35–40 girls; participating in riding means missing out on other age group activities; in 2023, they had 6 horses. Socials/Inter-Camp: socials with the boy’s camps begin in 6th grade and they have 2–3 a summer; they mainly socialize with Takajo, Androscoggin, and Cedar; the oldest age group did a tennis social with Manitou last summer since both their camps have rising 11th graders, and they will likely do it again in Summer 2024. Inter-camps: they typically host them with Mataponi, Vega, Pinecliff and Fernwood; based on interest from the campers they are planning to add more inter-camps, specifically in basketball and soccer.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical Team: they have a doctor onsite, and 7 nurses; the medical center is open 24/7. Lewiston is the closest hospital with urgent cares between 5 to 10 min away. Food & Service: they are doing a kitchen renovation and just hired a new chef; the new chef has experience working with sororities and so there will be an emphasis on balanced meals. Special Diets: they have a dedicated chef. Seating: at the beginning of the summer, they sit with their bunks, but eventually they sit by age groups. Food Service: a combination of family, cafeteria and buffet style.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The camp community is really a sisterhood that connects all the campers to each other. Rich traditions—the camp is almost 120 years old; campers of all ages develop a love and admiration for the traditions (ex. Sing). Sense of community—to instill in all campers a sense of pride and belonging (illustrated from the bunk circle where all campers from youngest to oldest live in the same area). Friday Night Get Together/Religion: they do a Friday Night Get Together with some non-denominational prayer, but it is REALLY a camp get-together where they talk about the meaning of camp and friendship (counselors will speak, and age groups will perform a song). Have provided Shabbat when requested for small group.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation/Airports: will pick up and drop-off from Boston and Portland Airport. Extra Fees: transportation, long trips & canteen; horseback riding (only activity with an extra fee). Uniform: THEY ARE A UNIFORM CAMP. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Tripp Lake is a nearly 120-year-old uniform girls’ camp with a nurturing mission and a strong sense of sisterhood fostered by a bunk circle where all ages live together and rich traditions (ex. Sing). It runs a 7-week program (with a one-time 4-week option for first-year campers in grades 1–4), hosts about 265–275 campers, and draws girls from New York, California, Florida, Illinois, New Jersey, and abroad. Tennis is a big focus with 18 lighted courts and daily play, Tuesday is trip day for the entire camp, and socials/intercamps begin in 6th grade. Families should note airport pick-up from Boston and Portland, a robust medical team on site, extra fees (transportation/long trips/canteen and riding), and that the camp is transitioning to new co-directors with Director Emerita support.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Bunks are laid out in ONE circle to create community; the camp community is really a sisterhood.
  - Camp is almost 120 years old with rich traditions (ex. Sing).
  - Tennis is a BIG focus: 18 courts with lights; girls play every day in groups of 4:1 coach (plus pickleball).
  - Very few activity age requirements; campers are encouraged to try new things & take on new challenges.
  - Tuesday is trip day for the entire camp; older girls have additional age group nights out.
  - Medical center open 24/7 with a doctor onsite and 7 nurses; urgent care 5–10 min away; closest hospital Lewiston.
  - Kitchen renovation and a new chef with an emphasis on balanced meals; dedicated chef for special diets.
  - Airports: will pick up and drop-off from Boston and Portland Airport.
  - Strong alumni engagement with a Director of Alumni Relations (Georgia Sussman) and networking support.
  - Beginning Summer 2024 all bunks will have 2 counselors.
  - First-year 4-week option for grades 1st–4th (one-time).



#### Lowlights

  - Bunks have sink & toilet but NO showers; shower houses by age group (lower, mid, upper).
  - Horseback riding requires an extra fee and means missing out on other age group activities; in 2023 they had 6 horses.
  - Extra fees for transportation, long trips & canteen.
  - THEY ARE A UNIFORM CAMP.
  - Oldest new campers allowed must be rising 9th graders (may limit late entry).
  - Camp is transitioning to new directors (though Director Emerita will be around for support).
  - Specific waterfront activity offerings beyond lake swimming not detailed.



#### Similar Camps

  - Mataponi
  - Vega
  - Pinecliff
  - Fernwood



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Presenters & Directors Katie Milne: Co-Director of the camp. She has been at the camp for 2 years as the Associate Director of Camp. PRESENTER Liz Kahn: Co-Director of the camp. She is a former camper and counselor. This will be her first summer as Director at camp. PRESENTER Leslie Konigsberg Levy: Director Emerita. She is a former camper, counselor, and director. She will be around for support this summer as the camp transitions to new directors. Philosophy The camp’s mission is to provide a nurturing environment that enhances campers value & self- worth. They have 4 main objectives: 1. To provide a well-rounded program that encourages campers to try new things & take on new challenges. 2. To provide a sense of community. To instill in all campers a sense of pride and belonging. This is illustrated from the bunk circle where all campers from youngest to oldest live in the same area. a. The camp community is really a sisterhood that connects all the campers to each other. 3. To have a camp full of rich traditions. The camp is almost 120 years old. Campers of all ages develop a love and admiration for the traditions. (ex. Sing). 4. To develop an appreciation for nature. They swim in the lake and take advantage of their beautiful surroundings. Camp Size Last summer, Tripp Lake had 265 campers. The goal for Summer 2024 is 275 campers. Dates/Length of Camp Dates: Saturday, June 22 nd to Thursday, August 8 th First Year Camper: first year campers for in grades 1 st to 4 th are allowed to come to camp for 4 weeks their first summer. This is a one-time occurrence as this camp runs a 7-week program. \*Some exceptions to the 7 weeks will be made for international campers Camper Demographic Overview 1. Geographic: campers come from all over. The top 5 states are: New York, California, Florida, Illinois (i.e. Chicago), and New Jersey a. NYC: most private schools and a lot of public schools are represented in the camp population, but no one school has a lot of kids from the same grade who attends. 2. Age Range: the youngest campers are finishing 1 st grade, and the oldest campers are going into 11 th grade. a. Most campers start after completing 2 nd or 3 rd grade. b. Senior campers are the rising 10 th & 11 th graders. For Summer 2024, they have 27 10 th graders coming back. These girls are the leaders of the camp and responsible for creating/continuing the culture & spirit of camp (i.e. Color War Captains). 3. International: they have 5 campers from Spain 4. New Campers Age Limit: the oldest new campers they will allow must be rising 9 th graders. a. This past summer, they had a rising 9 th grader from Spain who did great. (GROUP MOMs play a large role in the success of older new campers transitioning). 5. Alumni Kids: account for about 35% of the campers. Alumni The camp prioritizes helping former campers maintain a lifelong connection to camp. 1. They hired a Director of Alumni Relations: Georgia Sussman. 2. They help former campers network for college, jobs, etc… Bunks Bunks: all bunks are laid out in ONE circle to create community. 1. Bunk Size: each bunk has about 6 campers. (The age group is about 40 to 50 campers). 2. Request Form/Friends: all campers can make requests. The camp will take into consideration but prioritize creating a good dynamic among the girls. (Though by middle school the bunks are pretty set). 3. Amenities: bunks have sink & toilet but NO showers. 4. Shower House: behind every age group is a shower house. a. There are 3 shower houses: lower, mid, and upper camp. 5. Counselors: beginning in Summer 2024 all bunks will have 2 counselors. Activities & Extras (programming discussed) 1. Age Requirements for Activities: very few activities have age requirements since they can do most (i.e. ropes course) in an age-appropriate way. a. ONLY black & white photography and jewelry making have requirements. MUST be a rising 7 th grader. 2. Tennis: this is a BIG focus for the camp. (They also have pickleball). a. They have 18 courts with lights. b. Girls play every day, and they work in group of 4 girls to 1 coach. c. Courts are down by the lake. 3. Extra Fee Programming: only horseback riding requires and extra fee. a. Horseback riding: is a small program with about 35-40 girls. Participating in riding means missing out on other age group activities. In 2023, they had 6 horses. 4. Traveling Around camp: a. Lower campers move around as an age group. Transportation Airports: will pick up and drop-off from Boston and Portland Airport Extra Fees Transportation, Long Trips & Canteen Uniform THEY ARE A UNIFORM CAMP. Friday Night Get Together/Religion They do a Friday Night Get Together with some non-denominational prayer, but it is REALLY a camp get-together where they talk about the meaning of camp and friendship. (i.e. Counselors will speak, and age groups will perform a song). 1. Have provided Shabbat when requested for small group Socials/Inter-Camp 1. Socials: socials with the boy’s camps begin in 6 th grade and they have 2-3 a summer. a. They mainly socialize with Takajo, Androscoggin, and Cedar. b. The oldest age group did a tennis social with Manitou last summer since both their camps have rising 11 th graders, and they will likely do it again in Summer 2024. 2. Inter-camps: they typically host them with Mataponi, Vega, Pinecliff and Fernwood. a. Based on interest from the campers they are planning to add more inter-camps. Specifically in basketball and soccer. Medical Team 1. They have a doctor onsite, and 7 nurses. The medical center is open 24/7 2. Lewiston is the closest hospital with urgent cares between 5 to 10 min away. Food & Service They are doing a kitchen renovation and just hired a new chef. 1. New Chef: the new chef has experience working with sororities and so there will be an emphasis on balanced meals. 2. Special Diets: they have a dedicated chef. 3. Seating: at the beginning of the summer, they sit with their bunks, but eventually they move sit by age groups. 4. Food Service: is a combination of family, cafeteria and buffet style. Trips Program Tuesday is trip day for the entire camp. In addition, the older girls have additional age group night’s out where they do fun outings (i.e. go to a Portland Sea Dogs baseball game).

title: Summit Camp — Shari — 8-29-23
text:
# Summit Camp



## 1 · Program Snapshot

BASIC DATA – various session options; 2, 3, 4, 9 wk options. About 30 kids do full summer. About 250 campers at a time at sleepaway program. Ages: @ sleepaway camp 7-21, Travel Program 15-23 (Costa Rica, Greece & Italy, Royal Caribbean Cruise), Weekender during school year 13-21 (1 wknd/ month at camp, 5 days in FL @ Disney over New Years. Available for 1st time campers in addition to current campers), The Center program 18-23 (life skills, jobs, social interaction, independence). He says they’re the surrounders of good people. Summit goals: having fun, gain independence and self esteem, work on social skills and executive functioning, making friends. He said they’re not going to change them, they help them gain life skills (which does ultimately help change them). See website for more detail.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Summit Camp – Shari & Lisa B. toured by Shep Baum 1 July 2023. Has a 12 y/o daughter w/ learning disabilities and growth challenges (see story on website) so he and his wife bought Summit. They also run similar programs in NYC and are expanding there. He knows every child & staff member at camp IN DEPTH. They also do have some neurotypical campers who may have social issues w/ no friends at other camps/were kicked out but through Summit’s programming and focus on social skills they find their happy place. They just don’t fit in somewhere else as Shep described, similar to The Bad News Bears, wide-mixture of types of campers. They may be great with adults but it’s peers they struggle with. He CANNOT take: suicidal, psychiatric, schizophrenic, or physically harmful to self or others. They had 2 non-binary (bunked by gender at birth) and 2 transgender (bunked by how they identified) campers in 2023. He had the largest influx of 16 year olds in 2023 as 1st time campers and they did great. Counselors are educators, studying education/special education. They have more staff than campers. Three seminars/wk for counselors. Staff members are closely evaluated whether they’re trying their hardest, have to be 100% there. He sent a staff member home not b/c he did anything wrong but Shep felt he wasn’t doing the best he could. Shep felt terrible having to do so but said he couldn’t take a chance and feels strongly about the camper care and attention given to the campers 24/7. Counselors return 3-4 years usually.



## 3 · Living Quarters

ACCOMMODATIONS – traditional cabins, spacious, 6-8 campers w/ 3-4 staff members, electricity, fans, bunk beds have railings, facilities in bunk. Laundry done weekly and comes back as a group sort and fold (life skill). The Center does their own laundry.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

AQUATICS – swimming lessons optional. Semi-private lake, banana boating but no other motorized activities. Aquapark, kayaks, canoes, row boats, no SUPS, heated pool. TRIPS – some Delaware River row boating.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING – 7 periods a day. Wake up to soft music. Have breakfast, chores, 2 periods, rest hour, lunch, 3 periods, other activities with a friend. Mostly single sex activities except at The Center and all are structured with some choice within the structures.



## 6 · Program Highlights

ACTIVITIES – The Cave, bounce houses, movie theatre, Chill Out Room for quiet time, salon for personal hygiene lessons, Life Skill Building w/ Home Ec, cooking, etc. Martial arts, yoga, videography, science, mini-golf and many more. CREATIVE – Engineering and edible engineering, 3D printing, DJ mixing and editing, computers, green screen, woodworking, ceramics. ORIENTEERING – high ropes, low ropes, zip line, climbing wall, mountain biking, trampoline, archery, survival, OLS. NATURE – goats, chickens. Shep has a dog that does roam freely (Oreo) and the kids love him.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – 6 nurses, virtual Dr, Wayne Memorial 12 miles away. Can take a diabetic probably but need to have in-depth chat with parents to assess if they can handle. DINING – industrial looking, very basic, air conditioned. Sit by cabin, two seatings, family style (older kids serve). They’re not kosher anymore but most of their meat is kosher b/c they’ve always shared delivery with kosher girls camp across lake so just kept it when he bought camp (have 3 kids who are kosher and parents send some food but they can provide also). Variety of options (vegan, pasta, salad bar, etc). Nut aware (serve WOW butter). Can handle allergies. 3 snacks/ day.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Having visited Summit under previous ownership, Lisa and I were extremely impressed and moved by the care, energy and genuine attention they are giving to their community. When one of the campers came up to Shep to say thank you for making me feel good yesterday we were teary and felt like he truly gave so much to his campers. Our tour was primarily focused on seeing the facilities and meeting many of the campers along the way so the notes below may reflect a lack of specific info like where buses are from, etc. We recommend seeing it for yourself to get the true essence of the program and the campers. Shep attended Echo Lake and took on the tradition of giving out Fuzzies at Summit during flagpole. RELIGIOUS LIFE – optional Fri PM candles and prayers by Shep, surprisingly a lot of kids attend. Saturday service offered but not every week. No traditional dinner, no transport to Church.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

TRANSPORT – Bus to camp, no buses home. Parents pick up at end of child’s session. Some chaperoned flights. COMMUNICATION/TECH – No phone calls home, one way email. Limit time on electronics but no cellular or wifi capabilities. Packages allowed but no food (they open it and he joked that he eats it if they send). Post up to 400 photos daily, facial recognition. Mondays they send a movie from the night before. INTAKE – Shep asks that we NOT check the box on our leads for parent requesting no phone calls. He MUST speak with each parent to assess if the child will be a good fit. Perhaps put in comment section wait 1 wk to contact parent instead. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Summit serves a wide mixture of campers, including some neurotypical kids with social issues who just don’t fit in somewhere else, with a strong focus on social skills, executive functioning, independence, and making friends. The day is highly structured (7 periods) with some choice, and camper care is intensive with more staff than campers and close supervision 24/7. They cannot accept campers who are suicidal, psychiatric, schizophrenic, or physically harmful to self or others, and intake includes a required parent call with Shep to assess fit. Families should expect no phone calls home, one-way email, bus to camp with parent pick-up at session end, and a semi-private lake and heated pool plus extensive creative, adventure, and life-skills programming.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Care, energy and genuine attention they are giving to their community.
  - He knows every child & staff member at camp IN DEPTH.
  - More staff than campers; counselors are educators/special ed with three seminars/wk.
  - Focus on social skills, executive functioning, independence, and making friends.
  - Post up to 400 photos daily, facial recognition; Monday night movie sent.
  - Semi-private lake + heated pool; Aquapark, kayaks, canoes, row boats.
  - Variety of options (vegan, pasta, salad bar, etc). 3 snacks/ day.
  - Tradition of giving out Fuzzies at flagpole.



#### Lowlights

  - Bus to camp, no buses home (parents pick up).
  - No phone calls home; one way email; limit time on electronics; no cellular or wifi capabilities.
  - Dining room industrial looking, very basic.
  - Not kosher anymore (though much of the meat is kosher).
  - No SUPS and no other motorized activities beyond banana boating.
  - He CANNOT take: suicidal, psychiatric, schizophrenic, or physically harmful to self or others.
  - Must speak with each parent before enrollment; asks to wait 1 wk to contact parent on leads.
  - Some details like where buses are from were not covered on the tour.



#### Similar Camps

  - Lee Mar
  - Akeela



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They also run similar programs in NYC and are expanding there. He knows every child & staff member at camp IN DEPTH. Having visited Summit under previous ownership, Lisa and I were extremely impressed and moved by the care, energy and genuine attention they are giving to their community. When one of the campers came up to Shep to say “thank you for making me feel good yesterday” we were teary and felt like he truly gave so much to his campers. Our tour was primarily focused on seeing the facilities and meeting many of the campers along the way so the notes below may reflect a lack of specific info like where buses are from, etc. We recommend seeing it for yourself to get the true essence of the program and the campers.
