title: TRAVEL FOR TEENS — AMANDA — 11-23-24
text:
# TRAVEL FOR TEENS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Travel for Teens offers cultural, language immersion, and service trips to over 45 countries, designed to foster independence and passion for travel. Most Popular Destinations: Middle School: Spain & Portugal, Costa Rica, and Greece. High School/Older Teens: Japan, Tanzania, Croatia, and Alpine Adventures. New Offerings: Shorter trips (e.g., 6-7 days in Costa Rica, Barcelona, Amalfi Coast). Combined longer trips, like Japan and South Korea. Payment plans for families to manage trip costs over time.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Enhanced Staff Training: Three-round interview process followed by in-depth background checks. In-person training sessions simulate real-life challenges (e.g., handling excluded participants or minor injuries). Staff retention increased to 70%, supported by higher pay and focused training for middle school-specific needs. Differences Between Age Groups: Middle school trips are more structured, with staff experienced in working with younger children. High school trips allow more independence and limited supervised alcohol consumption for 18+ participants in legal countries. Leadership note: Maggie and Ned emphasize Travel for Teens’ commitment to flexibility, inclusivity, and high-quality experiences.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations prioritize centrally located, clean, and safe facilities, typically 3-star hotels or bed-and-breakfasts. Rustic options like Belize and Fiji do not have air conditioning but are suited for adventurous travelers. No camping, no bed-sharing (except on Croatia’s boat trip), and single-room supplements are available upon request. Staff personally vet accommodations before booking. Rooming Policy for Transgender Participants: Room assignments are based on passport gender, with open communication between families and staff.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Authentic Experiences emphasize active participation, like trying local cuisines (e.g., Viking sushi in Iceland) or using public transport in Paris. Meals: Participants choose their meals from menus rather than set group menus, accommodating dietary restrictions. Activities foster independence, confidence, and practical travel skills, such as navigating maps and exploring cities. Middle school trips are more structured; high school trips allow more independence and limited supervised alcohol consumption for 18+ participants in legal countries.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Authentic experiences and active participation (e.g., Viking sushi in Iceland, public transport in Paris). Skill building in independence, confidence, and practical travel skills (navigating maps, exploring cities). Popular destinations span Spain & Portugal, Costa Rica, Greece, Japan, Tanzania, Croatia, and Alpine Adventures. New flexibility through shorter trips, combined longer itineraries (e.g., Japan and South Korea), and payment plans. Future Plans: Commitment to flexibility, inclusivity, and high-quality experiences; suggestions such as gender ratio transparency will be reviewed.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Enhanced safety through a three-round interview process, in-depth background checks, and in-person scenario training. A 24/7 risk management team operates globally, ensuring real-time responses to issues. For emergencies, staff take immediate action (e.g., medical visits or rerouting trips in case of unrest or natural disasters). Improved policies have reduced disciplinary issues, with fewer participants being sent home for rule violations. Medical and emergency situations have included urgent care for bug bites, food poisoning, and natural disasters like mudslides in Italy; parents are kept informed during incidents. Flexibility in itineraries: during emergencies (e.g., Munich shooting, Peru civil unrest), trips are rerouted or replaced with safer alternatives. Meals: Participants choose from menus rather than set group menus, accommodating dietary restrictions.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Designed to foster independence and passion for travel through authentic, hands-on cultural experiences. Emphasis on active participation, confidence-building, and practical travel skills. Inclusive tone, with leadership emphasizing flexibility, inclusivity, and high-quality experiences. Improved policies have reduced disciplinary issues.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Flights: Optional group flights are arranged from hubs like JFK, LAX, and Miami. Families can book flights independently, with staff meeting participants at destinations. Parent Communication: Parents are kept informed during incidents. Bi-weekly webinars cover flights, packing tips, and expectations. Costs: Single-room supplement is estimated at $100-150 per night, depending on the trip. Payment plans are available for families to manage trip costs over time. Gender ratios on some trips can be skewed (e.g., 10 girls and 1 boy); policy changes to provide earlier updates and flexibility for families are being considered.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Travel for Teens offers cultural, language immersion, and service trips in 45+ countries that build independence, confidence, and practical travel skills. Accommodations are centrally located and vetted, with clear policies and a global 24/7 risk management team to handle safety and emergencies. Programs balance structure for middle schoolers with more independence for high schoolers, and meals are chosen individually to accommodate dietary needs. Families can leverage optional group flights, payment plans, and webinars, with ongoing efforts to improve transparency around gender ratios.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Cultural, language immersion, and service trips to over 45 countries
  - Authentic, hands-on experiences (e.g., trying local cuisines, using public transport)
  - 24/7 global risk management with proactive rerouting during emergencies
  - Enhanced staff training and 70% staff retention
  - Centrally located, vetted accommodations; no camping
  - Payment plans and a mix of shorter and combined longer trips



#### Lowlights

  - Some trips have skewed gender ratios and parents noted late notification
  - Rustic options like Belize and Fiji do not have air conditioning
  - Room assignments for transgender participants are based on passport gender
  - Limited supervised alcohol consumption for 18+ in legal countries may not fit all families
  - Single-room supplement can add $100-150 per night



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

1\. Program Offerings and Updates ? Core Focus: Travel for Teens offers cultural, language immersion, and service trips to over 45 countries, designed to foster independence and passion for travel. ? Most Popular Destinations: o Middle School: Spain & Portugal, Costa Rica, and Greece. o High School/Older Teens: Japan, Tanzania, Croatia, and Alpine Adventures. ? New Offerings: o Shorter trips (e.g., 6-7 days in Costa Rica, Barcelona, Amalfi Coast). o Combined longer trips, like Japan and South Korea. o Payment plans for families to manage trip costs over time. 2. Accommodations and Logistics ? Accommodations: o Centrally located, clean, and safe facilities are prioritized, typically 3-star hotels or bed-and-breakfasts. Rustic options like Belize and Fiji do not have air conditioning but are suited for adventurous travelers. o No camping, no bed-sharing (except on Croatia?s boat trip), and single-room supplements are available upon request. o Staff personally vet accommodations before booking. ? Rooming Policy for Transgender Participants: Room assignments are based on passport gender, with open communication between families and staff. ? Flights: o Optional group flights are arranged from hubs like JFK, LAX, and Miami. o Families can book flights independently, with staff meeting participants at destinations. 3. Staffing and Safety ? Enhanced Staff Training: o Three-round interview process followed by in-depth background checks. o In-person training sessions simulate real-life challenges (e.g., handling excluded participants or minor injuries). o Staff retention increased to 70%, supported by higher pay and focused training for middle school-specific needs. ? Risk Management: o A 24/7 risk management team operates globally, ensuring real-time responses to issues. o For emergencies, staff take immediate action (e.g., medical visits or rerouting trips in case of unrest or natural disasters). o Improved policies have reduced disciplinary issues, with fewer participants being sent home for rule violations. 4. Cultural and Travel Skills Development ? Authentic Experiences: o Emphasis on active participation, like trying local cuisines (e.g., Viking sushi in Iceland) or using public transport in Paris. o Meals: Participants choose their meals from menus rather than set group menus, accommodating dietary restrictions. ? Skill Building: o Activities foster independence, confidence, and practical travel skills, such as navigating maps and exploring cities. 5. Handling Feedback and Issues ? Gender Ratios on Trips: o Some trips have skewed ratios (e.g., 10 girls and 1 boy). Parents expressed concerns about late notification. o Policy changes to provide earlier updates and flexibility for families are being considered. ? Medical and Emergency Situations: o Examples shared include urgent care for bug bites, food poisoning, and natural disasters like mudslides in Italy. Parents are kept informed during incidents. ? Flexibility in Itineraries: o During emergencies (e.g., Munich shooting, Peru civil unrest), trips are rerouted or replaced with safer alternatives. 6. Questions and Additional Updates ? Single-Room Supplement Costs: Estimated at $100-150 per night, depending on the trip. ? Differences Between Age Groups: o Middle school trips are more structured, with staff experienced in working with younger children. o High school trips allow more independence and limited supervised alcohol consumption for 18+ participants in legal countries. ? Bi-Weekly Webinars: Topics include flights, packing tips, and expectations. 7. Closing Remarks ? Future Plans: Maggie and Ned emphasize Travel for Teens? commitment to flexibility, inclusivity, and high-quality experiences. Suggestions from attendees, such as gender ratio transparency, will be reviewed.

title: Southwoods — Jennifer — 7-10-24
text:
# Southwoods



## 1 · Program Snapshot

This is the first summer with Carl and Maddie Acosta as directors so a lot of changes going on. Session options – 2, 3 or 2 weeks. Many campers combining and doing 5 weeks but only a handful staying all 7 weeks. Current numbers: Session 1 - 206 campers, Session 2 - 206 campers, Session 3 - 160 campers. Their ideal number as they grow is 250 campers.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jennifer, Beth, Shari and Lisa toured on 7/10/24 with new director Carl Acosta. This is the first summer with Carl and Maddie Acosta as directors so a lot of changes going on. Unknown camper profile beyond session counts.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: Typical bunk is about 10 kids and 4 counselors. Toilets and sinks in bunk but use shower house. All new mattresses this year. Counselors unpack for campers for all ages.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake: Nice lake at bottom of hill that campers go through tunnel to get to; with motorized and nonmotorized. They do a swim assessment and must do swim lessons if do not get the highest level.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule: For younger campers – 2 assigned activities plus 4 electives; older campers – 1 assigned bunk activity and rest elective. On first day of camp – campers rate activities as gotta have it, like it or do not like and then elective schedule is made based on that.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Arts and Crafts: Seemed on the more basic side, has a kiln but currently not working. Offers basic, simple cooking. New this year: circus and alpacas (came from Hilltop). Covered outdoor gymnastics pavilion. Offers all the standard sports but do not consider themselves a very competitive sports camp. Full color war at end of session 2. Maddie very passionate about horticulture so wants to add gardening program. Capital improvements planned: want to add a pool within 3 years, expand the health center and expand staff housing.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical: currently has 3 nurses on staff and 1 doctor but doctor only able to be there for 4 weeks. Next year will have a doctor for all 7 weeks and are looking for a mental health specialist. Dining Hall: Campers eat in 2 sittings. Sit at rectangular tables, food is served buffet style. They are peanut free, nut aware but feel they could not currently handle a camper with a serious food allergy. Canteen included in the price. Plan to expand the health center.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

We were there in the midst of a horrible thunderstorm so hard to get a good tour of the camp. Carl seemed a little overwhelmed and it appears to be a camp in the midst of a lot of changes as their first years as directors. Carl and Maddie seem to have a lot of good ideas for the future. They do not consider themselves a very competitive sports camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Offered buses from NYC, Westchester, NJ and Philadelphia – extra cost of $200 roundtrip. Trunks are shipped in advance and counselors unpack for campers for all ages. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown. Extra costs beyond bus: Unknown. Canteen included in the price.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Southwoods is in its first summer with Carl and Maddie Acosta as directors, so there are a lot of changes going on and some good ideas for the future. The program offers 2, 3 or 2 week sessions, with many campers combining to do 5 weeks and only a handful staying all 7 weeks, and uses a choice-heavy schedule where campers rate activities as gotta have it, like it or do not like. Facilities include a nice lake with motorized and nonmotorized options, standard sports, a covered outdoor gymnastics pavilion, and new offerings like circus and alpacas, with capital improvements planned. This is a friendly, not very competitive sports camp that seems in the midst of a lot of changes, with all new mattresses, supportive counselors, and buses from NYC, Westchester, NJ and Philadelphia.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Nice lake at bottom of hill that campers go through tunnel to get to; with motorized and nonmotorized.
  - Choice-based schedule with campers rating activities (gotta have it / like it / do not like) to build electives.
  - New this year: circus and alpacas (came from Hilltop).
  - Covered outdoor gymnastics pavilion.
  - All new mattresses this year.
  - Canteen included in the price.
  - Bunks: about 10 kids and 4 counselors.
  - Buses from NYC, Westchester, NJ and Philadelphia – $200 roundtrip.
  - Capital improvements planned (pool within 3 years, expand the health center and staff housing).
  - Maddie very passionate about horticulture and wants to add gardening program.



#### Lowlights

  - Arts and Crafts seemed on the more basic side; kiln currently not working.
  - They are peanut free, nut aware but feel they could not currently handle a camper with a serious food allergy.
  - Medical: doctor only able to be there for 4 weeks this summer; looking for a mental health specialist.
  - We were there in the midst of a horrible thunderstorm so hard to get a good tour.
  - Carl seemed a little overwhelmed; camp appears to be in the midst of a lot of changes as their first years as directors.
  - Do not consider themselves a very competitive sports camp (could be a downside for highly competitive athletes).



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

SOUTHWOODS Jennifer, Beth, Shari and Lisa toured on 7/10/24 with new director Carl Acosta This is the first summer with Carl and Maddie Acosta as directors so a lot of changes going on. Session options – 2,3 or 2 weeks. Many campers combining and doing 5 weeks but only a handful staying all 7 weeks. Current numbers: Session 1 - 206 campers, Session 2 - 206 campers, Session 3- 160 campers. Their ideal number as they grow is 250 campers. Offered buses from NYC, Westchester, NJ and Philadelphia – extra cost of $ 200 roundtrip. Trunks are shipped in advance and counselors unpack for campers for all ages. Schedule: For younger campers – 2 assigned activities plus 4 electives; older campers – 1 assigned bunk activity and rest elective On first day of camp – campers rate activities as gotta have it,like it or do not like and then elective schedule is made based on that. Bunks: Typical bunk is about 10 kids and 4 counselors. Toilets and sinks in bunk but use shower house. All new mattresses this year. Medical: currently has 3 nurses on staff and 1 doctor but doctor only able to be there for 4 weeks. Next year will have a doctor for all 7 weeks and are looking for a mental health specialist. Dining Hall: Campers eat in 2 sittings. Sit at rectangular tables, food is served buffet style. They are peanut free , nut aware but feel they could not currently handle a camper with a serious food allergy. Canteen included in the price Lake: Nice lake at bottom of hill that campers go through tunnel to get to; with motorized and nonmotorized. They do a swim assessment and must do swim lessons if do not get the highest level Arts and Crafts: Seemed on the more basic side, has a kiln but currently not working Offers basic, simple cooking. New this year: circus and alpacas ( came from Hilltop) Covered outdoor gymnastics pavilion Offers all the standard sports but do not consider themselves a very competitive sports camp Full color war at end of session 2 Maddie very passionate about horticulture so wants to add gardening program Capital improvements planned: want to add a pool within 3 years, expand the health center and expand staff housing Overall Impression: We were there in the midst of a horrible thunderstorm so hard to get a good tour of the camp. Carl seemed a little overwhelmed and it appears to be a camp in the midst of a lot of changes as their first years as directors. Carl and Maddie seem to have a lot of good ideas for the future.

title: Starlight — Risa — 7-10-24
text:
# Starlight



## 1 · Program Snapshot

CAMP STARLIGHT, Poconos, PA. Part of Wayne Country Camps. Summer 2024 has 520 Campers. Camp calls itself “Brother/Sister” but seems more like a coed camp.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

7/8/24, Risa Goldberg, Nathasha Kreizman, Lana Ast & Riley McDonough toured with Haley Miller Gothelf. Also met David Miller and Haley’s husband Robbie Gothelf. Robbie was previously at Timber Lake and brings his enthusiasm and experience from TL to Starlight. 300 staff. More international staff than previous years. No international campers. 20% LI, 20% NYC, 20% Westchester, 20% NJ, 30 campers from FL, 30 campers from CA, & a few from Dallas, TX.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks – lots of bunk junk (in girls’ bunks). 8-12 campers/3-4 counselors. Wood, single beds with some bunk beds. Fans. Beds assigned before camp. 1 Large bunk with oldest campers for boys & girls; about 24 campers.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lakeside – Separate side for Girls; separate for Boys (side by side in same lake). Swim instruction in pool & lake (mostly lake) for all campers except 14 & 15 yr olds. Lots of huge water toys. Motorized water sports.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 Periods/Day. 2 Choice. Can do private lessons during that time. No + cost. Every sport offered. “No one sport is more popular than another.”



## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports - Separate fields for Girls & Boys. All instructors are professionals in their sport. Program Heads are adults. Synthetic Ice Rink – camp provides ice skates. 2024 New Addition – Pickleball Glow at entrance to camp. Can play pickleball at night. Driving Range. Creative Arts - Large Art Center. Indoor Movie Theatre. Theatre - 5 Shows/summer. Everyone participates. Same theatre director at camp for 56 years. “Starlight Studios” with sports broadcasting, 3D printing, STEM type activities in basement of Canteen Bldg. Private guitar & piano lessons offered. No + $. “Carriage House” with spring board gymnastics floor, spin bikes for 12yr olds, dance. Cooking - Large Culinary Center since 2022.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall - Offers Gluten Free, Nut Free, Diabetics YES Kosher (grandfathered in). All meals coed. Sit by bunk in rectangular tables. Lunch picnic style. Friday traditional Shabbat dinner. Blessing with wine & challah. 20 minute service led by campers. Daily towel service for showers & lakefront/pool. Laundry service sent out. Air Conditioning in Rec Hall, Carriage House (Fitness Center), Cooking. DR on staff. PA & 7 Nurses.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp calls itself “Brother/Sister” but seems more like a coed camp. All meals coed. Directors are extremely enthusiastic and creative. All staff wear uniforms. Campers do not.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

2025 Tuition $16,550. Busses: 3 NYC, 4 Westchester, 3 NJ, 1 South NJ/Phila, 4 LI. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Private lessons during choice time and private guitar & piano lessons offered. No + cost / No + $. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

For parents who want a manicured camp with every sport and elective, Camp Starlight fits. Camp calls itself “Brother/Sister” but seems more like a coed camp, with all meals coed and separate Girls and Boys sides at the lake and on sports fields. Summer 2024 has 520 campers, a large tri-state population, and 300 staff (all instructors are professionals; Program Heads are adults). Standouts include motorized water sports and huge water toys, Synthetic Ice Rink and Pickleball Glow, Starlight Studios and a large Culinary Center, and robust dietary accommodations including Gluten Free, Nut Free, Diabetics, and YES Kosher.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Manicured camp with every sport and elective.
  - Directors are extremely enthusiastic and creative.
  - All instructors are professionals in their sport; Program Heads are adults.
  - Synthetic Ice Rink (camp provides ice skates).
  - 2024 New Addition – Pickleball Glow at entrance to camp; can play pickleball at night.
  - Starlight Studios with sports broadcasting, 3D printing, STEM type activities.
  - Theatre - 5 Shows/summer. Everyone participates. Same theatre director at camp for 56 years.
  - Cooking - Large Culinary Center since 2022.
  - Motorized water sports; lots of huge water toys.
  - Daily towel service for showers & lakefront/pool.
  - Private lessons during choice time and private guitar & piano lessons offered. No + cost / No + $.
  - Large Art Center; Indoor Movie Theatre.



#### Lowlights

  - Bunks – lots of bunk junk (in girls’ bunks).
  - 1 Large bunk with oldest campers for boys & girls; about 24 campers.
  - Camp calls itself “Brother/Sister” but seems more like a coed camp.



#### Similar Camps

  - IHC
  - Trail End
  - Tyler Hill
  - Timber Lake
  - Pontiac
  - Lokanda
  - Towanda (?)



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Also met David Miller and Haley’s husband Robbie Gothelf. Robbie was previously at Timber Lake and brings his enthusiasm and experience from TL to Starlight. Summer 2024 has 520 Campers. Camp calls itself “Brother/Sister” but seems more like a coed camp. 2025 Tuition $16,550 Dining Hall - Offers Gluten Free, Nut Free, Diabetics YES Kosher (grandfathered in) All meals coed. Sit by bunk in rectangular tables. Lunch picnic style. Friday traditional Shabbat dinner. Blessing with wine & challah. 20 minute service led by campers. Lakeside – Separate side for Girls; separate for Boys. (side by side in same lake). Swim instruction in pool & lake (mostly lake) for all campers except 14 & 15 yr olds. Lots of huge water toys. Motorized water sports. Daily towel service for showers & lakefront/pool. Bunks – lots of bunk junk (in girls’ bunks). - 8-12 campers/3-4 counselors - Wood, single beds with some bunk beds. - Fans - Beds assigned before camp. - 1 Large bunk with oldest campers for boys & girls; about 24 campers. 300 staff. More international staff than previous years. No international campers Busses: 3 NYC, 4 Westchester, 3 NJ, 1 South NJ/Phila, 4 LI. 20% LI, 20% NYC, 20% Westchester, 20% NJ, 30 campers from FL, 30 campers from CA, & a few from Dallas, TX. Sports - Separate fields for Girls & Boys. All instructors are professionals in their sport. Program Heads are adults. No + $ “Carriage House” with spring board gymnastics floor, spin bikes for 12yr olds, dance. Cooking - Large Culinary Center since 2022. All staff wear uniforms. Campers do not. Laundry service sent out. Air Conditioning in Rec Hall, Carriage House (Fitness Center), Cooking. DR on staff. PA & 7 Nurses. title: Tamakwa — Ashley — 8-13-23
text:
# Tamakwa



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Tamakwa, Algonquin Park, Ontario. Ashley Garson and Brooke Rotstein Toured July 2023. (2) 4 week sessions or full summer. 2 week session (1 time only) during the beginning of the second sessions, for first time campers in 1st-3rd grades. Also offer a 1 day teaser program and a mini session (3 days). VERY unique and beautiful setting. There are no motorized vehicles on camp, so everyone has to take a boat over to the camp property.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Tour given by Les Hartsman (Director). 275 campers, 300 max. 50% Americans—Very geographically diverse (Michigan, LA, NY, Florida).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins are beautiful, girls have bathrooms in the cabin (including showers), boys use shower houses. All were in great condition.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Campers are required to bring their own life jackets with them to camp. Sailing, paddle board, wind surfing are big activities. No motorized water sports\!



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

1st 3 activities are choice, afternoon are by cabin. Once a week mandatory hike in the afternoon—cook out in the wilderness.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Trips are mandatory each month (session). Can go on trips up to 15 days. We toured at the beginning of the second month and ALL campers were learning trip skills (canoeing, fire building, tent building, etc.). 1/2 court tennis/pickle only, no full courts. Climbing wall. Have color war (siblings and cousins are on the same team). All day program (survivor day).



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Full-time doctor on camp, campers bring their own meds with them. 40 minutes from hospital. Have the ability to airlift/extract campers from trips if emergency, trips have Sat phones with them. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This is a truly beautiful camp, unlike any other, and hard to describe to someone who hasn’t actually seen it. Has an intimate, family feeling to it and everyone knew each other. Prayer to start all meals. Friday blessings at dinner and Friday Service—after services there is "hobby time". This camp is not for everyone, but the campers there were SO happy\!



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Offer a shuttle from YYZ (Toronto Pearson) airport to camp. There are no motorized vehicles on camp, so everyone has to take a boat over to the camp property. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

VERY unique and beautiful setting in Algonquin Park; there are no motorized vehicles on camp, so everyone has to take a boat over to the camp property. (2) 4 week sessions or full summer, with a 2 week session (1 time only) for first time campers in 1st-3rd grades, plus a 1 day teaser and a mini session (3 days). For the more outdoorsy camper: trips are mandatory each month (session) and can go on trips up to 15 days, with a once a week mandatory hike in the afternoon. Has an intimate, family feeling and everyone knew each other—this camp is not for everyone, but the campers there were SO happy; it’s an amazing, different option for campers from everywhere\!



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Truly beautiful camp, unlike any other
  - Has an intimate, family feeling to it and everyone knew each other
  - Boat-only access; there are no motorized vehicles on camp
  - Outdoorsy tripping culture: trips are mandatory each month (session) and can go on trips up to 15 days
  - Sailing, paddle board, wind surfing are big activities; no motorized water sports
  - Offer a shuttle from YYZ (Toronto Pearson) airport to camp
  - 275 campers, 300 max; 50% Americans—Very geographically diverse (Michigan, LA, NY, Florida)
  - Girls have bathrooms in the cabin (including showers); boys use shower houses; all were in great condition
  - Full-time doctor on camp; 40 minutes from hospital; trips have Sat phones and airlift/extract capability



#### Lowlights

  - 1/2 court tennis/pickle only, no full courts
  - Campers are required to bring their own life jackets with them to camp
  - Trips are mandatory each month (session)
  - This camp is not for everyone
  - No motorized water sports



#### Similar Camps

  - White Pine
  - Wahanowin
  - Manitou
  - outdoorsy, tripping camps



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Tamakwa Algonquin Park, Ontario Ashley Garson and Brooke Rotstein Toured July 2023 Tour given by Les Hartsman (Director) 275 campers, 300 max 50% Americans—Very geographically diverse (Michigan, LA, NY, Florida) Offer a shuttle from YYZ (Toronto Pearson) airport to camp (2) 4 week sessions or full summer 2 week session (1 time only) during the beginning of the second sessions, for first time campers in 1 st -3 rd grades. Also offer a 1 day teaser program and a mini session (3 days). VERY unique and beautiful setting. There are no motorized vehicles on camp, so everyone has to take a boat over to the camp property. For the more outdoorsy camper. Trips are mandatory each month (session). Can go on trips up to 15 days. We toured at the beginning of the second month and ALL campers were learning trip skills (canoeing, fire building, tent building, etc.). Campers are required to bring their own life jackets with them to camp. Once a week mandatory hike in the afternoon—cook out in the wilderness. Cabins are beautiful, girls have bathrooms in the cabin (including showers) boys use shower houses. All were in great condition. 1 st 3 activities are choice, afternoon are by cabin ½ court tennis/pickle only, no full courts. Sailing, paddle board, wind surfing are big activities. No motorized water sports\! Climbing wall Prayer to start all meals Friday blessings at dinner and Friday Service—after services there is “hobby time” Have color war (siblings and cousins are on the same team) All day program (survivor day) Full-time doctor on camp, campers bring their own meds with them 40 minutes from hospital Have the ability to airlift/extract campers from trips if emergency, trips have Sat phones with them. This is a truly beautiful camp, unlike any other, and hard to describe to someone who hasn’t actually seen it. Has an intimate, family feeling to it and everyone knew each other. This camp is not for everyone, but the campers there were SO happy\! Would give with other camps in the same area (White Pine, Wahanowin, Manitou), and also with camps that are more outdoorsy, tripping camps. It’s an amazing, different option for campers from everywhere\!

title: USDAN — LAURA — 7-26-19
text:
# USDAN



## 1 · Program Snapshot

USDAN is an ARTS day camp located in Wheatley Heights, New York (Long Island not far from Huntington). Campers can go for 3/4/7 weeks. The majority do 7 and they have close to 1600 campers\! This property is HUGE, 140 acres\! Camp is from 10-3.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We were toured by the Director, Andrew Copper. Majority of kids from Long Island. Instructors are experienced artists and teachers. In general, 1 year of experience required for music programs (including reading music) but no experience for theater, dance and chorus.



## 3 · Living Quarters

As a day camp, living quarters are not applicable. The grounds are shady, well-kept and filled with trees. Activities are held in beautiful open air pavilions. There is a beautiful entrance gate with security (we all received visitor stickers) and then a long road leading towards the main building parking lot with security there as well.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is a pool (swimming is optional).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Camp is from 10-3. 2nd grade and older choose one 2-hour major and a 1-hour minor. They also they select 1 recreational activity (swim, quidditch, tennis, dance, hoops and nets, archery or yoga). Discovery program is pre-K through 1st grade and have counselors with them all day (older campers travel alone). They also have a pre-set program. Younger and older campers are not in activities together.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Areas of study include all areas of fine art, fashion design, sewing, photography, music, dance, theater, writing, chess, yoga, archery and tennis (additional money), farming/cooking program. Activities are held in beautiful open air pavilions.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There is a beautiful entrance gate with security (we all received visitor stickers) and then a long road leading towards the main building parking lot with security there as well. Health services: Unknown. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The grounds are shady, well-kept and filled with trees. Activities are held in beautiful open air pavilions. Younger and older campers are not in activities together.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Majority of kids from Long Island but they do run buses from Long Island, Brooklyn, Bronx, Queens, Westchester and Manhattan. Parents invited to performances during the summer. Photos posted daily and a weekly newsletter. Tennis (additional money). Tech policy: Unknown. Other extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

USDAN is an ARTS day camp in Wheatley Heights, New York (Long Island not far from Huntington) on a HUGE 140-acre property with close to 1600 campers. Camp runs 10-3 for 3/4/7 weeks (most do 7) and offers all areas of fine art, fashion design, sewing, photography, music, dance, theater, writing, chess, yoga, archery, tennis (additional money), and a farming/cooking program. 2nd grade and older choose one 2-hour major, a 1-hour minor, and one recreational activity, while the Discovery program (pre-K through 1st) follows a pre-set program with counselors all day and older campers travel alone. Buses run from Long Island, Brooklyn, Bronx, Queens, Westchester and Manhattan; there’s a pool (swimming is optional), security at the entrance and main lot, and parents receive daily photos and a weekly newsletter.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - This property is HUGE, 140 acres\!
  - They have close to 1600 campers\!
  - Activities are held in beautiful open air pavilions.
  - Instructors are experienced artists and teachers.
  - Areas of study include all areas of fine art, fashion design, sewing, photography, music, dance, theater, writing, chess, yoga, archery and tennis (additional money), farming/cooking program.
  - They do run buses from Long Island, Brooklyn, Bronx, Queens, Westchester and Manhattan.
  - There is a beautiful entrance gate with security (we all received visitor stickers) and then a long road leading towards the main building parking lot with security there as well.
  - Photos posted daily and a weekly newsletter.



#### Lowlights

  - Tennis (additional money).
  - Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: TWIN CREEKS — LINDSEY — 10-10-19
text:
# TWIN CREEKS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Sessions: 2 weeks. Each session does the same thing so really intended to be a 2 week camp only. 2020 Start Date: June 21.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers: 250-300 kids. About 10% international. 60% from DC/MD/VA. There are some kids from Philly and Florida. Staff: 100 staff. About 30% international. Wear staff shirts. Religion: none, but he would guess 50% of kids are Jewish. Director: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: there is a main village with girls bunks on one half and boys on the other. There are no bathrooms in the bunk. Bunks have 18 beds- usually 15 kids and 3 staff. Seniors are up on the hill.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront: lake slide and bouncy toys. Waterskiing is off property and included in the price. They can canoe and kayak. Pool not heated. He is resurfacing it this year. Instructional swim will be 2-3 times over the two weeks. Bathing Suit Policy: Unknown.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 3 bunk activities and 2 choice. Of the choice they are 1 masterclass (everyday the whole session) and 1 bunk choice. The sports are instructional.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Trip Day: no. They do a waterski trip, and the older kids go on a mountain bike trip and white water rafting. Color war 1 day every session, team for life. Range of activities: zipline and ropes, creek snorkeling, flag football, rugby, lacrosse, horseback riding, indoor basketball area, theater, lit tennis and basketball courts, woodworking, jewelry making, campfire cooking and culinary, dance (no gymnastics), gaga, volleyball.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining hall: nice with fans. 2 seatings and sit by bunk. Buffet style with lots of options. Nut free. Health Center: not air conditioned. 2 nurses. Can take diabetics. Hospital is 20 min away. Do not require vaccines\!\!\!



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Color war 1 day every session, team for life. Wear staff shirts. Religion: none, but he would guess 50% of kids are Jewish.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses: from Reston. They also do a pickup at Dulles. Tuition: $2999. Electronics: none. Email: two-way through Camp Minder. Phone calls: one. Packages allowed but no food. Photos: yes. Visiting Day: none.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Twin Creeks runs 2-week sessions (each session does the same thing so really intended to be a 2 week camp only). Enrollment is 250–300 kids (about 10% international; 60% from DC/MD/VA), with 100 staff (about 30% international). Programming is 3 bunk activities and 2 choice (including 1 masterclass everyday the whole session), instructional sports, a 1-day Color War each session, and trips such as waterskiing (included), mountain biking, and white water rafting for older campers. Families get buses from Reston or a Dulles pickup, no electronics, two-way email through Camp Minder, one phone call, packages allowed but no food, photos yes, and tuition is $2999.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Waterskiing is off property and included in the price.
  - Color war 1 day every session, team for life.
  - Programming: 3 bunk activities and 2 choice (including 1 masterclass everyday the whole session).
  - Range of activities: zipline and ropes, creek snorkeling, flag football, rugby, lacrosse, horseback riding, indoor basketball area, theater, lit tennis and basketball courts, woodworking, jewelry making, campfire cooking and culinary, dance (no gymnastics), gaga, volleyball.
  - Dining hall: nice with fans. Buffet style with lots of options. Nut free.
  - Buses: from Reston. They also do a pickup at Dulles.
  - About 10% international campers; about 30% international staff.



#### Lowlights

  - There are no bathrooms in the bunk.
  - Health Center: not air conditioned.
  - Do not require vaccines\!\!\!
  - Pool not heated.
  - Visiting Day: none.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: TOWANDA — EMILY — 7-13-23
text:
# TOWANDA



## 1 · Program Snapshot

7-week program. Case-by-case basis for one 4-week option. 440 kids. Will take kids as young as rising 1st. Kids come from: Tristate area, Maryland/DC, Philly, Florida. This camp is an all-around traditional camp. Thursdays are designated as trip days for the camp. There is a uniform policy with different themes on different days.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Very big alumni population, and a lot of the upper staff has been there for over 30 years. LITs (Leaders in Training) serve in various capacities at the camp. There is a staff area that includes a pub while counselors have time off.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunking: 8-12 campers 4 counselors. The camp rearranges the bunks every year, mixing kids up. Campers do NOT need to bring their own towels, sheets, and pillows.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Pool is heated and is right by the lake which is fully motorized. Kids come down to the waterfront in clothes. They have bathing suits already there. Change out bathing suits once a week.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 6 period day. Lower camp has one option period. Upper camp has two. During the options period, campers can participate in various activities, including pickleball, which will have six courts built. 95% of evening activities are ALL CAMP events.



## 6 · Program Highlights

There is a driving range and a building for mini golf. They occasionally take the kids off-site to play golf. There is a study hall available with Wi-Fi access. 3 plays throughout the summer. The camp has tree houses for activities like yoga. There is an animal option available at the camp. Quidditch is now offered as an activity. The camp has a master camp program that offers additional time in many different areas.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining at the camp involves sitting with kids from your age group and rotating arrangements. Everyone eats at once. Tables are switched based on age groups. There are two ER doctors on staff at the camp.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The camp follows an old-school approach and does not micromanage the campers. When families tour camp, the kids will be separated from the parents. Religion is part of the camp's program in that they say Motzi on Friday nights.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Cell phones are not allowed, and campers are only permitted one call per summer. There is a study hall available with Wi-Fi access. Thursdays are designated as trip days for the camp. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication beyond the one call: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

This camp is an all-around traditional camp with a 7-week program (case-by-case for one 4-week option) and an old-school approach. It serves 440 kids, will take kids as young as rising 1st, and draws from the Tristate area, Maryland/DC, Philly, and Florida, with a very big alumni population and a lot of the upper staff there for over 30 years. Programming includes a 6 period day with choice (lower camp one option, upper camp two), 95% ALL CAMP evening activities, trip days on Thursdays, and a heated pool by a fully motorized lake. Cell phones are not allowed (one call per summer), there is a uniform policy with themed days, and they say Motzi on Friday nights.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All-around traditional camp with an old-school approach; does not micromanage
  - Strong alumni and upper staff tenure (30+ years)
  - 6 period day with choice (lower 1 option; upper 2 options)
  - 95% of evening activities are ALL CAMP events
  - Heated pool by a fully motorized lake
  - Unique options: Quidditch, animal option, tree houses for yoga
  - Driving range and mini golf; occasional off-site golf
  - Master camp program for additional time in many areas
  - Study hall with Wi-Fi
  - Two ER doctors on staff
  - Uniform policy with themed days
  - Trip days on Thursdays



#### Lowlights

  - Cell phones are not allowed; only one call per summer
  - Case-by-case for one 4-week option only
  - Bunks are rearranged every year, mixing kids up
  - Kids are separated from parents during tours
  - Bathing suits kept at waterfront and changed out once a week
  - Lake is fully motorized (preference-dependent)



#### Similar Camps

  - Pine Forest
  - Canadensis
  - Nockamixon
  - Westmont
  - Chenawanda



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

7-week program. Case by case bases for 1 x 4 week option. 440 kids. Will take kids as young as rising 1 st . Kids come from: Tristate area, Maryland/DC, Philly, Florida Very big alumni popular and a lot of the upper staff has been there for over 30 years. The camp follows an old-school approach and does not micromanage the campers. When families tour camp, the kids will be separated from the parents. There is a uniform policy with different themes on different days. Thursdays are designated as trip days for the camp. Bunking: 8-12 campers 4 counselors. The camp rearranges the bunks every year, mixing kids up. Campers do NOT need to bring their own towels, sheets, and pillows. Programming: 6 period day. Lower camp has one option period. Upper camp has two. During the options period, campers can participate in various activities, including pickleball, which will have six courts built. 95% of evening activities are ALL CAMP events. Dining: Dining at the camp involves sitting with kids from your age group and rotating arrangements. Everyone eats at once. There is a driving range and a building for mini golf. They occasionally take the kids off-site to play golf. There is a study hall available with Wi-Fi access. 3 plays throughout the summer. The camp has tree houses for activities like yoga. There is an animal option available at the camp. Quidditch is now offered as an activity. Cell phones are not allowed, and campers are only permitted one call per summer. Religion is part of the camp's program in that they say Motzi on Friday nights. The camp has a master camp program that offers additional time in many different areas. There is a staff area that includes a pub while counselors have time off. There are two ER doctors on staff at the camp. Tables are switched based on age groups. LITs (Leaders in Training) serve in various capacities at the camp. Pool is heated and is right by the lake which is fully motorized. Kids come down to the waterfront in clothes. They have bathing suits already there. Change out bathing suits once a week. This camp is an all-around traditional camp. As far as competition and facilities, and depending what the family is looking for, I would recommend this with Pine Forest, Canadensis, Nockamixon, Westmont and Chenawanda.

title: Taunton — Laura — 1-24-24
text:
# Taunton



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Taunton Summer School is an academic program with co-curricular activities and excursions. Taunton School is based in the South West of England, in Somerset. The campus is all in one site, where the Prep School, the Senior School and the International School are located. The summer program is using the premises of the Prep School and the Senior school and students have access to the sports facilities of all schools (including 2 Olympic swimming pools, football pitches, sports fields, tennis courts, etc). The teachers and staff of the summer programs are from Taunton School. They offer two summer programs: Junior Program from 8 to 12 years’ old; Senior Program from 13 to 17 years’ old. The summer programs start on June 29th for periods of two weeks. They have a cap of 10% per nationality/spoken language. They also offer a Pre Sessional Program at the end of August, ideal for students who will integrate the English system from September.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

They offer two summer programs: Junior Program from 8 to 12 years’ old; Senior Program from 13 to 17 years’ old. The teachers and staff of the summer programs are from Taunton School. They have a cap of 10% per nationality/spoken language. They do a placement test on arrival to put children in the right course. They have small classes (maximum 12 students but ideally 8).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Boarding Facilities: They use boarding houses from the Prep and Senior Schools. In the Junior program students typically share rooms for 3, whereas in the Senior program they can share a room in 2 or 3 or have a single room. They typically try to mix nationalities in the rooms to encourage students to speak English.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Students have access to the sports facilities of all schools (including 2 Olympic swimming pools, football pitches, sports fields, tennis courts, etc).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Taunton Summer School is a “Boarding school” like experience with lessons from Monday to Friday, activities and 3 excursions a week (Wednesday afternoon, Saturday and Sunday all day, except arrival and departure day). English lessons are up to 20 h/week for the Junior course and 25h/week for the Senior course. They offer two different English courses: Core English (corresponding to level from A1 to B2) and Advanced English (corresponding to level C1-C2). Activities are different for Junior course and Senior Course. Junior course activities are to be chosen between: Academic, Multi Sports, Cookery and STEM (these activities take place in the afternoons from Monday to Friday). Students can choose one activity per week. Senior course activities are to be chosen between: Academic, Future Business Leaders, Performing Arts, STEM, English Enrichment, Tennis Academy, Football Academy (these activities take place in the mornings from Monday to Friday).



## 6 · Program Highlights

English learning is the core of their program. They also offer a Pre Sessional Program at the end of August, ideal for students who will integrate the English system from September. This can be a 10 or 15 days program. It consists of 3 hours of English + 2 hours of Study Skills per day + activities and sports.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Unknown



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Taunton Summer School is a “Boarding school” like experience. They typically try to mix nationalities in the rooms to encourage students to speak English. The teachers and staff of the summer programs are from Taunton School. They have a cap of 10% per nationality/spoken language.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Taunton School is based in the South West of England, in Somerset. The closest towns are Bristol and Exeter and London is 1h 40 minutes away. The school is only 10 minutes walking distance from Taunton village and close to beaches and historical cities. They offer free transfers from Bristol Airport and London Airports. Parent communication, tech policy and extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Taunton Summer School is an academic program with co-curricular activities and excursions. English learning is the core of their program, with small classes (maximum 12 students but ideally 8) and two different English courses: Core English (corresponding to level from A1 to B2) and Advanced English (corresponding to level C1-C2). It is a “Boarding school” like experience with lessons from Monday to Friday, activities and 3 excursions a week, and English lessons up to 20 h/week for the Junior course and 25h/week for the Senior course. They offer two summer programs (Junior 8 to 12; Senior 13 to 17) plus a Pre Sessional Program at the end of August, and the campus offers access to extensive sports facilities (including 2 Olympic swimming pools, football pitches, sports fields, tennis courts, etc).



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Small classes (maximum 12 students but ideally 8).
  - They offer two different English courses: Core English (A1-B2) and Advanced English (C1-C2).
  - Activities and 3 excursions a week (Wednesday afternoon, Saturday and Sunday all day).
  - Access to excellent facilities, including 2 Olympic swimming pools, football pitches, sports fields, tennis courts.
  - Pre Sessional Program (10 or 15 days) with 3 hours of English + 2 hours of Study Skills per day + activities and sports.
  - Free transfers from Bristol Airport and London Airports.
  - Cap of 10% per nationality/spoken language.



#### Lowlights

  - Food details: Unknown.
  - Health and safety policies: Unknown.
  - Parent communication and tech policy: Unknown.
  - Waterfront programming beyond pool access: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: TWIN CREEKS — 11-22-24
text:
# TWIN CREEKS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Celebrating 25 years in West Virginia, known for its two-week sessions. Focuses on youth development through a structured camp experience with a beginning, middle, and end. Two-week sessions remain the core offering, with strong camper loyalty to session dates. Four-week options available (two sessions back-to-back) for older campers.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Presented by Iain McClements. Two-week sessions remain the core offering, with strong camper loyalty to session dates.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Focuses on youth development through a structured camp experience with a beginning, middle, and end.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Doubling Dream Week sessions (1-week introductory program) for younger campers. Expansion of arts programming, including a new outdoor theater and woodshop renovations. Two-week sessions remain the core offering, with strong camper loyalty to session dates. Four-week options available (two sessions back-to-back) for older campers.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Unknown



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Celebrating 25 years in West Virginia, known for its two-week sessions. Strong camper loyalty to session dates.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Offers chaperoned flights from Florida and buses from DC. Considering expanding transport options from Pennsylvania. Plans to collaborate with Twin Creeks on transportation options for families from Philadelphia and surrounding areas.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Celebrating 25 years in West Virginia, Twin Creeks is known for its two-week sessions. It focuses on youth development through a structured camp experience with a beginning, middle, and end. Doubling Dream Week sessions (1-week introductory program) for younger campers and an expansion of arts programming, including a new outdoor theater and woodshop renovations, are notable updates. Transportation includes chaperoned flights from Florida and buses from DC, with four-week options available (two sessions back-to-back) for older campers.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Celebrating 25 years in West Virginia.
  - Two-week sessions remain the core offering, with strong camper loyalty to session dates.
  - Doubling Dream Week sessions (1-week introductory program) for younger campers.
  - Expansion of arts programming, including a new outdoor theater and woodshop renovations.
  - Chaperoned flights from Florida and buses from DC.
  - Considering expanding transport options from Pennsylvania.



#### Lowlights

  - Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

1\. Alpengirl Camp – Presented by Gabby McElwain • Overview: o An adventure travel camp for teen girls (ages 11-17). o Programs are 1-2 weeks in length, focusing on outdoor camping and exploration. o Unique in its lack of a traditional campus, with trips starting and ending at airports. o Locations: Northern Rocky Mountains, Pacific Northwest, and new expansions to Maine and Iceland. • New Offerings: o Maine Expedition: An 8-day adventure for middle and high schoolers. o Iceland Trip: A nearly sold-out program for high school students. o Mother-Daughter Adventures: Popular sea kayaking and backpacking programs (recently filled). • Core Features: o Multi-adventure trips combining activities like backpacking, horsepacking, yoga, and hot springs. o Emphasis on fostering a love for the outdoors, building confidence, and teaching camping skills. o Staff includes certified wilderness responders for safety. • Target Audience: o Girls new to outdoor experiences. Most participants are first-time backpackers. o A safe, supportive, all-girls environment tailored for hygiene, confidence, and outdoor skills. 2. Camp Jeanne d'Arc – Presented by Sandy • Overview: o Traditional all-girls camp in the Adirondacks of New York for ages 7-16. o Rooted in values of faith, endurance, courage, and confidence. o Offers 2-7 week programs with a focus on fostering strong, independent girls. • Core Features: o Traditional camp activities like horseback riding, water skiing, and arts and crafts. o Strong international presence, with campers from Puerto Rico, Mexico, and beyond. o Faith-based optional activities like Catholic mass and mindfulness sessions. • New Initiatives: o Owl Program: For older campers, including leadership skills, adventure trips, and service opportunities. o Progressive skill-building over years, culminating in a unique wilderness trip for older campers. o Cultural experiences: Raised flags for Argentina and Azerbaijan last year to honor new campers’ home countries. • Transportation: o Offers airport pickups at Montreal and Burlington for international and domestic campers, respectively. 3. Runoia Camp – Presented by Alex Jackson • Overview: o Traditional girls’ camp in Maine with a family-oriented approach. o Offers a range of session lengths and is known for accommodating “quirky” or anxious campers. o Strong emphasis on communication with families, especially first-time camp parents. • New Features: o Harmony Heights: A 10-day program bridging younger kids’ transition into longer camps. o Mother-Daughter Weekends: Open to extended family members. o Leadership Training Program: A 3-week offering for 16-year-olds, including those new to Renoia. • Equestrian Program: o Includes 2 h of mounted instruction and 2 h of ground instruction daily, plus evening horse-related activities. 4. Twin Creeks Camp – Presented by Iain McClements • Overview: o Celebrating 25 years in West Virginia, known for its two-week sessions. o Focuses on youth development through a structured camp experience with a beginning, middle, and end. • New Features: o Doubling Dream Week sessions (1-week introductory program) for younger campers. o Expansion of arts programming, including a new outdoor theater and woodshop renovations. • Programs: o Two-week sessions remain the core offering, with strong camper loyalty to session dates. o Four-week options available (two sessions back-to-back) for older campers. • Transportation: o Offers chaperoned flights from Florida and buses from DC. Considering expanding transport options from Pennsylvania. 5. Camp Hidden Meadows – Presented by Sam Bloom • Overview: o Located in West Virginia, focuses on 1-2 week programs for younger campers. o Provides an unstructured, nature-based experience with an emphasis on "choosing your own adventure." o Director Sam Bloom, with 30+ years of experience, highlighted his fresh perspective on the camp’s unique offerings. • Programs: o Activities include hiking, fly fishing, pottery, and rocketry. o Adventure offerings like zip-lining, Alpine towers, and overnight backpacking. o Farm-based programs with animals like alpacas, chickens, pigs, and bunnies. • Key Features: o Flexible scheduling for parents looking for short-term programs. o Cool summer temperatures in West Virginia, a major draw for families from warmer climates like Florida. • Future Plans: o Improving staff training for a more cohesive camper experience despite quick session turnovers. o Plans to collaborate with Twin Creeks on transportation options for families from Philadelphia and surrounding areas.

title: Takajo — Liz — 7-18-23
text:
# Takajo



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Takajo, Naples, Maine. Toured on 7/6/23 by Liz Hochman Mak, Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin. 7 weeks (4 weeks/first year only). Oldest campers finishing 9th grade. Boys only, under 400 boys, 45-50 per grade. Very sporty camp with sports being a huge focus. Camp broken down into 3 different sections by age with different fields/facilities by age. Sister camp is Tripp Lake. Jeff’s sister, Leslie is owner/director. Jeff mentioned that camp is filling up earlier and earlier each year and kids starting younger. Getting lots of kids going into 2nd grade now.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Met with Jeff Konigsberg (owner/director) and then toured with two staff members which were great and very knowledgeable. Spoke very highly of Jeff and dedication towards campers. Comes to each bunk often and talks to all the kids. Knows a lot about them. Jeff bought camp at age 28. Kids from all over CA, FL, Manhattan, Boston, CT, NJ, PA. In every bunk 1st-4th grade there is a female counselor that spends day, helps with certain things, and has a bed in bunk but doesn’t sleep in bunk.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Shower houses. No showers in bunks. Kids go as a bunk in robes to shower house together. Full towel service.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers travel by "hobby" or "skill group" throughout camp. Do not travel by bunk.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Big Brother program. Each camper has a big brother throughout summer and a camp family they eat with once per week. Helps with transitions. Tree house area in woods where they do camp outs. New Pickle ball courts.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining room-air conditioned. Don’t sit by bunk.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp very rooted in tradition and respecting values/traditions/history.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Very sporty camp with sports being a huge focus. Boys only, under 400 boys with 45-50 per grade; oldest campers finishing 9th grade. 7 weeks (4 weeks/first year only), with campers traveling by "hobby" or "skill group" and a Big Brother program that helps with transitions. Camp very rooted in tradition and respecting values/traditions/history in Naples, Maine, with sister camp Tripp Lake.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Jeff Konigsberg (owner/director) comes to each bunk often, talks to all the kids, and knows a lot about them.
  - Toured with two staff members which were great and very knowledgeable.
  - Big Brother program and a camp family they eat with once per week; helps with transitions.
  - Air-conditioned dining room.
  - Tree house area in woods where they do camp outs.
  - New Pickle ball courts.
  - Full towel service.
  - Camp broken down into 3 different sections by age with different fields/facilities by age.



#### Lowlights

  - Shower houses; no showers in bunks (kids go as a bunk in robes to shower house together).
  - Don’t sit by bunk in the dining room.
  - Camp is filling up earlier and earlier each year and kids starting younger.



#### Similar Camps

  - Cedar
  - Manitou
  - Brant Lake
  - Winadu
  - Winaukee
  - Greylock

title: Stagedoor Manor — Jennifer — 7-2-23
text:
# Stagedoor Manor



## 1 · Program Snapshot

They offer three 3 week sessions. Campers can stay for 2 but not 3 sessions. Age of Campers – 11-18 so rising 6th graders to graduated high school. Focus of the day is all on theater. Each camper is in only 1 show per session. They have 8 theaters on campus. Campers submit a video prior to attending camp; based on their video, the camp staff assigns them to a part in a show so decisions are made before camp. They are judged and placed in show based on their personality, vocal range and monologue.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jennifer, Beth, Amanda, Lisa and Shari toured Stagedoor Manor on 6/27/23 with Barbara, director. Cindy, the owner of the camp also toured with us for a little while as Barbara got called away. The camp has a ratio of 3:1 female to males.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Camp is an old Catskill hotel that has been converted to a camp. We did not get to see the rooms but they bunk 6 girls to a room and 4 boys to a room. Each room has its own bathroom. In regards to transgender, they are very open and accepting. If a camper has begun the process of transitioning physically with hormones/surgery- they will be placed in a room with the gender they identify as. Otherwise, are bunked based on anatomy.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is a pool on campus but Barb says it is rarely used.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule is 2.5 hours of rehearsal in am. 2.5 hours rehearsal pm plus three 1 hour classes. All campers must take classes in movement, acting and voice (or diction if not a singer).



## 6 · Program Highlights

8 theaters on campus. Focus of the day is all on theater. Campers submit a video prior to attending camp; based on their video, the camp staff assigns them to a part in a show so decisions are made before camp. They are judged and placed in show based on their personality, vocal range and monologue.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining hall is in the main building and it is open seating but they do take attendance. They serve buffet style and are nut free.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

In regards to transgender, they are very open and accepting. Focus of the day is all on theater.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Most campers get driven up to camp but will pick up from airport. There is also a canteen which they do need money for but money will be returned if not spent. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown. Additional extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Stagedoor Manor offers three 3 week sessions for ages 11-18 (rising 6th graders to graduated high school), with the focus of the day all on theater. Campers submit a video prior to attending; roles are assigned before camp based on personality, vocal range and monologue, and each camper is in only 1 show per session. Housing is in an old Catskill hotel converted to a camp, with rooms (6 girls to a room and 4 boys to a room), each with its own bathroom, and the camp is very open and accepting regarding transgender rooming. Dining is buffet style and nut free, there is a pool that is rarely used, most campers are driven up but airport pick-up is available, and there is a canteen that requires money but unused money is returned.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Focus of the day is all on theater.
  - They offer three 3 week sessions.
  - Campers can stay for 2 but not 3 sessions.
  - Age of Campers – 11-18 so rising 6 th graders to graduated high school.
  - They have 8 theaters on campus.
  - Schedule is 2.5 hours of rehearsal in am. 2.5 hours rehearsal pm plus three 1 hour classes.
  - All campers must take classes in movement, acting and voice ( or diction if not a singer).
  - Dining hall is in the main building and it is open seating but they do take attendance.
  - They serve buffet style and are nut free.
  - Each room has its own bathroom.
  - In regards to transgender, they are very open and accepting.
  - Most campers get driven up to camp but will pick up from airport.
  - Campers submit a video prior to attending camp.
  - Based on their video, the camp staff assigns them to a part in a show so decisions are made before camp.
  - They are judged and placed in show based on their personality, vocal range and monologue.
  - Each camper is in only 1 show per session.



#### Lowlights

  - There is a pool on campus but Barb says it is rarely used.
  - We did not get to see the rooms.
  - Campers can stay for 2 but not 3 sessions.
  - The camp has a ratio of 3:1 female to males.



#### Similar Camps

Based on their video, the camp staff assigns them to a part in a show so decisions are made before camp. They are judged and placed in show based on their personality, vocal range and monologue. title: Southwoods — Rachel — 11-8-23
text:
# Southwoods



## 1 · Program Snapshot

On November 8th, we did a Zoom with Camp Southwoods. Camp Southwoods is located in the Adirondack Mountains in NY. It has an exquisite lake and every kind of water sport. Recently the program has undergone a "facelift". The whole focus of the camp is to utilize the amazing outdoor setting they are in to the fullest. They want kids to feel connected to the outdoors. They offer a wide variety of all types of activities. There are several sessions to choose from, including a 2 and a 3-week option. Campers can stay for two sessions.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

The directors are Carl and Maddie Acosta. They divide campers by age rather than gender. There is a CIT program for rising juniors (age 16) where they can get lifeguard and CPR certified. Also, leadership training. It is open to everyone and not just previous campers. They are adding trained staff for activities such as canoeing and rock climbing.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The staff will assign beds.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Camp Southwoods has an exquisite lake and every kind of water sport.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Younger campers do more cabin programming, and older campers choose more electives. There are several sessions to choose from, including a 2 and a 3-week option. Campers can stay for two sessions. There are camp trips offered as well, and every camper must sign up for one. You can choose which one.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Recently the program has undergone a "facelift". This includes a circus program and animal option being added. The Circus area will be impressive and they have hired a circus director from Brazil to start. They have also added three alpacas as part of the nature program. Previously there were no animals, but Camp Hilltop closed and the parents asked for animals here. They are adding trained staff for activities such as canoeing and rock climbing.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There is no religious affiliation. The dining hall can seat up to 300 and they have an outdoor area as well.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The goal is to get to know each camper and family and form lasting relationships. More like a boutique feel. Not impersonal. That is what makes Camp Southwoods stand out from the rest, along with a beautiful setting and amazing activities. They also teach kids to create meaningful relationships, be kind, and try new things. They want kids to feel connected to the outdoors.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

You can fly into JFK and get a bus to camp, which takes about 4.5 hours. There are also buses from Philadelphia, Westchester, etc. The bus fee is $100 each way. There is a canteen but no extra fees. Parent communication and tech policies: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Southwoods is located in the Adirondack Mountains in NY with an exquisite lake and every kind of water sport. The whole focus of the camp is to utilize the amazing outdoor setting to the fullest, teach kids to create meaningful relationships, be kind, and try new things. They offer a wide variety of all types of activities, with younger campers doing more cabin programming and older campers choosing more electives; there are 2 and 3-week sessions and campers can stay for two sessions. The type of child who does well here is a child that is average and not expecting to focus and excel on a particular activity, and families will find a boutique feel that is not impersonal.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Exquisite lake and every kind of water sport.
  - Recently the program has undergone a "facelift" with a circus program and animal option being added.
  - Circus area will be impressive and they have hired a circus director from Brazil to start.
  - Three alpacas as part of the nature program.
  - Trained staff for activities such as canoeing and rock climbing.
  - Younger campers do more cabin programming, and older campers choose more electives.
  - Camp trips offered; every camper must sign up for one and you can choose which one.
  - CIT program for rising juniors (age 16) with lifeguard and CPR certified and leadership training.
  - Boutique feel; goal is to get to know each camper and family and form lasting relationships.
  - You can fly into JFK and get a bus to camp (about 4.5 hours); bus fee is $100 each way.



#### Lowlights

  - Long bus ride from JFK (about 4.5 hours).
  - Not expecting to focus and excel on a particular activity.
  - The staff will assign beds.
  - Every camper must sign up for one camp trip.
  - Parent communication and tech policies: Unknown.
  - Recently the program has undergone a "facelift".



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

On November 8 th , we did a zoom with Camp Southwoods. The directors are Carl and Maddie Acosta. Camp Southwoods is located in the Adirondack Mountains in NY. It has an exquisite lake and every kind of water sport. Recently the program has undergone a “facelift”. This includes a circus program and animal option being added. The Circus area will be impressive and they have hired a circus director from Brazil to start. They have also added three alpacas as part of the nature program. Previously there were no animals, but Camp Hilltop closed and the parents asked for animals here. The whole focus of the camp is to utilize the amazing outdoor setting they are in to the fullest. They are adding trained staff for activities such as canoeing and rock climbing. They want kids to feel connected to the outdoors. The type of child who does well here is a child that is average and not expecting to focus and excel on a particular activity. They offer a wide variety of all types of activities. Younger campers do more cabin programming, and older campers choose more electives. There are several sessions to choose from, including a 2 and a 3-week option. Campers can stay for two sessions. They divide campers by age rather than gender. There is a CIT program for rising juniors (age 16) where they can get lifeguard and CPR certified. Also, leadership training. It is open to everyone and not just previous campers. You can fly into JFK and get a bus to camp, which takes about 4.5 hours. There are also buses from Philadelphia, Westchester, etc. The bus fee is $100 each way. There is no religious affiliation. The dining hall can seat up to 300 and they have an outdoor area as well. There is a canteen but no extra fees. The staff will assign beds. There are camp trips offered as well, and every camper must sign up for one. You can choose which one. # Starlight



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Starlight Tour 7-12-22. Starlight submitted Questionnaire on 2-22-22. Extensive and varied program.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by Robbie Getholf and Haley Miller Getholf, also saw David Miller Beth, Lisa, Shari, Amanda.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown



## 6 · Program Highlights

All program areas are of the highest caliber. No weaknesses detected in any program area.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

New Culinary Center "Farm House" is outrageous\! Gorgeous, huge building with cathedral ceilings that looks like a Montana Lodge.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Impression: Addition of Robbie Getholf (from Timber Lake) is a tremendous boost to the energy and vibe at camp. The future of Starlight is in good hands.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Updates: 2 buses NYC (Museum of Natural History); 2 buses Westchester, NY; 2 buses Philadelphia (King of Prussia, PA); 3 buses Long Island (Great Neck, NY); Chaperoned flight from/to FL (40 campers).



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Starlight offers an extensive and varied program. All program areas are of the highest caliber, with no weaknesses detected. The new Culinary Center "Farm House" is outrageous, a gorgeous, huge building with cathedral ceilings that looks like a Montana Lodge. Travel options include buses from NYC, Westchester, Philadelphia, and Long Island, plus a chaperoned flight from/to FL (40 campers), and the addition of Robbie Getholf (from Timber Lake) is a tremendous boost to the energy and vibe at camp.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Extensive and varied program.
  - All program areas are of the highest caliber.
  - No weaknesses detected in any program area.
  - New Culinary Center "Farm House" is outrageous.
  - Addition of Robbie Getholf (from Timber Lake) is a tremendous boost to the energy and vibe at camp.
  - The future of Starlight is in good hands.
  - Travel updates include multiple buses and a chaperoned flight from/to FL (40 campers).



#### Lowlights

  - Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Starlight Tour 7-12-22 Toured by Robbie Getholf and Haley Miller Getholf, also saw David Miller Beth, Lisa, Shari, Amanda Starlight submitted Questionnaire on 2-22-22 Updates: 2 buses NYC (Museum of Natural History) 2 buses Westchester, NY 2 buses Philadelphia (King of Prussia, PA) 3 buses Long Island (Great Neck, NY) Chaperoned flight from/to FL (40 campers) New Culinary Center “Farm House” is outrageous\! Gorgeous, huge building with cathedral ceilings that looks like a Montana Lodge. Extensive and varied program All program areas are of the highest caliber. No weaknesses detected in any program area. Impression: Addition of Robbie Getholf (from Timber Lake) is a tremendous boost to the energy and vibe at camp. The future of Starlight is in good hands.
