title: TIMBERLANE WISCONSIN —8-1-22
text:
# TIMBERLANE WISCONSIN



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Approx. 150 boys each session (180 is completely full). This was a comfortable number for them this year. 40-60 boys typically stay full summer. Youngest cabin is 8 y/o, finished 2nd grade (12 campers in 2022). SUPER well rounded activity choices (approx. 32 choices). BIG tripping program. All campers are required to take OWLS (Outdoor wilderness living skills).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Approx. 150 boys each session (180 is completely full). 40-60 boys typically stay full summer. Youngest cabin is 8 y/o, finished 2nd grade (12 campers in 2022). 40% from Chicago. 50+ boys from St. Louis. Dallas/Houston. 3 counselors minimum (CITS are counselors).



## 3 · Living Quarters

15 cabins, max 14 campers (most are in 6-10 range). Bunk beds in cabins, counselors sleep in separate room. Toilets, sinks, and showers in all cabins. 3 counselors minimum (CITS are counselors).



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Water skiing, water sports.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

4 activity periods (all choice by week) - 2 am, 2 pm. 2 free periods daily. Activities are based on skill level (not age or cabin-based)—safety focused. SUPER well rounded activity choices (approx. 32 choices). Ex: Yoga, pickleball, big stage for performances, weight room, all sports, gymnastics, rocketry, riflery, archery, water skiing, water sports, mountain biking, floor hockey building, gaga, wood crafts, etc. 75 ft rock wall and bouldering cave.



## 6 · Program Highlights

BIG tripping program - all elective trips, boys can sign up for trips they are interested in once at camp. Shortest trip 3 days/2 nights. 10 trip leaders (wilderness first aid certified). 35-40 trips per summer. All campers are required to take OWLS (Outdoor wilderness living skills). Special Programs: UN Day/Titan Day. Blue vs White. Competitions with other camps. Casino night (favorite evening program). Friday Campfire (No Religion). Socials with local girls camps.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Can accommodate most dietary restrictions (gluten free, lactose free, vegetarian, tree nut free) - they do serve peanut products. Canteen almost every night (candy/snacks). 3 RNs on staff. Do not use Camp Meds.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This was a comfortable number for them this year. "Being smaller is better."



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bus campers from Minneapolis airport if they fly. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Approx. 150 boys each session (180 is completely full), and this was a comfortable number for them this year. Campers choose from SUPER well rounded activity choices (approx. 32 choices) with 4 activity periods (2 am, 2 pm) and 2 free periods daily. BIG tripping program with elective sign-ups; All campers are required to take OWLS (Outdoor wilderness living skills). Cabins have toilets, sinks, and showers, with bunk beds and counselors in a separate room; youngest cabin is 8 y/o, finished 2nd grade.



### Internal Insights

  - SUPER well rounded activity choices (approx. 32 choices) incl. Yoga, pickleball, big stage for performances, weight room, all sports, gymnastics, rocketry, riflery, archery, water skiing, water sports, mountain biking, floor hockey building, gaga, wood crafts, etc.

  - All campers are required to take OWLS (Outdoor wilderness living skills).
  - 15 cabins, max 14 campers (most are in 6-10 range) with toilets, sinks, and showers in all cabins; counselors sleep in a separate room.
  - Can accommodate most dietary restrictions (gluten free, lactose free, vegetarian, tree nut free).
  - 3 RNs on staff.
  - UN Day/Titan Day; Blue vs White; Competitions with other camps; Casino night (favorite evening program); Friday Campfire (No Religion); socials with local girls camps.
  - 40% from Chicago; 50+ boys from St. Louis; Dallas/Houston; bus campers from Minneapolis airport if they fly.



#### Lowlights

  - They do serve peanut products.
  - Do not use Camp Meds.
  - Parent communication: Unknown.

title: VEGA —  7-7-22
text:
# VEGA



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Girls only, uniform camp. Offer 6.5 weeks and very limited 4 week option (less than 5%) - 300 girls. Youngest campers are going into 2nd grade (have 8 campers).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

40 girls in an age group. 15-20% NYC, 40 campers from FL, 30 from LA, Chicago, Philly, DC, Arizona.



## 3 · Living Quarters

9-10 campers in a bunk with 3 counselors. Bunks stay the same each summer.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

11 & under have swim and land sports daily. One piece bathing suits. Have a crew boat.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 5 periods in a day, 2 with division, 3 choice. 11 & under have swim and land sports daily. Cooking for 10 & up, ski jumping for 13 & up, stained glass for older girls.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Have a crew boat, a station to restring tennis racquets, big indoor field house, digital media center, rock band. Intercamps with Laurel and Matoaka. Golden Loon Awards- for random acts of kindness. If you get 3 then get a bracelet. Extras: horses, ice skating, clinics & private tennis.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining- 2 seatings for breakfast and dinner but one for lunch, sit by age group (assigned). 1 DR, 6 Nurses. 15 girls didn’t come back this summer due to vaccination.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall impression- camp was beautiful, manicured, clean and very good instruction everywhere. Girls were busy and happy. Brother camps are Takajo, Cedar, Andro, Manitou & Caribou.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from Westchester and Boston. Video calls- four 15 minute calls. Extras: horses, ice skating, clinics & private tennis.

Girls only, uniform camp. Offer 6.5 weeks and very limited 4 week option (less than 5%) - 300 girls, with youngest campers going into 2nd grade (have 8 campers). Programming: 5 periods in a day, 2 with division, 3 choice; 11 & under have swim and land sports daily, with options like cooking for 10 & up, ski jumping for 13 & up, and stained glass for older girls. title: Somerset — Karen — 8-10-22
text:
# Somerset



## 1 · Program Snapshot

CAMP SOMERSET (with Karen Meister, Aline Rosenfeld, Monica Hirsch and Pilar Vidal) Toured by Kate Auerbach, it was such a pleasure to see the camp for the first time. They are running about 180 first session, 265 second session. About half of the girls stay full session. Somerset closed in 1986 and they bought it in 2016. They opened in 2018 and just about everything was new. Camp looks great and certainly a work in progress and she took us around and said... and this is where I hope to have a building for.... She mentioned a theater building with real seats (a la Manitou??) and an indoor gymnasium. They want to empower girls through being active.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

The staff is 50% international. The campers are 1/3 New England, 1/3 tri-state area, and 1/3 from elsewhere including international. Kate played sports. She went to Kenwood Evergreen and dreams of the same experience she had at camp with sports, all the different activities, great spirit etc. Kate and Josh are terrific and we wish them the best success as they continue to tweak the offerings of Camp Somerset.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Ninth graders are in a special 'sorority type house' together. It is one building with a fridge and Keurig machine and nice area for hanging out. Towel and linen service is provided to all. They also provide a Somerset blanket. Most cabins are 10 kids with 4 counselors. Wood bunk beds with cubbies and drawers.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They have a pool. The lake has water toys and all the motor and non-motor boats. Skiing 3 times during the stay per session. End the day with all water sports for the whole camp.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

This is NOT an elective camp. The girls are assigned to all the activities that camp has to offer by division. There are about 4 bunks per division. So a girl may only visit softball three times during their 3 week session. Once entering 7th grade, they get a little more choice. There are 3 morning periods and 2 afternoon periods and end the day with all water sports for the whole camp. You can earn patches simply by trying something. 4 days per week are typical through the schedule. Then Sunday and Thursday, they have lazy mornings and some flexibility to pick what they want to do. Tuesday is trip day. Every girl will go on a tent/hiking trip.



## 6 · Program Highlights

They have all the land sports, frisbee golf, spin, yoga, martial arts, gymnastics, tae kwon do or self defense. Drama, music, dance, 4 plays/summer, archery. Everything is there to do. Horses are offered but 3 miles away and no one really has jumped on it. Non-athletic activities include a garden, culinary, rocketry, digital photography, arts, sewing, ceramics, jewelry making, and a whole stem area. Each week they have a brother sister gathering with Manitou. Socials for 7th and up ie roller blading or community service together. A different flag is raised daily for country, pride, breast cancer awareness etc.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

5 nurses on staff and share the doc with Manitou. Dining hall is woody and airy, and you sit by cabin. All allergies can be managed. Josh used to own restaurants so it is important to him. They are not loud in the dining hall but spirited at times.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The girl who goes to Somerset wants to play. And she likes to participate in competition. She will be encouraged to try hard to win, but will also be taught to lose gracefully. Overall impression – a nice and distinct alternative to Matoaka. Felt a different girl vibe there, but it is not yet as developed as Matoaka.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No technology allowed and parents can email, they will print out and deliver to the girls. Girls must write back. Uniform is blue shorts with any of a number of Somerset shirts. One piece bathing suits required. Travel logistics: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp looks great and is a work in progress, with just about everything new since they opened in 2018. This is NOT an elective camp; girls are assigned to activities by division, with a little more choice starting in 7th grade and lazy-morning flexibility on Sundays and Thursdays. They have all the land and water sports (they end the day with all water sports for the whole camp), plus drama, music, arts, STEM, and trips. Overall impression – a nice and distinct alternative to Matoaka, though it is not yet as developed as Matoaka.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Camp looks great and certainly a work in progress.
  - They opened in 2018 and just about everything was new.
  - They want to empower girls through being active.
  - The staff is 50% international.
  - The campers are 1/3 New England, 1/3 tri-state area, and 1/3 from elsewhere including international.
  - End the day with all water sports for the whole camp.
  - Each week they have a brother sister gathering with Manitou.
  - 5 nurses on staff and share the doc with Manitou.
  - All allergies can be managed. Josh used to own restaurants so it is important to him.
  - Towel and linen service is provided to all. They also provide a Somerset blanket.
  - Ninth graders are in a special 'sorority type house' together.
  - You can earn patches simply by trying something.
  - Tuesday is trip day. Every girl will go on a tent/hiking trip.
  - They are not loud in the dining hall but spirited at times.
  - Kate and Josh are terrific.



#### Lowlights

  - We were a bit uncomfortable with the forced try everything schedule because it doesn't allow for enough time to really get good at anything.
  - Felt a different girl vibe there, but it is not yet as developed as Matoaka.
  - Horses are offered but 3 miles away and no one really has jumped on it.



#### Similar Camps

  - Matoaka
  - Manitou



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

CAMP SOMERSET (with Karen Meister, Aline Rosenfeld, Monica Hirsch and Pilar Vidal) Toured by Kate Auerbach, it was such a pleasure to see the camp for the first time. They are running about 180 first session, 265 second session. About half of the girls stay full session. Kate played sports. She went to Kenwood Evergreen and dreams of the same experience she had at camp with sports, all the different activities, great spirit etc. Somerset closed in 1986 and they bought it in 2016. The opened in 2018 and just about everything was new. Camp looks great and certainly a work in progress and she took us around and said… and this is where I hope to have a building for…. She mentioned a theater building with real seats (a la Manitou??) and an indoor gymnasium. They have a pool, all the land sports, frisbee golf, spin, yoga, martial arts, gymnastics, tae kwon do or self defense. Drama, music, dance, 4 plays/summer, archery.Everything is there to do. They want to empower girls through being active. Horses are offered but 3 miles away and no one really has jumped on it. The staff is 50% international. The campers are 1/3 New England, 1/3 tri-state area, and 1/3 from elsewhere including international. This is NOT an elective camp. The girls are assigned to all the activities that camp has to offer by division. There are about 4 bunks per division. So a girl may only visit softball three times during their 3 week session. Once entering 7 th grade, they get a little more choice. There are 3 morning periods and 2 afternoon periods and end the day with all water sports for the whole camp. You can earn patches simply by trying something. 4 days per week are typical through the schedule. Then Sunday and Thursday, they have lazy mornings and some flexibility to pick what they want to do. The girl who goes to Somerset wants to play. And she likes to participate in competition. She will be encouraged to try hard to win, but will also be taught to lose gracefully. Ninth graders are in a special “sorority type house” together. It is one building with a fridge and Keurig machine and nice area for hanging out. Towel and linen service IS provided to all. They also provide a Somerset blanket. Uniform is blue shorts with any of a number of Somerset shirts. One piece bathing suits required. No technology allowed and parents can email, they will print out and deliver to the girls. Girls must write back. 5 nurses on staff and share the doc with Manitou. Non-athletic activities include a garden, culinary, rocketry, digital photography, arts, sewing, ceramics, jewelry making, and a whole stem area. Most cabins are 10 kids with 4 counselors. Wood bunk beds with cubbies and drawers. Tuesday is trip day. Every girl will go on a tent/hiking trip. Dining hall is woody and airy, and you sit by cabin. All allergies can be managed. Josh used to own restaurants so it is important to him. They are not loud in the dining hall but spirited at times. A different flag is raised daily for country, pride, breast cancer awareness etc. The lake has water toys and all the motor and non-motor boats. Skiing 3 times during the stay per session. Each week they have a brother sister gathering with Manitou. Socials for 7 th and up ie roller blading or community service together. Overall impression – a nice and distinct alternative to Matoaka. Felt a different girl vibe there, but it is not yet as developed as Matoaka. We were a bit uncomfortable with the forced try everything schedule because it doesn’t allow for enough time to really get good at anything. But with the two special days, there are opportunities to focus a bit more. Kate and Josh are terrific and we wish them the best success as they continue to tweak the offerings of Camp Somerset.

title: TRAVEL FOR TEENS — AMANDA — 1-10-24
text:
# TRAVEL FOR TEENS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

TFT has been around since 2003 and began with a trip to Paris. Offer classic teen tours, language immersion programs (Spain, Costa Rica & France), community service programs (animal service—elephants in Thailand, animal sanctuaries in Costa Rica, Kangaroos and Wallabies in Australia, Hawaii), and working with people and children (healthcare in Costa Rica & Indonesia) as well as photography programs (Iceland, France & Italy). Photo program is taught by a professional photographer. Programs are separated by age (middle school, 9th & 10th graders, older teens, college age). They don’t offer combo trips anymore. Have one college trip—Greece College Trip, no CS on this trip, not a lot of structure, offer suggestions for where to visit, stay and eat. Graduation seniors can participate on Older Teens trips. Community service trips are for 9th–12th graders. Students can register as undecided on a trip until 2/1. Trips tend to fill up by the end of January.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jayden Korber (412) 443-4644 and Maggie Valz (404) 883-0514 presented. Jayden is a staff developer and has been with TFT since 2017. Maggie is the sales manager and has been with TFT past 8 years. Day to day operations are run by Ned and Tori. They have an 8 to 1 student to staff ratio—typically 3 staff on each program. Full time dedicated staff and recruitment team. European staff training. Coaches for teen who need extra support (extra $$$).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations & Safety: stay at centrally located hotels—in the cities, never stay on the outskirts. Will provide the names of the hotels once registered for a trip. Get rooms in a block. More rustic accommodations in Belize and Fiji. Stay at hostels in Switzerland & Scandinavia. Can have up to 6 students in a room.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Have one college trip—Greece College Trip, no CS on this trip, not a lot of structure, offer suggestions for where to visit, stay and eat. Phones are allowed but encourage students not to be on them during sight-seeing, meals, etc. Will talk to parents if there’s an issue, set up screen time. Purchase tickets in advance for museums and tourist attractions (always a line for the Eiffel Tower, even with advanced tickets).



## 6 · Program Highlights

What Makes TFT Different - small and diverse programs (24 students max on a trip, smaller trips have 12-18 students) - 75% of travelers come solo - extensive experience in Europe and the most comprehensive European programs and staff - widest variety of programs available - nightly photos and email updates from trip directors - programs are separated by age (middle school, 9th & 10th graders, older teens, college age) - coaches for teen who need extra support (extra $$$) - 24 hr. emergency line and coverage across all time zones - personal director program visits - TFT offers Authentic Travel—programs are designed as if you were visiting a friend who lives in that country - TFT has been using the same tour guides and contacts for years - they don’t offer combo trips anymore - have one college trip—Greece College Trip, no CS on this trip, not a lot of structure, offer suggestions for where to visit, stay and eat. What’s New? - Brand new system—all information is in one place - Full time dedicated staff and recruitment team - South Africa & Tanzania—17 day service & safari trips - European staff training - Monthly webinars—have new and past families - Suggest looking at TikTok for student made perspective videos on various trips.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

24 hr. emergency line and coverage across all time zones. Accommodations & Safety: stay at centrally located hotels—in the cities, never stay on the outskirts; get rooms in a block; will provide the names of the hotels once registered for a trip. Purchase tickets in advance for museums and tourist attractions (always a line for the Eiffel Tower, even with advanced tickets). Olympics in Paris this summer so will be an upcharge for hotels on 7/26–8/11 trips in the area; will not go to the Olympics.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

TFT offers Authentic Travel—programs are designed as if you were visiting a friend who lives in that country. 75% of travelers come solo. Extensive experience in Europe and the most comprehensive European programs and staff. TFT has been using the same tour guides and contacts for years. Personal director program visits. Monthly webinars—have new and past families. Suggest looking at TikTok for student made perspective videos on various trips.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Students can register as undecided on a trip until 2/1. Trips tend to fill up by the end of January. Nightly photos and email updates from trip directors. Phones are allowed but encourage students not to be on them during sight-seeing, meals, etc.; will talk to parents if there’s an issue, set up screen time. Brand new system—all information is in one place. Monthly webinars—have new and past families. Offer Spring school trips. Can do private trips for groups of friends/families. No GAP year programs, at the moment. No family trips. Coaches for teen who need extra support (extra $$$). Will provide the names of the hotels once registered for a trip. Olympics in Paris this summer so will be an upcharge for hotels on 7/26–8/11 trips in the area; will not go to the Olympics.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Travel For Teens (TFT) has been around since 2003 and began with a trip to Paris. They offer classic teen tours, language immersion programs (Spain, Costa Rica & France), community service programs (animal service—elephants in Thailand; animal sanctuaries in Costa Rica; Kangaroos and Wallabies in Australia; Hawaii) and working with people and children (healthcare in Costa Rica & Indonesia), as well as photography programs (Iceland, France & Italy). Small and diverse programs (24 students max on a trip, smaller trips have 12–18 students), an 8 to 1 student to staff ratio (typically 3 staff on each program), and programs separated by age support 75% of travelers who come solo. Families get nightly photos and email updates from trip directors, a 24 hr. emergency line and coverage across all time zones, and centrally located hotels with programs designed for Authentic Travel (as if you were visiting a friend who lives in that country).



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Small and diverse programs (24 students max on a trip, smaller trips have 12-18 students)
  - 75% of travelers come solo
  - 8 to 1 student to staff ratio—typically 3 staff on each program
  - Photo program is taught by a professional photographer
  - Extensive experience in Europe and the most comprehensive European programs and staff
  - Nightly photos and email updates from trip directors
  - Programs are separated by age (middle school, 9th & 10th graders, older teens, college age)
  - Coaches for teen who need extra support (extra $$$)
  - 24 hr. emergency line and coverage across all time zones
  - Personal director program visits
  - Brand new system—all information is in one place
  - South Africa & Tanzania—17 day service & safari trips
  - European staff training
  - Monthly webinars—have new and past families
  - Suggest looking at TikTok for student made perspective videos on various trips
  - TFT offers Authentic Travel—programs are designed as if you were visiting a friend who lives in that country
  - TFT has been using the same tour guides and contacts for years



#### Lowlights

  - They don’t offer combo trips anymore
  - No GAP year programs, at the moment
  - No family trips
  - More rustic accommodations in Belize and Fiji
  - Stay at hostels in Switzerland & Scandinavia
  - Can have up to 6 students in a room
  - Olympics in Paris this summer so will be an upcharge for hotels on 7/26–8/11 trips in the area; will not go to the Olympics
  - Will provide the names of the hotels once registered for a trip
  - Not a lot of structure (Greece College Trip)



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

Jayden Korber (412) 443-4644 and Maggie Valz (404) 883-0514 presented. Jayden is a staff developer and has been with TFT since 2017. Maggie is the sales manager and has been with TFT past 8 years. Trips tend to fill up by the end of January. They have an 8 to 1 student to staff ratio- typically 3 staff on each program. Offer classic teen tours, language immersion programs (Spain, Costa Rica & France), community service programs (animal service- elephants in Thailand, animal sanctuaries in Costa Rica, Kangaroos and Wallabies in Australia, Hawaii), & working with ppl and children (healthcare in Costa Rica & Indonesia) as well as photography programs (Iceland, France & Italy). Photo program is taught by a professional photographer. What Makes TFT Different - small and diverse programs (24 students max on a trip, smaller trips have 12-18 students) - 75% of travelers come solo - extensive experience in Europe and the most comprehensive European programs and staff - widest variety of programs available - nightly photos and email updates from trip directors - programs are separated by age (middle school, 9 th & 10 th graders, older teens, college age) - coaches for teen who need extra support (extra $$$) - 24 hr. emergency line and coverage across all time zones - personal director program visits - TFT offers Authentic Travel- programs are designed as if you were visiting a friend who lives in that country - TFT has been using the same tour guides and contacts for years - they don?t offer combo trips anymore - have one college trip- Greece College Trip, no CS on this trip, not a lot of structure, offer suggestions for where to visit, stay and eat What?s New? - Brand new system- all information is in one place - Full time dedicated staff and recruitment team - South Africa & Tanzania- 17 day service & safari trips - European staff training - Monthly webinars- have new and past families - Suggest looking at TikTok for student made perspective videos on various trips - phones are allowed but encourage students not to on them during sight-seeing, meals, etc. Will talk to parents if there?s an issue, set up screen time - No GAP year programs, at the moment - No family trips - offer Spring school trips - can do private trips for groups of friends/families - graduation seniors can participate on Older Teens trips - community service trips are for 9 th -12 th graders Accommodations & Safety - stay at centrally located hotels- in the cities, never stay on the outskirts - will provide the names of the hotels once registered for a trip - get rooms in a block - more rustic accommodations in Belize and Fiji - stay at hostels in Switzerland & Scandinavia - can have up to 6 students in a room - purchase tickets in advance for museums and tourist attractions (always a line for the Eiffel Tower, even with advanced tickets) - Olympics in Paris this summer so will be an upcharge for hotels on 7/26-8/11 trips in the area, will not go to the Olympics

title: TIMBER TOPS — LINDSEY — 7-5-23
text:
# TIMBER TOPS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

At Timber Tops there are 330 girls, with 240 being full-season campers.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

The heads of some activities are male, such as ropes and tennis.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunk assignments are organized by session.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Younger campers have socials once a week, those entering the 9th grade have them twice weekly, and by the time they reach 10th and 11th grades, campers have socials almost every night. Campers up to rising 6th graders follow a structured program, allowing them to develop their skills and interests.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Timber Tops engages in intercamp sports with Pine Forest and Lake Owego. The bigger trips are done with Lake Owego. Timber Tops actively participates in Wayne County camp intercamp games.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The dining hall is air conditioned and half of the camp eats during each sitting, but the oldest campers have the privilege of staying for both sittings. On Friday nights dinner features challah and soup.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Following this meal, a friendship campfire takes place, commencing with the Motzi blessing, candles, and "wine". Socials are primarily organized with Owego.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

The camp facilitates intercamp mail, enabling campers to stay connected with friends/family at other camps within the PFC group.

title: VISIONS SERVICE ADVENTURES — ELIZA — 11-8-22
text:
# VISIONS SERVICE ADVENTURES



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Visions is in its 35th year of service adventures. They are a leading in their field and serve as risk management benchmarking group. Visions is known for its long-term relationships—between the families of students, amongst the communities they are located in, and with the volunteers. Big focus on cultural immersion. Programs were 100% full last year with waitlists. Programs in the Dominican Republic, British Virgin Islands (BVI), Montana, and Peru. All programs include service work that goes hand in hand with the culture, and the students are really immersed with the locals.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ages that participate most commonly are 16–17. The type of student who does Visions is one who is unique, marches to the beat of their own drum, but really is a place for all kids (athletes, musicians, boys, girls). Most students attend the program not knowing anyone. Great for all walks of life. One parent commented that it was an opportunity for their child to see the world truly from a different perspective and that was life changing for their child. All students must be entering 9th grade to do the program, but it skews older than freshman. Leader teams are composed of returned Peace Corps volunteers, teachers, graduate students, wilderness instructors, carpenters and professionals from the host community. Minimum age is 21, but most are in mid-20s, early 30s.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Very much a no frills living situation. VISIONS is not a homestay program. Instead, we live together as a group and spend our days engaging with neighbors and local friends. This format allows for greater flexibility in scheduling the service projects and program itineraries. Some programs include a “day stay” (done in pairs) with host families.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

In BVI, they do a lot of carpentry, and also teach swimming lessons. Other waterfront and aquatics details: Unknown.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

9-day program is 30–35 hours of service; 80–90 hours for longer program. Four days a week, participants get to choose their project site, working until early or mid-afternoon when we then have time for recreation and cultural activities. One day a week, every participant joins the “homebase crew,” cooking breakfast, cleaning, doing laundry and shopping at local markets for the rest of the day’s meals. Weekends are for excursions and travel to popular tourist sites as well as off the beaten path destinations. Three to four nights a week, we have group meetings that last about an hour; this slow-down time allows everyone to get to know each other while reflecting on our volunteer and cultural experience. Issues that arise naturally in a group of people who live and work closely together are acknowledged and addressed. Group meetings help bind everyone together as we work toward common goals.



## 6 · Program Highlights

There is a mix of service, exploration and language immersion (when possible). Lots of community building, with an inclusive group meeting each week that is a very important piece of the program. In Montana on Blackfoot Reservation they attend sweat lodges, among other things. In BVI, they do a lot of carpentry, and also teach swimming lessons. In Peru they focus on water conservation. Kids get life-changing experience where they learn to work as a team, they cook and clean (one day a week), they fully unplug (no longer doing calls home), and they really learn gratitude and empathy.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Rotating homebase crews prepare breakfasts and lunches that are plentiful and varied. Scrambled eggs, pancakes, French toast, yogurt, fruit and cereal are among the regular options for breakfast. Lunches feature sandwiches, hearty salads, fruit, and leftovers. VISIONS employs local cooks who prepare dinners that blend Western fare and local cuisine. Transgender—case by case basis, always have a neutral area, but hope to be able to know in advance. If nothing major changes with COVID, will not be requiring vaccines.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Big focus on cultural immersion and long-term relationships with communities, families, and volunteers. Lots of community building with inclusive group meetings that help bind everyone together as we work toward common goals. VISIONS is not religiously or politically affiliated. Some program locations may offer an opportunity to explore the religion and religious monuments of our host communities, but all programs are secular. VISIONS is not a teen tour or “voluntourism” program. We live in our host communities for the duration of the program and use the weekends for excursions. Participants become part of the fabric of daily life, building a “home away from home.” Dress code is important because it reflects the values of the community.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: They fully unplug (no longer doing calls home). Tech policy beyond unplugging: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Visions is in its 35th year of service adventures with a big focus on cultural immersion and long-term relationships. Programs were 100% full last year with waitlists. Kids get a life-changing experience where they learn to work as a team, fully unplug, and really learn gratitude and empathy. The type of student who does Visions is one who is unique, marches to the beat of their own drum, but it really is a place for all kids.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Leading in their field and a risk management benchmarking group.
  - Known for long-term relationships with communities, families, and volunteers.
  - Programs include deep cultural immersion and community building.
  - Programs were 100% full last year with waitlists.
  - Leader teams include returned Peace Corps volunteers, teachers, graduate students, wilderness instructors, carpenters, and local professionals (minimum age 21; most in mid-20s to early 30s).
  - Four days a week participants choose project sites; weekends for excursions; one day on homebase crew.
  - Three to four nights a week group meetings for reflection and cohesion.
  - Service hours: 30–35 for 9-day; 80–90 for longer programs.
  - Local cooks prepare dinners blending Western fare and local cuisine.
  - Montana Blackfoot Reservation sweat lodges; BVI carpentry and swim instruction; Peru water conservation.
  - Not religiously or politically affiliated; not a teen tour or “voluntourism.”



#### Lowlights

  - Very much a no frills living situation.
  - Dress code is important because it reflects the values of the community.
  - Transgender—case by case basis, always have a neutral area, but hope to be able to know in advance.
  - If nothing major changes with COVID, will not be requiring vaccines.
  - Not a homestay program (group living instead), with only some “day stay” experiences.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They are a leading in their field and serve as risk management bench marking group. - Visions is known for it’s long term relationships—between the families of students, amongst the communities they are located in and with the volunteers. - Big focus on cultural immersion - Programs were 100% full last year with waitlists WHO ATTENDS - Ages that participate most commonly are 16 -17. title: Scatico — Allison — 7-13-23
text:
# Scatico



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Traditional co-ed camp with a 1 week trial for 3rd grade or younger, 4 weeks or full-session 7 weeks. Scatico has been around for 102 years and really takes pride in their history. Founded as an all boys camp then moved to the current location and became co-ed later. Improvements to camp include a large expansion to the Health Center and a BBQ deck. In general camp is simple, clean but could use a little sprucing up. Not a camp full of bells and whistles.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by Nicki Fleishner, Asst. Director (one of David & Diana's daughters; the other is Audrey who is the Athletic Director). Nicki is the 4th generation to run Scatico. Nicki has a 5-month-old, Henry. 40% of campers are children of alumni. 80% of bunk counselors are also alumni. 25% from Westchester, 20% NYC, rest from general tri-state area and 10% CA. Currently have 137 girls and 130 boys.



## 3 · Living Quarters

They do not take bunk requests. No bunk beds. Beds are wood with cubbies for shoes under each bed. Lots of shelves and cubbies for clothes. Bunks have 4 toilets, 3 showers and 3 sinks. Kids bring their own towels. Head Counselor's bunk on girl's side has a covered deck with schedules posted.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake is pretty; boys and girls have separate waterfronts so girls can wear bikinis if they want. They added a wibbet last year but no motor sports on it and they don't take kids out. Have a fishing pier that was also new last year. NO Pool.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Camp is structured for younger kids but they have more choice as they get older. They blow the bugle and make announcements throughout the day. Girls get a camp sister that is different every summer. Kids get canteen after lunch once a week. They do inter-camps with Pontiac and there are optional tournaments.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Some activities include: golf, cooking, dance, yoga, gymnastics (in big barn where doors open to let air in), arts and crafts (just got 4 new sewing machines), OA (have flying squirrel), woodshop (kids can use the machines). There is an indoor basketball court, 4 tennis courts, street hockey, archery, music shack (those kids put on a talent show). Another covered pavilion for sports and countless other fields (some of which are lit). Hiking trip is big as well as trip to Lake Placid for older kids. Oldest kids go to Dominican Republic, stay at eco-hotel for a week where they run a day camp. Nicki said a Scatico kid should like team sports. Boys and Girls are pretty separate. They even do color wars separately. Canteen has air hockey, ping-pong, foosball.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health Center has AC, 4 nurses and 1 doctor on site at all times. North Dutchess Hospital is 18 minutes away. Dining Hall is basically two buildings with kitchen in the middle. Boys are on one side and girls on the other. There is a shared deck where the kids can mingle after meals. Kids eat family style and sit by division. Girls were singing and chanting through the meals and seemed happy and spirited. Kids get AM and PM snack. There is no food in bunks.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Uniform where kids have to wear something either green or white unless it's 'teams day' where they might wear blue, red or yellow as well. Boys and Girls are pretty separate. They even do color wars separately. Girls were singing and chanting through the meals and seemed happy and spirited. This camp is not for a kid (or parent) who wants glitzy or bells and whistles but if you're looking for a camp run by people who know how to run a camp full of 'nice', somewhat sporty kids in a traditional atmosphere then this is the place for them.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Added bus service this year for free but some parents still like to come up and drop off kids. Camp unpacks them all regardless of age. Technology is not allowed. Everything is included except the two big trips. Parent communication: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Scatico is a traditional co-ed camp that has been around for 102 years and really takes pride in their history. It offers a 1-week trial for 3rd grade or younger, 4 weeks, or a full-session 7 weeks, with a simple, clean setting that is not full of bells and whistles. Boys and girls are pretty separate (even color war), the program is structured for younger kids with more choice as they get older, and a Scatico kid should like team sports. Everything is included except the two big trips, and the camp is run by people who know how to run a camp full of 'nice', somewhat sporty kids in a traditional atmosphere.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Traditional co-ed camp with 102 years of history; Nicki is the 4th generation to run Scatico.
  - 40% of campers are children of alumni; 80% of bunk counselors are also alumni.
  - Health Center has AC, 4 nurses and 1 doctor on site at all times; North Dutchess Hospital is 18 minutes away.
  - Pretty lake with separate waterfronts; added a wibbet and a new fishing pier last year.
  - Robust activity menu (golf, cooking, dance, yoga, gymnastics, arts and crafts with 4 new sewing machines, OA with flying squirrel, woodshop, indoor basketball, 4 tennis courts, street hockey, archery, music shack/talent show).
  - Hiking trip is big; older kids go to Lake Placid; oldest go to the Dominican Republic to run a day camp.
  - Uniform culture and family-style meals; girls seemed happy and spirited, singing and chanting through meals.
  - Added free bus service; camp unpacks all campers regardless of age.



#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - No motor sports on the lake and they don't take kids out.
  - Boys and Girls are pretty separate (even color wars separately).
  - They do not take bunk requests.
  - Technology is not allowed.
  - In general camp is simple, clean but could use a little sprucing up; not a camp full of bells and whistles.



#### Similar Camps

  - Schodack
  - Bacco
  - Echo Lake
  - Watitoh

Nicky \> has a 5 month old, Henry Traditional Co-ed Camp. 1 week trial for 3rd grade or younger, 4 weeks or full-session 7 weeks Scatico has been around for 102 years and really takes pride in their history. They added a wibbet last year but no motor sports on it and they don't take kids out. Have a fishing pier that was also new last year. NO Pool. \> Health Center has AC, 4 nurses and 1 doctor on site at all times. North Dutchess Hospital is 18 minutes away. \> Boys and Girls are pretty separate. They even do color wars separately. \> They do not take bunk requests. No bunk beds. Beds are wood with cubbies for shoes under each bed. Lots of shelves and cubbies for clothes. Bunks have 4 toilets, 3 showers and 3 sinks. Kids bring their own towels. Technology is not allowed. \> Head Counselor's bunk on girl's side has a covered deck with schedules posted. They blow the bugle and make announcements throughout the day. Girls get a camp sister that is different every summer. \> Some activities include: golf, cooking, dance, yoga, gymnastics (in big barn where doors open to let air in), arts and crafts (just got 4 new sewing machines), OA (have flying squirrel, woodshop (kids can use the machines). There is an indoor basketball court, 4 tennis courts, street hockey, archery, music shack (those kids put on a talent show). Another covered pavilion for sports and countless other fields (some of which are lit). \> Kids get canteen after lunch once a week. There is no food in bunks but get AM and PM snack. Canteen has air hockey, ping-pong, foosball. They do inter-camps with Pontiac and there are optional tournaments. Nicki said a Scatico kid should like team sports. \> Hiking trip is big as well as trip to Lake Placid for older kids. Oldest kids go to Dominican Republic, stay at eco-hotel for a week where they run a day camp. \> Everything is included except the two big trips. Compare themselves to Schodack, Bacco, Echo Lake and Watitoh. This camp is not for a kid (or parent) who wants glitzy or bells and whistles but if you're looking for a camp run by people who know how to run a camp full of "nice", somewhat sporty kids in a traditional atmosphere then this is the place for them. \>




### Author: Allison

### Updated: 2025-08-27

title: WA-KLO — LISA — 8-31-22
text:
# WA-KLO



## 1 · Program Snapshot

ALL GIRLS. They have the capacity for 144. Please look at Profile for other info.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

They had 135 girls (ALL GIRLS), with 32 staying for full summer. They have 35 counselors, they want 40. Most of their staff are international. Director: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The cabins were nice, knotty pine, one of each sink, toilet and shower. Metal bunk beds, campers can sleep on top. They live out of their hard trunks. The old bunks have graffiti but they have put up plaques so there is less room. Shower house was clean with real doors to the showers and curtains in individual dressing area, with extra sinks.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Beautiful Lake: instructional swim 5/week until level 4 of Red Cross is reached. 3 motorboats, they kayak to the island, have all the boating activities as well. They offer CREW for ages 12 and up.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming is 4 CHOICE plus one required instructional swim. Daily timetable structure beyond this: Unknown.



## 6 · Program Highlights

A\&C: they added stained glass, need to be 12 yrs old and older. They have ceramics. Dance studio is upstairs, mirrors and bars, wood floor with AC. Nice. Sports: Huge indoor field house for volleyball and basketball and rainy-day facility. Fitness is body weight and free weights. They have a clay tennis court with weeds grown, they do not use. Gymnastics is an outdoor pavilion with beam and vault. Theater: they perform one musical and one play per session. They also offer fencing and mountain biking. Horseback riding is strong, they have 12 horses, one ring, with trail rides and jumping. They try to get the same horses year to year. Petcare is a large area with a donkey, mini pony, goats, sheep, 2 calves, and rabbits. This is a popular activity along with archery and waterskiing.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: sit by bunk or division, will be assigned by the session. New decks to sit outside for meals. Fruit bowl and chips always out. Am/pm snacks and left-over sandwiches. Nut free, allergy aware. Health and safety details beyond allergies: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Pine needles, pinecones and sand makes for a very shady camp. Uniformed. NO Religion. Sunday campfire. Nice and laid-back, down to earth girls.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Most parents drop their girls off at camp. Travel logistics beyond drop-off: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Older new girls would do well here. Nice and laid-back, down to earth girls. ALL GIRLS with a Beautiful Lake, instructional swim 5/week until level 4 of Red Cross is reached, and strong horseback riding. Programming is 4 CHOICE plus one required instructional swim.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Beautiful Lake: instructional swim 5/week until level 4 of Red Cross is reached.
  - 3 motorboats, they kayak to the island, have all the boating activities as well.
  - They offer CREW for ages 12 and up.
  - Horseback riding is strong, they have 12 horses, one ring, with trail rides and jumping.
  - Huge indoor field house for volleyball and basketball and rainy-day facility.
  - Dance studio is upstairs, mirrors and bars, wood floor with AC.
  - Petcare is a large area with a donkey, mini pony, goats, sheep, 2 calves, and rabbits.
  - Nut free, allergy aware.
  - New decks to sit outside for meals.
  - Most of their staff are international.



#### Lowlights

  - They have a clay tennis court with weeds grown, they do not use.
  - They have 35 counselors, they want 40.
  - The old bunks have graffiti but they have put up plaques so there is less room.



#### Similar Camps

  - Walden ME

They also offer fencing and mountain biking. Horseback riding is strong, they have 12 horses, one ring, with trail rides and jumping. They try to get the same horses year to year. Petcare is a large area with a donkey, mini pony, goats, sheep, 2 calves, and rabbits. This is a popular activity along with archery and waterskiing. title: Susquehannock — Jennifer — 7-10-23
text:
# Susquehannock



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Susquehannock is a nonprofit sports camp in Poconos; hill camp is for the boys, girls-lake camp. They currently have 70 boys and 60 girls at camp, I believe they have the capacity for 100 on each side. They offer 2, 4 or 6 week session. We were there during the 2 week session; 4 week session does not start until 2 week session ends so there is no overlap. They do give out a lot of scholarships and currently have 32 campers on full scholarships. Emphasis is on sports. Campers are evaluated on their athletic skills at the beginning of camp and then they are placed on a team with campers of similar skills. All campers are expected to play all the different sports with their team which is 10 different team sports. Campers also do swimming and tennis. All sports are single sex. Campers do not have to be great athletes but do have to like to play. They do not play any intercamps.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jennifer and Beth toured with director, Jeff Bell on 6/30/23. We really like the director, Jeff Bell and his philosophy. Camper counts: 70 boys and 60 girls; believed capacity 100 on each side. Staff details: Unknown. Camper ages/grades: Unknown. Staff training/tenure: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: Very rustic, old bunks with both single and bunk beds. There is electricity in the bunks and campers bring their own fans. Campers live out of their trunks. No facilities in the bunks. We did not see the bathrooms or shower house but was told the toilets are non flush – basically like outhouse.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Swimming: No pool. Lake is lovely and at the bottom of the hill. Campers receive swim lessons in the lake until age 14 or pass swim test. Non motorized water sports only. They do have an aquaglide, slide into the lake, canoes, kayaks, paddleboards and sailboats.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers do get 1 elective and it is usually educational based. Campers also do swimming and tennis. Every afternoon, there is an orange vs blue competition followed by free swim in the lake.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Art is done in the art barn and is very simple. Campers were working on making cutting boards and ceramic pots to take home to their families. They do have 1 pottery wheel and a kiln. The cabins will put on skits at night but there is no real theater program. Trips: there have been no trips since COVID. Campers do an overnight campout on the grounds of camp.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: Old and rustic, campers do eat family style with their bunks. They are nut free. They do have a canteen which they go to every other day. They do not allow food from home. There is no religion at camp but campers do say grace at meals. Medical/health services: Unknown. Safety policies beyond waterfront: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Emphasis is on sports. All sports are single sex. Campers do not have to be great athletes but do have to like to play. I did send a few campers here last summer (all on scholarships) and they all loved it and returned this summer. Overall impression: We really like the director, Jeff Bell and his philosophy. The hard sell for this camp is the bathrooms so needs to be for a sports loving camper who does not mind roughing it.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Communication: There are no scheduled calls home and there is no visiting day. Campers can receive one way email and they do post pictures and use facial recognition. Travel logistics: Unknown. Tech policy (devices): Unknown. Extra costs: Canteen every other day; specifics Unknown. Packing/gear: Campers live out of their trunks; they bring their own fans.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Susquehannock is a nonprofit sports camp in the Poconos; Hill Camp is for the boys and Girls-Lake Camp for the girls, with 2, 4 or 6 week session and no overlap between sessions. They do give out a lot of scholarships and currently have 32 campers on full scholarships. Emphasis is on sports: campers are evaluated on their athletic skills and placed on a team with campers of similar skills, and all campers are expected to play 10 different team sports. Bunks are very rustic with no facilities and non flush toilets (basically like an outhouse), the lake is lovely with non motorized water sports plus an orange vs blue competition followed by free swim, and this needs to be for a sports loving camper who does not mind roughing it; we really like the director, Jeff Bell and his philosophy.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - We really like the director, Jeff Bell and his philosophy.
  - Nonprofit sports camp with strong emphasis on sports and team play.
  - Generous scholarships; currently 32 campers on full scholarships.
  - Orange vs blue competition every afternoon followed by free swim.
  - Lakefront with aquaglide, slide, canoes, kayaks, paddleboards and sailboats.
  - Nut free and family style dining.



#### Lowlights

  - Very rustic bunks with no facilities; toilets are non flush – basically like an outhouse.
  - No pool.
  - Art is very simple; no real theater program.
  - There have been no trips since COVID.
  - No scheduled calls home and no visiting day.
  - The hard sell for this camp is the bathrooms.



#### Similar Camps

  - YMCA camps



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

SUSQUEHANNOCK Jennifer and Beth toured with director, Jeff Bell on 6/30/23 Susquehannock is a nonprofit sports camp in Poconos; hill camp is for the boys, girls-lake camp They currently have 70 boys and 60 girls at camp, I believe they have the capacity for 100 on each side. They offer 2, 4 or 6 week session. We were there during the 2 week session; 4 week session does not start until 2 week session ends so there is no overlap. They do give out a lot of scholarships and currently have 32 campers on full scholarships. Emphasis is on sports. Campers are evaluated on their athletic skills at the beginning of camp and then they are placed on a team with campers of similar skills. All campers are expected to play all the different sports with their team which is 10 different team sports. Campers also do swimming and tennis. All sports are single sex . Campers do not have to be great athletes but do have to like to play. Campers do get 1 elective and it is usually educational based. They do not play any intercamps. Every afternoon, there is an orange vs blue competition followed by free swim in the lake. Bunks: Very rustic, old bunks with both single and bunk beds. There is electricity in the bunks and campers bring their own fans. Campers live out of their trunks. No facilities in the bunks. We did not see the bathrooms or shower house but was told the toilets are non flush – basically like outhouse. Dining Hall: Old and rustic, campers do eat family style with their bunks . They are nut free. They do have a canteen which they go to every other day. They do not allow food from home. There is no religion at camp but campers do say grace at meals. Communication: There are no scheduled calls home and there is no visiting day. Campers can receive one way email and they do post pictures and use facial recognition. Swimming: No pool. Lake is lovely and at the bottom of the hill. Campers receive swim lessons in the lake until age 14 or pass swim test. Non motorized water sports only. They do have an aquaglide, slide into the lake, canoes, kayaks, paddleboards and sailboats. Art: Art is done in the art barn and is very simple. Campers were working on making cutting boards and ceramic pots to take home to their families. They do have 1 pottery wheel and a kiln. The cabins will put on skits at night but there is no real theater program. Trips: there have been no trips since COVID. Campers do an overnight campout on the grounds of camp. Overall impression: We really like the director, Jeff Bell and his philosophy. I did send a few campers here last summer ( all on scholarships) and they all loved it and returned this summer. The hard sell for this camp is the bathrooms so needs to be for a sports loving camper who does not mind roughing it. I would give with some of the YMCA camps as they are all rustic and provide generous scholarships.

title: Round Lake — 8-11-22
text:
# Round Lake



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Round Lake is an inclusion program within both Nahjeewah and Cedar Lake’s camps. Campers at Round Lake go up to age 18. Then after 18, they have introduced an intern program where they had 6 campers with jobs on campus like photographer alongside the camp photographer.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We were toured by Aryn Barer for Round Lake and Cedar Lake. She is the director for Round Lake. Round Lake’s most typical campers may have ADHD, anxiety, mid to high autism or learning differences. The upper staff at Round Lake has training with backgrounds such as social worker, therapists, special ed teacher. Counselors can be in school for one of these things or have siblings with special needs. There are inclusion supervisors and coordinators.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks have about 8 kids and 3 counselors.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers spend their day alongside Nahjeewah and Cedar Lake campers in all areas with a few exceptions. They do have some areas just for the Round Lake kids like mountainbiking, chess, drama, and areas in arts and crafts that are modified. Some campers require counselors to travel with them and some have counselors check in campers to their activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Teen Camp - 10th and 11th graders. They do some bigger trips like a 5 week trip to Israel or a smaller trip to Costa Rica and Chicago. There are several Round Lake Campers in this program.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Staff calls parents 1-2x/week to check in. Campers can continue therapy via zoom with their therapists. Camp also provides ot/speech therapy/tutor to go along with the child’s IEP. Safety: Unknown. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Round Lake is an inclusion program within both Nahjeewah and Cedar Lake’s camps, with campers spending their day alongside those campers in all areas with a few exceptions. They do have some areas just for the Round Lake kids.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Staff calls parents 1-2x/week to check in. Campers can continue therapy via zoom with their therapists. Travel logistics: Unknown. Tech policy beyond therapy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Round Lake is an inclusion program within both Nahjeewah and Cedar Lake’s camps. Round Lake’s most typical campers may have ADHD, anxiety, mid to high autism or learning differences. Campers at Round Lake go up to age 18, then after 18, they have introduced an intern program where they had 6 campers with jobs on campus like photographer alongside the camp photographer. Campers spend their day alongside Nahjeewah and Cedar Lake campers in all areas with a few exceptions, and staff calls parents 1-2x/week to check in.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Inclusion program within both Nahjeewah and Cedar Lake’s camps.
  - Typical campers may have ADHD, anxiety, mid to high autism or learning differences.
  - Bunks have about 8 kids and 3 counselors.
  - The upper staff has training with backgrounds such as social worker, therapists, special ed teacher.
  - There are inclusion supervisors and coordinators.
  - Camp also provides ot/speech therapy/tutor to go along with the child’s IEP.
  - Staff calls parents 1-2x/week to check in.
  - Campers can continue therapy via zoom with their therapists.
  - Teen Camp (10th and 11th graders) with bigger trips like a 5 week trip to Israel or a smaller trip to Costa Rica and Chicago.
  - Intern program after 18 with jobs on campus like photographer alongside the camp photographer.



#### Lowlights

None noted.

title: TRIPP LAKE — LIZ — 7-18-23
text:
# TRIPP LAKE



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Started in 1911 by two NYC teachers. 7 weeks/Girls only/uniform camp. 270 girls. Oldest girls entering 11th grade. ONE TIME 4 week option for younger campers… Tripp Lake.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured camp on 7/6/23 myself (Liz Hochman Mak), Michele Gershwin and Emily Rothenberg. We were given a great tour by Katie (Associate Director). Met Leslie (Director) briefly, her brother is Jeff, the director/owner at Takajo. 215 staff. Mostly female staff. Male staff are primarily the coaches. 30-40 girls per age group. “Advisor” assigned for each age group and assistant rotates with the in bunk counselors.



## 3 · Living Quarters

6 bunks per age. Smaller bunks with 7-8 girls per bunk and 2 counselors. All bunks are in a semi circle. Older girls bunks are very small.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Beautiful camp with lake at center point of camp and can see from many of the camp locations. Very strong swim program. Swim mandated at camp.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Structured programming when younger. 5th grade and up have electives and structured/bunk activities. Always rotating groups within camp so girls are always with different girls. Every Tuesday are trip days to local attractions, park, service oriented etc.



## 6 · Program Highlights

1913 theatre which has a ton of tradition and character. Various plays during summer depending on age group. Amazing art building with various rooms. Re built in 2018. Air conditioned. Arts and crafts/jewelry making, pottery, nature, digital, culinary and photography. 18 tennis courts at camp. Very strong tennis program. 2 week tennis academy offered and camp brings in a tennis pro. Great sports fields and sports program. Great climbing course, ropes course through woods. Color War goes on throughout the summer. Nice gymnastics area. Gymnastics shows at camp. Brother/Sister days with Takajo. Socials with Cedar, Skyelmar, Wildwood. Horseback riding offered (additional fee).



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Unknown



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This camp is very rooted in tradition. In their lodge, they have a mini “museum” with their history and various pics and items. They also have a list of current campers with their family members that have attended camp. Brother camp is Takajo-Jeff Koingsberg (Leslie’s brother is owner/director).



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Families from NY, CT, FL, CA, PA, Chicago etc. NYC/Westchester buses. Horseback riding offered (additional fee). Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Other extra costs: Unknown. Travel logistics beyond NYC/Westchester buses: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Girls only, uniform camp rooted in tradition with a beautiful lake at the center of camp. Structured programming when younger, with 5th grade and up having electives and structured/bunk activities, plus always rotating groups so girls are always with different girls. Very strong swim and tennis, an amazing art building (rebuilt in 2018, air conditioned), theater from 1913, climbing/ropes, Color War, gymnastics shows, trip days, and Brother/Sister days with Takajo, plus socials with Cedar, Skyelmar, Wildwood. ONE TIME 4 week option for younger campers within a 7-week season.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 7 weeks/Girls only/uniform camp.
  - 270 girls.
  - 215 staff. Mostly female staff. Male staff are primarily the coaches.
  - Very strong swim program. Swim mandated at camp.
  - 18 tennis courts at camp. Very strong tennis program. 2 week tennis academy offered and camp brings in a tennis pro.
  - Amazing art building with various rooms. Re built in 2018. Air conditioned.
  - 1913 theatre which has a ton of tradition and character. Various plays during summer depending on age group.
  - Great sports fields and sports program.
  - Great climbing course, ropes course through woods.
  - Color War goes on throughout the summer.
  - Nice gymnastics area. Gymnastics shows at camp.
  - Every Tuesday are trip days to local attractions, park, service oriented etc.
  - Brother/Sister days with Takajo. Socials with Cedar, Skyelmar, Wildwood.
  - “Advisor” assigned for each age group and assistant rotates with the in bunk counselors.
  - 6 bunks per age. Smaller bunks with 7-8 girls per bunk and 2 counselors. All bunks are in a semi circle.
  - This camp is very rooted in tradition.
  - In their lodge, they have a mini “museum” with their history and various pics and items. They also have a list of current campers with their family members that have attended camp.



#### Lowlights

  - Older girls bunks are very small.
  - ONE TIME 4 week option for younger campers… Tripp Lake.



#### Similar Camps

  - Mataponi
  - Danbee
  - Vega
  - Raquette Lake
  - other all girls camps



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Toured camp on 7/6/23 myself (Liz Hochman Mak), Michele Gershwin and Emily Rothenberg. We were given a great tour by Katie (Associate Director), Met Leslie (Director) briefly, her brother is Jeff, the director/owner at Takajo. Started in 1911 by two NYC teachers. -7 weeks/Girls only/uniform camp. -270 girls -215 staff. Mostly female staff. Male staff are primarily the coaches. -oldest girls entering 11 th grade - Families from NY, CT,FL,CA,PA, Chicago etc -NYC/Westchester buses -6 bunks per age. Smaller bunks with 7-8 girls per bunk and 2 counselors. All bunks are in a semi circle. Older girls bunks are very small. 30-40 girls per age group. “Advisor” assigned for each age group and assistant rotates with the in bunk counselors. Always rotating groups within camp so girls are always with different girls. -very strong swim program. Swim mandated at camp. -1913 theatre which has a ton of tradition and character. Various plays during summer depending on age group. -Structured programming when younger. 5 th grade and up have electives and structured/bunk activities. -Amazing art building with various rooms. Re built in 2018. Air conditioned. Arts and crafts/jewelry making, pottery, nature, digital, culinary and photography. -Brother camp is Takajo-Jeff Koingsberg (Leslie’s brother is owner/director). Brother/Sister days with Takajo. -Socials with Cedar, Skyelmar, Wildwood - Beautiful camp with lake at center point of camp and can see from many of the camp locations. -This camp is very rooted in tradition. In their lodge, they have a mini “museum” with their history and various pics and items. They also have a list of current campers with their family members that have attended camp. -18 tennis courts at camp. Very strong tennis program. 2 week tennis academy offered and camp brings in a tennis pro. -great sports fields and sports program. -Horseback riding offered (additional fee) -Every Tuesday are trip days to local attractions, park, service oriented etc. - Great climbing course, ropes course through woods -Color War goes on throughout the summer. -Nice gymnastics area. Gymnastics shows at camp. -Can give with Mataponi, Danbee, Vega, Raquette Lake or other all girls camps. ONE TIME 4 week option for younger campers… Tripp Lake

title: Skylemar — Michele — 7-10-23
text:
# Skylemar



## 1 · Program Snapshot

This is an ALL BOYS camp, 250 boys currently, 30 cabins, 2:1 camper to staff ratio which is very unusual (lots of care and attention). Program is 7 weeks, or can do 21 days or 22 day sessions. Overall impression was VERY good. A very buttoned up, sporty, beautiful boys camp with a lot of heart and soul. 95% retention rate (not surprising).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

25% NY/Tri state kids, 5-10% Colorado, 10-15% international campers. He said all of their staff is domestic - no foreign counselors. Girl counselors called NBC’s (non-bunk counselors) were spraying them with sunscreen. Arlene came over and met us (very nice and seems very hands on w/communication) and she spends a lot of time working out the bunks each year. Shep, her husband goes in and says goodnight to every single boy according to Nick and that’s important to them. They will do zooms or home visits (Nick lives in MD but comes to NY and Arlene and Shep live in Maine full time now).



## 3 · Living Quarters

White bunks, very clean feel. Bunks: very nice, showers in the back. All of the bunks are lined up together but senior Hall is further away from the younger kids bunks so they have their own area and they aspire to get to that.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Water program is Red Cross, the waterfront is gorgeous - clear water\!\!\! He said it’s a VERY clean lake but he said they won’t force a kid to swim but kids generally like the water front - has some fun stuff like. Unknown.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

He said this is a “more structured” camp with 7 periods and only 1 elective. They often play sports at night and also allow 1 elective at night. Art is a popular elective even though the boys are very sports oriented, they love it (they can also choose things like broadcasting, tie dying media arts, etc). Program is 7 weeks, or can do 21 days or 22 day sessions.



## 6 · Program Highlights

They were playing Wildwood, Cedar and Takajo in a basketball tournament (you can give this with any of those camps). Mini golf course - very cute - on campus and a beautiful driving range and short golf course (a few holes). Special days like olympics (brothers may not be on same team) 6 teams. Their motto is “cool to care” CTC and they have black bracelets they wear with that saying on it. 3 brother/sister nights with local girls camps like Pine Cliff, Tripp, Mataponi, Walden and Vega. And sometimes they do one big night with lots of camps at same time and it’s very fun, he said.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

NO Nuts, No packages, seemed firm on that. Infirmary: 2 Doctors and 7 nurses (lots of little sports injuries but they can handle most of it or there is a hospital nearby). No vax policy and that even worked during covid so they’re sticking with it.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Beautiful entrance, very clean feel and very sporty vibe. Their motto is “cool to care” CTC. Arlene came over and met us (very nice and seems very hands on w/communication). Overall impression was VERY good.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Arlene writes a daily blog, very hands on w/communication. They will post photos 1x a week, they’re not into posting tons of pics. They will do zooms or home visits (Nick lives in MD but comes to NY and Arlene and Shep live in Maine full time now). Travel logistics: Unknown. Tech beyond photos: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown. Michele Gershwin The Camp Experts & Teen Summers In partnership with Joanne Paltrowitz 917-929-9906 Direct Michele@campexperts.com IG: campexpert\_michele.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Skylemar is an ALL BOYS camp with a very clean feel and very sporty vibe, 250 boys currently, 30 cabins, and an unusually strong 2:1 camper to staff ratio. It’s a more structured program with 7 periods and only 1 elective (plus an optional evening elective), with popular choices like art, broadcasting, tie dying and media arts, alongside constant sports and a gorgeous, very clean waterfront. Health and safety are buttoned up (NO Nuts, No packages; Infirmary with 2 Doctors and 7 nurses; Red Cross swim), and parent communication runs through Arlene’s daily blog with photos posted 1x a week. Overall impression was VERY good—a buttoned up, sporty, beautiful boys camp with a lot of heart and soul, and a 95% retention rate.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 2:1 camper to staff ratio (lots of care and attention).
  - Gorgeous, VERY clean lake; Red Cross water program.
  - Mini golf course - very cute - plus a driving range and short golf course.
  - Art is a popular elective even though the boys are very sports oriented.
  - Daily blog from Arlene; very hands on with communication.
  - Special days like olympics (6 teams) and frequent intercamp tournaments.
  - 95% retention rate.
  - NO Nuts policy; strong health center (2 Doctors and 7 nurses).
  - “Cool to care” (CTC) culture and black bracelets.



#### Lowlights

  - They will post photos 1x a week (not into posting tons of pics).
  - More structured camp with 7 periods and only 1 elective.
  - No packages.
  - No vax policy (they’re sticking with it).
  - All staff is domestic - no foreign counselors.



#### Similar Camps

  - Wildwood
  - Cedar
  - Takajo
  - Pine Cliff
  - Tripp
  - Mataponi
  - Walden
  - Vega



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Here is our write up for Camp Skylemar: We had a great tour with Nick (Shep’s son) on a very hot day. Beautiful entrance, white bunks, very clean feel and very sporty vibe. Arlene writes a daily blog, she came over and met us (very nice and seems very hands on w/communication) and she spends a lot of time working out the bunks each year. A very buttoned up, sporty, beautiful boys camp with a lot of heart and soul. 95% retention rate (not surprising) Michele Gershwin The Camp Experts & Teen Summers In partnership with Joanne Paltrowitz 917-929-9906 Direct Michele@campexperts.com IG: campexpert\_michele
