title: Romaca — Emily — 7-13-23
text:
# Romaca



## 1 · Program Snapshot

7 weeks only all girl’s camp. 5 Week Option 1x for 2024.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Girls are mostly from Westchester, NYC, NJ and Long Island. Brothers attend Winadu, MNK and Greylock. The oldest kids are entering 11th grade and serving as trainee summer floaters. Next year, a new staff lounge will be introduced.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunking: Look like log cabins. Very cute with curtains on the windows. 6-8 kids and 2 counselors, and they switch bunks every year. They offer towel service. Bed assignments are made, and campers can make up to three bunk requests.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

No Pool. Motorized Lake across the street from camp. Girls walk over a bridge to get there.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 3 AM, 2 PM activities. Younger campers have 2 elective options. For campers entering 7th grade and up, they choose their activities, including a spin studio.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Gorgeous ART building by the lake. Horses are available. There is one overnight trip per year. Each division puts on a show, with one show per week, which is mandatory. Olympics is 3 days. 4 countries are represented.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining: Girls all eat at the same time. They sit with their bunk at round tables. They cannot get up until the whole bunk is done eating. Can handle all allergies.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

No uniform. Big Sister: Every girl at camp is paired with a camp sister for the entire summer, starting from mid-May. They support each other, find one another at camp, and even tuck each other in at night. Brother/Sister at Greylock every Wednesday. Social events begin in 6th grade.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Emily and Lara toured with Carly Tucker. Tours are available to families every Tuesday and Saturday. Jeff and Carly organize family tours, and children usually accompany their parents unless it's a large tour, in which case they go separately. Visiting day is always on a Sunday. The camp only posts pictures on Instagram, managed by an alumni counselor responsible for social media and special events. There are 4 designated FaceTime calls. Horses are available at an extra cost.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Romaca is a 7 weeks only all girl’s camp, with a 5 Week Option 1x for 2024. Programming features 3 AM and 2 PM activities, with more choice for campers entering 7th grade and up (including a spin studio). There is no pool; aquatics happen on a motorized lake across the street, reached by walking over a bridge. Community highlights include the Big Sister program, a gorgeous ART building by the lake, 3-day Olympics with 4 countries, weekly divisional shows, and one overnight trip per year.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 7 weeks only all girl’s camp; 5 Week Option 1x for 2024.
  - Girls are mostly from Westchester, NYC, NJ and Long Island.
  - Programming: 3 AM, 2 PM activities; 7th grade+ choose, including spin studio.
  - Gorgeous ART building by the lake.
  - Big Sister program (pairing begins mid-May; tuck-ins at night).
  - Olympics is 3 days with 4 countries.
  - Each division puts on a show weekly (mandatory).
  - One overnight trip per year.
  - Bunks look like log cabins; 6-8 campers + 2 counselors; towel service.
  - Campers switch bunks every year; up to three bunk requests; bed assignments are made.
  - Dining together at round tables; cannot get up until the whole bunk is done; can handle all allergies.
  - Tours on Tuesday and Saturday; visiting day Sunday.
  - Photos only on Instagram; 4 designated FaceTime calls.
  - Horses are available.
  - Brother/Sister at Greylock every Wednesday; social events begin in 6th grade.
  - Next year, a new staff lounge will be introduced.



#### Lowlights

  - No Pool.
  - Motorized lake across the street; girls walk over a bridge to get there.
  - The camp only posts pictures on Instagram.
  - Horses are available at an extra cost.
  - Each division show is mandatory.
  - They cannot get up until the whole bunk is done eating.



#### Similar Camps

  - Taconic
  - Pinecliff
  - Robindel
  - Wayne
  - Danbee



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Romaca: Emily and Lara toured with Carly Tucker Tours are available to families every Tuesday and Saturday. Jeff and Carly organize family tours, and children usually accompany their parents unless it's a large tour, in which case they go separately. Visiting day is always on a Sunday. 7 weeks only all girl’s camp. 5 Week Option 1x for 2024. Brothers attend Winadu, MNK and Greylock. Brother/Sister at Greylock every Wednesday. Social events begin in 6th grade. Girls are mostly from Westchester, NYC, NJ and Long Island. No uniform. No Pool. Motorized Lake across the street from camp. Girls walk over a bridge to get there. Gorgeous ART building by the lake. The camp only posts pictures on Instagram, managed by an alumni counselor responsible for social media and special events. There are 4 designated FaceTime calls. Programming: 3 AM, 2 PM activities. Younger campers have 2 elective options . For campers entering 7th grade and up, they choose their activities, including a spin studio. Bunking: Look like log cabins. Very cute with curtains on the windows. 6-8 kids and 2 counselors, and they switch bunks every year. They offer towel service. The oldest kids entering 11th grade and serving as trainee summer floaters. Bed assignments are made, and campers can make up to three bunk requests. Big Sister : Every girl at camp is paired with a camp sister for the entire summer, starting from mid-May. They support each other, find one another at camp, and even tuck each other in at night. Olympics is 3 days. 4 countries are represented. Dining: Girls all eat at the same time. They sit with their bunk at round tables. They cannot get up until the whole bunk is done eating. Can handle all allergies. Horses are available at an extra cost. There is one overnight trip per year. Each division puts on a show, with one show per week, which is mandatory. Next year, a new staff lounge will be introduced. Romaca can also look at: Taconic, Pinecliff, Robindel, Wayne, Danbee Bottom of Form

title: Shohola — Shari — 8-13-23
text:
# Shohola

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Shohola – Shari & Lisa B. toured by Duncan Barger 30 June 2023. BASIC DATA – session lengths 2, 3, 4, 7. 120 boys 1st session, 140 2nd session. Camp is full at 150. About 10% stay full summer, 3 & 4 wks most popular. Ages 7-16 (16 is CIT).

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Siblings at Netimus and see each other on Sundays. Demographics from greater DC metro area, greater NY/NJ/CT, Philly. 30 international boys. 77 staff overall, like to have 85. Average age of staff is 20. 30 of them were previous campers. About 10 scholarship kids from various locations. 10-15 females on camp, none are cabin counselors.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

ACCOMODATIONS – all cabins in a semi-circle on top of hill except 3 older age groups in separate area; 16 year olds live in tents. 8-10 campers per cabin, 2-4 staff, live out of trunks, facilities in bunk and shower house. Boys have duty to clean bunks and shower houses (w/ chemicals) but they’re also professionally cleaned. They mix cabins w/ session lengths. Older campers are welcome. OD is inside cabin to get them quiet and settled & is then excused for 1 hour then back in cabin. Rotating counselor in the grove monitors cabin activity/noise. A/c window units.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

AQUATICS – Instruction 1 hr every other day (A,B day scheduling), until level 5 passed. Small lake shared with Pine Forest and Lake Greeley for boating (canoe, kayak, SUP, sailing, windsurfing). Water-ski, wakeboard & tube (only after learn to ski) at Lake Wallenpaupack, not Saturdays. No pool\!

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING – 5 periods/day, all choice on arrival sign up for whole session and can change after 4 wks if staying. Summer long color war, 1 contest/week for the whole summer, upper camp and lower camp separate.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

SPORTS – All sports work on skills, drills and then scrimmages and leagues in the evenings. Soccer, basketball, lacrosse, flag football, baseball, softball, volleyball, rugby, tennis, street hockey. Golf – driving range on property; can also bring off camp to a course if interest among campers; they bring their own clubs. Rebuilding intercamp activities. Also have pickleball. Conditioning classes at open air pavilion. Hb riding on premises, 5 horses, English style, stable mgmt., trail & ring rides, extra cost, about 20 kids participate. CREATIVE – very basic painting, drawing, ceramics, outdoor cooking. Woodworking a little more extensive including silver shop in rec hall where they solder wires making jewelry, key chains, etc. Theatre and rock band (guitar, drums, piano) class are together for 1 overall performance per summer. Instruments brought from home. STEAM/HIGH TECH – digital & darkroom photography, radio, rocketry. ORIENTEERING/OUTDOORS – 5-6 high ropes, a few low ropes, in woods (very nice setting along the creek), not self-belayed and they will learn to belay; zip line; climbing wall; archery; riflery; mtn biking. Some outdoor living skills (mostly cooking, not campcraft). TRIPS – Seniors go to movie. Big trips program, all optional: mtn biking, canoeing, kayaking, rock climbing, hiking, ww rafting 1x/session, all age appropriate.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – 2 nurses on staff, dr on call. Wayne Memorial 20 mins away, urgent care 10 mins away. Boys are reminded to do tick checks in shower regularly, especially after hikes. DINING/ALLERGIES – no separate allergy supervisor, kitchen area or chef. Only counselors monitor. One seating, mixed ages throughout. Boys have dining hall duty 1x/week (set-up, clean-up, etc). Cannot handle some allergies (celiac), not peanut free (mostly trip food pb\&j & canteen products w/ nuts) but if a camper has an airborne allergy, they just won’t serve anything with that in it. Canteen 3x/wk w/$ from deposit on file; can get candy or ice cream; also open for sundries 5x/wk. Fruit available all the time.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

RELIGIOUS LIFE – not anything formal, generic optional grace before meals. Weekly thought Quaker meeting-like. They do very little by cabin, all of the boys know each other. Dog in lake/roams freely.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

TRAVEL – Airport pickup at EWR & JFK; buses from DC, NYC (The Met), Philly. 30 international boys. PARENT COMMUNICATION – no limit to \# of phone calls but they’re only during meal times after the 1st week and parents call in, so first come first served and may get busy signal sometimes. Email both ways; no packages but they do get some; no food; age appropriate contents. Letter writing required on Sundays. Post photos daily for the most part. VISITING – visiting day only for the boys who stay in overlapped sessions. EXTRA COSTS/OTHER – Canteen 3x/wk via deposit on file. Hb riding is extra cost.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Shohola offers 2, 3, 4, and 7 week sessions for boys ages 7–16 (16 is CIT), with 120–140 campers per session and a max of 150. The program is 5 periods/day and all choice, with sign-up on arrival for the whole session (can change after 4 weeks if staying) and a summer long color war with weekly contests. Waterfront is a small shared lake for boating and instruction 1 hr every other day until level 5 is passed, plus water-ski/wakeboard/tubing at Lake Wallenpaupack; no pool. When asked what type of boy would do best here Duncan said they can create a schedule for anyone who is sporty and for someone who can’t catch a ball; they can opt in for team sports vs. solo sports and water.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All-choice 5 periods/day with arrival sign-up and ability to change after 4 wks.
  - Robust sports, aquatics (instruction every other day, Lake Wallenpaupack skiing), ropes, and optional trips.
  - Mixed-age dining and a culture that does very little by cabin so all of the boys know each other.
  - Airport pickup (EWR & JFK) and buses from DC, NYC, Philly; 30 international boys.
  - Summer long color war with weekly contests; upper and lower camp separate.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Cannot handle some allergies (celiac); not peanut free.
  - No pool.
  - Staffing below target (77 staff; like to have 85).
  - Phone calls only during meal times after the 1st week; first come first served and may get a busy signal.
  - Intercamp activities are being rebuilt.
  - Visiting day only for boys in overlapped sessions.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

They can opt in for team sports vs. solo sports and water.

  
  

### Author: Shari

title: Saginaw — Mindy — 6-28-22
text:
# Saginaw

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Saginaw – June 28, 2023. Overall programming and bunks all still exactly the same as Jennifer described on 8/25/22 so I will not repeat that information. 2-week (1st–5th graders) / 4-week / 3-week / 7-week option. This year they are currently at 428 campers so their numbers are increasing. Camp is run like a brother/sister camp. They eat separate and have all separate activities except for evening activities and swimming is also co-ed. There were not any big capital improvements done in what seemed like awhile; newest parts of camp were the refillable water stations located all over, new AC in the dining hall, and 3 new pickleball courts.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by Jessica Petkov and Jay purposefully came to greet me at the gate house which I thought was very nice as well. Majority of campers are from the tri state area with a larger Maryland/DC group. They have a handful of alumni campers from California, Massachusetts, Tennessee. I really liked how they have so many staff around the campers at all times. Bunk counselors never leave their bunks and then every activity has specialized counselors at the station. The counselors however did not wear staff shirts or anything and I had a hard time differentiating who was staff and who was a camper. Camp is family run and has been for 2 generations.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunk counselors never leave their bunks. Unknown details on cabin layouts, bathrooms, AC in bunks, or bunk sizes.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake: They have two lakes, one for fishing, canoeing, kayaking, and the other for water tubing, wakeboarding, and jet skiing. All private but very small. Swimming is also co-ed. Girls are allowed to wear bikinis.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Evening activities are co-ed. Canteen: campers go to canteen every night except for Sundays where they have an ice cream party. I also noticed soda machines located around camp. Campers may get a $1 from the canteen and get a soda whenever they want. Theater: They put on one show a summer and it is at the end of the first 4 weeks. Every camper who wants to be in it has a part. Unknown specifics on daily schedule blocks or elective choice structure.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Since tradition is huge at Saginaw, so is Color War. All campers get very involved but it is only at the end of the summer. They offer College Days in the first session but it is not as "beloved" as color war. Sports: It is a very sporty camp without strong instruction. Most campers (especially senior boys) simply love to play sports all day long. They do have intercamp league games with Nock-A-Mixon, Green Lane, and Kweebec but it’s only once or twice a summer. Arts: They offer all of the traditional arts along with sewing and they have a kiln and pottery wheels but the art department did not appear very robust. The art center does have AC. Pickleball has become a big hit and already had pickle ball tournaments. Extras: private tennis, golf, gymnastics lessons. They do have a golf range on premises but a golf course is local and can be used by the campers for extra $. Special activities for campers grade 6 and higher: Honda dirt bikes, scuba, riflery, certain wood working projects.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: They are peanut aware. They do not cook with anything with peanuts nor does anything at the canteen have peanuts, but they do single peanut butter packets located in the back of the dining hall for those who want it. They serve everything buffet style. They can accommodate all allergies on a case by case basis and they have an allergy liaison who works in the kitchen to coordinate all allergies. They often have parents send in food to be prepared in a safe space for their children with food allergies. They have a few celiac campers and the parents send in most of their food. Every Wednesday is a cook out. Health Center: 4–5 nurses weekly with one doctor. They are all rotating parents. They usually have a close urgent care but it has closed down so right now the closest medical attention is the Christina Hospital. They are reopening something closer so they will use it when it becomes available. Transgender Policy: Jessica said she hasn’t had to deal with this yet but assumes they will sooner rather than later; she assumed she would bunk the child by the way they were born but again said it would be situational and she would work directly with the family.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Traditions: I saw all of the senior girls in pink and black and asked if it was a color war/college day type activity. Jessica said the senior girls always dress up in themes on Wednesdays but it is not camp organized; rather the girls discuss and plan these outfits in the off season. She said they do this often throughout the summer. Therefore this is not a camp I would recommend for a senior girl to start in. Friday Night Service: each age group leads a different Friday night with camp themes. They do light Shabbat candles and often have a traditional Friday night dinner but there is no other mention of religion. Uniform: they do not have any uniform and staff does not wear anything to differentiate themselves. Overall impression: I felt Saginaw was like a local day camp but with sleepovers. A well rounded, social kid will do fine there. It seems like a fun “hang out” camp that does not excel in any special area but seems to have a warm, positive vibe throughout camp.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

There was a small dirt path to get to camp with a gate house and I was stopped there to check in before Jessica came to get me on a golf cart. Chaperoned flight from Florida. Trips: They offer 2 whole camp trips to Hershey Park and a local baseball game around July 4th to watch the game and fireworks. They also have some divisional day trips but no overnight trips even for the senior campers. Canteen and extras/costs: campers go to canteen every night except Sundays; soda machines located around camp; campers may get a $1 from the canteen and get a soda whenever they want. They do have a golf range on premises but a golf course is local and can be used by the campers for extra $. Parent communication and tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

I felt Saginaw was like a local day camp but with sleepovers. A well rounded, social kid will do fine there. It seems like a fun "hang out" camp that does not excel in any special area but seems to have a warm, positive vibe throughout camp. Camp is family run and has been for 2 generations.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Warm, positive vibe throughout camp.
  - So many staff around the campers at all times.
  - Bunk counselors never leave their bunks and every activity has specialized counselors at the station.
  - Refillable water stations all over; new AC in the dining hall; 3 new pickleball courts.
  - Color War tradition; College Days offered first session.
  - Two lakes with boating and watersports (tubing, wakeboarding, jet skiing).
  - Extras: private tennis, golf, gymnastics; special activities for grade 6+ (Honda dirt bikes, scuba, riflery, wood working).
  - Canteen every night; ice cream party on Sundays.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Art department did not appear very robust.
  - Very sporty camp without strong instruction.
  - Counselors did not wear staff shirts; hard to differentiate staff from campers.
  - No overnight trips even for the senior campers.
  - Not a camp I would recommend for a senior girl to start in.
  - There were not any big capital improvements done in what seemed like awhile.
  - Closest urgent care closed; Christina Hospital is current option until something closer reopens.
  - All private lakes but very small.
  - Transgender policy unclear and situational.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Nock-A-Mixon
  - Green Lane
  - Kweebec

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Majority of campers are from the tri state area with a larger Maryland/DC group. They have a handful of alumni campers from California, Massachusetts, Tennessee. • Overall programming and bunks all still exactly the same as Jennifer described on 8/25/22 so I will not repeat that information. • This year they are currently at 428 campers so their numbers are increasing. • Camp is run like a brother/sister camp. title: VEGA — ALINE — 8-27-22
text:
# VEGA

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Girls camp. Manicured. 300 girls. Very few go partial summer. 1936 founder; 1975 bought his parents. Beauty after happiness.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

They start 6-7 years; most popular 8-9 years; 13-14 year still can come to the camp; 15 years old no. 1 general counselor and 3 activity counselors.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Nice paint on the cabins. 10 girls / cabin; 3 counselors up to 12 years old. 20 girls / cabin older; 4 counselors 14 years older. Linen provided no extra cost. Showers: carry caddy. Housekeeping staff cleans the bathrooms; inside bathrooms and restrooms just for older girls. NOT for the younger girls. Wood is being replaced.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront skiing. Nice temperature of the lake. Waterfront is beautiful. “Zip” lane: cable that goes till the waterfront.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Girls that want to do activities have a wide range of choices. Lights go off depending on the age of each cabin. Every week they camp out by cabin - each cabins get to go.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Music Studio. Gymnastics. Tennis. Inside volleyball and basketball / air conditioned. Air conditioned theatre. Cooking facility 10 and up. They get bracelets for achievements. Horses extra cost.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining by age group and they rotate each week. Nut free, Gluten free, Kosher style, Vegan. Say Grace. Non denominational.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Uniforms in the camp. Cheering and spirited. Individual experience. Population is homogeneous. They rather not have lots of Spanish friends so they can speak English like everyone. Say goodbye hard part after a summer experience in VEGA. Camp fire where they enhance the values of camp. Vega values - SUNSET - believe in yourself, Build unity, Practice gratitude, Live with integrity, Have empathy.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

International pickups. Escort flights. 4 FaceTime phone calls. They post photos with face recognition. Horses extra cost. Linen provided no extra cost.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Girls camp, manicured, with 300 girls. Very few go partial summer, and they start 6-7 years (most popular 8-9 years); 13-14 year still can come to the camp; 15 years old no. Girls that want to do activities have a wide range of choices across gymnastics, tennis, music studio, cooking (10 and up), and a beautiful waterfront with skiing and a “Zip” lane. Uniforms in the camp, cheering and spirited, with Vega values - SUNSET - believe in yourself, Build unity, Practice gratitude, Live with integrity, Have empathy.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Waterfront is beautiful; nice temperature of the lake.
  - Music Studio; Air conditioned theatre; inside volleyball and basketball / air conditioned.
  - Nut free, Gluten free, Kosher style, Vegan.
  - They post photos with face recognition.
  - International pickups; Escort flights.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Population is homogeneous.
  - They rather not have lots of Spanish friends so they can speak English like everyone.
  - Inside bathrooms and restrooms just for older girls. NOT for the younger girls.
  - Very few go partial summer.
  - Uniforms in the camp.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Girls camp Manicured Nice paint on the cabins 300 girls 10 girls / cabin 3 counselors up to 12 years old 20 girls / cabin older 4 counselors 14 years older Very few go partial summer Uniforms in the camp They Start 6-7 years most popular 8-9 years 13 -14 year still can come to the camp 15 years old no Music Studio Dinning by Age group and they rotate each week Nut free, Gluten free, Kosher style, Vegan Linen provided no extra cost Cheering and spirited Say Grace Non denominational Cooking facility 10 and up Gymnastics Waterfront skiing Tennis Horses extra cost 1936 founder 1975 bought his parents Beauty after happiness International pick up’s Escort flights Wood is being replaced Air conditioned theatre Individual experience Population is homogeneous They rather not have lots of Spanish friends so they can speak English like everyone 4 face time phone calls They post photos with face recognition 1 general counselor and 3 activity counselors. Say goodbye hard part after a summer experience in VEGA Girls that want to do activities have a wide range of choices. They get bracelet for achievements Nice temperature of the lake Waterfront is beautiful “Zip” lane: cable that goes till the waterfront. Lights go off depending on the age of each cabin Inside volleyball and basketballs / air conditioned Showers Carry / caddy Housekeeping staff cleans the bathrooms, inside bathrooms and restrooms just for older girls. NOT for the younger girls. Camp fire Where they enhance the values of camp. Vega values - SUNSET - believe in yourself, Build unity, Practice gratitude, Live with integrity, Have empathy. Every week they camp out by cabin- each cabins get to go.

title: WALDEN (NY) — LARA — 12-20-18
text:
# WALDEN (NY)

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Walden is a coed session camp located in the Adirondack Mountains in NY. Lauren bought the camp 15 years ago and has grown it in size to appx. 500 campers with 65% attending full summer. Camp is a continuing program broken into two 3 ½ week sessions with visiting day right in the middle as pick up day for first session and the following day drop off for second session campers. First and second session are priced equal and Lauren is proud to say they have been full second session for a number of years. Camp has a very rustic/outdoorsy feel. It is set on a mountain side with the lake being at the bottom along with the 3 story lodge being the focal point. Lauren says they have finally created "curb appeal" for the camp.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers are mostly from the tri-state area, Florida and Canada. There are 12-14 campers in the bunks. Lauren takes requests but says if a bunk is working they try to keep things the same from summer to summer. Religion: Campers are 85-90% Jewish and 60% of the staff are Jewish. They have mandatory Friday night Jewish services 25-30 minutes in length. 50-60% of their staff is returning each summer. All staff wears staff shirts daily. Lauren is so well spoken and passionate about being a camp owner/director it comes through within minutes of meeting her. She really just wants a kind camper who isn’t interested in being the best at everything but is interested in trying everything. She wants her campers to succeed and if she doesn’t feel like a child is the right fit she will be honest with the parent and let them know Walden is not for them.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Every bunk has been redone since Lauren took over and all bunk beds are brand new as well. There are showers in all the girls bunks. Half the boys bunks have showers while others have a shower house. It is random each summer who has the shower house. They were going to do away with it however, Lauren said the campers prefer the shower house as there is much less of a wait. The 3 story lodge is the focal point. Looking Ahead: They recently redid all the camps septic and built 2 building for staff housing like mini motel rooms. They have approval to build a 2 story building in 2020 that will be used as an activity center but can later be transformed into a double cabin if they choose.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

One morning period and one afternoon period are group water periods in the lake. Swim instruction is taught in both the pool and the lake. Their semi-private lake has an island in the middle that they host overnights on throughout the summer.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have all camp tribals for 1st session campers that is a 4 team event and traditional 2 team Color War at the end of second session. They compete against Echo Lake, Brant Lake, Baco/Chenowah, Southwoods. They do a spring gathering for new campers and a leadership retreat in Montreal.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health Center: brand new and air conditioned. One 24 hr doctor rotating weekly. 6 nurses and 2 assistant nurses. Two rooms for overnight campers w/Jack and Jill bathroom. Can handle all allergies. They have 2 people assigned for allergy foods. Peanut and treenut free in all the products they serve. Campers sit by group at both round and rectangular tables and eat buffet style. There are two seatings. Lower camp first and upper camp second with all camp line-up in between the seatings.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Lauren likes to "push kindness" and has zero tolerance for bullying on camp. It seems that Walden is a nurturing environment where kids can be themselves. There is no big/little sister program.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Lauren sends a daily email to parents with updates and they post pictures daily. They do not allow packages nor bunk gifts. Campers are required to wear Walden shirts for trips and intercamps. All staff wears staff shirts daily. Two phone calls a session. Parents can email in, no email out. No electronics allowed. Additional fees for camp baggage. They ask second session campers to come up by bus from either Paramus, NJ or Westchester NY. They will allow parents to drop off but they strongly advise taking the bus. Visiting day is right in the middle as pick up day for first session and the following day drop off for second session campers. There is no winter reunion. They do a spring gathering for new campers.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Walden is a coed session camp located in the Adirondack Mountains in NY with a very rustic/outdoorsy feel. Camp is a continuing program broken into two 3 ½ week sessions (appx. 500 campers, 65% attending full summer), and Lauren is proud to say they have been full second session for a number of years. Walden runs a 7 period day with 5 group and 2 choice, swim instruction in both the pool and the lake, and overnights on their semi-private lake island. Lauren likes to "push kindness" and has zero tolerance for bullying on camp; it seems that Walden is a nurturing environment where kids can be themselves.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Set on a mountain side with the lake at the bottom and the 3 story lodge as the focal point.
  - Brand new and air conditioned Health Center (one 24 hr doctor rotating weekly; 6 nurses and 2 assistant nurses).
  - Peanut and treenut free in all the products they serve; can handle all allergies with 2 people assigned for allergy foods.
  - 7 period day with 5 group and 2 choice; choice periods mixed age but never coed.
  - Swim instruction in both the pool and the lake; one morning and one afternoon group water period in the lake.
  - Semi-private lake with an island hosting overnights; all camp tribals (4 team) and Color War (2 team).
  - Daily email to parents with updates and daily photos.
  - 50-60% of their staff is returning each summer; all staff wears staff shirts daily.
  - They have finally created "curb appeal" for the camp.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Mandatory Friday night Jewish services 25-30 minutes in length.
  - No electronics allowed.
  - They do not allow packages nor bunk gifts.
  - Additional fees for camp baggage.
  - Lauren does not like to do weekly trips out of camp.
  - There is no winter reunion.
  - Second session campers asked to come up by bus from Paramus, NJ or Westchester NY (parent drop-off allowed but strongly advised to take the bus).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Echo Lake
  - Brant Lake
  - Baco/Chenowah
  - Southwoods

Lauren likes to “push kindness” and has zero tolerance for bullying on camp. Campers/Bunks: Campers are mostly from the tri-state area, Florida and Canada. There are 12-14 campers in the bunks. There is no big/little sister program. Looking Ahead: They recently redid all the camps septic and built 2 building for staff housing like mini motel rooms. They have approval to build a 2 story building in 2020 that will be used as an activity center but can later be transformed into a double cabin if they choose. Thoughts: Lauren is so well spoken and passionate about being a camp owner/director it comes through within minutes of meeting her. She really just wants a kind camper who isn’t interested in being the best at everything but is interested in trying everything. She wants her campers to succeed and if she doesn’t feel like a child is the right fit she will be honest with the parent and let them know Walden is not for them. It seems that Walden is a nurturing environment where kids can be themselves.

  
  

### Author: LARA

title: TYLER HILL — LINDSEY BINSTOCK — 7-13-22
text:
# TYLER HILL

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Sessions: 7 weeks. Campers: 500 / 2022 = 520. NO INTERNATIONAL. Not uniformed except when out of camp. 2020 Start Date: June 27. Tuition: $12,700 with incentive ( or $12,950 without) includes everything but camp trucking.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

2019 Emily, Emily and I (Lindsey B) were toured by Andy and Wendy. Staff: 30-40% former campers. 10% international. They wear staff shirts. Each division head (a grown up) has an ipad with them with their own email and Instagram.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Boys and girls are on separate sides of the camp. After 4 th grade they keep the bunks the same. They have female counselors who are with the little boys. The youngest bunk had 10 kids and 5 counselors. They are air conditioned, with very nice bathrooms (the showers have glass doors). Beds are wooden and individual except bunk beds with staff on top. Laundry is done individually. Staff unpack the kids and choose their beds. You may make 3 requests. Towel Service yes- bunk and pool/lake.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront: 2 lakes. Pool heated. Instructional swim in the morning up to 5 th grade. Bathing Suit Policy: one piece only.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 3 periods in the AM, 3 in the PM. One elective in the morning, one in the afternoon. The scheduled activities are major sports and swimming. The rest are electives. Trip Day: no designated day. There were kids playing sports during rest hour.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Color war 5 days and competitive. Change teams each year. Olympics for 1 day before visiting day. Range of activities: no horseback riding. Cooking, mountain biking, woodshop, candle making, gymnastics, a new dance studio, MMA, roller hockey and street hockey. There is a music recording studio, digital arts, 3 printing, robotics. 3 or 4 shows- big one this year was legally blonde. Added Ninja Warrior course. Glamping on campus.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: whole camp eats at once. Beautiful dining hall with lots of options. Family style with staff getting the food. Salad bar, deli bar, fruit. Nut free. Gluten free kitchen, vegetarian station. Canteen: every night but no dessert in the dining hall. There were kids in there during lunch for a special pizza lunch. Health Center: air conditioned with 9 nurses and a doctor. Must be vaccinated.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religion: Friday night Shabbat meal with the 3 blessings. Starfish program at dinner. After dinner they back to girls camp for brother sister time. They wear their Tyler hill shirts. Impression: highest caliber of everything – Poconos Style. Attracts families with high caliber expectations.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses: NYC (2 Museum of Natural History), NJ (4 Paramus), LI (5 College of Old Westbury), Westchester (go to Paramus, NJ). Flights from Florida (70 campers) and LA. Electronics: none. Email: one way. Phone Calls: 4. Packages none. Photos: yes with facial recognition. Visiting Day no tipping, no bunk gifts. Tours group and individual. Parents and kids tour separately. Will tour any day but mostly the weekends. 2 rookie days- one for new families and one for current siblings.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

TYLER HILL runs a 7-week session for 500 campers (2022 = 520) with NO INTERNATIONAL. Programming is 3 periods in the AM and 3 in the PM, with one elective in the morning and one in the afternoon; the scheduled activities are major sports and swimming and the rest are electives. Whole camp eats at once in a beautiful dining hall with lots of options (nut free, gluten free kitchen, vegetarian station), and the Health Center is air conditioned with 9 nurses and a doctor (must be vaccinated). Impression: highest caliber of everything – Poconos Style; attracts families with high caliber expectations.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Highest caliber of everything – Poconos Style.
  - Beautiful dining hall with lots of options; whole camp eats at once.
  - Nut free. Gluten free kitchen, vegetarian station.
  - Air conditioned bunks and Health Center; very nice bathrooms (the showers have glass doors).

#### Lowlights

title: WATITOH — TAMI — 7-17-24
text:
# WATITOH

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Watitoh is a coed camp with 220 campers. They are at capacity this year. Campers can go for 4 weeks until 5th grade. Entering 3rd grade is the youngest campers and 11th graders are the oldest. They are filled up in October for the next summer. 92% is their return rate. There are some international kids and they are more flexible with their dates. Most campers are from New York, 20 from Florida, and there are chaperoned flights from Boston, LA, Denver and Texas.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp Experts: Tami Feldman, Alyssa Greenberger, Karen Meister, Monica Hirsch, Pilar Vidal, Carolina Lautenberg. Tour: Britton, Director - 11th summer. They have a specialist at every area of camp.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Each cabin has two toilets, showers and sinks. There are no bunk requests.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront is twice a day and they alternate with the boys and the girls. They do a swap of land and lake activities with the boys and girls so activities are by gender. They have instructional swim, kayaking, canoeing, sailing and waterskiing. There is no pool.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

There is a hobby period during which they choose an activity for a week. Every Saturday night they have an evening activity.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have cooking - wontons, smoothies, cake pops, sushi and learn knife skills. Nature and science are offered and every year they have different animals. There are 6 tennis courts, mini basketball, hockey and intermural sports. They also offer dance and gymnastics.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

They can accommodate allergies and they talk to the parents with the child of allergies before they come to camp. They are nut free, kosher style but not kosher meats. They eat all meals together but the boys and girls sit separately with their bunks. The oldest boys are the waiters and serve all the meals. They do the kiddush, prayers and have a traditional Shabbat dinner - brisket, chicken, potato latkes.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Every Saturday night they have an evening activity. They have a Shabbat tradition. They wear white, do the kiddush, prayers and have a traditional Shabbat dinner - brisket, chicken, potato latkes. They do have non Jewish kids. They have a big and little program. Girls each have their own traditions and boys also do. There are shared traditions as well.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

There are chaperoned flights from Boston, LA, Denver and Texas. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Watitoh is a coed camp with 220 campers and a 92% return rate that fills up in October for the next summer. Campers can go for 4 weeks until 5th grade; entering 3rd grade is the youngest campers and 11th graders are the oldest. Waterfront is twice a day with instructional swim, kayaking, canoeing, sailing and waterskiing, plus a hobby period where campers choose an activity for a week, cooking, nature/science with animals, tennis, basketball, hockey, dance and gymnastics. They have a Shabbat tradition with kiddush and a traditional dinner, are nut free and kosher style, can accommodate allergies, and offer chaperoned flights from Boston, LA, Denver and Texas.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 92% return rate.
  - Filled up in October for the next summer; at capacity this year.
  - Waterfront is twice a day; activities include instructional swim, kayaking, canoeing, sailing and waterskiing.
  - Hobby period choice weekly; cooking includes wontons, smoothies, cake pops, sushi; knife skills.
  - Nature and science with different animals every year.
  - 6 tennis courts; mini basketball, hockey and intermural sports; dance and gymnastics.
  - Nut free; kosher style (not kosher meats); allergy accommodation with pre-camp parent conversations.
  - Specialist at every area of camp.
  - Big and little program; strong traditions (Shabbat; girls’ and boys’ traditions; shared traditions).
  - Chaperoned flights from Boston, LA, Denver and Texas; some international kids with flexible dates.
  - They can serve the athletic and non athletic child.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Activities are by gender (swap of land and lake; boys and girls alternate waterfront).
  - There is no pool.
  - No bunk requests.
  - Boys and girls sit separately with their bunks at meals.
  - Director notes a child that would not fit would be impulsive, needs mental health counseling, needs a break from social stimulation, or one on one.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Experts: Tami Feldman, Alyssa Greenberger, Karen Meister, Monica Hirsch, Pilar Vidal, Carolina Lautenberg Tour: Britton, Director- 11 th summer Camp Watitoh is a coed camp with 220 campers. They are at capacity this year. Campers can go for 4 weeks until 5 th grade. Entering 3 rd grade is the youngest campers and 11 th graders are the oldest. They are filled up in October for the next summer. 92% is their return rate. There are some international kids and they are more flexible with their dates. Most campers are from New York, 20 from Florida, and there is a chaperoned flights from Boston, LA, Denver and Texas. Every Saturday night they have an evening activity. They have a Shabbat tradition. They wear white, do the kiddush, prayers and have a traditional Shabbat dinner- brisket, chicken, potato latkes. They do have non Jewish kids. They eat all meals together but the boys and girls sit separately with their bunks. They have a big and little program. The oldest boys are the waiters and serve all the meals. They can accommodate allergies and they talk to the parents with the child of allergies before they come to camp. They are nut free, kosher style but not kosher meats. Waterfront is twice a day and they alternate with the boys and the girls. They do a swap of land and lake activities with the boys and girls so activities are by gender. They have instructional swim, kayaking, canoeing, sailing and waterskiing. There is a hobby period which they choose an activity for a week. They have cooking- wontons, smoothies, cake pops, sushi and learn knife skills. Nature and science are offered and every year they have different animals. There are 6 tennis courts, mini basketball, hockey and intermural sports. They have a specialist at every area of camp. There is no pool. They also offer dance and gymnastics. Girls each have their own traditions and boys also do. There are shared traditions as well. There are no bunk requests. Each cabin has two toilets, showers and sinks. From the director: Every type of child would like Camp Watitoh but a child that would not fit would be impulsive, needs mental health counseling, needs a break from social stimulation, or one on one. They can serve the athletic and non athletic child.

title: Robin Hood — Lisa — 8-23-22
text:
# Robin Hood

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

ROBIN HOOD JULY 2022. Coed, 430 campers, 90% are full summer. He was not having a 2nd session. 117 new campers this summer. 180 counselors.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Woody showed us around. His campers are low key kids. He had 7 scholarship kids. 180 counselors; 80 of his counselors were campers.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks are wooden bunk beds with facilities inside the bunk. Double bunks with 16 campers/4 counselors. There are some different kinds of bunks for older kids.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Their Lake was very active, it wraps around camp so there are 2 sides with 4 motorboats and a pontoon boat. NO pool.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They travel by bunk, very structured programming, but get more choice as they age. 5-day color war at the end of the summer. They do half day hikes that are mandatory, but then other adventure day programs are optional.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

A\&C is basic, no cooking, woodworking was a nice size. Performing Arts: nice space, 3/summer talent shows and skits, no big plays or musicals. No music program. Dance is hip hop, yoga and Zumba, no bars or mirrors. No Gymnastics this summer. Fitness for ages 12+ was open air with weights and spin. All sports offered including Pickleball. Some tournaments but not many this summer with other camps. NO golf. We did not see horses, but they offer this. Street Hockey in their rink. They offer Hiking, Archery, Riflery. No sleepouts this summer.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: one seating, 2nd oldest groups on the patio, sit by bunk, cafeteria style, very spirited. One Doctor (Pediatric ER) with 6 RNs, 25 minutes to Memorial Hospital, the Covid vaccination was required.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

He is a bit unfocused but upbeat. Lots of music was blasting. It felt a little messy everywhere we went. Since they had not had much rain, the fields were yellowing. They offer an Optional Shabbat service 20-30 minutes; they also offer transportation to church on Sunday.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Chaperoned flight from FL /25 campers and CA/ 40 campers. Visiting Day is at the 4-week mark, and the partial campers head home the next day.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

A\&C is basic and woodworking was a nice size; Performing Arts has 3/summer talent shows and skits, no big plays or musicals, and no music program; Dance is hip hop, yoga and Zumba. Dining Hall is very spirited, there is a 5-day color war at the end of the summer, and his campers are low key kids.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Very structured programming, but get more choice as they age.
  - Their Lake was very active, it wraps around camp so there are 2 sides with 4 motorboats and a pontoon boat.
  - 80 of his counselors were campers.
  - Dining Hall: very spirited.
  - Fitness for ages 12+ was open air with weights and spin.
  - All sports offered including Pickleball.
  - Optional Shabbat service 20-30 minutes; transportation to church on Sunday.
  - 5-day color war at the end of the summer.
  - Chaperoned flight from FL /25 campers and CA/ 40 campers.
  - One Doctor (Pediatric ER) with 6 RNs; 25 minutes to Memorial Hospital; the Covid vaccination was required.

  

#### Lowlights

  - It felt a little messy everywhere we went.
  - He is a bit unfocused.
  - NO pool.
  - A\&C is basic, no cooking.
  - No music program.
  - No Gymnastics this summer.

  - NO golf.
  - No sleepouts this summer.
  - Since they had not had much rain, the fields were yellowing.
  - He was not having a 2nd session.
  - We did not see horses.

title: Greylock — Lara — 7-12-23
text:
# Greylock

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Greylock, full session all boys camp, has 350 campers.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lara and Emily S. toured with Ed, one of the head counselors and former head of lakefront. While staff members wear uniforms, campers do not have a uniform requirement.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Younger campers bunks have six campers and three counselors. There are no showers in the bunks. There are shower houses with staggered shower times. Toilets and sinks in bunks.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There are four swimming areas available in the lake, and motor sports and paddleboarding are popular as well. There is no pool. All campers swim at least once a day, and there is one recreational swim period.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The program is organized based on grade level, with juniors and up to 11th grade participating in three morning activities that rotate. Afternoons include elective options and swimming. Older and middle kids have an additional elective in the afternoon, and older kids have longer periods dedicated to purely elective activities.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

A camp-wide flag football program is implemented with no tackling or touching. The theater program at the camp includes eight shows, one for each age group. The oldest boys perform in an opening show, which is a musical adaptation of "Casey at the Bat." The junior side includes participation from all campers, while the senior side has an optional co-ed show. There are also weekly brother-sister events and socials.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining is staggered with two and a half seatings. Support staff is served first, followed by junior and senior meals. Entry to the dining hall is staggered, but all junior campers eat together, though the group assignments may change. Dining is kosher style with optional services on Fridays. Campers who wish to keep kosher have to send up their own food. The medical staff at Camp Greylock is primarily female, and they have an administrator who oversees the health center and takes care of off-camp medical needs. The camp has seven nurses and/or athletic trainers available. All areas for night activities are well lit.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Evening activities focus on large group non-competitive activities. The camp recently built a new pavilion and eliminated tennis hitting cages to create more hangout space.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation to the camp is provided by four buses from Manhattan, Westchester, New Jersey, and Long Island. For campers flying from Florida, there is a chaperoned flight option with an additional fee for families coming from different areas. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policies: Unknown. Other extra costs: Unknown (beyond the chaperoned flight additional fee).

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Greylock is a full-session, all-boys program with 350 campers and a structured, grade-based schedule. Mornings rotate through three activities, while afternoons emphasize electives and daily swimming in a lakefront with four swim areas (no pool). Highlights include an extensive theater program with eight shows, a camp-wide no-contact flag football program, and non-competitive evening activities. Dining is kosher style with optional Friday services, showers are in centralized houses (not bunks), and transportation is available from NYC-area hubs with a chaperoned Florida flight option.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Full session all-boys camp with 350 campers.
  - Grade-based program with rotating mornings and elective-heavy afternoons.
  - Four lake swim areas; all campers swim at least once daily; one recreational swim period.
  - Motor sports and paddleboarding are popular.
  - Theater program includes eight shows, including an opening "Casey at the Bat" musical.
  - Camp-wide flag football with no tackling or touching.
  - Evening activities are large group and non-competitive.
  - Weekly brother-sister events and socials.
  - New pavilion and added hangout space.
  - All areas for night activities are well lit.
  - Seven nurses/athletic trainers and a health center administrator.
  - Buses from Manhattan, Westchester, New Jersey, and Long Island; chaperoned Florida flight option.

  

#### Lowlights

  - There is no pool.
  - There are no showers in the bunks (shower houses with staggered times).
  - Campers who wish to keep kosher have to send up their own food.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Greylock – Lara and Emily S. toured with Ed one of the head counselors and former head of lakefront Camp Greylock, full session all boys camp has 350 campers. While staff members wear uniforms, campers do not have a uniform requirement. Younger campers bunks have six campers and three counselors. There are no showers in the bunks. There are shower houses with staggered shower times. Toilets and sinks in bunks. The program is organized based on grade level, with juniors and up to 11th grade participating in three morning activities that rotate. Afternoons include elective options and swimming. Older and middle kids have an additional elective in the afternoon, and older kids have longer periods dedicated to purely elective activities. There are four swimming areas available in the lake, and motor sports and paddleboarding are popular as well. There is no pool. All campers swim at least once a day, and there is one recreational swim period. A camp-wide flag football program is implemented with no tackling or touching. The theater program at the camp includes eight shows, one for each age group. The oldest boys perform in an opening show, which is a musical adaptation of "Casey at the Bat." The junior side includes participation from all campers, while the senior side has an optional co-ed show. Evening activities focus on large group non-competitive activities. The camp recently built a new pavilion and eliminated tennis hitting cages to create more hangout space. All areas for night activities are well lit. There are also weekly brother-sister events and socials. Dining is staggered with two and a half seatings. Support staff is served first, followed by junior and senior meals. Entry to the dining hall is staggered, but all junior campers eat together, though the group assignments may change. Dining is kosher style with optional services on Fridays. Campers who wish to keep kosher have to send up their own food. The medical staff at Camp Greylock is primarily female, and they have an administrator who oversees the health center and takes care of off-camp medical needs. The camp has seven nurses and/or athletic trainers available. Transportation to the camp is provided by four buses from Manhattan, Westchester, New Jersey, and Long Island. For campers flying from Florida, there is a chaperoned flight option with an additional fee for families coming from different areas.

  
  

### Author: Lara

title: Lenox — Carolina — 7-28-24
text:
# Lenox

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Lenox Write up done by Carolina Lautenberg. Visited with Karen Meister, Tammy Feldman, Pilar Vidal, Monica Hirsch and Alyssa Greenberger on 07/18/2024. Total of 250 kids, 75% stay full summer. They come from NY, NJ, CT, FL, some international from Spain, France, Israel, from 7 to 16 yo. It is their 107th summer (started in 1918 as boys only camp, changed to co-ed in 1984). They describe themselves as a fun traditional camp with high-level sports.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Owners Rich and son Morgan welcomed and toured us. LENOX CAMPER - Kids who are very active, engaged, enjoy sports.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

BUNKS: 8 campers + 2/3 counselors. Bathrooms and Showers inside.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lakefront with waterski, paddleboard, sailings.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They offer 40 different activities, many as group (by age) and 2 electives per day. Privates for tennis, soccer, gymnastics, horses.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

FACILITIES Large indoor theater for talent show, theater ("Mean Girls"), parties (all camp fit there for rainy days). 11 tennis courts, 3 baseball fields, 2 soccer fields, full gymnastic programs indoor. Activities include dance, theater, ninja warrior, indoor courts, fitness center, spinning, ropes course, zipline, tramp ball, arts with t-shirt printing, ceramics, arts and crafts, Lego room. Incredible cooking facilities with label reading, sushi making, high level cooking.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Guard at the entrance, very welcoming and called the office to authorize our entrance. Medical center has 5 nurses and a rotating doctor. FOOD Dining family style - nut free, gluten free. Can accommodate kosher (meat sent by parents).

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very beautiful and organized office. 80% kids are Jewish - they do Friday services optional, girls bake chala, light candles. Girls and boys programs are separate most of the time.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Total of 250 kids, 75% stay full summer, from 7 to 16 yo and coming from NY, NJ, CT, FL, with some international from Spain, France, Israel. They describe themselves as a fun traditional camp with high-level sports and offer 40 different activities with many as group (by age) and 2 electives per day. Dining family style - nut free, gluten free, and can accommodate kosher (meat sent by parents). Lakefront with waterski, paddleboard, sailings, plus facilities like a large indoor theater, 11 tennis courts, and full gymnastic programs indoor.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Kids seemed very happy/active/engaged and the facilities are great.
  - Guard at the entrance, very welcoming.
  - Owners Rich and son Morgan welcomed and toured us.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Owners were not excited/enthusiastic and very mellow.
  - Son Morgan doesn’t seem to be a camp person.
  - We are uncertain about how this will manifest in the forthcoming years.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Lenox Write up done by Carolina Lautenberg Visited with Karen Meister, Tammy Feldman, Pilar Vidal, Monica Hirsch and Alyssa Greenberger on 07/18/2024 Guard at the entrance, very welcoming and called the office to authorize our entrance. Owners Rich and son Morgan welcomed and toured us Very beautiful and organized office. Total of 250 kids, 75% stay full summer. They come from NY, NJ, CT, FL, some international from Spain, Frane Israel) from 7 to 16 yo 80% kids are jewish - they do friday services optional, girls bake chala, light candle girls and boys programs are separate most of the time Medical center has 5 nurses and a rotating doctor BUNKS: 8 campers + 2/3 counselors. Bathrooms and Showers inside FACILITIES Large indoor theater for talent show, theater (Mean Girls), parties (all camp fit there for rainy days) it is their 107th summer (started in 1918 as boys only camp, changed to co- ed in 1984) 11 tennis courts, 3 baseball fields, 2 soccer fields full gymnastic programs indoor ACTIVITIES They offer 40 different activities, many as group (by age) and 2 electives per day privates for tennis, soccer, gymnastics, horses dance, theater, ninja warrior, indoor courts, fitness center, spinning, ropes course, zipline, tramp ball arts with t-shirt printing, ceramics, arts and crafts lakefront with waterski, paddleboard, sailings Lego room Incredible cooking facilities with label reading, sushi making, high level cooking LENOX CAMPER - Kids who are very active, engaged, enjoy sports They describe themselves as a fun traditional camp with high-level sports FOOD Dining family style - nut free, gluten free. Can accommodate kosher (meat sent by parents) OUR FEELING: Although the kids seemed very happy/active/engaged and the facilities are great... Owners were not excited/enthusiastic and very mellow. title: Fairview — Natasha — 11-22-22
text:
# Fairview

### Author: Natasha

### Updated: 2025-08-26

  

## Sheet Metadata

|  |  |
| :-: | :-: |
| \*\*Write Up Name\*\* | Fairview- Natasha |
| \*\*Date\*\* | 11/22/22 |
| \*\*Created By: Full Name\*\* | David Highfill |
| \*\*Created Date\*\* | 8/1/25 |
| \*\*Partner: Account Name\*\* | FAIRVIEW LAKE YMCA- BLUE MT. DAY |
| \*\*Write Up ID\*\* | a0bPm00000IMuUG |

  
  

## Contents

  - Author: Natasha
  - Updated: 2025-08-26
  - Sheet Metadata
  - Contents

  
  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

There are many different options at Fairview with 1 week, 2 week and 3 week sessions depending on the camp. The most popular sessions are 3 and 6 weeks. For summer 2022, they had 250-350 per session. Camp Laurel Ridge – girls entering 5-10; Camp Kittatinny – boys entering 5-10; Camp Minisink – co-ed – entering grades 2-4; Camp Tupelo – co-ed entering 5-10; Ranch Camp – Co-ed and girls – entering 4-10; Environmental Trips for Challenge (ETC) – entering 4-11; Specialty camps – various ages/1-2 weeks – A\&C camp, Discovery Camp, Farm Camp, Sailing and Windsurfing camp, Taste of Tradition Camp, Wet and Wild Camp; CIT Camp – entering 11.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Natasha and Lana toured Fairview Lake on 8/10/22. We were toured by Matt Lifschultz, Director and Alumni Coordinator. Matt grew up in Millburn, NJ and started as a camper at Fairview in 1999. He joined the staff in 2009 and became the Director in 2019. Most of the campers are from NJ, but they do have a handful of international kids, and kids from various other states (MD, NY, PA). Fairview has a 60-70% return rate.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

BUNKING – 8-10 campers/2 counselors – will put a cot in if needed.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

LAKE – all swimming takes place in the lake – there is no pool. The lake is huge with only electric and manual boats/toys allowed to protect the health of the lake. There are 12 private homes on the lake, but they are respectful. Sailing is one of the most popular activities at FV, and they also have kayaks, rowboats, paddleboards, etc.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

COLOR WAR – on same team for life; siblings on same team. Color war is one time during a 3 week session and lasts 2 days.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Most popular activities: Sailing, paddle boarding, riflery, archery and A\&C. They have an entire room dedicated to candle making. They have an indoor climbing wall in addition to an outdoor climbing wall and zipline. RIFLERY – very popular, with a focus all about safety; BB guns and bolt action 22s (smallest caliber) shoot only paper and metal targets. TRIPS – white water rafting, state fair, water park and minor league baseball games.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

DINING HALL – Entire camp eats together – served family style or buffet – fruit is always available. The DH is clean, spacious and well ventilated. Picnic tables are outside and campers can eat there too. They are nut free, but have some items that are made in the same facility as nuts. They can handle all allergies. HEALTH CENTER – there is a nurse 24 hours and the hospital is 15 minutes away, plus an urgent care. AC – office and health center.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Fairview has a lot to offer, with many options. It is welcoming, accepting and diverse. The facilities are clean and the grounds are very pretty. The camp is used during the year for events. Campers were very friendly, busy and happy.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

All campers are brought to camp by car. There is no bussing provided, but vans are sent to the airport. PICTURES – Use SmugMug – but not every day. CANTEEN – Fairview’s canteen is a store setting in a building called The Trading Post. Families set up an account and their camper can use the money to buy candy, chips, etc., and they can also buy Fairview swag. They use it as a tool to help kids understand the value of money. They have canteen at The Trading Post 2x a day. The first time is free – after lunch – and it’s a healthier option.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Fairview Lake YMCA Camp offers many different options with 1, 2 and 3 week sessions depending on the camp, with the most popular sessions at 3 and 6 weeks and 250–350 campers per session in 2022. All swimming takes place in the lake (no pool), with sailing, paddle boarding, riflery, archery and A\&C among the most popular activities, plus trips and a strong safety focus. Entire camp eats together in a clean, spacious and well-ventilated dining hall that is nut free and can handle all allergies; there is a nurse 24 hours and the hospital is 15 minutes away. All campers are brought by car (no bussing, vans are sent to the airport), SmugMug photos are used but not every day, and the vibe is welcoming, accepting and diverse with friendly, busy and happy campers.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Sailing is one of the most popular activities at FV; kayaks, rowboats and paddleboards are offered.
  - RIFLERY – very popular and safety-focused; BB guns and bolt action 22s shooting only paper and metal targets.
  - COLOR WAR – same team for life (siblings together); one time during a 3 week session and lasts 2 days.
  - Entire camp eats together; dining is family style or buffet; fruit is always available.
  - Nut free and can handle all allergies.
  - Nurse on site 24 hours; hospital 15 minutes away; urgent care nearby.
  - CANTEEN at The Trading Post 2x a day; first time is free after lunch and is a healthier option; used to teach value of money.
  - Lake-only swimming (no pool); only electric and manual boats to protect the lake.
  - 60-70% return rate; campers from NJ with some from MD, NY, PA and international.
  - Indoor and outdoor climbing walls plus a zipline; dedicated candle making room; strong A\&C.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No bussing provided (car drop-off only; airport vans available).
  - No pool (all swimming is in the lake).
  - Pictures via SmugMug are not every day.
  - Nut free but some items are made in the same facility as nuts.
  - There are 12 private homes on the lake (noted as respectful).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Camp Mason
  - Speers
  - Frost Valley

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They have BB guns and bolt action 22s which hold the smallest caliber – shoot only paper and metal targets. PICTURES – Use smug mug – but not every day TRIPS – white water rafting, state fair, water park and minor league baseball games Most popular activities: Sailing, paddle boarding, riflery, archery and A\&C. They have an entire room dedicated to candle making. They have an indoor climbing wall in addition to an outdoor climbing wall and zipline. Fairview has a lot offer, which many options. It is welcoming, accepting and diverse. The facilities are clean and the grounds are very pretty. The camp is used during the year for events. Families who are looking at Fairview are also looking at Camp Mason, Speers, and Frost Valley. Campers were very friendly, busy and happy.

title: Wolfeboro — Monica — 8-28-25
text:
# Wolfeboro

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Wolfeboro is a unique Camp–School program in New Hampshire that blends outdoor living with intensive academics. While it looks and feels like a rustic summer camp—with platform tents, wooded classrooms, and daily outdoor activities—its core mission is remediation, enrichment, and executive functioning development. The program runs 5 weeks (with a 3-week option for younger students).  
Location: New Hampshire, USA  
Length: 5 weeks (younger students: 3 weeks)  
Capacity: \~100 campers  
Ages: 11–18 (average age 15; youngest accepted is 11/12)  
Class size: 4–5 students per class  
Special feature: Integration of academics and camp life; individualized learning plan created with each child’s current school

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Wolfeboro serves students who need academic remediation, enrichment, or executive functioning skills. Campers may be:  
\- Students struggling in school who need structure and individualized attention  
\- Strong students seeking enrichment and academic rigor  
\- Teenagers aiming to strengthen organization, study habits, and independence  
The population is racially and culturally diverse, with participants from 11 countries and 19 U.S. states.  
Faculty: Academic teachers are separate from residential (camp life) staff.  
Faculty Tenure: Director Brian has led the program for 36 years and is so passionate about it; Admissions Director Kathleen for 14 years.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Campers live in platform tents with one roommate. Life is intentionally rustic: no technology, no rest hour, and most daily activities happen outdoors, even in the rain. Bedtime is 10:00 PM.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Choice-based activities include kayaking, canoeing, and sailing. Crew program (6–7 AM) offered for an additional $1,500, taught by an Andover coach.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Individualized Learning Plans: Set up in collaboration with each camper’s school prior to arrival.  
Daily Structure: Students receive grades daily in every class; study hall and homework are mandatory and independent.  
Afternoons function like a traditional camp: Choice-based activities (kayaking, canoeing, sailing, sports, board games, goofy intramurals, etc.).  
Two trips each weekend (amusement parks, outdoor excursions, etc.).  
Three afternoon activities daily; no rest hour.  
Bedtime is 10:00 PM.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Special feature: Integration of academics and camp life; individualized learning plan created with each child’s current school. Core Skills: Focus on organization, transitions, structure, and process—transferable tools for success in school and beyond. Class size: 4–5 students per class. Key Differentiator: Wolfeboro is not just a camp—it is a summer academic institution disguised as a camp, designed to teach structure, organization, and independence while still delivering a full outdoors experience.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining: Sit-down meals where students rotate as waiters; seating changes weekly to foster social connections. Food: Can accommodate halal and kosher diets as well as allergies and intolerances. Health and safety: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Tone: Formal structure balanced with “goofy” and fun intramural traditions. Life is intentionally rustic: no technology, no rest hour, and most daily activities happen outdoors, even in the rain. The population is racially and culturally diverse, with participants from 11 countries and 19 U.S. states. Key Differentiator: Wolfeboro is not just a camp—it is a summer academic institution disguised as a camp.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Phones: Limited to 40 minutes per day. No technology. Extra costs: Crew program (6–7 AM) offered for an additional $1,500.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Wolfeboro is a unique Camp–School program in New Hampshire that blends outdoor living with intensive academics. It serves students who need academic remediation, enrichment, or executive functioning skills, with individualized learning plans created with each child’s current school. The program is intentionally rustic with platform tents, formal structure, and a full outdoors experience, while afternoons function like a traditional camp with choice-based activities and weekend trips. It is not just a camp—it is a summer academic institution disguised as a camp, designed to teach structure, organization, and independence.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Integration of academics and camp life with individualized learning plans.
  - Core Skills focus on organization, transitions, structure, and process.
  - Small class size: 4–5 students per class.
  - Daily grades in every class; study hall and homework are mandatory and independent.
  - Afternoons function like a traditional camp with choice-based activities and weekend trips.
  - Crew program (6–7 AM) taught by an Andover coach (additional $1,500).
  - Racially and culturally diverse community from 11 countries and 19 U.S. states.
  - Director Brian has led the program for 36 years; Admissions Director Kathleen for 14 years.
  - Academic teachers are separate from residential staff.
  - Capacity \~100 campers; 5-week program with a 3-week option for younger students.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Life is intentionally rustic: platform tents, no technology, no rest hour.
  - Most daily activities happen outdoors, even in the rain.
  - Phones are limited to 40 minutes per day.
  - Formal structure with daily grades, study hall, and homework.
  - Crew program is early (6–7 AM) and has an extra cost of $1,500.
  - Health and safety specifics: Unknown.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown
