title: Maine Arts — 7-31-20
text:
# Maine Arts

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Name of Camp: Maine Arts Camp. Founded: 2004. Director: Rick Mades. Season: Summer. Type of Camp: Sleep away, coed. Specialty: Creative Arts (Maine Arts Camp) and Sports (Sports Camp at Maine Arts Camp). These are completely separate programs. Location: At Thomas College in Waterville, Maine (general area: North-East). ACA Accredited: No, but licensed by the state of Maine. Ages: 9-16. Enrollment (per program): Maine Arts Camp \~ 75 girls, 25 boys; Sports Camp at Maine Arts Camp \~ 25 girls, 25 boys. International campers: Minimal. Phone: 561-865-4330. Email: maineartscamprick@gmail.com. Website: https://maineartscamp.com/

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Typical camper: At Maine Arts Camp (since 2004), we attract creative kids/teens, with the largest number being 12-15 years old. Campers looking for an inclusive, small, creative camp community find like-minded campers and staff. For our new Sports Camp, campers/staff will have separate programming and dorm space. Campers who like to play sports, want to get better at their craft, and be part of a small camp community will do well with us. We are not about competition, but improving skills, making friends, and fostering personal growth. Religious majority: None. Gender/non-conforming policy: We use campers’ preferred names and pronouns, provided by their parents in our registration Portal. For dorm living, campers generally live with the gender of campers that they were assigned at birth. Camper/Counselor Ratio: 3:1 or better. Staff counts: 35 for Arts Camp, an extra 8-14 for Sports Camp, depending on enrollment. Director style: nurturing, limited enrollment, individualized programming, strong camper support. Years as Director: 16. Staff ages: Dorm counselors are generally 19-30 years old. Teaching staff is generally 30-70 years old. Nurses are generally 45-70 years old.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

College dorms: For Maine Arts Camp, the girls live in an air conditioned dorm built in 2014. It has double rooms, along with common bathrooms. Each half of the floor has 8 bathrooms and showers. The Maine Arts Camp boys live in a different dorm. This dorm is broken up into 3 sections, with one for Arts Camp boys, and the others for Sports Camp campers. That dorm is not air conditioned, has double rooms, common bathrooms and a large lounge. Linens: Yes. Which facilities are air-conditioned: Girls dorm, dining hall/cafeteria, theatre, dance studio, art classrooms, film and photography classrooms. Night security: 24 hour security provided by Thomas College.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront: Off premise at Camp Tracy in Sidney, Maine – kayaking only. We have 11 kayaks. Trips/Tours for aquatics: We take the campers off campus 1-2 days per two week session for swimming at a Maine lake. This will include Lake St. George State Park.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Selection of schedule is: Structured/planned. For Maine Arts Camp, campers choose 10 activities for each two week session. They do so in advance and receive their schedules in June, before camp starts. For the new Sports Camp, campers will choose a major and two minors for each two week session, along with a daily elective choice. Those electives will, for the most part, be taught by Maine Arts Camp staff, so Sports Camp campers can try some creative activities.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Mood: We are as non-competitive as you will find anywhere. For the Arts Camp, we generally don’t offer team sports, while for the Sports Camp, the focus is on skill development and personal growth, not winning or losing. Writing: We do offer an extensive writing program at Maine Arts Camp. Activities generally include Creative Writing, Fiction Writing and Script Writing. Fine Arts: Extensive programming in the visual arts and culinary arts, along with film making and photography. We have multiple classrooms set up for visual arts, film, and photography. They are all air conditioned in a modern classroom building at Thomas College. Visual arts activities include (as offered): Animation, Arts & Crafts, Batik, Book Arts, Calligraphy, Cartooning, Ceramics, Cooking, Drawing, Fashion Design, Glass Fusion, Jewelry Making, Kite Making, Lamp Working, Leather, Linoleum Block, Metal Working, Mosaics, Origami, Painting, Photography, Print Making, Puppetry, Sculpture, Sewing/Fabric Art, Silk Screen, Silver Smith, Stained Glass, Tie Dye, Weaving, Woodworking. Performing Arts: We have 100% use of the on campus theatre, dance studio and a classroom set up as a music studio. All of these spaces are air conditioned. Theatre activities generally include acting, directing, musical theatre, improvisation. Music activities include ukulele, guitar, rock shop, chorus and songwriting. Dance activities include hip hop, modern/contemporary, lyrical ballet, jazz, tap and cardio dance. Showcases: We have a Final Showcase at the end of each two week session. Work from visual arts are displayed, film activities are shown on a constant loop, culinary arts treats are eaten outside the theatre for the Final Performance. This includes theatre, dance and music activities. Sports Camp: No teams, intercamp competition or anything competitive. Focus on skill development, with campers coming home and being better at their chosen sports. Facilities include 100% use of an outdoor grass field for soccer, lacrosse, ultimate frisbee, capture the flag and more; a softball field that can also be used for wiffle ball and kick ball; and an indoor multi-purpose gym for tennis, along with basketball, soccer and lacrosse for rainy days. Most lead instructors are college or experienced high school coaches. Tennis: We have a strong tennis program, led by the head tennis coach at Thomas College. Additional sports offerings may include: Archery, Baseball/Softball, Basketball, Bicycling, Bowling, Canoeing, Fitness, Golf, Hiking, Kayaking, Lacrosse, Martial Arts, Riflery, Rowing/Crew, Sailing, Soccer, Swimming, Tennis, Track and Field, Volleyball, Yoga; other sports not listed: wiffle ball, possibly pickle ball. Transportation for trips: We use Northeast Charter and their school buses.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical: Health Center: Yes. Nurses: There are 3 nurses per two week session, two full time and one part time. Nearest Hospital: Two hospitals in Waterville, both within 5-10 minutes. There is also a walk in clinic in Waterville. We have standing orders set up with Waterville Pediatrics. Allergy Management: Peanut Allergy: We are nut free. Celiac/Gluten: Thomas College can accommodate celiac/gluten free. ADD/ADHD: Yes. Asthma: Yes. Other allergies: Parents can contact camp director about other needs. Medication: Mail order – Winslow Pharmacy, the town over from Waterville. Vaccinations: All staff and campers will need to be current on vaccinations, including for COVID-19 next summer. Special Needs: We generally can accommodate ADD/ADHD, some learning disabilities, campers who need help with social skills (to some extent). We usually have an occupational therapist and/or a special needs teacher on staff. Safety/Security: 24 hour security provided by Thomas College. Emergency plan: The emergency plan is part of our staff handbook and reviewed during training week. Dining: Dining Hall: Above average, air conditioned. Number of seatings: 2. Food type: Standard, vegetarian. Peanut free: Yes. Prepared by: Food service. Serving style: Buffet. Salad bar: Yes. Can a camper bring their own food? It would be stored with a counselor if it is snacks. Parents can check with camp director.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Campers looking for an inclusive, small, creative camp community find like-minded campers and staff. We are not about competition, but improving skills, making friends, and fostering personal growth. We are as non-competitive as you will find anywhere. Director style: nurturing, limited enrollment, individualized programming, strong camper support.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Communications: Telephone policy: There are no phone calls for two week campers. For four week campers whose parents do not visit, we set up a phone on transition weekend. Email policy: Parents can email their camper. The emails are printed daily and distributed with the regular mail. One package (not food) is allowed per session. Visiting policy: No visiting days for two week campers. For four week campers, parents can visit on the last day of the first session. We can set up individual tours. Transportation: We provide airport pickup and drop off at Portland (Maine) Jetport. For campers flying to camp, there is no charge for pickup or drop off. Payments and fees: Accept MasterCard, Visa, American Express. No extra fees. Rates/Dates: https://maineartscamp.com/summer-camp/rates-and-dates.aspx. Promotional items: Generally a detailed intro email, along with links to our website.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

At Maine Arts Camp, we attract creative kids/teens (ages 9-16), with the largest number being 12-15 years old, who want an inclusive, small, creative camp community. The program is structured/planned with strong choice: arts campers pre-select 10 activities per two-week session, while Sports Camp campers choose a major, two minors, and a daily elective. We are as non-competitive as you will find anywhere, focusing on improving skills, making friends, and fostering personal growth. Facilities include many air-conditioned program spaces and a girls dorm (boys dorm is not air conditioned), with off-premise kayaking and lake swim trips, strong health supports, and free Portland Jetport transfers.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Inclusive, small, creative camp community; strong non-competitive culture.
  - Extensive fine and performing arts, culinary, film, photography, and writing programs.
  - Structured choice scheduling with individualized programming and strong camper support.
  - Many air-conditioned program spaces (theatre, dance studio, art/film/photo classrooms) and girls dorm.
  - Robust medical setup (3 nurses/session, nearby hospitals, nut-free, celiac/gluten-free capable).
  - Free airport pickup/dropoff at Portland (Maine) Jetport.
  - Sports Camp focuses on skill development with quality facilities and experienced coaches.
  - Final Showcase every two weeks featuring visual arts, film, culinary, theatre, dance, and music.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Not ACA accredited (licensed by the state of Maine).
  - Boys dorm is not air conditioned.
  - Waterfront is limited to off-premise kayaking; lake swimming only 1-2 days per session.
  - No visiting days for two-week campers and limited phone access.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Marimeta — 8-3-22
text:
# Marimeta

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Marimeta for Girls. Eagle River, WI. Opening Registration September 1, 2022. In 2023: Mini Metas Rookie Program ONLY 2nd Session - going into 2nd or 3rd grade.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Director: Jami Kasel (former Special Ed Teacher). 160 campers 1st session. 135 campers 2nd session. 180 is completely full. 40% stay full summer. 85% of campers are from Illinois (Chicagoland Area). Will limit campers from areas based on numbers when able to. Looking for diversity in location and types of campers.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

1 shower house for all of camp, located near the lake. Cabins all have toilets and sinks. All campers switch beds every Sunday (within their cabin) - counselors assign beds.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Camp is on a private lake. Swimming is required for all campers. Kayak/canoe skills class also required. Water skiing is popular activity. Older campers go on a big sailing trip.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

3 activities in the AM (45 minutes each). 2 activities in the PM. Kindness & Gratitude Period required. Activities are ranked and then assigned to campers. Keep same schedule for 2 weeks at a time. Tennis required every other day.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

High ropes course, cooking, new gaga pit, pottery, arts & crafts. Special Programming: Mari vs Meta. Red vs White. Friday nights are capture the flag and campfire (not religious). Socials with local boys camps. Lots of evening programs include singing and dancing. BIG Big/Lil Sister program for campers.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Eat most meals outside, have round tables in lodge (mess hall). Campers do not sit with their cabins. Mondays they get table assignments for the week to get to know other campers and counselors. Mess hall is nut free, but camp is NOT (they cannot handle airborne allergies or cross contamination). Canteen everyday in the afternoon. Fruit is out all day for girls to eat. Could handle camper with Diabetes (have 14 year old camper currently). Use Rosen’s Pharmacy in Chicago for medications for campers.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Dancing and singing are big part of camp culture - campers bring costumes and outfits for performances.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

One way email available from parents to campers. Unknown travel logistics. Unknown tech policy. Unknown extra costs.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Marimeta for Girls in Eagle River, WI is on a private lake with required swimming and kayak/canoe skills, and water skiing is a popular activity. The daily program has 3 activities in the AM (45 minutes each), 2 activities in the PM, a required Kindness & Gratitude Period, and tennis required every other day. Campers eat most meals outside with weekly mixed table assignments; the mess hall is nut free, but camp is NOT, and they could handle a camper with Diabetes and use Rosen’s Pharmacy in Chicago for medications. Traditions and programs like Mari vs Meta, Red vs White, Friday night capture the flag and campfire, socials with local boys camps, and lots of evening singing and dancing sit alongside high ropes, cooking, a new gaga pit, pottery, and arts & crafts.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Private lake; swimming is required for all campers; kayak/canoe skills class also required; water skiing is popular.
  - 3 AM activities (45 minutes each) and 2 PM activities with schedules kept for 2 weeks; rankings to assign activities; tennis required every other day.
  - High ropes course, cooking, new gaga pit, pottery, arts & crafts.
  - Special Programming: Mari vs Meta, Red vs White, Friday nights capture the flag and campfire (not religious), socials with local boys camps.
  - Dancing and singing are big part of camp culture; campers bring costumes and outfits for performances.
  - Eat most meals outside with round tables; weekly table assignments to get to know other campers and counselors; fruit is out all day; canteen everyday in the afternoon.
  - Mess hall is nut free; could handle camper with Diabetes; uses Rosen’s Pharmacy in Chicago for medications.
  - BIG Big/Lil Sister program for campers.
  - Older campers go on a big sailing trip.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Camp is NOT nut free (cannot handle airborne allergies or cross contamination).
  - 1 shower house for all of camp.
  - All campers switch beds every Sunday within their cabin.
  - 85% of campers are from Illinois; will limit campers from areas based on numbers when able to.
  - One way email only from parents to campers.
  - 180 is completely full.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Chippewa Ranch Camp
  - Camp Agawak
  - Lake of the Woods

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Pine Forest — Allison — 8-22-22
text:
# Pine Forest 2022

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp didn’t have many campers – just 6th graders and below because they were out for kindness day. 11th graders were out of camp as well seeing colleges in Boston. 11th graders also went on a trip to Costa Rica. 420 campers – split 50/50 boys and girls. 7-week camp – one-time partial option. Going into 12th grade is a CIT. Girls have one flagpole line-up and boys have a separate flagpole line-up.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Allison, Lara and Emily S. were toured by Lisa Fayne. No break between campers and counselors. Counselors always in staff shirts. Demographics: Westchester, Connecticut, NYC, Cherry Hill, DC, Florida, California. Majority of counselors are Jewish.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: 8–10 campers in a bunk with 3 counselors in each bunk. Bunk beds. Fans in the bunks that pull out hot air.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Have a boys’ heated pool and a girls’ heated pool. Up through 6th grade mandatory instructional swim – 4 times a week. Offer towel service at pool and lake – but kids can bring their own towels if they want. Use their own towels for shower. Lake: man-made lake. Lake is down a really really far path and then across a road. Campers go to the lake about 2–3 times a week. Share the lake with Lake Greely and Shohola. Non-motorized – take campers off camp to waterski. By 5th grade you can waterski 2–3 times per summer. They can sail, canoe, kayak on the lake. Most kids go to the lake and hang for a few periods since it is such a long walk. Going into 9th grade the beach becomes co-ed.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6th grade and below are structured. Activities are done by age group not by bunk. 6 periods a day. Always have a sport once a day. More choice as the camper gets older. Younger campers have 1 or 2 choices per day. Up through 6th grade mandatory instructional swim – 4 times a week. Mandatory tennis 3 times a week.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Have lit fields. Have a cooking facility. Cooking, archery, gymnastics, and dance are choice activities. Extras: Golf (take off camp), private cooking classes, private tennis, horseback riding (off site), 1:1 fitness. Camp brings in a local specialist who teaches their specialty for a few days; while we were there we saw a dance specialty in session. 2 plays a summer – play is co-ed. Indoor theater. Hiking is an activity as they have a lot of hiking trails around camp. Arts area – arts and crafts, jewelry, general arts, crafts, ceramics, STEM, woodworking – craft area was nothing special. Martial arts. Indoor field house is very old school. Spin studio for campers 7th grade and older. Small dinky gym. Video broadcasting. 2 gaga courts. Ninja warrior course. Big climbing wall, swing and zip line.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Meals: Girls and boys have different meal seating. Girls and boys never eat together. All campers sit with their bunks in the dining room. They have one designated person (Lauren) who handles all special dietary needs. They said they can accommodate any dietary need. Can handle Kosher. Nut free. Have a salad bar. Brothers and sisters meet up between the two seatings (girls and boys) on porch. Every Thursday the entire camp eats outside. Dining room is air conditioned. Low-key canteen. Medical: Have a camp doctor on site at all times (a parent). 4 nurses at all times.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religion: Have a Friday night service with blessing over challah, wine and candles. Say hamotzi over dinner every night.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Trips – campers go with their age groups. Summer 2022 they had 3 explorer days. In the spring they host a new camper weekend when the families sleep in the bunks, and they have wine and cheese for the parents while the kids interact. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Pine Forest is a 7-week camp with 420 campers – split 50/50 boys and girls. Up through 6th grade mandatory instructional swim – 4 times a week, and there is mandatory tennis 3 times a week, with 6 periods a day and more choice as the camper gets older. Many elements run separately by gender (separate flagpole line-ups, different meal seating, and boys’ and girls’ heated pools), with Friday night service with blessing over challah, wine and candles and saying hamotzi over dinner every night. The lake is down a really really far path and then across a road, so most kids go to the lake and hang for a few periods since it is such a long walk, and by 5th grade you can waterski 2–3 times per summer.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 7-week camp – one-time partial option
  - 420 campers – split 50/50 boys and girls
  - Activities are done by age group, with 6 periods a day and always a sport once a day
  - Up through 6th grade mandatory instructional swim – 4 times a week
  - Mandatory tennis 3 times a week
  - Boys’ and girls’ heated pools
  - Camp brings in a local specialist; we saw a dance specialty in session
  - 2 plays a summer – play is co-ed; indoor theater
  - Lit fields, ninja warrior course, big climbing wall, swing and zip line
  - Dining room is air conditioned; salad bar; can accommodate any dietary need (Kosher, nut free)
  - Have a camp doctor on site at all times (a parent) and 4 nurses at all times

  

#### Lowlights

  - Lake is down a really really far path and then across a road
  - Indoor field house is very old school
  - Small dinky gym
  - Arts/crafts craft area was nothing special
  - Lake is shared with Lake Greely and Shohola
  - Non-motorized lake; waterski requires going off camp
  - Girls and boys have different meal seating and never eat together

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Allison, Lara and Emily S. were toured by Lisa Fayne. Camp didn’t have many campers – just 6 th graders and below because they were out for kindness day 11 th graders were out of camp as well seeing colleges in Boston. 11 th graders also went on a trip to Costa Rica. 420 campers – split 50/50 boys and girls No break between campers and counselors. Going into 12 th grade is a CIT Girls have one flagpole line up and boys have a separate flagpole line up 7 week camp – one time partial option Meals Girls and Boys have different meal seating. Girls and Boys never eat together All campers sit with their bunks in the dining room They have one designated person (Lauren) who handles all special dietary needs They said they can accommodate any dietary need Can handle Kosher Nut Free Have a salad bar Brothers and Sisters meet up between the two seating’s (girls and boys) on porch Every Thursday the entire camp eats outside. Dining room is air conditioned Low Key Canteen Bunks 8-10 campers in a bunk with 3 counselors in each bunk Counselors always in staff shirts Bunk Beds Fans in the bunks that pull out hot air Demographics Westchester Connecticut NYC Cherry Hill DC Florida California Activities 6th grade and below are structured Activities are done by age group not by bunk 6 periods a day Always have a sport once a day More choice as the camper gets older Younger campers have 1 or 2 choices per day Have lit fields Have a boys heated pool and a girls heated pool Up through 6 th grade mandatory instructional swim – 4 times a week Have cooking facility Mandatory tennis 3 times a week Cooking, archery, gymnastics, dance are choice activities Extras: Golf (take off of camp) Private cooking classes Private Tennis Horseback riding (off site) 1:1 fitness Offer towel service at pool and lake – but kids can bring their own towels if they want Use their own towels for shower Camp brings in a local specialist who teaches their specialty for a few days. While we were there we saw a dance specialty in session. 2 plays a summer – play is co-ed Indoor theater Hiking is an activity as they have a lot of hiking trails around camp Arts area – arts and crafts, jewlery, general arts, crafts, ceramics, STEM, woodworking – craft area was nothing special Martial arts Indoor field house is very old school Spin studio for campers 7 th grade and older Small dinky gym Video Broadcasting 2 ga ga courts Ninja warrior course Big climbing wall, swing and zip line Lake Man made lake Lake is down a really really far path and then across a road Campers go to the lake about 2-3 times a week Share the lake with Lake Greely and Shohola Non motorized – take campers off of camp to waterski By 5 th grade you can waterski 2-3 times per summer They can sail, canoe, kayak on the lake Most kids go to the lake and hang for a few periods since it is such a long walk Going into 9 th grade the beach becomes co-ed Religion Have a Friday night service with blessing over challah, wine and candles Say hamotzi over dinner every night Majority of counselors are Jewish Medical Have a camp doctor on site at all times (a parent) 4 nurses at all times Trips – Campers go with their age groups Summer 2022 they had 3 explorer days In the spring they host a new camper weekend when the families sleep in the bunks. They have wine and cheese for the parents while the kids interact.

title: Wekeela — 7-7-22
text:
# Wekeela

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Visited on 7/7/22 along with Emily Salant, Eliza, Wendy and Joanne. Wekeela has run the last 3 years (ran in 2020 with 300 campers). It’s their 100th year\! Co-ed camp. Semi uniform on B & D days or Wekeela gear. Offer 2 weeks (2nd session only), limit it to 1–2 kids per age group, any age can do it but mostly younger campers do, 3.5 & 7 weeks. 50% do full session.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

275–280 campers per session, 425 in total. Kids Camp—into 2nd–7th grade; Teen Camp—into 8th–11th grade (LIT); Jr counselor in bunks into 12th grade. 65% of staff are former campers. Counselors move up with their campers year to year. Staff wears staff shirts. Campers are coming from 29 different states.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

1 cabin per age group (12–15 campers in a cabin). Every cabin has a theme. Jr counselor in bunks into 12th grade.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Private lake. 5 motor boats; water ski is a double period—2 hours.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Most activities are co-ed, structure with some choice. Have 4 day rotation with 5th day as a Wekeela Day A, B, C, D days. 5 periods in a day, 2 periods in the AM and 3 periods in the PM, each 50 mins. Pushed the start of day back by 40 mins so campers/staff can sleep in. Have seen happier staff and campers. 9 activity areas in a 4 day rotation include: waterski, waterfront, land sports, creative arts, performing arts, outdoor adventure, tripping, tennis (instructors are college to pro level), culinary (different theme each week). Everyone does everything. Morning lineup with flag raising.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Nice game room for Teen Camp at nights. Kids can use during the day for rainy days. 1st session—1 show, 2nd session—musical review. 7 tennis courts, 4 pickleball courts, some have lights. Do College Weekend with 4 teams—1st session, Color War—2nd session. Outdoor Adventure—25 elements—high and low ropes course, fire building skills, 3-sided rock wall and natural rock wall on campus. Goat farm and organic farm. Fitness Center for staff and Teen Camp. No intercamps this summer but usually play with Caribou, Laurel, Laurel South. Turned it into spin studio and small skate park. Want to possibly build an indoor gym/field house for next summer.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Ephram met us at our cars with the camp nurse to give us Covid tests. The nurse was very friendly. 5 full time nurses, 1 doc & 1 student nurse. Dining room—one seating, sit by bunk, family style, allergy specialist. No canteen. Discontinued gymnastics this summer (too much to have to keep cleaning due to Covid).

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

We were toured by Jonathan (Ephram’s son), very warm and knowledgeable. Morning lineup with flag raising, preach kindness, gratitude & positivity. Give nominations for good deeds and success. At first, he seemed a bit unfriendly but then warmed up. Overall impression—camp looked great, well-manicured with beautiful flowers. Very nice facilities. Campers were friendly and seemed engaged.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Optional/extra $$$: horseback riding, golf, surfing, ice hockey/skating (all morning, rent out ice 2x a week). Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Co-ed camp in its 100th year with session options of 2 weeks (2nd session only, limited to 1–2 kids per age group), 3.5 & 7 weeks; 50% do full session. Most activities are co-ed, structure with some choice, with a 4 day rotation (A, B, C, D days), 5 periods a day (2 AM and 3 PM, each 50 mins), and they pushed the start of day back by 40 mins so campers/staff can sleep in. Private lake with 5 motor boats; water ski is a double period—2 hours, plus Outdoor Adventure (25 elements), tennis (instructors are college to pro level), creative and performing arts, and more. Overall impression—camp looked great, well-manicured with beautiful flowers; very nice facilities; campers were friendly and seemed engaged.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Overall impression—camp looked great, well-manicured with beautiful flowers.
  - Very nice facilities.
  - Campers were friendly and seemed engaged.
  - Private lake. 5 motor boats; water ski is a double period—2 hours.
  - Outdoor Adventure—25 elements—high and low ropes course, fire building skills, 3-sided rock wall and natural rock wall on campus.
  - 65% of staff are former campers; counselors move up with their campers year to year.
  - Morning lineup with flag raising; preach kindness, gratitude & positivity.
  - 7 tennis courts, 4 pickleball courts, some have lights.
  - Tennis instructors are college to pro level.
  - Fitness Center for staff and Teen Camp.
  - Nice game room for Teen Camp at nights; kids can use during the day for rainy days.
  - It’s their 100th year\!

  

#### Lowlights

  - At first, he seemed a bit unfriendly but then warmed up.
  - Discontinued gymnastics this summer (too much to have to keep cleaning due to Covid).
  - No intercamps this summer.
  - No canteen.
  - Want to possibly build an indoor gym/field house for next summer.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Birchmont
  - Laurel
  - Laurel South
  - Kohut

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Visited on 7/7/22 along with Emily Salant, Eliza, Wendy and Joanne. Ephram met us at our cars with the camp nurse to give us Covid tests. At first, he seemed a bit unfriendly but then warmed up. The nurse was very friendly though. We were toured by Jonathan (Ephram’s son), very warm and knowledgeable. Wekeela has run the last 3 years (ran in 2020 with 300 campers). It’s their 100 th year\! - Co-ed camp. Semi uniform on B & D days or Wekeela gear. Offer 2 weeks (2 nd session only), limit it to 1-2 kids per age group, any age can do it but mostly younger campers do, 3.5 & 7 weeks. 50% do full session. - 275-280 campers per session, 425 in total. Kids camp- into 2 nd -7 th grade, Teen Camp- into 8 th - 11 th grade (LIT), Jr counselor in bunks into 12 th grade. - 65% of staff are former campers. Counselors move up with their campers year to year. Staff wears staff shirts. - Campers are coming from 29 different states. 1 cabin per age group (12-15 campers in a cabin). Every cabin has a theme. - Most activities are co-ed, structure with some choice. Have 4 day rotation with 5 th day as a Wekeela Day A, B, C, D days. 5 periods in a day, 2 periods in the AM and 3 periods in the PM, each 50 mins. Pushed the start of day back by 40m mins so campers/staff can sleep in. Have seen happier staff and campers. - 9 activity areas in a 4 day rotation include; waterski, waterfront, land sports, creative arts, performing arts, outdoor adventure, tripping, tennis (instructor are college to pro level), culinary (different theme each week). Everyone does everything. Discontinued gymnastics this summer (too much to have to keep cleaning due to Covid), turned it into spin studio and small skate park. - Morning lineup with flag raising, preach kindness, gratitude & positivity. Give nominations for good deeds and success - Dining room- one seating, sit by bunk, family style, allergy specialist. No canteen. - Private lake. 5 motor boats, water ski is a double period- 2 hours. - Nice game room for teen camp at nights. Kids can use during the day for rainy days. - 1 st session- 1 show, 2 nd session- musical review. - 5 full time nurses, 1 doc & 1 student nurse - 7 tennis courts, 4 pickleball courts, some have lights - Do College Weekend with 4 teams- 1 st session, Color War- 2 nd session - Outdoor Adventure- 25 elements-high and low ropes course, fire building skills, 3-sided rock wall and natural rock wall on campus. Goat farm and organic farm. - Fitness Center for staff and Teen Camp - No intercamps this summer but usually play with Caribou, Laurel, Laurel South - Optional/extra $$$: horseback riding, golf, surfing, ice hockey/skating (all morning, rent out ice 2x a week) - Want to possibly build an indoor gym/field house for next summer Overall impression- camp looked great, well-manicured with beautiful flowers. Very nice facilities. Campers were friendly and seemed engaged. You could give this camp with Birchmont, Laurel, Laurel South & Kohut.

title: Nock-a-Mixon- Allison — 8-1-22
text:
# Nock-a-Mixon- Allison

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Nock-a-Mixon Tour 7/29/22. Toured by Gary Glaser. Stats: Co-ed, 7 weeks ONLY. Currently have a little over 500 campers, 2nd grade–11th, have CIT (call it ACT—associate counselor training) program. Very large group of older kids (46 sixteen-year-olds this summer). They only do one overnight; he said kids want to be at camp. He keeps the kids at camp by scaling all the activities. For example, the rock-climbing wall has a side for younger kids, a more advanced one and then an "impossible" side that the oldest kids try to conquer.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

85% of staff is former campers and 90% of camp return year over year, even international staff. Only had 9 first time counselors which allows him to be very picky with staff. Met one of the first-time counselors and he was lovely. Very large group of older kids (46 sixteen-year-olds this summer). Going into 11th grade campers can join the ACT program and have to go through formal interview process; boys wear ties. Toured by Gary Glaser.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

12–14 kids per cabin, 4 staff in cabin with them until 14 years old, then 3 counselors, then 2 at 16. No AC in bunks (only in office and health center). Do have electricity, exhaust and big fans in each bunk. Toilets and showers in each bunk as well. Laundry is done off-site weekly.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They have two lakes, one has inflatables, canoes, kayaks, paddleboats, something called a corkle. Kids were playing on lake and looked happy. The other lake has jet ski that they use to pull kids around on a tube. They do NOT have waterskiing.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Part structured, part choice (electives—they call hobbies). Swim is mandatory until 13. Do Color War, Olympics and College Day.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

DO have aeroball (very cool trampoline par/game), beautiful new gymnastics space with cool automatic garage doors that allow the space to be opened up on nice days, stunning amphitheater that can fit the whole camp (has movie screen and high-tech features like wireless mics), mini-golf course that he claims is the most difficult ever, driving range, every sport field imaginable. Lots of covered spaces for kids to stay cool and piped water stations and bathrooms all over campus so kids don’t have to hike far or be without. Also have STEM, arts and crafts, woodworking (no power tools), cooking, dance and spin bikes. Loved the cute little nature hut with animals (ducks and pigs this year—the pig was trying to escape while we were there). They also have a garden that the kids can tend to and then gather and cook the food. Other Capital Improvements: Kitchen is a large undertaking but recently added new hockey/indoor soccer court, 2nd story to office, expanded 2 cabins, added pickleball and tennis courts.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Always have a doctor or nurse on staff (Gary’s wife is actually a nurse). Do short 20 minute service on Fridays in both Hebrew and English, blessings before every meal, Shabbat dinner on Fridays. Eat together as a camp. Have separate allergy kitchen and staff and can handle most allergies. Can accommodate a Kosher family. Child will mostly eat vegetarian but he will occasionally get a case of kosher meatballs, hot dogs, turkey, etc., but he doesn’t promise Kosher meat at every meal. Next year he is doubling the size of the kitchen, remodeling gluten-free kitchen as well. Lots of covered spaces for kids to stay cool and piped water stations and bathrooms all over campus so kids don’t have to hike far or be without.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall Thoughts: Gary is clearly very passionate about camp and is always thinking what improvement he can make next. The campers seemed happy and well-cared for. We met a few of the older girls who were all well-spoken about their love of camp. The fact that their campers and staff return year after year says a lot. We also witnessed a staff member handing out her kindness shirt to a camper and it nearly brought me to tears. Gary gives each staff member a kindness t-shirt and they choose a camper who embodies kindness to give it to. It's a very pretty camp and they do a good job keeping kids engaged. It has its fair share of sporty kids but, they have enough to keep even the least athletic kid happy.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Have busses from NYC, White Plains, (2) in NJ, (3) in Philly, (2) in Long Island (Manhasset), MD, chaperoned flights from LA and FL. Get two or three calls home each summer, use CampMinder and have facial recognition software. Not a uniformed camp. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Co-ed, 7 weeks ONLY with a little over 500 campers (2nd grade–11th) and an ACT (associate counselor training) program. Part structured, part choice (they call electives "hobbies"), with swim mandatory until 13 and big events like Color War, Olympics and College Day. Two lakes offer inflatables, canoes, kayaks, paddleboats and tubing behind a jet ski (no waterskiing), and campus highlights include a beautiful new gymnastics space, a stunning amphitheater, mini-golf, a driving range, aeroball and more. The campers seemed happy and well-cared for, and traditions like the kindness t-shirt underscore a warm culture; they eat together as a camp and can accommodate most allergies and a Kosher family.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Co-ed, 7 weeks ONLY; a little over 500 campers, 2nd grade–11th.
  - 85% of staff is former campers and 90% of camp return year over year, even international staff.
  - Two lakes with inflatables, canoes, kayaks, paddleboats, a corkle, and tubing behind a jet ski (no waterskiing).
  - Part structured, part choice (electives—their 'hobbies'); swim is mandatory until 13.
  - Beautiful new gymnastics space, stunning amphitheater, mini-golf, driving range, aeroball, pickleball and tennis courts, new hockey/indoor soccer court.
  - Separate allergy kitchen; can handle most allergies; can accommodate a Kosher family.
  - Kindness t-shirt tradition recognizing campers who embody kindness.
  - Lots of covered spaces, piped water stations, and bathrooms all over campus.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No AC in bunks (only in office and health center).
  - They do NOT have waterskiing.
  - Only do one overnight.
  - Not a uniformed camp.
  - Swim is mandatory until 13.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

For example, the rock-climbing wall has a side for younger kids, a more advanced one and then an “impossible” side that the oldest kids try to conquer. 85% of staff is former campers and 90% of camp return year over year, even international staff. Only had 9 first time counselors which allows him to be very picky with staff. Met one of the first-time counselors and he was lovely. Going into 11 th grade campers can join the ACT program and have to go through formal interview process, boys wear ties. Have busses from NYC, White Plains, (2) in NJ, (3) in Philly, (2) in Long Island (Manhasset), MD, chaperoned flights from LA and FL. Kids were playing on lake and looked happy. The other lake has jet ski that they use to pull kids around on a tube. They do NOT have waterskiing. DO have aeroball (very cool trampoline par/game), beautiful new gymnastics space with cool automatic garage doors that allow the space to be opened up on nice days, stunning ampitheater that can fit the whole camp (has movie screen and high-tech features like wireless mics), mini-golf course that he claims is the most difficult ever, driving range, every sport field imaginable. Lots of covered spaces for kids to stay cool and piped water stations and bathrooms all over campus so kids don’t have to hike far or be without. Also have STEM, arts and crafts, woodworking) no power tools), cooking, dance and spin bikes. They also have a garden that the kids can tend to and then gather and cook the food. We met a few of the older girls who were all well-spoken about their love of camp. The fact that their campers and staff return year after year says a lot. We also witnessed a staff member handing out her kindness shirt to a camper and it nearly brought me to tears. title: Laurel —  8-22-22
text:
# Laurel

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

515 happy campers. FULL season is 6 weeks and few days. No partial seasons. Elective base program: means a lot of choice, everyday. Very manicured camp, flowers and clean. Beautiful water front. Non uniformed.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

International campers from: China, Mexico, Spain, England, France. 85-90% come back next year. Very welcoming with older campers for the first time. Staff experts such as college athletes. Real Chef as a staff for the kitchen activities. Great facility nice staff, competitive dancers.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Indoor facility space. Laundry every week, towels are provided.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Beautiful water front. Swimming.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Elective base program: means a lot of choice, everyday. A B S day: A Monday, Wednesday; B Tuesdays and Thursdays; and S days for some special event days. Tennis and Swimming then they can look at Craft or discovery. Gymnastics / Play is a must and some activities they choose with their parents.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Cooking facilities. Real Chef as a staff for the kitchen activities. Radio Station. Staff experts such as college athletes. Climbing walls are huge. No golfing on campus.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Good Security at the entrance. Doctor lives on camp at the health center. Dining room is assigned already - no one has that moment: "gosh where I am going to sit today". Kitchen smells good, air conditioning inside. Buffet style. No kosher style. Lots of Jewish: jala y matze ball with chicken on Fridays.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Philosophy: setting KIDS for Success. Camp is about growing up and finding a sense of self, where can they feel great. Very manicured camp, flowers and clean. Cheering. Non denominational. Very welcoming with older campers for the first time. Great facility nice staff, competitive dancers.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

International students can leave early. FaceTime 3 times per session with parents. Snail mail encouraged between campers and parents. Camp sends photos to parents through App. Extra fee for horse riding.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Elective base program with A/B/S days and lots of choice every day. FULL season is 6 weeks and a few days (no partial seasons). 515 happy campers, international and non denominational, with 85–90% returning. Beautiful water front, manicured grounds, great facility, nice staff, and a welcoming vibe.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Good Security at the entrance
  - Elective base program: means a lot of choice, everyday
  - Doctor lives on camp at the health center
  - Real Chef as a staff for the kitchen activities
  - Radio Station
  - Climbing walls are huge
  - Very manicured camp, flowers and clean
  - Beautiful water front
  - 85-90% come back next year

  

#### Lowlights

  - No partial seasons
  - No golfing on campus
  - No kosher style
  - Extra fee for horse riding

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

Radio Station, Staff experts such as college athletes Doctor lives on camp at the health center Laundry every week , towels are provided Very manicured camp, flowers and clean. Beautiful water front Non uniformed International campers from: China, Mexico, Spain, England, France. 85-90% come back next year. Very welcoming with older campers for the first time. Extra fee for horse riding No golfing on campus Climbing walls are huge Kitchen smells well, air conditioning inside Buffet style No kosher style Lots of Jewish: jala y matze ball with chicken on Fridays Cheering Non denominational FaceTime 3 times per session with parents Snail mail encouraged between campers and parents Camp send fotos to parents through App.

title: Interlaken —7-22-22
text:
# Interlaken

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Interlaken JCC, Eagle River, WI. Affiliated with the Milwaukee JCC. 221 campers 1st session; 240 campers 2nd session; Approx. 30 stay all summer.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Director: Toni Davison-Levenberg. 30 CITS, 130 Staff. 30-35% from Milwaukee/Madison. 50 campers each session from Chicago; Denver, California, Arizona. Incoming 3rd graders are youngest.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Central wash houses with showers, toilets and sinks - NONE in cabins. All gender bathrooms are available. Utilize pop up changing pods within the cabins.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Pool and Lake. All campers have to take swimming in the pool. Beautiful waterfront. Water skiing, boating/canoeing, fishing, swim area.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

3 choice activities AM; 3 Blocks in PM - choice, swim, cabin time. Switch activities every two weeks (twice a session). All activities are co-ed.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

CIT program (30 CITS). Beautiful waterfront. Water skiing, boating/canoeing, fishing. Shabbat walk, Israeli dancing and song session after dinner.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There is a security gate to get into camp. Kosher food at camp. Fruit out all day. Canteen 2x/week. 2 Diabetic campers in 2022, nurses and doctors on staff.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Shabbat: Friday: Shabbat walk, "pick up each cabin", light candles, 45 minute services and special dinner, Israeli dancing and song session after dinner. Saturday: 10:15-11:30 services, choice activities for the rest of the day.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Interlaken JCC in Eagle River, WI is affiliated with the Milwaukee JCC and serves 221 campers 1st session and 240 campers 2nd session, with Approx. 30 staying all summer. Incoming 3rd graders are youngest, and the community includes 30-35% from Milwaukee/Madison and 50 campers each session from Chicago, plus Denver, California, Arizona. Pool and Lake with a beautiful waterfront: all campers have to take swimming in the pool, and activities include water skiing, boating/canoeing, fishing, and a swim area; all activities are co-ed with 3 choice activities AM and 3 Blocks in PM that switch every two weeks. Kosher food at camp, fruit out all day, canteen 2x/week, a security gate, and nurses and doctors on staff support health and safety, and Shabbat includes a Friday Shabbat walk with candle lighting, services, special dinner, Israeli dancing and song session, with Saturday services and choice activities.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Beautiful waterfront; pool and lake with water skiing, boating/canoeing, fishing.
  - All campers have to take swimming in the pool.
  - 3 choice activities AM; 3 Blocks in PM; switch activities every two weeks.
  - All activities are co-ed.
  - Kosher food at camp; fruit out all day; canteen 2x/week.
  - Shabbat walk, Israeli dancing and song session after dinner.
  - Security gate; nurses and doctors on staff.
  - 30 CITS; 130 Staff.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Central wash houses with showers, toilets and sinks - NONE in cabins.
  - Utilize pop up changing pods within the cabins.

  - URJ Camp Harlam
  - Camp Nah-Jee-Wah
  - Camp Seneca Lake
  - Blue Star Camp
  - Berkshire Hills Eisenberg Camps
  - Round Lake Camp
  - Camp Pembroke

title: Manitou —  8-30-22
text:
# Manitou

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

All boys; 360 campers; 200 staff. Sporty all around, the campers travel through the summer by interest. Color war at this moment happening. Half session.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

32 CIT which were campers for many years. Relation on transparency and trust: the camp director with campers and parents.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins well kept with lots of light. International kids are NOT in the same bunk. Linen provided for internationals campers. “Mum Away” from home service. And clean up professional for toilets and showers twice a week.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Early risers club: with a counselor 6:00 -7:30 am meet in a cabin and go off to play indoor soccer ball, basketball, dodge ball, gaga, cycling, pingpong table, fencing. For the boys who just can’t wait to start the day. If there is a camper that will do it everyday of the summer they get a lobster dinner with the director of the camp. 7:30 Flag every Morning. 8:00 breakfast. 8:30 cleanup. 1 week they try everything and after trying all the sports - they choose what they want to do.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Huge theater - very important to audition and do the play as to perform and decorate the place. TV music production studios very important even if they are older. Pizza oven culinary area. Culinary and Gardening. European hand ball & Rugby (Soccer with you hands). There is also a Camp band. Tennis huge. Street hockey as well on campus. Mountain bikes ridden on 13 acres in the wood.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Milk and cookies as a snack for 2nd to 5 th graders. Hydration stations all over: THEY HAVE sunscreen and bug spray and water.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

It’s a place you are going to Dream the rest of their life. Not for the kid who just wants to hang around. It’s a secular camp; Catholic kids can ask for Sunday service or Friday night service can be provided for Jewish campers. Relation on transparency and trust: the camp director with campers and parents.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Mail emails for parents and campers. Text message for internationals. 15 minutes phone calls. Blog for parents. Crypto currency for every camper so they can exchange if they want anything from the canteen.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

All boys; 360 campers and 200 staff, sporty all around, the campers travel through the summer by interest. Daily rhythm includes Early Risers Club (6:00–7:30 am) for indoor soccer ball, basketball, dodge ball, gaga, cycling, ping pong, and fencing, with 7:30 Flag, 8:00 breakfast, 8:30 cleanup, and after 1 week they try everything then choose what they want to do. Huge theater, TV music production studios, a pizza oven culinary area (Culinary and Gardening), tennis huge, European hand ball & Rugby, street hockey, mountain bikes ridden on 13 acres in the wood, and there is also a Camp band. Not for the kid who just wants to hang around; it’s a secular camp with optional Sunday or Friday night services, strong hydration and sun/bug protection, and active parent communication.

  

### Internal Insights

  - There is also a Camp band.

  - Milk and cookies as a snack for 2nd to 5 th graders.
  - Hydration stations All over: THEY HAVE sunscreen and bug spray and water.

  - Cabins well kept with lots of light.
  - Linen provided for internationals campers.
  - “Mum Away” from home service.
  - And clean up professional for toilets and showers twice a week.
  - Relation on transparency and trust: the camp director with campers and parents.

#### Lowlights

# Ivy Bound

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Ivy Bound now has live online Academic Help for our tutoring students\! We meet at 7:15p eastern each Tuesday for English, and 7:15p eastern Thursday for Math and Science. There is no additional fee for Ivy Bound students. These drop-in sessions are held on www.Ivybound.GoSchoolBox.com . Student alone or student-with-parent can log into Help Line for homework help.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Info for Grades 5 - 9. Student alone or student-with-parent can log into Help Line for homework help. An Ivy Bound tutor should be able to answer and explain most or all student questions and possibly give advice for tackling similar tough problems.

Unknown

Unknown

Tuesdays (English) and Thursdays (Math & Science) 7:15pm – 8:05pm Eastern. We meet at 7:15p eastern each Tuesday for English, and 7:15p eastern Thursday for Math and Science. In the absence of student questions, the tutor still will have 20 - 25 minutes of helpful "lecture and problem-solving". The ideal is to have a photo or an online document that shows the full problem, and upload it as you start the session.

There is no additional fee for Ivy Bound students. An Ivy Bound tutor should be able to answer and explain most or all student questions and possibly give advice for tackling similar tough problems. Drop-in sessions for Grades 5 - 9.

Unknown

Please contact Ivy Bound to become an academic tutoring client or if already an academic client, to have your email and your student's email added to the sessions. There is no additional fee for Ivy Bound students.

title: WALT WHITMAN — 9-16-24
text:
# WALT WHITMAN

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Natasha, Emily, Lindsey and Allison toured Walt Whitman on July 29, 2024. We were met by Directors Jed and Carolyn Dorfman, and toured by Carolyn. Jed and Carolyn live in Larchmont in the off-season. Walt was maxed this summer with 425 campers; 60% from New York City, Brooklyn, Westchester; small number from Connecticut and New Jersey; Carolyn said there were only 2 Long Island families currently. 4 or 3 week sessions are options - Carolyn said you can do it a 'few times' but it’s a 7wk camp. Programming: 6 periods per day by gender and age, with a weekly elective in the AM and a daily choice for period 4. An alpha sport kid would not do well here, but kids have to participate and like sports. Finishing 4th try to keep the kids together. Finishing 8th/9th mix the bunks; everything is choice at that point. New pickle ball this summer. New dining hall for 2026 (construction starts the day after camp ends 2025). Intercamps with Moose, Kingswood, Pemi, Wico, Merriwood, Robindel.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Directors Jed and Carolyn Dorfman; toured by Carolyn. Jed and Carolyn live in Larchmont in the off-season. 70% American 30% International. 70% Jewish. Have to take 2 years off to be counselors. Finishing 8th don’t see looking at Walt. Campers we met were super happy, kind and down to Earth.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Travel by bunk. 3 bunks by division - 8-10 kids per bunk. Shower houses on both sides, plus showers in bunk rooms. For older campers: one big bunk divided into 4 parts - all connected. Some of the bunks have balconies that look out on the lake.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Heated pool. Instructional swim finishing 6th in pool. Water ski is on Tilton lake near Kingswood - they take a van there. They take time to have LAMs - lake appreciate moments.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 6 periods per day by gender and age. There is a weekly elective in the AM, and then a daily choice for period 4. Finishing 4th try to keep the kids together. Finishing 8th/9th mix the bunks; everything is choice at that point. An alpha sport kid would not do well here, but kids have to participate and like sports.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

1st session they have Halloween; 2nd session they have color war. Dance - mostly TikTok dances - but don’t post. 7 shows each summer - every age group does a show. Overnight camping program starts early - age 7. Hiking trips are coed; brother/sister bunks do the hiking trips and meals together. Intercamps with Moose, Kingswood, Pemi, Wico, Merriwood, Robindel. Cute Walt things: they take time to have LAMs - lake appreciate moments - and they have a 'tooth club' (when campers lose teeth at camp - once a week they tally it up - in competition with other camps in the area). New pickle ball this summer.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There was a manned gate upon arrival and a state police officer stationed on the road as we drove in. Health Center - 6 nurses, a trainer and rotating doctor weekly - 2 student nurses. Have a psychologist/SW - mostly for staff. Optional Friday service. 70% Jewish; 100 show up for optional Shabbat. Dining Hall and coed: Coed tables at meals assigned. Allergy - everything up on a stage so if there’s any food for allergies, dairy free, gluten free, they’ll go up to the stage in the dining hall.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Campers we met were super happy, kind and down to Earth. Optional Friday service; 100 show up for optional Shabbat. Dance is mostly TikTok dances - but don’t post. An alpha sport kid would not do well here.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Seven buses from Purchase, NY (sit with bunk) and one from Boston. Chaperoned flights from the DC, LA, Austin. Extra $ - Transportation, horseback riding, private tennis lessons. Camping trips – biking trips / Maine trip is extra. Photos posted daily; photographers are assigned an age group. Staff lounge has WiFi - only place on camp.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Walt was maxed this summer with 425 campers; 60% from New York City, Brooklyn, Westchester, with a small number from Connecticut and New Jersey. It’s a 7wk camp with 4 or 3 week sessions as options; programming is 6 periods per day by gender and age, with a weekly elective in the AM and a daily choice for period 4. An alpha sport kid would not do well here, but kids have to participate and like sports. Campers we met were super happy, kind and down to Earth.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Manned gate upon arrival and a state police officer stationed on the road.
  - Maxed this summer with 425 campers; strong NYC/Westchester presence.
  - Weekly elective and daily choice; everything is choice for oldest campers.
  - New pickle ball this summer.
  - New dining hall for 2026 (construction starts the day after camp ends 2025).
  - Health Center: 6 nurses, a trainer, rotating doctor weekly, plus 2 student nurses; psychologist/SW mostly for staff.
  - Heated pool; instructional swim finishing 6th; water ski on Tilton Lake (van transport).
  - Intercamps with Moose, Kingswood, Pemi, Wico, Merriwood, Robindel.
  - Photos posted daily; photographers are assigned an age group.
  - Optional Friday service; 70% Jewish; 100 show up for optional Shabbat.

  

#### Lowlights

  - An alpha sport kid would not do well here.
  - Staff lounge has WiFi - the only place on camp.
  - Extra costs: transportation, horseback riding, private tennis lessons, camping trips (biking trips / Maine trip).
  - Have to take 2 years off to be counselors.
  - Finishing 8th don’t see looking at Walt.
  - Only 2 Long Island families currently.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Walden NY
  - Echo Lake
  - Watitoh
  - Taconic

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Natasha, Emily, Lindsey and Allison toured Walt Whitman on July 29, 2024 There was a manned gate upon arrival and a state police officer stationed on the road as we drove in. We were met by Directors Jed and Carolyn Dorfman, and toured by Carolyn. Jed and Carolyn live in Larchmont in the off-season. Walt was maxed this summer with 425 campers 60% from New York City, Brooklyn, Westchester Small number from Connecticut and New Jersey Seven buses from Purchase, NY (sit with bunk) and one from Boston Chaperoned flights from the DC,LA, Austin Carolyn said there were only 2 Long Island families currently 4 or 3 week sessions are options- Carolyn said you can do it a “few times” but it’s a 7wk camp Finishing 4th try to keep the kids together Finishing 8th/9th mix the bunks Everything is choice at that point - one big bunk divided into 4 parts- all connected Shower houses on both sides Plus showers in bunk rooms Have to take 2 years off to be counselors 70% American 30% International 1st session they have Halloween 2 nd session they have color war Staff lounge has WiFi- only place on camp New pickle ball this summer New dining hall for 2026 (construction starts the day after camp ends 2025) Programming: 6 periods per day By gender and age There is a weekly elective in the AM And then a daily choice for period 4 An alpha sport kid would not do well here, but kids have to participate and like sports Travel by bunk 3 bunks by division - 8-10 kids per bunk Overnight camping program starts early - age 7 Finishing 8th don’t see looking at Walt Extra $- Transportation, horseback riding, private tennis lessons Camping trips – biking trips / Maine trip is extra Photos posted daily Photographers are assigned an age group Intercamps with Moose, Kingswood, Pemi, Wico, Merriwood, Robindel Dance - mostly TikTok dances - but don’t post 7 shows each summer- every age group does a show Health Center - 6 nurses, a trainer and rotating doctor weekly - 2 student nurses Have a psychologist/SW- mostly for staff Optional Friday service 70% Jewish 100 show up for optional Shabbat Dining Hall and coed: Coed tables at meals assigned Allergy-everything up on a stage so if there’s any food for allergies, dairy free, gluten free, they’ll go up to the stage in the dining hall Hiking trips are coed Brother/ sister bunks do the hiking trips and meals together Heated pool Instructional swim finishing 6th in pool Water ski is on Tilton lake near Kingswood-they take a van there Cute Walt things: they take time to have Lams- lake appreciate moments b/c some of the bunks have balconies that look out on the lake They have a “tooth club”- when campers lose teeth at camp- once a week they tally it up-in competition with other camps in the area Families touring Walt also touring-Walden NY, Echo Lake, Watitoh, Taconic Campers we met were super happy, kind and down to Earth.

title: Nah-Jee-Wah — 8-11-22
text:
# Nah-Jee-Wah

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Lana and Natasha toured Nah-Jee-Wah, Cedar Lake and Round Lake on 8/10/22. We were toured by Carrie Youngs-director. Camp offers 12-day, 3, 4, 6, 7 week sessions. 40% of campers stay full summer. We were there the last week of camp and they were down to 160 kids. They have about 360 kids in their biggest session. Nah-Jee-Wah is for campers up to and including 6th grade.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We were toured by Carrie Youngs-director. Kids mainly come from NJ/NYC/LI. Generally 12 kids/4 counselors per bunk. Boys bunks for 3rd and 4th grade have a female counselor that travels with them and they are considering adding this for 5th grade boys too. There is an incentive program for kids to keep their bunks clean.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

There are several horseshoes of bunks on camp divided by age. The bunks were mostly renovated to knotty pine walls and nicer bathrooms but the outside could use a coat of paint and some new wooden steps. Generally 12 kids/4 counselors per bunk. Boys bunks for 3rd and 4th grade have a female counselor that travels with them and they are considering adding this for 5th grade boys too. The bunks were very clean.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is a small man-made lake with a few water toys, paddleboards, kayaks. No waterskiing but they do banana boat 1x/week. There is also a pool.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

There are 6 periods a day, 2 of which are electives. One of the scheduled periods is instructional swim in either the lake or pool, one is instructional athletics. There are LOTS of electives on camp including pottery wheel and hand ceramics, woodshop, painting, chess, cooking, rocketry, science, lego robotics and coding, 3d printing. There was a high and low ropes course. There was a skate park but it was closed for the summer because it did not pass inspection. The athletics fields are across a main road from the rest of camp and staff crosses the kids. The fields are nice; some have lights. They have gymnastics and dance on that side of the camp as well. They offer baseball, soccer, tennis, lacrosse, basketball.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have a 1 week intensive program where campers can pay an extra $350 to have specialized sports instruction from the Red Bulls, Nets, Israeli basketball team. Some of the projects the kids work on have religious meaning such as matzah and challah covers in art, menorahs in ceramics. The kids do an overnight on camp. There were no trips or intercamps this year. They would like to get back to intercamps but they likely won’t do trips until the kids get to Cedar Lake.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

We parked outside a gate (newly added to camp 5 weeks ago) and called Carrie to let us into camp. They do have a guard at another post at camp. The athletics fields are across a main road from the rest of camp and staff crosses the kids. Camp is kosher. Breakfast and lunch are dairy and dinner is meat. About 10% of campers keep kosher at home. The motzi is said before meals. Camp had never been nut-free but the nutfree policy was put into place halfway through this summer. That may change for next year.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Each week at camp they select a different Jewish value to teach campers and integrate informally into camp. There is a Friday night and Saturday morning service. They are about 45 minutes long and held in an outdoor amphitheater. They described the service as low key and filled with dancing and singing. She said they don’t compete with more religious camps.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

They offer buses from tristate but said most families have been driving to camp to save on the transportation fee. They do offer scholarships to families in need. There is no sibling discount offered. Campers email home 1x/week. There were no trips or intercamps this year; they likely won’t do trips until the kids get to Cedar Lake. Tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Nah-Jee-Wah is for campers up to and including 6th grade and offers 12-day, 3, 4, 6, and 7 week sessions. There are 6 periods a day, 2 of which are electives, with LOTS of electives on camp and instructional swim and athletics. Camp is kosher, each week they select a different Jewish value to teach campers and integrate informally into camp, and there is a Friday night and Saturday morning service that is low key and filled with dancing and singing. Facilities include a small man-made lake and a pool, high and low ropes, strong athletics across a main road, and renovated bunks with knotty pine walls and nicer bathrooms.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - LOTS of electives on camp including pottery wheel and hand ceramics, woodshop, painting, chess, cooking, rocketry, science, lego robotics and coding, 3d printing.
  - High and low ropes course.
  - 1 week intensive program (extra $350) with specialized sports instruction from the Red Bulls, Nets, Israeli basketball team.
  - Renovated bunks to knotty pine walls and nicer bathrooms; bunks were very clean.
  - Small man-made lake with water toys, paddleboards, kayaks, plus a pool; banana boat 1x/week.
  - Jewish values integrated weekly; Friday night and Saturday morning services are low key and filled with dancing and singing.
  - Fields are nice; some have lights.
  - Kids mainly come from NJ/NYC/LI.
  - They do offer scholarships to families in need.

  

#### Lowlights

  - The athletics fields are across a main road from the rest of camp and staff crosses the kids.
  - Skate park was closed for the summer because it did not pass inspection.
  - Camp had never been nut-free but the nutfree policy was put into place halfway through this summer; that may change for next year.
  - The outside of bunks could use a coat of paint and some new wooden steps.
  - There were no trips or intercamps this year; trips likely wait until Cedar Lake.
  - No waterskiing.
  - There is no sibling discount offered.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Silver Lake
  - Chipinaw
  - Canadensis
  - Echo

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

One of the scheduled periods is instructional swim in either the lake or pool, one is instructional athletics. There are LOTS of electives on camp including pottery wheel and hand ceramics, woodshop, painting, chess, cooking, rocketry,science, lego robotics and coding, 3d printing. title: Rafa Nadal Academy —1-28-23
text:
# Rafa Nadal Academy

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Sales - Rafa Nadal Academy by Movistar (Rafa Nadal Academy by Movistar) 27 ene 2023, 12:19 CET.  
The New Summer Camp Tennis & Language is ideal for true tennis lovers who also wish to improve their level of English/Spanish with experienced native teachers.  
It will take place from 18th June to 27th August 2023 and you will be able to choose as many weeks as you wish (subject to availability). Check-in and check-out will be on Sunday.  
It is a program for players from 8 to 18 years old.  
The players will be able to train with the Rafa Nadal Academy by Movistar training system and improve technique, physical condition and skills on court while enjoying multiple recreational activities in an incomparable setting like Mallorca.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Players: 8 to 18 years old; young people from all over the world will coexist.  
Language teachers: experienced native teachers.  
Daily classes taught by the teaching team of the Rafa Nadal International School, a bilingual school located in the same facilities as the Academy.  
Thanks to sessions given by teachers from the Rafa Nadal Academy by Movistar, young tennis players will receive training related to nutrition, physical preparation and values such as effort, humility, tolerance, patience, respect and integrity.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodation in the Supervised Residence within the Academy\*.  
Breakfast and dinner\*.  
Lunch and snacks.  
\* Only included for players staying at the Academy's supervised residence.  
The Summer Camp Tennis & Language is also available as No Boarding (does not include accommodation, breakfast, dinner, transfer service to and from the airport and medical insurance; it does include lunch with the rest of the players and the snacks we serve throughout the day).

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Program available from 18th June to 27th August 2023; Sundays are check in and check out days. You will be able to choose as many weeks as you wish (subject to availability).  
12.5 hours per week of tennis training. Ratio court/player 1:4.  
Tennis competition on Saturdays.  
8 hours a week of English/Spanish classes with native teachers.  
10 hours per week of sports activities / physical preparation.  
5 hours per week of "Building a Champion" activities.  
8 hours per week of leisure and recreational activities in Mallorca.  
BBQ dinner and trophy presentation on Saturday night.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Train with the Rafa Nadal Academy by Movistar training system and improve technique, physical condition and skills on court while enjoying multiple recreational activities in an incomparable setting like Mallorca.  
Simultaneously improve English/Spanish with daily classes taught by the teaching team of the Rafa Nadal International School (bilingual school located in the same facilities as the Academy).  
“Building a Champion” program: sessions on nutrition, physical preparation and values such as effort, humility, tolerance, patience, respect and integrity.  
Guided visit to Rafa Nadal Museum.  
Welcome pack.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical care and physiotherapy service (if necessary)\*.  
Medical insurance with Mapfre for No Boarding has an additional cost of 50,00 € per week per player. If you do not book this medical insurance with us, please note, the week before the arrival date, it is mandatory to send a copy of the player’s private insurance card to Players Desk (playersdesk@rafanadalacademy.com). It is not possible to book a Junior Program No Boarding without a private medical insurance.  
Breakfast and dinner\*; lunch and snacks included.  
\* Only included for players staying at the Academy's supervised residence.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

A unique opportunity for tennis, fun, leisure and values to come together through a campus where young people from all over the world will coexist.  
Incomparable setting like Mallorca.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transfer service to and from the airport\*.  
\* Only included for players staying at the Academy's supervised residence.  
The Summer Camp Tennis & Language is also available as No Boarding (does not include accommodation, breakfast, dinner, transfer service to and from the airport and medical insurance; it does include lunch and snacks).  
Please send: Chosen week; number of Junior Players (if more than one, please indicate if they are brothers / sisters); Boarding (with accommodation) or No Boarding (without accommodation) so we can send you a quotation.  
If you prefer a phone call to explain you the details of the program, please send your telephone number (including country code) and your country of residence. We will contact you as soon as possible.  
At the end of this e-mail you have the brochure attached.  
Parent communication and tech: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

The New Summer Camp Tennis & Language is ideal for true tennis lovers who also wish to improve their level of English/Spanish. It runs weekly from 18th June to 27th August 2023 with Sunday check-in and check-out for players ages 8 to 18. Players train with the Rafa Nadal Academy by Movistar training system, attend daily language classes, and enjoy sports, leisure, and “Building a Champion” activities in Mallorca. Boarding includes the supervised residence, breakfast and dinner, airport transfers, and medical care/physiotherapy; a No Boarding option is available with required private medical insurance or optional Mapfre insurance.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Unknown

  

#### Lowlights

  - Unknown

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Runoia — 8-30-22

# Runoia

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Just Girls. 150 campers. Sessions: 3 and a half week; 6 and a half week. Oldest camp in the country. Almost full for next season: maybe a few spaces open in October 2022. Enviado desde mi iPhone.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

7-8 years to 15 age of campers; 16 training programe for counselor. Counselors are instructors too in the camp. Suburban Maryland, New Jersey.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Nice cabins with curtains. 12 campers and 4 counselors per cabin.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront quite fun: water-skiing, paddle board, canoeing, windsurfing, swimming lessons. There were 90 campers out to a beach.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Horses. Archery & Rifles. Great arts and crafts. Kids like the art at camp.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

3 Nurses on campus. Very small dining, very cosy, and Mexican music at the kitchen.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Run by woman; great power woman feel. Progressive minded / aware and liberal. Kindness. It´s a rustic camp. Home like Camp. Jewish population Looking for a non Jewish camp.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Internationals: France Mexico UK Brasil Argentina Colombia arrived 1 day before camp starts. Logan airport in Boston. Portland maybe, and chaperone service are available. No Uniform at camp but yes for outcome trip. Low Technology play but fun and inclusive with all who want to participate.

It’s a safe space for building confidence; will enable the camper to grow as a person where counselors are instructors too in the camp.
