title: WHERE THERE BE DRAGONS — EMILY — 10-18-22
text:
# WHERE THERE BE DRAGONS

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Founded in 1993, in its 30th year. Based out of Boulder, Colorado providing immersive learning for high school summer programs, gap year programs, adult programs and partnering schools. Where There Be Dragons is an experiential approach to learning. Summer programs tend to last 4 or 6 weeks. Gap year can get college credit through Portland State. Each program has a theme such as marine conservation, social justice, sustainable agriculture. None of the trips are to tourist destinations. Travel includes places like China, Asia, Latin America, Africa and the United States. Trips include Indonesia, Cambodia, Morocco, Taiwan, Bhutan, Thailand, Nepal, Bolivia, Guatemala and others. Have not returned to China since pandemic. The 9 components of every Where There Be Dragons program is as follows: Rugged Travel, Homestays, Language Study, Trekking, Learning Service, Independent Study, Comparative Religion/Spiritual, Development Studies, Focus of Inquiry.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Must have finished first year of high school. Attracts curious, motivated students who want to learn about the world and learn more about themselves. Instructors are experts who create the course. There are 3 instructors to 12 students. They try to have a good ratio of boys to girls. Students are from all over including international. Alex is based in Tucson, Arizona.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

They start in eco lodges then progress to homestays. The gap year trips always include homestays. USA programs do not include homestay.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The trip always starts out with icebreakers/team building. Where There Be Dragons is an experiential approach to learning. Most assignments are reflection papers. Each program has a theme such as marine conservation, social justice, sustainable agriculture. The 9 components include Independent Study.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

The trips are unfiltered and immersed in the local culture. None of the trips are to tourist destinations. Travel includes places like China, Asia, Latin America, Africa and the United States. Trips include Indonesia, Cambodia, Morocco, Taiwan, Bhutan, Thailand, Nepal, Bolivia, Guatemala and others. The USA Gap Semester is in Rio Grande which includes Texas, Arizona and New Mexico. The Summer program is Colorado, Lake Superior and British Columbia and focuses mostly on outdoor wilderness exploration. The 9 components of every Where There Be Dragons program: Rugged Travel, Homestays, Language Study, Trekking, Learning Service, Independent Study, Comparative Religion/Spiritual, Development Studies, Focus of Inquiry.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There is always risk management to make sure the students are safe. Food: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The theme of Where There be Dragons is cultural immersion, personal growth, self-reflection. The trips are unfiltered and immersed in the local culture. Attracts curious, motivated students who want to learn about the world and learn more about themselves.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Students interested can call the office and speak to someone on the team. Then fill out an application which is rolling admissions and includes a 30 min interview and $850 deposit. The trips are mostly digital free. Occasionally, kids can text their families. Families can check the Yak Board which posts what the students are doing and pictures. Programs tend to fill up by March. Website: Dragons | Immersive Travel Abroad Programs For Youth & Adults (wheretherebedragons.com). Alex Biddle is at alex@wheretherebedragons.com. WhatsApp: 330-614-1384.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Founded in 1993 and based in Boulder, Colorado, Where There Be Dragons provides immersive learning for high school summer programs, gap year programs, adult programs and partnering schools. Programs are experiential, unfiltered, and immersed in the local culture with themes such as marine conservation, social justice, and sustainable agriculture. Summer programs tend to last 4 or 6 weeks with 3 instructors to 12 students and most assignments are reflection papers; trips are mostly digital free. None of the trips are to tourist destinations, and there is always risk management to make sure the students are safe.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Founded in 1993; immersive, experiential approach with cultural immersion, personal growth, self-reflection.
  - 3 instructors to 12 students.
  - Gap year can get college credit through Portland State.
  - Homestays (gap year always; most programs progress from eco lodges to homestays).
  - Nine core components: Rugged Travel, Homestays, Language Study, Trekking, Learning Service, Independent Study, Comparative Religion/Spiritual, Development Studies, Focus of Inquiry.
  - Trips include Indonesia, Cambodia, Morocco, Taiwan, Bhutan, Thailand, Nepal, Bolivia, Guatemala and others.
  - USA Gap Semester: Rio Grande (Texas, Arizona, New Mexico); USA Summer: Colorado, Lake Superior, British Columbia; outdoor wilderness exploration.
  - Mostly digital free with Yak Board updates; programs tend to fill up by March.

  

#### Lowlights

  - USA programs do not include homestay.
  - Have not returned to China since pandemic.
  - Trips are mostly digital free.
  - None of the trips are to tourist destinations.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Founded in 1993, in its 30 th year. Based out of Boulder, Colorado providing immersive learning for high school summer programs, gap year programs, adult programs and partnering schools. Alex is based in Tuscon, Arizona. Travel includes places like China, Asia, Latin America, Africa and the United States. Summer programs tend to last 4 or 6 weeks. Must have finished first year of high school. Gap year can get college credit through Portland State. None of the trips are to tourist destinations. The gap year trips always include homestays. The theme of Where There be Dragons, is cultural immersion, personal growth, self-reflection. Attracts curious, motivated students who want to learn about the world and learn more about themselves. The trips are “unfliltered” and immersed in the local culture. Instructors are experts who create the course. There are 3 instructors to 12 students. Where There Be Dragons is an experiential approach to learning. Most assignments are reflection papers. Each program has a theme such as marine conservation, social justice, sustainable agriculture. The trip always starts out with icebreakers/team building. They start in eco lodges then progress to homestays. There is always risk management to make sure the students are safe. The 9 components of every Where There Be Dragons program is as follows: -Rugged Travel -Homestays -Language Study -Trekking -Learning Service -Independent Study -Comparative Religion/Spiritual -Development Studies -Focus of Inquiry Trips include Indonesia, Cambodia, Morocco, Taiwan, Bhutan, Thailand, Nepal, Bolivia, Guatemala and others. Have not returned to China since pandemic. Students interested can call the office and speak to someone on the team. Then fill out an application which is rolling admissions and includes a 30 min interview and $850 deposit. The trips are mostly digital free. Occasionally, kids can text their families. Families can check the Yak Board which posts what the students are doing and pictures. They try to have a good ratio of boys to girls. The USA Gap Semester is in Rio Grande which includes Texas, Arizona and New Mexico. The Summer program is Colorado, Lake Superior and British Columbia and focuses mostly on outdoor wilderness exploration. USA programs do not include homestay. Students are from all over including international. There is a program comparison chart on the website that compares the trips. Website: Dragons | Immersive Travel Abroad Programs For Youth & Adults (wheretherebedragons.com) Alex Biddle is at alex@wheretherebedragons.com Whats App: 330-614-1384 Programs tend to fill up by March.

title: Robindel — Shari — 9-1-22
text:
# Robindel

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

2022 write up: Shari, Lisa B, Beth & Jennifer toured by Jolly Corley. Jolly been at camp since 2008, directing with Cori since 2011; now under new ownership (not alum) and they will remain the directors for next 5 (hopefully more) years. New ownership giving major operational budget and capital improvement budgets (new welcome center for 2023, ropes course completed, lodge attached to dining hall redone; also some invisible improvements—roofs, septic, etc). Very traditional w/ camp history, fireplace, books, have b’day parties. Please see CE Remote for additional information for completed questionnaire by director.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

About 140 full summer campers in total (they cap at 225); 153 campers in first session; 120 staff. Tri-state about 30% w/ most from NYC. 30–35% alum families. Campers from FL, CA, Midwest, MA, DC/MD, etc. Brothers at Winaukee (about 20) & Cedar mostly but also Greylock, Takajo (4), Skylemar, Andro. Jolly been at camp since 2008, directing with Cori since 2011; under new ownership (not alum) they will remain the directors for next 5 (hopefully more) years.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations—spacious, dark-ish, w/ new bathrooms. Girls make own beds so they don’t bring a lot of 'stuff.' They don’t keep the bunks the same each year but have grandfathered some of the older groups due to the changes happening at camp last 2 years. Lodge attached to dining hall redone, very traditional w/ camp history, fireplace, books; have b’day parties.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

On Lake Winnipesaukee. 8/9/10th graders not getting swim instruction. Girls take swim daily. Water skiing is one of the most popular activities.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

3p in am, 3p in pm, 50mins each. Move w/ division. Girls take swim and tennis daily. Up to into 5th pick last period each day; into 6th & 7th pick 2 periods; into 8/9/10th pick all each week.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Most popular activities are tennis, water skiing & arts and crafts. SPORTS—intercamps w/ Wicosuta, Evergreen, Mataponi, Robin Hood, Birchmont and Merriwood. Horses on property (about 40 girls ride). CREATIVE—very bright and organized, all in one building, 2 levels w/ garage doors that make it feel open and airy. Cooking done in dining hall and they were making challahs for shabbat since it was a Friday, but they make other items and will expand the program. Performing arts and gymnastics in another building w/ stage; campers sit on spring floor to watch play (Matilda and a musical review this year) so gymnastics equipment moved…a bit messy but spacious and they make do. TRIPS—Youngest do one mandatory overnight, off-site. Hikes optional, about 12–15 girls participate per hike. Also some day trips (polar caves, and others listed in questionnaire). ORIENTEERING—brand new and beautiful in center of camp: high, low, zip-line, climbing wall, giant swing, tree house. Ropes course completed.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL/EMOTIONAL—on camp w/ Dr. Leiber (ER doctor), a parent who is orthopedist and one parent who is internal med/pediatrics (rotating). Also do telehealth appts. Hospitals: Laconia, Plymouth and Dartmouth (depending on what is needed will depend on which hospital they use). Big sisters assigned by geographic location (home), challenges they may have that may relate to another girl similarly, and each girl has 2–3 big sisters at camp. DINING—Tues & Sun eat outside. Tables change weekly, assigned w/in age group. Peanut products re-introduced in 2022, separate area in dining hall, but still tree-nut free. Can do peanut desensitization therapy.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

RELIGIOUS LIFE—new outdoor stage by water where shabbat occurs. Values (this week was Respect) and prayers; wear white; 90% Jewish campership. Per Jolly: they are nurturing personal growth and have complete transparency. She could not emphasize enough that the values of Robindel will remain the same including Tradition, Respect, Honesty, Empathy, Friendship and Spirit and they will live by those values tried and true. When asked about the "alum mom who wants this or that special treatment for her daughter" her answer was "we have had a couple of those already and we have said NO to their requests. If we make an exception for one, we would have to do for all and we are trying to be fair to everyone." 1/2 of these girls came back b/c the other 1/2 were not (they weren’t good girls) and Jolly feels that this group is very welcoming. We were all REALLY happy with what Jolly was doing with camp and left with complete trust and faith in her (and Cori’s) leadership, energy and love of camp.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Canteen: at stage by water, 2x/week; bring a letter for home. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication (beyond letters for canteen): Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

New ownership is giving major operational budget and capital improvement budgets (new welcome center for 2023, ropes course completed, lodge attached to dining hall redone) while Jolly (at camp since 2008, directing with Cori since 2011) continues to lead. Programming is 3p in am and 3p in pm (50mins each), girls take swim and tennis daily, and choice increases by age. On Lake Winnipesaukee with intercamp sports, horses on property, bright creative spaces, and a brand new orienteering/ropes area, the most popular activities are tennis, water skiing & arts and crafts. A 13year-old will do fine at Robindel, and we left with complete trust and faith in her (and Cori’s) leadership, energy and love of camp.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Jolly been at camp since 2008, directing with Cori since 2011; they will remain the directors for next 5 (hopefully more) years.
  - New ownership giving major operational budget and capital improvement budgets (new welcome center for 2023; lodge attached to dining hall redone; invisible improvements—roofs, septic).
  - On Lake Winnipesaukee; water skiing popular; girls take swim and tennis daily.
  - Strong activity mix: intercamp sports; horses on property (about 40 girls ride); bright and organized creative building; performing arts and gymnastics; brand new orienteering (high, low, zip-line, climbing wall, giant swing, tree house).
  - Clear values and culture: Shabbat on new outdoor stage by water; 90% Jewish campership; nurturing personal growth and complete transparency.
  - Medical coverage on camp w/ ER doctor and rotating parent specialists; telehealth appts; hospitals in Laconia, Plymouth and Dartmouth.
  - Dining flexibility: Tues & Sun eat outside; tables change weekly; tree-nut free with a separate peanut area; can do peanut desensitization therapy.
  - Big sisters assigned by geographic location/challenges (2–3 big sisters per camper).

  

#### Lowlights

  - 8/9/10th graders not getting swim instruction.
  - Performing arts and gymnastics building can be a bit messy but spacious and they make do.
  - Accommodations described as spacious, dark-ish.
  - They don’t keep the bunks the same each year (some older groups grandfathered).
  - New ownership (not alum) may be a change for some families.
  - Peanut products re-introduced in 2022 (separate area; still tree-nut free).
  - 1/2 of these girls came back b/c the other 1/2 were not (they weren’t good girls).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Wicosuta
  - Evergreen
  - Mataponi
  - Robin Hood
  - Birchmont
  - Merriwood

title: WALDEN (MICHIGAN) — MARGARET — 8-10-17
text:
# WALDEN (MICHIGAN)

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

ACA constantly awarding 100%, highly accredited. 250 campers, co-ed - 60% Michigan, then Chicago, Cleveland, Dallas, Florida… few from all over including CA & International. 2 week sessions, 4 weeks, 8 weeks.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by Liz & Scott - owners/directors. Liz is second generation owner. 4 units each side (girls/boys) rising 3-4th, 5-6th, 7-8th, 9-11th. 16 yrs oldest camper then required to take 2 yrs off before applying as staff (10%). Priority if hiring staff is based on what they teach, what skill they will lead. Ex: must be lifeguard certified to be on waterfront. Cabin life training second step: Expert online training tools and motivational speakers. Beg/Intermediate level sports, college players teach. 84% J+.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins have bathrooms/showers within (12 campers per).

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront director has been there 35 years, always on cutting edge ex: aqua buddy to start new skiers w/ease. Must pass swim test for waterfront activities but no requirement for lessons afterwards. All water sports offered, sailing is huge. Waterfront is beautiful, lake is not private, but extremely quiet & clean.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Activities selected within 2 week sessions: 8 activities: 4 A-day, 4 B-day (2 morn, 2 aft-hour & 1/2 each) and 2 specials - can sign up for any choice. No cabin activity except some eve programs by cabin, otherwise all campers in activity groups.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Tennis competition vs. Belvedere local country club. Archery has advanced trip. Overnights optional. 23 horses, English & Western. Adopt-a-horse program for summer, grooming. Gardening class field trip to local farmers market. Animal Care popular activity - baby animals on farm - has goats, chickens, bunnies, cats… kids love, great for shy. Tree climbing with billet, no ropes, zip line at Millcreek. Newspaper every day, Radio… 4 weeks musical (Shrek).

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

No nuts, can accommodate all allergies dairy/egg/gluten free options.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

WALDEN “TRUE”: TRUST, RESPECT, UNITY, EMPATHY: Circles entire curriculum, girls/boys middle school & bullying emphasis w/staff training.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Horses extra $250/2 weeks. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Other extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

ACA constantly awarding 100%, highly accredited, co-ed camp of 250 campers on a beautiful, extremely quiet & clean lake (not private). Sessions are 2, 4 or 8 weeks, with campers choosing 8 activities (4 A-day, 4 B-day) plus 2 specials, and no cabin activity except some evening programs by cabin. Waterfront is a standout (all water sports offered, sailing is huge), led by a director there 35 years; must pass swim test for waterfront activities. Program highlights include tennis competition vs. Belvedere, an archery advanced trip, optional overnights, a robust riding program (23 horses, English & Western), Animal Care with baby animals, Newspaper every day and a 4 weeks musical; no nuts and can accommodate all allergies.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - ACA constantly awarding 100%, highly accredited.
  - Waterfront director has been there 35 years; all water sports offered, sailing is huge.
  - Cabins have bathrooms/showers within (12 campers per).
  - Strong choice-based schedule: 8 activities (A/B days) + 2 specials.
  - No nuts, can accommodate all allergies (dairy/egg/gluten free).
  - Values-based WALDEN “TRUE”: TRUST, RESPECT, UNITY, EMPATHY.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Lake is not private.
  - Must pass swim test for waterfront activities but no requirement for lessons afterwards.
  - Beg/Intermediate level sports (may not suit advanced athletes).
  - Tree climbing with billet, no ropes.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Toured by Liz & Scott- owners/directors. Liz is second generation owner ACA constantly awarding 100%, highly accredited 250 campers, co-ed-60% Michigan, then Chicago, Cleveland, Dallas, Florida…few from all over including CA & International 84% J+ Cabins have bathrooms/showers within (12 campers per) 2 week sessions, 4 weeks, 8 weeks Activities selected within 2 week sessions: 8 activities: 4 A-day, 4 B-day (2 morn, 2 aft-hour & 1/2 each) and 2 specials-can sign up for any choice No cabin activity except some eve programs by cabin, otherwise all campers in activity groups 4 units each side (girls/boys) rising 3-4 th , 5-6 th , 7-8 th , 9-11 th 16 yrs oldest camper then required to take 2 yrs off before applying as staff (10%) Priority if hiring staff is based on what they teach, what skill they will lead Ex: must be lifeguard certified to be on waterfront. title: TENNIS EUROPE & North America — LINDSEY — 11-29-16
text:
# TENNIS EUROPE & North America

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Presentation by Gary Weiner. Actually Tennis Europe and North America. Goal: Give kids the opportunity to play a lot of matches in a short period of time against good competition. It’s not a teen tour; it’s a serious minded tennis program first, then sightseeing. Early week is a lot of tournaments; then as they play out they will do lessons and will do more and more sightseeing. Socialize with the European kids. Enter the kids in every draw possible (singles and doubles). Ratings: Anything in North America automatically goes into the system; in Europe they have a different system but after they get a full list of who they played and rating. 95% of time in Europe is outside the cities; the tournaments are in the suburbs; will go into the city to sightsee; cut Paris out of the mix. The kids really bond because of the shared interest. Get final evaluation at the end of the trip.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Who do they take: Don’t take beginners, but take kids who just mastered the basics (good forehand, backhand and can get serve in). Take some who are very advanced. Eligibility determination: Three references – 2 from school, 1 other; can take rankings into account. Size and Grouping: Groups are more like 12-16 kids in Europe; 18-22 kids per group in America. Trips in California and Midwest are a little bigger (will go to 22; had 28 on a trip last year but it was too big). On North American trips a little wider ability level. Book by age and ability. About 50/50 girls and boys. Don’t take more than 2-3 players who know each other. Will accommodate kids with anxiety. Who goes on the trips? 2 or 3 full time coaches who are with each group (not kids\! professionals). The 2 directors travel between the trips.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Breakfast at hotel 90% of the time. Rotate roommates.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Then go see sites or beach activity.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Early week is a lot of tournaments; then as they play out they will do lessons and will do more and more sightseeing. Enter the kids in every draw possible (singles and doubles). Try to limit sitting around time: Have kids chart the other kids’ matches; practice 2-4 hours a day; then go see sites or beach activity; try to save the major sites for the kids all to see. If a kid makes the semis or the finals then they will want to watch and cheer that kid on.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Not just about tennis; try and make it a life world experience. Socialize with the European kids. Get final evaluation at the end of the trip. What is different about the domestic programs? North America: Can get a rating (important for playing in college) – star system, Universal Tennis. Visit colleges (UCLA). Practice in San Diego. Vancouver, Denver. They also do Adult trips.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Can accommodate medication and allergies; depending on what it is the kids can hold it; all depends on kids’ comfort level; can deal with epipens. Rules: No drinking, no drugs. Send the rules to the parents 2 weeks before the trip and everyone signs off; then have them sign a player pledge before they get off the plane. Haven’t had to send anyone home in 5 years because the focus is on playing in the tournaments. 2 webinars before they go: One on mental toughness; one on stretching and training the right way. Always a stretching session. Food: Breakfast at hotel 90% of the time. Take them to restaurants for lunch and dinner (in California - California Pizza Kitchen, Cheesecake).

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The kids really bond because of the shared interest. Cell phones: Yes can have, but tell the kids to put them away so they can bond; not allowed at meals etc. Not just about tennis; try and make it a life world experience.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Everything is included in price except trips to and from Europe, lunch, extras. On average kid spends $100 a week. Cell phones: Yes can have, but tell the kids to put them away so they can bond; not allowed at meals etc. Logistics: Gary travels from place to place and is always available on cell phone. Will make decision in March what trips they aren’t running; right now not sure if Swiss Alps is going to run. When are the trips: 26 days is longest trip; can offer programs that are shorter. Start trips beginning on June 24 through the end of summer. One trip goes to France: Barcelona, French Riviera, Holland. No Paris.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Tennis Europe and North America is a serious minded tennis program (not a teen tour) with the goal to give kids the opportunity to play a lot of matches in a short period of time against good competition. They don’t take beginners, but do take kids who just mastered the basics and some who are very advanced, and they enter players in every draw possible (singles and doubles) with tournaments early, lessons as draws play out, and increasing sightseeing. Groups are 12-16 in Europe and 18-22 in North America with 2-3 full time professional coaches per group, breakfast at hotels 90% of the time, restaurants for meals, and clear rules, safety, and support (including medication/allergies and a player pledge). Everything is included except flights to/from Europe, lunch, and extras (average spend about $100 a week); trips start beginning on June 24 through the end of summer (longest 26 days), and 95% of Europe time is in suburbs with city sightseeing (no Paris).

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Serious minded tennis program first; then sightseeing.
  - Goal: Give kids the opportunity to play a lot of matches in a short period of time against good competition.
  - Enter the kids in every draw possible (singles and doubles).
  - Socialize with the European kids.
  - Get final evaluation at the end of the trip.
  - North America: Can get a rating (star system, Universal Tennis) important for playing in college; visit colleges (UCLA); practice in San Diego; Vancouver, Denver.
  - Groups 12-16 (Europe) and 18-22 (America); 2-3 full time coaches (professionals).
  - Rules and focus on tournaments support good behavior; haven’t had to send anyone home in 5 years.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Don’t take beginners.
  - Everything is included except trips to and from Europe, lunch, and extras (on average kid spends $100 a week).
  - 95% of time in Europe is outside the cities; cut Paris out of the mix; no Paris.
  - Will make decision in March what trips they aren’t running; not sure if Swiss Alps is going to run.
  - Trips in California and Midwest are a little bigger (had 28 on a trip last year but it was too big).
  - Cell phones allowed but restricted (not allowed at meals).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Scatico — Lisa — 7-30-23
text:
# Scatico

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Scatico has 265 campers and is run like a brother/sister camp. Full summer, offer a first time partial summer at any age. They offer a one week option for rising 2nd graders.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lisa and Shari were toured by Nicki Fleischner and dad David greeted us. David and Nicki both reside on the UWS, most campers from there, Westchester and Northern NJ; very few from Long Island. They are growing in LA and San Fran. 80% of the bunk counselors used to be campers. It is about 70% J.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

The youngest bunk has 26 campers with 8 counselors in a large bunk.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

At the lake boys/girls separate sides, no waterskiing, no pool, lots of boating, a few toys in the lake.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 activities/day for girls, 6 for boys. They move by division, mostly structured. Older campers have one day/week coed. No overnights, only for the 2 oldest age groups. Every age group has one big day trip.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Girls go to the boys’ side for woodworking. Art is basic. Ceramics has 5 wheels. Old school theater, more skits/talent shows than true productions. They have gymnastics, music lessons (mostly guitar), and dance as well. Fishing is popular with the boys; they participate in all the tournaments with the Berkshire camps. No horses; they offer street hockey.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining hall is old-school. Loosely kosher style. Before meals families can hang on porch together. Optional Friday eve service, 25% go.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This is a throw back camp, very low-key feel, a family feel.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Scatico is run like a brother/sister camp with 265 campers. It is full summer, with a first time partial summer at any age and a one week option for rising 2nd graders. This is a throw back camp with a very low-key, family feel, a loosely kosher style dining hall, and an optional Friday eve service (25% go). Days are mostly structured (5 activities/day for girls, 6 for boys; move by division; older campers have one day/week coed) with lots of boating on the lake (no waterskiing, no pool) and classic activities like woodworking, basic art, ceramics (5 wheels), skits/talent shows, gymnastics, guitar, dance, fishing, and street hockey.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Throw back camp, very low-key feel, a family feel.
  - Brother/sister camp structure; older campers have one day/week coed.
  - Flexible lengths: full summer, first time partial summer at any age, and a one week option for rising 2nd graders.
  - High counselor continuity: 80% of the bunk counselors used to be campers.
  - Lots of boating; participates in all the tournaments with the Berkshire camps.
  - Strong NYC/Westchester/Northern NJ base; growing in LA and San Fran.
  - Loosely kosher style; optional Friday eve service (25% go).

  

#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - No waterskiing.
  - Art is basic.
  - No horses.
  - No overnights, only for the 2 oldest age groups.
  - At the lake boys/girls separate sides.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Watitoh
  - Baco/Chenawah
  - Schodack

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

SCATICO 7-27-23 LISA AND SHARI WERE TOURED BY NICKI FLEISCHNER AND DAD DAVID GREETED US. THEY HAVE 265 CAMPERS THIS CAMP IS RUN LIKE A BROTHER/SISTER CAMP. FULL SUMMER, OFFER A FIRST TIME PARTIAL SUMMER AT ANY AGE. THEY OFFER A ONE WEEK OPTION FOR RISING 2 ND GRADERS. THIS IS A THROW BACK CAMP, VERY LOW-KEY FEEL, A FAMILY FEEL. DAVID AND NICKI BOTH RESIDE ON THE UWS, MOST CAMPERS FROM THERE, WESTCHESTER AND NORTHERN NJ. VERY FEW FROM LONG ISLAND. THEY ARE GROWING IN LA AND SAN FRAN. 80% OF THE BUNK COUNSELORS USED TO BE CAMPERS. 5 ACTIVITIES/DAY FOR GIRLS, 6 FOR BOYS. THEY MOVE BY DIVISION, MOSTLY STRUCTURED. OLDER CAMPERS HAVE ONE DAY/WEEK COED. THE YOUNGEST BUNK HAS 26 CAMPERS WITH 8 COUNSELORS IN A LARGE BUNK. DINING HALL IS OLD-SCHOOL. LOOSELY KOSHER STYLE, BEFORE MEALS FAMILIES CAN HANG ON PORCH TOGETHER. OPTIONAL FRIDAY EVE SERVICE, 25% GO, IT IS ABOUT 70% J. AT THE LAKE BOYS/GIRLS SEPARATE SIDES, NO WATERSKIING, NO POOL, LOTS OF BOATING, A FEW TOYS IN THE LAKE. GIRLS GO TO THE BOYS’ SIDE FOR WOODWORKING, ART IS BASIC, CERAMICS HAS 5 WHEELS. OLD SCHOOL THEATER, MORE SKITS/TALENT SHOWS THAN TRUE PRODUCTIONS. THEY HAVE GYMNASTICS, MUSIC LESSONS, MOSTLY GUITAR, DANCE AS WELL. FISHING IS POPULAR WITH THE BOYS; THEY PARTICIPATE IN ALL THE TOURNAMENTS WITH THE BERKSHIRE CAMPS. NO HORSES, THEY OFFER STREET HOCKEY. NO OVERNIGHTS, ONLY FOR THE 2 OLDEST AGE GROUPS. EVERY AGE GROUP HAS ONE BIG DAY TRIP. OTHER SIMILAR CAMPS: WATITOH, BACO/CHENAWAH, SCHODACK.

title: Winaukee — SHARI — 9-1-22
text:
# Winaukee



## 1 · Program Snapshot

BASIC DATA – 150 campers (72 mainland, 62 island, 18 rookies), 375 capacity. 61 new campers in ’22. 100 staff. Campers from NYC, Westchester, FL, MA & CT mostly (others listed in questionnaire in remote). 7-12 year olds on mainland, 13+ on Island. Please see CE Remote for additional information for completed questionnaire by director.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Shari, Lisa B, Beth & Jennifer toured by Matt Jackowitz (previously at Echo Lake 10 yrs and Walt Whitman 5yrs w/ wife Ashley) & Jon (Poff) Poffenberger. Campers from NYC, Westchester, FL, MA & CT mostly (others listed in questionnaire in remote). 100 staff. 25 females on camp. Will introduce big bro program in ’23.



## 3 · Living Quarters

ACCOMMODATIONS – bathrooms shared by 2 bunks. Private changing areas.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

AQUATICS – 2-3x/wk.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING – 3 electives, 3 bunk activities (2 sports, 1 swim) repeats over 5 days.



## 6 · Program Highlights

SPORTS – intercamps w/ Takumseh, Indian Acres, West End House. Fitness on Island for 13+ offering strength and conditioning and 3/day runs offered. Use The Dome (indoor space) that is traditional facility w/ high ceiling & flags hanging for basketball, street hockey and other sports listed in questionnaire. Wiffleball stadium. Tennis director, Izzy, been at Winaukee for 32 years. 3 clay courts, 6 hard. Offer pickleball too. CREATIVE – woodworking messy, not very popular but about 6-8 campers opt in. No power tools. Traditional playhouse w/ plaques and fireplace that they use for movie night. TRIPS – Mt. Washington overnight, Rattlesnake Island. 2 overnights mandatory each age group per summer. 1x/summer canoe out to spot they cleared for s’mores, cookout and sleep. Back to camp in am. College days occur over 1st 4wks, color war at end of summer.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

DINING – Sit by bunk except for cookouts, one seating for now since camp is small. Fruit out all day, snacks am and pm. Separate dining halls for Mainland and Island. Some covid on Island. Covid on camp seemed to throw Matt off a bit (also wife very pregnant so maybe that contributed to distracted demeanor).



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

RELIGIOUS LIFE – 20-30 min optional service before BBQ & usually do with Island campers and mainland campers together but separate tonight b/c some covid on Island. Then an all camp service lead by campers - a kid on the Island recorded the service and they will play it on mainland. Majority of campers are Jewish. Overall impressions: it was a very hot day and our tour was mediocre. Poff seemed much more organized and in-tune with things happening. Camp looked ok but a bit sleepy.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Winaukee is for a boy who enjoys sports and water activities. Not much ‘other’ stuff at this time. Camp looked ok but a bit sleepy. Hoping we can help them turn things around for 2023 and beyond.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Winaukee is for a boy who enjoys sports and water activities.
  - SPORTS – intercamps w/ Takumseh, Indian Acres, West End House.
  - Use The Dome (indoor space) that is traditional facility w/ high ceiling & flags hanging for basketball, street hockey and other sports.
  - Wiffleball stadium.
  - Tennis director, Izzy, been at Winaukee for 32 years. 3 clay courts, 6 hard. Offer pickleball too.
  - Fitness on Island for 13+ offering strength and conditioning and 3/day runs offered.
  - TRIPS – Mt. Washington overnight, Rattlesnake Island.
  - 2 overnights mandatory each age group per summer.
  - 1x/summer canoe out to spot they cleared for s’mores, cookout and sleep.
  - Fruit out all day, snacks am and pm.
  - AQUATICS – 2-3x/wk.
  - PROGRAMMING – 3 electives, 3 bunk activities (2 sports, 1 swim) repeats over 5 days.
  - College days occur over 1st 4wks, color war at end of summer.
  - Majority of campers are Jewish; optional services led by campers.



#### Lowlights

  - Overall impressions: it was a very hot day and our tour was mediocre.
  - Covid on camp seemed to throw Matt off a bit (also wife very pregnant so maybe that contributed to distracted demeanor).
  - Camp looked ok but a bit sleepy.
  - Not much ‘other’ stuff at this time.
  - Woodworking messy, not very popular; no power tools.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

Fitness on Island for 13+ offering strength and conditioning and 3/day runs offered. Use The Dome (indoor space) that is traditional facility w/ high ceiling & flags hanging for basketball, street hockey and other sports listed in questionnaire. Wiffleball stadium. title: WINAUKEE — EMILY & MICHELLE — 2-11-24
text:
# WINAUKEE



## 1 · Program Snapshot

They do get a good amount of international campers and tend to bunk them together. Camp allows 5 weeks for those campers but generally would prefer kids to choose 4, 6, or 7. They are considering moving toward 2 sessions in the future. She said it’s not ideal having so many different start and stop options. Friday night - optional Jewish service or optional non denominational service. She said the campers are much more diverse than they were in the 80’s/90s. August 27th: Alumni 105th reunion, they’d like to get more alumni kids so feel free to spread the word.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

1st - 4th grade: JR camp, counselors are between 19-25 for the youngest boys plus a head counselor and a group leader. 5th-7th: mainland camp (around 75-100 campers). 7th-9th: "island" camp (around 50 campers). Pofff is in charge of the island campers.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They will have more sailing options (and a regatta) this summer and, in the next year or two, adding rowing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Weds is "trip day" - they will do a day trip or they will stay in hotels when they get older, and everyone can also do an overnight camping trip to the Island as well. There are 2 color wars so if kids only stay for a session they will experience it and then again at the end, plus a big all camp party. "Playdates" (when they’re younger) and socials when they’re older with Robindel and Wicosuta quite often; can be 2-3 times per week for island campers.



## 6 · Program Highlights

NEW for 2024: Makerspace (which will be fun art projects like slime, chemistry etc., not 3D printing or anything techy). They realized the boys wanted more art options. There are 2 color wars and a big all camp party. More sailing options (and a regatta) this summer and, in the next year or two, adding rowing. Frequent "playdates"/socials with Robindel and Wicosuta, especially for island campers.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

NO dr on site; they do Telehealth and/or go to urgent care 20 mins away but they do have nurses on staff. She said it’s easier that way and works well. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

She said the campers are much more diverse than they were in the 80’s/90s. Friday night - optional Jewish service or optional non denominational service. Emily and I met with Ashleigh from Winaukee today and she is very sweet.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

1 visiting day (used to be a weekend but now it’s just one day to keep it simple). Only place they are offering busing from right now is White Plains, NY (no more city or CT). They will have multiple buses from that location to camp. 1st 4 weeks: 2 phone calls; then 1-2 calls during the last 3 weeks. MP3 only, nothing with wifi; no electronics—she feels strongly they need a break from technology. She will be in Philly and DC this weekend and next week if anyone wants her to meet clients. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Winaukee offers 4, 6, or 7 week options (5 weeks allowed for international campers who are often bunked together) and is considering moving to two sessions. The program features two Color Wars, frequent socials with sister camps, and a weekly trip day with overnights as boys get older. Health is supported by on-site nurses with Telehealth and urgent care 20 minutes away, and the camp has a strong no‑electronics policy (MP3 only, nothing with wifi). Transportation is currently only from White Plains, NY, with one visiting day and scheduled parent phone calls.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - NEW for 2024: Makerspace (fun art projects like slime and chemistry; not techy/3D printing).
  - More sailing options and a regatta; adding rowing in the next year or two.
  - Two Color Wars plus a big all camp party.
  - Weds is "trip day" with day trips, hotel stays for older boys, and an overnight to the Island.
  - Frequent playdates/socials with Robindel and Wicosuta (2-3x/week for island campers).
  - Diverse camper population and optional Friday night services.



#### Lowlights

  - No doctor on site; relies on Telehealth and urgent care 20 mins away (nurses on staff).
  - Only busing from White Plains, NY (no more city or CT).
  - Many different start/stop options are not ideal; camp is considering moving toward 2 sessions.
  - They need more campers in every group except for 5th and 6th grade is FULL.



#### Similar Camps

  - Wicosuta
  - Robindel



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Emily and I met with Ashleigh from Winaukee today and she is very sweet. Just a few key points in case anyone wants an update for this summer: They do get a good amount of international campers and tend to bunk them together. Camp allows 5 weeks for those campers but generally would prefer kids to choose 4, 6, or 7. They are considering moving toward 2 sessions in the future. She said it’s not ideal having so many difference start and stop options. She said it’s easier that way and works well. They will have multiple buses from that location to camp. 1st 4 weeks: 2 phone calls Then 1-2 calls during the last 3 weeks There are 2 color wars so if kids only stay for a session they will experience it and then again at the end plus a big all camp party “Playdates” (when they’re younger) and socials when they’re older with Robindel and Wicosutta quite often. Can be 2-3 times per week for island campers Weds is "trip day" - they will do a day trip or they will stay in hotels when they get older except everyone can also do an overnight camping trip to the Island as well NEW for 2024: Makerspace (which will be fun art projects like slime, chemistry etc not 3d printing or anything techy) They realized the boys wanted more art options \*see Ashleighs email earlier this week Also, they will have more sailing options (and a regatta) this summer and next year or two, adding rowing August 27th: Alumni 105th reunion, they’d like to get more alumni kids so feel free to spread the word She will be in Philly and DC this weekend and next week if anyone wants her to meet clients Also they need more campers in every group except for 5th and 6th grade Is FULL

title: WALDEN (NY) — BETH — 7-14-24
text:
# WALDEN (NY)

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp is thriving and bursting at the seams; continue to invest in property and build to 500 coeds grades 2-9. 65-70% stay 7 weeks; also offer 3.5 weeks. 10th graders are CITs who must stay 7 weeks and have been a camper.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Coed; grades 2-9; 10th graders are CITs who must stay 7 weeks and have been a camper. 300 staff; 50-60% International. 80-90% of campers are Jewish; 80-90% of staff are not. Co-Director Robin writes a daily email.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: All redone with knotty pine. TIGHT/OVERFILLED and HOT\! Fan on floor. 4 staff; 14 campers. Wood bunk beds. 2 showers, 2 sinks, 2 toilets for 18 people. Some of the boys have a shower house. Individual laundry off-site 1x week. Cubbies, closets, and drawers. Cabin assignments may or may not be mixed year to year depending on enrollment and need. Counselors unpack for campers for all ages. Facilities cleaned by housekeeping staff. Permit hair dryer, makeup allowed but rarely used.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront: Large, beautiful lake with island plus small heated pool. Swim 7/10 days until 6th grade; waterfront is NOT coed. 1 piece swim suits. Free swim/lessons in lake and pool. 3 waterski boats and 2 tubing boats. Boating – all types; sailing; aquapark.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 7 periods per day; 3 am & 4 pm; includes a waterfront period 2x day. AM = 1 water, 1 assigned, 1 choice; PM = 2 assigned, 1 choice, 1 free play. Travel by bunk and division; coed meals; coed play; coed evenings; coed special events. Special Days 2x week. 1st session Walden Games for 3 days with 4 teams; 2nd session Color War for 4 days Blue & Gray.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Creative Arts: Extensive with sophisticated projects; tie dye, knitting. 4 sewing machines. 7 pottery wheels. Cooking: New, fantastic, a/c facility that can hold whole division; allergy friendly; recipe level increases with age. Woodworking: basic with hand tools. Performing Arts: Old theater (will build new one in future); Big Ass fans. 2 shows per session; 1 older & 1 younger; whoever wants to can participate. 2 dance studios – 1 new and still have the older one; fans; hip hop, Zumba, yoga. (No music or circus.) Gymnastics: Large with all equipment and spring floor. Sports: All sports offered plus pickleball, gaga, go carts, mountain boarding with track, and martial arts. Fitness: bootcamp style. Golf: on-site indoor simulator with 3 bays & a/c; off-site 2x session to Cronin’s (25 minutes); extra cost. Hockey: 1 older rink for street and roller. Horses: off-site (15 minutes away); about 30 campers ride; 2x session; extra cost. Nature: NO animals or garden. Outdoor Adventure: Adventureland with extensive ropes courses, climbing wall, Ninja Warrior; outdoor living skills; archery, mountain biking, mountain boarding. Optional overnights on island in lake. Hike on-site up side of mountain; takes 40-60 minutes to reach the top. Field trips: 6 Flags each session. 15 year olds have 2 trips per session: 2 days each to Niagara Falls & Burlington, VT; 2 days each to Newport & Montreal. For future: Will build a field house for sports.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: Large, bright, 2 seatings. Buffet line with lots of stations and lots of options – extensive salad bar, pasta bar, soup bar, sandwich bar; fruit/yogurt/cereal. Can handle all allergies with separate prep, supervisor, and chef, no nuts or peanuts. Younger campers have morning snack; all campers have afternoon snack and evening snack. Candy canteen every other day. Families bring food for visiting day which campers can keep for 2 days. Medical: new A/C building with 7 nurses; 1 rotating parent doctor; hospital in Glens Falls (25 minutes); Urgent Care in Warrensburg (10 minutes).

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp focuses on kindness, community, willingness to join in. Nurturing, loving, large camp; busy, fun, active, yet still low-key. So much love and care makes large size work. Religion: Friday evening short J service followed by campership Shabbat dinner with challah. Do not wear anything special. 80-90% of campers are Jewish; 80-90% of staff are not.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses: 6 buses from White Plains that are for Westchester, NYC and 20-30 campers from Long Island; 2 buses from Paramus, NJ; 1 bus from Cherry Hill, NJ that includes Philadelphia; 1 bus from Montreal. Chaperoned flight from MD/D.C. and FL (45 campers). Bring food on bus that can be kept for a few days. Communication: 2 calls per half session. Camera and Music without screens permitted. No packages. Not required to write letters. Post 500+ photos nightly with facial recognition; also Co-Director Robin writes a daily email. Extra costs noted: Golf off-site to Cronin’s; Horses off-site.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp is thriving and bursting at the seams; continue to invest in property and build to 500 coeds grades 2-9. Nurturing, loving, large camp; busy, fun, active, yet still low-key. Camp focuses on kindness, community, willingness to join in. So much love and care makes large size work.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Large, beautiful lake with island plus small heated pool
  - Swim 7/10 days until 6th grade; 3 waterski boats and 2 tubing boats; Boating – all types; sailing; aquapark
  - Creative Arts: Extensive with sophisticated projects; tie dye, knitting; 4 sewing machines; 7 pottery wheels
  - Cooking: New, fantastic, a/c facility that can hold whole division; allergy friendly; recipe level increases with age
  - Adventureland with extensive ropes courses, climbing wall, Ninja Warrior; outdoor living skills; archery, mountain biking, mountain boarding
  - Dining Hall buffet with lots of stations and lots of options – extensive salad bar, pasta bar, soup bar, sandwich bar; fruit/yogurt/cereal
  - Can handle all allergies with separate prep, supervisor, and chef, no nuts or peanuts
  - Medical: new A/C building with 7 nurses; 1 rotating parent doctor

  - Gymnastics: Large with all equipment and spring floor
  - Golf: on-site indoor simulator with 3 bays & a/c
  - For future: Will build a field house for sports

  

#### Lowlights

  - Cabins: TIGHT/OVERFILLED and HOT\! Fan on floor
  - Old theater (will build new one in future)
  - Nature: NO animals or garden
  - Some of the boys have a shower house
  - Waterfront is NOT coed
  - Golf off-site to Cronin’s (25 minutes); extra cost; Horses off-site (15 minutes); extra cost

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Might give with Birchmont (though no Jewish observance)
  - Kenwood Evergreen (though softer on sports)
  - Walt Whitman (though more rustic and outdoorsy)
  - Watitoh (though many less facilities and smaller)
  - Kenmont Kenwood (if only want half summer forever)
  - Echo Lake
  - Iroquois Springs
  - Chenawanda (if North Jersey family)
  - Chestnut Lake (possibly but leadership not as warm and fuzzy and no J observance)
  - Pine Forest (for the hamisch but not the facilities)
  - Westmont

title: WESTCOAST CONNECTION — ASHLEY — 11-30-21
text:
# WESTCOAST CONNECTION

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Mitch Lerner and Ira Solomon 2021: Successful summer with programs in the US, Costa Rica, Greece and Iceland. No covid cases on the programs during the summer, protocols in place. 2022: All participants and staff must be vaccinated--no exceptions\! No more “deluxe camping” - hotels and dorms - clients are not looking for outdoor and camping trips. Why Choose WCC? - 26 full time staff in multiple offices - staff, supervisors and management visit trips and locations regularly - 24/7 communication centers in NYC and Montreal - More exclusive middle school departures, and departures for 11th and 12th grade.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Mitch Lerner and Ira Solomon. 26 full time staff in multiple offices. Staff, supervisors and management visit trips and locations regularly. More exclusive middle school departures, and departures for 11th and 12th grade.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

No more “deluxe camping” - hotels and dorms. NYU accommodations (shared). Hotel accommodations (M Social Hotel). Simple hotels and B\&B accommodations, 2-3 star accommodations (similar to other community service programs). On the campus of UCLA.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Career Experiences--Three Programs  
1\. Wall Street Experience  
   a. Partner with the New York Institute of Finance, NYU accommodations (shared)  
   b. Guest speakers, Fund manager & mergers and acquisitions group projects  
   c. For completing 9-12th grade (mostly 10th & 11th grade)  
   d. 100 student maximum, Commuter option available (lower price point)  
   e. 80% male, 20% female in 2021  
2\. Fashion Experience NYC  
   a. Partner with LIM College, operates on LIM’s Midtown East campus  
   b. Hotel accommodations (M Social Hotel)  
   c. Relationships with over 800 companies, learn about the business aspect of fashion (merchandising, social media, design), in class and interactive experiences  
   d. Two groups of 50, commuter option available (lower price point)  
   e. July 10 and July 24  
   f. For completing 9th-12th grade  
3\. Medical Assistance in Ecuador  
   a. Partner is MEDLIFE  
   b. Shadow medical professionals, work with indigenous locals, set up mobile clinics, very hands on  
   c. Two groups of 24 students, For completing 9-11th grade  
   d. 40 service hours in clinics and development project, hands on, focused service hours  
   e. Simple hotels and B\&B accommodations, 2-3 star accommodations (similar to other community service programs)

Pre-College Enrichment Los Angeles  
\- On the campus of UCLA  
\- varied and substantial academic offerings (business, law, social sciences, sports, etc.)  
\- UCLA professors and faculty, local industry professionals  
\- on-site academic coordinators, improving things in real time  
\- 140 students in 2019, great reviews, 80% of students come from United States which enhances the social experience  
\- no additional fees, great value ($1,500 less than comparable program)

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

No covid cases on the programs during the summer, protocols in place. 2022: All participants and staff must be vaccinated--no exceptions\! 24/7 communication centers in NYC and Montreal. Alcohol policy: On Tour in Europe (11th & 12th Grade)--go by local customs, can order one beer or one wine at dinner and/or evening activity with staff present, drinking during the day and buying bottles when staff is not present--dismissal from program. All other programs--dismissal from program for drinking or drugs.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Great reviews. 80% of students come from United States which enhances the social experience.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

WEBSITE info: Westcoastconnection.com/quick-view. Easy way to see all the programs on one page. Copy the links and paste into email, they will stay as a clickable link. Way to filter out the trips you are looking for (i.e. middle school only, USA only). Search for programs by date--travel between (can put in exact dates of when students are available). Click on the grade of the student on the homepage. Then filter out trips, dates, age, interest, etc. Commuter option available (lower price point). No additional fees, great value ($1,500 less than comparable program).

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Westcoast Connection ran successful programs in the US, Costa Rica, Greece and Iceland in 2021 with no covid cases and protocols in place. For 2022, all participants and staff must be vaccinated—no exceptions—and the program moved away from “deluxe camping” to hotels and dorms. Signature options include Career Experiences (Wall Street Experience, Fashion Experience NYC, and Medical Assistance in Ecuador) and Pre-College Enrichment Los Angeles on the campus of UCLA. Families can expect substantial academic and career exploration offerings, 24/7 communication centers in NYC and Montreal, commuter options on select programs, and no additional fees on UCLA Pre-College (a reported $1,500 less than comparable program).

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Successful summer with programs in the US, Costa Rica, Greece and Iceland.
  - No covid cases on the programs during the summer, protocols in place.
  - 26 full time staff in multiple offices.
  - 24/7 communication centers in NYC and Montreal.
  - More exclusive middle school departures, and departures for 11th and 12th grade.
  - Pre-College Enrichment Los Angeles on the campus of UCLA with varied and substantial academic offerings; no additional fees, great value ($1,500 less than comparable program).
  - Career Experiences: Wall Street Experience (NYIF/NYU), Fashion Experience NYC (LIM/M Social), and Medical Assistance in Ecuador (MEDLIFE) with defined group sizes and service hours.
  - Easy website quick-view to filter by grade, dates, destination, and interests.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No more “deluxe camping” - hotels and dorms - clients are not looking for outdoor and camping trips.
  - On Tour in Europe (11th & 12th Grade) go by local customs: can order one beer or one wine at dinner and/or evening activity with staff present.
  - All other programs: dismissal from program for drinking or drugs.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: WOODMONT DAY CAMP — SHARI — 7-21-21
text:
# WOODMONT DAY CAMP

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Ilisha Borek (owner 15 years) toured us & we saw Sam along the way. Camp was bustling: campers active in every program area and looked happy\! Session options 3 or more weeks, up to 8 weeks. Ages 3 (potty trained) to 15 & mini day option for little ones. 700 campers overall: about 500 do 8 wks, 3 & 4 wks about 550-600. 220 staff. Ratios are about 5-7 campers to 1 staff: about 15-16 campers per group and 3-5 year olds have 3 staff, 1st & 2nd grade have 3 staff, 3rd & up have 2 staff. CIT's are campers 15 years old (into 10th grade). Into 8 & 9th grade teen travel. No sleep away option/affiliation. Only teen travel overnights in typical year.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ilisha Borek (owner 15 years) toured us & we saw Sam along the way. Can accommodate LGTBQ campers but has not been an issue yet. 220 staff. Ratios are about 5-7 campers to 1 staff: about 15-16 campers per group and 3-5 year olds have 3 staff, 1st & 2nd grade have 3 staff, 3rd & up have 2 staff. CIT's are campers 15 years old (into 10th grade). Into 8 & 9th grade teen travel. Visiting nights by grade (not in '21).

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Three-five year olds have their own village so they eat separately from older kids. Done by age group, meet in one spot and counselors split them up, otherwise they move together by cabin all day. No sleep away option/affiliation.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Aquatics: 6 heated pools all levels from zero entry to diving @ 8ft. 1 instructional swim (separated within age group by ability) and 1 free swim for all every day. Provide towel service; bring own bathing suit, goggles and swim shoes daily. One piece only for camper and counselors. Also small lake with some boating, fishing & 'water park.'

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

No electives in 2021 but usually 1st-4th grades = 2 electives, 5 & 6 grades = 3 electives, 7th = 4 electives. Done by age group, meet in one spot and counselors split them up, otherwise they move together by cabin all day. Age restrictions around camp: adventure, zipline & playground begin 3rd grade. Archery begin 2nd grade. Week \#7 Woodlympics. Weds is special event day; when we visited there was a DJ on huge new outdoor, open-side covered basketball pavilion. Nature: woven into everyone's schedule. 1 instructional swim and 1 free swim for all every day.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Performing Arts & Creative: drama and improv games, no shows. Innovation and legoland, wearable art, rocketry, magic, dance (movement, yoga), gymnastics tent (tumbling and balance beam). Music involves guitar and history of music and maybe write songs. Sports: soccer, baseball, gaga, basketball, fencing. Bikes & go-carts. Pickleball & tennis. They provide all equipment. Horseback riding extra cost up to 1x/week, off camp about 10-15 campers participate. Adventure: high ropes, low ropes, zipline, eagles nest & 3 zips, climbing wall, archery, trail biking, ninja course. Nature: organic garden, petting, grooming, feeding etc (pig, chicken, cow, sheep, goats). Cooking Program: No baking in '21 but do some other cooking. Edible necklaces was one example. Allergy specialist works with cooking instructor.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Nearest hospital 12 minutes, Urgent care 10 minutes away. 2 nurses on staff, can take diabetics. Dining/Food: all outdoor in '21 and will continue in '22. Usually 3 seatings, allergy specialist on staff, no peanut butter, can handle all allergies. Snacks: morning dry snack, afternoon fruit & ice cream. Three-five year olds have their own village so they eat separately from older kids. Cooking Program: No baking in '21 but do some other cooking (edible necklaces was one example). Allergy specialist works with cooking instructor.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Ilisha described Woodmont as a place that is nurturing, not all sports, not too competitive, campers like to try new things in safe environment, they're open-minded, kind, willing to try and wants to have fun\! Camp was bustling: campers active in every program area and looked happy\! Visiting nights by grade (not in '21). Post camp gatherings: Fall pumpkin party, spring fling both for new leads and enrolled families.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses on Avenue corners from UWS, downtown & Westchester (WP Bowling alley) in addition to Rockland buses. A staff member on all buses. No electronics on buses. Extra costs for pictures. Discount on tuition if parents drive campers. Tipping optional with guidelines.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Session options are 3 or more weeks up to 8 weeks for ages 3 (potty trained) to 15, with a mini day option for little ones. The program is nurturing, not all sports, not too competitive, and encourages campers to try new things in a safe environment with daily instructional and free swim. Transportation includes staffed buses from the UWS, downtown, Westchester (WP Bowling alley) and Rockland, with no electronics on buses. Families should expect strong aquatics, adventure, creative arts, and nature woven into everyone’s schedule, plus special events like Woodlympics and weekly Wednesday events.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 6 heated pools from zero entry to 8ft, with 1 instructional and 1 free swim daily for all.
  - Robust adventure (high/low ropes, zipline, eagles nest, climbing wall, ninja course) and nature with organic garden and farm animals.
  - Allergy specialist on staff; all outdoor dining in '21 and will continue in '22; no peanut butter; can handle all allergies.
  - Buses with staff from UWS, downtown, Westchester (WP Bowling alley) and Rockland; no electronics on buses.
  - Electives by grade in a typical year (1st-4th: 2; 5th-6th: 3; 7th: 4).
  - Week \#7 Woodlympics and Wednesday special event days.
  - Towel service provided; clear swimsuit policy.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No sleep away option/affiliation (only teen travel overnights in a typical year).
  - No electives in 2021.
  - Age restrictions: adventure/zipline/playground begin 3rd grade; archery begins 2nd grade.
  - Extra costs for pictures and horseback riding; tipping optional (with guidelines).
  - Visiting nights by grade were not held in '21.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

\-Ilisha Borek (owner 15 years) toured us & we saw Sam along the way. -Camp was bustling: campers active in every program area and looked happy\! -Session options 3 or more weeks, up to 8 weeks. Ages 3 (potty trained) to 15 & mini day option for little ones. Can accommodate LGTBQ campers but has not been an issue yet. 700 campers overall: about 500 do 8 wks, 3&4 wks about 550-600. 220 staff. Ratios are about 5-7 campers to 1 staff: about 15-16 campers per group and 3-5 year olds have 3 staff, 1 st & 2 nd grade have 3 staff, 3 rd & up have 2 staff. CIT’s are campers 15 years old (into 10 th grade). Into 8&9 th grade teen travel. Visiting nights by grade (not in ’21) -Buses on Avenue corners from UWS, downtown & Westchester (WP Bowling alley) in addition to Rockland buses. A staff member on all buses. No electronics on buses. -Extra costs for pictures. Discount on tuition if parents drive campers. Tipping optional with guidelines. -No sleep away option/affiliation. Only teen travel overnights in typical year. -Age restrictions around camp: adventure, zipline & playground-begin 3rd grade. Archery begin 2 nd grade. No electives in 2021 but usually 1 st -4 th grades = 2 electives, 5&6 grades= 3 electives, 7 th = 4electives. Done by age group, meet in one spot and counselors split them up, otherwise they move together by cabin all day. -Week \#7 Woodlympics -Weds is special event day, when we visited there was a DJ on huge new outdoor, open-side covered basketball pavilion. -Nearest hospital 12 minutes, Urgent care 10 minutes away, 2 nurses on staff, can take diabetics. -Dining/Food: all outdoor in ’21 and will continue in ’22. Usually 3 seatings, allergy specialist on staff, no peanut butter, can handle all allergies. Snacks: morning dry snack, afternoon fruit & ice cream. Three-five year olds have their own village so they eat separately from older kids. Cooking Program: No baking in ’21 but do some other cooking. Edible necklaces was one example. Allergy specialist works with cooking instructor. -Performing Arts & Creative: drama and improv games, no shows. Innovation and legoland, wearable art, rocketry, magic, dance (movement, yoga), gymnastics tent (tumbling and balance beam). Music involves guitar and history of music and maybe write songs. -Sports: soccer, baseball, gaga, basketball, fencing. Horseback riding extra cost up to 1x/week, off camp about 10-15 campers participate. Bikes & go-carts. Pickleball & tennis. They provide all equipment. -Adventure: high ropes, low ropes, zipline, eagles nest & 3 zips, climbing wall, archery, trail biking, ninja course. -Nature: woven into everyone’s schedule. Organic garden, petting, grooming, feeding etc (pig, chicken, cow, sheep, goats) -Aquatics: 6 heated pools all levels from zero entry to diving @ 8ft. 1 instructional swim (separated w/in age group by ability) and 1 free swim for all every day. Provide towel service, bring own bathing suit, goggles and swim shoes daily. One piece only for camper and counselors. Also small lake with some boating, fishing & ‘water park.’ -Ilisha described Woodmont as a place that is nurturing, not all sports, not too competitive, campers like to try new things in safe environment, they’re open-minded, kind, willing to try and wants to have fun\! -Post camp gatherings: Fall pumpkin party, spring fling both for new leads and enrolled families.

title: XKEYS — CARRIE MONMOUTH — 7-20-16
text:
# XKEYS

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Monmouth School (Boys), Monmouth Wales. Toured with Rachel Daniel who is the deputy camp director and a full-time XKEYS employee. This was XKEYS first week at this facility which is new to them. The camp is based at Monmouth Boys school but has also had access to some of the linked Girls School Facilities when needed (other side of town). This is a lovely campus by a river on the edge of a very sleepy town. The kids have to cross a road to get to the river and to the sporting but they are accompagnied and there are lights for part of it. The teenage girls are also located across the road in one of the boarding houses. Sole use of both sites. Total Capacity for this year was planned at 100 per week. 1st 2 weeks they are at full capacity, lower for the last 2. Total possible capacity unless they expand to the girls site would be 130 per week. Majority are doing 2- 3 weeks, a few for 4 weeks and some just for one. Ages 8-17.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ages 8-17, 15% with friends. 13-15 is the average age. The week I visited they had 5 8 year olds. Currently 50/50 male –female but expect it to follow normal XKEYS pattern of slightly higher female ratio. Mostly European, some Brits. Less Asians and Middle Eastern than at XKEYS activity (Norfolk). Toured with Rachel Daniel who is the deputy camp director and a full-time XKEYS employee. Professional teachers for photography and performance. For sport a mix of their own staff and some outsiders. EFL offered and being run by the head of their programme from Norwich. First aiders on staff.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Range of accommodation in different boarding houses. Teenage girls in very high quality singles with ensuites. Modern with common areas and private garden. Younger girls and boys on the main campus in a mix of singles, doubles, triples and one 5-6. Some ensuite, others share a bathroom on the hall. The teenage girls are also located across the road in one of the boarding houses. Kids do not have keys to the rooms BUT are not allowed back in their rooms during the day and all boarding houses are key coded. Bedtime is 9:30-10pm depending on age. Can’t get the younger ones in earlier as the evening activities are for all. Electronic Policy – are allowed their phones at all times. Free WIFI. Asked not to use phones during classes etc.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Water & Adventure proving to be the most attractive option and is already fully booked for all sessions. This is subcontracted out to a 3rd party supplier but is done off the Monmouth rowing club premises, across from the school. They have already added additional sessions – bookings with the outside supplier are done in sets of 9 (ie one instructor/set of kayaks etc). Facilities in general are excellent. 25m indoor swimming pool with workout room for 16+. The kids have to cross a road to get to the river and to the sporting but they are accompagnied and there are lights for part of it.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Three other options – photography, sport and performance generating equal interest. Professional teachers for photography and performance. Photography they can bring their own camera or they have a number that can be shared and they hire in video and computers as needed. Excellent computer suite. Drama has both a proper auditorium as well as a separate drama space to work in. For sport a mix of their own staff and some outsiders. Sport is an emphasis on learning the rules then practicing. So for example they learn’t rounders one day and then played it – the kids requested it the next day and they did it again. Its not a camp for someone who wants to play the same sport every day. Normally all ages are mixed but for sport they will separate out the youngsters. NOTE: Once an activity has been booked it can’t be changed by the student on arrival. EFL offered and being run by the head of their programme from Norwich. I saw 2 classes – all teens. Everyone is frankly at a very good level already. Capacity for each EFL class is 8 kids. EFL runs in the afternoon as an elective (the main activities are in the morning).

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Facilities in general are excellent. All weather turf – essential for the UK. Huge playing fields with proper sports hall next to it with full facilities. Excellent computer suite. Drama has both a proper auditorium as well as a separate drama space to work in. Water & Adventure proving to be the most attractive option. XKEYS staff are very excited about this location and Rachel said they were very keen to look into more excursions in Wales for next year.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Cafeteria is family style, self service. Rachel’s view was the food here was the best of all of their venues. All allergies and food requirements can be accommodated. First aiders on staff. Local surgery within a mile and the nearest hospital is in Abergaveny (15 minutes). The kids have to cross a road to get to the river and to the sporting but they are accompagnied and there are lights for part of it. All boarding houses are key coded.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This is a lovely campus by a river on the edge of a very sleepy town. Teenagers are allowed out around town (16+) but in pairs during set “free” time periods and have to report back. They have done a walk with them first – there is very little to see\! Others will go out in groups with their counselors for an ice cream etc at some point during the week. This is a great addition to the XKEYS portfolio and I can really see it growing. A lovely location with a really good feel.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transport is by Monmouth mini buses to local sites. They have taken them out hiking to the Forest of Dean, will be doing trips to the Millenium stadium in Cardiff (home of Welsh Rugby) and to Bath. Teenagers are allowed out around town (16+) but in pairs during set “free” time periods and have to report back. Others will go out in groups with their counselors for an ice cream etc at some point during the week. Electronic Policy – are allowed their phones at all times. Free WIFI. Asked not to use phones during classes etc. Parent communication: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown. Airport/coach transfers: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

XKEYS at Monmouth School (Boys) in Monmouth, Wales is a lovely campus by a river on the edge of a very sleepy town, with sole use of both sites and planned capacity of 100 per week (up to 130). Ages 8-17 attend for 1-4 weeks (majority 2-3), with a current 50/50 male–female mix and mostly European campers. Water & Adventure is the most attractive option (run off the Monmouth rowing club premises by a 3rd party), alongside photography, sport and performance, plus EFL in the afternoon as an elective. Accommodation ranges from very high quality singles with ensuites for teenage girls to a mix of singles/doubles/triples for younger girls and boys; food is family style and was viewed as the best of their venues, with strong safety measures and excellent facilities.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Lovely campus by a river; facilities in general are excellent (25m indoor pool, all weather turf, huge playing fields, proper sports hall).
  - Water & Adventure extremely popular; added sessions; professional teachers for photography and performance; excellent computer suite; proper auditorium.
  - Food here was the best of all of their venues; all allergies and food requirements can be accommodated.
  - XKEYS staff very excited; keen to look into more excursions in Wales next year; great addition to the portfolio with a really good feel.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Kids have to cross a road to get to the river and sporting (accompanied, lights for part of it).
  - Once an activity has been booked it can’t be changed on arrival.
  - Its not a camp for someone who wants to play the same sport every day.
  - Water & Adventure already fully booked for all sessions (demand may exceed supply).
  - This was XKEYS first week at this facility which is new to them.
  - Kids do not have keys to the rooms and are not allowed back in their rooms during the day.
  - Teenage girls located across the road in one of the boarding houses.
  - Electronics allowed at all times (may not suit all families).
  - Very little to see in town; limited off-campus independence (16+ in pairs during set times).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)
