title: YPI 2019 — EMILY — 7-8-25
text:
# YPI 2019

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

YPI runs a summer program out of Suffield Academy, a boarding school in Connecticut from August 4th – 17th. Kids can sign up for 1 week or 2 weeks. Kids range from ages 11 to 17. YPI is not for profit. The program offers Visual Arts (Multimedia, Photography and Fashion Design), Creative Writing (Poetry and Short Story), Performing Arts (Songwriting, Jazz Combo and Electronic Music Composition), Musical Theater, Dance and Movement and Acting. Kids pick one specialty and work on it intensely over the week. The work culminates in an end of week performance that the whole camp sees. The parents do not attend the performance.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Kids come mostly from the tri state area but also Chicago, Florida and Virginia. This summer there were 50 kids the first week and 30 the second week that attended YPI. They can take 75 kids max per week. The Director, Jeff has been doing this for 29 years. He is very passionate about the kids and the program. The teachers are all professionals in their field.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

They live in the dorms at Suffield Academy. The dorms are nice and clean but not air conditioned but they have big fans. There are nice bathrooms, showers and sinks on each hall. There is also laundry. There is an adult on each hall supervising the kids.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The campus has a pool, and pool time is offered during recreation.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Daily schedule: 8–8:30 AM Breakfast; 8:30–10 AM First class Block; 10–10:30 AM Break; 10:30–12 PM Second Class Block; 12:15–1:30 PM Lunch; 1:30–3:15 PM Third Class Block; 3:30–5 PM Recreation; 5:15–6 PM Dinner; 6:30 PM Town Meeting; 8–9:30 PM Evening Activity. For recreation and evening activity the kids do activities like yoga, capture the flag, soccer, Frisbee, pool, wiffle ball, karaoke, etc. Kids pick one specialty and work on it intensely over the week. Kids get a lot of input into the work they are doing; they get to suggest moves for dance and choose what to paint or photograph with the guidance of the teachers.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

The campus is amazing with a huge Athletic complex, fields, pool and dining hall. The painting studio, dance studio, theatre and recording studios are all amazing. The dance studio has wood floor with bars and mirrors. The art studio has lots of easels and paints and the theatre is large with a stage. The recording studio has all the technology needed and instruments; the kids record their music. The darkroom is large and they offer digital and traditional photography.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There are two Nurses for the program and access to a Doctor nearby. The dining hall has very good food. They have a salad bar and hot buffet at every meal. They offer gluten free and vegetarian. The campus is challenging for kids who need wheelchairs.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The philosophy at YPI is very accepting and nurturing. Kids get a lot of input into the work they are doing. They get to suggest moves for dance and choose what to paint or photograph with the guidance of the teachers. The type of kids that succeeds at YPI is a creative child who needs a nurturing and accepting small community and would like to focus intensely on one aspect of the arts.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Location: Suffield Academy in Connecticut. Parent attendance: The parents do not attend the performance. Tech policy: Kids have limited use of cell phones; they are mostly allowed to use phones when they get back to their rooms and do not use their phones during activity time. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

YPI runs a 1–2 week not-for-profit arts program at Suffield Academy in Connecticut for ages 11–17. Campers pick one specialty (Visual Arts, Creative Writing, Performing Arts, Musical Theater, Dance and Movement, or Acting) and work intensely toward an end-of-week performance seen by the whole camp (parents do not attend). The campus and studios are impressive—dance, theatre, recording studio, and darkroom—and the day follows a structured schedule with recreation and evening activities. The philosophy is very accepting and nurturing with professional teachers and a small-community feel; dorms are clean but not air conditioned, food is very good with gluten free and vegetarian options, and there are two nurses with a nearby doctor.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Impressive facilities: painting studio, dance studio, theatre and recording studios are all amazing.
  - Large darkroom; digital and traditional photography; kids record their music.
  - Very accepting and nurturing philosophy; kids have a lot of input into their work.
  - Teachers are all professionals in their field.
  - Structured daily schedule with recreation and evening activities (yoga, capture the flag, soccer, Frisbee, pool, wiffle ball, karaoke, etc.).
  - Good food with salad bar and hot buffet at every meal; gluten free and vegetarian offered.
  - Two Nurses for the program and access to a Doctor nearby.
  - Small session sizes (50 first week, 30 second week), max 75 per week.
  - Beautiful campus at Suffield Academy with huge athletic complex, fields, pool and dining hall.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Dorms are not air conditioned (but they have big fans).
  - The campus is challenging for kids who need wheelchairs.
  - Parents do not attend the performance.
  - Kids have limited use of cell phones; not during activity time.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They live in the dorms at Suffield. Kids come mostly from the tri state area but also Chicago, Florida and Virginia. This summer there were 50 kids the first week and 30 the second week that attended YPI. They can take 75 kids max per week. The Director, Jeff has been doing this for 29 years. He is very passionate about the kids and the program. YPI is not for profit. The program offers Visual Arts (Multimedia, Photography and Fashion Design). Creative Writing (Poetry and Short Story). Performing Arts (Songwriting, Jazz Combo and Electronic Music Composition), Musical Theater, Dance and Movement and Acting. Kids pick one specialty and work on it intensely over the week. The work culminates in an end of week performance that the whole camp sees. The parents do not attend the performance. The kids live in the dorms at Suffield Academy. The campus is amazing with a huge Athletic complex, fields, pool and dining hall. The dorms are nice and clean but not air conditioned but they have big fans. There are nice bathrooms, showers and sinks on each hall. There is also laundry. There is an adult on each hall supervising the kids. The dining hall has very good food. They have a salad bar and hot buffet at every meal. They offer gluten free and vegetarian. The campus is challenging for kids who need wheelchairs. There are two Nurses for the program and access to a Doctor nearby. The painting studio, dance studio, theatre and recording studios are all amazing\!\!\! title: Quaystage — David Highfill — 11-18-22
text:
# Quaystage

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Quaystage Training is a British sailing company founded in 2014 in Scotland. Relaunched in 2019. Ben Swain is the director, with his very capable assistant Miriam. Years of experience. For summer 2023 Programmes in the UK include: (1) PIONEERS (5 or 7 days, ages 13 to 17, Solent and South Coast based, all levels of sailing, ideal for beginners). (2) BUCCANEERS (ages 14 to 18, 10 or 14 day sailing trip, UK and Europe, designed for adventurous teens with a knowledge of sailing). New for 2023 EXPLORER is a 14 day trip in Greece, Athens based, to explore the Aolian Islands (cultural, cooking, hiking, watersports). GAP YEAR sailing programme proposed for October to December 2023 for 18 to 24 year olds on a Tall Ship trip (3 months) from the UK to the Caribbean via Spain, Morocco, Portugal and the Canaries. FAMILY TRIP scheduled August 15 to 19 in Southampton and the Solent (children from the age of 6 welcome).

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

For ages 13 to 17 (PIONEERS), 14 to 18 (BUCCANEERS), 18 to 24 year olds (GAP YEAR). Both courses attract at least 35% international students. All need a basic knowledge of English. Staff ratio 8 students to two staff. Aged 24 to 28 (some older). All staff DBS checked and have had previous experience with young people. First aid training. Skipper and mates. Ben Swain the director. His very capable assistant Miriam.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

2 berth cabins. Girls on one boys on the other. 10 students on a boat (PIONEERS). 8 students on a boat (EXPLORER). Yachts are being chartered from Sunsail.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Activities: Sailing, Adventure, Water sports. Solent and South Coast based. Water can be cold but students not bothered. Some bring wetsuits but end up not using them. Athens based EXPLORER to explore the Aolian Islands. Watersports.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Responsible for chores and on deck safety. RYA Tuition and certification and Duke of Edinburgh Residential (Gold Level). Marine Biology.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Marine Biology. RYA Tuition and certification and Duke of Edinburgh Residential (Gold Level). Cultural. Cooking. Hiking. Watersports. Designed for adventurous teens with a knowledge of sailing. Ideal for beginners. Tall Ship trip (UK to the Caribbean via Spain, Morocco, Portugal and the Canaries). FAMILY TRIP scheduled August 15 to 19 (Southampton and the Solent; children from the age of 6 welcome). Quaystage are hoping Camp Experts will bring in more internationals.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Code of conduct very important. As is safety. All staff DBS checked and have had previous experience with young people. First aid training. Daily contact from boats to shore. So point of contact for parents. Food: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Designed for adventurous teens with a knowledge of sailing. Ideal for beginners. Both courses attract at least 35% international students. All need a basic knowledge of English. Quaystage are hoping Camp Experts will bring in more internationals.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

PIONEERS dates and costs: Costs for 5 days £995 (but £945 if paid in full when booking). August 9 to 14; August 14 to 18. Costs for 7 days £1295 or £1230. August 19 to 25. BUCCANEERS: 10 or 14 day sailing trip (UK and Europe); no dates or prices on the website. EXPLORER (Greece): Dates will be July 9 to 22; July 23 to August 5; August 6 to August 19. Cost is £3465 (£3291). Yachts are being chartered from Sunsail who will be organising flights from the UK for students if needed. FAMILY TRIP Scheduled August 15 to 19 (Southampton and the Solent). Daily contact from boats to shore (point of contact for parents). Tech: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Quaystage Training is a British sailing company founded in 2014 in Scotland and relaunched in 2019. For summer 2023 Programmes in the UK include PIONEERS (ages 13 to 17, 5 or 7 days, Solent and South Coast based, all levels of sailing, ideal for beginners) and BUCCANEERS (ages 14 to 18, 10 or 14 day sailing trip in the UK and Europe, designed for adventurous teens with a knowledge of sailing). New for 2023 EXPLORER is a 14 day trip in Greece, Athens based, to explore the Aolian Islands with cultural, cooking, hiking and watersports, with yachts chartered from Sunsail who will be organising flights from the UK for students if needed. Both courses attract at least 35% international students, all need a basic knowledge of English, with RYA Tuition and certification and Duke of Edinburgh Residential (Gold Level).

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - RYA Tuition and certification and Duke of Edinburgh Residential (Gold Level).
  - Designed for adventurous teens with a knowledge of sailing.
  - At least 35% international students.
  - Daily contact from boats to shore.
  - Yachts are being chartered from Sunsail who will be organising flights from the UK for students if needed.
  - Marine Biology.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Water can be cold but students not bothered.
  - no dates or prices on the website (BUCCANEERS).
  - GAP YEAR sailing programme proposed for October to December 2023.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Quaystage Training a British Sailing company founded in 2014 in Scotland . Relaunched in 2019 Ben Swain the director. His very capable assistant Miriam. Years of experience. For summer 2023 Programmes in the UK: (1) PIONEERS: 5 or 7 days. For ages 13 to 17: Solent and South Coast based. Water can be cold but students not bothered. Some bring wetsuits but end up not using them. All levels of Sailing. Ideal for beginners. 2 berth cabins. Girls on one boys on the other. Marine Biology. Costs for 5 days £995 (but £945 if paid in full when booking). August 9 to 14: August 14 to 18: Costs for 7 days £1295 or £1230. title: Pinewood — Rachel — 7-20-23
text:
# Pinewood

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Pinewood is in Hendersonville, NC, very close to the main area where the stores and restaurants are. It was on a road just down from there. The camp offers either 2 three and a half week sessions or a full session lasting about 7.5 weeks. It is an all around variety camp with about 350 campers at a time. Majority of the campers are from Florida and most come because their parents went there or from word of mouth from friends. There is no religious aspect to the camp. Majority of the campers are Jewish.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

They have a return rate of about 85%. Majority of the campers are from Florida and most come because their parents went there or from word of mouth from friends. There are 2 or 3 counselor per cabin and most are international. Sue Lyons seemed to know almost every camper by name as we drove around, leading to an intimate air.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Boys cabins and Girls cabins are situated on different sides of the camp. Each bunk has a bathroom with a couple of toilets and showers. There are 2 or 3 counselor per cabin and most are international.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is a lake with a pontoon boat, an obstacle course, and jet skis that pull the campers in tubes. They also drive the campers once a week to a larger lake in S Carolina, 30 min away. There is a swimming pool. There is also water skiing.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The activities are done with their bunks. Daily timetable specifics and elective choice structure: Unknown.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

There is archery, arts, go karts, a swimming pool, and an indoor facility for sports and programming. There is also water skiing and horseback riding for no additional charge. The horseback riding is just basic with a few horses and trail riding. There is an ice cream place they get to go a few times a session.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There are two dining rooms. Three meals a day and a snack is given in between. Something semi healthy like pirates booty or graham crackers but nothing too junky. There is no canteen for campers to buy any snacks. As far as medical care, they have an infirmary with a few nurses on staff. And there is an urgent care, a pediatric office and a hospital all right outside the camp that they use if needed. If a camper is homesick they will notify the parent if the child insists, but then tell the camper that "mom says you need to stay at camp." They have few problems with serious cases of homesickness.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

On July 20, 2023, Rachel Weiner toured Camp Pinewood in Hendersonville, NC. The weather was raining so it was not possible to take a walking tour. The director, Sue Lyons, took me around camp in her car. Sue Lyons seemed to know almost every camper by name as we drove around, leading to an intimate air. The campers seemed to be having fun, despite the pouring rain. My overall impression of Camp Pinewood is that is an overall nice, basic camp. There is nothing overly impressive about it but the kids seemed happy and to be having fun. We talked to a few campers who were chatty and busy with the activities.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics to camp: Unknown. They also drive the campers once a week to a larger lake in S Carolina, 30 min away. Parent communication: If a camper is homesick they will notify the parent if the child insists. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: water skiing and horseback riding for no additional charge; there is no canteen for campers to buy any snacks. You can attend for 12 days as a younger first time camper but they don’t advertise that on the website—you have to call and specially arrange it.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Pinewood is an all around variety camp in Hendersonville, NC, very close to the main area where the stores and restaurants are. The camp offers either 2 three and a half week sessions or a full session lasting about 7.5 weeks, with about 350 campers at a time and a return rate of about 85%. There is a lake with a pontoon boat, an obstacle course, jet skis that pull campers in tubes, plus archery, arts, go karts, a swimming pool, an indoor facility, water skiing and basic horseback riding for no additional charge. My overall impression of Camp Pinewood is that is an overall nice, basic camp—nothing overly impressive about it but the kids seemed happy and to be having fun.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Very close to Hendersonville’s main area with stores and restaurants.
  - Director Sue Lyons seemed to know almost every camper by name, leading to an intimate air.
  - About 350 campers at a time; majority from Florida; majority of the campers are Jewish; no religious aspect.
  - Return rate of about 85%.
  - Lake with pontoon boat, obstacle course, jet skis for tubing; weekly trip to a larger lake in South Carolina (30 min).
  - Water skiing and horseback riding for no additional charge.
  - Bunks have bathrooms with a couple of toilets and showers.
  - Infirmary with a few nurses on staff; urgent care, pediatric office and hospital right outside camp.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Weather was raining so it was not possible to take a walking tour.
  - There is nothing overly impressive about it.
  - The activities are done with their bunks (less individual choice implied).
  - The horseback riding is just basic with a few horses and trail riding.
  - There is no canteen for campers to buy any snacks.
  - 12-day option for younger first time campers is not advertised on the website; you have to call and specially arrange it.
  - Tech policy: Unknown.
  - Travel logistics: Unknown.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

The director, Sue Lyons, took me around camp in her car. The camp is situated in Hendersonville very close to the main area where the stores and restaurants are. It was on a road just down from there. The camp offers either 2 three and half week sessions or a full session lasting about 7.5 weeks. Majority of the campers are from Florida and most come because their parents went there or from word of mouth from friends. The camp is an all around variety camp with about 350 campers at a time. Sue Lyons seemed to know almost every camper by name as we drove around, leading to an intimate air. The campers seemed to be having fun, despite the pouring rain. They have a return rate of about 85%. There are 2 or 3 counselor per cabin and most are international. Boys cabins and Girls cabins are situated on different sides of the camp. Each bunk has a bathroom with a couple of toilets and showers. You can attend for 12 days as a younger first time camper but they don’t advertise that on the website. You have to call and specially arrange it. There is no religious aspect to the camp. Majority of the campers are Jewish. There is a lake with a pontoon boat, an obstacle course, and jet skis that pull the campers in tubes. They also drive the campers once a week to a larger lake in S Carolina, 30 min away. There is archery, arts, go karts, a swimming pool, an indoor facility for sports and programming. The activities are done with their bunks. There is also water skiing and horseback riding for no additional charge. The horseback riding is just basic with a few horses and trail riding. There are two dining rooms. Three meals a day and a snack is given in between. Something semi healthy like pirates booty or graham crackers but nothing too junky. There is no canteen for campers to buy any snacks. There is an ice cream place they get to go a few times a session. As far as medical care, they have an infirmary with a few nurses on staff. And there is an urgent care, a pediatric office and a hospital all right outside the camp that they use if needed. If a camper is homesick they will notify the parent if the child insists, but then tell the camper that “mom says you need to stay at camp.” They have few problems with serious cases of homesickness. My overall impression of Camp Pinewood is that is an overall nice, basic camp. There is nothing overly impressive about it but the kids seemed happy and to be having fun. title: WATITOH — LARA — 7-12-23
text:
# WATITOH

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Watitoh has 230 campers, with an even split between boys and girls. There is a one-time 4-week option for campers entering up to 5th grade. Overall, Watitoh offers a well-structured program with a focus on both land and water activities. They prioritize camper comfort and engagement by providing various options and ensuring continuity in staff.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Over 50% of the staff are returning members, ensuring continuity and familiarity for the campers. Over 50% return. Director: Unknown.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

There are approximately 1-2 bunks per age group and 8-10 campers per group. Older boys have shower houses while the rest of the bunks have showers. They have been remodeling the bathrooms for several summers now and are almost complete.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

One morning period is dedicated to waterfront activities, with waterfront instructions provided during the first period.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The daily camp schedule includes two morning periods, one dedicated to waterfront activities and the other to land-based activities. Waterfront instructions are provided during the first period, while land-based activities are structured and supervised during the second period. In the afternoon, campers have the freedom to choose their hobbies for the week. Girls have a more general choice, while boys have stricter guidelines for land-based activities. There are also two structured land periods and a nature and science program. The camp is actively increasing outdoor evening activities for campers since Covid.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports facilities include 6 tennis courts, 2 baseball fields, and a volleyball court, gymnastics pavilion and climbing/ropes course. However, lacrosse is not offered as a sport, with the camp focusing on soccer, volleyball, and tennis. They offer extra tennis on an as needed basis. Watitoh hosts two plays.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Seating arrangements in the dining hall are organized by bunk. The camp follows a kosher-style dining approach and has special traditions for Shabbat, including wearing white. A 10-minute service takes place before the evening activity on Fridays. Prayers are said before every meal, with breakfast and dinner following the traditions of the Girl Scouts and Boy Scouts, respectively. Lunch follows a motzi tradition. Health and safety practices: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Well-structured program with continuity in staff and increasing outdoor evening activities since Covid.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation to the camp is provided by four Westchester buses. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Watitoh has 230 campers, with an even split between boys and girls, and offers a well-structured program with a focus on both land and water activities. The daily schedule includes two morning periods (waterfront instruction and structured land) and afternoon hobbies, with girls having a more general choice and boys following stricter guidelines for land-based activities. The camp features strong sports facilities, two plays, and kosher-style dining with Shabbat traditions including wearing white and a 10-minute Friday service. Over 50% of staff return, and transportation is provided by four Westchester buses.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Well-structured program with a focus on both land and water activities.
  - Over 50% of the staff are returning members, ensuring continuity and familiarity.
  - Afternoon choice with weekly hobbies; nature and science program.
  - Sports facilities include 6 tennis courts, 2 baseball fields, a volleyball court, gymnastics pavilion, and climbing/ropes course.
  - They offer extra tennis on an as needed basis.
  - Watitoh hosts two plays.
  - Kosher-style dining with Shabbat traditions (wearing white, 10-minute Friday service, prayers before meals).
  - Increasing outdoor evening activities since Covid.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Lacrosse is not offered.
  - Boys have stricter guidelines for land-based activities.
  - Bathrooms have been remodeling for several summers (almost complete).
  - One-time 4-week option is limited to campers entering up to 5th grade.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Walt Whitman
  - Echo Lake
  - Scatico
  - Schodack
  - If single sex, I use smaller camps mostly up north.

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Watitoh has 230 campers, with an even split between boys and girls. There is a one time 4 week option for campers entering up to 5 th grade. There are approximately 1-2 bunks per age group and 8-10 campers per group. The daily camp schedule includes two morning periods, one dedicated to waterfront activities and the other to land-based activities. Waterfront instructions are provided during the first period, while land-based activities are structured and supervised during the second period. In the afternoon, campers have the freedom to choose their hobbies for the week. Girls have a more general choice, while boys have stricter guidelines for land-based activities. There are also two structured land periods and a nature and science program. Watitoh hosts two plays. The camp is actively increasing outdoor evening activities for campers since Covid. Sports facilities include 6 tennis courts, 2 baseball fields, and a volleyball court, gymnastics pavilion and climbing;/ropes course. However, lacrosse is not offered as a sport, with the camp focusing on soccer, volleyball, and tennis. They offer extra tennis on an as needed basis. Seating arrangements in the dining hall are organized by bunk. The camp follows a kosher-style dining approach and has special traditions for Shabbat, including wearing white. A 10-minute service takes place before the evening activity on Fridays. Prayers are said before every meal, with breakfast and dinner following the traditions of the Girl Scouts and Boy Scouts, respectively. Lunch follows a motzi tradition. Older boys have shower houses while the rest of the bunks have showers. They have been remolding the bathrooms for several summers now and are almost complete. Transportation to the camp is provided by four Westchester buses. Over 50% of the staff are returning members, ensuring continuity and familiarity for the campers. Overall, Watitoh offers a well-structured program with a focus on both land and water activities. They prioritize camper comfort and engagement by providing various options and ensuring continuity in staff. Over 50% return. Watitoh can also look at: Walt Whitman, echo lake, sometimes Scatico. Sometimes Schodack. If single sex, I use smaller camps mostly up north.

title: Pontiac — Lisa — 7-27-23
text:
# Pontiac

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Coed. They have 500 campers. Full 7 weeks but offer partial camping for first timers up until entering 5th grade.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lisa and Shari were toured by Michael Etra, and Ricky and Kenny (both are retired ENT specialists) sat in with us to gather information. They are very on top of everything going on. They have 75 adult staff, with a CAT: Camper Awareness Team which assists with many campers’ issues. They move the counselors and group leaders up through the years so that they are with the same campers.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks are large and have AC, as many of their facilities have, which are all very nice and updated.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They travel to Lake Copake to waterski which is 5 min away. 2 pools.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They have 5 periods/day, 2 in the AM, 2 in the PM and a general swim. They travel by division. They travel to their activities by division as well.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Private instruction no extra charge. Golf, ice hockey and horses are all off premises in South Kent CT, about 50 min away. Nice creative arts with many offerings, woodworking, models room; cooking is a huge room with AC. Theater is big, 2 shows/summer. Movie theater also. Spin studio, circus trapeze. Gymnastics is nice. They get all top coaches (they say). They participate in all athletic tournaments; they have a turf field and a Ninja Warrior course. They offer archery, zip lining, 2 climbing walls, a small assortment of hi/low ropes. No trip day, but sometimes go to the beach, a waterpark or a baseball game. Off course they offer longer overnight trips once they get older. Their 16-17 yr olds go out West for 9 nights.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

10 nurses and 2 doctors… no surprise there as Kenny and Ricky are Drs. They eat by division but mix it up. Canteen has an ice cream shop, pizza and other food, all with AC.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Friday evening only the blessings, no Shabbat dinner but they have challah. Camp looked great and the campers seem well taken care of.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

50-60 on the chaperoned flight from Florida. 35% are from Long Island. The rest are Tri-State. They have a spending account they can use to buy soda from the machines.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Pontiac is a coed, 7-week camp with about 500 campers and a clear divisional structure. First timers can do partial camping up until entering 5th grade. Facilities are very nice and updated, with AC in bunks and many program areas, two pools, and Lake Copake waterskiing 5 minutes away, plus a wide slate of sports, arts, and adventure. Camp looked great and the campers seem well taken care of.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Bunks are large and have AC; facilities are very nice and updated.
  - Private instruction no extra charge.
  - 10 nurses and 2 doctors; Kenny and Ricky are Drs.
  - CAT: Camper Awareness Team which assists with many campers’ issues.
  - They move the counselors and group leaders up through the years so that they are with the same campers.
  - They travel to Lake Copake to waterski (5 min away) and have 2 pools.
  - They participate in all athletic tournaments; turf field and a Ninja Warrior course.
  - Nice creative arts; woodworking, models room; cooking is a huge room with AC.
  - Spin studio, circus trapeze; gymnastics is nice.
  - They get all top coaches (they say).
  - 50-60 on the chaperoned flight from Florida.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Golf, ice hockey and horses are off premises in South Kent CT, about 50 min away.
  - No trip day.
  - Friday evening only the blessings, no Shabbat dinner (but they have challah).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - TYLER HILL
  - TRAILS END
  - STARLIGHT
  - CHENAWANDA
  - WESTMONT
  - TACONIC
  - ECHO LAKE
  - TIMBER LAKE
  - LOKANDA
  - LAUREL

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

PONTIAC 7-27-23 LISA AND SHARI WERE TOURED BY MICHAEL ETRA AND RICKY AND KENNY (BOTH ARE RETIRED ENT SPECIALISTS) SAT IN WITH US TO GATHER INFORMATION. THEY ARE VERY ON TOP OF EVERYTHING GOING ON. THEY HAVE 500 CAMPERS. COED. FULL 7 WEEKS BUT OFFER PARTIAL CAMPING FOR FIRST TIMERS UP UNTIL ENTERING 5 TH GRADE. 50-60 ON THE CHAPERONED FLIGHT FROM FLORIDA. 35% ARE FROM LONG ISLAND. THE REST ARE TRI-STATE. THEY HAVE 5 PERIODS/DAY, 2 IN THE AM, 2 IN THE PM AND A GENERL SWIM. THEY TRAVEL BY DIVISION. THEY MOVE THE COUSELORS AND GROUP LEADERS UP THROUGH THE YEARS SO THAT THEY ARE WITH THE SAME CAMPERS. THEY HAVE 75 ADULT STAFF\! WITH A CAT: CAMPER AWARENESS TEAM WHICH ASSISTS WITH MANY CAMPERS ISSUES. PRIVATE INSTRUCTION NO EXTRA CHARGE. GOLF, ICE HOCKEY AND HORSES ARE ALL OFF PREMISES IN SOUTH KENT CT, ABOUT 50 MIN AWAY. BUNKS ARE LARGE AND HAVE AC AS MANY OF THEIR FACILITIES HAVE, WHICH ARE ALL VERY NICE AND UPDATED. THEY EAT BY DIVISION BUT MIX IT UP… THEY TRAVEL TO THEIR ACTIVITIES BY DIVISION AS WELL. CANTEEN HAS AN ICE CREAM SHOP, PIZZA AND OTHER FOOD, ALL WITH AC. THEY HAVE A SPENDING ACCOUNT THEY CAN USE TO BUY SODA FROM THE MACHINES. 10 NURSES AND 2 DOCTORS…NO SURPRISE THERE AS KENNY AND RICKY ARE DRS. FRIDAY EVENING ONLY THE BLESSINGS, NO SHABBAT DINNER BUT THEY HAVE CHALLAH. THEY TRAVEL TO LAKE COPAKE TO WATERSKI WHICH IS 5 MIN AWAY. 2 POOLS. NICE CREATIVE ARTS WITH MANY OFFERINGS, WOODWORKING, MODELS ROOM, COOKING IS A HUGE ROOM WITH AC. THEATER IS BIG, 2 SHOWS/SUMMER. MOVIE THEATER ALSO. SPIN STUDIO, CIRCUS TRAPEZE. GYMNASTICS IS NICE. title: WINADU — PILAR — 8-27-24
text:
# WINADU

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Located in the Berkshires in Pittsfield, MA. Little over 400 boys. Sessions 7 weeks, with an option of 5 weeks for 1st year campers and youngest kids (going into 1st, 2nd and 3rd). Part of CampGroup; Jared and Jill Shapiro and Mike and Alissa Girling are the camp directors. They also own Berkshire United. Sports Broadcasting, Play by Play is held at camp.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Met by Jill who is in charge of everyone coming in and out of camp. We arrived just after breakfast, some kids still wearing pjs other getting ready for tournaments. Toured by Jill Shapiro, toured by Jared Shapiro and Alissa Girling. Jared and Jill Shapiro and Mike and Alissa Girling are the camp directors. They also own Berkshire United and are part of CampGroup. Demographics: Little over 400 boys; they go by entering grade. Younger division campers get assigned a big brother. Majority of families are Jewish, many families where one parent is Jewish. Some campers are 3rd generation. Judaism is not part of day to day or weekly program. 16 states, Dominican Republic, Mexico, some from Europe. They would like more international boys. They are willing to create spots for them. Partial LIT program treated separately. They go to camp for a week. Then went to Hawaii for 2 weeks with the girls from Bryn Mawr, and then they back for the last 4 weeks. Staff is 270 aprox. Female staff assigned to bunks up to 8th grade. They don’t sleep in the bunks and are staff for activities. Socials with Danbee.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks 7 to 9 boys and 3 counselors on each side of bunks. Bathroom shared by both sides. Campers clean their own bunks except for showers and toilets. Senior boys have a lounge and can sleep in. Female staff assigned to bunks up to 8th grade. They don’t sleep in the bunks and are staff for activities.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Pool. Swim instruction. Heated. Lake, canoeing, paddleboarding, sailing, aqua park, kayaking, skiing. One daily period is lake or pool on regular days.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They meet at flagpole every morning and have a moment to compliment someone who has done something special. Regular days are Tuesday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. 2 periods in the morning. Periods are an hour and 15 minutes long. 3 periods in the afternoon. One is lake or pool, the other is clinic, hard core instruction. Changes every week. 40 activities available. 3rd period is selective picked every day. More like a hobby. At night, activities with their age group, like campfire, scavenger hunts. Every boy plays on 3 teams. More like a rec league. No activities by bunk. Exposure periods for boys finishing 1st through 3rd grade, no choice, planned before campers arrive. Special days are Monday, Wednesday and Thursday. They do trips inter camps and tournaments. Following week was a trip week.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Boys can earn stripes for spirit, service, sportsmanship, cooperation, table manners, campership (caring for others). Once they get all 6 they are part of the Winadu Circle. Sports: Baseball, soccer, roller and ice hockey (extra), basketball, flag football, lacrosse, tennis, golf, fencing, skateboarding, martial arts, performance. Ice hockey only for those who play at home. 15 min away. 30 hours in the 1st 5 weeks of camp. The boys in 6th grade and up get another 25 hours that they can opt into. Very popular. They rent the entire rink. Golf. 10 experiences at extra cost. Includes lessons at camp golf range and off premises golf course. Courts are all together by sport, all divisions use the same courts. Heads of sports are very experience. They are full time staff. Equipment can be brought from home if they have it. All equipment is kept at the fields. No walking around with equipment. Water station, no water bottles. They host many tournaments because they have many fields. Activities: In house band. Graphic design. Woodshop. Archery. Band. Piano lessons. Arial and adventure which includes camping. Arts & Crafts (big with lots of kids). Grilling for all older boys. Bar Mitzvah lessons (will have extra charge). Trip to Montreal with Danbee girls and weeklong trip to Ohio State Univ. and Univ of Michigan. Socials with Danbee. Sports Broadcasting, Play by Play is held at camp. Uniforms 3 days a week and for leagues and competitions.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Everyone eats together. 3 meals a day. Buffet style, served at the buffet. Salad bar. Lunch sandwich bar. Grill every night, hot dogs, hamburgers, grilled chicken, steak, and salmon on rotation. Always a pasta bar. Allergy kitchen including gluten, egg, nuts and sesame (aware). Kosher is available. Some parents send meats and they prepare them. Alyssa will make something for the boys that don’t like what is offered. Lots of fun activities around meals. They set up a food court with franchise like stations. Friday night dinner event has live music, songs and at the end every head and specialty staff recognize a camper in the middle of the circle for their effort and their attitude. Also celebrate their value system, and read out boys who earn our stripes. Health Center: Doctor lives at camp. 7 RNs, 2 student nurses. Head nurse has been with them for 10 summers. Berkshire Medical Center across the street. They follow the CDC concussion protocol. Jared micro manages progression.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Met by Jill who is in charge of everyone coming in and out of camp. We arrived just after breakfast, some kids still wearing pjs other getting ready for tournaments. Music started playing when activities started. They meet at flagpole every morning and have a moment to compliment someone who has done something special. Boys can earn stripes for spirit, service, sportsmanship, cooperation, table manners, campership (caring for others). Once they get all 6 they are part of the Winadu Circle. Majority of families are Jewish, many families where one parent is Jewish. Some campers are 3rd generation. Judaism is not part of day to day or weekly program. They offer bar mitzvah lessons and have challah French toast. No Shabbat, but they do a special dinner. Friday night dinner has live music, songs and recognition. Jared said, "We are not a sports camp. Far from it. We are a well-rounded traditional summer camp that happens to have an incredible sports program" and "Athletically or non-athletically, irrelevant". "Suitable for a boy who is willing to participate". Not sure if we got that impression.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation: Chaperoned buses to and from New York and Boston areas. Airport pickup is from Bradley, Westchester, and Logan. Communication: They visit all enrolled campers to encourage open communication. Sessions: 7 weeks, with an option of 5 weeks for 1st year campers and youngest kids (going into 1st, 2nd and 3rd). This year very few kids stayed for 5 weeks. Admissions: They had a waiting list very early, some spaces opened before the summer. We can call to ask for a specific space because families in the waiting list usually go to other camps. Extra costs: Ice hockey (extra). Golf: 10 experiences at extra cost. Bar Mitzvah lessons (will have extra charge). Tech: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Located in the Berkshires in Pittsfield, MA, Winadu is "a well-rounded traditional summer camp that happens to have an incredible sports program." Little over 400 boys attend 7-week sessions (with an option of 5 weeks for 1st year campers and youngest kids), with 40 activities, rec leagues where every boy plays on 3 teams, and a daily schedule mixing lake or pool, clinics, and hobby-style choice. Judaism is not part of day to day or weekly program, though they offer bar mitzvah lessons and have challah French toast, and Friday night dinner has live music, songs and recognition. Health and safety are robust with a doctor living at camp, 7 RNs, Berkshire Medical Center across the street, and CDC concussion protocol.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Heads of sports are very experience; they are full time staff.
  - They host many tournaments because they have many fields.
  - Doctor lives at camp; Berkshire Medical Center across the street.
  - Allergy kitchen including gluten, egg, nuts and sesame (aware); Kosher is available; Alyssa will make something for the boys that don’t like what is offered.
  - They visit all enrolled campers to encourage open communication.
  - Friday night dinner event has live music, songs and recognition; also celebrate their value system, and read out boys who earn our stripes.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Jared: "We are not a sports camp..." Not sure if we got that impression.
  - Very few kids stayed for 5 weeks this year.
  - Waiting list very early; some spaces opened before the summer.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Greylock
  - Mah Kee Nac
  - Equinunk
  - Wayne
  - Takajo
  - Cedar
  - Androscoggin

title: WILLIAM LAWRENCE — LISA — 8-31-22
text:
# WILLIAM LAWRENCE

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

They retired the cross that used to be visible as they originally had an Episcopalian affiliation; they are non-profit. They fundraise through their alumni.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jessica Starkman (previously from Cody) is now Assistant Director; she is wonderful and easy to work with. Nat Crane (has been there for 33 years and is the Director). Both toured us around. They had 70 boys, only 10 are full summer, most stay for 4 weeks, 15-20 stay for 2 weeks. 10% are legacy campers. Most of the boys are from MA, but they have a growing Brooklyn and Ridgewood campership. They have ADD boys, new older boys would feel welcomed. No women counselors.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: 8 campers/2 counselors, NO electricity, metal bunk beds and trunks. 3 bath/shower houses, on the old side. They mix up the cabins each summer. Their new Centennial Lodge is all Knotty pine and very nice; they have their weight room there. The balcony has gorgeous views of the mountains.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lower Beach Pond; we got a ride down the hill…a bit steep and long but beautiful lake. They do motorized activities and boating with a trampoline. NO sailing. Fly Fishing can do every day. Free Swim daily. Instructional swim until age 13 (as part of morning choice).

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 6 periods. 3 am periods they choose weekly which includes instructional swim until age 13. In the afternoons it is sports based. Then Free Swim.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have Color War all summer long. Same team for life. NO A\&C now…when they grow larger. Cooking at Campfire. Woodworking has weekly projects, max 6 boys. Honor Society: is optional, there are 18 OLS tests. Archery, riflery, climbing tower, cutting down a tree; they create their own fire by collecting kindling and keep it going until breakfast. NO performing arts. Sports are Non-competitive. Disco Dodgeball, arena soccer, tennis, street hockey. They play other camps: small YMCA camps and Camp Mi-Te-Na and Camp Nokomis. Saturday night is Movie night, they get a soda and one box of candy. 5 High rope elements, 7 Low ropes, No zip line but a climbing wall as well as Skeet shooting. Trips: older campers go on WW rafting and Canoe trips. Minor League bb games. Juniors do not go off site, they can canoe across the lake and sleep there.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: Older campers are waiters, there is a Chef, they sit by bunk, they say non- religious grace. Buffet style, nut free, can handle allergies. Canteen is fruit only. One RN; the Dr is at Huggins Hospital, 20 min away.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Happy Boys\! They have many camp chores but not the toilets, the counselors do that. Campers mow the grass. They play in the rain.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

All drive to camp and there is a shuttle service from Boston Logan Airport. There is NO Visiting Day; 7 week campers can see their parents in the middle. No phone calls home. Will allow packages. Tech: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Any age would do well here; Happy Boys\! Programming is 6 periods with morning choice (including instructional swim until age 13), afternoons sports based, and Free Swim, with non-competitive sports and Color War all summer long (same team for life). Cabins are 8 campers/2 counselors with NO electricity and metal bunk beds, with 3 older bath/shower houses; Lower Beach Pond is beautiful and the new Centennial Lodge (knotty pine) has a weight room and balcony views of the mountains. Families should note NO A\&C now, NO performing arts, NO Visiting Day, no sailing, no women counselors, and no phone calls home.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Assistant Director Jessica Starkman (previously from Cody) is wonderful and easy to work with.
  - Director Nat Crane has been there for 33 years.
  - Color War all summer long. Same team for life.
  - Lower Beach Pond is beautiful; boating with a trampoline; motorized activities.
  - New Centennial Lodge is all Knotty pine and very nice; weight room; balcony has gorgeous views of the mountains.
  - Non-competitive sports with Disco Dodgeball, arena soccer, tennis, street hockey.
  - Honor Society with 18 OLS tests and skills like archery, riflery, climbing tower, cutting down a tree, and creating their own fire.
  - Trips include WW rafting, Canoe trips, and Minor League bb games; juniors canoe across the lake and sleep there.
  - Dining is buffet style, nut free, can handle allergies; older campers are waiters; non-religious grace.

  

#### Lowlights

  - NO A\&C now.
  - NO performing arts.
  - No women counselors.
  - There is NO Visiting Day (7 week campers can see their parents in the middle).
  - No phone calls home.
  - NO sailing.
  - Cabins have NO electricity; bath/shower houses are on the old side.
  - The hill down to Lower Beach Pond is a bit steep and long.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Kingswood
  - Kabeyun

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Jessica Starkman (previously from Cody) is now Assistant Director; she is wonderful and easy to work with. Nat Crane (has been there for 33 years and is the Director). Both toured us around. They had 70 boys, only 10 are full summer, most stay for 4 weeks, 15-20 stay for 2 weeks. 10% are legacy campers. They retired the cross that use to be visible as they originally had an Episcopalian affiliation, they are non-profit. They fundraise through their alumni. Most of the boys are from MA, but they have a growing Brooklyn and Ridgewood campership. All drive to camp and there is a shuttle service from Boston Logan Airport. Happy Boys\! They have many camp chores but not the toilets, the counselors do that. Campers mow the grass…there is NO Visiting Day, 7 week campers can see their parents in the middle. Programming: 6 periods, 3 am periods they choose weekly which includes instructional swim until age 13. In the afternoons it is sports based. Then Free Swim. They have Color War all summer long. Same team for life. Cabins: 8 campers/2 counselors, NO electricity, metal bunk beds and trunks. 3 bath/shower houses, on the old side. They mix up the cabins each summer. They have ADD boys, new older boys would feel welcomed. Dining Hall: Older campers are waiters, there is a Chef, they sit by bunk, they say non- religious grace. Buffet style, nut free, can handle allergies. Canteen is fruit only. One RN, the Dr is at Huggins Hospital, 20 min away. No women counselors. No phone calls home. Will allow packages. Lower Beach Pond, we got a ride down the hill…a bit steep and long but beautiful lake. They do motorized activities and boating with a trampoline. NO sailing. Fly Fishing can do every day. NO A\&C now…when they grow larger. They play in the rain. Cooking at Campfire. Woodworking has weekly projects, max 6 boys. Honor Society: is optional, there are 18 OLS tests. Archery, riflery, climbing tower, cutting down a tree, they create their own fire by collecting kindling and keep it going until breakfast. NO performing arts. Sports are Non-competitive. Disco Dodgeball, arena soccer, tennis, street hockey. They play other camps: small YMCA camps and Camp Mi-Te-Na and Camp Nokomis. Saturday night is Movie night, they get a soda and one box of candy. 5 High rope elements, 7 Low ropes, No zip line but a climbing wall as well as Skeet shooting. Trips: older campers go on WW rafting and Canoe trips. Minor League bb games. Juniors do not go off site, they can canoe across the lake and sleep there. Any age would do well here. Their new Centennial Lodge is all Knotty pine and very nice, they have their weight room there. The balcony has gorgeous views of the mountains. Can give with Kingswood and Kabeyun.

title: WILLIAM LAWRENCE — DARA — 9-17-24
text:
# WILLIAM LAWRENCE

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Can accommodate 150 boys each session. 2024 – Session 1 – 60 campers, 4 weeks (or first or last 2 weeks). Session 2 – 80 campers, 3 weeks (or last 2 weeks). 3rd-8th grade – can do 2-week option; 9-10th grade must do a full session. No campers stay the full summer. Considering less options for 2025. 11th grade – LIT – pay $1000 for the summer but if they stay they get a $500 refund. 12th grade – Jr Counselor. Kids primarily from the Northeast, mostly from Boston suburbs. 10% international campers, 70% international staff. Hold Olympics in each session.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by: David Conover – Executive Director & Jessica Paquette – Associate Director. Nat Crane is retiring as ED after 32 years. Kids primarily from the Northeast, mostly from Boston suburbs; 10% international campers. 70% international staff. Women at camp – Jessica, Nurse, 2 office staff, business manager. Planning to add camp mom/aunt for 2025.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunked by grade, but group is mixed grades (3rd-6th, 7th-8th, 9th-10th). Bunks – no electricity. Campers live out of their trunks – no cubbies or drawers. Showers, sinks & toilets all in shower houses. Counselors have separate back area in each cabin; 3-4 bunk staff for younger kids, 1-2 for older. Younger campers shower during mid-day siesta time; older campers shower at 8:50.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront – Lower Beach Pond. They are the only motor boat allowed on the lake – water skiing, tubing. Fishing, fly fishing, swimming, non-motorized boating. Every counselor on staff is lifeguard certified. Free swim every day 4-6pm. Instructional swim – during activity periods; mandatory until entering 8th grade. Swim assessment for all campers at start of each session.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

7:00am Reveille. 7:30am Flagpole. 8:50pm evening program ends. 9:15 bugle – back to bunks. 9:30 TAPS (lights out for younger campers; older campers can have flashlight time). Activities by age group & interest – sign up for a week at a time. Street hockey, arena soccer, gaga pits, volleyball, 2 tennis courts; adding pickleball for 2025.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Hold Olympics in each session. Overnights: 4 two-night overnight trips for 9-10th graders per session (optional, no extra $$). 4 one-night overnight trips for 7th-8th graders per session (optional, no extra $$). “Sister” camp 3 hours away – Fleur de Lis Camp. No socials but each camp hosts each other for a day long competition. Camp wide event held a few times during the session was taking place when we were there and all boys were spirited and dressed by color and into their games.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Full time nurse lives in infirmary. Huggins Hospital 11 minutes down the road. Sit by cabin in the dining hall. Nut free and can accommodate any allergy.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Their religion is nature. Morning flagpole with entire camp with a message of the day. Very nature-oriented boys camp. No socials but each camp hosts each other for a day long competition.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Get to camp on your own. No phone calls, no emails. Pictures posted. Looking for a better system. Optional overnights have no extra $$. 11th grade – LIT – pay $1000 for the summer but if they stay they get a $500 refund.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Can accommodate 150 boys each session; 2024 Session 1: 60 campers for 4 weeks (or first or last 2 weeks); Session 2: 80 campers for 3 weeks (or last 2 weeks). 3rd-8th grade can do a 2-week option; 9-10th grade must do a full session; 11th grade is LIT ($1000 for the summer with a $500 refund if they stay); 12th grade is Jr Counselor. Very nature-oriented boys camp on Lower Beach Pond with the only motor boat on the lake for water skiing and tubing, plus fishing, swimming, and non-motorized boating. Kids primarily from the Northeast (mostly Boston suburbs) with 10% international campers and 70% international staff; no phone calls, no emails—pictures posted.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Very nature-oriented boys camp.
  - Hold Olympics in each session.
  - They are the only motor boat allowed on the lake – water skiing, tubing.
  - Every counselor on staff is lifeguard certified.
  - Full time nurse lives in infirmary; Huggins Hospital 11 minutes down the road.
  - Optional overnights with no extra $$.
  - Morning flagpole with entire camp with a message of the day.
  - 10% international campers, 70% international staff.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No phone calls, no emails; pictures posted (looking for a better system).
  - Bunks – no electricity.
  - Campers live out of their trunks – no cubbies or drawers.
  - Get to camp on your own.
  - Considering less options for 2025.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Fleur de Lis Camp

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Planning to add camp mom/aunt for 2025 - “Sister” camp 3 hours away – Fleur de Lis Camp. No socials but each camp hosts each other for a day long competition DINING & BUNKS - Sit by cabin in dining hall. - Nut free and can accommodate any allergy - Bunked by grade, but group is mixed grades (3 rd -6 th , 7 th -8 th , 9 th -10 th ) - Bunks – no electricity - Campers live out of their trunks – no cubbies or drawers - Showers, sinks & toilets all in shower houses - Counselors have separate back area in each cabin. 3-4 bunk staff for younger kids, 1-2 for older. - Younger campers shower during mid-day siesta time. - Older campers shower at 8:50. SCHEDULE & ACTIVITES 7:00am Revelry 7:30am - Flagpole 8:50pm – evening program ends 9:15 bugle – back to bunks 9:30 TAPS (lights out for younger campers, older campers can have flashlight time) - Activities by age group & interest – sign up for a week at a time - Street hockey, arena soccer, gaga pits, volleyball, 2 tennis courts, adding pickleball for 2025. Waterfront - Lower Beach Pond - They are the only motor boat allowed on the lake – water skiing, tubing - Fishing, fly fishing, swimming, non-motorized boating - Every counselor on staff is lifeguard certified - Free swim - Every day 4-6pm - Instructional swim – during activity periods. Mandatory until entering 8 th grade - Swim assessment for all campers at start of each session Overnights 4 2-night overnight trips for 9-10 th graders per session. Optional but no extra $$ 4 1-night overnight trips for 7 th -8 th graders per session. Optional but no extra $$ Very nature-oriented boys camp. Camp wide event held a few times during the session was taking place when we were there and all boys were spirited and dressed by color and into their games.

title: WIGWAM — SHARI — 8-8-17
text:
# WIGWAM

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Toured by Seth Moit (Bob Strauss out of camp with campers at Spelling Bee where many of the ME camps participate). 179 boys, 80% stay full summer, option to do 5 weeks, Bob is flexible but encourages 7 weeks.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

65% of campers are from DC to Maine, about 20 int’l boys, rest from other states. 50—60% of campers are jewish.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

7-12yr olds in bunks, 13+ in tents. OD in bunk/tent @ bedtime.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront (their \#1 activity). ALL BOYS MUST do waterfront, and all boys must do tennis or golf. Offer Lifeguard Cert for older campers.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Sundays boys sign up for sports for the week, 2 daily choices. Uniform on Sundays. Movie night on Saturdays and nightly evening snack.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Color war 4 days. Socials w/ Tapawingo, Walden (similar culturally), and Mataponi. Have fencing, a library, legos, rocketry (launch every Sunday), digital photo, video & radio. Other non-sports activities: Good a/c for boys camp (model clay, tie dye, duct tape art, glass fusing, silversmith, etc). Cooking club & for trips outdoor cooking/grilling, 1 big show/summer then talent shows & similar other performances, music (guitar, drums, harmonica-all performed at club night and talent shows). Climbing wall on property but also take to Monkey Trunks off camp 1x/week. Camp craft mandatory for younger campers. Overnight trips all optional, most do at least 1/summer.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Tick & lice checks weekly on Sundays. No religious services but challah on Fridays. Grace at meals is generic. Other religious observances available if needed. 1 seating in dining hall. Baker on prem 4-5 days/week, PB on head table, head chef in charge of all allergies, sit by cabin.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Philosophy: No cuts, if you show up you’re on the team\! “A play camp, not a sports camp.” Socials w/ Tapawingo, Walden (similar culturally), and Mataponi. We met a new 13 year old and he was very happy there. Dog on property unleashed.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Allowed packages, no nuts and include enough items for whole bunk. No restriction on phone calls but no homesick calls. Music only devices & handheld devices only in bunk. Travel logistics: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Wigwam is “A play camp, not a sports camp,” with 179 boys and a flexible session structure (80% stay full summer; option to do 5 weeks; Bob is flexible but encourages 7 weeks). No cuts—if you show up you’re on the team\! All boys must do tennis or golf, and waterfront (their \#1 activity), and they offer Lifeguard Cert for older campers. This camp will not work for “a child who wants to play 1 thing all day; if he wants air conditioning, doesn’t like spiders and is scared of animals in the woods Wigwam is wrong camp for him.”

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Waterfront is their \#1 activity; ALL BOYS MUST do waterfront, and they offer Lifeguard Cert for older campers.
  - Philosophy: No cuts, if you show up you’re on the team\! A play camp, not a sports camp.
  - Sundays boys sign up for sports for the week; 2 daily choices.
  - Color war 4 days.
  - Rich non-sports program: fencing, library, legos, rocketry (launch every Sunday), digital photo, video & radio; model clay, tie dye, duct tape art, glass fusing, silversmith, cooking club, music (guitar, drums, harmonica), shows and talent nights.
  - Climbing wall on property and Monkey Trunks off camp 1x/week; overnight trips optional (most do at least 1/summer).
  - Tick & lice checks weekly on Sundays; head chef in charge of all allergies; baker on prem 4-5 days/week.
  - Socials w/ Tapawingo, Walden (similar culturally), and Mataponi.
  - 7–12 yr olds in bunks, 13+ in tents; OD in bunk/tent @ bedtime.

  

#### Lowlights

  - This camp will not work for “a child who wants to play 1 thing all day.”
  - If he wants air conditioning, doesn’t like spiders and is scared of animals in the woods Wigwam is wrong camp for him.
  - 13+ in tents.
  - Dog on property unleashed.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Tapawingo
  - Walden
  - Mataponi

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Grace at meals is generic. 50—60% of campers are jewish & other religious observances available if needed -7-12yr olds in bunks, 13+ in tents. OD in bunk/tent @ bedtime -1seating in dining hall. Baker on prem 4-5 days/week, PB on head table, head chef in charge of all allergies, sit by cabin. -movie night on Saturdays and nightly evening snack. -Philosophy: No cuts, if you show up you’re on the team\! “A play camp, not a sports camp.” But ALL BOYS MUST do: tennis or golf, and waterfront (their \#1 activity). Offer Lifeguard Cert for older campers. -Other non-sports activities: Good a/c for boys camp (model clay, tie dye, duct tape art, glass fusing, silversmith, etc). Cooking club & for trips outdoor cooking/grilling, 1 big show/summer then talent shows & similar other performances, music (guitar, drums, harmonica-all performed at club night and talent shows), climbing wall on property but also take to Monkey Trunks off camp 1x/week. Camp craft mandatory for younger campers. Overnight trips all optional, most do at least 1/summer. -This camp will not work for “a child who wants to play 1 thing all day. title: Camp Pontiac — Allison — 7-13-23
text:
# Camp Pontiac

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Toured by Matt Etra (Kenny's son) and Lindsay Roth (Ricky's daughter). Co-ed 7 week traditional camp with a twist as kids don't move by bunk so it gives them more choice to try new things but not a true "choice" camp. Kids start at going into 2nd or 3rd (they bunk together) up to CIT's (going into Junior year) then Junior counselor. Kenny and Ricky bought camp in 1995 and are still involved but kids are day-to-day. Have about 500 kids.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Matt believes in not just having the best recreational activities for kids but everything should be "an A+" including the emotional and social needs of children. This is why he likes to have "adults" working at camp. Over 70 adults (parents) work at camp as program leads, directors and group leaders. In addition to "Camp Moms" for youngest boys they also have GOBS (girls on boy's side) to help them. Camper to counselor ratio is 2:1 for youngest kids then lessens as they get older. Kenny and Ricky bought camp in 1995 and are still involved but kids are day-to-day.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Almost everything has AC, including bunks. You then come upon the bunks of their oldest campers and junior counselors. Next is the boy's side. Boys have their own fields and two pools. They also have locker rooms where they store all their sports equipment. Each kid has a wooden plaque with their name over their cubby that they take home at the end of year and their player card on the wall. Keeps bunks from getting smelly. Each bunk has AC and large fans. They have cubbies for personal items and a large closet for clothes. Each bunk has 3 toilets, sinks and showers as well as a toothbrush holder to keep kid's organized. Each bunk also has updated electrical and sprinklers to keep kid's safe in case of fires. Kids bring their own for showers.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is a lake but they don't have motorized vehicles on it. Do have a waterpark with inflatables. Take kids off site to Copake lake for motorized water sports. Boy's side has two pools, girl's side has one. Girl's side pool has a beach area with lounge chairs and cabanas. Girls have to wear one piece. They provide towels at the waterfront.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Kids have two AM periods with 30 minutes in between then same after lunch. Kids don't move by bunk so it gives them more choice to try new things but not a true "choice" camp.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

As far as activities go, they have it all and often multiples as they might have one on girl's side and one on boy's side: Gymnastics, spin and dance are in one building. Under a covered pavilion we met a Dr. of Physical Therapy & Injury Prevention, leading a bunch of girls in a whole body work-out that focuses on improving balance. There are two craft buildings. One is more fine arts with pottery (they have a kiln) and glass blowing (which very few other camps have). The second arts building had very cool and trendy activities like tie dye, using resin to make chess boards and custom water bottles. They do two plays (one for older kids and one for younger kids). They also have a wood and model shop (although the kids are not actually allowed to use the equipment) as well as robotics and drones. Cooking center is very big and run by Matt's Mom, Karen, and she does trendy foods you might find on TikTok. Kids also get a cookbook of everything they made at the end of the summer. Other activities include: Golf range (serious kids go out of camp 2x/week to country club and it's extra money), horseback riding is also extra as they take kids down the street. There is a rock wall, ropes course, circus with trapeze, martial arts studio, pickleball, mini-golf course, indoor basketball, roller hockey and volleyball building. They also have a turf field that is lit for nighttime games. Trips: They have the treetop village which is set a bit away from camp where kids can "camp out" for a night. 6th grade and under go on day trips. 8th grade goes to Philly & DC, Oldest kids go out West. Trips are extra money.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Camp has a gate at security and all guests are asked to wear visitor tags. In center of the camp is the main office, Canteen and Dining Hall. Dining Hall has music playing and looks like a mall food court with different style foods (all Pontiac branded of course). Dining hall can fit whole camp but they often stagger it. Counselors get the food and bring it to tables on trays. They are nut and sesame free and can handle a diabetic child.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This camp screams spirit. Literally everything at camp is Red and White and emblazoned with a CP or named something with a "P." Camp is large but they've placed everything so it's easy to navigate. There is a large picnic area at the entrance where they have sibling bbqs weekly and lunch or dinner twice a week picnic style. To the left is the Movie theater that fits the whole camp and an ice cream shop. Ice cream shop is for older kids as a hang out spot and they take younger kids as special treats. Canteen is every night and looks like a 1950's diner. Has ping-pong, pool and arcade machines. Matt said a Pontiac kid doesn't have to be super sporty but they have to like sports. Based on the kids we saw (boy's in full baseball uniform and soccer inter-camps going on) it seems to be the majority do play sports. They play Grey Loch, Wahnee, Timber Lake, Lennox and Winadu.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. They do a modified "uniform": kids just have to wear something red, white or gray. Extra costs: Golf range (serious kids go out of camp 2x/week to country club and it's extra money), horseback riding is also extra as they take kids down the street. Trips are extra money.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Co-ed 7 week traditional camp with a twist as kids don't move by bunk so it gives them more choice to try new things but not a true "choice" camp. This camp screams spirit; literally everything at camp is Red and White and emblazoned with a CP or named something with a "P." Almost everything has AC, including bunks, and activities span gymnastics, dance, pottery with a kiln, glass blowing, robotics and drones, a ropes course, circus with trapeze, and a turf field that is lit for nighttime games. Matt said a Pontiac kid doesn't have to be super sporty but they have to like sports, and they play Grey Loch, Wahnee, Timber Lake, Lennox and Winadu.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Over 70 adults (parents) work at camp as program leads, directors and group leaders.
  - Almost everything has AC, including bunks.
  - Glass blowing (which very few other camps have).
  - Cooking center is run by Matt's Mom, Karen, with trendy foods you might find on TikTok; kids get a cookbook of everything they made.
  - Dining Hall looks like a mall food court (all Pontiac branded of course).
  - Gated security and visitor tags.
  - Turf field that is lit for nighttime games.
  - Canteen is every night and looks like a 1950's diner (ping-pong, pool and arcade machines).
  - Camper to counselor ratio is 2:1 for youngest kids then lessens as they get older.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Not a true "choice" camp.
  - Girls have to wear one piece.
  - Kids are not actually allowed to use the equipment in the wood and model shop.
  - Golf and horseback riding are extra; trips are extra money.
  - A Pontiac kid doesn't have to be super sporty but they have to like sports.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Timber Lake
  - IHC
  - Tyler Hill
  - Chippinaw
  - Trail's End

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: WHITE PINE — ASHLEY — 8-14-23
text:
# WHITE PINE

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp White Pine, Haliburton, Ontario. Co-ed 450 campers July session, smaller in August; 250 stay all summer.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

40-50 kids from the USA (California, Florida, NY, DC, Pittsburgh). Toured by Jen Ostfield, Admin Director and Adam Kronick, Director. Ashley Garson and Brooke Rotstein.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Ski is HUGE—Lots of H2O sports. BIG lakefront. Mandatory Canoe trips 1st month.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 activity periods—3 are with their cabins. Midnight special program (July session). All Day Program/Color War (August Session). Music Lunch (Big dance party). Bro/sis Lunch (Get to sit with siblings).

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Every activity you could think of, BIG arts program (director's sister is an artist). Offer Private music lessons and have an awesome rock band program (Kids sign up for the band, they have special practices, and perform at different events at camp).

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Professional chef and pastry chef, food is outstanding. Peanut/tree nut free (all camps in Canada are). Eat with cabins, mix of family style and buffet, big salad bar at lunch/dinner.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall, beautiful camp, facilities are gorgeous, BIG lakefront, etc. Has a true family feeling and the directors are so warm. This is an amazing option if someone is looking to go somewhere different. People are very “midwest” feeling.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Have a shuttle from YYZ (Toronto Pearson) airport. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp White Pine in Haliburton, Ontario is a co-ed camp with 450 campers in the July session (smaller in August) and 250 who stay all summer. Overall, beautiful camp, facilities are gorgeous, BIG lakefront, etc. Has a true family feeling and the directors are so warm. This is an amazing option if someone is looking to go somewhere different.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Ski is HUGE—Lots of H2O sports
  - BIG lakefront
  - Every activity you could think of, BIG arts program (director's sister is an artist)
  - Offer Private music lessons
  - Awesome rock band program
  - Mandatory Canoe trips 1st month
  - Professional chef and pastry chef, food is outstanding
  - Peanut/tree nut free (all camps in Canada are)
  - Big salad bar at lunch/dinner
  - Music Lunch (Big dance party)
  - Bro/sis Lunch (Get to sit with siblings)
  - 5 activity periods—3 are with their cabins
  - Have a shuttle from YYZ (Toronto Pearson) airport
  - Has a true family feeling and the directors are so warm
  - Overall, beautiful camp, facilities are gorgeous

  

#### Lowlights

  - Living quarters: Unknown
  - Parent communication: Unknown
  - Tech: Unknown
  - Extra costs: Unknown

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Timberlane
  - Tamakwa
  - Wahanowin
  - Manitou
  - Walden (Michigan)

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp White Pine Haliburton, Ontario Ashley Garson and Brooke Rotstein Toured Toured by Jen Ostfield, Admin Drector and Adam Kronick, Director Co-Ed 450 campers July session, smaller in August 250 stay all summer 40-50 kids from the USA (California, Florida, NY, DC, Pittsburgh) Have a shuttle from YYZ (Toronto Pearson) aiport 5 activity periods—3 are with their cabins Ski is HUGE—Lots of H2O sports Every activity you could think of, BIG arts program (directors sister is an artist) Mandatory Canoe trips 1 st month Offer Private music lessons and have an awesome rock band program (Kids sign up for the band, they have special practices, and perform at different events at camp) Midnight special program (July session) All Day Program/Color War (August Session) Music Lunch (Big dance party) Bro/sis Lunch (Get to sit with siblings) Professional chef and pastry chef, food is outstanding Peanut/tree nut free (all camps in Canada are) Eat with cabins, mix of family style and buffet, big salad bar at lunch/dinner Overall, beautiful camp, facilities are gorgeous, BIG lakefront, etc. Has a true family feeling and the directors are so warm. This is an amazing option if someone is looking to go somewhere different. I would give with the other camps in the area (Timberlane, Tamakwa, Wahanowin, Manitou), as well as other haimish, co-ed camps (Would definitely give with Walden (Michigan). People are very “midwest” feeling.
