title: Pontiac — Emily — 7-14-23
text:
# Pontiac

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Co-ed and uniform. Campers wear Red, White, Grey or Black. 500 kids in total. Security at the camp gate.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Pontiac – Toured with Michael Etra (Owner/Director), Lara and Emily Salant. Family run: Stacey is responsible for parent liaison duties. Lindsay oversees the girls, and Michael takes care of the boys. 33% LI, 25% Westchester, 25% New Jersey, 15% Florida, and 10% Maryland with handfuls of kids in LA and Colorado. Around 50% of the staff members are camp alumni, and 35% are international. CIT campers are in transition and will be entering 12th grade as JCs (Junior Counselors).

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunking: Each bunk consists of 13 to 14 kids, maintaining a 1:2 ratio. In the lower camp, the campers do not choose their own clothes; the camp selects the attire. The campers are not allowed in the cubby room. The shower area has 5 showers, 4 sinks, and 4 wood beds. New campers are mixed with returning campers. Bunk changes are possible. Rugs and fat heads are not allowed.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There are two pool sides, two lake sides, and a water park side. They go water-skiing at Copake Lake. Swimming instructions are available for children up to sixth grade. Campers wear one-piece bathing suits or tankinis, and the swimming pools are separated by gender.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 5 periods. 2 AM, 2 PM and general swim. Private lessons are available for all activities during the rest hour. In the morning, there is a meeting where sign-ups occur. Clinics are one-week sessions with the same activities. Selective activities are offered twice a week as electives. Fridays are dedicated to special events, with novelty events for girls and sports-related activities for boys, including a half day of Davis Cup tennis.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

There are two plays performed at the camp. The camp has an outdoor amphitheater for performances. The fields vary in size to accommodate different age groups. Golf is offered as a half-day activity once a week, and additional activities include ice hockey and horseback riding, each for 1 hour and a half. Pickleball – 6 courts.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical: There are approximately 10 to 12 nurses and 2 doctors at the camp. Separate health centers are available for girls and boys. They also have a mental health team which is proactive in addressing campers' mental well-being. Dining: 6 halls in one building, and meals are served family-style. Girls have separate line-ups for the lower and upper camps, and seating is rotated within each division. The camp accommodates all allergies and currently has 3 diabetic campers, not accepting more now. The camp canteen is open every day. Barbecue meals are provided 2 to 3 times a week for dinner. Security at the camp gate.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Family run with clear roles and oversight. Co-ed and uniform; campers wear Red, White, Grey or Black. Gender-separated swimming pools. Fridays highlight special events (novelty events for girls and sports-related activities for boys). Two plays and an outdoor amphitheater add to camp traditions.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Photos/Communication: 3 photographers / 3 videographers. Group leaders carry phones and take pictures as they walk around the camp. 2 phone calls are allowed before and after visiting day. Travel logistics: Unknown. Tech policy for campers: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Family run and co-ed, Pontiac is a uniform camp where campers wear Red, White, Grey or Black. There are 500 kids in total, with security at the camp gate and a robust medical team with approximately 10 to 12 nurses and 2 doctors plus a proactive mental health team. Programming runs 5 periods (2 AM, 2 PM and general swim) with sign-ups each morning, electives and one-week clinics, and private lessons during rest hour. Waterfront is strong with two pool sides, two lake sides, a water park side, and water-skiing at Copake Lake, alongside highlights like two plays, an outdoor amphitheater, golf, ice hockey, horseback riding, and 6 pickleball courts.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Family run leadership (Stacey parent liaison; Lindsay oversees girls; Michael takes care of boys).
  - Two pool sides, two lake sides, and a water park side; water-skiing at Copake Lake.
  - Programming: 5 periods with morning sign-ups, electives offered twice a week, and one-week clinics; private lessons during rest hour.
  - Medical depth: approximately 10 to 12 nurses and 2 doctors; separate health centers for girls and boys; proactive mental health team.
  - Dining: 6 halls in one building, family-style, canteen open every day; barbecue dinner 2–3 times a week.
  - Performing arts: two plays and an outdoor amphitheater.
  - Sports variety: golf (half day once a week), ice hockey, horseback riding (each for 1 hour and a half), and Pickleball – 6 courts.
  - Media/communication: 3 photographers/3 videographers; group leaders carry phones and take pictures; 2 phone calls allowed before and after visiting day.
  - Bunking flexibility: new campers mixed with returning campers; bunk changes are possible.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Lower camp: campers do not choose their own clothes; the camp selects the attire.
  - Campers are not allowed in the cubby room.
  - Rugs and fat heads are not allowed.
  - Swimming pools are separated by gender.
  - Currently has 3 diabetic campers, not accepting more now.
  - Uniform requirement (Red, White, Grey or Black).
  - Bunk shower area note: 5 showers, 4 sinks, and 4 wood beds.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Timber Lake
  - Lokanda
  - Trails End
  - Tyler Hill
  - IHC
  - Chipinaw
  - Taconic
  - All girls: Blue Ridge/Bryn Mawr

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Pontiac – Toured with Michael Etra Owner Director Lara and Emily Salant Toured with Family Run: Stacey is responsible for parent liaison duties. Lindsay oversee the girls, and Michael takes care of the boys. Security at the camp gate Co-ed and Uniform. Campers wear Red, White, Grey or Black. 500 kids in total. 33% LI, 25% Westchester, 25% New Jersey, 15% Florida, and 10% Maryland with handfuls of kids in LA and Colorado Around 50% of the staff members are camp alumni, and 35% are international. CIT campers are in transition and will be entering 12th grade as JCs (Junior Counselors). Programming: 5 periods. 2 AM, 2 PM and general swim. Private lessons are available for all activities during the rest hour. In the morning, there is a meeting where sign-ups occur. Clinics one-week sessions with the same activities. Selective activities are offered twice a week as electives. Water: There are two pool sides, two lake sides, and a water park side. They go water-skiing at Copake Lake. Swimming instructions are available for children up to sixth grade. Campers wear one-piece bathing suits or tankinis, and the swimming pools are separated by gender. Photos/Communication: 3 photographers / 3 videographers. Group leaders carry phones and take pictures as they walk around the camp. 2 Phone calls are allowed before and after visiting day. Bunking : Each bunk consists of 13 to 14 kids, maintaining a 1:2 ratio . In the lower camp, the campers do not choose their own clothes; the camp selects the attire. The campers are not allowed in the cubby room. The shower area has 5 showers, 4 sinks, and 4 wood beds. New campers are mixed with returning campers. Bunk changes are possible. Rugs and fat heads are not allowed. Dining: 6 halls in one building, and meals are served family-style. The camp has an outdoor amphitheater for performances. The fields vary in size to accommodate different age groups. Golf is offered as a half-day activity once a week, and additional activities include ice hockey and horseback riding, each for 1 hour and a half. Pickleball – 6 Courts Pontiac, can also look at: timber Lake, Lokanda, trails end, Tyler hill, IHC, possibly Chipinaw and possibly taconic if it’s about location. All girls: blue ridge/bryn mawr

title: WORLD STRIDES-OXBRIDGE — DENISE — 11-20-20
text:
# WORLD STRIDES/OXBRIDGE

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Founded about 37 years ago by a university professor Richard Michaelis – Executive Director. 1-month program in Oxford, Cambridge, Paris, Montpellier, Barcelona, Salamanca, NYC, with a Paris connection (add-on) for 1 week for UK, Montpellier & Barcelona programs. There is a 2-week option in Cambridge and in August, Oxford has 1 or 2 week intense and concentrated courses. Hoping to add Italy in the future. Also, online programming to be available in the next couple of months. Grades 8-12, but they try to look at age rather than grade. (8-9 preps, 10-12 traditions) All classes are taught by university professors, researchers, professionals (ultimately people who can speak to the experience of the career and experts in their fields). Oxbridge does provide grade reports and info/details for the students’ transcripts when the program is complete.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Students come from all over the world representing 80 to 90 countries. Very international program. The students are usually academic, good students that tend to apply and go to good universities. Anyone can apply to the program, but they tend to be good students, looking for international travel experience and excited by learning. Part of the application process is to submit a 500-word essay of why they want to attend, what subject they want to study, and what they want to get out of the program. These statements are collected and given to the teachers, who then create a syllabus from what the students are looking for. Their faculty is vast and very experienced. They list each faculty member on their website. All of these extra programs are run by a team of professional educators whose sole job is to create these specialized programs. Founded by a university professor Richard Michaelis – Executive Director.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Students will have single rooms and fewer students in residences in general. They will try to give private bathrooms where they can. Room type and housing setup outside of Covid adjustments: Unknown. Food service/meals: Unknown.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Intense programs where they pick a major subject that is studied 6 days a week for 3.5 hours in the morning and then a minor for a couple of hours in the afternoon. Taught differently than a typical classroom setting, where the learning is experiential, project based, hands on, and uses the town resources as much as possible. They teach the subject in the town where they are studying and tailor the subjects to the venue (for example, Oxford is an easy place to teach history by visiting sites, museums, etc.). When not in class, there are hundreds of activities offered throughout the month to keep the students busy and engaged. They are given choices of activities every day (sports, Ted Talks, museum visits, expert lecturers, etc.). There are also big trips throughout the program to see major sites (London, Stonehenge, Eiffel Tower, etc.) as well as fun time activities like dances/parties, yoga, talent nights, etc. The teachers then create a syllabus from what the students are looking for. Essentially, they teach to the expectations of the students.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

This is what makes them stand out from their competitors - teaching programs through experiential learning, cultural/language immersion and intellectual immersion. Another way that Oxbridge stands out from competitors is the large range of subjects they offer (over 150 different choices). Examples include “Culture & Cuisine” in Paris, Barcelona and Montpellier or “Fashion” in Paris, NYC and Barcelona. Language immersion programs are capped at 40-50 students because they want to make sure the students are always speaking French or Spanish. This program is equivalent to an entire year of language learning. Their faculty is vast and very experienced.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

All staff have criminal and safety checks before hired. There is a zero-tolerance policy and kids have been sent home for breaking the rules. They are very strict with the important things (drugs, alcohol, safety, etc.). Covid-19 – they will limit class size (normally 8-15) to 8 max. Students will have single rooms and fewer students in residences in general, and they will try to give private bathrooms where they can. Mealtimes are still uncertain and will need to wait until closer to the date of program. Will hire extra staff for each program to deal with Covid-19. If there is a safe vaccine available, it will most likely be required before the start of the trip, as will Covid-19 testing. Since they are under their parent company, World Strides, they will follow their medical protocols as well as protocols from the authorities. Food details beyond mealtime status: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They want the kids to have a university experience. The goal of the program is to boost student morale and self-confidence to who they can be and who they will become. This is more important than getting straight As and just doing all the work in the typical classroom setting that they experience all year long. They do not want the student to feel pressured, since this is the summer and they are usually learning about subjects they can’t take during the normal school year. Very international program.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation to the programs is organized and traveled by the individual students. When they apply to the program, they can share their travel arrangements, and if another student is traveling from the same city, they can organize to travel together. Oxbridge will pick every student up from the airport and deliver them to their program, and they bring them back to the airport at the end of the program as well. There will not be a full refund due to Covid-19 this year. Students are encouraged to buy the insurance ($199) and then will just lose their deposit ($500). Deposits can be used for next year and/or transferred to a sibling. Apply before Jan. 31st and you get a discount of $500 off your trip. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Tuition and other extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Founded about 37 years ago by a university professor, Richard Michaelis – Executive Director, Oxbridge offers 1-month programs in Oxford, Cambridge, Paris, Montpellier, Barcelona, Salamanca, and NYC, with a Paris connection add-on and 2-week options in Cambridge and short August intensives in Oxford. Grades 8-12 choose a major studied 6 days a week for 3.5 hours in the morning and a minor in the afternoon, taught by university professors, researchers, and professionals through experiential, project-based, hands-on learning tailored to the city, with over 150 different choices. Students from 80 to 90 countries enjoy a university experience with daily choice activities and big trips, and Oxbridge provides grade reports and transcript details at the end. The program emphasizes cultural/language and intellectual immersion and student confidence rather than pressure, with strict safety standards and airport pickup/return; language immersion tracks are capped to ensure speaking French or Spanish.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Experiential, location-based teaching that uses town resources and experts.
  - Over 150 different subject choices, including specialized tracks like Culture & Cuisine and Fashion.
  - International cohort representing 80 to 90 countries.
  - Faculty are university professors, researchers, and professionals; vast and experienced.
  - Structured major/minor model with daily choice activities and big trips.
  - Airport pickup and return included.
  - Language immersion capped at 40–50 and equivalent to a year of language learning.
  - Application essay informs the syllabus; teachers teach to student expectations.
  - Strict safety culture with background checks and zero-tolerance policies; follows World Strides medical protocols.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Waterfront and aquatics: Unknown.
  - Food specifics and mealtimes are uncertain; food details otherwise unknown.
  - No full refunds due to Covid-19; reliance on insurance and deposit loss.
  - Travel to programs is organized by individual students/families.
  - Tech policy and parent communication details: Unknown.
  - Housing details outside of Covid adjustments: Unknown.
  - Online programming noted as coming in the next couple of months (availability timing unclear).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Summerfuel
  - Oxford Royale
  - Summer Discovery

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Founded about 37 years ago by a university professor Richard Michaelis– Executive Director 1-month program in Oxford, Cambridge, Paris, Montpellier, Barcelona, Salamanca, NYC with a Paris connection (add-on) for 1 week for UK, Montpellier & Barcelona programs. There is a 2- week option in Cambridge and in August, Oxford has 1 or 2 week intense and concentrated courses. Hoping to add Italy in the future. Also, online programming to be available in the next couple of months. Grades 8-12, but they try to look at age rather than grade. (8-9 preps, 10-12 traditions) Intense programs where they pick a major subject that is studied 6 days a week for 3.5 hours in the morning and then a minor for a couple of hours in the afternoon. Taught differently than a typical classroom setting, where the learning is experiential, project based, hands on, and use the town resources as much as possible. They teach the subject in the town where they are studying. They are able to tailor the subjects to the venue. For ex. Oxford is an easy place to teach history by visiting sites, museums, etc. This is what makes them stand out from their competitors - teaching programs through experiential learning, cultural/language immersion and intellectual immersion. Another way that Oxbridge stands out from competitors is the large range of subjects they offer (over 150 different choices). Examples of some of the programs are “Culture & Cuisine” in Paris, Barcelona and Montpellier or “Fashion” in Paris, NYC and Barcelona. Their faculty is vast and very experienced. They list each faculty member on their website. Students come from all over the world representing 80 to 90 countries. Very international program. title: Runoia — Heather — 3-6-24
text:
# Runoia

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

All girls choice-based program located in MAINE. Offers three sessions (1 st starts late June-mid July, 2 nd starts the next consecutive day after first session campers leave-mid August, Full session runs throughout). Most campers stay for one session. Non-denominational, with international campers. Approximately 150 campers, 70 adults. Harmony Camp: for 6-10 year olds-their Rookie Camp-3 weeks; runs concurrently with second session of main camp.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

All girls. Non-denominational, with international campers. Approximately 150 campers, 70 adults. Director profile: Unknown.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Shower Houses: Dedicated shower time scheduled with three shower houses with four shower stalls each and individual changing rooms. Harmony Camp: Bathrooms and Showers in newer cabins.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Offers Advanced Skipper and Windsurfing Queen.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Most campers stay for one session and choose 4 periods from 30+ activities in 2- and 3-day blocks each week with emphasis on independence, teamwork and self-confidence. Unstructured, free play every day.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Offers: Advanced Equestrian, Advanced Skipper, Windsurfing Queen, American Archer, Queen of the Shot; more traditional summer camp activities, including arts and crafts, 40 foot climbing tower, and offer sports but are not a ‘sports camp’. There is a separate Equestrian program for all levels of achievement, with their own herd of horses and some leases. Harmony Camp: for 6-10 year olds-their Rookie Camp-3 weeks; runs concurrently with second session of main camp.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health and Medical: On staff health team attends to any on-camp needs; needs cared for by doctors, orthopedists, and other medical specialists who are within a 5-20 minute drive; local ambulance service staffed with trained EMT available at all times. Maine General Hospital in Augusta is go-to emergency facility. Food: Fresh fruit, yogurt and cereal bar, twice daily salad bar available. 2/3 of meals together in Dining Hall in mixed age family style seating, 1/3 meals picnic style. Special dietary restrictions met with ease; vegetarian meals available at every meal.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Choice-based program with emphasis on independence, teamwork and self-confidence. Unstructured, free play every day. Non-denominational, with international campers.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Runoia is an all girls, choice-based program located in Maine. It offers three sessions (1st starts late June–mid July, 2nd starts the next consecutive day after first session campers leave–mid August, and a Full session runs throughout), and most campers stay for one session. Campers choose 4 periods from 30+ activities in 2- and 3-day blocks each week, with unstructured, free play every day and an emphasis on independence, teamwork and self-confidence. Signature offerings include Advanced Equestrian (with a separate equestrian program and their own herd), Advanced Skipper, Windsurfing Queen, American Archer, Queen of the Shot, plus traditional activities like arts and crafts and a 40 foot climbing tower.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All girls choice-based program with 30+ activities and daily free play.
  - Three-session structure with most campers attending one session.
  - Approx. 150 campers and 70 adults.
  - Strong equestrian program with own herd and some leases; advanced recognition tracks (Advanced Skipper, Windsurfing Queen, American Archer, Queen of the Shot).
  - Dedicated shower houses with individual changing rooms; newer cabins with bathrooms/showers for Harmony Camp.
  - Robust food options (fresh fruit/yogurt/cereal bar; twice-daily salad bar; vegetarian meals at every meal).
  - On-staff health team; nearby medical specialists 5–20 minutes away; EMT ambulance access; Maine General Hospital in Augusta for emergencies.
  - Harmony Camp: Rookie Camp for 6–10 year olds (3 weeks) during second session.
  - Non-denominational, with international campers.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Unknown: director profile and leadership tenure.
  - Unknown: travel logistics to camp.
  - Unknown: parent communication practices and tech policy.
  - Unknown: extra costs and tuition details.
  - Not a ‘sports camp’ (offers sports but does not position as one).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Quinebarge — Alyssa — 1-17-24
text:
# Quinebarge

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Zoom led by co-director Nick Hercules on 1/17/2024. Nick described Quinebarge as the "most traditional camp you can find." He could not stress enough how the community is very accepting and they pride themselves on being intentionally diverse and inclusive bringing in campers from all backgrounds. Nick stated that they want all of their campers to be accepted and they take this very seriously. Their "secret sauce" is making sure that all kids make at least 1 friend, if not 2-3.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

The typical Quinebarge camper is the "misunderstood kid or the outcast with not many friends." Nick really acknowledged that at Quinebarge these kids will be treated like normal kids finally. Super sporty kids would not do well at Quinebarge as sport instruction and intercamps are generally not offered. DEMOGRAPHICS: ages 6-16 years; 100-120 kids/session with 60 staff members; 80% are 2 week campers. They run 4/6/8 week options but a very low % attend beyond 4 weeks. 75% are from NY/New England with lots of Boston kids. 90-95% of staff is international. Nick mentioned they do have a group of Spanish speaking campers when asked. A minimum of 20% of campers and staff identify with the LGBTQ+ community. Co-director: Nick Hercules.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

BUNKS: all staff are activity specialists and cabin counselors. There are 6-8 campers with 2-3 staff members per bunk so a very small camper:staff ratio. Girls’ bunks have bathrooms and boys’ bunks have showerhouses. There are yurts on camp. Please note that transgendered campers are bunked by what each camper identifies as and this goes for staff as well. Nick stated very clearly that policies related to this matter are well marked on the camp website. They will not out any camper or staff member according to Nick.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lakefront includes tubing/boating/inflatable course the size of a football field. There is no pool. Swim lessons are in the lake and required daily up to age 15 for 20-30 min. They have 2 motorboats for tubing and are considering adding waterskiing for summer 2024.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING: all choice and choose every day because they want campers to want to show up to activities and make new friends.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They offer activities in adventure (ropes/climbing/ninja course), recreational sports including tennis with clay courts, archery, nature (including chickens and rabbits), arts (including woodshop/ceramics/drama), and lakefront (including tubing/boating/inflatable course the size of a football field). They do have horseback riding and it is included in tuition. Sport instruction and intercamps are generally not offered.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

DINING: Meals are served buffet style with the aim to have a protein/starch/vegetable on each plate for the hot meal. There is a salad bar also lunch/dinner. At breakfast there is a yogurt/cereal bar along with the hot meal. They can accommodate gluten free/vegetarian/vegan diets. MEDICAL: They have 2 nurses on staff at all times. One of them lives in the health center 24/7. Medications are distributed in the mornings and evenings. They use CampMinder for this. They are 15 minutes from the nearest hospital. According to Nick "a lot of their kids are ADD/ADHD" and they can handle ASD campers depending on their level of verbal communication.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very accepting community that prides itself on being intentionally diverse and inclusive, bringing in campers from all backgrounds. They want all of their campers to be accepted and they take this very seriously. Their "secret sauce" is making sure that all kids make at least 1 friend, if not 2-3. The typical camper is the misunderstood kid or the outcast with not many friends who will be treated like normal kids finally.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

TRANSPORTATION: they do not provide organized transportation from any one location but will provide airport pickups at a cost of $250 (will work with families if they cannot afford this fee). PRICING: $4275 for a 2 week session. There is a 1st time camper discount of 10% that is very clearly marked on the website. Nick estimates that 99% of campers do not pay full price and more than half get extra help. If a camper is flying to camp and needs an airport pickup but cannot afford the cost they will figure it out. They are very aware of the financial burden of camp and do not want to turn anyone away that may not fulfill their mission of diversity and inclusion. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Quinebarge is the "most traditional camp you can find" with an intentionally diverse and inclusive community where they want all campers to be accepted. Programming is all choice and campers choose every day, with activities in adventure, arts, nature, recreational sports, and a big lakefront (no pool) where swim lessons are required daily up to age 15 for 20-30 minutes. Typical ages are 6-16 with 100-120 kids per session, small bunks (6-8 campers with 2-3 staff), and a minimum of 20% of campers and staff identify with the LGBTQ+ community. Super sporty kids would not do well here, but the "misunderstood kid or the outcast with not many friends" will find a very accepting place focused on making sure every child makes at least 1 friend, if not 2-3; tuition is $4,275 for 2 weeks with a 10% first-time discount and extensive financial help.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Nick described Quinebarge as the "most traditional camp you can find."
  - They pride themselves on being intentionally diverse and inclusive bringing in campers from all backgrounds.
  - Their "secret sauce" is making sure that all kids make at least 1 friend, if not 2-3.
  - Programming is all choice and campers choose every day.
  - Horseback riding is included in tuition.
  - Lakefront includes tubing/boating and an inflatable course the size of a football field.
  - Swim lessons are in the lake and required daily up to age 15 for 20-30 min.
  - There are 6-8 campers with 2-3 staff members per bunk.
  - A minimum of 20% of campers and staff identify with the LGBTQ+ community.
  - 90-95% of staff is international.
  - 99% of campers do not pay full price and more than half get extra help.
  - They will not out any camper or staff member.
  - They have 2 motorboats and are considering adding waterskiing for summer 2024.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Super sporty kids would not do well at Quinebarge as sport instruction and intercamps are generally not offered.
  - There is no pool.
  - Boys’ bunks have showerhouses.
  - They do not provide organized transportation from any one location.
  - Airport pickups cost $250 (will work with families if they cannot afford this fee).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Pinecliffe — Lindsey — 8-16-24
text:
# Pinecliffe

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

200 girls. At capacity. Some rising 2nd but bulk of kids come rising 3 or 4th. Only camp on the lake; say it’s a “residential lake”. Offer all sports. Everything is included. One Time 4 week but very limited and most kids stay.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Sue — owner; Patti; daughter Marcy; daughter-in-law (married to Sue’s son). Marcy met us. Maja (Maya) does tours. Campers give the tours with Patti or Marci etc. Some former campers as counselors. Many Americans as counselors. 50/50 international staff. Men as coaches. Kitchen staff all from Mexico. All counselors specialist and go up with the ages. No cit program. Must leave for a few years. Will take older new kids and do ok. They are used to getting new kids hear and there. Mix up bunks every year. Older girls don’t walk with staff. But younger kids do.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks have electricity. 2 toilets and 2 sinks. Oldest girls have shower in bunk; otherwise shower house. Uniform bedding up through 6th grade… “Then they get pb teen comforter orwhatever”. Bring towels. The pack - gymnastics but can fit all of camp in here. Air conditioned\!\!\!\! Ac in infirmary, gymnastics, library.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Everyone swims 2x a day. Lessons daily. Waterskiing and kneeboarding and tubing (no wakeboarding or wake surfing). Pontoon boat with a slide. One blow up they put out for 2nd half of the summer. US sailing. American canoe certification. Both optional. Can do lifeguard certification. Only camp on the lake; say it’s a “residential lake”.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule - Mix of assigned (either group or cabin… maybe 2 ages combined) and then an elective. Afternoon is similar. Electives give extra time for practice. They have trips that go out daily. Camping etc. Canoe trips. Snacks during the day. 11 am - cheese stick, granola bar, goldfish etc. Wednesday and Sunday nights can wear clothes and to socials. Picnic Wednesday and sundays out here.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Offer all sports: Softball, basketball, lax, field hockey, soccer, volleyball, track and field. Kids love sports but don’t know how to do it at 8 and 9. Professional coaches. All have coaching qualification. Jewelry making. 7 shows in 7 weeks (musicals). Everyone can participate. You don’t have to do anything you don’t want. A capella. Used to host a capella competition. Color war all summer. Captains elected but position for every Senior girl who wants one. Saturday and Sundays all summer. Brown and blue. Siblings and cousins same team. Diff teams every year. Camp government they call cabinet with campers. Bouldering center with certified instructor. The pack - gymnastics but can fit all of camp in here. Air conditioned\!\!\!\! Archery. Everyone has a camp sister. Will meet before camp and get their first “Ellie and teddy”.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

4 pediatric rns, urgent care. Doctors on weekends. Dining hall: Everyone eats at once. Family Style. Changes tables every week. Peanut aware. Can handle allergies. Have pbj station. Vegetarian yes. Hesitated but said yes to gluten free. Buns in wrapper etc. Ac in infirmary.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Can’t talk about my campers but pinecliffe welcomes all kids. The world is changing. Nondenominational services - changes nights. 80% Jewish. All cheering at assembly. Uniform every day. One piece bathing suit. Even that is uniform. But on weekend they can wear their own and no one does. Oldest girls get their own swag. Wednesday and Sunday nights can wear clothes and to socials. Very spirited with Beads and face paint. Everyone has a camp sister. Will meet before camp and get their first “Ellie and teddy”. Siblings and cousins same team. Diff teams every year. Older girls don’t walk with staff. But younger kids do.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

The brothers go everywhere. Andro, Winnebago, takahoe, cedar, skylemar, wildwood. Have done things with modin. Tristate, Boston, Philly, la and San Fran, Texas, Chicago. Westchester bus. Visiting day coming up on Friday. Passed family touring. Dad in Princeton shirt and mom in jean shorts and golden goose. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

At capacity with 200 girls, Pinecliffe serves some rising 2nd but the bulk of kids come rising 3 or 4th. Only camp on the lake — a “residential lake” — with everyone swimming 2x a day and lessons daily, plus waterskiing, kneeboarding and tubing (no wakeboarding or wake surfing). They offer all sports with professional coaches, 7 shows in 7 weeks, color war all summer, trips that go out daily, and electives that give extra time for practice. Family Style dining, peanut aware with allergy accommodations, 4 pediatric RNs and doctors on weekends, and a uniform every day create a structured, spirited culture with camp sisters and lots of cheering at assembly.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Only camp on the lake; say it’s a “residential lake”.
  - Everyone swims 2x a day; lessons daily.
  - Professional coaches; all have coaching qualification.
  - US sailing and American canoe certification (optional).
  - Can do lifeguard certification.
  - 7 shows in 7 weeks (musicals); everyone can participate.
  - Color war all summer with roles for every Senior girl who wants one.
  - Bouldering center; The pack (gymnastics) can fit all of camp; air conditioned.
  - Family Style dining with table changes weekly; peanut aware; can handle allergies; PBJ station; vegetarian yes.
  - 4 pediatric RNs; urgent care; doctors on weekends.
  - Camp sister program; very spirited with beads and face paint.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No cit program; must leave for a few years.
  - One Time 4 week but very limited and most kids stay.
  - No wakeboarding or wake surfing.
  - Uniform every day, including one-piece bathing suit.
  - Uniform bedding up through 6th grade.
  - Hesitated but said yes to gluten free.
  - Mix up bunks every year.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Andro
  - Winnebago
  - Takahoe
  - Cedar
  - Skylemar
  - Wildwood
  - Modin

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

At capacity Some rising 2nd but bulk of kids come rising 3 or 4th The brothers go everywhere. Andro, Winnebago, takahoe, cedar, skylemar, wildwood Have done things with modin. Tristate, Boston, Philly, la and San Fran, Texas, Chicago Westchester bus Some former campers as counselors Many Americans as counselors Maja (maya) does tours Dining hall Everyone eats at once. Family Style. Changes tables every week Peanut aware Can handle allergies. Have pbj station Vegetarian yes. Hesitated but said yes to gluten free. Buns in wrapper etc Bunks have electricity 4 pediatric rns, urgent care Doctors on weekends Snacks during the day. 11 am - cheese stick, granola bar, goldfish etc Offer all sports Softball, basketball, lax, field hockey, soccer, volleyball, track and field Kids love sports but don’t know how to do it at 8 and 9 Electives give extra time for practice Everything is included Professional coaches All have coaching qualification Can do lifeguard certification Everyone swims 2x a day. Lessons daily Only camp on the lake Say it’s a “residential lake” Waterskiing and kneeboarding and tubing (no wakeboarding or wake surfing) Pontoon boat with a slide One blow up they put out for 2nd half of the summer One Time 4 week but very limited and most kids stay Jewelry making Campers give the tours with Patti or Marci etc Schedule- Mix of assigned (either group or cabin… maybe 2 ages combined) and the an elective Afternoon is similar. They have trips that go out daily Camping etc Canoe trips Rising 9th - bar harbor Rising 10th - go to Quebec No cit program Must leave for a few years Will take older new kids and do ok They are used to getting new kids hear and there Mix up bunks every year US sailing American canoe certification Both optional Men as coaches 7 shows in 7 weeks (musicals) Everyone can participate You don’t have to do anything you don’t want A capella Used to host a capella competition Color war all summer Captains elected but position for every Senior girl who wants one Saturday and Sundays all summer Brown and blue Siblings and cousins same team Diff teams every year Camp government they call cabinet with campers Bouldering center with certified instructor. The pack - gymnastics but can fit all of camp in here. Air conditioned\!\!\!\! Ac in infirmary, gymnastics, library Archery Can’t talk about my campers but pinecliffe welcomes all kids. The world is changing. Nondenominational services - changes nights 80% Jewish All cheering at assembly Uniform every day One piece bathing suit Even that is uniform But on weekend they can wear their own and no one does Oldest girls get their own swag Wednesday and Sunday nights can wear clothes and to socials Bring backpacks to activity with their stuff Bring towels Uniform bedding up through 6th grade… “Then they get pb teen comforter orwhatever” 2 toilets and 2 sinks Oldest girls have shower in bunk Otherwise shower house Everyone has a camp sister Will meet before camp and get their first “Ellie and teddy” Very spirited with Beads and face paint Older girls don’t walk with staff. title: WILDWOOD — LANA — 9-30-23
text:
# WILDWOOD

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

173 total campers - they want more. Max 200. Offer 4 week session - 38 4 week campers. Allowed to come back as 4 week second summer but rare. Biggest population from NYC/Westchester. Also NJ/SFO/Boston. Sister camp Pinecliffe. Also have sisters at Fernwood and Mataponi. Not Walden bc attract different families.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by owner Louis (live in Armonk and was former camper) who has owner for 6 years and wife Chelsea who went to Blue Ridge. Teddy coowns. Louis and Chelsea have 3 boys under 7. Both are teachers during year. We toured when most kids were at cedar for tournament. Female staff impt. 8/9/10 year old. Each bunk has.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

5-6 kids/bunk. 2 staff. Sink/toilet in bunk. Shower houses. Toying with idea of towel service for next year. Road goes thru middle of camp. 5 private houses at end. Creek runs thru lower camp and senior camp.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

2 of those are tennis/waterfront. Canoeing.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 periods/day. 2 of those are tennis/waterfront. 3 periods of elective. Only with age group. Choose from a list. Offer ice hockey tues/thurs nights 1.5 hrs at Bridgton sports hockey is extra. Every Wednesday trip day. Every group does overnight camp out. 12+ goes longer trips. Bar harbor Vermont Montreal.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They say camp is as competitive as you want it to be. Try to serve sporty and non sporty kids. Arts /crafts. Ceramics woodworking. Podcasting and interviews of week. Music-percussion/guitar. Box and mountain biking course. Outdoor adventure. Hip hop dance. Tournaments with Cedar skylemar takajo andro.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Full time doctor on staff and 3 nurses. Dining hall sit by bunk and then by division. Family style at table. Salad bar cold cut bar. Nut free. Allergy chef separate area in kitchen. Lots of snacks between meals. Snack 2x/ day. And canteen 2x week. Lobster or steak banquet at end of summer.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Friday 15 min service. Between dinner and campfire. 70% J. Hebrew prayers Challah and grape juice at cookout. Then campfire where they do skits and talk about values and goals for week. Impressions-Louis and Chelsea were warm and well liked by the kids that crossed our paths.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Biggest population from Nyc/Westchester. Also Nj/sfo/Boston. Not uniform. Every Wednesday trip day. 12+ goes longer trips. Bar harbor Vermont Montreal. No extra fees for trips\! No packages unless birthday. 3 calls a summer. Ice hockey is extra.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Owned by Louis (lives in Armonk and was a former camper) for 6 years, with co-owner Teddy, and Chelsea who went to Blue Ridge. 173 total campers - they want more, max 200, and they offer a 4 week session (38 4 week campers; allowed to come back as 4 week second summer but rare). They say camp is as competitive as you want it to be and try to serve sporty and non sporty kids with tournaments with Cedar, Skylemar, Takajo, Andro alongside arts/crafts, podcasting, music, outdoor adventure, hip hop dance. 70% J with a Friday 15 min service, Hebrew prayers with Challah and grape juice, then campfire; no extra fees for trips, not uniform, and 3 calls a summer.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Louis and Chelsea were warm and well liked by the kids that crossed our paths.
  - No extra fees for trips\!
  - Nut free. Allergy chef separate area in kitchen.
  - They say camp is as competitive as you want it to be.
  - Podcasting and interviews of week.
  - Box and mountain biking course.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Road goes thru middle of camp.
  - They want more campers.
  - Hockey is extra.
  - No packages unless birthday.
  - Toying with idea of towel service for next year.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Cedar
  - Andro
  - Takajo
  - Skylemar
  - Manitou

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Both are teachers during year. We toured when most kids were at cedar for tournament. 173 total campers -they want more. Max 200. Offer 4 week session -38 4 week campers. Allowed to come back as 4 week second summer but rare Biggest population from Nyc/westchester . Also Nj/sfo/Boston Not uniform Tournaments with Cedar skylemar takajo andro. Sister camp Pinecliffe. Also have sisters at Fernwood and Mataponi. Not Walden bc attract different families. Do Friday 15 min service. Between dinner and campfire. 70% J. Hebrew prayers Challah and grape juice at cookout. Then campfire where they do skits and talk about values and goals for week. Road goes thru middle of camp. 5 private houses at end. Creek runs thru lower camp and senior camp Female staff impt. 8/9/10 year old. Each bunk has. Full time doctor on staff and 3 nurses 5 periods/day. 2 of those are tennis/waterfront. 3 periods of elective. Only with age group. Choose from a list. Offer ice hockey tues/thurs nights 1.5 hrs at Bridgton sports hockey is extra. They say camp is as competitive as you want it to be. Try to serve sporty and non sporty kids. Arts /crafts. Ceramics woodworking. Podcasting and interviews of week. Music-percussion/guitar. Box and mountain biking course. Outdoor adventure. Hip hop dance. 5-6 kids/bunk. 2 staff. Sink/toilet in bunk. Shower houses. Toying with idea of towel service for next year. Every Wednesday trip day. Canoeing amusement park. Every group does overnight camp out. 12+ goes longer trips. Bar harbor Vermont Montreal. No extra fees for trips\! No packages unless birthday. Dining hall sit by bunk and then by division. Lobster or steak banquet at end of summer. Family style at table. Salad bar cold cut bar. Nut free. Allergy chef separate area in kitchen. Lots of snacks between meals. Snack 2x/ day. title: TON-A-WANDAH — LISA — 6-30-16
text:
# TON-A-WANDAH

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

The camp is owned by Billy and Judy Haynes, however I was there on a change-over day, so Erin and her husband Garrett were overseeing things. All girls. 135 first session, 200 2nd session and 210 3rd session. They offer 3 three-week sessions, 3 two-week sessions, and one week at the end. Ages 5-15, CIT at 16. Big draw from FL, SC, GA, NC. Many int'l counselors. No visiting day. Color war is Tribe Day: 3 Tribes, generational. No brother camp. They have one dance/session with one of the boy camps.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

All girls, ages 5-15, CIT at 16. Big draw from FL, SC, GA, NC. Many int'l counselors. The camp is owned by Billy and Judy Haynes; on a change-over day Erin and her husband Garrett were overseeing things. Frankie, a lovely office administrator, took me around. Very welcoming to older girls just coming to camp. Not prissy girls. Brothers attend: Camp Carolina, Falling Creek, Rockmont, Pinnacle.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: hot at night (according to Frankie, she was very honest). 7 campers/3 counselors, nice bunks, shower houses, but 2 sinks/2 toilets in bunk. The girls live out of their trunks. They have a few dressers of drawers. No AC anywhere. CD player in the cabin for music.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The lake was larger in comparison, no motorized; they had toys. The girls go fishing, have canoes, kayaks, paddleboards. High Ropes and Zip line across the lake. Kayaking trips as well.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 activity periods, 3am/3pm, all choice. Schedules set every evening by staff based on the girls choice prior to camp, can change after the first week.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

A\&C: no looms, woodburning, ceramics, 4 wheels, exposed kiln, glass fusion and copperwork. Cooking in back of the office, make snacks, varies by session. Performing Arts: one show/session, they have a costume room, dance/drama review, a play and dance last week. Dance: wood floor, mirrors, bars, but old. Offer many types, but depends on the staff. Indoor tumbling, no real gymnastics. No music lessons but voice. Digital photography. Newsletter. Nice old indoor gym, soccer, basketball, softball, fitness is done outside, yoga, kickboxing, volleyball. The one field is across the road with the chapel. 2 tennis courts, one was cracked. No golf. Horses: 1/3-1/2 of the girls ride, 2 hours/day, 6 times/week, $400 for 2 weeks. It is a regular activity 3 days/week. Horsemanship as well. High Ropes and Zip line across the lake. Overnights one/session, rafting is done by an outside company as is ziplining. Offer advanced mountaineering. Kayaking trips as well. Archery and riflery. They have an herb garden.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

They have a parent/camp doctor who lives in town, 3 RNs. NO AC in health center. Hospital 15 min away in Hendersonville. Have taken diabetics, just depends. Non-denominational Chapel service on Sunday. Must cross a road at the back of camp to get to Chapel, counselors help them. Dining Hall is nice, old and rustic, touching the lake, 1st week sit by cabin, last week with tribes. Tree nut free, have PB on request. Can handle some allergies. Offer Canteen M-Sat am and pm; they have products with nuts in canteen. Sing a prayer in DH, mention "the Lord". Have transportation to mass.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very welcoming to older girls just coming to camp. Not prissy girls. Color war is Tribe Day: 3 Tribes, generational. Non-denominational Chapel service on Sunday. Popular activities seem the same as the other camps: horses, A\&C, outdoor adventure. Newsletter.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Big draw from FL, SC, GA, NC. No chaperoned flights. No visiting day. Allowed packages 1/week, no calls home. CD player in the cabin for music. They have one dance/session with one of the boy camps. Brothers attend: Camp Carolina, Falling Creek, Rockmont, Pinnacle. Have transportation to mass. Horses: $400 for 2 weeks.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Ton-A-Wandah is an all-girls camp owned by Billy and Judy Haynes offering 3 three-week sessions, 3 two-week sessions, and one week at the end for ages 5-15 (CIT at 16). The program has 6 activity periods, all choice, with schedules set every evening and the option to change after the first week; popular activities include horses, A\&C, and outdoor adventure. Facilities are rustic with no AC anywhere (bunks can be hot at night), a nice old dining hall touching the lake, and a larger non-motorized lake with canoes, kayaks, paddleboards, and a zip line across the lake. Policies include no visiting day, no chaperoned flights, one package per week, no calls home, and they are very welcoming to older girls just coming to camp.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All girls; ages 5-15, CIT at 16; 3 three-week sessions, 3 two-week sessions, and one week at the end.
  - 6 activity periods, all choice; schedules set every evening; can change after the first week.
  - High Ropes and Zip line across the lake; overnights one/session; offer advanced mountaineering; kayaking trips; archery and riflery.
  - Horses: 1/3-1/2 of the girls ride; 2 hours/day, 6 times/week; $400 for 2 weeks; horsemanship as well.
  - A\&C depth: woodburning, ceramics with 4 wheels, exposed kiln, glass fusion, copperwork.
  - Nice old indoor gym; broad sports menu (soccer, basketball, softball, yoga, kickboxing, volleyball).
  - Very welcoming to older girls just coming to camp.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No AC anywhere; NO AC in health center; bunks hot at night.
  - Must cross a road at the back of camp to get to Chapel; the one field is across the road with the chapel.
  - 2 tennis courts, one was cracked.
  - No visiting day; no calls home; no chaperoned flights.
  - Tree nut free but they have products with nuts in canteen.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Keystone
  - Rockbrook
  - Green Cove
  - Kahdalea

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

The camp is owned by Billy and Judy Haynes, however I was there on a change- over day, so Erin and her husband Garrett were overseeing things. Frankie, a lovely office administrator took me around. All girls, 135 first session, 200 2 nd session and 210 3 rd session, they offer 3 three-week sessions, 3 two-week sessions..and one week at the end. Ages 5-15, CIT at 16. Big draw from FL, SC, GA, NC. No chaperoned flights. Many int’l counselors. No visiting day. 6 activity periods, 3am/3pm, all choice, schedules set every evening by staff based on the girls choice prior to camp. can change after the first week. Color war isTribe Day: 3 Tribes, generational. No brother camp. Brothers attend: Camp Carolina, Falling Creek, Rockmont, Pinnacle. They have one dance/session with one of the boy camps. Allowed packages 1/week, no calls home, CD player in the cabin for music. They have a parent/camp doctor who lives in town, 3 RNs. NO AC in health center. Hospital 15 in away in Hendersonville. Have taken diabetics, just depends. Non-denominational Chapel service on Sunday. Have transportation to mass. Must cross a road at the back of camp to get to Chapel, counselors help them. Bunks: hot at night (according to Frankie, she was very honest), 7 campers/3 counselors, nice bunks, shower houses, but 2 sinks/2 toilets in bunk. The girls live out of their trunks. They have a few dressers of drawers. No AC anywhere. Dining Hall is nice, old and rustic, touching the lake, 1 st week sit by cabin, last week with tribes. Tree nut free, have PB on request. Sing a prayer in DH, mention “the Lord”. Can handle some allergies. Offer Canteen M-Sat am and pm, they have products with nuts in canteen. A\&C: , no looms, woodburning, ceramics, 4 wheels, exposed kiln, glass fusion and copperwork Cooking in back of the office, make snacks, varies by session. Performing Arts: one show/session, they have a costume room, dance/drama review, a play and dance last week. Dance: wood floor, mirrors, bars, but old. Offer many types, but depends on the staff. Indoor tumbling, no real gymnastics. No music lessons but voice. Digital photography. Newsletter. Nice old indoor gym, soccer, basketball, softball, fitness is done outside, yoga, kickboxing, volleyball. The one field is across the road with the chapel. 2 tennis courts, one was cracked. No golf. Horses: 1/3-1/2 of the girls ride, 2 hours/day, 6 times/week, $400 for 2 weeks. It is a regular activity 3 days/week. Horsemanship as well. High Ropes and Zip line across the lake. Overnights one/session, rafting is done by an outside company as is ziplining. Offer advanced mountaineering. Kayaking trips as well. Archery and riflery. They have an herb garden. The lake was larger in comparison, no motorized, they had toys, the girls go fishing, have canoes, kayaks, paddleboards. Very welcoming to older girls just coming to camp. Not prissy girls. Popular activities seem the same as the other camps,,,,horses, A\&C, outdoor adventure. Give with Keystone, Rockbrook, Green Cove, Kahdalea.

title: Pali Overnight Adventures — Melissa — 9-11-23
text:
# Pali Overnight Adventures

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

August 2023 Tour of Pali Adventure Camp was hosted by Amy. \# of campers: 400 per week. 100-140 campers each week are staying more than 1 week. They are open 365 days a year so summer camp is just a small piece of their revenue. They have a huge following from school districts across the state for mini camps.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Staff: The counselors have 6 weeks of training and 2 weeks of orientation. All are drug tested and background checked. They are trained in recognizing and resolving bullying, and facilitating cabin interactions. Director: Unknown.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Facilities: A good number of boys cabins are newly constructed and they are building 8 more new cabins. This will increase capacity to 440. They are nice enough cabins with showers inside. The cabins do not have AC but the weather is mild due to the elevation. Teens are housed separately and they dine on a separate floor as well. They do their best to group 2 weekers together but there are times when a camper has to move cabins week to week.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

One thing to note: Big Bear Lake is where all water activities take place and it’s about 35 mins from the camp. The video on the website makes it look like they are right on the water so take note of that to manage expectations.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Pali Adventure is structured where campers pre select their 'Speciality' before arriving at camp. The specialty takes place for the first 3 hours of the day and then the next 3 hours are 'electives or free choice', which are signed up for on a daily basis from an iPad in their cabins. All specialties are co-ed except for Girl Power. She mentioned that their Dance specialty is harder to fill up and doesn’t sound like it’s for the experienced dancer.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Some of their specialties are really strong: 1. Magic - The instructor is a magician from the Magic Castle, a very famous magic institution in LA 2. Creative Arts - Film, broadcasting, culinary arts 3. Movie Makeup - incredible program and the kids in this specialty love it. The day I visited they were doing zombie makeup 4. Rock Star - This specialty fills up quickly and their recording studio is professional grade.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Food: Buffet style with typical options. Kids are required to drink 1 cup of water at every meal before they can have other drinks. The dining hall for the younger kids was loud and boisterous. Kids eat together as a cabin.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

I was very impressed with the grounds and the facilities. It’s a very large camp that has lots of different areas. Pali has a poor return rate at 40%. It’s definitely hit or miss. I think it’s hard for the kids to bond with their cabin mates because they spend very little time together other than meals and bedtime.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from San Diego, Orange County, and LA. One nice thing about Pali is that the tuition covers everything so there are no other fees. Electives are signed up for on a daily basis from an iPad in their cabins. Parent communication: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Pali has a poor return rate at 40%. It’s definitely hit or miss. I think it’s hard for the kids to bond with their cabin mates because they spend very little time together other than meals and bedtime. If you have a client with siblings or two friends who go together I would be more optimistic about having a good experience.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - I was very impressed with the grounds and the facilities.
  - Some of their specialties are really strong (Magic with a Magic Castle instructor; Creative Arts—film, broadcasting, culinary arts; Movie Makeup; Rock Star with a professional-grade recording studio).
  - Rock Star fills up quickly.
  - The counselors have 6 weeks of training and 2 weeks of orientation, are drug tested and background checked, and are trained in recognizing and resolving bullying and facilitating cabin interactions.
  - One nice thing about Pali is that the tuition covers everything so there are no other fees.
  - They are open 365 days a year and have a huge following from school districts across the state for mini camps.
  - Buses from San Diego, Orange County, and LA.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Pali has a poor return rate at 40%.
  - It’s definitely hit or miss.
  - I think it’s hard for the kids to bond with their cabin mates because they spend very little time together other than meals and bedtime.
  - Big Bear Lake is where all water activities take place and it’s about 35 mins from the camp; the website video makes it look like they are right on the water.
  - Dance specialty is harder to fill up and doesn’t sound like it’s for the experienced dancer.
  - The dining hall for the younger kids was loud and boisterous.
  - The cabins do not have AC (weather is mild due to the elevation).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They do their best to group 2 weekers together but there are times when a camper has to move cabins week to week. Buses from San Diego, Orange County, and LA They are open 365 days a year so summer camp is just a small piece of their revenue. They have a huge following from school districts across the state for mini camps. Pali Adventure is structured where campers pre select their “Speciality” before arriving at camp. The specialty takes place for the first 3 hours of the day and then the next 3 hours are “electives or free choice”, which are signed up for on a daily basis from an iPad in their cabins. Some of their specialites are really strong: 1. Magic-The instructor is a magician from the Magic Castle, a very famous magic institiion in LA 2. Creative Arts-Film, broadcasting, culinary arts 3. Movie Make up-incredible program and the kids in this specialty love it. The day I visited they were doing zombie makeup 4. Rock Star-This specialty fills up quickly and their recording studio is professional grade All specialties are co-ed except for Girl Power. She mentioned that their Dance specialty is harder to fill up and doesn’t sound like it’s for the experienced dancer. One thing to note: Big Bear Lake is where all water activities take place and it’s about 35 mins from the camp. The video on the website makes it look like they are right on the water so take note of that to manage expectations. Facilities: A good number of boys cabins are newly constructed and they are building 8 more new cabins. This will increase capacity to 440. They are nice enough cabins with showers inside. The cabins do not have AC but the weather is mild due to the elevation. Teens are housed separately and they dine on a separate floor as well. Food: Buffet style with typical options. Kids are required to drink 1 cup of water at every meal before they can have other drinks. The dining hall for the younger kids was loud and boisterous. Kids eat together as a cabin. Staff: The counselors have 6 weeks of training and 2 weeks of orientation. All are drug tested and background checked. They are trained in recognizing and resolving bullying, and facilitating cabin interactions. One nice thing about Pali is that the tuition covers everything so there are no other fees. Conclusion: Pali has a poor return rate at 40%. It’s definitely hit or miss. I think it’s hard for the kids to bond with their cabin mates because they spend very little time together other than meals and bedtime. If you have a client with siblings or two friends who go together I would be more optimistic about having a good experience. I recommend Pali somewhat cautiously otherwise. I was very impressed with the grounds and the facilities. It’s a very large camp that has lots of different areas.

title: WYONEGONIC FOR GIRLS — Joanne — 8-28-19
text:
# WYONEGONIC FOR GIRLS

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Wyonegonic has a two-year leadership training program for 16 to 18 year old girls. WYONEGONIC PATHWAYS: Designed for a limited number of new families who wish to consider a two-week season for girls entering 7th grade or younger. Junior Camp: 3rd – 5th grades; Intermediate Camp: 5th – 7th grades; Senior Camp: 8th – 10th grades. Our brother camp Winona, owned and operated separately, is located five miles from Wyonegonic on the same lake.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

UNIT ASSIGNMENT: Final cabin assignments are determined prior to camper arrival. Junior Camp: 3rd – 5th grades; Intermediate Camp: 5th – 7th grades; Senior Camp: 8th – 10th grades. Wyonegonic has a two-year leadership training program for 16 to 18 year old girls. For further information about the program, application process and special tuition, please contact the Camp Directors. WYONEGONIC PATHWAYS: Designed for a limited number of new families who wish to consider a two-week season for girls entering 7th grade or younger. Wyonegonic campers and counselors wear forest green or white shirts and green, white or tan khaki shorts.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

FOOTLOCKERS: Campers need a footlocker for everyday living in their cabins. They may be shipped to camp via courier or UPS. You can set up your account with Wyonegonic’s preferred vendor for delivery and pick-up. Campers may borrow a gently used footlocker from Wyonegonic, if arrangements are made in advance. BEDDING: A pillow, bed linens, and wool blankets will be provided by the camp. We suggest your child bring a sleeping bag for trips. Family Camp: Meals are served family style in the camp dining hall and families stay in cabins.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Family Camp activities include swimming, canoeing, sailing, windsurfing, and waterskiing.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Participation is encouraged for the entire time of the session dates.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

FAMILY CAMP: At the close of the regular camper season, Wyonegonic welcomes families. Parents and their children may sign up to enjoy the relaxed atmosphere of Wyonegonic and participate in camp activities such as swimming, hiking, canoeing, sailing, windsurfing, waterskiing, tennis and archery. Meals are served family style in the camp dining hall and families stay in cabins. WINTER REUNIONS: Joint reunions with our brother camp, Winona, are held during the school year. Prospective and current families, alumnae and friends are cordially invited to bring guests. Since many brother-sister campers attend camps, Winona and Wyonegonic plan the same dates for the camp season, winter reunions, and brother-sister visitations during the summer. Parents and guests are invited to the Saturday evening camp cookout.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

CAMPER HEALTH FORMS are mandatory for all campers. This includes completion of an online Health History Form completed by the parent and a health exam form completed by a physician. A copy of your insurance card (both sides) must be on record at the Health Center. Individual prescription and OTC medication must be approved by a physician; and must arrive to camp in the original container with instructions in English for dispensing purposes. Tuition covers health center care in camp. Family Camp meals are served family style in the camp dining hall.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Wyonegonic campers and counselors wear forest green or white shirts and green, white or tan khaki shorts. All clothing and personal items must be plainly marked with the camper’s full name. Name tapes are a necessity. Joint reunions with our brother camp, Winona, are held during the school year. Parents and guests are invited to the Saturday evening camp cookout.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

REGISTRATION PROCEDURE: Please send the completed registration with $500 deposit. Registrations are time-sensitive and available space will be confirmed upon receipt. Returning campers are guaranteed a space for the 2020 season by returning a completed registration form and deposit by November 1, 2019. Session changes for returning campers can be requested for consideration but are not guaranteed, unless space is available. TUITION: Tuition not only covers room, board, program and staff instruction, but also postage, laundry, bedding, sundries, camp picture poster, address book, special transportation for out-of-camp trips, horseback riding, and health center care in camp. Any applicable taxes will be billed in 2020. TUITION REFUND POLICY: $100 of the $500 deposit is a service charge and non-refundable. The balance of $400 is refundable if Wyonegonic is notified before February 1, 2020. There are no deposit refunds after February 1. No reduction is made for late arrivals or early departures. In the event of absence from Wyonegonic due to illness for a period of more than two weeks, financial loss will be shared equally between the camp and parents. 7 WEEK TUITION: All inclusive tuition of $9200 is payable as follows: $500 deposit with registration form; $2900 due March 1, 2020; $2900 due April 1, 2020; $2900 due May 1, 2020. A reduced tuition of $8750 is honored for full payment received by November 1, 2019. A reduced tuition of $8900 is honored for full payment received by December 31, 2019. 3 1/2 WEEK TUITION: All inclusive tuition of $6200 is payable as follows: $500 deposit with registration form; $1900 due March 1, 2020; $1900 due April 1, 2020; $1900 due May 1, 2020. A reduced tuition fee of $5900 is honored for full payment received by November 1, 2019. A reduced tuition fee of $6000 is honored for full payment received by December 31, 2019. Payment for tuition/ travel fees must be drawn from a USA bank or Visa/ MasterCard. CAMP CLOTHING: Wyonegonic campers and counselors wear forest green or white shirts and green, white or tan khaki shorts. Camp clothing can be purchased through Amerasport, our official outfitter. The order form will be sent in February and is also accessible at www.amerasport.com. All clothing and personal items must be plainly marked with the camper’s full name. Name tapes are a necessity. FOOTLOCKERS: They may be shipped to camp via courier or UPS. You can set up your account with Wyonegonic’s preferred vendor for delivery and pick-up. INTERNATIONAL and WESTERN US STATES INFORMATION: When arranging flights, fly on June 24 or July 17 into Boston; fly home on July 18 or August 10. Footlockers may be purchased through our official outfitter and winter storage is available for a $50 fee. No footlockers are shipped out of the country at the end of the season. TRAVEL: Detailed travel instructions will be sent to all registered campers in March. Independent flights are met by Wyonegonic and Winona representatives in Boston, MA and Portland, ME. Wyonegonic and Winona representatives will also chaperone campers from Southern Connecticut by bus charter. VISITORS: Parents are welcome to visit ten days after your child arrives. One visit is permitted each session. Contact the office in advance to take your camper out of camp for a meal or family time. Overnight visits are not permitted. Taking friends out of camp is not permitted. Parents and guests are invited to the Saturday evening camp cookout. Parents who visit during the week may join us for buffet lunch. COMMUNICATION: Weekly summer newsletters are sent electronically. Please make sure we have the best email address for camp administrative communications and place info@wyonegonic.com in your address book to ensure receiving camp email. TELEPHONE: Campers are not permitted to use the telephone. Camper birthdays are the exception. We suggest parents call during mealtimes. Parents are welcome to call the Camp Directors for updates. EMAIL: Camp office email addresses are for camp business, not camper correspondence. Parents can set up an account through the parent log-in system to send e-mail communications to their camper(s). WINTER REUNIONS: For a complete listing of reunion locations and dates, please visit our calendar at www.wyonegonic.com

We suggest your child bring a sleeping bag for trips. CAMP CLOTHING: Wyonegonic campers and counselors wear forest green or white shirts and green, white or tan khaki shorts. Camp clothing can be purchased through Amerasport, our official outfitter. The order form will be sent in February and is also accessible at www.amerasport.com. All clothing and personal items must be plainly marked with the camper’s full name. Name tapes are a necessity. INTERNATIONAL and WESTERN US STATES INFORMATION: When arranging flights, fly on June 24 or July 17 into Boston; fly home on July 18 or August 10. Footlockers may be purchased through our official outfitter and winter storage is available for a $50 fee. No footlockers are shipped out of the country at the end of the season. Payment for tuition/ travel fees must be drawn from a USA bank or Visa/ MasterCard. VISITORS: Parents are welcome to visit ten days after your child arrives. One visit is permitted each session. Contact the office in advance to take your camper out of camp for a meal or family time. Overnight visits are not permitted. Taking friends out of camp is not permitted. Parents and guests are invited to the Saturday evening camp cookout. Parents who visit during the week may join us for buffet lunch. CAMPER HEALTH FORMS are mandatory for all campers. This includes completion of an online Health History Form completed by the parent and a health exam form completed by a physician. A copy of your insurance card (both sides) must be on record at the Health Center. Individual prescription and OTC medication must be approved by a physician; and must arrive to camp in the original container with instructions in English for dispensing purposes. COMMUNICATION: Weekly summer newsletters are sent electronically. Please make sure we have the best email address for camp administrative communications and place info@wyonegonic.com in your address book to ensure receiving camp email. TELEPHONE: Campers are not permitted to use the telephone. Camper birthdays are the exception. We suggest parents call during mealtimes. Parents are welcome to call the Camp Directors for updates. EMAIL: Camp office email addresses are for camp business, not camper correspondence. Parents can set up an account through the parent log-in system to send e-mail communications to their camper(s). WINTER REUNIONS: Joint reunions with our brother camp, Winona, are held during the school year. Prospective and current families, alumnae and friends are cordially invited to bring guests. For a complete listing of reunion locations and dates, please visit our calendar at www.wyonegonic.com TRAVEL: Detailed travel instructions will be sent to all registered campers in March. Independent flights are met by Wyonegonic and Winona representatives in Boston, MA and Portland, ME. Wyonegonic and Winona representatives will also chaperone campers from Southern Connecticut by bus charter. FAMILY CAMP: At the close of the regular camper season, Wyonegonic welcomes families. Parents and their children may sign up to enjoy the relaxed atmosphere of Wyonegonic and participate in camp activities such as swimming, hiking, canoeing, sailing, windsurfing, waterskiing, tennis and archery. Meals are served family style in the camp dining hall and families stay in cabins. A Family Camp registration form is available online at www.wyonegonic.com WINONA: Our brother camp Winona, owned and operated separately, is located five miles from Wyonegonic on the same lake. Since many brother-sister campers attend camps, Winona and Wyonegonic plan the same dates for the camp season, winter reunions, and brother-sister visitations during the summer. Winona Camps for Boys 35 Winona Rd Bridgton, ME 04009 207.647.3721 information@winonacamps.com www.winonacamps.com
