title: Saginaw — Jennifer — 8-28-22
text:
# Saginaw

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp had 407 campers 2022, 285 did full summer. Offers 7, 4, or 3 week sessions. 2 week intro 1 time only for rising 1st - 5th. 80 percent of campers are Jewish.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured on 8/25/22 with Ally – one of directors; Jessica and Mike who are owners and directors were on vacation. Typically 2 bunk counselors and 1 specialist in the bunk. Ally said they get a lot of MD campers as it is one of the first camp North and when parents do not want to go as far as the Poconos.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: younger campers usually 8-10 kids in bunk and older campers may have 12. Typically 2 bunk counselors and 1 specialist in the bunk. No bunk beds for younger kids. Bunks were very clean and bathrooms were nice - showers had doors. Bunks typically have 2 showers, sinks, toilets in each bunk. Laundry done weekly on site, 2 campers per bag.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Swim – lake is very small. Only used for water sports. No swimming in the lake – only in the pools. They offer waterskiing, wakeboarding and tubing - pulled on jet ski. Kayaking and Canoeing is done in a creek in the back of camp. There are 2 pools next to each other at camp. Swim instruction mandatory daily until age 10 then every other day for ages 11-12. Younger kids do free swim and instructional swim daily. Also offer scuba in the pool but cannot get certified.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Scheduling – 6 periods a day. Younger campers get 1 elective per day and older kids get 2. Campers do the same elective 3 days in a row as project based. Most popular electives - ceramics, movie making, silk screening. Almost activities are single sex except night time programming.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Theater – 1 show during week 4 and a mini review at the end of camp. Participation in show is optional. Art – basic arts and crafts, ceramics and silkscreening are most popular. Also offer a small STEAM program for younger kids which is more art than science based and done as a bunk activity. Cooking – offered as a bunk activity only. Do mostly desserts, can handle most allergies. Sports – just added 3 new pickleball courts. Has indoor gymnastics facility with all the equipment. Offers mini golf and driving range on site. Campers can go off site to play golf about 1 time per week. Extra private lessons are available in tennis, gymnastics, golf and waterskiing. In non COVID times, do intercamps with Nock a Mixon, Kweebec, Green Lane. Trips – no trips this year due to COVID but typically do 1 theme park in each half of camp. 11 and 12 year olds do a day white water rafting trip. 9th and 10th graders sometimes go on a local shopping trip and movies.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall – campers eat with bunk. Girls and boys eat separately. Food is buffet style. They are nut aware. No nut products in canteen. Food is kosher style – no bacon, pork and shellfish, and no cheese on burgers. They do serve peanut butter in packets and have peanut free tables. Only serve water with lunch and dinner. There are vending machines with soda on campus but only older campers allowed. Canteen is every day but Sunday when they serve ice cream sundaes. Medical – typically 1 doctor and 3-4 nurses on site. Health center is air conditioned as well as the dining hall and office. Urgent care - 25 min away, hospital 10 minutes. Use camp meds for medicine.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religious Life – very short Shabbat service on Fri nights. Do serve matzo ball soup every Fri night. Transgender policy – they had several nonbinary campers this year who had been attending camp since young. These campers remained in their same bunks as before but correct pronouns were used. She says they have not had a full conversation yet about transgender campers and probably not ready for one in 2023. Overall impression: I did not get to tour while campers in session due to a bout of COVID so may not have gotten the full impression. Ally very nice and personable. I have several neighbors who have sent their kids to Saginaw and rave about it but I did feel more like I was at a day camp.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from Cherry Hill, Bala, Willow Grove. Luggage is picked up 1 week in advance and is unpacked for ages 6-12 - $75 fee for bags. Communication – post pictures daily. Kids must write home 3x per week. NO phone calls home. For siblings at camp, there is family time every Saturday. Currently offering early enrollment discount through 10/31/22. Also offering very generous discounts for younger 1st time campers.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp had 407 campers 2022, 285 did full summer. Offers 7, 4, or 3 week sessions, with a 2 week intro 1 time only for rising 1st - 5th. Ally feels the best type of camper is Saginaw is someone who wants to try everything. I think it is a good fit for a Jewish family looking for a less expensive camp with sessions.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Ally very nice and personable.
  - I have several neighbors who have sent their kids to Saginaw and rave about it.
  - Bunks were very clean and bathrooms were nice - showers had doors.
  - Nut aware; food is kosher style.
  - Just added 3 new pickleball courts; indoor gymnastics facility; two pools; waterskiing/wakeboarding/tubing.
  - Very generous discounts for younger 1st time campers.

  

#### Lowlights

  - I did not get to tour while campers in session due to a bout of COVID so may not have gotten the full impression.
  - I did feel more like I was at a day camp.
  - Lake is very small; no swimming in the lake.
  - They have not had a full conversation yet about transgender campers and probably not ready for one in 2023.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Green Lane
  - Nock a Mixon
  - Kweebec

These campers remained in their same bunks as before but correct pronouns were used. She says they have not had a full conversation yet about transgender campers and probably not ready for one in 2023. Trips – no trips this year due to COVID but typically do 1 theme park in each half of camp. 11 and 12 year olds do a day white water rafting trip. 9 th and 10 th graders sometimes go on a local shopping trip and movies. Ally feels the best type of camper is Saginaw is someone who wants to try everything. Currently offering early enrollment discount through 10/31/22. Also offering very generous discounts for younger 1 st time campers. Overall impression : I did not get to tour while campers in session due to a bout of COVID so may not have gotten the full impression. Ally very nice and personable. I have several neighbors who have sent their kids to Saginaw and rave about it but I did feel more like I was at a day camp. I think it is a good fit for a Jewish family looking for a less expensive camp with sessions. I would give it with Green Lane, Nock a Mixon, Kweebec.

title: WESTMONT — BETH — 7-17-23
text:
# WESTMONT

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

6/30/23 tour with Joey Silberfein (Beth and Jennifer). 2023 updates in red. 7/13/22 tour with Joey Silberfein (Beth, Lisa, Shari, Amanda). Westmont submitted our form which is in the remote. 600 campers.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

School psychologist Lauren on staff; 180/260 staff in cabins. Fred and Minna have both passed away though listed on the form.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Keep bunks together as much as possible. Older campers can have hair dryer / flat iron / make up.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Towels provided for cabins, pool, and, lake.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Evening leagues 1x week. Flag football very popular.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Canteen (updated from old dining room look) has video games and pop a shot; chocolate making program based there. Creative Arts was very impressive. New 3-D printer.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

OD can be inside or outside. Serve challah and chicken on Friday evening.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

No religion; non-sectarian. Impression: well-priced; great value; warm and upbeat vibe. Spirited and well-run.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

28 from Philly – small MD bus stops in Philly to pick them up; FL chaperoned flight; all other campers take bus from Westchester since assigned bus with age division for trip to camp. Updates: 3 buses from Westchester (Tapanzee, MY; NYC also leaves from there); 5 buses from East Brunswick/Livingston, NJ; 6 buses from Long Island; 1 mini-bus from Philadelphia (Plymouth Meeting, PA); Chaperoned flight from FL (10-15 campers). Parent communication: Unknown. Tech & extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Westmont has 600 campers and felt well-priced with great value. The vibe was warm and upbeat, spirited and well-run. Travel is easy with assigned buses by age division from Westchester, multiple NJ and Long Island routes, a mini-bus from Philadelphia, and a chaperoned flight from FL. Program highlights include evening leagues (flag football very popular), an updated Canteen with video games and pop a shot plus chocolate making, and very impressive Creative Arts with a new 3-D printer.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Impression: well-priced; great value; warm and upbeat vibe
  - Spirited and well-run
  - Creative Arts was very impressive
  - New 3-D printer
  - Canteen (updated from old dining room look) has video games and pop a shot; chocolate making program based there
  - Evening leagues 1x week
  - Flag football very popular
  - Towels provided for cabins, pool, and, lake
  - Keep bunks together as much as possible
  - School psychologist Lauren on staff; 180/260 staff in cabins
  - No religion; non-sectarian
  - OD can be inside or outside

  

#### Lowlights

  - Unknown

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Canadensis
  - Towanda
  - Trails End
  - Chipinaw
  - Iroquois Springs
  - Taconic

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

6/30/23 tour with Joey Silberfein (Beth and Jennifer) 2023 updates in red 7/13/22 tour with Joey Silberfein (Beth, Lisa, Shari, Amanda) Fred and Minna have both passed away though listed on the form Westmont submitted our form which is in the remote. 600 campers; 28 from Philly – small MD bus stops in Philly to pick them up; FL chaperoned flight; all other campers take bus from Westchester since assigned bus with age division for trip to camp School psychologist Lauren on staff; 180/260 staff in cabins Updates: 3 buses from Westchester (Tapanzee, MY; NYC also leaves from there) 5 buses from East Brunswick/Livingston, NJ 6 buses from Long Island 1 mini-bus from Philadelphia (Plymouth Meeting, PA) Chaperoned flight from FL (10-15 campers) Evening leagues 1x week Flag football very popular Towels provided for cabins, pool, and, lake Keep bunks together as much as possible OD can be inside or outside Older campers can have hair dryer / flat iron / make up Canteen (updated from old dining room look) has video games and pop a shot; chocolate making program based there No religion; serve challah and chicken on Friday evening; non-sectarian Creative Arts was very impressive New 3-D printer Impression: well-priced; great value; warm and upbeat vibe Spirited and well-run Would give with Canadensis, Towanda, possibly Trails End, Chipinaw, possibly Iroquois Springs, Taconic

title: WEEQUAHIC — EMILY — 7-23-19
text:
# WEEQUAHIC

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

At Weequahic, the kids go for either 3 week sessions or for 6 weeks which is considered full summer. There is an early bird discount to sign up by October 1. Trip days are once a session. Kids have canteen every other night. There is a whole separate teen canteen and teen center for the kids which is very appealing; their bunks are separated from the little kids.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We had a tour with Cole Kelly, the Director who lives in the south during the winter. Kids come from Long Island, NYC, NJ, CT, Westchester, Philly, Baltimore, Florida and Atlanta. He retains many older kids because of that. There are camp moms for the younger boys that are available to them but don't sleep in the bunks. Bunks mostly stay together from one year to the next.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

The bunks are clean and organized. There are box fans in the window. A cleaning service cleans the bathrooms and showers. There are 3 showers and 3 bathrooms in each bunk. There are about 11 kids and 3 counselors per bunk. Their bunks are separated from the little kids as well.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is an outdoor heated pool and a lake where they do all the waterfront activities. The lake has motorized boats and aquatic slides. Next year, they plan to re-do the beach and get a new boat.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Activities are split into 6 periods. The first two periods are electives. Period 3 is a Bunk Activity. Then lunch and rest hour. Periods 4 and 5 are electives. Period 6 is "Wee Explore" which is a daily choice that gives you options but keeps you with your age group. You can switch your minor every other day. Kids who want to compete can play in inter-camps in Wayne County. Trip days are once a session. Kids have canteen every other night.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Weequahic offers a very large variety of activities ranging from all sports like basketball, soccer, baseball, flag football, lacrosse etc. The athletic fields are well taken care of. They also offer Media, theatre, culinary, dance, gymnastics, fashion design, pottery, ropes, climbing wall, water sports. The sports fields are very nice and the arts and crafts and media rooms are clean and air conditioned inside. They have very nice equipment for gymnastics and a nice culinary room. They also made their own mini-golf course which looks fun. They have a Ninja Warriors course. There is a whole separate teen canteen and teen center for the kids which is very appealing.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The dining hall was just redone. It is large and clean. There are two seatings in the dining hall. The kids sit by bunk; 4th grade and down do family style. The older campers have buffet. The camp can handle gluten free, nut free, celiac and kids with diabetes. The camp offers salad bar and fruit in addition to the other meals. There is an ER Doctor and 4 Nurses at camp. The health center is air conditioned.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

There is a Friday night camp fire but it is not a religious service. They talk about camp values, Gratitude, Attitude and Courage. The kids looked very happy and busy.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Many parents choose to drop their kids at camp but bus service is offered. The counselors unpack the kids before they get to camp. Kids can make one phone call per session. The only extra cost is for Horseback riding off campus. Cole will run tours anytime. He does individual family tours.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kids who succeed at Weequahic are grounded and active and like to participate in various activities. They enjoy the ability to have a structured choice program. Overall, Weequahic is a great all around camp that offers many activities and would work out great for a camper who only wants to attend camp 3 weeks or 6 weeks. The kids looked very happy and busy.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Clean, air-conditioned arts and crafts and media rooms; very nice equipment for gymnastics and a nice culinary room.
  - Outdoor heated pool and a lake with motorized boats and aquatic slides.
  - Structured 6-period day with significant choice, including Wee Explore; minors switch every other day.
  - Athletic fields are well taken care of; sports fields are very nice.
  - Teen canteen and teen center; older bunks separated from little kids; helps retain many older kids.
  - Dining hall just redone; large, clean; can handle gluten free, nut free, celiac, and kids with diabetes; salad bar and fruit.
  - ER Doctor and 4 Nurses; air-conditioned health center.
  - Counselors unpack kids before arrival; bus service offered.
  - Mini-golf course and Ninja Warriors course.
  - Early bird discount to sign up by October 1.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Kids can make one phone call per session.
  - Horseback riding off campus is an extra cost.
  - Camp moms for the younger boys are available to them but don't sleep in the bunks.
  - Trip days are once a session.
  - Bunks use box fans in the window.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

There is an early bird discount to sign up by October 1. Kids come from Long Island, NYC, NJ, CT, Westchester, Philly, Baltimore, Florida and Atlanta. Period 6 is ?Wee Explore? which is a daily choice that gives you options but keeps you with your age group. You can switch your minor every other day. Kids who want to compete can play in inter-camps in Wayne County. The only extra cost is for Horseback riding off campus. They also offer Media, theatre, culinary, dance, gymnastics, fashion design, pottery, ropes, climbing wall, water sports. The sports fields are very nice and the arts and crafts and media rooms are clean and air conditioned inside. They have very nice equipment for gymnastics and a nice culinary room. A cleaning service cleans the bathrooms and showers. There are 3 showers, 3 bathrooms in each bunk. There are about 11 kids and 3 counselors per bunk. There is an ER Doctor and 4 Nurses at camp. The health center is air conditioned. Kids can make one phone call per session. There is a Friday night camp fire but it is not a religious service. Kids who succeed at Weequahic are grounded and active and like to participate in various activities. They enjoy the ability to have a structured choice program. Overall, Weequahic is a great all around camp that offers many activities and would work out great for a camper who only wants to attend camp 3 weeks or 6 weeks. The kids looked very happy and busy.

title: Island Lake — Natasha — 7-9-24
text:
# Island Lake

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Island Lake is divided into 2 sessions- 4 and 3 weeks. In the last few years they have allowed campers to sign up for the first two weeks of session two with the option to extend, but they are doing away with this possibility. Currently they have 530 campers in Session 1 and anticipate 460/470 for Session 2. 50% stay all 7 weeks. Camp is all choice - divided into majors and minors.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Island Lake Natasha, Risa, Lana, Michelle B, and Riley toured Island Lake on 7/8/2024. We met Odie (Owner/Director) upon arrival, but were toured by Stacey Rubenstein, Assistant Director. Summer 2024 marks the 4th year Odie and Monica have been running Island Lake. Odie, Monica and Stacey were previously at Lindenmere.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks are 12 to 13 kids per bunk, organized by interests. Roof renovations- all bunks and buildings now have new roofs. New staff housing and lounge.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake- 3 water-ski boats and tubing are very popular. The lake is accessible via golf cart- across the road from camp.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Camp is all choice - divided into majors and minors. 3 majors in the AM and 3 minors in the PM. Majors are picked before camp and are all session long. Amanda in the office will help new campers with this process. Minors are chosen daily and sorted out during rest hour the day before.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Odie and Monica have invested $4 million back into camp since they have taken over. Improvements include: a new motorboat, roof renovations (all bunks and buildings now have new roofs), new spin studio, renovated art pavilion, new basketball, new cooking studio, and new staff housing and lounge. Scheduled for 2025- new gymnastics space with spring floors. Notable activities: magic; great robotics/stem program with 3D printers; big skatepark; 11 tennis courts and 6 pickleball courts; lake (3 water-ski boats and tubing are very popular); Horseback Riding (includes indoor and outdoor facilities, trails, and jumping; if HB is your major, you can also participate in grooming and barn care); impressive circus; brand new cooking studio; Outdoor Adventures (includes low and high ropes courses, fort building, and outdoor crew building where campers build a canoe from tree branches); Theater (two shows per session plus a cabaret; campers are every part of the production- crew, lighting, costumes; they have an activity called 'Theater Tech'; every kid gets a part if they want one). Color war- one time - second session. Trips- Sky Zone, bowling, movies, and water parks. Campfires every Friday to foster a sense of community and spirit, no religion.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

24 hour security- a state trooper is always parked at the gate at the top of camp. Health Center- recently renovated. Doctors rotate weekly, seven full-time nurses. Dining hall- one seating, sit by bunk, can handle all allergies but a conversation will be had with Odie before camp.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

We visited on an atypical day - Halloween Day. Campers and staff were dressed up in various costumes, ranging from superheroes, to campers pretending to be counselors by wearing staff shirts. They were all very into it. Camp looked great and campers were enthusiastic and happy.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses provided for all sessions. Trips- Sky Zone, bowling, movies, and water parks. Golf and HB are not extra $. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Additional travel logistics: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Island Lake is a perfect option for someone looking for sessions. Camp looked great and campers were enthusiastic and happy. Stacey mentioned a kid who is obsessed with sports would not do well there. A kid who wants to play basketball, swim and do ceramics will do great.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Camp is all choice - divided into majors and minors (3 majors in the AM and 3 minors in the PM).
  - Majors are picked before camp and are all session long; Amanda in the office will help new campers with this process.
  - Minors are chosen daily and sorted out during rest hour the day before.
  - Odie and Monica have invested $4 million back into camp since they have taken over.
  - Improvements include: a new motorboat, roof renovations, new spin studio, renovated art pavilion, new basketball, new cooking studio, and new staff housing and lounge.
  - Scheduled for 2025- new gymnastics space with spring floors.
  - Great robotics/stem program with 3D printers and magic.
  - Big skatepark; 11 tennis courts and 6 pickleball courts.
  - Lake program with 3 water-ski boats and very popular tubing.
  - Horseback Riding includes indoor and outdoor facilities, trails, and jumping; if HB is your major, you can also participate in grooming and barn care.
  - Impressive circus.
  - Outdoor Adventures includes low and high ropes courses, fort building, and outdoor crew building where campers build a canoe from tree branches.
  - Theater: two shows per session plus a cabaret; Theater Tech; campers are every part of the production; every kid gets a part if they want one.
  - Color war- one time - second session.
  - Campfires every Friday to foster a sense of community and spirit, no religion.
  - 24 hour security- a state trooper is always parked at the gate at the top of camp.
  - Health Center- recently renovated; doctors rotate weekly; seven full-time nurses.
  - Dining hall- one seating, sit by bunk, can handle all allergies after a conversation with Odie before camp.
  - Buses provided for all sessions.
  - Golf and HB are not extra $.

  

#### Lowlights

  - They are doing away with the possibility to sign up for the first two weeks of session two with the option to extend.
  - The lake is accessible via golf cart- across the road from camp.
  - A kid who is obsessed with sports would not do well there.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Weequahic
  - Independent Lake
  - Lindenmere

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Island Lake Natasha, Risa, Lana, Michelle B, and Riley toured Island Lake on 7/8/2024. We met Odie (Owner/Director) upon arrival, but were toured by Stacey Rubenstein, Assistant Director. Summer 2024 marks the 4th year Odie and Monica have been running Island Lake. Odie, Monica and Stacey were previously at Lindenmere. We visited on an atypical day - Halloween Day. Campers and staff were dressed up in various costumes, ranging from superheroes, to campers pretending to be counselors by wearing staff shirts. They were all very into it. Island lake is divided into 2 sessions- 4 and 3 weeks. In the last few years they have allowed campers to sign up for the first two weeks of session two with the option to extend, but they are doing away with this possibility. Currently they have 530 campers in Session 1 and anticipate 460/470 for Session 2. 50% stay all 7 weeks. Camp is all choice - divided into majors and minors. 3 majors in the AM and 3 minors in the PM. Majors are picked before camp and are all session long. Amanda in the office will help new campers with this process. Minors are chosen daily and sorted out during rest hour the day before. Odie and Monica have invested $4 million back into camp since they have taken over Improvements include: - a new motorboat - roof renovations- all bunks and buildings now have new roofs - new spin studio - renovated art pavilion - new basketball -new cooking studio -new staff housing and lounge Scheduled for 2025- new gymnastics space with spring floors Dining hall- one seating, sit by bunk, can handle all allergies but a conversation will be had with Odie bf camp Notable activities: -magic -great robotics/stem program with 3D printers -big skatepark -11 tennis courts and 6 pickleball courts - lake- 3 water-ski boats and tubing are very popular. The lake is accessible via golf cart- across the road from camp. -Horseback Riding- Includes indoor and outdoor facilities, trails, and jumping. If HB is your major, you can also participate in grooming and barn care - impressive circus -brand new cooking studio -Outdoor Adventures-includes low and high ropes courses, fort building, and outdoor crew building where campers build a canoe from tree branches. - Theater- two shows per session plus a cabaret. Campers are every part of the production- crew, lighting, costumes - they have an activity called “Theater Tech”- Every kid gets a part if they want one. -24 hour security- a state trooper is always parked at the gate at the top of camp - Health Center-recently renovated -Doctors rotate weekly, seven full-time nurses - color war- one time - second session - Trips- Sky Zone, bowling, movies, and water parks. - bunks are 12 to 13 kids per bunk, organized by interests - campfires every Friday to foster a sense of community and spirit, no religion. - buses provided for all sessions Golf and HB are not extra $ Island Lake is a perfect option for someone looking for sessions. Camp looked great and campers were enthusiastic and happy. Stacey mentioned a kid who is obsessed with sports would not do well there. A kid who wants to play basketball, swim and do ceramics will do great. I would give with Weequahic, Independent Lake and Lindenmere.

title: Kippewa — Karen — 8-25-22
text:
# Kippewa

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

KIPPEWA Girls camp (brother and sister with COBBOSSEE). 210 girls each session. Completely booked by December/January; 80% come back next year. 2-week campus in the making – 2-week session. NO CIT program yet.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ages in camp: 6–13 in the 2-week session. International campers from Mexico, UK, Hong Kong, Venezuela – but they all live in Miami. International kids: 10–15 each session. Where does the population come from: 40% New York State, Boston, Florida, Washington DC, Atlanta, Chicago, San Francisco, LA, Denver/Colorado, Seattle/Oregon, Mexico City. Cabins: don’t take new 15 year old campers. 3 counselors in the cabins. Director: Unknown.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

9–11 campers in the cabins and 3 counselors, bathrooms and restrooms inside. All cabins have toilets, sinks and showers inside.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront: mile swimming in the lake, waterskiing.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They choose for Dance: Ballet or the specific one they want.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

1 play per session: 20–25 campers participate. Campfires every Friday. Camp Trips: adventure wilderness: canoeing, hiking. Most Popular: arts & crafts, stained glass, Tennis, rock climbing, archery. Studios: ceramics, jewelry, fine arts, arts and crafts. Printmaking, sewing, video making, photo lab.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Nurse on campus; local medical office; 2–3 good hospitals near; telehealth visit as well. Allergy or special needs: Kosher style, vegan, dairy free, gluten free, nut free.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The campers that do not fit are the competitive ones as socially competitive. No religious services.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

To get to camp: Boston area buses, Portland airport with Cobbossee (chaperoned can also be), and NY area. 1 Phone Call per session in a 4 weeks session, but in a 2 week session NO phone calls. 1-way emails parents-to-campers; provide stationery. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kippewa is a Girls camp (brother and sister with Cobbossee) with 210 girls each session and is completely booked by December/January with about 80% coming back the next year. They are building a 2-week campus – a 2-week session for ages 6–13 – and cabins have 9–11 campers with 3 counselors and bathrooms inside. Most popular are arts & crafts (stained glass), Tennis, rock climbing, archery, plus studios for ceramics, jewelry, fine arts, printmaking, sewing, video making, and a photo lab, along with waterfront mile swimming in the lake and waterskiing, campfires every Friday, and an adventure wilderness trips program (canoeing, hiking). The campers that do not fit are the competitive ones as socially competitive; there is one play per session (20–25 campers participate), they choose for Dance: Ballet or the specific one they want, and the camp has a nurse on campus, telehealth, and can meet kosher style, vegan, dairy free, gluten free, and nut free needs, with one phone call per 4-week session (none in 2-week) and one-way emails (provide stationery).

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Completely booked by December/January.
  - 80% come back next year.
  - All cabins have toilets, sinks and showers inside.
  - Waterfront: mile swimming in the lake and waterskiing.
  - Most Popular: arts & crafts, stained glass, Tennis, rock climbing, archery.
  - Studios: ceramics, jewelry, fine arts, printmaking, sewing, video making, photo lab.
  - Nurse on campus; local medical office; 2–3 good hospitals near; telehealth.
  - Campfires every Friday.
  - 1 play per session (20–25 campers participate).
  - International kids: 10–15 each session.

  

#### Lowlights

  - The campers that do not fit are the competitive ones as socially competitive.
  - No religious services.
  - NO CIT program yet.
  - Cabins don’t take new 15 year old campers.
  - 1 Phone Call per session in a 4 weeks session; in a 2 week session NO phone calls.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Camp Micah — Karen — 8-23-22
text:
# Camp Micah

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Micah is a Jewish co-ed private camp with stunning facilities, top-notch programming, equipment and activity choices. They have 290 kids per session and they sell out in September. Sessions are 3.5 weeks long. Younger kids that stay 1 session vs all summer is 50/50 but as they grow older more and more stay full summer. FACILITIES - they have amazing facilities and everything is in impeccable condition. The fields are very well taken care of. Great balance between sports, arts and outdoor skills and using the great outdoors of Maine.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

CAMP MICAH Monica, Karen, Pilar, Aline visited on August 2nd 2022. We were toured by Jody, one of the directors. We met Mark as well. Jody was so nice. She took us around on a golf cart. DEMOGRAPHICS/LOGISTICS - 35% of campers come from New England, 35% from NY/NJ and the remaining from other US urban and suburban locations. They almost get no internationals because they sell out so early in the fall. CAMPER PROFILE/CAMP PHILOSOPHY - there is a very good balance between the arts and athletics. Camp suits equally both types of children. The kids seemed very nice, sociable, happy and proud about their camp. We talked to some really lovely kids. The girls’ village they now refer to as SABAGO rather than girls as they do have non-binary kids and are trying to be more sensitive.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

BUNKING – Campers typically live in a bunk with 8-9 campers and 2-3 counselors. Bunks are equipped with 2 hot-water showers, 2 sinks, and 2 toilets. Kids clean but professionals sanitize bathrooms. Cabins look great and well organized.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

LAKE/POOL– Lake is beautiful and there is a great heated pool as well. They have both motorized and non-motorized water sports. They have instructional swim until they pass the test in 5th grade.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMING – camp has guided choice in younger years and the programming becomes more full-choice based as kids grow older. Everyone goes on an overnight with their bunk which becomes more challenging and a longer trip out as they grow older. CANTEEN - they have canteen and a rec room with ping pong, air hockey etc for free time/evenings. On Saturday mornings there is a buffet brunch and kids sleep in.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have a great gymnastics program, dance and fitness for older campers. They offer hiking which is very popular. They have a great ninja course, two gaga pits in addition to most land sports and both motorized and non-motorized water sports. 9th grade boys build a sukkah as part of programming. Their last year of camp they travel to Israel and Poland. (When Campers are 16).

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There was armed security at the gate of camp. Jody mentioned not being happy about that but feeling that it was best. DINING HALL – We started tour there. Dining hall is huge, beautiful and very clean. There was a wonderful smell of homemade cookies when we went in. Meals are served family style by bunk. They can handle all food allergies. They do serve peanut butter in a separate area. Food is kosher style (no pork, no shellfish) but meat is not kosher. They can accommodate a real kosher camper. On Saturday mornings there is a buffet brunch and kids sleep in. We also saw the chef barbecuing outside and it looked and smelled amazing. The last night of camp is a banquet and kids dress up for that.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp Micah is a warm, caring environment. Strong emphasis on communal activity: shabbat, prayers at meals (jamotzi and abbreviated Birkat Hamazon), meal times. SERVICES – They do a communal Shabbat service on Friday nights run by the camp grandma who is 80 years old and is a rabbi. They do a community service program called "Tikunamatata". The whole camp sings Oseh Shalom every night and Modeh Ani together every morning. They raise the US and Israel flag every day and lower the flags every evening as a camp activity.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Micah is a Jewish co-ed private camp with stunning facilities, top-notch programming, and a warm, caring environment. Sessions are 3.5 weeks with 290 campers per session, and they sell out in September. The program balances arts, athletics, and outdoor skills, with guided choice for younger campers that becomes more full-choice as kids grow older, plus a beautiful lake and a great heated pool. Communal culture includes Shabbat, prayers at meals, nightly Oseh Shalom and morning Modeh Ani, and a Friday night service led by the camp grandma who is an 80-year-old rabbi.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Stunning facilities, top-notch programming, equipment and activity choices.
  - Amazing facilities and everything is in impeccable condition.
  - Very good balance between the arts and athletics; camp suits equally both types of children.
  - Strong emphasis on communal activity: shabbat, prayers at meals, and singing together.
  - Beautiful lake and a great heated pool; both motorized and non-motorized water sports.
  - Guided choice in younger years moving to more full-choice as campers grow older.
  - They can handle all food allergies and can accommodate a real kosher camper.
  - Their last year of camp they travel to Israel and Poland. (When Campers are 16).
  - Warm, caring environment; kids seemed very nice, sociable, happy and proud about their camp.

  

#### Lowlights

  - There was armed security at the gate of camp. Jody mentioned not being happy about that but feeling that it was best.
  - They almost get no internationals because they sell out so early in the fall.
  - Food is kosher style (no pork, no shellfish) but meat is not kosher.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

CAMP MICAH Monica, Karen, Pilar, Aline visited on August 2nd 2022. We were toured by Jody, one of the directors. We met Mark as well. Jody was so nice. She took us around on a golf cart. There was armed security at the gate of camp. Jody mentioned not being happy about that but feeling that it was best. Micah is a Jewish co-ed private camp with stunning facilities, top-notch programming, equipment and activity choices. They have 290 kids per session and they sell out in September. Sessions are 3.5 weeks long. Younger kids that stay 1 session vs all summer is 50/50 but as they grow older more and more stay full summer. The girls’ village they now refer to as SABAGO rather than girls as they do have non-binary kids and are trying to be more sensitive. DEMOGRAPHICS/LOGISTICS - 35% of campers come from New England, 35% from NY/NJ and the remaining from other US urban and suburban locations. They almost get no internationals because they sell out so early in the fall. BUNKING – Campers typically live in a bunk with 8-9 campers and 2-3 counselors. Bunks are equipped with 2 hot-water showers, 2 sinks, and 2 toilets. Kids clean but professionals sanitize bathrooms. Cabins look great and well organized. DINING HALL – We started tour there. Dining hall is huge, beautiful and very clean. There was a wonderful smell of homemade cookies when we went in. Meals are served family style by bunk. They can handle all food allergies. They do serve peanut butter in a separate area. Food is kosher style (no pork, no shellfish) but meat is not kosher. They can accommodate a real kosher camper. On Saturday mornings there is a buffet brunch and kids sleep in. We also saw the chef barbecuing outside and it looked and smelled amazing. The last night of camp is a banquet and kids dress up for that. SERVICES – They do a communal Shabbat service on Friday nights run by the camp grandma who is 80 years old and is a rabbi. They do a community service program called “Tikunamatata”. The whole camp sings Oseh Shalom every night and Modeh Ani together every morning. They raise the US and Israel flag every day and lower the flags every evening as a camp activity. CAMPER PROFILE/CAMP PHILOSOPHY - there is a very good balance between the arts and athletics. Camp suits equally both types of children. The kids seemed very nice, sociable, happy and proud about their camp. We talked to some really lovely kids. PROGRAMING – camp has guided choice in younger years and the programming becomes more full-choice based as kids grow older. They have instructional swim until they pass the test in 5th grade. They have a great gymnastics program, dance and fitness for older campers. They offer hiking which is very popular. Everyone goes on an overnight with their bunk which becomes more challenging and a longer trip out as they grow older. They have a great ninja course, two gaga pits in addition to most land sports and both motorized and non-motorized water sports. 9th grade boys build a sukkah as part of programming. Their last year of camp they travel to Israel and Poland. (When Campers are 16). LAKE/POOL– Lake is beautiful and there is a great heated pool as well. CANTEEN - they have canteen and a rec room with ping pong, air hockey etc for free time/evenings. FACILITIES - they have amazing facilities and everything is in impeccable condition. The fields are very well taken care of. Camp Micah is a warm, caring environment. Strong emphasis on communal activity: shabbat, prayers at meals (jamotzi and abbreviated Birkat Hamazon), meal times. Great balance between sports, arts and outdoor skills and using the great outdoors of Maine.

  
  

### Author: Karen

title: Les Elfes — Julie — 12-7-22
text:
# Les Elfes

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Les Elfes International began in 1987; they are in business 35 years. Family owned and they extend that sentiment to their philosophy; strive to create a family environment. Located on a ski and mountain resort in Verbier built by the owners. Offer Summer, Winter, and Spring programs (Winter: December-April; Spring: April-June; Summer: June-August). Ages 6-17 years old. Small program with a maximum of 140 children per week. 26% of campers return for at least a second year. Sessions: minimum of 2 weeks; can do 3 or longer if the family would like. Camp is divided into three age groups: 6-10, 11-14, 15-17. Children come from all around the world, over 65 countries represented. Language spoken on the program is English. Program fills up by March.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Camp is divided into three age groups: 6-10, 11-14, 15-17. Children come from all around the world, over 65 countries represented. Language spoken on the program is English. Staff: 80% of staff work year round; open contract so many staff members work for multiple years; staff truly understands program and knows the kids. Director: Unknown.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations are in Swiss style chalets. One chalet for girls; one chalet for boys. Four children per room. Girls have four single beds per room; boys have two bunk beds per room. There are bathrooms inside each room. There is an area called Reception where passports and spending money are held; children can go to reception before an excursion to take out money. Staff helps kids to budget so they do not spend all their money at the beginning of their session. Common area in each chalet.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Activities take place either on the mountain or at the lake. Waterfront and aquatics include tubing, kayaking, sailing, swimming, and surfing (in a pool with artificial waves).

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Day camp sessions: must stay five consecutive days; mixed with residential campers; drop off 8:30, can pick up any time after 5pm. Language classes – 5 hours per week (English, French, German, Spanish or Mandarin). Additional courses: children choose one additional course for one day during their session; course offerings are arts/cooking/music, etiquette/table manners, leadership training, and first aid/mountain fitness. Campers spend one night at a different chalet and participate in “camping” activities (campfire, etc.).

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities are co-ed and take place either on the mountain or at the lake. Activities include: ropes course, zipline, rock climbing, tennis, golf, biking, e-biking (older kids only), hiking, tubing, kayaking, sailing, swimming, surfing (in a pool with artificial waves), coding, indoor skydiving. Offer golf one afternoon every two weeks (introductory course). Winter adds skiing and snowboarding. Offer paragliding for an extra fee. Private tennis, golf and horseback offered for an extra fee. A sports center is located next to the campus; children are taken in groups there to participate in all types of sports. At the end of the session, campers give a presentation on their culture. At the end of the session, there is a prize giving day where children receive language and sports certificates. Campers go on excursions such as a chocolate factory, cheese shop and the Charlie Chaplin Museum.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining: A dining room is located in the chalet. Four meals served: breakfast, lunch, snack, and dinner. There is also a restaurant on the mountain that is used for lunch when children are engaging in activities and there is not enough time to get back to the chalet; this occurs usually one time per week. Kitchen staff can accommodate for all allergies and dietary needs. Security: staff are trained for two weeks before every session; first aid training is given; criminal background checks; 24/7 nurse and night guard; cameras are in all corridors and common areas. Medical clinic nearby and hospital 10 minutes by helicopter.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Family owned and they extend that sentiment to their philosophy; strive to create a family environment. Co-ed. Small program with a maximum of 140 children per week. Children come from all around the world, over 65 countries represented; language spoken on the program is English. Staff truly understands program and knows the kids.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Camp has a booth at the airport to help with arrivals. Day camp: drop off 8:30, can pick up any time after 5pm. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: paragliding; private tennis, golf and horseback.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Les Elfes International is a family owned camp on a ski and mountain resort in Verbier offering Summer, Winter, and Spring programs for ages 6-17. It is a small, co-ed, international program (over 65 countries represented) with English as the language spoken and a maximum of 140 children per week. Activities take place either on the mountain or at the lake and include ropes course, zipline, rock climbing, coding, indoor skydiving, tubing, kayaking, sailing, swimming, and surfing (in a pool with artificial waves), with winter adding skiing and snowboarding. Sessions are a minimum of 2 weeks (day camp must stay five consecutive days), language classes are 5 hours per week, and there are end-of-session cultural presentations, prize giving, and excursions.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Family owned; strive to create a family environment.
  - Located on a ski and mountain resort in Verbier built by the owners.
  - Small program with a maximum of 140 children per week.
  - Over 65 countries represented; language spoken is English.
  - 80% of staff work year round; many staff members work for multiple years.
  - Security: 24/7 nurse and night guard; cameras in all corridors and common areas.
  - Sports center located next to campus.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Program fills up by March.
  - Sessions: minimum of 2 weeks.
  - Extra fees for paragliding and private tennis, golf and horseback.
  - Director: Unknown.
  - Parent communication: Unknown.
  - Tech policy: Unknown.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Can do 3 or longer if the family would like • Day camp sessions -Must stay five consecutive days - Mixed with residential campers -drop off 8:30, can pick up any time after 5pm • Camp is divided into three age groups: 6-10, 11-14, 15-17 • Children come from all around the world, over 65 countries represented • Language spoken on the program is English • Staff -80% of staff work year round -open contract so many staff members work for multiple years -staff truly understands program and knows the kids • Accommodations -Swiss style chalet -One chalet for girls -One chalet for boys -Four children per room -Girls have four single beds per room -Boys have two bunk beds per room -There are bathrooms inside each room -There is an area called Reception where passports and spending money are held. Children can go to reception before an excursion to take out money. Staff helps kids to budget so they do not spend all their money at the beginning of their session. -common area in each chalet • Dining -A dining room is located in the chalet -Four meals served: breakfast, lunch, snack, and dinner -There is also a restaurant on the mountain that is used for lunch when children are engaging in activities and there is not enough time to get back to the chalet. This occurs usually one time per week. -Kitchen staff can accommodate for all allergies and dietary needs • A sports center is located next to the campus. Children are taken in groups there to participate in all types of sports • Activities -co-ed -take place either on the mountain or at the lake -activities include: ropes course, zipline, rock climbing, tennis, golf, biking, e-biking (older kids only), hiking, tubing, kayaking, sailing, swimming, surfing (in a pool with artificial waves), coding, indoor skydiving -offer golf one afternoon every two weeks-introductory course -language classes – 5 hours per week, English, French, German, Spanish or Mandarin -Winter adds skiing and snowboarding -campers spend one night at a different chalet and participate in “camping” activities (campfire, etc.) -offer paragliding for an extra fee -private tennis, golf and horseback offered for an extra fee • Additional courses -Children choose one additional course for one day during their session -Course offerings are arts/cooking/music, etiquette/table manners, leadership training, and first aid/mountain fitness • At the end of the session, campers give a presentation on their culture • At the end of the session, there is a prize giving day where children receive language and sports certificates • Campers go on excursions such as a chocolate factory, cheese shop and the Charlie Chaplin Museum • Security -staff are trained for two weeks before every session -first aid training is given -criminal background checks -24/7 nurse and night guard -cameras are in all corridors and common areas -medical clinic nearby and hospital 10 minutes by helicopter -Camp has a booth at the airport to help with arrivals • Program fills up by March

title: Lee Mar — Emily — 11-2-23
text:
# Lee Mar

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Lee Mar is an ACA accredited camp located in Lackawaxen, Pennsylvania (5 minutes from Pine Forest). It was founded in 1953. It’s a 6.5-week camp for kids with special needs ages 7 – 21 years old. Kids at camp tend to have learning disabilities, Down Syndrome, Autism Spectrum Disorder, Sensory Processing Disorder, Prader Willi Syndrome etc. Kids can be nonverbal but, need to have receptive language. Only allow partial session for kids 7-11 the first summer. Don’t advertise partial. Ages 12 and up always fill up so can’t offer partial session. Have about 100 campers (60 males and 40 females). They do offer some scholarships. Kids come from all over the US and also Puerto Rico, Egypt, Israel and England.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ari Segal - Director Ari@leemar.com 215-658-1708. Lynsey Trohoske – Assistant Director Lynsey@leemar.com. Directors conduct an extensive interview with parents to see if camp is a good fit. To see if kid is a good fit, Directors ask parents what type of classroom setting their child is in, how they see their child and how their child sees themselves. Also ask if they sleep through the night, any emotional/behavioral concerns and have them email the IEP. Also ask if they have taken part in a buddy group or Special Olympics. Lee Mar does not take kids with extreme behavior issues. Kids with just ADHD or ADD often don’t see themselves fitting in with campers at Lee Mar. Lee Mar has had a few kids who "identify" differently but no actual transgender kids. Kids MUST be potty trained and fairly independent with grooming skills. There are no CIT’s at camp. Counselors tend to be between the ages of 19-22. They are studying Special Ed, Speech or OT/PT or have some experience in an educational, therapeutic setting. 1:3 Ratio of Counselors to kids. Counselors arrive 1 week early and do a specific orientation on how to work with special needs kids using role play etc. There is high return rate of counselors and campers.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

All bunks, dining hall and facilities are air conditioned. Camp is built on hills so it is not accessible for kids in wheelchairs. Kids have individual showers in the bunk with curtains. Staff help and supervise showers, etc. Camp improvement projects going on now include building one more indoor/outdoor facility and redoing amphitheater. When kids arrive at camp, their luggage is all unpacked and setup so they can head right into routine and structure.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Kids do boat type activities in the lake and swim lessons in the pool. Recreation includes swimming and paddleboarding.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Program is very structured. There are 6 periods in a day, 3 morning and 3 afternoons. Kids get 2-4 speech sessions a week, 4 academic sessions in a week and skills training. There is 1 special event per week which has fun themes such as July 4th, Olympics, Crazy Hair Day etc. Rest of the week is recreational typical camp activities. Camp follows the child’s IEP (Individualized Education Plan) from school.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Educational activities at camp include Speech Therapy, Academics, Vocational and Job Training, Occupational Therapy, Daily Living Skills and Physical Therapy. Recreation activities include sports, drama, hiking, arts and crafts, therapeutic horseback riding, swimming, paddleboarding, photography etc.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

2-3 Health Center Staff (Doctor and Nurses) at all times. Staff brings meds to the kids at bunks or at meals. They keep track of their weight as some of the kids with Autism are very picky eaters and parents don’t want them to lose too much weight. They have had a few kids with seizure disorders and diabetes but no kids on a vent or feeding tube, that is more appropriate for a medical camp. They can manage celiac and food allergies. Camp is 40 minutes from the hospital so they tend to deal with medical issues at camp when possible.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

When parents call camp, either Ari or Lynsey pick up the phone and parents love that.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No buses to camp, all parents drop off and pick up the kids. Parents fly the kids in and the camp is 1/5 hours from Newark Airport. Since Covid, they have eliminated Visiting Day and that is working very well for parents and kids. Instead, they have 2 Facetime calls during the summer. They also post pictures and communicate about activities on camp app. Ari and Lynsey suggest interested parents view the website and watch the YouTube videos of staff and camper interviews. Gives a good feel for the camp. A brand new camp website is coming out in 3 weeks, Ari will send the link to Joanne when its ready. Dates for 2024 are June 22 to August 5 for $14,900. They do offer some scholarships.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Lee Mar is an ACA accredited camp in Lackawaxen, Pennsylvania for kids with special needs ages 7–21. The program is very structured with 6 periods in a day and integrates Speech Therapy, Academics, Vocational and Job Training, Occupational Therapy, Daily Living Skills and Physical Therapy alongside typical camp activities. Lee Mar does not take kids with extreme behavior issues; kids must be potty trained and have receptive language, and the Directors conduct an extensive interview and review the IEP to see if camp is a good fit. Dates for 2024 are June 22 to August 5 for $14,900; no buses (all parents drop off and pick up), Visiting Day is eliminated with 2 Facetime calls and photos on the camp app.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Camp Lee Mar is an ACA accredited camp located in Lackawaxen, Pennsylvania (5 minutes from Pine Forest).
  - It was founded in 1953.
  - It’s a 6.5-week camp for kids with special needs ages 7 – 21 years old.
  - Program is very structured.
  - 1:3 Ratio of Counselors to kids.
  - All bunks, dining hall and facilities are air conditioned.
  - There is high return rate of counselors and campers.
  - They do offer some scholarships.
  - Kids come from all over the US and also Puerto Rico, Egypt, Israel and England.
  - Directors conduct an extensive interview with parents to see if camp is a good fit.
  - They also post pictures and communicate about activities on camp app.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Camp is built on hills so it is not accessible for kids in wheelchairs.
  - Lee Mar does not take kids with extreme behavior issues.
  - Kids MUST be potty trained and fairly independent with grooming skills.
  - No buses to camp, all parents drop off and pick up the kids.
  - Since Covid, they have eliminated Visiting Day.
  - Camp is 40 minutes from the hospital so they tend to deal with medical issues at camp when possible.
  - Only allow partial session for kids 7-11 the first summer. Don’t advertise partial. Ages 12 and up always fill up so can’t offer partial session.
  - They have had a few kids with seizure disorders and diabetes but no kids on a vent or feeding tube, that is more appropriate for a medical camp.
  - Kids with just ADHD or ADD often don’t see themselves fitting in with campers at Lee Mar.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Laurel South — Lana — 8-13-23
text:
# Laurel South

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Laurel South 415 kids, 87% return rate, 300 person wait list. 4 week sessions; will not allow to stay full summer. Sessions are totally the same and repeat even fireworks that first session gets July 4. Impressions - beautiful 4 week only session camp that fills fast.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Roger and Dagni directors. Partners with Laurel. All in ownership of Laurel and Kenwood/Kenmont. Have spent 31 years here. Live in Gainesville Florida and sometimes Georgia. Crimson and Drew Assistant directors. Live in Gainesville and have 3 year old son Jackson. Crimson toured us. Have more quirky kids than a 7 week camp. Half of kids sports oriented. Have some celebrity kids. Kids from La/nyc/Florida. 85% campers J.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Team sports and swimming are not coed activities. Instructional swimming up till 6th grade then complete choice.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

All choice. 10 activities total. 5 A day 5 B day - 2 team sports, tennis, instructional swimming up till 6th grade then complete choice.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Most popular sports soccer lax pickle ball. Nature program with pigs and goats. Stem lego building digital photography rocketry podcasting ceramics. Fitness strength and conditioning 7-9th graders and then a training center for younger kids without weights. 2 plays/session. One young kids one old kids. Color war 2 days each session. Intercamp with Cedar Mataponi Agawam Crescent Cove. Little brother little sister program and can take classes with them. Horseback riding and fly fishing extra costs. Roger fly fishes at home during year. Trips 8th grade and up. White water rafting in maine. Acadia National park. Bar Harbour. 7th graders white mountains. 5th/6th graders water park. Hiking club voluntary by age.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Nut free can handle all other allergies. Health center full time doctor. 10 nurses and nurse practitioner. Friday night Shabbat dinner and motzi. Not religious but cultural. Challah brisket matzah ball soup. Breakfast/dinner family style by bunk. Lunch buffet and can sit anywhere they want.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Beautiful camp but not a country club according to Roger. Have more quirky kids than a 7 week camp. Half of kids sports oriented. Have some celebrity kids. Whole camp roads covered in gravel. No dirt roads. Kids were active, engaged and friendly.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Kids from La/nyc/Florida. Jfk flight or purchase bus for kids from tristate. 2 calls/session. 1/4 kids make no calls. Visiting day is last day of camp. 75% of parents come for last day rest went home bus or flight. No phones allowed. No uniforms or towel service. Post pics also have videographer. Horseback riding and fly fishing extra costs.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Beautiful 4 week only session camp that fills fast. All choice with 10 activities total (5 A day, 5 B day) and instructional swimming up till 6th grade then complete choice. Kids were active, engaged and friendly. I’d give with other session camps in Maine along with TLW and other 4 week only camps.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 87% return rate; 300 person wait list.
  - Beautiful 4 week only session camp that fills fast.
  - All choice. 10 activities total.
  - Health center full time doctor. 10 nurses and nurse practitioner.
  - Kids were active, engaged and friendly.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Will not allow to stay full summer.
  - Sessions are totally the same and repeat even fireworks that first session gets July 4.
  - 2 calls/session. 1/4 kids make no calls.
  - No phones allowed.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - TLW
  - other session camps in Maine
  - other 4 week only camps

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Laurel south 415 kids 87% return rate 300 person wait list Roger and Dagni directors. Partners with Laurel. All in ownership of Laurel and Kenwood/Kenmont. Have spent 31 years here. Live in Gainesville Florida and sometimes Georgia. Crimson and Drew Assistant directors. Live in Gainesville and have 3 year old son Jackson. Crimson toured us 4 week sessions. Will not allow to stay full summer. Sessions are totally the same and repeat even fireworks that first session gets July 4 Have more quirky kids than a 7 week camp. Half of kids sports oriented. Have some celebrity kids. Team sports and swimming are not coed activities. Beautiful camp but not a country club according to Roger. Whole camp roads covered in gravel. No dirt roads. Kids from La/nyc/Florida. Jfk flight or purchase bus for kids from tristate. 2 calls/session. 1/4 kids make no calls. Visiting day is last day of camp. 75% of parents come for last day rest went home bus or flight. 85% campers J. Friday night Shabbat dinner and motzi. Not religious but cultural. Challah brisket matzah ball soup. Breakfast/dinner family style by bunk. Lunch buffet and can sit anywhere they want. Nut free can handle all other allergies. All choice. 10 activities total. 5 a day 5 b day-2 team sports, tennis, instructional swimming up till 6th grade then complete choice. Most popular sports soccer lax pickle ball. Nature program with pigs and goats. Stem lego building digital photography rocketry podcasting ceramics fitness strength and conditioning 7-9th graders and then a training center for younger kids without weights 2 plays/session. One young kids one old kids. Horseback riding and fly fishing extra costs. Roger fly fishes at home during year No uniforms or towel service Canteen every other day Post pics also have videographer Color war 2 days each session. Intercamp with Cedar Mataponi Agawam Crescent Cove Little brother little sister program and can take classes with them No phones allowed Health center full time doctor. 10 nurses and nurse practitioner Trips 8th grade and up. White water rafting in maine. Acadia National park. Bar Harbour 7th graders white mountains 5th/6th graders water park Hiking club voluntary by age Impressions-beautiful 4 week only session camp that fills fast. Kids were active, engaged and friendly. I’d give with other session camps in Maine along with TLW and other 4 week only camps.

title: Independent Lake — Lisa — 8-22-23
text:
# Independent Lake

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

INDEPENDENT LAKE JULY 2022. 1st session had 550 campers, 2nd had 630. 180 staff. Starting at age 6. All choice.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We were toured by Jeff Gould and his wife Brooke. 1st session had 550 campers, 2nd had 630. 180 staff. Starting at age 6, most parents drive their kids up to camp, he wants parents to see the improvements. They are on the liberal side with intentional diversity. This camp is the most diverse I’ve seen. They had 110 international campers. 30 kids from FL, will chaperone in 2023 for first and second sessions.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: had screens with menu. Jeff redid 54 bunks and the rest will be done this fall. 12 campers and 2 counselors. Bunk beds, all new windows. Up the hill is Elk View where the kids ages 6-12 live. Lakeside is down the hill for ages 13-16. Parents can unpack for their kids.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

NO swim instruction required. 2 motorboats, huge aquapark, with all the boating and heated pool. Westmont is across the lake.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Just like Majors and Minors, they refer to them as Primes and Dailies. 3 Primes and 2 Dailies every day. All choice, 40% of the activities are Coed.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

A\&C: Paint, tye dye, woodburning, stained glass, sewing, knitting, Beading, mosaics, 4 ceramic wheels, 2 kilns exposed. Digital Arts is big, 25 Imac computers, they film pranks (jackass), do editing, digital phot, graphic arts, video. Performing Arts: 2 shows/session, lots of Theater Tech. Circus has trapeze, silks, juggling, Magic, etc. 2 Dance studios. Music is big: Guitar, drums, keyboards, they have 7-8 bands, Voice, Recording Studios, Rock Band, DJing, all with AC. Fitness Pavilion, more cross fit. Live Action Role Play, dungeons & dragons. Sports fields are up above all the arts buildings, a considerable walk, Soccer and Basketball, skate park, go-carts ( I only saw 3). Golf driving range, Tennis ( extra $ for private lessons), and Horses are away from everything on their own. 9 horses, the instructor is from Medijin Columbia, can go every other day, English, Western and vaulting, 68 campers ride. All new High ropes, low ropes and an indoor climbing wall. Refurbishing the Gym.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: Big Ass fans, not traditional looking, no plaques, more like a cafeteria. They have a DJ with music/sound system and some live productions at meals. Sit by bunk. Mid-afternoon there is an ice cream truck. Canteen every night and is extra $$. Nut free. Hot dog bar for lunch and dinner. 1 Doctor, 4 RNs.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They are on the liberal side with intentional diversity. This camp is the most diverse I’ve seen. No religion.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Starting at age 6, most parents drive their kids up to camp, he wants parents to see the improvements. Parents can unpack for their kids. 30 kids from FL, will chaperone in 2023 for first and second sessions. Canteen every night and is extra $$. Tennis ( extra $ for private lessons).

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Independent Lake is on the liberal side with intentional diversity and was the most diverse camp I’ve seen. The program is all choice with 3 Primes and 2 Dailies every day, and there are big strengths in Digital Arts, music, circus, performing arts, and a huge aquapark with a heated pool. Cabins are being refurbished (54 bunks redone) with 12 campers and 2 counselors, with Elk View for ages 6-12 and Lakeside for ages 13-16. Families will find a nut-free dining hall with DJ-led meals, a nightly canteen (extra $$), and on-site medical coverage (1 Doctor, 4 RNs).

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All choice, 40% of the activities are Coed.
  - Just like Majors and Minors, they refer to them as Primes and Dailies.
  - 3 Primes and 2 Dailies every day.
  - Digital Arts is big, 25 Imac computers, they film pranks (jackass), do editing, digital phot, graphic arts, video.
  - Music is big: Guitar, drums, keyboards, they have 7-8 bands, Voice, Recording Studios, Rock Band, DJing, all with AC.
  - Circus has trapeze, silks, juggling, Magic, etc.
  - 2 motorboats, huge aquapark, with all the boating and heated pool.
  - All new High ropes, low ropes and an indoor climbing wall.
  - Nut free.
  - 1 Doctor, 4 RNs.
  - They have a DJ with music/sound system and some live productions at meals.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Sports fields are up above all the arts buildings, a considerable walk.
  - Canteen every night and is extra $$.
  - Tennis ( extra $ for private lessons).
  - go-carts ( I only saw 3).
  - Refurbishing the Gym.
  - NO swim instruction required.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Mondamin — Karen — 7-8-24
text:
# Mondamin

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Mondamin (Boys) July 3, 2024 tour given by Aiden Shaw - Camp Director. Camp Expert: Tami Feldman, Atlanta, Georgia. Camp Mondamin is an all boys family owned camp. It was founded in 1933 and is located in Zirconia, North Carolina which is about 15 minutes outside of Hendersonville. The sister camp is Green Cove. It is a big tripping camp. The most important thing with Mondamin is their skill progression. It is not a competitive camp.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Most of the campers come from the south. They do have a couple campers from Taiwan and Hawaii this summer. This summer they only have about 80 campers. They are trying to build the camp back up. The camp was closed in 2023 due to a staffing issue. They had about 160 campers enrolled and the month before camp started, they didn’t hire enough staff and had to close for the summer. Because of that, they had to get all new administration at the camp. Tour given by Aiden Shaw - Camp Director. Aiden was a former counselor at the camp and this is his first summer as Director. Because camp was closed last year, he worked at the sister camp, Green Cove.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

The cabins were a bit rustic. There are bunk beds but no facilities in the cabins. Sink, showers and toilets are on a hill.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Their lake activities are very popular. They had everything\! They also have sailing. There is a zip line that ends with a big splash in the lake. The campers also build a canoe every year from start to finish. They then use the canoes on the lake. They do paddle competitions with other camps. The girls at Green Cove come over and use some of the lake facilities.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

There are no schedules at the camp. You can choose your activities daily. Most of the campers leave to go on trips every day. It is a very relaxed camp. They sing songs at announcements every morning. They do this as a whole camp. There is an evening activity each night.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have 3, 5 and 6 day trips that the boys can go on as well as day trips. They focus on learning everything about 1 or 2 activities. For example, their mountain biking program is huge. The child can learn everything about the mountain bikes - how to fix it and certain things with riding and then when they get proficient in it they can go on a trip. They want to teach you all the skills you need to know. There is a zip line that ends with a big splash in the lake, tennis, pickleball (new this year), gardening, arts and crafts, basketball, soccer, gaga but there was an emphasis that it is not a big ball sport camp. They also have pottery. Fishing is new this year. They have a nature cabin - snakes, roaches, fish that the campers caught, lizards, a gecko. The kids can adopt an animal in nature and take care of it. They also had chickens. They do have a barn with horses. The girls at Green Cove come over and use some of the lake facilities and the boys go over there to use their high ropes. Sunday is a campfire and they tell stories and reflect on the week. Campers give trip reports and tell all about their time on a trip - how it was, if it was hard, who they would recommend it to. The directors run the campfire and the aides help build the fire.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

They have snacks every day - fresh fruit and sometimes chips or granola bars. They sit as a cabin and eat family style. The food is brought to your table and you pass it around. Health and safety specifics: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

It is a very relaxed camp. The most important thing with Mondamin is their skill progression. It is not a competitive camp. Aiden described it as competing with yourself. They do not give awards, but you are rewarded with advanced trips as you build your skills. The campfire was interesting. It was surrounded by water. It was very relaxing and under really tall trees.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Located in Zirconia, North Carolina which is about 15 minutes outside of Hendersonville. Hendersonville and Brevard aren’t that far if looking for a girls camp for a sibling. They do have a scholarship program that was founded by alumni. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown. Transportation/logistics details beyond location: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Mondamin is an all boys family owned camp founded in 1933 and located in Zirconia, North Carolina, about 15 minutes outside of Hendersonville. It is a big tripping camp with no schedules; you can choose your activities daily and most campers leave to go on trips every day. The most important thing with Mondamin is their skill progression—it is not a competitive camp (competing with yourself), and you are rewarded with advanced trips as you build your skills. I would recommend this camp to a boy that loves adventure since they go on trips every day and focus on mastering a skill, and to a boy that loves watersports and even building since they do build a canoe every summer.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - It is a big tripping camp (3, 5 and 6 day trips as well as day trips).
  - The most important thing with Mondamin is their skill progression (competing with yourself; no awards; rewarded with advanced trips).
  - Their mountain biking program is huge.
  - The campers also build a canoe every year from start to finish and use the canoes on the lake.
  - Their lake activities are very popular (they had everything; sailing; zip line splash).
  - There are no schedules at the camp; you can choose your activities daily.
  - Sunday is a campfire with trip reports; the campfire was surrounded by water and under really tall trees.
  - They do have a scholarship program that was founded by alumni.
  - The sister camp is Green Cove; Aiden did mention that it is an extremely hard camp to get into (always a waitlist filled with alumni campers).

  

#### Lowlights

  - The camp was closed in 2023 due to a staffing issue (about 160 campers enrolled; didn’t hire enough staff and had to close).
  - Because of that, they had to get all new administration; this summer they only have about 80 campers (trying to build the camp back up).
  - The cabins were a bit rustic; no facilities in the cabins (sink, showers and toilets are on a hill).
  - It is not a big ball sport camp.
  - Aiden was a former counselor and this is his first summer as Director.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Green Cove

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

It was founded in 1933 and is located in Zirconia, North Carolina which is about 15 minutes outside of Hendersonville. The camp was closed in 2023 due to a staffing issue. They had about 160 campers enrolled and the month before camp started, they didn’t hire enough staff and had to close for the summer. Because of that, they had to get all new administration at the camp. This summer they only have about 80 campers. They are trying to build the camp back up. Aiden was a former counselor at the camp and this is his first summer as Director. Because camp was closed last year, he worked at the sister camp, Green Cove. The camp is a big tripping camp. They always have a waitlist and it is filled with alumni campers. Hendersonville and Brevard aren’t that far if looking for a girls camp for a sibling.
