title: North Star — Natasha — 8-13-23
text:
# North Star

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp North Star - co-ed - offers 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7-week options. The most popular session is the first four weeks; they had 212 campers and can take 215.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Directors Steve (used to run Julian Krinsky) and Brooke Bernstein toured us. Brooke is a former MSW. 9 campers to 3 counselors per bunk. Staff training is an essential aspect of the camp's preparation, with orientation lasting 9.5 to 14 days. Camp North Star brings in experts, like Jake Schott from the Summer Camp Society, to provide motivational talks to staff. TRANS/Non-binary - They do not have mixed bunks as of yet but strive to create a welcoming environment for all campers, including those who identify as non-binary or transgender. They do have non-binary at camp but live by parts born with.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

The bunks have a giant island-like piece of furniture for clothes storage in the middle with the beds around it.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake – private with various water activities, including water skiing (at another part of the lake), paddleboarding, fishing, sailboats, and water toys.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Evening activities include both all-camp events and separate activities. Color war is called Eco Challenge and World Games, with challenges during the first two weeks of the summer.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Culinary program is very popular - campers can learn campfire cooking, including making dishes like Mac and cheese over a flame. There is also traditional culinary in the dining hall. Music is big - keyboard, ukulele, and rock band activities. Basketball and mountain biking very popular – ride all over camp. Video production and Dungeons and Dragons also big. Golf and HB riding off camp. North Star has lots of special events - dances, banquets, and the RICHs ceremony, which celebrates the values of respect, independence, caring, and honor of campers; counselors nominate campers who exemplify these values. At the end of the 4-week and 7-week sessions, the camp honors campers with the RICHs award, recognizing those who embody the camp's core values and contribute positively to the camp community. Special "Sundaes on Sundays" is also a looked forward to event.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

HC - 3 nurses on staff; lots of urgent care nearby. Camp is nut-free and does not allow food in the bunks. Campers with diabetes cannot be accommodated with the absence of Wi-Fi. Covid-related changes that have stuck: no parents going into bunks during drop-off, handwashing stations throughout the camp, outdoor dining, and the discontinuation of package deliveries.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Natasha, Risa, Lindsey and Lana toured North Star on 7/20/23. Steve and Brooke were enthusiastic tour guides and passionate about camp. Campers stopped to chat with them wherever we went. Camp is welcoming, warm and low-key.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No Visiting Day – but parents can visit on changeover day. Extra $ - HB, bus to camp, laundry. Travel: bus to camp available. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp North Star is co-ed and offers 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7-week options, with the first four weeks the most popular. Camp is welcoming, warm and low-key, and a great option for a client wanting a shorter session. The private lake supports water skiing (at another part of the lake), paddleboarding, fishing, sailboats, and water toys. Music is big and the culinary program is very popular, with campers learning campfire cooking and traditional culinary in the dining hall.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Directors Steve (used to run Julian Krinsky) and Brooke Bernstein were enthusiastic tour guides and passionate about camp.
  - Campers stopped to chat with them wherever we went.
  - Camp is welcoming, warm and low-key.
  - Private lake with water skiing, paddleboarding, fishing, sailboats, and water toys.
  - Culinary program is very popular; campers can learn campfire cooking, including making Mac and cheese over a flame.
  - Music is big - keyboard, ukulele, and rock band activities.
  - Basketball and mountain biking very popular – ride all over camp.
  - Video production and Dungeons and Dragons also big.
  - North Star has lots of special events - dances, banquets, and the RICHs ceremony.
  - Special "Sundaes on Sundays" is also a looked forward to event.
  - Evening activities include both all-camp events and separate activities.
  - Staff training orientation lasts 9.5 to 14 days and includes experts like Jake Schott from the Summer Camp Society.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No Visiting Day – but parents can visit on changeover day.
  - Camp is nut-free and does not allow food in the bunks.
  - Campers with diabetes cannot be accommodated with the absence of Wi-Fi.
  - TRANS/Non-binary - do not have mixed bunks as of yet; do have non-binary at camp but live by parts born with.
  - Extra $ - HB, bus to camp, laundry.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Kohut
  - Kingsley Pines

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

NORTH STAR Natasha, Risa, Lindsey and Lana toured North Star on 7/20/23. Directors Steve (used to run Julian Krinsky) and Brooke Bernstein toured us. Brooke is a former MSW. Camp North Star - co-ed-offers, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7-week options. The most popular session is the first four weeks, they had 212 campers and can take 215. 9 campers to 3 counselors per bunk. The bunks have a giant island-like piece of furniture for clothes storage in the middle w/ the beds around it. No Visiting Day – but parents can visit on changeover day HC- 3 nurses on staff, lots of urgent care nearby Extra $ - HB, bus to camp, laundry TRANS/Non-binary- They do not have mixed bunks as of yet but strive to create a welcoming environment for all campers, including those who identify as non-binary or transgender. They do have non-binary at camp but live by parts born with.. Staff training is an essential aspect of the camp's preparation, with orientation lasting 9.5 to 14 days. Camp North Star brings in experts, like Jake Schott from the Summer Camp Society, to provide motivational talks to staff. Camp is nut-free and does not allow food in the bunks. Campers with Diabetes cannot be accommodated with the absence of Wi-Fi. Lake – private with various water activities, including water skiing (at another part of the lake), paddleboarding, fishing, sailboats, and water toys. Culinary program is very popular -campers can learn campfire cooking, including making dishes like Mac and cheese over a flame. There is also traditional culinary in the dining hall. Music is big - keyboard, ukulele, and rock band activities. basketball and mountain biking very popular – ride all over camp video production and Dungeons and Dragons also big Golf and HB riding off camp Covid-related changes that have stuck: no parents going into bunks during drop-off, handwashing stations throughout the camp, outdoor dining and the discontinuation of package deliveries. North Star has lots of special events -dances, banquets, and the RICHs ceremony, which celebrates the values of respect, independence, caring, and honor of campers, Counselors nominate campers who exemplify these values. At the end of the 4-week and 7-week sessions, the camp honors campers with the RICHs award, recognizing those who embody the camp's core values and contribute positively to the camp community. Special "Sundaes on Sundays" is also a looked forward to event. Color war is called Eco Challenge and World Games, with challenges during the first two weeks of the summer. Evening activities include both all-camp events and separate activities. Steve and Brooke were enthusiastic tour guides and passionate about camp. Campers stopped to chat with them wherever we went. Camp is welcoming, warm and low-key, A great option for a client wanting a shorter session. I would give with Kohut and Kingsley Pines.

title: Lake Greeley — Emily — 7-11-24
text:
# Lake Greeley

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

LAKE GREELEY CAMP, Greeley, PA. Offer 2-week sessions into mid-August (8 weeks) but can go for more than one. Anywhere from 150 to 220 kids per session; max capacity is 300. Co-ed. Non religious affiliation. Brothers own Cayuga and Lohikan. Most kids stay for 2 or 4 weeks. Camp is pretty well priced at about $3800 for 2 weeks and goes through mid August.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Counselors are 90% International. After 10th grade, there is an LIT program. Offer a rookie week June 23-29 for campers age 6-13 who have not attended camp before. 07/02/24 visit by Emily Rothenberg, Michele Gershwin, Michelle Burger and Heather Pasquale; toured by Matt Sr and Matt Jr Buynak.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks have about 12 kids with 3 counselors. Showers, sinks and bathrooms in the bunk. Bunks very basic but clean.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake is shared with Shohola. Non motorized activities such as paddle board, kayaking, canoe. Cross the road to get to upper camp and lake.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Grade 5th and up all elective. Before that it is more travel with cabin structure. Sign up elective activities each day. Every morning whole camp meets at flagpole; we saw flagpole meeting and it looked very positive and motivating. Evening activity is usually by junior and senior camp, sometimes whole camp together. Canteen is once per day.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Many activities are offered including skatepark, tennis, basketball, lacrosse, soccer, flag football, circus, bungee tramp, Gaga, music, gymnastics, dance, martial arts, horseback, archery, riflery, creative arts, photography, egames, adventure, rock wall, ziplines. Horseback riding is NOT an extra cost. Have rings and trails, do jumping. At end of summer do a horse show, have stable management. There are 8 horses. Play intercamps against mostly Cayuga and Lohikan and sometimes Pine Forest, Netimus and Shohola. Off camp trips can include Dorney Park, baseball game, hiking and canoe trips. Have an indoor gymnasium for multiple use, outdoor amphitheater. Olympics are second half of summer.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Basic and clean dining hall. Junior camp eats separately from senior camp. Peanut aware camp. Can take kids that don’t have severe allergies, also handle gluten free and vegetarian. 3 RNs on site. Hospital and Walk In Clinic nearby. Only Health Center is air conditioned.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall, Lake Greeley attracts very nice and down to earth kids. It is more of a recreation camp than competitive where you can do a bit of everything. The grounds are quite woodsy and basic.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No buses, parent drop off only. Kids are not allowed to have cell phones. Only extra cost is canteen fund.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Lake Greeley is a co-ed, non religious camp in Greeley, PA offering 2-week sessions into mid-August (8 weeks), with most kids staying for 2 or 4 weeks. It attracts very nice and down to earth kids and is more of a recreation camp than competitive where you can do a bit of everything. Grade 5th and up is all elective with sign-ups each day, younger campers follow more travel with cabin structure, and every morning the whole camp meets at the flagpole. Camp is pretty well priced at about $3800 for 2 weeks and goes through mid August.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Horseback riding is NOT an extra cost (8 horses, rings and trails, do jumping, end-of-summer horse show, stable management).
  - Many activities are offered including skatepark, circus, bungee tramp, rock wall, ziplines, egames, and creative arts.
  - Every morning whole camp meets at flagpole; it looked very positive and motivating.
  - Counselors are 90% International.
  - Play intercamps against mostly Cayuga and Lohikan and sometimes Pine Forest, Netimus and Shohola.
  - Olympics are second half of summer.
  - Indoor gymnasium and outdoor amphitheater.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Only Health Center is air conditioned.
  - Bunks very basic.
  - No buses, parent drop off only.
  - Cross the road to get to upper camp and lake.
  - Peanut aware camp. Can take kids that don’t have severe allergies.
  - The grounds are quite woodsy and basic.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Cayuga
  - Lohikan
  - Pine Forest
  - Netimus
  - Shohola

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Max capacity is 300 Co-ed. Grade 5 th and up all elective. Before that its more travel with cabin structure. Sign up elective activities each day Non religious affiliation. title: Menominee — Renee — 7-10-23
text:
# Menominee

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Menominee for Boys. Eagle River, WI. Director: Jason Feldgreber. Registration opens August 1st, 2022. 150 campers 1st session, 170 campers 2nd session (190 is max). 1/3 are 7 week (full summer). There is a 2 week rookie program for younger campers. 50% Chicago. Other areas: Scottsdale, Denver, LA/SF.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Director: Jason Feldgreber. 150 campers 1st session, 170 campers 2nd session (190 is max). 1/3 are 7 week (full summer). There is a 2 week rookie program for younger campers. 50% Chicago. Other areas: Scottsdale, Denver, LA/SF.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Smaller cabins/higher ratios. NO bunk beds, single cots only. Toilets and sinks in cabins, central shower houses. Counselors sleep in same area as campers.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Located on a quiet chain of lakes, they are the most action on their lake. Big ski and fishing program. Nice, sand bottom swimming area.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

All campers choose their sports (everyone makes a team). 6 choice instructional periods in the AM (2 week rotations). General periods with unstructured activities in AM also. Games/Leagues in PM.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Goal setting is big deal at camp—progress is tracked all summer and a profile is given at the end of the summer. Have the opportunity to win Gold, Silver, Bronze or Master in activities. Campers accumulate pins that they can take home. Rec hall houses skit nights, talent shows, commercial nights—EPs are always some kind of performance. Special Programming: King of the Camp (1st Session)—teams last for your entire career at camp; includes competitions for points (sports and other activities); can choose which activities they want to participate in for King of Camp. Leagues/Competitions. Big competition in 2nd session with Camp Kawaga. Green vs White color war (teams change every year). Sunday Campfire (Not religious). Sand volleyball, (coming soon) turf workout facility with cardio equipment, indoor bball and pickle ball. 9 hole golf course on camp grounds with a 400 yd driving range—campers can go to the local golf course on Sundays (at least 2 4somes each Sunday); offer golf clinics from Chicago golf pro. Tennis pro on staff—offers private, semi-private, and group lessons for an extra fee—$70/hour for private lesson.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Have a big mess hall and also eat outside—buffet style meals with salad bar at lunch and dinner—can accommodate specialty diets. 2 RNs full time on camp. Unsure about accommodating diabetic campers, would need to have the appropriate medical staff in place in order to enroll.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Grounds are beautiful with LOTS of open space (fields). Rec hall houses skit nights, talent shows, commercial nights—EPs are always some kind of performance. Sunday Campfire (Not religious).

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Tennis pro on staff—offers private, semi-private, and group lessons for an extra fee—$70/hour for private lesson. Golf clinics from Chicago golf pro (cost not specified). Campers can go to the local golf course on Sundays (at least 2 4somes each Sunday).

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

For a sports oriented, well-rounded boy. Must like to play sports\!\! Does not need to be super competitive. All campers choose their sports (everyone makes a team).

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Goal setting is big deal at camp—progress is tracked all summer and a profile is given at the end of the summer.
  - Opportunities to earn Gold, Silver, Bronze or Master; campers accumulate pins to take home.
  - Located on a quiet chain of lakes with a big ski and fishing program and a nice, sand bottom swimming area.
  - 6 choice instructional periods in the AM (2 week rotations) plus Games/Leagues in PM.
  - Special programs: King of the Camp, Green vs White color war, big competition with Camp Kawaga, Sunday Campfire.
  - 9 hole golf course on camp grounds with a 400 yd driving range; golf clinics from a Chicago golf pro.
  - Tennis pro on staff; private, semi-private, and group lessons.
  - Grounds are beautiful with LOTS of open space (fields).

  

#### Lowlights

  - Unsure about accommodating diabetic campers—would need appropriate medical staff in place.
  - Central shower houses (toilets and sinks in cabins, showers not in-cabin).
  - Travel logistics, parent communication, and tech policy are Unknown.
  - (Coming soon) turf workout facility—may not be available yet.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Camp Kawaga
  - Camp Horseshoe
  - Camp Ojibwa (for a very sports oriented, competitive boy)
  - Camp Timberlane (for a not as sporty, well-rounded boy)

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Menominee for Boys Registration opens August 1 st , 2022 Eagle River, WI Director: Jason Feldgreber 150 campers 1 st session 170 campers 2 nd session (190 is max) 1/3 are 7 week (full summer) There is a 2 week rookie program for younger campers 50% Chicago Other areas: Scottsdale, Denver, LA/SF Located on a quiet chain of lakes, they are the most action on their lake -big ski and fishing program -nice, sand bottom swimming area Goal setting is big deal at camp -progress is tracked all summer and a profile is given at the end of the summer -Have the opportunity to win Gold, Silver, Bronze or Master in activities -campers accumulate pins that they can take home Smaller cabins/higher ratios -NO bunk beds, single cots only -toilets and sinks in cabins, central shower houses -counselors sleep in same area as campers Grounds are beautiful with LOTS of open space (fields) All campers choose their sports (everyone makes a team) -6 choice instructional periods in the AM (2 week rotations) -general periods with unstructured activities in AM also -Games/Leagues in PM Sand volleyball, (coming soon) turf workout facility with cardio equipment, indoor bball and pickle ball Rec hall houses skit nights, talent shows, commercial nights—EPs are always some kind of performance Special Programming: -King of the Camp (1 st Session)—teams last for your entire career at camp -includes competitions for points (sports and other activities) -can choose which activities they want to participate in for King of Camp -Leagues/Competitions -big competition in 2 nd session with Camp Kawaga -Green vs White color war (teams change every year) -Sunday Campfire (Not religious) Have a big mess hall and also eat outside -buffet style meals with salad bar at lunch and dinner -can accommodate specialty diets 2 RNs full time on camp -Unsure about accommodating diabetic campers, would need to have the appropriate medical staff in place in order to enroll 9 hole golf course on camp grounds with a 400 yd driving range -campers can go to the local golf course on Sundays (at least 2 4somes each Sunday) -offer golf clinics from Chicago golf pro Tennis pro on staff -offers private, semi-private, and group lessons for an extra fee -$70/hour for private lesson \*For a sports oriented, well-rounded boy. Must like to play sports\!\! Does not need to be super competitive. Give with: Camp Kawaga, Camp Horseshoe, Camp Ojibwa (for a very sports oriented, competitive boy), Camp Timberlane (for a not as sporty, well-rounded boy)

title: Lake Owego — Michele — 7-5-24
text:
# Lake Owego

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Freedo said he built it up from 160 boys now around 300 since he came over (from camp Winaukee). Most important to them: sports, arts, outdoor adventure & fun. 1st session: 3 1/2 weeks; many stay full summer (7 weeks). Sports: Intercamp games against Wayne county camps and Pine Forrest (so close by). Olympics during 1st session, color war during 2nd.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lake Owego camp Write up by Michele G. Visited by Michelle B, Emily R, Heather P. 7/2/2024. Toured with Jeff “Freedo” Freedman. Stopped to talk to many of the boys and was very impressed, they’re very sweet, good manners, down to earth and sporty with no attitude, they really love this camp\! He said he really wants an inclusive environment and likes to teach the boys to be very nice to each other and to the staff. Freedo said he built it up from 160 boys now around 300 since he came over (from camp Winaukee). He wants diversity in his staff so he introduced us to counselors from Rwanda and people from around the world. Nice soccer & basketball staff that we met. Trying to enforce fun over winning. 30% international staff which is important to him but also likes to get college athletes; 40-50% return rate for staff. He likes to have an 8:2 camper to staff ratio at all times. Leadership: We also met Kyle who is his co-Director (more quiet). His daughter runs the equestrian program, all of Freedo’s kids are college athletes and work there in different sports instruction (he said it’s high level) great for kids who want to learn and be challenged. Population: 85% Jewish.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Camp is “tastefully rustic” in his words. Capital improvements for 2025: The “grove” picnic table area (outdoor eating area under the trees).

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake is small but private and has all the floating toys, soft boats no motorized boats. They also have a nice pool. Unique electives include water polo (the boys love it).

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Outdoor adventure: Ropes course: 20 elements. They do tenting, hiking, cooking out (tonight they’re learning how to use a smoker to bbq and the boys love it). 100 mile canoe trip, Shohola Falls hiking trip etc. also have mountain biking as an elective. Electives: water polo and fencing which the boys love. Culinary arts elective— they go to CIA on field trips. “TOPS” golf is very popular (driving range) elective and “TOPS” baseball which is more like a clinic style/lessons.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports: 6 tennis courts; they have all field sports and some unique electives like water polo and fencing which the boys love. Intercamp games against Wayne county camps and Pine Forrest (so close by). Arts: Boys are doing 2 musicals this summer and they love it (one is Newsies). Arts & crafts and woodworking are popular. Outdoor adventure: Ropes course: 20 elements; tenting, hiking, cooking out; 100 mile canoe trip; Shohola Falls hiking trip; mountain biking as an elective. Olympics during 1st session, color war during 2nd. Trying to enforce fun over winning.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical: 3 nurses on staff and a Dr who rotates between PF, CTT & LO everyday. Food: Great with allergies (similar to PF & CTT); area is clearly labeled in the dining room for “special diets”. He said the dining room gets loud and is super fun/energetic/spirited. A/C in dining room; said they have very good food.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

He said he really wants an inclusive environment and likes to teach the boys to be very nice to each other and to the staff. Stopped to talk to many of the boys and was very impressed, they’re very sweet, good manners, down to earth and sporty with no attitude, they really love this camp\! Trying to enforce fun over winning. Population: 85% Jewish, they do have a very reform Shabbat service on Fri nights and food is kosher style. He said even the non Jewish kids and counselors like to attend services because it’s inclusive more values based. Dining room gets loud and is super fun/energetic/spirited. Camp is “tastefully rustic.”

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Golf is extra $ if they go to woodloch (golf course nearby) and tennis lessons are extra $.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Inclusive, “tastefully rustic” boys’ camp with sports, arts, outdoor adventure & fun at its core. Jeff “Freedo” Freedman emphasizes fun over winning, good manners, and diversity, with 30% international staff and an 8:2 camper to staff ratio. Aquatics include a small but private lake with floating toys and a pool; highlights range from a 20‑element ropes course and 100‑mile canoe trip to two musicals and unique electives like water polo, fencing, culinary arts, and “TOPS” golf/baseball. 85% Jewish with very reform Shabbat and kosher‑style food; strong medical coverage and allergy‑friendly dining with A/C.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - He said he really wants an inclusive environment and likes to teach the boys to be very nice to each other and to the staff.
  - Stopped to talk to many of the boys and was very impressed, they’re very sweet, good manners, down to earth and sporty with no attitude.
  - Freedo said he built it up from 160 boys now around 300 since he came over (from camp Winaukee).
  - 30% international staff; likes to get college athletes; 40-50% return rate for staff.
  - He likes to have an 8:2 camper to staff ratio at all times.
  - Outdoor adventure: Ropes course: 20 elements; tenting, hiking, cooking out (learning how to use a smoker to bbq).
  - 100 mile canoe trip; Shohola Falls hiking trip; mountain biking as an elective.
  - Lake is small but private with floating toys; soft boats, no motorized boats; plus a nice pool.
  - Sports: 6 tennis courts; all field sports; unique electives like water polo and fencing; intercamp games against Wayne county camps and Pine Forrest.
  - Boys are doing 2 musicals this summer (one is Newsies); arts & crafts and woodworking are popular.
  - Culinary arts elective with CIA field trips.
  - A/C in dining room; great with allergies; clearly labeled “special diets” area.
  - Olympics during 1st session; color war during 2nd.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Lake is small.
  - Dining room gets loud.
  - Golf is extra $ if they go to woodloch (golf course nearby) and tennis lessons are extra $.
  - Camp is “tastefully rustic.”

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Wayne for boys
  - Skylemar
  - Cedar
  - Winaukee

title: Merriwood — Dara — 9-14-24
text:
# Merriwood

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Seven weeks or 1st Session - 4 weeks, 2nd Session - 3 weeks - 140 Girls. About half stay full summer. 2 new buildings in 2024 – The Mill (open air rec center/lodge. No stage) and The Gym. Don’t do overnights, not a big trip camp. Offer a day trip to go hiking. Every morning they raise the flag together, have a word of the day. Play TAPS when they lower the flag.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

MERRIWOOD July 25, 2024 Joanne, Wendy & Dara. Toured by: Sue Miller. Campers from 26 states (primarily from Boston to DC) and 5 countries. Natalie Harris – Assistant Director, Carolyn Miles – Head Counselor, Carl Mullen – artist in residence. Susan’s husband runs the waterfront, and her son Steven is onsite. Brothers of Merriwood girls go to camp @ Pemi, Deerwood, Kingswood, Moosilauke.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks - 8-10 campers and 3 counselors. Counselors have their own room within the bunks. Sinks & toilets in bunk, shower house outside.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront - Crew. 2 motor boats – water skiing, tubing, etc. Offer junior lifeguarding. All non-motorized boating – kayak, canoe, sailing, etc. All campers take swim lessons (6 days/week).

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 Periods in a day. Schedule kept all summer. 8-10 years old – schedules made for them, 11 yo & up – pick themselves. All campers take swim lessons (6 days/week) and tennis lessons 4x or 6x/week. 6 all-purpose hard tennis courts. 1 pickleball court (not popular there). Rockwall, archery. Mountain biking, running class. Junior garden – 8-10 year olds plant & manage it. Horseback riding – 5-7 horses (additional $$). Soccer, lax, field hockey. Theatre & art program – do a play 1st session. Intercamps with Walt Whitman, Wicosuta, Robindell.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Two new buildings in 2024 – The Mill (open air rec center/lodge, no stage) and The Gym. Artist in residence: Carl Mullen. Junior garden for 8-10 year olds to plant & manage. Theatre & art program – do a play 1st session. Intercamps with Walt Whitman, Wicosuta, Robindell.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall - Girls sit at assigned tables that change every week. All girls mixed across ages to get to know everyone. Oldest campers serve – family style. Health/medical specifics: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Every morning they raise the flag together, have a word of the day. Play TAPS when they lower the flag.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation to/from camp - Bus from NYC/CT at start/end of 7 weeks. Will pick up at Boston Logan the day before camp starts. 3-week campers come the day after visiting day. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Horseback riding (additional $$).

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Seven weeks or 1st Session - 4 weeks, 2nd Session - 3 weeks - 140 Girls. About half stay full summer. Don’t do overnights, not a big trip camp; offer a day trip to go hiking. Campers from 26 states (primarily from Boston to DC) and 5 countries.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 2 new buildings in 2024 – The Mill (open air rec center/lodge. No stage) and The Gym.
  - All campers take swim lessons (6 days/week) and tennis lessons 4x or 6x/week.
  - Artist in residence: Carl Mullen.
  - Intercamps with Walt Whitman, Wicosuta, Robindell.
  - Junior garden – 8-10 year olds plant & manage it.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Don’t do overnights, not a big trip camp.
  - The Mill has no stage.
  - 1 pickleball court (not popular there).
  - Shower house outside.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

MERRIWOOD July 25, 2024 Joanne, Wendy & Dara Toured by: Sue Miller - Seven weeks or 1 st Session - 4 weeks, 2 nd Session - 3 weeks - 140 Girls. About half stay full summer - 2 new buildings in 2024 – The Mill (open air rec center/lodge. No stage) and The Gym - Don’t do overnights, not a big trip camp. Offer a day trip to go hiking - Every morning they raise the flag together, have a word of the day. title: KenMont-KenWood — Jaime — 12-23-22
text:
# KenMont/KenWood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

KenMont KenWood Meeting Jaime Altman, Lisa Borg, Joanne Paltrowitz, Julie Rosenberg Dec. 21, 2022. We met with Brad Lerman, Owner/Director. Brad was a former Camp Laurel counselor. He was also a practicing Lawyer for 12 years. Born and raised in Boca, Brad hopes to grow K-K. There are 7 members of the administrative (Behind the scenes) team of K-K. As of 12/21/22, camp is 70% enrolled. 65%-70% of campers identify as Jewish. K-K is split into 2 sessions. First session is 3.5 weeks, Second session is 2 weeks. In 2023, session 1 will be 24 days. Campers are only allowed to stay for 1 session (similar to Laurel South). Currently, the camp has roughly 350 campers per session, with the 5 year plan being 500 campers per session. The majority of the campers come from the tri-state area, although not as many from Long Island, due to the length of camp. About 40 kids from the South Florida area.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Owner/Director: Brad Lerman. Brad was a former Camp Laurel counselor and a practicing Lawyer for 12 years. Born and raised in Boca, Brad hopes to grow K-K and believes in promoting within, saying that staff retention is better for the camp and the staff. There are 7 members of the administrative (behind the scenes) team. The majority of the campers come from the tri-state area, although not as many from Long Island, due to the length of camp; about 40 kids from the South Florida area; 65%-70% identify as Jewish. The last entry point should be rising 9th graders. BIG BROTHER/BIG SISTER: There is a big brother/big sister at camp (assigned once campers are at camp). Brad and Dani added a camp mom in the summer of 2022; this was a welcome addition and will be returning in 2023. The camp mom is a person that anyone can talk to, get a hug from and is generally just a warm face; counselors and campers benefitted from having a person like this at camp.



## 3 · Living Quarters

K-K is a uniform camp. Campers should have 14 K-K shirts and 1 hoodie. Shorts should be blue, although there is not a short uniform requirement (they do prefer Navy shorts). Girls are required to have a one piece bathing suit. Staff do wear K-K Staff shirts. Bunks are preassigned and Brad meets with each camper prior to camp to learn about the campers. Beds are preassigned, and counselors unpack campers prior to campers arrival at camp. Laundry is done on premises. Sheets and towels are provided for international campers only.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They have an exceptional waterfront and have a pool. Instructional swim is a requirement until 6th grade and is primarily done in the lake. The waterfront is a pride for K-K. This is a semi-private (private homes) lake. They have 4 speedboats (all in working condition) and campers can waterski 4 times per session.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

K-K prides itself on having the well-rounded camper. Electives and required periods vary with each age group: Rising 2nd–5th graders have 4 required and 2 elective; Rising 6th and 7th grade have 3 and 3; 8–10th graders have 2 required and 4 elective. K-K encourages campers to try new activities. K-K does have out of camp trips, which include ice cream and mini golf. Rising 9th graders: CO-ED rafting trip (day trip). Rising 10th graders: CO-ED overnight trip to Boston. Rising 8th graders will have CO-ED evening activities. K-K will participate with intercamp competition, as long as the camp is within 90 minutes of K-K.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Brad was very excited to discuss the capital improvements to the facilities. This includes paint on the exterior of all bunks, resurfacing all courts, a new fieldhouse (including fieldhouse flooring), new ropes course. These are the immediate capital improvements. There are 50 activities to choose from; this year, they will be adding in digital photography. They have a 9 hole golf course. 3 holes are used for golf. They have soccer golf (think soccer ball sized hole). They also have a wonderful skatepark. They will be adding an on-site campground for overnights (behind the ropes course). THEY DO NOT HAVE ICE HOCKEY. Horseback riding is an additional expense. Horses are at the Kent School (a 15 minute drive). They will take 10 kids per session. Campers will go after lunch and ride for 1 hour. This is considered an elective. COLOR WAR: There is color war at camp. 2nd session (2022) color war was broken with skydivers.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Bugs are not an issue for the camp. They spray 3 times for mosquitos and ticks. Campers have both lice and tick check. Summer 2022, 1 camper had a tick and they were immediately evaluated by the health center with no circumstances. After the 2022 season, K-K evaluated their survey results, and decided to change their food service. Will be using Sodexo. Sodexo is a national company, who provides food service to educational institutions. It is similar to a "corporate catering". They provide quality food service. (SIDENOTE: Jaime has eaten Sodexo and has been impressed with both the quality and variety of the food). Laurel South also uses Sodexo and has had much success. COVID POLICY (2023): Brad will be following the state of Connecticut protocol, and the recommendations of his medical boards. Campers SHOULD BE vaccinated. He will make MEDICAL EXEMPTIONS ONLY. But that does require paperwork for parents to submit to the state. GENDER IDENTITY: Brad believes that as a true brother/sister camp, it might be healthy to place opposite sex into opposite sex cabins, though he is open to discussing with families on a need basis.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

There is an optional Friday Evening service with Challah, Juice and candles. K-K prides itself on having the well-rounded camper and encourages campers to try new activities. COLOR WAR: There is color war at camp. BIG BROTHER/BIG SISTER: This is assigned once campers are at camp. Brad and Dani added a camp mom in the summer of 2022; this was a welcome addition and will be returning in 2023. The camp mom is a person that anyone can talk to, get a hug from and is generally just a warm face. Brad believes in promoting within, saying that staff retention is better for the camp and the staff. Brad values the Camp Experts and looks forward to continuing our partnership to KenMont KenWood. He does seem like he is here for the long haul and to restore the camp to its glory days, while keeping up with the evolution of camps.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Camp does have 1 charter flight from PBI. There are bus pickups from Paramus, NYC (86th/York) and White Plains. It is easiest to fly into JFK to arrive at Camp. TECHNOLOGY POLICY: MP3 players are allowed. MP3 players may not have a screen. Cell Phones are taken to the office, shut off and placed in the safe. Michelle will take all phone out of the safe the day BEFORE camp ends, and charge each device. Those campers that go to Boston are given their cell phones for the Boston trip, as a safety precaution. SCHOLARSHIPS: Brad will continue to honor the scholarship tradition, as a partial or need based scholarship. Extra costs: Horseback riding is an additional expense. Parent communication: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

KenMont/KenWood is a true brother/sister camp split into two sessions (in 2023, session 1 will be 24 days and session 2 is 2 weeks) with a one-session policy. It prides itself on having the well-rounded camper, offering 50 activities and an exceptional waterfront with required instructional swim until 6th grade, plus signature trips and Color War. Facilities are receiving immediate capital improvements (painted bunks, resurfaced courts, a new fieldhouse, new ropes course) and the food service is moving to Sodexo. Travel is straightforward with bus pickups, a PBI charter, and JFK as the easiest airport; tech is limited to no-screen MP3s and phones kept in the safe.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Exceptional waterfront with a semi-private lake, pool, required instructional swim until 6th grade, and waterski 4 times per session
  - 50 activities, adding digital photography; 9-hole golf (3 holes), soccer golf, and a wonderful skatepark
  - Immediate capital improvements: new fieldhouse (with flooring), resurfaced courts, painted bunks, new ropes course
  - Out-of-camp trips by age (rafting day trip for 9th; Boston overnight for 10th; co-ed evenings for 8th)
  - Big brother/big sister and a returning camp mom for added support
  - Sodexo food service (noted quality and variety; also used by Laurel South)
  - Charter flight from PBI; bus pickups from Paramus, NYC (86th/York), White Plains



#### Lowlights

  - Campers are only allowed to stay for 1 session
  - Uniform camp (14 K-K shirts and 1 hoodie; blue/navy shorts preferred; one-piece required for girls)
  - Horseback riding is an additional expense with limited spots (10 campers per session)
  - THEY DO NOT HAVE ICE HOCKEY
  - Tech-restrictive policy (no-screen MP3s; cell phones held in a safe)
  - Intercamp competition only within 90 minutes
  - Last entry point is rising 9th graders



#### Similar Camps

  - Laurel South



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

KenMont KenWood Meeting Jaime Altman, Lisa Borg, Joanne Paltrowitz, Julie Rosenberg Dec. 21, 2022 We met with Brad Lerman, Owner/Director. Brad was a former Camp Laurel counselor. He was also a practicing Lawyer for 12 years. Born and raised in Boca, Brad hopes to grow K-K. There are 7 memebers of the administrative (Behind the scenes) team of K-K. As of 12/21/22- Camp is 70% enrolled. 65%-70% of campers identify as Jewish. K-K is split into 2 sessions. First session is 3.5 weeks, Second session is 2 weeks. In 2023, session 1 will be 24 days. Campers are only allowed to stay for 1 session. (similar to Laurel South). The 2 week session will not be age restricted and campers will always be allowed to go for 2 weeks. Currently, the camp has roughly 350 campers per session, with the 5 year plan being 500 campers per session. The majority of the campers come from the tri-state area, although not as many from Long Island, due to the length of camp. About 40 kids from the South Florida area. Camp does have 1 charter flight from PBI. There are bus pickup s from Paramus, NYC (86 th /York) and White Plains. It is easiest to fly into JFK to arrive at Camp. The last entry point should be rising 9 th graders. K-K is a uniform camp. Campers should have 14 K-K shirts and 1 hoodie. Shorts should be blue, although there is not a short uniform requirement, although they do prefer Navy shorts. Girls are required to have a one piece bathing suit. Staff do wear K-K Staff shirts. Bunks are preassigned and Brad meets with each camper prior to camp to learn about the campers. Beds are preassigned, and counselors unpack campers prior to campers arrival at camp. Laundry is done on premises. Sheets and towels are provided FOR INTERNATIONAL CAMPERS ONLY Bugs are not an issue for the camp. They spray 3 times for mosquitos and ticks. Campers have both lice and tick check. Summer 2022, 1 camper had a tick and they were immediately evaluated by the health center with no circumstances. K-K prides itself on having the well-rounded camper. Electives and required periods vary with each age group. Rising 2 nd -5 th graders have 4 required and 2 elective, Rising 6 th and 7 th grade have 3 and 3; 8- 10 th graders have 2 required and 4 elective. K-K does have out of camp trips, which include ice cream and mini golf. Rising 9 th graders: CO-ED rafting trip. This is a day trip. Rising 10 th graders: CO-ED overnight trip to Boston. Rising 8 th graders will have CO-ED evening activities. K-K will participate with intercamp competition, as long as the camp is within 90 minutes of K-K. Brad was very excited to discuss the capital improvements to the facilities. This includes paint on the exterior of all bunks. Resurfacing all courts, a new fieldhouse (including fieldhouse flooring), new ropes course. These are the immediate capital improvements. K-K encourages campers to try new activities. There are 50 activities to choose from. This year, they will be adding in digital photography. They have a 9 hole golf course. 3 holes are used for golf. They have soccer golf (think soccer ball sized hole). They also have a wonderful skatepark. They will be adding an on-site campground for overnights. This will be behind the ropes course. They have an exceptional waterfront and have a pool. Instructional swim is a requirement until 6 th grade and is primarily done in the lake. The waterfront is a pride for K-K. This is a semi-private (private homes) lake. They have 4 speedboats (all in working condition) and campers can waterski 4 times per session. THEY DO NOT HAVE ICE HOCKEY Horseback riding is an additional expense. Horses are at the Kent School (a 15 minute drive) . They will take 10 kids per session. Campers will go after lunch and ride for 1 hour. This is considered an elective. After the 2022 season, K-K evaluated their survey results, and decided to change their food service. Will be using Sodexo. Sodexo is a national company, who provides food service to educational institutions. It is similar to a “corporate catering” They provide quality food service. (SIDENOTE: Jaime has eaten Sodexo and has been impressed with both the quality and variety of the food) Laurel South also uses Sodexo and has had much success. There is an optional Friday Evening service with Challah, Juice and candles. COLOR WAR: There is color war at camp. 2 nd session (2022) color war was broken with skydivers. TECHNOLOGY POLICY: MP3 players are allowed. MP3 players may not have a screen. Cell Phones are taken to the office, shut off and placed in the safe. Michelle will take all phone out of the safe the day BEFORE camp ends, and charge each device. Those campers that go to Boston are given their cell phones for the Boston trip, as a safety precaution. COVID POLICY (2023) Brad will be following the state of Connecticut protocol, and the recommendations of his medical boards. Campers SHOULD BE vaccinated. He will make MEDICAL EXEMPTIONS ONLY. But that does require paperwork for parents to submit to the state. SCHOLARSHIPS Brad will continue to honor the scholarship tradition, as a partial or need based scholarship. GENDER IDENTITY Brad believes that as a true brother/sister camp, it might be healthy to opposite sex into opposite sex cabins. Though he is open to discussing with families on a need basis. BIG BROTHER/BIG SISTER There is a big brother/big sister at camp. This is assigned once campers are at camp. Brad and Dani added a “camp mom” in the summer of 2022. This was a welcome addition and will be returning in 2023. The “camp mom” is a person that ANYONE can talk to, get a hug from and is generally just a warm face. Counselors and campers benefitted from having a person like this at camp. Brad believes in promoting within, saying that staff retention is better for the camp and the staff. Brad values the Camp Experts and looks forward to continuing our partnership to KenMont KenWood. He does seem like he is here for the long haul and to restore the camp to it’s glory days, while keeping up with the evolution of camps.




### Author: Jaime

### Updated: 2025-08-26

title: Laurel — Amanda — 7-7-22
text:
# Laurel



## 1 · Program Snapshot

7 weeks, co-ed, 515 campers—youngest camper going into 2nd grade and oldest are going into 10th grade. Programming: Structure with choice.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

I toured with Emily S., Wendy, Eliza and Joanne. We were toured by Jem (owner/director). “Controlled Days Off” for counselors—rent out a barn with live music, food, etc.



## 3 · Living Quarters

6 different campuses—each campus has its own ping-pong table, gaga & basketball area for chill time.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Instructional swim. One piece bathing suits. “Controlled trips” for campers this summer—beach and Splash Town.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 periods a day (50 mins each), A, B & S days. Finishing 4th grade—must do 2 team sports, general fitness, tennis, instructional swim and discovery/OA. Other periods are electives. Single sex activities except for arts & crafts.



## 6 · Program Highlights

2 musicals—Into the Woods & Press Start. Impressive rock studio and radio station/broadcasting, huge culinary facility, huge field house, great fitness center. Socials for 7th–9th graders (Wings and Things). Activity-based socials for 5th–6th graders. No socials for 2nd–4th graders. “Controlled trips” for campers—beach, ice cream, Fun Town, Splash Town & socials.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining—2 seatings, boys side & girls side, sit by bunk, AC. 2 Drs, 15 nurses, health center manager.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Every night they gather and talk about accomplishments. Overall impression—camp was beautiful, manicured, very large campus, well-rounded campers, high level of instruction.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Laurel is a 7-week, co-ed program with 515 campers and a structured-with-choice approach. Campers range from going into 2nd grade through going into 10th grade. The campus is beautiful, manicured, and very large, with impressive facilities and high-level instruction. Daily life balances required instruction with electives, age-appropriate socials, and community gatherings each night.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Beautiful, manicured, very large campus.
  - Well-rounded campers; high level of instruction.
  - Impressive rock studio and radio station/broadcasting.
  - Huge culinary facility, huge field house, great fitness center.
  - 2 musicals—Into the Woods & Press Start.
  - 6 different campuses—each with ping-pong, gaga & basketball for chill time.
  - Strong health center staffing (2 Drs, 15 nurses, health center manager).
  - Nightly gathering to talk about accomplishments.
  - Socials for 7th–9th graders (Wings and Things) and activity-based socials for 5th–6th graders.
  - “Controlled trips” (beach, ice cream, Fun Town, Splash Town) and “Controlled Days Off” for counselors.



#### Lowlights

  - Single sex activities except for arts & crafts.
  - No socials for 2nd–4th graders.
  - One piece bathing suits.



#### Similar Camps

  - Wekeela
  - Laurel South
  - Raquette Lake
  - Vega
  - Matoaka
  - Mataponi
  - Takajo
  - Manitou



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Laurel- 7/7/22- I toured with Emily S., Wendy, Eliza and Joanne. We were toured by Jem (owner/director). - 7 weeks, co-ed, 515 campers- youngest camper going into 2 nd grade and oldest are going into 10 th grade. - Programming: Structure with choice. 5 periods a day (50 mins each), A B & S days. Finishing 4 th grade- must do 2 team sports, general fitness, tennis, instructional swim and discovery/OA. Other periods are electives - Single sex activities except for arts & crafts - 6 different campuses- each campus has its own ping-pong table, gaga & basketball area for chill time - Socials for 7 th -9 th graders (Wings and Things) Activity based socials for 5 th -6 th graders No socials for 2 nd -4 th graders - “Controlled trips” for campers this summer- beach, ice cream, Fun Town, Splash Town & socials - “Controlled Days Off” for counselors- rent out a barn with live music, food, etc. - Dining- 2 seatings, boys side & girls side, sit by bunk, AC - 2 Drs, 15 nurses, health center manager - 2 musicals- Into the Woods & Press Start - Impressive rock studio and radio station/broadcasting, huge culinary facility, huge field house, great fitness center - Every night they gather and talk about accomplishments - One piece bathing suits Overall impression- camp was beautiful, manicured, very large campus, well-rounded campers, high level of instruction. You could give with Wekeela, Laurel South, Raquette Lake, Vega, Matoaka, Mataponi, Takajo & Manitou \*For additional basic info reference the camp profile in the database

title: Miniwanca — Ashley — 2-13-24
text:
# Miniwanca

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

AYF: American Youth Foundation Camps (presented February 13, 2024 by Kris Light, klight@ayf.com). Describes themselves as “Youth Empowerment Program”, values driven. Focus on the ‘why’ to help children develop their best selves. Explore living a balanced life (mental, physical, social, “R-fold”). Make a positive difference. 3 camps serve rising 3rd–12th grade; progression based; some wilderness trip programming; 1–5 weeks in length; offer transportation to/from airport for campers.  
Camp Miniwanca: Two separate single gender camps located in Michigan (250 boys/350 girls). Located on the shores of Lake Michigan (mile of private shoreline). Waterfront activities on Stony Lake. Hiking/out camping experiences located on property and off-property. Mostly St. Louis/Chicago (ORD closest). Provide bus from St. Louis. Parents are encouraged to pick up and drop off campers.  
Context (other AYF site): Camp Merrowvista located in New Hampshire (approx. 350 campers during the summer), 600 forested acres, single access road to camp. Waterfront activities are on Dan Hole Pond—Pristine and clear water, semi-private waterfront. Hiking/out camping experiences located on property. Mostly New York/Boston (BOS closest). Parents are encouraged to pick up and drop off campers.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Serve rising 3rd–12th grade; progression based. Camp Miniwanca runs two separate single gender camps (250 boys/350 girls). Campers come alone and with some friends; work hard to make sure everyone feels included.  
Staff: highly vetted, high retention rate, up to 4 weeks of staff training. Trip leaders 21+. Cabin leader has to have one year of college under their belt or equivalent.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bathroom/shower houses. Individual shower stalls. Electricity in bathrooms and youngest cabin, not in cabins. Encouraged NOT to use electricity.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Miniwanca is located on the shores of Lake Michigan (mile of private shoreline). Waterfront activities on Stony Lake.  
Merrowvista (context): waterfront activities are on Dan Hole Pond—Pristine and clear water, semi-private waterfront.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

\* Interest groups: 3 periods of the day, campers get to choose (aquatics, arts and crafts, performing arts, athletics, outdoor adventure). Strong focus on “trying new things”. Mixed throughout the camp, interact with kids outside of their cabin group (co-ed activities at Merrowvista).  
\* Village Time: cabin groups do an activity together (team challenges (ropes course), waterfront, service opportunity).  
\* All community activities: things the entire camp is doing together (evening programs, performances/shows, themed meals, etc.). Evening/morning reflection.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Four Trails Program: Wilderness adventure trips outside of camp with focus on challenge progression—backpacking, canoeing, kayaking. As campers get older, these groups are mixed gender. 3–5 week sessions. Hiking/out camping experiences located on property and off-property. Values driven “Youth Empowerment Program” that focuses on the ‘why’, balanced life (mental, physical, social, “R-fold”), and making a positive difference.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Allergies: Can accommodate most allergies—on a case by case basis. Parents are able to provide their own food if needed. Special Needs: Can accommodate most campers with ADD and ADHD. Full-time medical professional on staff.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Not religious camp, but “inclusive of conversations around religion”; engage a lot with conversations around inclusivity, religion, diversity, etc. Campers come alone and with some friends, work hard to make sure everyone feels included.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Offer transportation to/from airport for campers. Mostly St. Louis/Chicago (ORD closest). Provide bus from St. Louis. Parents are encouraged to pick up and drop off campers. NO cell phones for campers. Parent communication: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Miniwanca is part of the American Youth Foundation’s values‑driven “Youth Empowerment Program,” offering progression‑based camps for rising 3rd–12th graders. The camp runs two separate single gender programs on the shores of Lake Michigan with a mile of private shoreline and waterfront activities on Stony Lake. Daily life features three periods of camper‑choice Interest Groups, Village Time with cabin groups, All Community activities, and evening/morning reflection with a strong focus on trying new things. Highly vetted staff (up to 4 weeks of training) support allergy and special needs accommodations, and travel options include airport transportation and a bus from St. Louis (ORD closest); parents are encouraged to pick up and drop off.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Mile of private Lake Michigan shoreline with Stony Lake waterfront.
  - Progression‑based program with Four Trails wilderness trips (3–5 week sessions).
  - Highly vetted staff with up to 4 weeks of training; trip leaders 21+.
  - Strong values focus on the ‘why’, balanced life (“R‑fold”), and trying new things.
  - Airport transportation available; bus from St. Louis.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Electricity in bathrooms and youngest cabin, not in cabins; encouraged not to use electricity.
  - NO cell phones for campers.
  - Single gender programs (not co‑ed at Miniwanca).
  - Parent communication specifics and extra costs are Unknown.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Merrowvista
  - Other AYF (American Youth Foundation) camps

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

AYF: American Youth Foundation Camps February 13, 2024 Kris Light presented on behalf of AYF klight@ayf.com Describes themselves as “Youth Empowerment Program”, values driven -Focus on the ‘why’ to help children develop their best selves -Explore living a balanced life (mental, physical, social, “R-fold”) -make a positive difference 3 camps -serve rising 3 rd -12 th grade -progression based -some wilderness trip programming -1-5 weeks in length -offer transportation to/from airport for campers Camp Miniwanca (Two separate single gender camps) located in Michigan (250 boys/350 girls) -Located on the shores of Lake Michigan (mile of private shoreline) -Waterfront activities on Stony Lake -hiking/out camping experiences located on property and off-property -mostly st. Louis/Chicago (ORD closest) -provide bus from St. Louis -Parents are encouraged to pick up and drop off campers Camp Merrowvista located in New Hampshire (approx. 350 campers during the summer) -600 forested acres -Single access road to camp -waterfront activities are on Dan Hole Pond—Pristine and clear water, semi-private waterfront -hiking/out camping experiences located on property -mostly new york/boston (BOS closest) -Parents are encouraged to pick up and drop off campers Daily Activities \*Interest groups: 3 periods of the day, campers get to choose -aquatics, arts and crafts, performing arts, athletics, outdoor adventure -strong focus on “trying new things” -mixed throughout the camp, interact with kids outside of their cabin group (co-ed activities at Merrowvista) \* Village Time: cabin groups do an activity together -team challenges (ropes course) -waterfront -service opportunity \*All community activities: things the entire camp is doing together -evening programs, performances/shows, themed meals, etc. -evening/morning reflection Four Trails Program -Wilderness adventure trips outside of camp -focus on challenge progression -backpacking, canoeing, kayaking -As campers get older, these groups are mixed gender -3-5 week sessions Staff: highly vetted, high retention rate, up to 4 weeks of staff training, Trip leaders 21+, Cabin leader has to have one year of college under their belt or equivalent. Allergies: Can accommodate most allergies—on a case by case basis. Parents are able to provide their own food if needed. Special Needs: Can accommodate most campers with ADD and ADHD. \*Not religious camp, but “inclusive of conversations around religion”, engage a lot with conversations around inclusivity, religion, diversity, etc. Full-time medical professional on staff NO cell phones for campers Campers come alone and with some friends, work hard to make sure everyone feels included. Bathroom/shower houses. Individual shower stalls. Electricity in bathrooms and youngest cabin, not in cabins. Encouraged NOT to use electricity.

title: Merrowvista — Shari — 9-1-22
text:
# Merrowvista

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Part of AYF (American Youth Foundation). There were no campers when we visited; they were arriving the following day. Offer 2 (22 campers), 3 (50 campers 1st session, 120 2nd session) 7 wks (couple of campers but go home in-between sessions).

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Shari, Lisa B, Beth & Jennifer toured by Jamie O’Hagin – program director (her 1st summer year-round w/ Merrowvista but here 20 yrs part-time, husband is caretaker, they live on-site). Ages: into 3rd (8 y/o) to 18 (LIT). Campers mostly from New England & NY/NJ area but also others as well & int’l’s. Counselors 21+.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

ACCOMODATIONS – shared double cabin, 6-7 campers per side w/ 1-2 staff plus 1 in the middle. Adjoining w/ porch in-between, high ceiling, very nice. Built in wood bunk beds around perimeter of rooms. Hard trunks used. Electricity in younger cabins, older cabins no electricity (14+). BIF (Bathroom In the Friendly Forest) very bright, new clean. Adding gender neutral option. Laundry done on site, 2 wkrs = 1-2 times, 3 wkrs = 2 times, by group, sort at cabin. On Duty inside bunk for younger campers, outside present in village for older campers.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

AQUATICS – about a 7 minute walk downhill on path, beautiful, woodsy and waterfront is a very popular spot. Semi-private, non-motorized w/ sailing (2 periods), kayaking, canoeing, SUP. Lessons optional, parents decide. Wear functional swimwear that’s comfortable and appropriate attire for activities they’re doing. Water volleyball, distance swims to strawberry point.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING – 3p/day, 2am, 1pm, 1 hr each. Scheduled by interest groups, all choice on 2-3 day rotation. Outdoor adventure focus w/ other things woven in: water sports, performing arts, arts & crafts. PM = “Village Time”: cabin bonding time scheduled. “Your Time” = read, shower, relax, basketball. “Nights Doings” = after dinner talent show, capture-the-flag, etc.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

SPORTS – soccer, basketball, lacrosse (sometimes), tetherball, wiffleball, kickball, 4-square, volleyball, gaga, sometimes dance depending on staff they have. Separate activity center w/ indoor climbing & ropes course. High ceiling, clean, doors slide open and chimney is the climbing wall w/ rubber padded floor. Outside is a big amphitheater w/ layered boulders to sit on to view shows. The barn doors open to the padded floor area where performances can occur. Very cool, innovative well-utilized space. Low ropes course outdoors. Archery. CREATIVE – The “BONN” (Barn) & New Hampshire Lodge – open empty building, where they do improv, arguing for fun, games, etc. Add’l building The “New Jersey Lodge” w/ porch w/ arts and crafts: Shrinky dinks, tie dye, water color, journal making, stained glass, mod podge, pillow making, candle making, knitting, sculpting, beadwork, friendship bracelets. TRIPS – Overnights on and off-site. Sign up before camp and they’re building that program back up (was quiet last few summers for obvious reasons). Older campers backpack. One night overnight for 2wk campers. 14 y/o – 5 day, 4 night. 15 y/o – 6 night 7 day. 16 y/o – wayfinder service learning/community service. 17 y/o – 12 nights on Appalachian Trail. They get a special ceremony, fleece vest and a different color each year.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL/EMOTIONAL – No dr on site, 2 nurses. Use Huggins hospital 20 mins away. No urgent care nearby. Covid vaccination was required and they’re very careful w/ testing and masking for arrival next day. DINING – called “eating lodge” bright, new, high ceiling, wood floor, fireplace, clean. Sit by cabin, square table, folding chairs, family style, salad bar, fruit, sun butter always available but they are nut aware. They don’t serve nuts but cannot guarantee. Can handle all allergies. Dessert w/ dinner.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

PHILOSOPHY – Premise of camp is to balance themselves Spiritually, Mentally, Physically and Religiously. They should Aspire Nobly, Adventure Daringly and Serve Humbly. Inspiring kids to be the best. RELIGIOUS LIFE – interfaith, moment of gratitude before meal, reflective. At flag raising cabins take turns running. Chapel on camp is used 1-2x/wk for evening reflection, lead by older kids.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Pick-ups done at Logan and Manchester, extra cost. Parents mostly drive. No phone calls home except int’ls. One way email, encourage letter writing, post photos 2x/wk. Tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Merrowvista is a cool, throwback type camp. Diversity and acceptance seem like the priority. We did not see campers but Jamie did a great job describing the vibe. We would feel comfortable sending a camper who is not sports intense but more about community, outdoor adventure and nature.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Outdoor adventure focus w/ other things woven in
  - Cool, throwback type camp
  - Diversity and acceptance seem like the priority
  - Eating lodge bright, new, clean
  - Very cool, innovative well-utilized space (activity center)
  - Waterfront is a very popular spot
  - Interfaith, reflective
  - Counselors 21+

  

#### Lowlights

  - No dr on site, 2 nurses
  - No urgent care nearby
  - Older cabins no electricity (14+)
  - No phone calls home except int’ls
  - We did not see campers

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Built in wood bunk beds around perimeter of rooms. Hard trunks used. Electricity in younger cabins, older cabins no electricity (14+). BIF (Bathroom In the Friendly Forest) very bright, new clean. Adding gender neutral option. Laundry done on site, 2 wkrs=1-2 times, 3wkrs=2 times, by group, sort at cabin. On Duty inside bunk for younger campers, outside present in village for older campers. SPORTS- soccer, basketball, lacrosse (sometimes), tetherball, wiffleball, kickball, 4-square, volleyball, gaga, sometimes dance depending on staff they have. Separate activity center w/ indoor climbing & ropes course. High ceiling, clean, doors slide open and chimney is the climbing wall w/ rubber padded floor. Outside is a big amphitheater w/ layered boulders to sit on to view shows. The barn doors open to the padded floor area where performances can occur. Very cool, innovative well-utilized space. Low ropes course outdoors. Archery. CREATIVE- The “BONN” (Barn) & New Hampshire Lodge – open empty building, where they do improv, arguing for fun, games, etc. Add’l building The ‘New Jersey Lodge’ w/ porch w/ arts and crafts: Shrinky dinks, tie dye, water color, journal making, stained glass, mod podge, pillow making, candle making, knitting, sculpting, beadwork, friendship bracelets. AQUATICS – about a 7 minute walk downhill on path, beautiful, woodsy and waterfront is very popular spot. Semi-private, non-motorized w/ sailing (2 periods), kayaking, canoeing, SUP. Lessons optional, parents decide. Wear functional swimwear that’s comfortable and appropriate attire for activities they’re doing. Water volleyball, distance swims to strawberry point. TRIPS – Overnights on and off-site. Sign up before camp and they’re building that program back up (was quiet last few summers for obvious reasons). Older campers backpack. One night overnight for 2wk campers. 14y/o-5 day, 4 night. 15y/o 6 night 7 day. 16y/o wayfinder service learning/ community service. 17y/o 12 nights on Appalachian Trail. They get a special ceremony, fleece vest and a different color each year. Overall Impressions: Merrowvista is a cool, throwback type camp where diversity and acceptance seem like the priority. We did not see campers but Jamie did a great job describing the vibe and we would feel comfortable sending a camper who is not sports intense but more about community, outdoor adventure and nature.

  
  

### Author: Shari

### Updated: 2025-08-26

title: North Country Camps — Shari — 9-20-24
text:
# North Country Camps

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

North Country Camps Lincoln & Whippoorwill are very rustic (shady, lots of tree stumps, pine needles, buggy) and are a non-profit (foundation). Offer 3, 5 and 6 wks; about 60% do the full summer at "L," more at "W." 1st session had 84 campers at L, 105 @ W (capacity). Ages 8-15 (finish 9th grade), no LIT/CIT program. Friday afternoons are mixed activities w/ W for all ages and socials ages 13-15. The oldest group is a work group and they build a legacy project together for the camp (one group built a building on camp). Dogs roam freely, the campers seem to love them.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We toured Lincoln (the boys side, referred to as "L") rather quickly in anticipation of major storms and Whippoorwill (the girls’ side, "W") was much abbreviated. The boys mess hall was spirited, boys seemed very polite and well-mannered. Their PM activity choices occurred, the director was funny, gave clear instructions on thunder/lightening protocols for impending storms and we got a very warm community feeling. Campers from tri-state, VT, Boston, CT, FL, PA etc. 85% retention rate. About 35 staff at both L & W are former campers; about 1/3 are int’l. Older campers are welcomed at most ages, very accepting community.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations are rustic, live out of trunks, neat. 4-6 campers w/ 1 staff member, no electricity, no facilities inside bunk. Wash house nearby. Shower house open top w/ shower stalls and curtains, cleaned professionally. On duty counselor outside bunk at bedtime roving.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Two daily swim periods on a large, beautiful lake with sailboats, kayaks, windsurfing and slide; no motorized water sports. No bathing suit policy for girls. Once test is passed, can do all activities alone, otherwise w/ a counselor.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING – 2 daily choice in am @ L, one of which they will do all week long; 1 @ W, choices made daily. Focus is progressive skill building. At W, 5 weeks are the same way; at L they do 2wk blocks where choices change and they focus on skill building. Riding and ropes are their only co-ed activities.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

SPORTS – Soccer, basketball, lacrosse, baseball, cricket, rugby (low contact), pickleball @ W, frisbee, tennis (clay). HB riding on property, stable management, trail rides, English style, no extra cost. CREATIVE – Themed craft weeks (pottery week, print-making, windchime week, tie dye, etc). Woodworking basic. One play per session, very basic; boys do skits and skit practices. ORIENTEERING/NATURE – Extensive climbing options on camp (high, low, wall) and rock climbing trips. Archery, participate in Adirondack 46ers, day hikes, overnights, biking sometimes, 2-5 nights in length depending on the trip.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – 3 RN’s, consulting Dr in Boston area (virtual) shared by both L & W; pediatrician 20 min away; CVPH is 1 hour away. Self tick checks daily (reminders by staff since lots of trips and woodsy campground); will work w/ family of a diabetic. Telehealth can be arranged if need own Dr/psychologist etc. DINING – At L the boys all stand around their table as whole camp then all sit at once. Rustic, traditional w/ round tables mixed ages assigned weekly, rafters and plaques hung, 1 seating at each meal. Family style with shared responsibilities of waiting and setting tables, sweeping floor, scraping dishes. Fruit available, salad bar, no tree nuts, can accommodate all allergies. Snack at 11:30.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The boys mess hall was spirited, boys seemed very polite and well-mannered. The director was funny, gave clear instructions on thunder/lightening protocols for impending storms and we got a very warm community feeling. RELIGIOUS LIFE – None. No Grace. All faiths welcome\! All about inclusion and respect for diversity. For LGTBQIA population will be flexible w/ living scenarios; they have a lot of non-binary campers. Variety of choices; some may be sporty but like camp for the ‘other stuff.’ Not for a theatre arts camper but most enjoy camp and find their niche.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from Horace Mann school (in the Bronx), Rockland County and Albany. Airports used: Montreal, Albany, Burlington. Visiting day at 5week mark. No extra fees except airport pickup. Scholarships available ($150,000-170,000/ year to provide families in need). COMMUNICATION – 1st 2wks write letters then can arrange calls at meals & parents call in. Emails to directors only and they print out for campers. Packages allowed but no food, mostly necessities. Letter writing required. Weekly blog done; some pictures posted—SmugMug from the week posted sometimes but no guarantee; weekly communications overall. End of summer photo album. Transportation to church NOT available.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

North Country Camps Lincoln & Whippoorwill are very rustic, non-profit camps with a warm community feeling, offering 3, 5 and 6 week sessions for ages 8–15 (finish 9th grade). The program emphasizes progressive skill building with daily choices and extensive trips, plus two daily swim periods on a large, beautiful lake (no motorized water sports). Accommodations are rustic (live out of trunks; no electricity or facilities in bunks; wash house nearby) and dining is family style with allergy accommodations; medical support includes 3 RNs and telehealth if needed. Inclusive and accepting (flexible for LGBTQIA; many non-binary campers), with strong retention and scholarships; not for a theatre arts camper but most enjoy camp and find their niche.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Non-profit (foundation) with a very warm community feeling.
  - Very rustic camp, shady, lots of tree stumps, pine needles; spirited mess hall and polite, well-mannered boys.
  - Progressive skill building with daily choices; 2wk blocks at L.
  - Large, beautiful lake; two daily swim periods; sailing, kayaking, windsurfing and slide.
  - Extensive climbing on camp, rock trips, Adirondack 46ers, day hikes and 2–5 night trips.
  - Riding on property (English), stable management, trail rides; no extra cost.
  - Family-style dining; fruit and salad bar; no tree nuts; can accommodate all allergies.
  - Inclusion and respect for diversity; flexible for LGBTQIA; a lot of non-binary campers.
  - About 35 staff are former campers; about 1/3 are international; 85% retention rate.
  - Scholarships available ($150,000–170,000/year).
  - Dogs roam freely; the campers seem to love them.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Our data somewhat sparse due to storms on tour day.
  - Very rustic and buggy.
  - No LIT/CIT program.
  - Not for a theatre arts camper.
  - No electricity and no facilities inside bunk.
  - Some pictures posted sometimes but no guarantee.
  - 1st 2 weeks write letters; calls only arranged at meals after.
  - No motorized water sports.
  - Transportation to church NOT available.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Adirondack Camp
  - Wyonegonic
  - Mowglis
  - Aloha camps

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Or time at Whippoorwill (the girls’ side, referred to as “W” going forward) (a car ride up the street nextdoor to L) was much abbreviated but, again, we at least got to see it again to share info. The boys mess hall was spirited, boys seemed very polite and well-mannered.
