title: Fernwood — Risa — 7-31-23
text:
# Fernwood

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

7 weeks only, no exceptions. 225 campers. Campers not signing up together. Uniform and bedding uniform; doesn’t have to be logo uniform, just has to be green and white. Uniforms not required at socials.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ellyn Weinstein Black, owner/director, toured us. She lives in Fort Lauderdale; has 2 girls at camp and a son at Skylemar. Bought camp in 2018. Fritz/Christine, previous owners, still around in the office. 20% alumni kids; 40% staff are alumni. 35% tri state. LA/SFO/Miami Palm Beach. Brothers at Androscoggin, Skylemar, Cedar—2x summer each camp but 3x Skylemar/Cedar.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

3 bunks per age group; 10–12 bunk. Sinks/toilets in bunk and 3 shower houses in camp. No electricity in bunks. Towel service in bunk and at the lake.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

2-piece bathing suits allowed. Instructional swim required up to 15 years old. Required swimming and canoeing. Aquatics available within the choice-based program.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

24 credit program to use every day except Wednesday/Sunday. Select every day for the week. Required swimming and canoeing. Choose land sports, aquatics, arts. Mixed ages in activities helps relieve cliques. Counselors have attendance sheet. Can major after 8th grade and have more than 1 of a category. Can switch schedule 1x/week. Free choice once a day before lunch. Wednesday trip day—All outdoor trips: hiking, biking, etc.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Intercamps Mataponi and Pinecliffe. Tripp only does tennis. Lax, volleyball, soccer, field hockey. Everything except softball. Have riflery. Arts and crafts is fine arts: woodworking, fashion design, silversmithing, glass fusion, linoleum printing. Lots of kids reading around camp. 60 girls take horseback riding for extra fee. 3 musicals/summer.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Family style with buffet. Not nut free but nut aware. Can handle allergies: egg, milk, sesame, gluten.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Empower young girls into strong young women is their motto. Lots of kids reading around camp. Mixed ages in activities helps relieve cliques. Every 3 days table changes in the dining hall; Ellyn sits with the program director and makes the changes intentionally. All different ages. Whole camp knows each other. Campfire Sundays. 75% J; no Shabbat. But there is a service on Sundays; 13–15 year olds talk about things going on week. Impressions—Ellyn is down to earth and in it along with the kids. She knew the kids we walked by and they were friendly.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Chaperone flights from Florida/Chicago/Philly/Baltimore; buses from NYC or Westchester. No pictures sent till after camp. Ellyn calls new parents by day 4. Campers get 3 FaceTime phone calls since no photos. Uniform and bedding uniform; doesn’t have to be logo uniform, just has to be green and white. Uniforms not required at socials. 60 girls take horseback riding for extra fee.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

7 weeks only, no exceptions, with 225 campers and strong alumni roots (20% alumni kids; 40% staff are alumni). The 24 credit program runs every day except Wednesday/Sunday with required swimming and canoeing, free choice once a day, and the option to major after 8th grade. The camp leans into empowering young girls into strong young women, with mixed ages in activities and intentional dining table changes so the whole camp knows each other. Parent communication is old-school (no pictures sent till after camp), but Ellyn calls new parents by day 4 and campers get 3 FaceTime phone calls.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Ellyn is down to earth and in it along with the kids.
  - She knew the kids we walked by and they were friendly.
  - 24 credit program with required swim/canoe, free choice once a day, and majors after 8th grade.
  - Mixed ages in activities and intentional dining table changes help relieve cliques and the whole camp knows each other.
  - Strong fine arts (woodworking, fashion design, silversmithing, glass fusion, linoleum printing) plus field sports, riflery, tennis, horseback, and 3 musicals/summer.
  - Intercamps with Mataponi and Pinecliffe; chaperone flights and NYC/Westchester buses.
  - Alumni presence: 20% alumni kids; 40% staff are alumni.

  

#### Lowlights

  - 7 weeks only no exceptions.
  - No electricity in bunks.
  - No pictures sent till after camp.
  - Not nut free.
  - Everything except softball.
  - Campers not signing up together.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Pinecliffe
  - Tapawingo
  - Walden Maine
  - some of the Adirondacks and Berkshires brother/sister camps

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Fernwood Ellyn Weinstein Black/owner director toured us. She lives in Fort Lauderdale. has 2 girls at camp and son at Skylemar. Bought camp in 2018. Fritz/Christine previous owners still around in office. 225 campers. 7 weeks only no exceptions. 20% alumni kids. 40% staff are alumni. 35% tri state. La/sfo/Miami palm beach. Chaperone flights from Florida /Chicago/Philly/Baltimore/buses from nyc or westchester. Campers not signing up together. Uniform and bedding uniform. Doesn’t have to be logo uniform just has to be green and white. Brothers at Androscoggin skylemar cedar 2x summer each camp but 3x skylemar cedar. Uniforms not required at socials. 3 bunks /age group 10-12 bunk. Towel service in bunk and lake. Sinks/toilets in bunk and 3 shower houses in camp. No electricity in bunks. Empower young girls into strong young women is their motto. 2 piece bathing suits allowed. Instructional swim required up to 15 years old No pictures sent till after camp. Ellyn calls new parents by day 4. Campers get 3 FaceTime phone calls since no photos. Intercamps Mataponi and Pinecliffe. Tripp only does tennis. Lax volleyball soccer field hockey. Everything except softball 24 credit program to use everyday except wed/Sunday. Select every day for the week. Required swimming and canoeing. Choose land sports, aquatics, arts. Mixed ages in activities helps relieve cliques. Counselors have attendance sheet. Can major after 8th grade and have more than 1 of a category Can switch schedule 1x week. Free choice once a day before lunch. Have riflery. Wednesday trip day. All outdoor trips. Hiking biking etc Arts and crafts is fine arts. Woodworking/fashion design/silversmithing, glass fusion, linoleum printing. Lots of kids reading around camp. Every 3 days table changes in dining hall. Ellyn sits with program director and makes the changes intentionally. All different ages. Whole camp knows each other. Family style with buffet. Not nut free but but aware. Can handle allergies. Egg milk sesame gluten Campfire Sundays. 75% J no Shabbat. But there is a service on Sundays 13-15 year olds talk about things going on week. 60 girls take horseback riding for extra fee 3 musicals/summer Impressions-Ellyn is down to earth and in it along with the kids. She knew the kids we walked by and they were friendly. I’d give alongside Pinecliffe, Tapawingo, Walden Maine, and some of the Adirondacks and Berkshires brother/sister camps

title: Lake Owego — Shari — 7-28-23
text:
# Lake Owego

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Lake Owego Camp – Shari & Lisa B. toured by Jeff Freedman 29 June 2023. BASIC DATA – 275 boys 1st session, 245 2nd session (they had 160 when Jeff started), 60% do full summer. Campers from mid-Atlantic (biggest), NYC, Westchester, smattering from other states. No uniform. Jeff describes LOC as tastefully rustic, warm and nurturing. He knows every single boy very well (and we visited camp on day 6). A true brotherhood and outstanding community. 15 recipients of scholarships.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers from the mid-Atlantic (biggest), NYC, Westchester, and a smattering from other states. Staff retention rate 50%; previous campers who are staff are hand-picked, very limited. Jeff knows every single boy very well. Older campers welcome, any age; currently had a rising 9th grader from another camp and was doing well.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations are spacious, 8–10 campers with 4 staff; facilities in bunk. Keep bunks same every year; do not mix partial and full summer campers. Counselors help unpack campers upon arrival. OD inside bunk at bedtime.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Rising 2nd–6th swim 3x/wk. Private non-motorized small lake. They take campers to Lake Wallenpaupack to water ski.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 period day. Rising 2nd–6th grade have 2 electives in AM and 2 electives in PM. Travel by bunk for assigned periods, by division for choice.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

SPORTS – The best things they offer are tennis, basketball and soccer, but they do everything (flag football, baseball, batting cage, volleyball, gaga, lacrosse, driving range, roller & street hockey, pump track for biking), and the boys partake in all sports and non-sports activities. Large open-sided terrific facility in addition to fields. No cuts for inter-camp games. CREATIVE – Drawing, painting, pottery, tie dye, woodworking. They write their own plays and perform (Matt Tilda, Newsies); he said this is a big program. Music is big: guitar, drums, keyboard. STEAM/HIGH TECH – Digital photography, rocketry, sports broadcasting. NATURE – Camp craft, salamander hunt, frog hunt (not expansive but fun for some boys/downtime). COOKING – Cooking elective at the smoker, daily. ORIENTEERING/OUTDOOR – 28 elements (high and low combined), zip line, climbing wall, archery, fishing (regular and fly), OLS (map-reading, build fires, hikes). TRIPS – Some hiking trips and mountain biking trip (Jeff takes the boys). TRADITIONS – Olympics at end of 1st session (6 countries different each year, 4 days). Frontier week at end of 2nd session (6 tribes, 4 days, outdoor adventure focused: build house in woods, cook, etc.). PLANNED IMPROVEMENTS – More field space, upgrade some bunks, more ropes elements, would like to add crew.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – 3 RN’s, 2 PT’s, 1 dr. Honesdale Hospital 25 min away; Scranton 40 mins. Can handle diabetic. DINING – Separate allergy chef, sit by bunk, a/c, 1 seating. Campers help clean up. Variety provided daily at each meal. Can accommodate all allergies, nut free. Snacks 3x/day.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Jeff describes LOC as tastefully rustic, warm and nurturing. The many campers and staff we met were remarkable—personable, appreciative, extremely connected to camp & their friends, well-mannered—and said things to us about their experiences that made us teary. A true brotherhood and outstanding community. No uniform. Siblings see each other 2x/wk with Timber Tops via socials or general sibling visits. RELIGIOUS LIFE – 80% Jewish is his guess. Friday night service in Hebrew and English run by campers, blessings at meal on Friday only, traditional Shabbat dinner, then campfire where they give out rewards.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

COMMUNICATION – 1 phone call/session, one-way email, no packages, letter writing required 3x/wk. Post photos daily and Jeff sends his own photos he takes to new families. Jeff is in touch with new camper families 2x/wk. EXTRA COSTS – Baggage, golf (25 min away, 1x/wk, bring own clubs or they can provide), tennis (Superstar Tennis extra 6x/wk), hb riding (about 10 riders usually), trips.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Lake Owego Camp is tastefully rustic, warm and nurturing, with a true brotherhood and outstanding community. The program features a 6-period day with broad sports, creative arts, STEAM, outdoor adventure, plus signature Olympics and Frontier Week traditions. Families who value strong director involvement (Jeff knows every single boy), clear communication, and a nut-free dining program with a separate allergy chef will appreciate the care and structure here. Best for boys who want no-uniform, inclusive inter-camp play with no cuts, and a private non-motorized lake with water skiing at Lake Wallenpaupack.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Tastefully rustic, warm and nurturing; true brotherhood and outstanding community.
  - Director knows every single boy and communicates frequently with new families.
  - Broad program depth: strong tennis, basketball, soccer; creative arts and music are big.
  - Signature traditions: Olympics (1st session) and Frontier Week (2nd session).
  - Private non-motorized lake; water skiing at Lake Wallenpaupack.
  - Nut-free with separate allergy chef; snacks 3x/day; can accommodate all allergies.
  - Large open-sided sports facility; pump track, driving range; no cuts for inter-camp games.
  - Counselors help unpack; bunks keep groups the same year to year.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Staff retention rate 50%; previous camper staff very limited/hand-picked.
  - Nature program noted as "not expansive."
  - Rising 2nd–6th swim 3x/wk (may feel light for heavy swimmers).
  - Extra costs for baggage, golf, Superstar Tennis, hb riding, and trips.
  - Only 1 phone call per session; no packages; letter writing required 3x/wk.
  - Planned improvements still pending: more field space, upgraded bunks, more ropes elements.
  - Siblings see each other only 2x/wk via socials or sibling visits.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Lake Owego Camp – Shari & Lisa B. toured by Jeff Freedman 29 June 2023 BASIC DATA – 275 boys 1 st session, 245 2 nd session (they had 160 when Jeff started), 60% do full summer. Siblings see each other 2x/wk w/ Timber Tops via socials or general sibling visits. Campers from mid-Atlantic (biggest), NYC, Westchester, smattering from other states. Staff retention rate 50%, previous campers who are staff are hand-picked, very limited. No uniform. Jeff describes LOC as tastefully rustic, warm and nurturing. He knows every single boy very well (and we visited camp on day 6). The many campers and staff we met were remarkable. Personable, appreciative, extremely connected to camp & their friends, well-mannered and said things to us about their experiences that made us teary. A true brotherhood and outstanding community. 15 recipients of scholarships. Extra costs are baggage, golf (25 min away, 1x/wk, bring own clubs or they can provide), tennis (superstar tennis extra 6x/wk), hb riding (about 10 riders usually), trips. PROGRAMMING – 6 period day, rising 2 nd -6 th grade 2 electives in am, 2 electives in PM, travel by bunk for assigned periods, by division for choice. Olympics @ end of 1 st session, 6 countries different each year, 4 days. Frontier week @ end of 2 nd session, 6 tribes, 4 days, outdoor adventure focused (build house in woods, cook, etc). MEDICAL – 3 RN’s, 2 PT’s, 1 dr. Honesdale Hospital 25 min away, Scranton 40 mins. Can handle diabetic. COMMUNICATION – 1 phone call/session, one way email, no packages, letter writing required 3x/wk, post photos daily and Jeff sends his own photos he takes to new families. Jeff is in touch with new camper families 2x/wk. RELIGIOUS LIFE – 80% Jewish is his guess, Fri night service in Hebrew and English run by campers, blessings at meal on Friday only, traditional shabbat dinner, then campfire where they give out rewards. DINING- Separate allergy chef, sit by bunk, a/c, 1 seating, campers help clean up, variety provided daily at each meal, can accommodate all allergies, nut free, snacks 3x/day. ACCOMODATIONS – spacious, 8-10 campers w/ 4 staff, facilities in bunk, keep bunks same every year, do not mix partial and full summer campers. Counselors help unpack campers upon arrival. Older campers welcome, any age, currently had rising 9 th grader from another camp and was doing well. OD inside bunk at bedtime. SPORTS – the best things they offer are tennis, basketball and soccer but they do everything (flag football, baseball, batting cage, volleyball, gaga, lacrosse, driving range, roller & street hockey, pump track for biking), and the boys partake in all sports and non-sports activities. Large open-sided terrific facility in addition to fields. No cuts for inter-camp games. CREATIVE- drawing, painting, pottery, tie dye, woodworking. They write their own plays and perform (Matt Tilda, Newsies) he said this is a big program. Music is big: guitar, drums, keyboard. STEAM/HIGH TECH- digital photography, rocketry, sports broadcasting. NATURE – camp craft, salamander hunt, frog hunt (not expansive but fun for some boys/downtime) COOKING – cooking elective at the smoker, daily. ORIENTEERING – 28 elements (high and low combined), zip line, climbing wall, archery, fishing (regular and fly) OLS (map-reading, build fires, hikes) AQUATICS- Rising 2 nd -6 th swim 3x/wk. Private non-motorized small lake. Take to Lake Wallenpaupack to water ski. TRIPS – some hiking trips and mtn biking trip (Jeff takes the boys). PLANNED IMPROVEMENTS – more field space, upgrade some bunks, more ropes elements, would like to add crew.

title: Moosilauke — Dara — 9-17-24
text:
# Moosilauke

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp is in its 120th year. Miller family has owned it since 1938. Session 1 – 145 boys (4.5 weeks with 2.5 week option for the first part, or last 2 weeks for 7-10 year olds attending 1st time). Session 2 – 140 boys (2.5 weeks). 2 days in between each session to clean up. 25% of campers on financial aid. Each session has a social with Merriwood. Sisters of Moosilauke boys attend Merriwood and Wikosuta.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

MOOSILAUKE July 25, 2024 Joanne, Wendy & Dara. Toured by: Bill McMann. Campers – 8-15 years old, CIT 16 yo – pays minimal fee to attend, but still does some camper stuff, Jr. Counselors – 17 yo. 70% repeat staff. 10 women staff/counselors.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks - All have toilets & sinks. Shower house outside of bunks. New bath house built – 14 individual private stalls each with toilet, sink & shower. Junior rec hall up by the bunks with hang out space, foosball, TV for movie night. Locker room – each camper has his own open cubby to store sports equipment throughout their session.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront is really pretty and has everything – boating, fishing, canoes, paddleboard, kayak, water ski, wake board, wake surfing.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule & Activities. Mornings – 2 assigned periods. Afternoons – 2 choice periods. Land sports - 5 red clay tennis courts, 2 baseball fields, 2 basketball courts, 2 soccer fields, 1 lax, 1 mini lax. Arts & crafts, woodshop, outdoor adventure.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

All campers have to go on a wilderness overnight. Each session has a social with Merriwood. Sisters of Moosilauke boys attend Merriwood and Wikosuta.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

2 full time nurses at camp. Nurses station and full infirmary. Dartmouth Hitchcock Hospital 30 minutes away. Dining Hall - New kitchen built in 2020 - Served family style - They serve peanut butter but will take a nut allergy kid - Always have salad bar for lunch/dinner and breakfast cereal bar.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Encourage positive risk taking – can’t just do what you are comfortable with. It’s ok to fail.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation to Camp - Bus from Lincoln Center NYC & Greenwich, CT (extra fee). Will pick up from Boston Logan airport (extra fee). About 50% of campers get dropped off at camp by parents. Parent communication, tech policies, and other extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Moosilauke is in its 120th year and the Miller family has owned it since 1938. The program features Session 1 with 145 boys (4.5 weeks with a 2.5-week option, or last 2 weeks for 7–10 year olds attending 1st time) and Session 2 with 140 boys (2.5 weeks), with 2 days in between each session to clean up. Days run with 2 assigned periods in the morning and 2 choice periods in the afternoon, with extensive land sports, a really pretty waterfront with boating and watersports, plus arts & crafts, woodshop, and outdoor adventure. The culture encourages positive risk taking—can’t just do what you are comfortable with—and it’s ok to fail, with strong health support, family-style meals, and simple travel options from NYC, CT, and Boston.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Waterfront is really pretty and has everything – boating, fishing, canoes, paddleboard, kayak, water ski, wake board, wake surfing.
  - New bath house built – 14 individual private stalls each with toilet, sink & shower.
  - New kitchen built in 2020.
  - All campers have to go on a wilderness overnight.
  - 70% repeat staff.
  - 25% of campers on financial aid.
  - Each session has a social with Merriwood.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Shower house outside of bunks.
  - They serve peanut butter but will take a nut allergy kid.
  - Bus from Lincoln Center NYC & Greenwich, CT and Boston Logan airport pickup are extra fee.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Merriwood
  - Wikosuta

title: Greenwood Trails — Jennifer — 7-14-22
text:
# Greenwood Trails

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Greenwood Trails has 3 sessions - 2 weeks, 3 weeks, 2 weeks. They currently have about 150 kids each session, capacity is 170. Age groups - rising 3rd graders to rising 11th graders.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jenn, Beth, Shari and Lisa toured 6/2022 with Andy Hicks. Andy was very pleasant, formerly from Tioga and brought a lot of Tioga staff with him. Camp has some international campers but mostly from the Northeast - NY, CT, MA.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: 8-14 campers per bunk with 3-4 staff. Cabins do have fans and electricity.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront - Lake is very small, more like a pond. Campers do non motorized water sports at lake but do not swim in lake due to size. Swimming and swim lessons done in pool which is not heated. Swim lessons mandatory for rising 7th graders and younger.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: Elective based, 6 periods in a day - 2 majors, minors are chosen daily.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

According to Andy, most popular activities at camp are high ropes, drama and circus. They do not have a strong sports programming and are geared towards noncompetitive campers. Theater - put on 1 show per session, optional for campers. In Session 1 - they have RICC games. RICC is their philosophy - respect, integrity, care, courage. Color War is done in session 2 and Olympics done in session 3. Horses - off premises, about 25 min away. $55 per session, can do lessons and trail rides. There are no trips during camp and they do not compete in intercamp games. Camp is noncompetitive. Capital Improvements Planned - adding an additional camper, trying to add waterskiing, redoing the pool and adding to the circus program.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Did NOT require COVID vaccine for 2022. Dining Hall: currently eating all meals outside due to COVID. Sit in 1 seating with their bunk. Food is served family style, barbecue on Saturday nights. Camp is nut free and can accommodate all allergies. They have a Camp Mom for the first time this year who is handling all dietary issues.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

No religion at camp. Camp is noncompetitive and very low key. RICC is their philosophy - respect, integrity, care, courage. Andy the director very likeable and campers seemed happy that we saw.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

2/3rds of campers are driven to camp. Horses - off premises, about 25 min away. $55 per session. There are no trips during camp. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Other extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Greenwood Trails is very low key and noncompetitive, geared towards noncompetitive campers with a small lake and a pool for swimming. The camp runs 3 sessions - 2 weeks, 3 weeks, 2 weeks, with about 150 kids each session (capacity 170) and serves rising 3rd to rising 11th graders, mostly from the Northeast (NY, CT, MA). Programming is elective based with 6 periods a day (2 majors; minors chosen daily), and popular activities include high ropes, drama, circus, and an optional theater show each session. RICC is their philosophy (respect, integrity, care, courage), with RICC games in Session 1, Color War in Session 2, and Olympics in Session 3.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Andy was very pleasant, formerly from Tioga and brought a lot of Tioga staff with him.
  - Most popular activities at camp are high ropes, drama and circus.
  - Camp is nut free and can accommodate all allergies.
  - RICC is their philosophy - respect, integrity, care, courage.
  - Campers seemed happy.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Lake is very small, more like a pond; campers do not swim in the lake.
  - Pool is not heated.
  - They do not have a strong sports programming.
  - There are no trips during camp and they do not compete in intercamp games.
  - Did NOT require COVID vaccine for 2022.
  - Horses are off premises, about 25 min away, and cost $55 per session.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Kennybrook — Jennifer — 7-3-23
text:
# Kennybrook

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp has 2 weeks, 3.5 or 7 weeks. Getting a lot of campers who want 2 weeks - those campers can extend to 3.5 as in bunks with 3.5 weeks. Max number of campers – 300 campers – feel that is a big selling point for them as they know all their campers, directors very friendly and involved.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

KENNYBROOK Jennifer, Beth and Amanda toured camp on 6/26/23 with Morgan, daughter of owner. They have 2 bunks per age group so typically 25-30 girls/boys in each age group. Directors very friendly and involved.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins - 10-12 campers with 3 counselors; only counselors or older campers get top bunks, most beds are single bed. Bathrooms in bunks with 2 showers, sinks and toilets.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Activities: small man made lake on campus, campers taken to larger lake 10 min away for motorized water sports. Campers sign up for waterskiing. Banana boating is done with the group and usually 2 times per session. Heated pool on campus – younger campers take swim lessons 2 x per week.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Activities: 7 periods a day – 2 electives daily – 1 in am and 1 in pm. Have all the basic sports and pickleball courts this year. Can take private tennis, basketball or baseball lessons for extra price. Added robotics as an elective this year as well as horseback riding which is done off site for an extra cost.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Theater – done on stage in basketball area – very basic. Participation is not mandatory and do 1 show per session. For trips – younger campers go to water park. Campers finishing 8th and 9th grade fly out west.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall – campers eat with their bunks, food is served family style. Waiters/ Waitresses are campers who finished 10th grade. Can handle all allergies and kosher. Canteen is 7 days a week in the afternoon. Younger campers have milk and cookies nightly while older campers do canteen at night.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Optional Fri night candle lighting; approximately 30 campers attend. Also getting more non Jewish campers due to shorter sessions. Overall impression: Smaller no frills camp. We were there in the rain and campers seemed very happy, directors pride themselves on knowing all their campers. Well priced camp.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bags get picked up in advance and counselors unpack for them. Campers call home 1 x week even 2 week campers; parents have 1 way email option. Extra costs: can take private tennis, basketball or baseball lessons for extra price; horseback riding is off site for an extra cost. Other travel logistics: Unknown. Tech policy beyond parent email: Unknown. Additional costs beyond those noted: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Smaller no frills camp with a max of 300 campers where directors are very friendly and involved and pride themselves on knowing all their campers. Camp has 2 weeks, 3.5 or 7 weeks; getting a lot of campers who want 2 weeks, and those campers can extend to 3.5 as in bunks with 3.5 weeks. Activities run 7 periods a day with 2 electives daily, a small man made lake and a heated pool, plus basic sports with pickleball, new robotics, and trips (water park for younger; out west for 8th/9th grade). Dining is family style and they can handle all allergies and kosher; campers call home 1 x week and parents have a 1-way email option.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Smaller no frills camp.
  - Max number of campers – 300 campers – feel that is a big selling point for them.
  - Directors very friendly and involved; pride themselves on knowing all their campers.
  - Well priced camp.
  - Bags get picked up in advance and counselors unpack for them.
  - Optional Fri night candle lighting; approximately 30 campers attend.
  - Activities: 7 periods a day – 2 electives daily – 1 in am and 1 in pm.
  - Heated pool on campus; younger campers take swim lessons 2 x per week.
  - Small man made lake on campus and a larger lake 10 min away for motorized water sports; banana boating usually 2 times per session.
  - Added robotics as an elective this year; horseback riding off site for an extra cost.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Theater – very basic; done on stage in basketball area.
  - No frills.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Kingsley Pines — Michele — 7-10-23
text:
# Kingsley Pines

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Liz, Emily and Michele toured Kingsley Pines in Maine in early July and met with Drew Taylor (2nd generation owner; he bought it from his parents). They’re all about a sense of community, this is a very laid back camp and the kids seemed fun-loving and happy. This camp seems to focus mostly on arts, water sports and outdoor adventure. They do have traditional sports but the focus is not on winning. This is an all elective camp, tons of choices but nothing over the top (think: more rustic in feel). Camp is spread out and woodsy, no uniform. Can do 2 weeks, 3 weeks or 5. No-one does full 7. Michele Gershwin The Camp Experts & Teen Summers in partnership with Joanne Paltrowitz.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers are 8-16 years old. He gets kids from "all over." Drew Taylor is the 2nd generation owner (he bought it from his parents) and is very low key. He said his retention rate has been a little low recently and he’s working on getting campers to come back or extend. He is currently at 250 but wants more kids so he said to "please send them his way\!"

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Camp is spread out and woodsy. Arts and crafts were in little bunks/art shacks divided by specialty. Sleeping cabin details: Unknown.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The lake does have a blob, waterspouts, SUP, beach volleyball, etc.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

This is an all elective camp, tons of choices but nothing over the top (think: more rustic in feel). VERY co-ed; he said that all activities are co-ed including meals.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They are more focused on building self esteem and friendships and being a safe and relaxing place to spend the summer. The kids seemed fun-loving and happy. They had music going and the kids were dancing and singing while cooking.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

It was a very hot day so they were mostly at indoor activities like arts and crafts (in little bunks/art shacks divided by specialty) and culinary arts (held in the dining room which was very hot and old). Other health, safety, and food specifics: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They’re all about a sense of community, this is a very laid back camp and the kids seemed fun-loving and happy. VERY co-ed. Campers are 8-16 years old, has a campy/unplugged/natural vibe. We did notice a few "quirky" kids so it seems like a very open atmosphere, warm and loving but also just very chill in vibe. Owner is very low key too. No uniform.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

He gets kids from "all over" and there is no busing. He said parents like to drop off or they will pick up at airport. Tech: unplugged. Session one in 2023: (2 weeks) $4450 / 3 weeks $5950 / $9500 for 5 weeks. Extra costs or fees beyond this: Unknown. Parent communication specifics: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kingsley Pines in Maine is all about a sense of community; this is a very laid back camp and the kids seemed fun-loving and happy. This is an all elective camp with tons of choices (nothing over the top, more rustic in feel) that focuses mostly on arts, water sports and outdoor adventure, with traditional sports but the focus is not on winning; all activities are co-ed including meals. Campers are 8-16 years old, sessions can be 2 weeks, 3 weeks or 5 (no-one does full 7), and there is no busing—parents like to drop off or they will pick up at airport. The camp is spread out and woodsy with a lake that has a blob, SUP, beach volleyball, and an open, warm and loving, very chill vibe under low key 2nd generation owner Drew Taylor (he bought it from his parents).

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Sense of community; very laid back; kids seemed fun-loving and happy.
  - All elective camp; tons of choices; more rustic in feel.
  - Focus mostly on arts, water sports and outdoor adventure.
  - Traditional sports but the focus is not on winning.
  - More focused on building self esteem and friendships and being a safe and relaxing place.
  - VERY co-ed; all activities are co-ed including meals.
  - Camp is spread out and woodsy; no uniform.
  - Campy/unplugged/natural vibe; open atmosphere; warm and loving; very chill.
  - Lake has a blob, SUP, beach volleyball.
  - He gets kids from "all over"; airport pick up available.
  - 2-week, 3-week, or 5-week options.
  - Owner Drew Taylor is 2nd generation and very low key.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Dining room was very hot and old.
  - Retention rate has been a little low recently.
  - No busing.
  - He is currently at 250 but wants more kids.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Kohut
  - Crescent Cove
  - Southwoods

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Here is my write up for Kingsley Pines in Maine: Liz, Emily and Michele toured Kingsley Pines in early July and met with Drew Taylor \*2nd generation owner, he bought it from his parents) They’re all about a sense of community, this is a very laid back camp and the kids seemed fun-loving and happy. It was a very hot day so they were mostly at indoor activities like arts and crafts (in little bunks/art shacks divided by specialty) and culinary arts (held in the dining room which was very hot and old). But they had music going and the kids were dancing and singing while cooking. VERY co-ed, he said that all activities are co-ed including meals. He gets kids from “all over” and there is no busing. He said parents like to drop off or they will pick up at airport. This camp seems to focus mostly on arts, water sports and outdoor adventure. They do have traditional sports but the focus is not on winning. They are more focused on building self esteem and friendships and being a safe and relaxing place to spend the summer. This is an all elective camp, tons of choices but nothing over the top (think: more rustic in feel). The lake does have a blob, waterspouts, SUP, beach volleyball, etc. Camp is spread out and woodsy, no uniform, can do 2 weeks, 3 weeks or 5. No-one does full 7. He said his retention rate has been a little low recently and he’s working on getting campers to come back or extend. Campers are 8-16 years old, has a campy/unplugged/natural vibe - maybe give along with Kohut, Crescent Cove and Southwoods. We did notice a few “quirky” kids so it seems like a very open atmosphere, warm and loving but also just very chill in vibe. Owner is very low key too. He is currently at 250 but wants more kids so he said to "please send them his way\!" Session one in 2023: (2 weeks) $4450/ 3 weeks $5950/ $9500 for 5 weeks Michele Gershwin The Camp Experts & Teen Summers In partnership with Joanne Paltrowitz

title: Kimama Half Moon — Liz — 7-18-22
text:
# Kimama Half Moon

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Kimama Half Moon-Berkshires ("Best little Camp in the Berkshires"). Camp was bought out this year by Kimama; was Half Moon Camp before. Kimama runs many other camps in Israel and is their counterpart here in the US. It is a 100 year old camp. This camp is a day camp and a sleep away both operating on the same premises. 175 day campers/100 overnight campers.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Michele (Gershwin) and I (Liz Hochman Mak) toured camp on 7/9/22. Met with Abby Levine who is new Director since earlier this year. 100 staff. They have a very heavy international population (large Israeli population) with most kids coming internationally as well as the staff. Abby mentions the type of camper who would flourish "a child who likes a variety of activities in a screen free environment". This is an international camp with campers and staff - promote relationships with each other and developing "self". Activities are co ed. Abby Levine is very nice but quiet and reserved. Not outgoing. Probably better suited for a small camp like this.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Separate tents for the day campers and a separate area for them to hang during the day. Small and quaint dining room. Overnight living quarters: Unknown.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Small pool. Lake down the hill at bottom of camp.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Sessions - 1 wk/2wk/3wk. Elective program - electives changed weekly. 5 one hour activities. Variety of activities.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have a very small rec hall/play house for theatre and activities. New ropes course.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Camp is Kosher. Very small health center. 2/3 nurses on staff and pediatrician in town.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This is an international camp with campers and staff - promote relationships with each other and developing "self". They have a very heavy international population (large Israeli population). Abby mentions the type of camper who would flourish "a child who likes a variety of activities in a screen free environment". Activities are co ed.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No phone calls. No canteen. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kimama Half Moon-Berkshires is a 100 year old camp that was Half Moon Camp before and was bought out this year by Kimama, which runs many other camps in Israel and is their counterpart here in the US. It is a day camp and a sleep away both operating on the same premises with 175 day campers/100 overnight campers and 100 staff, and they have a very heavy international population (large Israeli population). Abby mentions the type of camper who would flourish "a child who likes a variety of activities in a screen free environment," with activities co ed. Sessions are 1 wk/2wk/3wk with an elective program (electives changed weekly; 5 one hour activities), a small pool and a lake down the hill, and a new ropes course.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - "Best little Camp in the Berkshires."
  - Kimama bought the camp this year; was Half Moon Camp before.
  - Kimama runs many other camps in Israel and is their counterpart here in the US.
  - 100 year old camp.
  - Day camp and sleep away both operating on the same premises.

  - Elective program; electives changed weekly.

  - New ropes course.
  - Small pool and a lake down the hill at bottom of camp.
  - Camp is Kosher.
  - 2/3 nurses on staff and pediatrician in town.
  - Activities are co ed.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Small pool.
  - Very small health center.
  - They have a very small rec hall/play house for theatre and activities.
  - No phone calls.
  - No canteen.
  - Abby Levine is very nice but quiet and reserved. Not outgoing. Probably better suited for a small camp like this.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Merriwood — Jennifer — 7-14-22
text:
# Merriwood

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

All girls camp, offers 7, 4 or 3 week sessions, approximately 135 girls per session, 60% of girls stay full summer, currently has 20 girls there on scholarship. Age range: finishing 2nd grade to finishing 9th.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jenn, Shari, Beth and Lisa toured 6/2022 with director, Susan. The greatest concentration of girls come from the Northeast (Boston- Washington DC). Brothers tend to attend Moosilauke, Pemi, Kingswood or Deerwood.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: typical is 7-12 campers per bunk with 3 counselors, counselors have a separate sleep area in the bunk. There are toilets and sinks in cabins but use shower houses.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Aquatics: swim instruction in lake for all ages. Offers motorized and nonmotorized water sports as well as big crew program.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Horses: 7 horses, girls can ride 3 times per week for extra cost. Dance and gymnastics outside. Theater program is small, do 1 play at end of 1st session and is voluntary. No cooking or music. Upcoming capital improvements planned – moving gymnastics pavilion indoors, adding an outdoor fireplace and lounge area.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: rustic with beautiful view of the lake. Campers all eat in 1 seating, 12 yr olds help serve. Do not serve peanut butter – say they are nut aware? They change who campers sit with at each meal. No canteen but have morning and afternoon snack and cookies and milk in their cabins before bed. Girls are assigned a big sister before they arrive at camp and girls sit with their big sisters for their first week of camp. No religion but do have a moment of silence before each meal.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall impression – very low key all girls camp, rustic and warm.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

The greatest concentration of girls come from the Northeast (Boston- Washington DC) and the majority of parents drive their girls to camp. Riding is an extra cost (girls can ride 3 times per week). Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Very low key all girls camp, rustic and warm. Offers 7, 4 or 3 week sessions with approximately 135 girls per session; 60% of girls stay full summer, and there are currently 20 girls there on scholarship. Age range: finishing 2nd grade to finishing 9th, with the greatest concentration of girls from the Northeast (Boston–Washington DC). Aquatics includes swim instruction in lake for all ages and motorized and nonmotorized water sports as well as a big crew program, plus riding (7 horses; girls can ride 3 times per week for extra cost) and a small, voluntary theater program.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All girls camp; very low key, rustic and warm.
  - 7, 4 or 3 week sessions; approximately 135 girls per session; 60% stay full summer; 20 girls on scholarship.
  - Age range: finishing 2nd grade to finishing 9th; greatest concentration from Northeast (Boston–Washington DC).
  - Swim instruction in lake for all ages; motorized and nonmotorized water sports; big crew program.
  - Cabins: 7–12 campers per bunk with 3 counselors; separate counselor sleep area; toilets and sinks in cabins; shower houses.
  - Dining Hall rustic with beautiful view of the lake; one seating; 12 yr olds help serve; seating changes each meal.
  - No canteen; morning and afternoon snack plus cookies and milk in cabins before bed.
  - Girls assigned a big sister and sit with them for first week.
  - Upcoming capital improvements: moving gymnastics pavilion indoors; adding an outdoor fireplace and lounge area.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Do not serve peanut butter – say they are nut aware?
  - No cooking or music.
  - Theater program is small (one play at end of 1st session; voluntary).
  - Dance and gymnastics outside (until pavilion moved indoors).
  - Use shower houses.
  - Parent communication and tech policy: Unknown.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Kohut — Michele — 7-8-23
text:
# Kohut

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Session camp, offering 2 sessions (around 190 campers in each). He said they would like 200 so feel free to send referrals their way. Camp is over 100 years old, very proud of the history and traditions. Overall vibe is very chill but also that Kohut is a well maintained, pretty camp with traditional values. Nice layout. Not hilly. Note: youngest campers can do 2 weeks only. MICHELE GERSHWIN The Camp Experts & Teen Summers In partnership with Joanne Paltrowitz 917-929-9906 cell

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Kamp Kohut notes 7/6 Liz, Emily and Michele toured with Rory who is the asst Director, very nice guy who has been there many years and worked his way up from counselor. Camp is around 40-50% Jewish, he said religion is a non issue. There are also a handful of international kids. No formal CIT program because they want kids to go do something else and come back as a counselor (more mature).

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: pretty, white exteriors. We were there on a 90 degree day so it was very hot & humid inside the bunks. Showers are in bunks but also they have shower houses if there is a line and kids don’t want to wait. Metal bed frames, no bunk beds so there is more space. Laundry is done on site.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The waterfront is very pretty, simple but has nice clear water and all the watersports you could want. Kids were on SUP, kayaking and sailing. Also lots of waterskiing. Even on a very hot day they were down by waterfront having fun.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 periods a day with one elective. They don’t move as a bunk but do have evening activities together.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Cute garden that the kids help tend to, with flowers and herbs. They have a radio station, newspaper and yearbook (showed it to us, very nicely done by the kids). They have a library tree where kids like to read. He said it’s popular place to hang out. There is a long path of pine trees that runs through camp, to the left and right are all the land sports including a nice woods area for outdoor adventure/ropes course.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Unknown

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall vibe is very chill but also that Kohut is a well maintained, pretty camp with traditional values. This is a uniform camp, kids looked happy and low key. Not a lot of spirit, more of a quiet camp. Activities are mostly not co-ed. Think of it more as brother/sister on one campus. They line up separately at flagpole, 2 lines. Can wave to sibling or see them at a meal.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Campers can have a call with parents (or choose not to) once a week. They don’t post many pics and don’t have Waldo - their parents don’t mind. Tech policy beyond photos: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Kohut is a session camp offering 2 sessions with around 190 campers in each, and they would like 200. Over 100 years old and very proud of the history and traditions, it feels chill, well maintained, and pretty with traditional values. The program runs 6 periods a day with one elective, activities are mostly not co-ed, and the waterfront is very pretty with clear water for SUP, kayaking, sailing, and lots of waterskiing. Weekly parent calls (no Waldo, few pics), a uniform and low-key vibe, and a two-week option for the youngest campers round out the experience.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Well maintained, pretty camp with traditional values.
  - Session camp, offering 2 sessions (around 190 campers in each).
  - Camp is over 100 years old, very proud of the history and traditions.
  - The waterfront is very pretty, simple but has nice clear water and all the watersports you could want.
  - Kids were on SUP, kayaking and sailing; also lots of waterskiing.
  - 6 periods a day with one elective.
  - They don’t move as a bunk but do have evening activities together.
  - Activities are mostly not co-ed; think of it more as brother/sister on one campus.
  - They have a radio station, newspaper and yearbook (very nicely done by the kids), plus a library tree and a cute garden.
  - There is a long path of pine trees through camp with land sports and an outdoor adventure/ropes course.
  - Laundry is done on site.
  - Nice layout. Not hilly.
  - Campers can have a call with parents once a week; they don’t post many pics and don’t have Waldo.
  - You could give this camp along with Skylemar and crescent cove. Both nearby.

  

#### Lowlights

  - We were there on a 90 degree day so it was very hot & humid inside the bunks.
  - Not a lot of spirit, more of a quiet camp.
  - They don’t post many pics and don’t have Waldo.
  - No formal CIT program because they want kids to go do something else and come back as a counselor (more mature).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Skylemar
  - Crescent Cove

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Overall vibe is very chill but also that kohut is a well maintained, pretty camp with traditional values. title: Kohut — Natasha — 8-13-23
text:
# Kohut

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Kohut is a session camp – 4 week sessions with a 2 week intro option. 170 kids currently with a maximum capacity of 175. The camp is evenly divided between boys and girls.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Natasha, Risa, Lindsey and Lana toured Kohut on 7/17/2023. Tour was given by Carly Vargas, daughter of owners/directors Dan and Lisa Tripler. Carly was very pregnant – met her husband on a birthright trip. The camp attracts campers primarily from the Northeast, with some campers coming from Texas, California (San Francisco and Los Angeles), and Florida. The camp offers no CIT program. Most activities are separated by gender. Non-binary/Trans – haven’t dealt with it yet – bunked by the parts you are born with.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

The camp is divided into boys' and girls' sides, and all bunks are named after private schools i.e. Choate and Fairfield. 10 campers to 3-4 counselors per bunk. Laundry facilities are on-site.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

LAKE - beautiful lake with a sandy bottom - Fernwood shares it - sometimes art activities take place by the lake. They have waterskiing, wakeboarding, fishing, canoeing – no water toys.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Elective-based scheduling with campers having seven periods of activities, one of which is a free choice. The camp has a media center and co-ed evening activities. Trips - once a week, nature-based trips such as fishing and sailing.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Kohut offers a wide range of activities, including mountain biking, gardening, field hockey, water skiing, archery, culinary arts, crew, and fencing. Tennis, baseball, and basketball the most popular activities. There's also an off-site option for horseback riding, which is an extra cost. The camp puts on two musicals each summer, ensuring that every camper gets a part. Kohut has its own radio station, where kids can record and listen to their recordings in their bunks. Kohut holds two Olympics events during the season – haven’t had intercamps since Covid.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall is family-style. Each week, the seating arrangements are mixed up, and campers are handpicked to foster a sense of community. The camp is well-equipped to cater to all allergies, including re-introducing nuts – have had a few of those situations this summer. Health Center - 3 to 4 nurses on-site, and they have access to a doctor through telehealth at any time. The nearest hospital is 15 minutes away.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Kohut was warm and fuzzy. Kids seemed happy and engaged, a nurturing place. No religion.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Campers from distant locations have the option of chaperoned flights and buses from New York, Connecticut, and Westchester. Daily pictures, and campers get a weekly phone call home. No packages. Campers are required to wear blue and white attire daily, but it doesn't necessarily have to include the camp's logo. Horseback riding is off-site and an extra cost.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Small, session-based coed camp (4-week sessions with a 2-week intro) for families seeking a warm, nurturing community and lots of choice. Campers build their days with seven periods (one free choice) across robust sports, arts, and waterfront, including a beautiful sandy-bottom lake, two musicals, and weekly nature-based trips. Communication and logistics are straightforward with daily pictures, weekly phone calls, and chaperoned travel from New York, Connecticut, and Westchester; campers wear blue and white daily. Note: most activities are separated by gender, there is no CIT program, no packages, and horseback riding is an extra cost.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Warm and fuzzy; kids seemed happy and engaged, a nurturing place.
  - Beautiful lake with a sandy bottom.
  - Elective-based scheduling with seven periods, one of which is a free choice.
  - Wide range of activities (mountain biking, gardening, field hockey, water skiing, archery, culinary arts, crew, fencing) with tennis, baseball, and basketball the most popular.
  - Kohut has its own radio station, where kids can record and listen to their recordings in their bunks.
  - Two musicals each summer with a part for every camper.
  - Weekly nature-based trips and two Olympics events during the season.
  - Strong allergy support and family-style dining with mixed, handpicked seating to foster community.
  - Health Center with 3–4 nurses, telehealth access to a doctor, and a hospital 15 minutes away.
  - Chaperoned flights and buses from New York, Connecticut, and Westchester; daily pictures and a weekly phone call home.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No CIT program.
  - Most activities are separated by gender.
  - Non-binary/Trans – haven’t dealt with it yet – bunked by the parts you are born with.
  - No water toys at the lake.
  - Off-site horseback riding is an extra cost.
  - No packages.
  - Haven’t had intercamps since Covid.
  - Campers are required to wear blue and white attire daily.
  - Lake is shared with Fernwood.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - North Star
  - Laurel South

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

KOHUT Natasha, Risa, Lindsey and Lana toured Kohut on 7/17/2023 Tour was given by Carly Vargas, daughter of owners/directors Dan and Lisa Tripler. Carly was very pregnant – met her husband on a birthright trip. Kohut is a session camp – 4 week sessions with a 2 week intro option. 170 kids currently with a maximum capacity of 175. The camp is evenly divided between boys and girls. The camp is divided into boys' and girls' sides, and all bunks are named after private schools i.e.Choate and Fairfield. The camp attracts campers primarily from the Northeast, with some campers coming from Texas, California (San Francisco and Los Angeles), and Florida. Campers from distant locations have the option of chaperoned flights and buses from New York, Connecticut, and Westchester. 10 campers to 3-4 counselors per bunk. The camp offers no CIT program. Dinin Hall is family-style. Each week, the seating arrangements are mixed up, and campers are handpicked to foster a sense of community. The camp is well-equipped to cater to all allergies, including re-introducing nuts- have had a few of those situations this summer. Elective-based scheduling with campers having seven periods of activities, one of which is a free choice. Kohut offers a wide range of activities, including mountain biking, gardening, field hockey, water skiing, archery, culinary arts, crew, and fencing. Most activities are separated by gender. The camp has a media center and co-ed evening activities. Kohut has it’s own radio station, where kids can record and listen to their recordings in their bunks. Tennis, baseball, and basketball the most popular activities. There's also an off-site option for horseback riding, which is an extra cost. The camp puts on two musicals each summer, ensuring that every camper gets a part. Kohut holds two Olympics events during the season – haven’t had intercamps since Covid. Trips - once a week, nature-based trips such as fishing and sailing. Campers are required to wear blue and white attire daily, but it doesn't necessarily have to include the camp's logo. LAKE - beautiful lake with a sandy bottom -Fernwood shares it - sometimes art activities take place by the lake. They have waterskiing, wakeboarding, fishing, canoeing – no water toys Daily pictures, and campers get a weekly phone call home. Non-binary/Trans – haven’t dealt with it yet – bunked by the parts you are born with No packages and no religion Health Center - 3 to 4 nurses on-site, and they have access to a doctor through telehealth at any time. The nearest hospital is 15 minutes away. Laundry facilities are on-site Kohut was warm and fuzzy. Kids seemed happy and engaged, a nurturing place. I would give North Star and Laurel South with Kohut.

title: Forest Lake — Jennifer — 7-25-24
text:
# Forest Lake

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Forest Lake is a brother/sister camp located in the Adirondacks. They offer a one time 2 week session for younger campers; otherwise session lengths are 3,4,5 or 7 weeks. This year they have about 100 campers first session, 70 campers second session, 10-15 campers staying full summer. They have the capacity for 180 campers. They do offer an English immersion program for international campers with tutoring in English.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

FOREST LAKE CAMP Jennifer, Lisa, Shari and Beth toured on 7/11/2024 with Alvi Baquero.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins – simple cabins with bunk beds, can have up to 12 campers with 2 staff. Facilities in the bunk.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront – nonmotorized lake, swim lessons done 3x week. Take a swim test to be in the deep end. If do not pass- must wear life jacket in the lake. No pool.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 2 periods in the morning – 1 is a sport, other is more relaxed activity; 2 periods in the afternoon – 1 is a sport and the other is waterfront. Campers get to choose all their activities daily except for waterfront. Camp does run as a brother/sister camp so activities are single sex. There is brother/sister time on Sundays. Twice a week, they also have coed barbecues.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Creative – what they offer in arts depends on the year and who they hire and what that person’s background is. Alvie said they are bringing woodworking back. Theater- done in rustic amphitheater, 1 show per session. New dance pavilion, no gymnastics. Three tennis courts and convert one for pickleball. For golf- they hit golf balls into a field and will take campers off site to a golf course. New climbing wall. They also have a zipline and obstacle course. Riflery done off site. According to Alvie, most popular activities at camp are archery, climbing wall, rugby, field hockey, soccer and hiking. Horses: 12 horses on site, about 8-10 campers ride per day. Riding is an additional fee.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall – campers eat family style except for when they have bbq which is family style, can accommodate all food allergies. They do get 2 snacks daily.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Alvie was great but camp seemed very basic. I might give it with some of the YMCA camps as think it would be best for a non Jewish camper looking for rustic, low key.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bus pickup in Paramus and Croton- Harmon, campers bring their bags with them. Canteen is 1 x week, is an extra charge and campers can buy soda and candy. Activities that cost extra – horseback riding, white water rafting and a boat adventure trip. He did mention scholarships are available.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Forest Lake is a brother/sister camp located in the Adirondacks. They offer a one time 2 week session for younger campers; otherwise session lengths are 3,4,5 or 7 weeks. Programming features 2 periods in the morning (1 sport and 1 more relaxed activity) and 2 in the afternoon (1 sport and the other is waterfront), with campers choosing all their activities daily except for waterfront. Alvie was great but camp seemed very basic, and I think it would be best for a non Jewish camper looking for rustic, low key.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Brother/sister camp located in the Adirondacks.
  - English immersion program for international campers with tutoring in English.
  - Campers get to choose all their activities daily except for waterfront.
  - Theater- done in rustic amphitheater, 1 show per session.
  - New dance pavilion.
  - New climbing wall.
  - Zipline and obstacle course.
  - Three tennis courts and convert one for pickleball.
  - Horses: 12 horses on site, about 8-10 campers ride per day.
  - Bus pickup in Paramus and Croton- Harmon, campers bring their bags with them.
  - Twice a week, they also have coed barbecues.
  - Scholarships are available.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Camp seemed very basic.
  - No pool.
  - Creative – what they offer in arts depends on the year and who they hire and what that person’s background is.
  - Canteen is 1 x week, is an extra charge and campers can buy soda and candy.
  - Activities that cost extra – horseback riding, white water rafting and a boat adventure trip.
  - Riflery done off site.
  - For golf- they hit golf balls into a field and will take campers off site to a golf course.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - YMCA camps

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Echo Lake — Jennifer — 7-24-24
text:
# Echo Lake

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Echo Lake is a coed 7 week camp with 500 campers. For younger campers—rising 3rd and 4th—offer a 1 time 4 week session.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jennifer, Shari, Lisa and Beth toured on 7/11/24 with Debbie who is the parent liaison. Demographics: 25-30% NYC, 25-30% Westchester, less than 10% from Long Island. She said there is no typical camper profile.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks – typical bunk is 10 campers, bunks have toilets and sinks, use a shower house.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Aquatics – 2 heated pools, lake is motorized but a little far.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Scheduling: 6 periods in a day – younger campers get 1 elective, next division gets 2 electives and then becomes fully elective after 8th grade. Campers participate in activities with their group which includes 2 bunks of same age. They do change up who is in each bunk each year.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have built a new building on campus called the Quad which is nice and doors open up like garage doors. In the Quad, they have canteen, a spin studio as well as a STEM/bioquest. Sports: not ultra competitive, no tryouts for intercamps, may need to try out for intercamps. Offers street and roller hockey, added pickleball, golf – driving range on campus – can go offsite to golf course about 2 x week for no additional fee, gymnastics in outdoor pavilion. No horses.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall – staggered seating for campers and campers sit with their groups. Breakfast and dinner are served family style. Health and safety: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Throughout the tour, Debbie stressed that they focus on values/not on winning – their mottos are be nice, try hard. Each night, they give out fuzzies to campers who have done something nice, tried something new etc (not for winning). She said it is a come as you are camp, no make up for girls etc and is a warm and inclusive environment. Religious Life: On Friday nights, the whole camp gets together for which Debbie described as a meeting with a little bit of Shabbat followed by Friday night chicken dinner.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

May offer a chaperoned flight from FL depending on number of campers from there. Parent communication, tech, and extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Echo Lake is a coed 7 week camp with 500 campers, with a one-time 4 week session for rising 3rd and 4th. Scheduling is 6 periods per day with increasing electives (fully elective after 8th grade), and campers participate with their group (2 bunks of the same age). They focus on values/not on winning—mottos are be nice, try hard—with nightly fuzzies; it is a come as you are, warm and inclusive environment, with a Friday night camp meeting with a little bit of Shabbat followed by Friday night chicken dinner. Facilities and program include 2 heated pools; the Quad (canteen, spin studio, STEM/bioquest); not ultra competitive sports (street and roller hockey, pickleball, golf with a driving range and offsite 2x/week for no additional fee, gymnastics in an outdoor pavilion); the lake is motorized but a little far.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Focus on values/not on winning – mottos are be nice, try hard; nightly fuzzies.
  - 6 periods per day with increasing electives; fully elective after 8th grade.
  - New building called the Quad with canteen, spin studio, and STEM/bioquest.
  - Two heated pools.
  - Golf with a driving range on campus and offsite 2x/week for no additional fee.
  - Warm, inclusive, come as you are environment.
  - Demographics: 25-30% NYC, 25-30% Westchester, less than 10% Long Island.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Lake is motorized but a little far.
  - No horses.
  - Sports are not ultra competitive; note the mixed message on intercamps tryouts.
  - Bunks use a shower house (bunks have toilets and sinks).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

ECH0 LAKE Jennifer, Shari, Lisa and Beth toured on 7/11/24 with Debbie who is the parent liaison Echo Lake is a coed 7 week camp with 500 campers. For younger campers- rising 3 rd and 4 th – offer a 1 time 4 week session. Demographics: 25-30% NYC, 25-30% Westchester, less than 10% from Long Island; may offer a chaperoned flight from FL depending on number of campers from there Scheduling: 6 periods in a day – younger campers get 1 elective , next division gets 2 electives and then becomes fully elective after 8 th grade Campers participate in activities with their group which includes 2 bunks of same age. They do change up who is in each bunk each year. Bunks – typical bunk is 10 campers, bunks have toilets and sinks, use a shower house. Dining Hall – staggered seating for campers and campers sit with their groups. Breakfast and dinner are served family style They have built a new building on campus called the Quad which is nice and doors opens up like garage doors. In the quad, they have canteen, a spin studio as well as a STEM/bioquest Religious Life: On Friday nights, the whole camp gets together for which Debbie described as a meeting with a little bit of Shabbat f/b Friday night chicken dinner Aquatics- 2 heated pools, lake is motorized but a little far Sports: not ultra competitive, no tryouts for intercamps, may need to try out for intercamps. Offers street and roller hockey, added pickleball, golf – driving range on campus – can go offsite to golf course about 2 x week for no additional fee, gymnastics in outdoor pavillion No horses Throughout the tour, Debbie stressed that they focus on values/not on winning – their mottos are be nice, try hard. Each night, they give out fuzzies to campers who have done something nice, tried something new etc ( not for winning). She said it is a come as you are camp, no make up for girls etc and is a warm and inclusive environment. She said there is no typical camper profile.

title: Golden Slipper — Emily — 7-5-24
text:
# Golden Slipper

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Stroudsburg, Pennsylvania. Golden Slipper is a not-for-profit camp with a Board of Directors. It is the 76th summer of camp. Work with Jewish Federation and One Happy Camper to get scholarship kids. Office is in Bucks County. Co-ed with about 225 campers (300 is the max capacity). Kids are 7-15 years old. Campers are not all Jewish. There are 2 sessions that are 3.5 weeks long; kids can stay for 7 weeks. Camp supplies all the sports equipment, they don’t expect kids to bring. Golden Slipper is a recreational camp where kids can enjoy activities and diversity.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Michele Gershwin and Emily Rothenberg touring with Justin Guida (Director for 6 years). Justin is a teacher in the off season. Specialist counselors also are bunk counselors. Counselors are 25% International. Some of the campers are on scholarship. This summer, 16 kids are coming to camp second session from Israel. The older kids are waiters.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

About 10 kids per bunk with bathrooms and showers in bunk. Boys and girls bunks are in separate areas on camp. Only A/C is in Health Center. Laundry is once a week. It is a woodsy, rustic feel.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is a lake and pool. Lake has no motor sports, just kayaking, paddle boarding etc. Kids get instructional swim (Red Cross).

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Program is elective/choice. Every morning flagpole. Evening activity tends to be all together as a camp.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities include sports (wiffle ball, soccer, baseball, basketball, volleyball), mountain biking, archery, riflery, tennis, pickleball, skatepark, arts and crafts, music, stem lab, robotics, gymnastics, rock wall. Gaga, Theater. Outdoor amphitheater. Kids camp out in tents on the property, make smores. Kids do not play intercamps. Kids do take some day trips to places like Dorney Park.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Food is kosher style. Can handle some food allergies. They are peanut aware. Dining hall has salad bar and cereal bar in morning. There is a health center with 2 RN’s on staff and 2 EMTs. Hospital and urgent care are 10 minutes away.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Do hold Friday night Shabbat services, Havdallah about Jewish mission and values, and services on Saturday. Jewish Values are: Respect - Kavod; Loving Kindness - Chesed; Repairing the World - Tikkun Olam; Family - Mishpacha; Friendship - Chaverim; Community - Kehilah; Service - Avodah. Campers are not all Jewish. Golden Slipper is a recreational camp where kids can enjoy activities and diversity. This is not a camp for kids looking for intense sports or instruction. Justin, the Director seems great.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Offer transportation from Philadelphia only. Canteen is no added cost. 3.5 weeks session is about $3900 and full session is about $7100. Camp is VERY well priced. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Golden Slipper in Stroudsburg, Pennsylvania is a not-for-profit, co-ed camp for ages 7-15 with about 225 campers (300 max) and two 3.5-week sessions; kids can stay 7 weeks. Golden Slipper is a recreational camp where kids can enjoy activities and diversity; this is not a camp for kids looking for intense sports or instruction. There is a lake and pool (no motor sports on the lake), instructional swim (Red Cross), robust sports and arts, day trips, and evening activity tends to be all together as a camp. Camp is VERY well priced (about $3900 for 3.5 weeks and about $7100 for the full session), offers transportation from Philadelphia only, and food is kosher style with peanut awareness and some allergies handled.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Golden Slipper is a not-for-profit camp with a Board of Directors.
  - It is the 76th summer of camp.
  - Work with Jewish Federation and One Happy Camper to get scholarship kids.
  - Camp is VERY well priced.
  - Program is elective/choice.
  - There is a lake and pool.
  - Kids get instructional swim (Red Cross).
  - There is a health center with 2 RN’s on staff and 2 EMTs. Hospital and urgent care are 10 minutes away.
  - Camp supplies all the sports equipment, they don’t expect kids to bring.
  - Counselors are 25% International.
  - This summer, 16 kids are coming to camp second session from Israel.
  - Outdoor amphitheater; kids camp out in tents on the property, make smores.
  - Dining hall has salad bar and cereal bar in morning.
  - Canteen is no added cost.
  - Woodsy, rustic feel.
  - Justin, the Director seems great.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Only A/C is in Health Center.
  - Kids do not play intercamps.
  - Lake has no motor sports.
  - Offer transportation from Philadelphia only.
  - This is not a camp for kids looking for intense sports or instruction.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

title: Jeanne D'Arc — Heather — 3-4-24
text:
# Jeanne D'Arc

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Jeanne D’Arc Zoom Write Up-2/29/24. Located in the Adirondacks. Strong Emphasis on building confidence in girls through leadership. 2, 3.5 and 7 week sessions.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Campers age 7-16. Staff and director profile: Unknown.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterskiing for additional fee.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They offer mountaineering hiking, gardening, exploring and field trips, tennis, basketball, field hockey, volleyball, soccer, riflery, archery, visual and performing arts. Have horseback riding for additional fee.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Hot meal provided at every meal, salad bar offered as alternative, will accommodate special dietary needs. Assigned table seating switched weekly.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Self proclaimed small family feel. Traditional, values based program.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Have horseback riding, waterskiing for additional fee. Travel logistics, parent communication, and tech policies: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Located in the Adirondacks, Campers age 7-16 attend 2, 3.5 and 7 week sessions. There is a strong emphasis on building confidence in girls through leadership and a traditional, values based program. Self proclaimed small family feel, great for first time campers. They offer mountaineering hiking, gardening, exploring and field trips, tennis, basketball, field hockey, volleyball, soccer, riflery, archery, visual and performing arts, with horseback riding and waterskiing for additional fee.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong Emphasis on building confidence in girls through leadership
  - Self proclaimed small family feel
  - Great for first time campers
  - Hot meal provided at every meal, salad bar offered as alternative, will accommodate special dietary needs
  - They offer mountaineering hiking, gardening, exploring and field trips, tennis, basketball, field hockey, volleyball, soccer, riflery, archery, visual and performing arts

  

#### Lowlights

  - Have horseback riding, waterskiing for additional fee
  - Living quarters: Unknown
  - Daily timetable & choice: Unknown
  - Travel logistics, parent communication, and tech policies: Unknown
  - Staff and director profile: Unknown

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Jeanne D’Arc Zoom Write Up-2/29/24 Located in the Adirondacks Campers age 7-16 Strong Emphasis on building confidence in girls through leadership 2, 3.5 and 7 week sessions They offer mountaineering hiking, gardening, exploring and field trips, tennis, basketball, field hockey, volleyball, soccer, riflery, archery, visual and performing arts Have horseback riding, waterskiing for additional fee Hot meal provided at every meal, salad bar offered as alternative, will accommodate special dietary needs Assigned table seating switched weekly Self proclaimed small family feel The ideal camper comes from an all-girls school, seeking traditional, values based program Great for first time campers

title: Menominee — Brooke — 7-18-24
text:
# Menominee

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Well rounded boys / sports camp. 2 week session during start of each four week session.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

140 kids first session/190 kids second session. Female staff. Counselor training program during pre-camp.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

No bunk beds. New shower house and laundry facility in 2024.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Great, fresh water.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Leagues can be chosen instead of assigned.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Invented trampball. 9 hole golf course. New greenhouse containing indoor athletic field and shelter in 2024. Still has traditional riflery.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Unknown

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Sunday night campfire. Bock band evening program, musicians ok.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Menominee is a well rounded boys / sports camp. They invented trampball and have a 9 hole golf course. Still has traditional riflery.
