title: Bryn Mawr — Shari — 8-13-23
text:
# Bryn Mawr

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Lake Bryn Mawr Camp – Shari & Lisa B. toured by Molly Glinert (former camper, not w/ camp staff full-time but answered most of our questions/very knowledgeable and professional). We talked with Dan and Jane Kagan @ end of tour, 30 June 2023. BASIC DATA – 330 girls, 7 wks w/ 5 wk option (about 30-40 girls opt for this and 80% extend) 1st summer only. Into 2nd grade through into 10th as campers, LIT into 11th and they are campers 1/2 the summer and staff the other half. Brothers at Winadu, Wayne and Trails End (they do a bro/sis dinner w/ TE). Uniform. Extras: trips, hb riding, Tennis Plus.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Shari & Lisa B. toured by Molly Glinert (former camper, not w/ camp staff full-time but answered most of our questions/very knowledgeable and professional). We talked with Dan and Jane Kagan @ end of tour, 30 June 2023. Some counselors were previous campers (didn’t get number). Into 2nd grade through into 10th as campers, LIT into 11th and they are campers 1/2 the summer and staff the other half. Dan has a dog but does not roam around, he keeps near him on leash.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

ACCOMODATIONS – 8-12 girls, 3-4 staff, facilities in bunks. Youngest campers all live together, and the oldest campers live together. Would keep bunks same year-to-year but only if it works. Counselors unpack campers for all ages. Older campers, up to into 8th, welcome. Girls bring make-up but barely use. Hair dryers & curling/flat iron collected. On duty @ bedtime inside bunk and also counselors outside bunk. Towel service provided (shower, pool, lake).

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

AQUATICS – Swim instruction in heated pool (across road) 6x/wk for 3rd–5th grade until pass swim test. No lifeguard cert offered for older campers, no swimsuit policy except for competitive swim must wear one piece. Two areas on lake: motorized (waterski, etc) & non-motorized (kayak, canoe, SUP, etc) & fishing. Separate staff pool for time off.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING – 5p/day; 2 AM, 3 PM. Into 2nd–5th grades all structured. Into 6th one elective/day and as older get more choice. Move by bunk or division depending on the activity.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Color war 5-6 days at end of summer, into 10th grade lead, 2 teams. Olympics 2 days and into 9th grade lead, 4 teams. SPORTS – Soccer, basketball, lacrosse, flag football, softball, volleyball, gaga, field hockey, tennis (extra cost for privates built into schedule every other day for 2hr blocks). CREATIVE – lots of variety aside from basics of drawing, painting, ceramics; sewing (big program - stuffed animals, pillows, etc - many machines), beading, gumball machine paper mâché. Cooking program in 2 rooms, cold and hot items, smoothies. Amphitheatre in addition to indoor theatre at field house. Three plays per summer separated by ages, participation is optional, tech theatre available. DANCE CENTER IS PHENOMINAL\! Four studios with ‘soft’ floor (lightly padded) for tap, jazz, hip hop (and more depending on year). Gymnastics is very extensive (ZERO missing equipment, added an extension to the bldg) Coach Bill been @ BM for 42 years\! Coached in Ohio & has 9 state championships. Can bring own routine and music. Bill can run program before breakfast if home coach wants. Juniors are assigned gymnastics. He runs 4 levels of programming: strength & flexibility, beginner gymnast, gymnasts @ home & wants to mess around at camp or gymnast @ home and wants extra at camp. Horses 5 minutes away at their own barn, an indoor arena, extra cost. No golf in ’23, had some campers golf off-camp in ’22 so can do if a family wants. ORIENTEERING – Outdoor high ropes (part self-belay), zip line, climbing wall (indoor climbing wall too), archery. They do community service projects at camp (she didn’t elaborate).

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – 8 RN’s, 1 MD (pediatrician, Internal medicine, CHOP researcher (Children’s Hosp of Philly) cardiologist, oncologist). Wayne Mem 20 mins away. Can take a diabetic. DINING – magnificent dining hall w/ a/c, 1 seating, Elena is allergy supervisor, separate allergy chef & kitchen, all allergies accommodated, and camp is sesame & nut free. Separate GF window. BBQ’s Sundays at beautiful apple orchard outside dining hall. 4pm daily snack (ice cream, fruit, gf option and dairy free option available). 9pm snack is savory usually. No canteen.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Big sister program (Peanut Night) Jr camper paired w/ Sr camper assigned in office and reveal at camp. Brothers at Winadu, Wayne and Trails End (they do a bro/sis dinner w/ TE). Uniform. RELIGIOUS LIFE – no services but say 2 prayers @ Friday dinner, wear all white on Friday eve, traditional shabbat dinner, say Shema at talent show after dinner.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses from NYC, Westchester, NJ, Philly, LI, MD van, other campers from FL, TX, CA, CO, VA, Spain & others. EWR pickup. COMMUNICATION – 2 phone calls/summer (one before VD, one after), one way emails, electronics music only, no packages, cell phones are stored in the office and into 9th–into 11th they use on phone days for limited time. Letter writing not required, post photos and videos daily & use facial recognition. Extras: trips, hb riding, Tennis Plus. Tennis privates are extra cost (privates built into schedule every other day for 2hr blocks). Horses 5 minutes away at their own barn, an indoor arena, extra cost. TRIPS – all ages go to Hershey and/or Dorney up to into 7th grade. Overnights as follows: Into 7th & 8th - Berkshires 2 nights, hotel, 4 girls/room. Into 9th - Boston 4 nights. Into 10th - Newport 4 nights. Into 11th (2023) Hawaii, 8 nights with Winadu 11th graders.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Lake Bryn Mawr Camp hosts 330 girls for a 7-week season with a 5-week option for 1st-summer campers. Programming is structured for younger campers with increasing choice by age, and features robust sports, creative arts, dance, gymnastics, ropes, and lake/pool aquatics. Health, safety, and food systems are strong, with 8 RNs, 1 MD, a magnificent A/C dining hall, separate allergy chef and kitchen, and a sesame & nut free campus. Family logistics and communication are clear with bus routes from major hubs, EWR pickup, defined phone/email policies, and a rich trips program.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - DANCE CENTER IS PHENOMINAL\!
  - Gymnastics is very extensive (ZERO missing equipment, added an extension to the bldg) Coach Bill been @ BM for 42 years\!
  - Magnificent dining hall w/ a/c.
  - Separate allergy chef & kitchen; camp is sesame & nut free; separate GF window.
  - 8 RN’s, 1 MD; Wayne Mem 20 mins away.
  - Color war 5-6 days; Olympics 2 days.
  - Big sister program (Peanut Night).
  - Two areas on lake: motorized & non-motorized; heated pool across road.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No lifeguard cert offered for older campers.
  - No golf in ’23.
  - No canteen.
  - No packages.
  - Cell phones are stored in the office.
  - Uniform.
  - Pool is across road.
  - Electronics music only.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

Lake Bryn Mawr Camp – Shari & Lisa B. toured by Molly Glinert (former camper, not w/ camp staff full-time but answered most of our questions/very knowledgeable and professional) We talked with Dan and Jane Kagan @ end of tour, 30 June 2023 BASIC DATA –330 girls, 7 wks w/ 5 wk option (about 30-40 girls opt for this and 80% extend) 1 st summer only. Into 2 nd grade through into 10 th as campers, LIT into 11 th and they are campers ½ the summer and staff the other half. Brothers at Winadu, Wayne and Trails End (they do a bro/sis dinner w/ TE). Letter writing not required, post photos and videos daily & use facial recognition. He runs 4 levels of programming: strength & flexibility, beginner gymnast, gymnasts @ home & wants to mess around at camp or gymnast @ home and wants extra at camp. Horses 5 minutes away at their own barn, an indoor arena, extra cost. No golf in ’23, had some campers golf off-camp in ’22 so can do if a family wants. Dan has a dog but does not roam around, he keeps near him on leash. ORIENTEERING – Outdoor high ropes (part self-belay), zip line, climbing wall (indoor climbing wall too), archery. They do community service projects at camp (she didn’t elaborate). AQUATICS- Swim instruction in heated pool (across road) 6x/wk for 3 rd – 5 th grade until pass swim test. No lifeguard cert offered for older campers, no swimsuit policy except for competitive swim must wear one piece. Two areas on lake: motorized (waterski, etc) & non-motorized (kayak, canoe, SUP, etc) & fishing. Separate staff pool for time off. TRIPS – all ages go to Hershey and/or Dorney up to into 7 th grade. Overnights as follows: Into 7 th & 8 th - Berkshires 2 nights, hotel, 4 girls/room. Into 9 th -Boston 4 nights. Into 10 th -Newport 4 nights. Into 11 th (2023) Hawaii, 8 nights with Winadu 11 th graders.

title: Emerson — Lara — 7-12-23
text:
# Emerson

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Emerson – Lara and Emily S. toured with Zack – the new director who took over in March and also moved from LA (worked at Eden Village out there) and got married\! Camp Emerson had 90 campers for the first session and 170 campers for the second session. Their goal is 225 campers. Campers can attend 2, 4 or 6 weeks. The camp does not have a traditional visiting day. They were recently bought by the Camp Group. The camp strives for an equal split of boys and girls, with slightly more girls overall.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Zack – the new director – took over in March and also moved from LA (worked at Eden Village out there) and got married. Zack is very mild mannered and soft spoken and wants to help grow and improve camp. They were recently bought by the Camp Group and were able to retain a third of their staff for this summer. The camp strives for an equal split of boys and girls, with slightly more girls overall. Campers are grouped by age, with lower grades through 4th grade in one group, and middle grades from 5th to 6th grade, along with campers through 10th grade, in another group.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

The camp is currently rehabbing their bunks, and they have a new bathhouse that is 10 years old. Each bunk houses 10 campers with two staff members. The camp plans to continue rehabbing their facilities. The old bunks have no facilities in the bunks at all. All sinks, toilets and showers are in the bath houses. The newer bunks have sinks and toilets. There is one on girls side and 3 on boys side. Both sides have new bathhouses as well.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is a heated pool and a pond where they have inflatables. All motor sports are off camp.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The daily schedule includes six periods, with three in the morning and three in the afternoon. All activities at the camp are elective-based, allowing campers to choose their interests.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

They have two baseball fields, one soccer field, and a third field for various activities. The camp has two hard courts and two clay courts for tennis. They offer activities such as rocketry and carbon dioxide-powered car races. The camp also has a large theater program and emphasizes art and science programs overall. They present a play a session. Zack has a lot of ideas and plans but for 2024 he wants to add a climbing wall and ropes course – the existing one was neglected but still has space in the woods – and a fire circle area by the dining hall for evening activities.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The camp has a medical director who visits once a session, and three full-time nurses are available to provide medical care. They can accommodate all allergies in the dining hall where they sit by bunk. There is no religion.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

This camp is not for a sporty kid. It is for a more artsy kid interested in arts and sciences. Zack is very mild mannered and soft spoken and wants to help grow and improve camp.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation is provided from Boston and Newton MA, White Plains, and Connecticut. The camp does not have a traditional visiting day. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Emerson had 90 campers for the first session and 170 campers for the second session, with a goal of 225 campers. Campers can attend 2, 4 or 6 weeks, and all activities at the camp are elective-based, with a large theater program and an emphasis on art and science programs overall. The camp has a heated pool and a pond where they have inflatables, plus fields and courts, rocketry and carbon dioxide-powered car races; this camp is not for a sporty kid — it is for a more artsy kid interested in arts and sciences. Transportation is provided from Boston and Newton MA, White Plains, and Connecticut, and the camp does not have a traditional visiting day.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - All activities at the camp are elective-based, allowing campers to choose their interests.
  - Large theater program; emphasizes art and science programs overall; they present a play a session.
  - Heated pool and a pond where they have inflatables.
  - Two baseball fields, one soccer field, a third field for various activities; two hard courts and two clay courts for tennis.
  - Transportation is provided from Boston and Newton MA, White Plains, and Connecticut.
  - They were recently bought by the Camp Group.
  - Zack has a lot of ideas and plans; for 2024 he wants to add a climbing wall and ropes course and a fire circle area by the dining hall for evening activities.
  - Can accommodate all allergies; three full-time nurses; a medical director who visits once a session.
  - Bunks are being rehabbed; newer bunks have sinks and toilets; both sides have new bathhouses.

  

#### Lowlights

  - This camp is not for a sporty kid.
  - They were able to retain a third of their staff for this summer.
  - The old bunks have no facilities in the bunks at all; all sinks, toilets and showers are in the bath houses.
  - The existing ropes course was neglected (space in the woods).
  - The camp does not have a traditional visiting day.
  - All motor sports are off camp.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: IHC — MIchelle — 7-26-24
text:
# IHC

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp IHC. Toured 7/24 with Risa Goldberg; tour led by Matty Layman. 7 weeks only. Armed security and security gate. Air conditioning not in bunks but in all main, large areas around camp.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

650 campers and 450 staff. 5 counselors in a bunk because “kids need a lot of staff.” Every area at camp has a director with specialists under him or her that plays the sport at the college level.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Boys and Girls bunks are separated. Air conditioning not in bunks but in all main, large areas around camp. Towel service. Laundry once a week. All body wash and shampoo provided; they discourage campers from bringing their own. No full length mirrors anywhere on campus. Trunks are provided by camp and families must use them. \*they are 38” which is significantly smaller than standard camp duffles.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Private lake with waterskis and boats (canoe kayak sailing) and very large impressive slide. Children are brought to a larger lake for longer stretches of time on the lake if they want. Wake/paddle boarding, fishing, water polo. Pool has showers and changing areas, large rock wall that campers can jump off, diving platforms, again music playing. Camp has a swim team that is very popular. Swim instruction for 6th grade and below.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Structured day traveling to activities with bunk. 5th grade and below- 1 elective per day. 6th and 7th grade- 2 electives per day. 8th-11th grade all elective. 10th grade goes out west for 4 full weeks. Line ups are not coed, activities are not co ed. Flag pole in morning and evening coed full camp together.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Activity offerings: Soccer, Tennis, Flag football, Ceramics, Culinary, Fine arts, Legos, Sewing, Stem Rocketry, Tye Dye, Gymnastics, Basketball, Lacrosse, Ropes course, Dance, Wake/paddle boarding, Fishing, Water polo, Pickleball, Beach volleyball, Archery, Mountain Biking, Hiking (elective only), Baseball with batting cages, Zip line, Skate park, Wiffle ball, Golf (campers taken off site), Horseback riding (campers taken off site), Music- keys, percussion, guitar, voice recording, podcasting, Chess (elective only), Rock band, Lacrosse, Roller and street hockey. Facilities: Fitness Center, indoor turf field, recording studio with dj booth, green room, 4 indoor tennis courts, indoor basketball courts, movie theater, brand new staff housing being built, impressive staff coffee bar (like Starbucks.). 3 canteen facilities; 1 outdoor and 2 indoor.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Armed security and security gate. Dining facility is indoor/outdoor. 2 seatings for meals broken up by age. Round tables. Waiters are counselors. Eat by bunk. Friday night Shabbat dinner with no religious aspect.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Music is always playing. Most of the music was classic rock. No awards of any kind. “No cut throat sports.” Camp is “accommodating.” Camp is “inclusive.” No uniform required. Only full piece bathing suits permitted. No full length mirrors anywhere on campus. No hair straighteners allowed. No makeup allowed. No crop tops allowed. Shorts must be appropriate length. Transgender policy: A girls bunk at camp currently houses a camper with a boy’s anatomy. Camper has a curtain blocking her body from being seen by bunkmates when she showers and changes. The entire age group was made aware. There has been nothing but warm and welcome sentiments from parents about this camper becoming part of the IHC community. “If you’re not ok with this situation, then our camp is not right for you.” “If this makes you uncomfortable you should choose a different camp.”

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Tuition for 2025 $16,900. Campers come from: Long Island (4 busses) NYC (3 busses) Westchester (3 busses) NJ (3 busses) Maryland, Philly, Texas, Boston, Chicago, Florida, California, Utah, Indiana. Golf (campers taken off site). Horseback riding (campers taken off site). Private one on one sports instruction offered for extra cost. Trunks are provided by camp and families must use them. \*they are 38” which is significantly smaller than standard camp duffles. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp IHC is a 7-week, structured program with 650 campers and 450 staff, and 5 counselors in a bunk because “kids need a lot of staff.” The day is bunk-based with electives increasing by age, a very popular swim team and instruction for younger campers, and a huge menu of sports, arts, and outdoor activities (including off-site golf and horseback riding). Culture is “inclusive,” “no cut throat sports,” no awards, clear dress-code guidelines, and Friday night Shabbat dinner with no religious aspect. Security is tight, facilities are extensive (private lake, pool with rock wall and diving platforms, indoor courts, recording studio, movie theater), and 10th grade goes out west for 4 full weeks.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Armed security and security gate.
  - 5 counselors in a bunk because “kids need a lot of staff.”
  - Every area at camp has a director with specialists under him or her that plays the sport at the college level.
  - Music is always playing. Most of the music was classic rock.
  - Private lake with waterskis, boats, and a very large impressive slide; pool with rock wall and diving platforms.
  - Swim team that is very popular; swim instruction for 6th grade and below.
  - Breadth of activities across sports, arts, outdoor adventure, and music (recording studio with dj booth, rock band, podcasting).
  - 4 indoor tennis courts, indoor basketball courts, indoor turf field, movie theater.
  - 3 canteen facilities; 1 outdoor and 2 indoor.
  - 10th grade goes out west for 4 full weeks.
  - Brand new staff housing being built; impressive staff coffee bar (like Starbucks.).
  - Towel service; laundry once a week; toiletries provided.
  - Inclusive stance, with clear communication to families.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Air conditioning not in bunks.
  - Activities are not co ed (line ups also not coed; only flag pole in morning and evening is coed full camp together).
  - Only full piece bathing suits permitted.
  - No full length mirrors anywhere on campus.
  - No hair straighteners allowed. No makeup allowed. No crop tops allowed.
  - Trunks are provided by camp and families must use them; they are 38” which is significantly smaller than standard camp duffles.
  - “If you’re not ok with this situation, then our camp is not right for you.”

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp IHC Toured 7/24 with Risa Goldberg Tour led by Matty Layman 7 weeks only 650 campers and 450 staff 5 counselors in a bunk because “kids need a lot of staff.” Tuition for 2025 $16,900 Campers come from: Long Island (4 busses) NYC (3 busses) Westchester (3 busses) NJ (3 busses) Maryland, Philly, Texas, Boston, Chicago, Florida, California, Utah, Indiana Armed security and security gate. Air conditioning not in bunks but in all main, large areas around camp. Structured day traveling to activities with bunk. 5th grade and below- 1 elective per day 6th and 7th grade- 2 electives per day 8th-11th grade all elective 10th grade goes out west for 4 full weeks. Music is always playing. Most of the music was classic rock. Dining facility is indoor/outdoor. 2 seatings for meals broken up by age. Round tables. Waiters are counselors. Eat by bunk. Pool has showers and changing areas, large rock wall that campers can jump off, diving platforms, again music playing. Camp has a swim team that is very popular. Swim instruction for 6th grade and below. Boys and Girls bunks are separated. Line ups are not coed, activities are not co ed. Flag pole in morning and evening coed full camp together. Trunks are provided by camp and families must use them. \*they are 38” which is significantly smaller than standard camp duffles. Friday night Shabbat dinner with no religious aspect. title: Forest Acres — Pilar — 8-1-22
text:
# Forest Acres

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Geoff and Lisa Newman (owners) live at Forest Acres. Next year is their 100th anniversary. 157 girls this summer. Max number is 160 girls. They have girls from Mexico and Colombia. 6 to 16 year olds. CIT’s are 17 year olds. Traditional camp with activities all day long. Lots of tradition. Instruction is camp style. Sessions 7wks, 4wks starting the first day of camp. Two 2wks sessions for those who have not completed 9th grade. Rookie camp for those who have not completed 5th grade.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Forest Acres Karen, Monica, Aline and Pilar V. August 1st, 2022. Toured by Moe Burness, who has been at camp since 1967. "This is an education." Geoff and Lisa Newman (owners) live at Forest Acres. They have girls from Mexico and Colombia. 6 to 16 year olds. CIT’s are 17 year olds. CIT 17 year olds 1/2 pay. They visit about 80% of families. Counselors are selected placing importance on how they are with kids. They need to be proficient in what they will teach. Can start at 14 but can be a challenge.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks Cabins on outside, activities on inside. All cabins face the fields except for 2. Cabins have 7 kids and 2 counselors. Bathroom+shower with hot water in cabin. Bathrooms have been upgraded in the past 3 to 4 years. 15 and 16 year olds live in a sorority type bunk with a lounge area with a piano and a fridge. Can hold up to 25 girls. They do their own laundry once a week. Blankets to internationals, everyone brings their own sheets and towels. Kids take turns to clean the cabins and bathrooms.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

At lake: 10 minute drive to a shared lake but only camp on lake Lovewell Pond with a gas tank so few boats even during the weekend. 3 boats, 30 kids at a time. 2 1/2 hour lake period. Water skiing, kneeboarding, wakeboarding, trampoline, paddle boarding, kayaking, sailing. Boys & girls come separate. Everyone with a life jacket at the dock. Cabin is used for overnights. It has a full bathroom, a fire pit and gas grill. Accredited American Red Cross certified swimming. All swim instructors are teachers at home. ARC Certification at camp if certification is from another country. 7 to 12 year olds go to instructional swim 5 times a week.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule 7 periods, 4 am, 3 pm. Campers get to pick between 2 to 4 activities in a block. The 7th is an open period with no team sports offered. 7 to 12 year olds go to instructional swim 5 times a week. Juniors go to everything. Points for activities are given to campers. Coed activities 7 to 10 maybe once a week. 11 and 12 once a week and maybe an extra activity. 13 to 16 twice a week. Coed play, campfire, cookout, tennis. Play is coed on the 4th week of camp. This year over 100 kids. Friday or Saturday bonfire, buffet meal. Mornings have one group activity. Afternoon all camp activity. Sunday evening friendship circle. Girls come in their pjs and stuffed animals, sing camp songs, handout recognitions for camper of the week for each cabin. Division counselors vote for 1 camper of the week for each age group, and other recognitions. Colorwar goes for 2 weeks and they don't do something every day. Might do a rally. It is about the song at the end.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities Sports Competitions with other camps. Accredited American Red Cross certified swimming. Volleyball, soccer, lacrosse, pickleball, softball, basketball, archery, golf, gaga pit, ninja warrior. Gym, gymnastics, acrobatics, spinning, yoga. Low and high ropes and zip line. Climbing at boys camp. Riding All horses come from the same college. Director has been with them since 1986, she knows all the horses, the instructors and can match them with the riders. ? of girls ride. 3 riding rings, trail rides, jumping, vaulting. Can also participate as a stable manager, with no extra fee. Girls were grooming the horses. They ride 5 times a week minimum. Can ride twice a day for an extra cost. Extra for riding, they sign up before camp starts. Girls bring their own equipment which is sent in a list before arrival. Competitions with other camps. Arts & Crafts Digital Photo. Sewing. Ceramics with 5 potter wheels. Cooking They learn meals. Chopping and cleaning. Artistic School of Rock had a great performance the day before. 50 girls participated. Will be relocated out to the parking lot with a counselor lounge on top. Dance style depends on the teacher. Theater. Next year they will do Grease so they expect lots of campers signing up. Will have a tutoring area where School of Rock is now.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health 2 nurses, 1 doctor. 2 hospitals 15 minutes away. 70 covid cases between 2 camps. They were put together until 2 negative tests. After covid they had a special ed teacher from Ohio taking care of mental health. No security at the gate. Dining Hall Eat by cabin, family style. Waiters serve. Sunday buffet meals. Breakfast bar, salad bar at lunch and dinner. Special area in the kitchen for gluten free, dairy free, allergies. Not a vegan camp. Nut free. Canteen 3 times a week, included in tuition.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very traditional. Traditional camp with activities all day long. Lots of tradition. Johnny Appleseed is their prayer. Birthdays they have a special wakeup song. Whole camp sings happy birthday and they skip around the dining hall. They get a special cake. "This is an education." They have a dog.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation Pickup from Boston Logan, Newton, Westchester County, NY. Escort from LA. Would escort for 10 campers. Canteen 3 times a week, included in tuition. Extra for riding, they sign up before camp starts. Can ride twice a day for an extra cost. Girls bring their own equipment which is sent in a list before arrival. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Traditional camp with activities all day long and lots of tradition. 6 to 16 year olds; CIT’s are 17 year olds; 157 girls this summer (max 160) with girls from Mexico and Colombia. Sessions are 7 weeks, 4 weeks starting the first day of camp, two 2-week sessions for those who have not completed 9th grade, and a Rookie camp for those who have not completed 5th grade. This camp is not for an over athlete; someone less athletic, more artistic, dance, drama, music, riding; if they are elite athletes they come to take a break.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Geoff and Lisa Newman (owners) live at Forest Acres; next year is their 100th anniversary.
  - Counselors are selected placing importance on how they are with kids; they need to be proficient in what they will teach.
  - Accredited American Red Cross certified swimming; all swim instructors are teachers at home.
  - Riding program: 3 riding rings, trail rides, jumping, vaulting; can also participate as a stable manager with no extra fee; they ride 5 times a week minimum.
  - Transportation pickup from Boston Logan, Newton, Westchester County, NY; escort from LA (would escort for 10 campers).
  - Sorority type bunk with a lounge area with a piano and a fridge for 15 and 16 year olds (can hold up to 25 girls).
  - After covid they had a special ed teacher from Ohio taking care of mental health.
  - They have girls from Mexico and Colombia.

  

#### Lowlights

  - No security at the gate.
  - 70 covid cases between 2 camps (they were put together until 2 negative tests).
  - At lake: 10 minute drive to a shared lake.
  - Not a vegan camp (nut free; special area for allergies).
  - They do their own laundry once a week.
  - Kids take turns to clean the cabins and bathrooms.
  - Can start at 14 but can be a challenge.
  - Boys & girls come separate; limited coed activities by age.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Forest Acres Karen, Monica, Aline and Pilar V. August 1st, 2022. Toured by Moe Burness, who has been at camp since 1967. “This is an education.” Geoff and Lisa Newman (owners) live at Forest Acres. Next year is their 100th anniversary. No security at the gate. They have a dog. 157 girls this summer. Max number is 160 girls. They have girls from Mexico and Colombia. 6 to 16 year olds. CIT’s are 17 year olds. They visit about 80% of families. Can start at 14 but can be a challenge. Traditional camp with activities all day long. Lots of tradition. Instruction is camp style. Counselors are selected placing importance on how they are with kids. They need to be proficient in what they will teach. This camp is not for an over athlete, someone less athletic, more artistic, dance, drama, music, riding. If they are elite athletes they come to take a break. Sessions 7wks, 4wks starting the first day of camp. Two 2wks sessions for those who have not completed 9th grade. Rookie camp for those who have not completed 5th grade. Health 2 nurses, 1 doctor. 2 hospitals 15 minutes away. 70 covid cases between 2 camps. They were put together until 2 negative tests. After covid they had a special ed teacher from Ohio taking care of mental health. Transportation Pickup from Boston Logan, Newton, Westchester County, NY. Escort from LA. Would escort for 10 campers. Bunks Cabins on outside, activities on inside. All cabins face the fields except for 2. Cabins have 7 kids and 2 counselors. Bathroom+shower with hot water in cabin. Bathrooms have been upgraded in the past 3 to 4 years. 15 and 16 year olds live in a sorority type bunk with a lounge area with a piano and a fridge. Can hold up to 25 girls. They do their own laundry once a week. Blankets to internationals, everyone brings their own sheets and towels. Kids take turns to clean the cabins and bathrooms. Dinning Hall Eat by cabin, family style. Waiters serve. Sunday buffet meals. Breakfast bar, salad bar at lunch and dinner. Special area in the kitchen for gluten free, dairy free, allergies. Not a vegan camp. Nut free. Birthdays they have a special wakeup song. Whole camp sings happy birthday and they skip around the dining hall. They get a special cake. Johnny Appleseed is their prayer. Canteen 3 times a week, included in tuition. Schedule 7 periods, 4 am, 3 pm. Campers get to pick between 2 to 4 activities in a block. The 7th is an open period with no team sports offered. 7 to 12 year olds go to instructional swim 5 times a week. Juniors go to everything. Points for activities are given to campers. CIT 17 year olds 1/2 pay. Coed activities 7 to 10 maybe once a week. 11 and 12 once a week and maybe an extra activity. 13 to 16 twice a week. Coed play, campfire, cookout, tennis. Play is coed on the 4th week of camp. This year over 100 kids. Friday or Saturday bonfire, buffet meal. Mornings have one group activity. Afternoon all camp activity. Sunday evening friendship circle. Girls come in their pjs and stuffed animals, sing camp songs, handout recognitions for camper of the week for each cabin. Division counselors vote for 1 camper of the week for each age group, and other recognitions. Very traditional. Colorwar goes for 2 weeks and they don't do something every day. Might do a rally. It is about the song at the end. Will have a tutoring area where School of Rock is now. Activities Sports Competitions with other camps. Accredited American Red Cross certified swimming. All swim instructors are teachers at home. ARC Certification at camp if certification is from another country. Volleyball, soccer, lacrosse, pickleball, softball, basketball, archery, golf, gaga pit, ninja warrior. Gym, gymnastics, acrobatics, spinning, yoga. Low and high ropes and zip line. Climbing at boys camp. Riding All horses come from the same college. Director has been with them since 1986, she knows all the horses, the instructors and can match them with the riders. ? of girls ride. 3 riding rings, trail rides, jumping, vaulting. Can also participate as a stable manager, with no extra fee. Girls were grooming the horses. They ride 5 times a week minimum. Can ride twice a day for an extra cost. Extra for riding, they sign up before camp starts. Girls bring their own equipment which is sent in a list before arrival. Competitions with other camps. Arts & Crafts Digital Photo. Sewing. Ceramics with 5 potter wheels. Cooking They learn meals. Chopping and cleaning. Artistic School of Rock had a great performance the day before. 50 girls participated. Will be relocated out to the parking lot with a counselor lounge on top. Dance style depends on the teacher. Theater. Next year they will do Grease so they expect lots of campers signing up. At lake: 10 minute drive to a shared lake but only camp on lake Lovewell Pond with a gas tank so few boats even during the weekend. 3 boats, 30 kids at a time. 2 1/2 hour lake period. Water skiing, kneeboarding, wakeboarding, trampoline, paddle boarding, kayaking, sailing. Boys & girls come separate. Everyone with a life jacket at the dock. Cabin is used for overnights. It has a full bathroom, a fire pit and gas grill.

title: AMDA — Melissa — 7-30-23
text:
# AMDA

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

AMDA - College of the Performing Arts. AMDA is the only BFA-granting performing arts college with campuses located in the heart of Hollywood and Manhattan. Great opportunity (especially) for student interested in entering AMDA College\! Audition training. Conservatory training environment. No experience necessary. For students 13 - 18 years old; students are with other students in same age group. Although no experience is necessary to enroll, this is a serious program. For the student who is serious about pursuing the performing arts as a career, this is a fantastic place to go. The programs for high school students are designed to give them a taste of what the college program is like. 25%-50% will ultimately become students at the college. They have an impressive alumni following, 2 of the characters from Hamilton, as well as various well known TV stars. The other impressive aspect is that the faculty for the high school programs are the same faculty for the college programs, and most of the faculty is “working faculty”, meaning they are in the business themselves and are well networked.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

For students 13 - 18 years old; students are with other students in same age group. Students we met were all super enthusiastic about the program and the outstanding support of the teachers. Although no experience is necessary to enroll, this is a serious program. The faculty for the high school programs are the same faculty for the college programs, and most of the faculty is working faculty, meaning they are in the business themselves and are well networked. Director: Unknown.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

The location for the AMDA Los Angeles campus is unbeatable, right in Hollywood around the corner from Hollywood and Vine. Six residences on or near main campus (3 dorms on campus and 3 off campus). Facilities either on campus or within walking distance. The high school summer programs usually use the on campus dorms. Dorms are separated by gender unless requested otherwise. There are RA’s on each floor. Shuttles available for facilities that are then 1 block away. Furnished rooms with WiFi and amenities. Enclosed courtyard outside Yucca Tower (main building) for socializing, studying, lunch, etc. The campus is situated right at Hollywood & Vine - with a view of the Hollywood sign. Facilities: Four theaters (black box theater and 2 laboratory theaters) with full theatrical lighting and sound plus climate control. AMDA Performing Arts library’s listening and viewing stations plus an expansive collection of scripts, scores and DVDs. Studios, private voice rooms, rehearsal equipment, dance studios, sprung floors, ballet barres, pianos and more in lots of rehearsal spaces.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Programs:  
1\. Acting for Stage and Screen (2 weeks) - Through intensive study and performance by working on-camera with working actors and directors in the business. Students learn how to communicate and to navigate the demands of the industry; learn to apply acting adjustments as directed; learn standard industry practices regarding preparation, communication, and professionalism; how to audition; engage in a collaborative production process. At the end of the program, students will have an in-camera scene for their actor’s reel that highlights their best work plus an opportunity to audition for AMDA College.  
2\. Musical Theater (2 weeks) - Explores classic and contemporary musical theater through coursework and work on vocal performance skills and development. They can train with experienced Broadway performers and directors. At the end of the program, students will have an in-camera scene for their actor’s reel that highlights their best work plus an opportunity to audition for AMDA College.  
3\. Dance Theater (2 weeks) - Total immersion in dance technique and performance by working with dancers and choreographers of the Broadway stage, film and television, and contemporary concert dance. Includes a dance-for-camera music-video experience.  
4\. Pop Star Recording Artist (2 weeks) - Dive into vocal performance styles from contemporary to jazz, gospel to acapella, blues, soul, country and R\&B focusing on the styles most suitable to their specific vocal type. Includes a professional recording studio experience.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

24 hour security. Dorms are separated by gender unless requested otherwise. There are RA’s on each floor. The AMDA Cafe offers healthy organic cuisine and is the social hub of the campus. They can also adapt the menu to any type of food restriction or allergy.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The whole vibe is magical. Students we met were all super enthusiastic about the program and the outstanding support of the teachers. The campus is situated right at Hollywood & Vine, with a view of the Hollywood sign. The AMDA Cafe is the social hub of the campus.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Locations:  
6305 Yucca St., Los Angeles, CA 90028 (323) 603-5900  
211 W. 61st St., New York, New York 10023 (212) 787-5300  
Facilities either on campus or within walking distance. Shuttles available for facilities that are then 1 block away.  
Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Although no experience is necessary to enroll, this is a serious program. For the student who is serious about pursuing the performing arts as a career, this is a fantastic place to go. The programs for high school students are designed to give them a taste of what the college program is like. Students we met were all super enthusiastic about the program and the outstanding support of the teachers.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Only BFA-granting performing arts college with campuses located in the heart of Hollywood and Manhattan.
  - Great opportunity for student interested in entering AMDA College.
  - Audition training and conservatory training environment.
  - Faculty for the high school programs are the same faculty for the college programs; most are working faculty and well networked.
  - Impressive alumni following (2 of the characters from Hamilton, various well known TV stars).
  - Programs culminate with an in-camera scene for the actor’s reel and an opportunity to audition for AMDA College.
  - LA campus location at Hollywood & Vine with view of the Hollywood sign.
  - Healthy organic cuisine with ability to adapt to any food restriction or allergy.
  - 24 hour security and RA’s on each floor.
  - Furnished rooms with WiFi and amenities; enclosed courtyard outside Yucca Tower.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Daily timetable and choice details not provided.
  - Parent communication, tech policy, and extra costs not specified.
  - Waterfront and aquatics: Unknown.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Camp Cedar — Liz — 7-22-23
text:
# Camp Cedar

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Cedar, Casco, Maine. Toured on 7/6/23 by Liz Hochman Mak, Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin. All boys. 7 week camp; can do 4/5 or 6 week option for flexibility (85% do 7 weeks). Oldest campers going into 10th grade. Very sporty camp with multiple sports being focus.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

We toured with Jeff Hacker (owner/director). Met his sister Sue who also runs camp with him as well as his wife. Lots from Manhattan, Boston, CT, NY etc. 95% Jewish according to Jeff. Minimal candle lighting on Friday night. Jeff was a very big athlete and played soccer and lacrosse at Brown. He coached Lacrosse at Yale for many years.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks: 4 counselors and approx. 10 campers.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Beautiful lake with waterskiing being a very big component and separate entry area dedicated to this.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Unknown.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Very sporty camp with multiple sports being focus including lacrosse, basketball, tennis, soccer, hockey and baseball. Outdoor skills area and outdoor cooking. Beautiful woodshop area overlooking lake; kids were making cutting boards and other great projects with wood burning carving as well. Wrestling room, batting cages, gaga courts, great fields, arts and crafts. Special rocketry house. Lots of basketball courts. Nice zip line and climbing walls. Indoor facility with basketball courts and kids were playing tennis, pickleball (multi purpose). Intercamps with camps such as Takajo, Skylemar, Wildwood. Some tournaments as well. Intercamps: up to 4th grade everyone participates and can go; 5th and up will do A and B teams. Nice frog pond where overnights often take place.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Milk and cookies daily. Jeff was very happy to give us some of his cookies that he won’t share recipe. Health and safety details: Unknown.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very rooted in tradition and history. His family started camp in 1954. Very values focused; awards for values given. Do a lot of community service stuff at camp and emphasized. 95% Jewish according to Jeff; minimal candle lighting on Friday night. Socials during summer with girls camps in area. Can be given with Takajo, Manitou, Brant Lake, Winadu, Winaukee, Greylock and other boys camps.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Cedar in Casco, Maine is an all-boys, very sporty camp rooted in tradition and history. It runs a 7 week camp with 4/5 or 6 week options for flexibility (85% do 7 weeks), with the oldest campers going into 10th grade. Highlights include a beautiful lake with a big waterskiing program, extensive sports (lacrosse, basketball, tennis, soccer, hockey, baseball), outdoor skills and cooking, woodshop, rocketry, climbing/zip, and strong indoor and field facilities. Intercamps and tournaments (Takajo, Skylemar, Wildwood) and socials with nearby girls camps are part of the summer, and the community is values-focused and 95% Jewish with minimal candle lighting on Friday night.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Very sporty camp with multiple sports being focus including lacrosse, basketball, tennis, soccer, hockey and baseball.
  - Beautiful lake with waterskiing being a very big component and separate entry area dedicated to this.
  - Very rooted in tradition and history; his family started camp in 1954.
  - Jeff was a very big athlete and played soccer and lacrosse at Brown; he coached Lacrosse at Yale for many years.
  - Outdoor skills area and outdoor cooking; beautiful woodshop area overlooking lake with wood burning carving.
  - Wrestling room, batting cages, gaga courts, great fields, arts and crafts; special rocketry house; lots of basketball courts; indoor multipurpose facility.
  - Intercamps and some tournaments; up to 4th grade everyone participates; 5th and up A and B teams.
  - Bunks: 4 counselors and approx. 10 campers.
  - Milk and cookies daily.
  - Socials during summer with girls camps in area.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Unknown

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Camp Cedar Casco, Maine Toured on 7/6/23 by Liz Hochman Mak, Emily Rothenberg and Michele Gershwin. We toured with Jeff Hacker (owner/director). Met his sister Sue who also runs camp with him as well as his wife. His family started camp in 1954. Very rooted in tradition and history. -All boys. -Lots from Manhattan, Boston, CT, NY etc. -95% Jewish according to Jeff. Minimal candle lighting on Friday night. -7 week camp. However, can do 4/5 or 6 week option for flexibility. Jeff mentioned some campers need this for other commitments at home with other sports etc. However, 85% do 7 weeks. -Oldest campers-going into 10 th grade -Very sporty camp with multiple sports being focus including lacrosse, basketball, tennis, soccer, hockey and baseball. -Jeff was a very big athlete and played soccer and lacrosse at Brown. He coached Lacrosse at Yale for many years. -Beautiful lake with waterskiing being a very big component and separate entry area dedicated to this. -Outdoor skills area and outdoor cooking. -Beautiful woodshop area overlooking lake. Kids were making cutting boards and other great projects with wood burning carving as well. -Intercamps with camps such as Takajo, Skylemar, Wildwood. Some tournaments as well. -Bunks- 4 counselors and approx. 10 campers -Very values focused. Awards for values given. Do a lot of community service stuff at camp and emphasized. -Wrestling room, batting cages, gaga courts, great fields, arts and crafts. Special rocketry house. Lots of basketball courts -Milk and cookies daily. Jeff very happy to give us some of his cookies that he won’t share recipe -Nice frog pond where overnights often take place. -Intercamps-up to 4 th grade everyone participates and can go. 5 th and up will do A and B teams. -Nice zip line and climbing walls. -Indoor facility with basketball courts and kids were playing tennis, pickleball (multi purpose) -Socials during summer with girls camps in area. title: Androscoggin — Amanda — 7-22-22
text:
# Androscoggin

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Boys only uniform camp for ages 7-15 (into 10th grade last year of being a camper). Approx. 220 boys (smaller than they’ve been in the past). 90% return rate. Offer 4 & 7 weeks (mix bunks).

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Androscoggin - 7/7/22 Toured with Emily Salant, Wendy, Eliza and Joanne by Peter (owner). Roberta was giving a family tour. 50-60% campers from tri-state, other areas are: Philly, MA, MD/DC, FL, CA, Atlanta & TX. 80-90% Jewish. Female counselor assigned (in addition to male counselors) for 10yrs old and younger for wake up and bedtime but they do not sleep in the bunks.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

1 shower house, 15 yr olds have own showers. If you go to the same school/town then you are not in the same bunk.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront offered; details not specified.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 8-11yr old’s- 5 structured activities, 2 free periods. 12-13yr old’s- 3 structured AM activities, PM elective and 2 free periods. 14-15yr old’s- all elective.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Peter said a typical Andro boy wants to do sports 30-40% of the day but is well-rounded with other activities such as waterfront, trips, arts & crafts (black/white photography, ceramics, media center, riflery, archery, etc.). Intercamps with Manitou, Caribou & Cobbossee. Sister camps are Fernwood, Pinecliff, Tripp, Vega, Mataponi. You could send a boy who enjoys sports but it isn’t everything.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

4 RN’s, 1 doc (stay for 2-3 weeks), student nurses. Dining-one seating, mix up bunks then divisions, round tables.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Optional Friday night service with candle lighting (10 mins). Overall impression- camp was in good shape, boys were busy with activities, instruction seemed casual.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Boys only uniform camp for ages 7-15 (into 10th grade last year of being a camper) with approx. 220 boys and a 90% return rate. Peter said a typical Andro boy wants to do sports 30-40% of the day but is well-rounded with other activities such as waterfront, trips, arts & crafts (black/white photography, ceramics, media center, riflery, archery, etc.). Optional Friday night service with candle lighting (10 mins) and 80-90% Jewish. Overall impression- camp was in good shape, boys were busy with activities, instruction seemed casual; you could send a boy who enjoys sports but it isn’t everything.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Well-rounded program with sports 30-40% of the day and other activities including waterfront, trips, arts & crafts (black/white photography, ceramics, media center, riflery, archery).
  - Intercamps with Manitou, Caribou & Cobbossee; sister camps are Fernwood, Pinecliff, Tripp, Vega, Mataponi.
  - Approx. 220 boys with a 90% return rate.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Instruction seemed casual.
  - Approx. 220 boys (smaller than they’ve been in the past).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - MKN
  - Cedar
  - Skylemar
  - Moosilauke
  - Kingswood

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Androscoggin - 7/7/22 Toured with Emily Salant, Wendy, Eliza and Joanne by Peter (owner). Roberta was giving a family tour. - Boys only uniform camp for ages 7-15 (into 10th grade last year of being a camper). Approx. 220 boys (smaller than they’ve been in the past). 90% return rate. Offer 4 & 7 weeks (mix bunks). - 50-60% campers from tri-state, other areas are: Philly, MA, MD/DC, FL, CA, Atlanta & TX. If you go to the same school/town then you are not in the same bunk. -Optional Friday night service with candle lighting (10 mins). 80-90% Jewish - Programming: 8-11yr old’s- 5 structured activities, 2 free periods 12-13yr old’s- 3 structured AM activities, PM elective and 2 free periods 14-15yr old’s- all elective - Female counselor assigned (in addition to male counselors) for 10yrs old and younger for wake up and bedtime but they do not sleep in the bunks. - 1 shower house, 15 yr olds have own showers - 4 RN’s, 1 doc (stay for 2-3 weeks), student nurses - Dining-one seating, mix up bunks then divisions, round tables - Intercamps with Manitou, Caribou & Cobbossee. Sister camps are Fernwood, Pinecliff, Tripp, Vega, Mataponi - Peter said a typical Andro boy wants to do sports 30-40% of the day but is well-rounded with other activities such as waterfront, trips, arts & crafts (black/white photography, ceramics, media center, riflery, archery, etc. Overall impression- camp was in good shape, boys were busy with activities, instruction seemed casual. You could send a boy who enjoys sports but it isn’t everything. Could give with MKN, Cedar, Skylemar, Moosilauke & Kingswood \*For additional basic info reference the camp profile in the database -

title: Chantemerle — Julie — 2-16-23
text:
# Chantemerle

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Private boarding school in Switzerland. Offers residential and day summer camp options. Camp is for ages 8-16. Approximately 65 students. Camp begins on June 25, but they can build a custom program for students beginning on June 1. Many families opt for three weeks - minimum of two weeks - will make exceptions and allow a child to come for one week. 6-8 children per group.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Over 30 nationalities represented (few Americans). English main conversational language in the summer.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Three children per room - almost all rooms have ensuite bathrooms - three rooms have a shared bathroom on the floor - rooms do not have A/C.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Strong in outdoor adventures - afternoon activities - watersports - excursions to the mountains.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Typical day - four hours of coursework in the morning - afternoon sports and excursions (two choice activities) - evening recreational activities. Students choose academic courses - languages - French, German, or English - Technology and Innovation - drone creation - Mathematics - for French speakers - all classes are project based and hands-on learning.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Sports Academies - taught by professional athletes - off campus - busing to site and back for dinner and evening activity - most students choose this option for one week out of the three (can do more on request) - additional cost. Strong in outdoor adventures - afternoon activities - watersports - excursions to the mountains. Trip to an amusement park in Germany offered two times during the summer - no additional cost. School highlights that they offer a one-year French immersion class.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Kitchen staff can accommodate all dietary needs including Kosher. School nurse on site - partnership with Pediatric Emergency care nearby - General doctor near the school as well - families have the option of purchasing health insurance through the school - international health insurance may be sufficient but must check with provider. No Covid vaccine requirement.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Over 30 nationalities represented (few Americans). English main conversational language in the summer.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Pick up and drop off at Geneva airport. Cell phones allowed - used one hour before lunch and one hour before dinner. Sports Academies - additional cost. Trip to an amusement park in Germany - no additional cost.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Private boarding school in Switzerland offering residential and day summer camp options for ages 8-16, with approximately 65 students. Camp begins on June 25, but they can build a custom program for students beginning on June 1; many families opt for three weeks (minimum of two weeks; will make exceptions and allow a child to come for one week). Typical day: four hours of coursework in the morning (students choose languages—French, German, or English; Technology and Innovation—drone creation; Mathematics for French speakers; all classes are project based and hands-on learning), afternoon sports and excursions (two choice activities), and evening recreational activities. Over 30 nationalities represented (few Americans), English main conversational language in the summer; pick up and drop off at Geneva airport; cell phones allowed one hour before lunch and one hour before dinner.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Sports Academies taught by professional athletes; off campus with busing; most students choose this option for one week out of the three (can do more on request).
  - Strong in outdoor adventures, watersports, and excursions to the mountains.
  - Kitchen staff can accommodate all dietary needs including Kosher.
  - School nurse on site with nearby Pediatric Emergency care and a general doctor.
  - Trip to an amusement park in Germany offered two times during the summer at no additional cost.
  - English is the main conversational language in the summer with over 30 nationalities represented.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Rooms do not have A/C.
  - Three rooms have a shared bathroom on the floor.
  - Sports Academies carry an additional cost.
  - Few Americans.
  - Cell phones only allowed one hour before lunch and one hour before dinner.
  - No Covid vaccine requirement.

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Chantemerle • Private boarding school in Switzerland • Offers residential and day summer camp options • Camp is for ages 8-16 • Approximately 65 students • Camp begins on June 25 th, but they can build a custom program for students beginning on June 1 st . • Many families opt for three weeks -minimum of two weeks -will make exceptions and allow a child to come for one week • Pick up and drop off at Geneva airport • Three children per room -almost all room have ensuite bathrooms -three rooms have a shared bathroom on the floor -rooms do no have A/C • 6-8 children per group • Kitchen staff can accommodate all dietary needs including Kosher • School nurse on site -partnership with Pediatric Emergency care nearby -General doctor near the school as well -families have the option of purchasing health insurance through the school-international health insurance may be sufficient but must check with provider • Cell phones allowed- used one hour before lunch and one hour before dinner • Typical day -four hours of coursework in the morning -afternoon sports and excursions (two choice activities) -evening recreational activities • Students choose academic courses -languages- French, German, or English -Technology and Innovation-drone creation -Mathematics-for French speakers -all classes are project based and hands-on learning • Sports Academies -taught by professional athletes -off campus -busing to site and back for dinner and evening activity -most students choose this option for one week out of the three (can do more on request) -additional cost • Strong in outdoor adventures -afternoon activities -watersports -excursions to the mountains • Trip to an amusement park in Germany offered two times during the summer-no additional cost • Over 30 nationalities represented (few Americans) • English main conversational language in the summer • No Covid vaccine requirement • School highlights that they offer a one-year French immersion class

title: Adirondack Camp — Shair — 8-2-24
text:
# Adirondack Camp

  

## 1 · Program Snapshot

Adirondack Camp – Shari, Lisa B., Beth and Jennifer toured 11 July 2024—toured by Shauna and met w/ Rikki. Shauna took over the camp when her parents passed. She has a daughter in Seattle and a son in Maine who will hopefully be with her at camp in 2025. BASIC DATA – various session options; 2wks (limited), 3.5, 4, 6, 7. Three 2wk options, 245 campers 1st session, 180 2nd session. 230 is sweet spot, 245 is a little crowded. About 35-40 campers do full summer. Age range is 7-16. 17 y/o are CIT’s. About 20 scholarship campers each summer but she would like to have more.

  

## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Lot of adult staff who have been there a long time on leadership team. Youngest counselor is 18. About 12 staff members were former campers. 50% international staff. 60% staff return rate. About 50% of demographic is from tri-state area then from FL, TX, OH, etc. 20% international campers.

  

## 3 · Living Quarters

Open air structure with metal singles and bunk beds; live out of trunks. Mosquito nets provided and tarps roll down for rain. It is VERY breezy on their peninsula so it doesn’t get very buggy. 12-13 campers with 3 staff members per cabin. No electricity. Can rent linens if needed; also can rent trunks and plastic drawers beside bed if needed. Wash house near bunks, professionally cleaned. Laundry done on-site, 1x/wk, group sort. They keep cabins the same each year, depending on the break up/numbers and dynamics. Partial and full summer campers mixed throughout cabins. On duty is inside the bunks at bedtime.

  

## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

On a peninsula, 3 gorgeous lakefront areas: sailing, motorized boating, non-motorized boating. 1 swim period is mandatory daily, rotating through the swim components with strokes, snorkel, safety and life saving. No bathing suit policy, just appropriate for activity. Extensive sailing w/ regatta’s. Kayaks, canoes, SUP, Super SUP, windsurfing, wake surf, waterski, banana boat, knee board, wake board, fishing. No aqua park, it’s not their thing.

  

## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

5 periods in a day, 4 are choice (scheduled on Sundays). 1 swim period is mandatory daily. Color war activities are throughout the summer 2x/wk and then at the end of each session (same team for life with family members). There is an adventure period woven into campers’ schedules.

  

## 6 · Program Highlights

Most popular activities are culinary arts, waterskiing, sailing and rock climbing (these last 2 are double periods). SPORTS – some soccer, basketball, volleyball, fencing. Intercamps with Forest Lake in several sports. CREATIVE – new ceramics center (built in 2020) extensive w/ kick wheels, electric wheels and kiln. Glass blowing & fusing, jewelry making, sewing (hand and machines). Culinary bldg next to lake a bit small but kids were making perogi’s for example and seemed to enjoy the activity. A local guest artist comes in each week and teaches 2-4 classes (ie: magician) or also can be in culinary (cheese making) dance (hip hop teacher). No performing arts shows except Friday night talent shows 2x/session and some improv. Music lessons in guitar and drums some weeks and rock bands are formed. STEAM/HIGH TECH – digital photo & video. TRIPS – lots of trips, chosen in advance when at camp (a few days before) and they partake in the LG5, 46ers club and Fire Towers in the area. There is an adventure period woven into campers schedules that could be circus acts, random acts of kindness around camp and lots more. Water sports, outdoor adventure and creative seem to be the main focus at AC.

  

## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – a/c in health center, 3 nurses at a time, 1 dr not always on camp but if needed urgent care at Ticonderoga UVM 9 miles away. Cannot take a diabetic. Adult on staff, Rachel, for emotional/social/mental well-being. RELIGIOUS LIFE – nothing formal, have all types of families, can transport to church on Sundays if needed. DINING – traditional looking w/ rafters, flags. Lovely, cozy feel. Sit by cabin, except for BBQ lunch and dinners they can sit anywhere. One seating, round & rectangular tables, family style and buffet sometimes. “Killer salad bar,” nut aware, can handle all allergies, fruit out all day. No canteen. Cookie break in afternoon.

  

## 8 · Vibe and Culture

AC is a GREAT camp w/ a glamping feel. Rustic but comfortable. Traditional-looking dining hall with rafters and flags; lovely, cozy feel. Color war activities throughout the summer, same team for life with family members. Friday night talent shows 2x/session and some improv. Rock bands are formed. Beach party for campers who don’t have parents who come (international or west coast, etc). Water sports, outdoor adventure and creative seem to be the main focus at AC.

  

## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Parents mostly drive campers up but have buses for 1st session only from Port Authority in NYC (about 30-40 campers) and makes stops along the way. Airport pick ups at Logan, Newark, Albany & Burlington. Visiting day at the end of 3 wks. COMMUNICATION – no calls home for 1st week then use judgement after that; birthday phone calls definitely. No electronics. Packages ok but no food. Weekly videos sent to parents and camper schedules. Letter writing is encouraged not required. Post pictures daily w/ facial recognition. Extra costs: store account $60 for sundries & camp paraphernalia, Ottawa trip (not a lot of kids), glass blowing, WW rafting trip, WW kayaking trip.

  

## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Adirondack Camp sits on a breezy peninsula with three gorgeous lakefront areas and a strong emphasis on water sports, outdoor adventure, and creative arts. Sessions range from limited 2-week options to 3.5, 4, 6, and 7 weeks, with about 230 campers as the sweet spot and ages 7–16 (17-year-old CITs). Campers choose four of five daily periods (one mandatory swim), with color war twice weekly and at each session’s end, and robust trips around the Adirondacks. The vibe is rustic but comfortable—almost a glamping feel—with nut-aware dining, no electronics, and thoughtful communication to parents.

  

### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - On a peninsula with 3 gorgeous lakefront areas; extensive sailing with regatta’s.
  - 5 periods/day with 4 choice (scheduled on Sundays); mandatory daily swim.
  - Strong creative program: new ceramics center (2020), glass blowing & fusing, jewelry, sewing.
  - Lots of trips (LG5, 46ers club, Fire Towers) and an adventure period woven into schedules.
  - “Killer salad bar,” nut aware, can handle all allergies; fruit out all day.
  - Adult mental health support (Rachel) on staff; a/c in health center; urgent care 9 miles away.
  - Internationally diverse campers (20%) and staff (50%); many long-tenured adult leaders.
  - Robust parent comms (weekly videos, daily photos with facial recognition); no electronics.
  - Buses for 1st session from Port Authority; airport pickups at Logan, Newark, Albany & Burlington.
  - Glamping feel—rustic but comfortable.

  

#### Lowlights

  - Cannot take a diabetic.
  - No aqua park.
  - No performing arts shows (only Friday night talent shows 2x/session and some improv).
  - Culinary building next to the lake is a bit small.
  - No electricity in cabins.
  - 1 doctor not always on camp (uses nearby urgent care).
  - At 245 campers it can feel a little crowded; 230 is the sweet spot.
  - Buses offered only for 1st session.
  - No canteen (store account covers sundries).

  

#### Similar Camps

  - Birch Rock
  - Netop
  - Winona
  - Wyonegonic
  - Runoia

  

## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

She has a daughter in Seattle and a son in Maine who will hopefully be with her at camp in 2025. BASIC DATA –various session options; 2wks (limited), 3.5, 4, 6, 7. Three 2wk options, 245 campers 1 st session, 180 2nd session. 230 is sweet spot, 245 is a little crowded. About 35-40 campers do full summer. Age range is 7-16. 17 y/o are CIT’s. Lot of adult staff who have been there a long time on leadership team. About 50% of demographic is from tri-state area then from FL, TX, OH, etc. Airport pick ups at Logan, Newark, Albany & Burlington. 20% international campers. Youngest counselor is 18. About 12 staff members were former campers. 50% international staff. 60% staff return rate. No general counselors, all are activity counselors. Visiting day at the end of 3 wks. Beach party for campers who don’t have parents who come (int’l or west coast, etc). Extra costs: store account $60 for sundries & camp paraphernalia, Ottowa trip (not a lot of kids), glass blowing, WW rafting trip, WW kayaking trip. PROGRAMMING – 5 periods in a day, 4 are choice (scheduled on Sundays). Color war activities are throughout the summer 2x/wk and then at the end of each session. Same team for life with family members. Most popular activities are culinary arts, waterskiing, sailing and rock climbing (these last 2 are double periods). MEDICAL – a/c in health center, 3 nurses at a time, 1 dr not always on camp but if needed urgent care at Ticonderoga UVM 9 miles away. Cannot take a diabetic. Adult on staff, Rachel, for emotional/social/mental well-being. COMMUNICATION-no calls home for 1 st week then use judgement after that, birthday phone calls definitely, no electronics, packages ok but no food. Weekly videos sent to parents and camper schedules. Letter writing is encouraged not required, post pictures daily w/ facial recognition. RELIGIOUS LIFE –nothing formal, have all types of families, can transport to church on Sundays if needed. One seating, round & rectangular tables, family style and buffet sometimes. “Killer salad bar,” nut aware, can handle all allergies, fruit out all day. No canteen. Cookie break in afternoon. ACCOMODATIONS – open air structure with metal singles and bunk beds, live out of trunks, mosquito nets provided and tarps roll down for rain.
