title: Encore Coda — Pilar — 8-23-22
text:
# Encore Coda



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Music camp with traditional camp experience. Originally an all boys camp in the 1900’s and shut down in late 1920’s. Ruth and Phil Saltman started camp Encore at their home in the 1950s with 2 students the first summer. In 1960 they purchased the current Encore Coda site and made a boys camp with music and traditional camp activities. In 1969 it was made coed and changed its name to Camp Encore Coda. Their son Jamie was born into camp and continued the legacy with his wife Ellen along with Cara Bergantino, who attended camp for several years as a camper, CIT, counselor, Head Counselor and Assistant Director, and was passed on the ownership and directorship in 2021. We met her briefly during our visit. https://encorecoda.com/elements/pages/camp-history-page/ Sessions 2, 3, 6 weeks most common is 3 weeks. Also offer a staccato session for first time campers which is 2 weeks for finishing 3rd and 4th grade. They get an introduction to camp. Grouped by finishing grade. Lower camp is 3rd to 6th, upper camp is 7th to 11th grade. Encore Coda Karen, Monica, Aline and Pilar V. August 1st, 2022.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by Director Andrew Neyer, who studied at Miami of Ohio, and met Nathan Hubbard, Director of music. Instructors They are grad students or professional musicians and music teachers. Most from the Boston and NY area. Counselors Almost all study music education or performance. They stay in the cabins with the campers. Some cabin counselors teach, but not very common. The ideal number is 120 students, this session they have 80 kids. They have fluctuated between 90 and 110 in the past few years. Usually 50% boys and girls. Return rate is around 65% to 70%. The vast majority of campers have to come from New England, but really come from all over. Before covid they had a few international campers. This summer they only have a few international campers.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks Size varies from 4 to 10 campers and 2 to 3 counselors. Cubbies for clothes. Linens and towels provided if needed at no charge. Girls cabins have bathrooms with showers. Boys cabins have shower houses with individual stalls and changing areas shared by 3 cabins. Boys and girls in different parts of camp. First year with a gender extensive cabin. The family needs to request to be in that cabin. In this session 8 in the cabin, of lower and intermediate age group, so they accommodated them in a cabin that has 2 sides to it with a shared bathroom. Counselors are also gender extensive and have helped kids throughout the sessions.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake Not private but feels like it because not much activity going on. Paddleboarding, canoeing, kayaking, swimming. Swimming instruction is compulsory for lower campers, once a day 5 days a week.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule Kids can build their schedule depending on what they are looking for. Some younger campers can be exploring music and playing an instrument for the first time, so they spend 50% of their time at music activities and the rest of the time at other activities. 8 periods a day, 4 need to be music. The other 4 they can choose the other electives. They also have practice sessions. They sign up to practice at individual studios, and counselors go around to check on them. Campers get one free period to relax and do an activity of their choice. Activities Campers choose in advance their non music activities. They have a Monday through Friday schedule. They can change their activities at any time during the session. Paddleboarding, canoeing, kayaking, swimming, tennis, basketball, ping pong, soccer, arts and crafts. On Saturdays and Sundays they have fun days. It used to be trip days to parks at beaches. Now that they stay on campus they have different activities like amazing race, capture the flag and other fun activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Instruments Most kids bring their instruments, when possible. They have all the electronic equipment they need. May rent instruments if needed. Lots of studios around camp for private and group practice. Singing, classical, theater, Jazz, all types. They have the opportunity to play in a variety of groups. Rock group, jazz combo, ensembles in general. Have had private opera lessons but will not do full opera productions. For theater they offer a musical, one for the lower campers and one for the upper campers. Lower campers were practicing for Shrek. Instruction Combos are put together based on experience, ability and age. All campers send in recordings of themselves so they can get an idea of where they are musically. Then, when they arrive they meet with their private instructors for a one on one session to see how they are doing. For younger campers they have introduction sessions with smaller groups. No audition needed to enter the camp. 1 hour of private instruction a week on their primary instrument and ½ an hour on their secondary instrument if they have one. 1 ½ hour of private instruction if they only have one instrument. Conservatory program A more advanced program. Mostly High school students preparing for college auditions. They need to audition in order to get into the program. If they don’t get in they can fill up their entire schedule with music in the camp program. 2 hour of private instruction a week on their primary instrument and 1 hour on their secondary instrument if they have one. Every week they have a campfire and on the last day of camp they all get together and perform. Next year they are planning to build an outdoor performance theater for outdoor concerts.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health 1 day nurse and 1 night nurse. Hospital 10 minutes away. No covid cases during camp sessions. They tested everyone on day 5 and went back to normal. We were asked to wear masks and saw kids wearing them because a counselor was exposed. They will test everyone again on the 5th day. No guard at the gate. Dining Overlooking the lake. Some tables outside. They sit by cabin. On special days they have buddy brunch, and the senior campers are paired up with the junior campers and form buddy groups, and will sit together. Intermediate campers have open seating on those days. They offer vegan, gluten free, dairy free options prepared in a separate part of the kitchen. Served at a separate part of the dining hall. The kitchen is nut free but they offer regular peanut butter at a separate table.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Music accompanied us throughout the tour. We even had the chance to hear a rock band practice and saxophone quartet practicing outside and lower camp children practicing for Shrek. Non denominational. Kids who want to attend services can be taken into town. Dog.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation Bus from Boston at the beginning of each session. Van picks up from Boston Logan. Phone calls are on special days with their own sim cards. Campers are allowed phones without SIM cards because they might have music on them. Most kids bring their instruments, when possible. May rent instruments if needed.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Encore Coda is a music camp with traditional camp experience on a lake, with sessions of 2, 3, or 6 weeks (most common is 3 weeks), plus a 2-week staccato session for first-time campers finishing 3rd and 4th grade. Kids can build their schedule with 8 periods a day (4 need to be music) plus practice sessions, a free period, and non-music activities like paddleboarding, canoeing, kayaking, swimming, tennis, basketball, ping pong, soccer, and arts and crafts. Instruction is robust (1 hour of private instruction a week on the primary instrument, plus combos and ensembles) with no audition needed to enter the camp, and there is also a Conservatory program for more advanced high school students preparing for college auditions. Bunks range from 4 to 10 campers with 2 to 3 counselors, dining overlooks the lake with strong dietary accommodations, 1 day nurse and 1 night nurse with a hospital 10 minutes away, and transportation includes a bus from Boston and a van pickup from Boston Logan.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Music camp with traditional camp experience.
  - Kids can build their schedule; 8 periods a day, 4 need to be music.
  - Strong private instruction and ensembles; no audition needed to enter the camp.
  - Conservatory program for advanced high school students.
  - Lots of studios around camp for private and group practice; electronic equipment available; may rent instruments if needed.
  - Gender extensive cabin option with counselor support.
  - Dining overlooks the lake; vegan, gluten free, dairy free options prepared in a separate part of the kitchen; nut free kitchen with peanut butter at a separate table.
  - 1 day nurse and 1 night nurse; hospital 10 minutes away.
  - Transportation: Bus from Boston; van pickup from Boston Logan.
  - Weekly campfire and end-of-camp performances.
  - Return rate is around 65% to 70%.



#### Lowlights

  - No guard at the gate.
  - Lake not private (though it feels like it).
  - Boys cabins have shower houses (shared by 3 cabins).
  - Enrollment this session 80 vs ideal number 120; fluctuated between 90 and 110 in the past few years.
  - Few international campers this summer.
  - Phone calls are on special days; campers are allowed phones without SIM cards.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Arrow Wood — Allison — 7-13-23
text:
# Arrow Wood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Co-ed Sports Focused Camp. Second Summer, last year had 36 kids for 3 weeks, have 72 enrollments this year. Could hold up to 225 campers once everything is renovated. Had an Explorer Day with five campers. Will do tours. Arrow Wood is built on the grounds of the former Camp Allegra, that was there for 50 years (1930's-1980's) then was Lakeside Christian Retreat until COVID.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Allow kids up to entering junior year. At 17 they become junior counselors (if they are right for the job) and they get paid. Most kids are from NY, NJ, CT, MA; six or seven from FL. Toured by Matt Linnick (Went to Michigan, wife is Stacy (with an "e") & Stacey Decter (went to college at IU with Alyssa at Winadu and is a good friend). Matt lives in the first building called "the big house." The other end is used as housing for group leaders and Directors.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The camp then opens up to the bunks formed in a "U" overlooking the stunning lake (technically a pond) which is large and clear. Boys bunks are on one side of U and girls on the other. Eventually they renovate and build a true "girl's side." Have renovated enough bunks to house their current campers. Bunks have jack and jill style bathrooms - 3 sinks, 3 toilets, 3 showers and cubbies for clothes. Large fans and new windows as well as breeze off the lake, keep it cool. Dining hall overlooks the lake and is decorated with "Wall of Champion's" banners, and kids eat with their bunk.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake (technically a pond) is large and clear. Lake has a motorboat, inflatables, sailing, paddleboard, fishing & kayak.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Day starts with whole camp 15-minute warm-up. Then there are 4 periods plus swim. First period is 90-minute intensive then they are cut to 60 minutes. Kids pick schedule and it is posted daily at the canteen. Canteen is also a cafe-style game room. Creative arts is simple (paint, clay, tie dye). Do inter-camps with Grey Lock and Winadoo. One trip a session to Basketball Hall of Fame and Six Flags.



## 6 · Program Highlights

When you enter camp you are greeted by brand new lit fields and courts. On the left is a large building that was the chapel and they've turned it into a weight center/gym on the first floor and sports marketing and media upstairs. Kids actually make most of their videos. There is a "fun zone" with gaga pit, pop a shots and corn hole. Hiking trail through the woods has a bridge over a stream (which they control the dam for, so no risk of flooding). Trail leads to future girl's side site and camp fire. There is a rock climbing wall and they will expand upon the Outdoor Adventure offerings. Have area in woods where they hold meditation day and yoga. Offer mountain biking and cross country. There is also a huge lakeside theater that they can use for indoor sports, to show movies, and campers wanted a talent show so they held one there.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health Center is run by a Nurse who lives on site and a Doctor of Sports Medicine is on call and only two miles away. They are 12 miles from an Urgent Care and Berkshire Medical is nearest hospital. An Athletic Trainer is also at camp all summer. Have cereal machines and are nut aware, not nut-free but can handle allergies and dietary restrictions.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Arrow Wood is built on the grounds of the former Camp Allegra, that was there for 50 years (1930's-1980's) then was Lakeside Christian Retreat until COVID. There is even a story about a mobster coming up to hide there. Kids actually make most of their videos. Campers wanted a talent show so they held one there.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Will do a visiting day at 3 weeks for kids who are there all summer and it's pick-up day for kids who are leaving after 3 weeks. Did have a van to camp from Westchester. Most kids are from NY, NJ, CT, MA; six or seven from FL. Will do tours. Had an Explorer Day with five campers. Kids get candy a few times a week. Only post photos of kids on Instagram or Facebook.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Co-ed Sports Focused Camp built on the grounds of the former Camp Allegra (1930's-1980's) and later Lakeside Christian Retreat. Second Summer: last year had 36 kids for 3 weeks and have 72 enrollments this year, with capacity up to 225 once everything is renovated. Bunks form a "U" overlooking a stunning lake with motorboat, inflatables, sailing, paddleboard, fishing & kayak, and kids pick schedule with 4 periods plus swim. Health Center is run by a Nurse on site with a Doctor of Sports Medicine on call two miles away, they are nut aware (not nut-free) and can handle allergies and dietary restrictions, and they post photos only on Instagram or Facebook.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Brand new lit fields and courts; weight center/gym and sports marketing and media upstairs where kids make most of their videos.
  - Stunning, clear lake with motorboat, inflatables, sailing, paddleboard, fishing & kayak.
  - Outdoor Adventure elements (hiking trail with bridge, rock wall, mountain biking, cross country, meditation and yoga areas).
  - Huge lakeside theater for indoor sports, movies, and talent show.
  - Health Center with on-site Nurse, Athletic Trainer all summer, and a Sports Medicine Doctor on call two miles away.



#### Lowlights

  - Creative arts is simple (paint, clay, tie dye).
  - Not nut-free (nut aware).
  - Still renovating and will eventually build a true "girl's side."
  - Early-stage growth (Second Summer; 36 campers last year, 72 enrollments this year).



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

There is even a story about a mobster coming up to hide there. \> When you enter camp you are greeted by brand new lit fields and courts. Matt lives in the first building called "the big house." On the left is a large building that was the chapel and they've turned it into a weight center/gym on the first floor and sports marketing and media upstairs. Kids actually make most of their videos. The other end is used as housing for group leaders and Directors. There is a "fun zone" with gaga pit, pop a shots and corn hole. title: WILDWOOD — WENDY — 10-10-23
text:
# WILDWOOD



## 1 · Program Snapshot

When he took over the camp had 89 boys. This summer they had 174. They could grow a little more, but don't see themselves going over 200, as they like the intimate size and really knowing their boys. There are about 25-30 boys in each age group. A large part of the program is elective based. Waterfront and tennis are always in the schedule (no opting out of those). He noted that they have gradually moved toward a slightly more well rounded program, whereas 10 years ago their campers were all about sports and competing. Wildwood offers the partial summer option (ideally the first year only but will allow after that). About 15% of boys did the partial summer last year.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Louis Kail (owner/director) spoke with us. Louis himself was a Wildwood camper, then counselor and took over the camp 7 years ago. He and his wife Chelsea (both educators) live in Westchester with their 3 young boys (one is now a Wildwood camper). Louis co-directs with Teddy Friedman (also was a former Wildwood camper) who is also an educator and basketball coach and has worked with several NYC private schools. Geographic pull - When Louis took over, it was predominantly NY & Boston. They still pull from there, but have a good amount from Florida, San Diego, San Fran, Mexico City. Chaperoned flight for FL.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Shower House for all campers (each age group has their own).



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront is always in the schedule (no opting out of those).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

A large part of the program is elective based, so campers could play with older or younger group on occasion, depending on their interests and sports they're choosing. Waterfront and tennis are always in the schedule (no opting out of those) but the other sports and activities are selected. Louis likes empowering them to choose, and finds that they don't just stick to the same two sports they play at home - rather, they try what their friends are enjoying, or try something new because they feel comfortable.



## 6 · Program Highlights

To appeal to boys who have a range of interests, they've recently added: new ceramics studio, percussion and guitar, podcast interviews and photography. Wildwood posts photos daily for parents, and some of the photos are taken by campers themselves. Arts & Crafts - pretty traditional (lanyard, drawing, tie dying). Evening Activities - sometimes whole camp, sometimes by age group, or other groupings - examples are casino night, hypnotist, talent shows, night leagues for sports, and occasional socials with nearby girls camps. They see PINECLIFFE the most, since their sibling overlap is biggest there. Also get together with FERNWOOD and MATAPONI and potentially other girls camps depending on the year. Color War is a 4 day event at the end of camp - "Blue & Gray", with different themes each year.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical - Full time doctor who is there 24/7, her own son is a camper there. 4-5 nurses round the clock. 10 minutes to urgent care in Bridgton ME. Dining Hall handles virtually any special diet or allergy, and is nut aware. Focus on variety, and balanced meals. They offer complete salad bar at every lunch and dinner. Sit by bunk with counselors, family style. Wednesdays = Cookout nights.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Louis described "The Wildwood Way" as a safe environment that supports the following: self discovery, connections, confidence, challenge. They like the intimate size and really knowing their boys. They have gradually moved toward a slightly more well rounded program, whereas 10 years ago their campers were all about sports and competing.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Chaperoned flight for FL. Wildwood posts photos daily for parents (some taken by campers). Extra Costs = Ice hockey program (2x per week off site), Tennis Plus (extra privates), Golf Plus (at 9 hole course) and All Day Rock Climbing Trip. Bus and canteen are included in tuition. No uniform and very few shirts/logo items required.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Wildwood is an intimate, elective-based boys camp that grew from 89 to 174 campers and does not see itself going over 200. Waterfront and tennis are always in the schedule (no opting out of those), with a gradually more well rounded program that now includes a new ceramics studio, percussion and guitar, podcast interviews, photography, and pretty traditional Arts & Crafts. Evenings bring casino night, hypnotist, talent shows, night leagues, occasional socials with nearby girls camps, and a 4 day Color War at the end of camp called "Blue & Gray". Families will appreciate the full-time doctor and 4-5 nurses, nut aware dining with salad bar, daily photos, chaperoned flight for FL, and that bus and canteen are included; partial summer is an option (about 15% did it last year).



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Intimate size and really knowing their boys; about 25-30 boys in each age group; don't see themselves going over 200.
  - A large part of the program is elective based.
  - Waterfront and tennis are always in the schedule (no opting out of those).
  - Gradually moved toward a slightly more well rounded program.
  - New ceramics studio, percussion and guitar, podcast interviews and photography.
  - Arts & Crafts - pretty traditional (lanyard, drawing, tie dying).
  - Color War is a 4 day event at the end of camp - "Blue & Gray", with different themes each year.
  - Wildwood posts photos daily for parents (some taken by campers).
  - Evening Activities: casino night, hypnotist, talent shows, night leagues for sports, and occasional socials with PINECLIFFE, FERNWOOD and MATAPONI.
  - Medical: Full time doctor 24/7 and 4-5 nurses; 10 minutes to urgent care in Bridgton ME.
  - Dining Hall handles virtually any special diet or allergy and is nut aware; complete salad bar at every lunch and dinner; Wednesdays = Cookout nights.
  - Geographic pull includes NY & Boston plus Florida, San Diego, San Fran, Mexico City; chaperoned flight for FL.
  - Bus and canteen are included in tuition. No uniform and very few shirts/logo items required.
  - Partial summer option (about 15% did the partial summer last year).



#### Lowlights

  - Extra Costs = Ice hockey program (2x per week off site), Tennis Plus (extra privates), Golf Plus (at 9 hole course) and All Day Rock Climbing Trip.
  - Shower House for all campers (each age group has their own).
  - Partial summer option ideally the first year only (will allow after that).



#### Similar Camps

  - Other boys camps in Maine
  - BRANT LAKE (it's a more intimate size and offers more choice than BLC to younger boys)
  - MKN
  - Greylock
  - Wayne for Boys



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Louis Kail (owner/director) spoke with us. Louis himself was a Wildwood camper, then counselor and took over the camp 7 years ago. He and his wife Chelsea (both educators) live in Westchester with their 3 young boys (one is now a Wildwood camper) Louis Co Directs with Teddy Friedman (also was a former Wildwood camper) who is also an educator and basketball coach and has worked with several NYC private schools. When he took over the camp had 89 boys. This summer they had 174. They could grow a little more, but don’t see themselves going over 200, as they like the intimate size and really knowing their boys. There are about 25- 30 boys in each age group. Waterfront and tennis are always in the schedule (no opting out of those) but the other sports and activities are selected. Louis likes empowering them to choose, and finds that they don’t just stick to the same two sports they play at home- rather, they try what their friends are enjoying, or try something new because they feel comfortable. He noted that they have gradually moved toward a slightly more “well rounded” program, whereas 10 years ago their campers were all about sports and competing. To appeal to boys who have a range of interests, they’ve recently added : new ceramics studio, percussion and guitar, podcast interviews and photography. Wildwood posts photos daily for parents, and some of the photos are taken by campers themselves. Arts & Crafts- pretty traditional (lanyard, drawing, tie dying) Evening Activities- sometimes whole camp, sometimes by age group, or other groupings- examples are casino night, hypnotist, talent shows, night leagues for sports, and occasional socials with nearby girls camps- They see PINECLIFFE the most, since their sibling overlap is biggest there. Also get together with FERNWOOD and MATAPONI and potentially other girls camps depending on the year. Color War is a 4 day event at the end of camp- “Blue & Gray”, different themes each year. Louis described “The Wildwood Way” as a safe environment that supports the following: - self discovery - connections -confidence - challenge Wildwood offers the partial summer option (ideally the first year only but will allow after that). About 15% of boys did the partial summer last year. Geographic pull- When Louis took over, it was predominantly NY & Boston. They still pull from there, but have a good amount from Florida, San Diego, San Fran, Mexico City. Chaperoned flight for FL. Medical- Full time doctor who is there 24/7, her own son is a camper there. 4-5 nurses round the clock. 10 minutes to urgent care in Bridgton ME. Dining Hall handles virtually any special diet or allergy, and is “nut aware”. Focus on variety, and balanced meals. They offer complete salad bar at every lunch and dinner. Sit by bunk with counselors, family style. Wednesdays = Cookout nights. -Shower House for all campers (each age group has their own) - Extra Costs = Ice hockey program (2x per week off site) , Tennis Plus (extra privates) Golf Plus (at 9 hole course) and All Day Rock Climbing Trip. - Bus and canteen are included in tuition. No uniform and very few shirts/logo items required. Who would I give WILDOOWD with? Other boys camps in Maine, BRANT LAKE (it’s a more intimate size and offers more choice than BLC to younger boys). Might give with MKN or Greylock or Wayne for Boys.

title: WAYNE FOR BOYS — MICHELE — 7-1-24
text:
# WAYNE FOR BOYS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Brother/sister with Wayne for Girls. Impressive fields everywhere! Very spread out. Also really great skate park and ninja park. Basketball courts are spread out all around camp.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Josh seems to have a wonderful, working relationship with his cousins Matt and Adena at Wayne for Girls. Bunks: 4 counselors in youngest bunks, 3 male and 1 female. The female obviously doesn’t sleep in bunk. “A Mom” is in charge of every age group, but after 4th grade there is no longer the extra female counselor for the bunk. They do have 8-10 fully scholarship kids a year that integrate beautifully.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Towel Service – for pool and bunks. Love the porches that are on every bunk and boys store all of their sports equipment out there. Bunks are separated into 3 areas, skid row for oldest campers 8th-10th, then area for middle camp, and different for lower. Only counselors have bunk beds.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake (same as girls side): beautiful for PA! They have all motorized boats and other lake toys/boats and fishing. Instructional swim takes place in the pool.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: structured for youngest campers with one elective a day, then older campers more electives with oldest have all elective periods. 1 period a day is league conference. They partake in intercamp games and everyone makes a team, except tennis and golf there are try outs. Boys can take private lessons in any sport they want and parents pay the coaches directly.



## 6 · Program Highlights

New high ropes course – lots of adventure course action with high/low ropes. Youngest bunks do a camp out. They do have a nice golf range and chipping field and take boys to a local golf course. Trips: 2nd-4th graders take 3-4 day trips, 5th grade and older all do overnight trips, 10th grade goes to Cleveland, 11th grade CA for a week. Impressive fields everywhere! Also really great skate park and ninja park. Basketball courts are spread out all around camp.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: Friday night they have family style dinner and say the blessings. Food usually served buffet style. They have lots of options with salad bar/pasta bar/sandwich bar. They consider themselves nut sensitive only for when off camp but in camp they are nut free. They feed the boys often! Mid morning snack, mid afternoon snack, and they do monitor the “junk”. New upgrades for future – new health center and solar paneling. They also have a school psychologist on staff as mental health is a big priority.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Wayne for Boys does not present as “pretty” as the Girls camp, but it’s also a boys camp. No religious services and focus on leadership values. They have a wonderful leadership recognition program. Brother/sister with Wayne for Girls. Older kids get together more with evening activities and they partake in a big carnival together. Brothers/sisters/cousins see each other twice a week.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses: West Chester, Long Island, Philly, with chaperoned flight from Florida and LA. Boys can take private lessons in any sport they want and parents pay the coaches directly. They do have 8-10 fully scholarship kids a year that integrate beautifully. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs beyond lessons: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Josh describes a Wayne boy as someone who likes to play a lot of sports, but doesn’t have to be super sporty. Brother/sister with Wayne for Girls, with older kids getting together more with evening activities and a big carnival, and brothers/sisters/cousins see each other twice a week. Impressive fields, a new high ropes course with high/low ropes, a beautiful-for-PA lake with all motorized boats, and really great skate and ninja parks stand out. Programming is structured for youngest campers with one elective a day and oldest have all elective periods, with leadership values, a wonderful leadership recognition program, and a school psychologist on staff as mental health is a big priority.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Towel Service – for pool and bunks
  - New high ropes course – lots of adventure course action with high/low ropes.
  - Impressive fields everywhere!
  - Also really great skate park and ninja park.
  - Basketball courts are spread out all around camp.
  - They do have a nice golf range and chipping field and take boys to a local golf course
  - Lake (same as girls side): beautiful for PA!
  - They have all motorized boats and other lake toys/boats and fishing.
  - Friday night they have family style dinner and say the blessings.
  - They have lots of options with salad bar/pasta bar/sandwich bar.
  - They feed the boys often! Mid morning snack, mid afternoon snack, and they do monitor the “junk”
  - They have a wonderful leadership recognition program.
  - They also have a school psychologist on staff as mental health is a big priority
  - Brother/sister with Wayne for Girls. Older kids get together more with evening activities and they partake in a big carnival together.
  - Buses: West Chester, Long Island, Philly, with chaperoned flight from Florida and LA.
  - They do have 8-10 fully scholarship kids a year that integrate beautifully
  - New upgrades for future – new health center and solar paneling



#### Lowlights

  - Wayne for Boys does not present as “pretty” as the Girls camp, but it’s also a boys camp
  - Very spread out.
  - After 4th grade there is no longer the extra female counselor for the bunk.
  - Except tennis and golf there are try outs.
  - Parents pay the coaches directly.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Blue Ridge-Equinunk — Lindsey — 10-2-23
text:
# Blue Ridge/Equinunk



## 1 · Program Snapshot

The guard gate serves as the entrance to the camp. During the day, the camp runs as two separate entities, each with its own philosophy. Every evening, campers come together for coed activities, with more opportunities as they grow older. Campers can easily walk back and forth between the two sides of the camp. A five-week program is offered as an option for younger children. Each age group consists of 4-5 bunks. New campers are distributed among 5th-grade bunks. The facilities are top notch. All facilities are well-lit for nighttime activities.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

There are 330 boys and 370 girls. The majority of campers come from the Northeastern United States. Out of the 60 general counselors at Camp Equinunk, 40 are former campers. Up to 5th grade, boys have women as group leaders and camp moms. No more than two campers from the same school are placed in the same bunk. Camp staff generally don't go back and forth between the boys' and girls' sides. There is an increasing number of kids entering 2nd grade. Equinunk Lindsey and Emily we toured by Stacy.



## 3 · Living Quarters

There is a shared bathroom between the two sides of each bunk, ensuring convenience for all campers. There is towel service, although campers need to bring their own sheets. Each age group consists of 4-5 bunks. No more than two campers from the same school are placed in the same bunk. The camp's garage doors can be raised and lowered to accommodate varying weather conditions.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The boys have a big slide to the pool, while the girls have a slide to the lake, and they switch. Campers have instructional swim lessons six days a week. The camp features a heated saltwater pool and a portion of the lake has an aluminum base for added enjoyment during water activities, including tubing and a water park. There's no uniform swimwear requirement, but boys and girls will be at the waterfront/pool at different times. The boys and girls share facilities for the hobby center, gymnastics and waterfront activities, including the ski dock.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

The daily schedule consists of six periods, with the lower camp focused on structured activities. Campers travel to activities within their division. Mini-camp spans three mornings, allowing campers to engage in the same activities consecutively. Private lessons are available at no extra cost.



## 6 · Program Highlights

The hobby center is divided with boys using it in the morning and girls in the afternoon, offering various activities in separate bays, including cave exploration, ninja warrior, STEAM, and cooking. The hobby center also features a zoo. There is a rock climbing cave and a ninja warrior course. There is an air-conditioned coed movie theater. Golf is only offered at Camp Equinunk. There is a putting green and a driving range. The sports are very strong. Campers are coached by professional coaches, often at the college or high school level. There is a dedicated whiffle ball stadium. There is a broadcast media program. A sports canteen is available for watching games. Blue Ridge boasts a beautiful new welcome center and includes a new area known as "woods," providing additional outdoor social space for campers. Plays for campers in 5th grade and above are coed, offering opportunities for collaborative performances. Camp Blue Ridge has "The Ridge," a fine arts facility where guest artists visit weekly to provide instruction. The facility includes a beautiful dance studio as well as the canteen. There is also cooking and they make cookbooks each summer, featuring recipes prepared by the campers themselves. A fire pit provides a cozy gathering spot. Additional facilities include another arts and crafts area and a spacious gymnastics center, which tends to attract lots of girls during rest hour for some extra fun.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The guard gate serves as the entrance to the camp. All facilities are well-lit for nighttime activities. The camp's dining hall offers a family-style dining experience, complete with plenty of cheering. The lower camp heads to lunch 15 minutes before the older kids to make sure they have time to eat. Boys visit the girls' area for canteen, which is not focused on candy.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

During the day, the camp runs as two separate entities, each with its own philosophy, and every evening campers come together for coed activities, with more opportunities as they grow older. Campers can easily walk back and forth between the two sides of the camp. Lots of singing in the dining hall! They provide songbooks. Color war activities for the boys and girls are completely separate. Plays for campers in 5th grade and above are coed, offering opportunities for collaborative performances. A fire pit provides a cozy gathering spot.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Trips are organized separately, except for the California trip, which is for campers entering 10th and 11th grade. On Sundays before dinner, siblings and cousins have a 45-minute opportunity to see each other. Additionally, every other Tuesday, there is a 45-minute session for siblings to spend time together. Campers are required to wear a full uniform, except during socials. Younger campers are limited to five non-uniformed outfits per summer. There is towel service, although campers need to bring their own sheets. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown. Travel logistics beyond the above trips and sibling visits: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Blue Ridge/Equinunk runs as two separate camps by day with shared coed traditions and activities in the evenings as campers get older. The program is structured with six daily periods, strong instruction (including six days a week of swim), and top notch facilities across sports, arts, and adventure. The culture is spirited and traditional with uniforms, family-style meals full of cheering and singing, and Color War run separately for boys and girls. Families seeking a classic brother-sister camp with strong coaching, extensive facilities, and thoughtful bunk placement will find a lot to like.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Strong sports with professional coaches and dedicated facilities (whiffle ball stadium, putting green, driving range)
  - Heated saltwater pool, tubing and water park on a lake with an aluminum base
  - Robust hobby/arts program including The Ridge with weekly guest artists and cookbook-making
  - Air-conditioned coed movie theater and well-lit facilities for nighttime activities
  - Private lessons available at no extra cost
  - Guard gate entrance and clear bunk placement policies
  - Siblings/cousins visiting windows and coed plays for 5th grade and up



#### Lowlights

  - Full uniform required (younger campers limited to five non-uniform outfits per summer)
  - Daytime separation of boys’ and girls’ sides; staff generally do not go back and forth
  - Color War activities are completely separate for boys and girls
  - Golf only offered at Camp Equinunk
  - Tech policy, parent communication details, and extra costs are Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Birchmont — Lisa — 8-23-22
text:
# Birchmont



## 1 · Program Snapshot

BIRCHMONT JUNE 2022 Coed. 1st Session 287, 2nd session 300.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

8-10 campers/bunk, 3-4 counselors, 2 cabins per age group. Staff sleep in the back. Director: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins are large, half of them have fireplaces; staff sleep in the back. 8-10 campers/bunk, 3-4 counselors, 2 cabins per age group.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake Wentworth is pretty but down a very steep hill; they have a bus to take the campers up and down. Swim instruction is daily through age 12. They can opt out if they test and pass. They have 4 motorboats, with different locations on the lake for boating, swimming and motorized activities. NO pool.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

50/50 structure and choice, they move by division. Teens have coed time together. They do sleep outs on premises. Day trips are water parks, day hikes and local beaches.



## 6 · Program Highlights

A&C: Basic art with Mosaic, Silk Screening, sewing with 3 machines, 4 wheels for ceramics, 2 kilns that are in a separate area. Woodworking is taught by Timothy who has been a camp many summers. They build mini race cars and race them. At the theater they have one full play or musical and one variety show. Dance was nice with mirrors and wood floors. Jazz, hip hop, yoga and Zumba. Fitness is a separate building with Spin and Gymnastics is NOT big. They play all sports including pickleball, and many fields are lit for evening play. The fields are nice and Tennis is big here, they play every day. 12 courts. Hockey is street hockey. Horses are on site, but we did not see them. 42 campers ride, they have a pony club and barn buddies. Max is 4/week, 1 hour long. They ride English style with trail rides. Ninja course is nice, they also have BB guns, a large garden with pear/apple/peach orchard.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall has TVs, rainy day space, can accommodate vegan and other food allergies along with nut free. Kids can buy soda from soda machines. They have cookie patrol in addition to their visits to the Canteen. 2 DRs, 4 nurses, Huggins Hospital nearest. They did not mandate the Covid vaccine.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

NO religious life. Lake Wentworth is pretty. The fields are nice. Dance was nice with mirrors and wood floors.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Many drive to camp, many fly to Portland. 42 from FL, chaperoned flight. Parent communication, tech policy, and extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Coed program with 50/50 structure and choice; they move by division. 1st Session 287, 2nd session 300. Cabins are large (8–10 campers/bunk, 3–4 counselors, staff sleep in the back) and the Dining Hall has TVs, rainy day space, and can accommodate vegan and other food allergies along with nut free. On pretty Lake Wentworth (no pool), swim instruction is daily through age 12 with 4 motorboats and different locations for boating, swimming and motorized activities, and the program plays all sports (tennis is big here, 12 courts, every day) plus A&C, theater, dance, fitness, riding, ninja course, BB guns, and more.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 50/50 structure and choice; they move by division.
  - Cabins are large; half of them have fireplaces.
  - Dining Hall has TVs, rainy day space, can accommodate vegan and other food allergies along with nut free.
  - Lake Wentworth is pretty.
  - Swim instruction is daily through age 12 (opt out if they test and pass).
  - 4 motorboats with different locations on the lake for boating, swimming and motorized activities.
  - Many fields are lit for evening play; the fields are nice.
  - Tennis is big here, they play every day. 12 courts.
  - Horses are on site; pony club and barn buddies.
  - Ninja course is nice; large garden with pear/apple/peach orchard.
  - They do sleep outs on premises and day trips to water parks, day hikes and local beaches.



#### Lowlights

  - Lake Wentworth is down a very steep hill; they have a bus to take the campers up and down.
  - NO pool.
  - Gymnastics is NOT big.
  - They did not mandate the Covid vaccine.
  - Horses are on site, but we did not see them.



#### Similar Camps

  - Robin Hood
  - Kenwood Evergreen
  - Walt Whitman (less sporty)



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Dining Hall has TVs, rainy day space, can accommodate vegan and other food allergies along with nut free. Kids can buy soda from soda machines. They have cookie patrol in addition to their visits to the Canteen. 2 DRs, 4 nurses, Huggins Hospital nearest. They did not mandate the Covid vaccine. NO religious life. Lake Wentworth is pretty but down a very steep hill, they have a bus to take the campers up and down. Swim instruction is daily through age 12. They can opt out if they test and pass. They have 4 motorboats, with different locations on the lake for boating, swimming and motorized activities. NO pool. Teens have coed time together. A&C: Basic art with Mosaic, Silk Screening, sewing with 3 machines, 4 wheels for ceramics, 2 kilns that are in a separate area. Woodworking is taught by Timothy who has been a camp many summers. They build mini race cars and race them. At the theater they have one full play or musical and one variety show. Dance was nice with mirrors and wood floors. Jazz, hip hop, yoga and Zumba. Fitness is a separate building with Spin and Gymnastics is NOT big. They play all sports including pickleball, and many fields are lit for evening play. The fields are nice and Tennis is big here, they play every day. 12 courts. Hockey is street hockey. Horses are on site, but we did not see them. 42 campers ride, they have a pony club and barn buddies. Max is 4/week, 1 hour long. They ride English style with trail rides. Ninja course is nice, they also have BB guns, a large garden with pear/apple/peach orchard. They do sleep outs on premises. Day trips are water parks, day hikes and local beaches. Can give with Robin Hood, Kenwood Evergreen and Walt Whitman(less sporty)

title: Akiba — Alyssa — 9-17-24
text:
# Akiba



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Akiba is back they declared! It will be run in the spirit of the traditional brother/sister setup that it had in the past with separate ball fields, pools, and having boys and girls separated in the dining hall. The goal is to be the nicest camp in the tri-state area. Camp details: aiming for 300 campers in 2025; capacity is 500 campers/200 staff; there will be 1 session only—6 weeks; you CAN NOT do any partial summer (even if you are an international family) and for 2025 the price is locked at $8600. Families that enroll for 2025 are guaranteed this tuition in perpetuity and for all siblings. Tuition is all inclusive including trip fees and towel service (only private tennis/off-site private golf/hb riding are extra). For summer 2025 they will not accept campers older than rising 8th grade.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

The Zoom was run by Cary Robinson, owner; also on the call were Howard Gordon, Maya White and Chase Robinson. Staff: capacity includes 200 staff. The weight room will have a specialist for older campers.



## 3 · Living Quarters

CABINS: 75 total; all campers will live in renovated bunks with single beds and all facilities inside bunks; boy bunks and girl bunks are very separate on camp; all bathrooms cleaned professionally and daily; there are fans and overhead lighting present in all camper bunks; every bed has its own cubbies and power outlets next to it; all renovated cabins have real windows that open and close; planning on 6-10 campers/2 staff in each bunk. LAUNDRY: every week; same day or next day service; towel service (included) at both the pools/lake and in the bunk.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Private lake surrounded on all sides by camp property; there is a 900ft zipline across the lake along with kayaking, paddleboarding, pedal boats, beginning sailing, and a new fishing pier. Two heated pools (boys/girls) with swim instruction, swim team, and potentially lifeguard instruction for those who are interested. Motorized boating will take place at Beltzville State Park which is a 25 min drive from camp; it will include jet-skis and advanced sailing. They repeated that this is definitely for 2025 and did not discuss future summers.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING: 6 periods—2 swim/water based, 2 athletic, 2 specialty and it repeats weekly; there is downtime after lunch and dinner. Travel by cabin and participate either by cabin or age group pending which activity is scheduled. They participate in intercamps usually with Pinemere/Lindenmere/Golden Slipper. Color war is called Blue & Gold. They will also have a big sibling/little sibling program.



## 6 · Program Highlights

INDOOR FACILITIES: 40,000 square feet total with air conditioning in the dining hall/rec hall/dance hall/canteen; the weight room will have a specialist for older campers. ATHLETIC FACILITIES: 11 tennis courts, 8 pickleball courts, 4 full/6 half basketball courts, 4 beach volleyball courts, 4 softball/kickball fields, 4 multi-sport full size fields for soccer/flag football/etc, 4 gaga pits; gymnastics will have equipment for all elements. OUTDOOR ADVENTURE: includes ATVs, mountain biking, 2 hiking trails (1/2 mi and 1 mi), climbing wall, multiple ziplines, drop zone and others.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

DINING HALL: buffet style and campers sit with their bunk; peanut free and separate prep areas in the kitchen for allergies; there will be a full-time nutritionist on staff as well; salad/pasta bars always available. Friday night dinner with challah/chicken/soup and bagels on Sunday. HEALTH CENTER: can handle Type 1 diabetic; 6 nurses full-time and a doctor that will rotate weekly; access to telehealth for specialists; 8 min from St. Luke’s hospital and 10 min to urgent care; 10-14 private rooms and can isolate at camp if needed.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Akiba is back they declared! It will be run in the spirit of the traditional brother/sister setup that it had in the past with separate ball fields, pools, and boys and girls separated in the dining hall. The goal is to be the nicest camp in the tri-state area. Religion: they are culturally Jewish; will have Friday night dinner and then a friendship service; bagels on Sunday. At this time NO blessings at meals.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

TRAVEL: there will be a chaperoned FL flight for 2025; would consider adding others if enough campers from a specific area; prefer Newark as their airport for pickups. Costs: for 2025 the price is locked at $8600, and families that enroll for 2025 are guaranteed this tuition in perpetuity and for all siblings. Tuition is all inclusive including trip fees and towel service (only private tennis/off-site private golf/hb riding are extra). Session/length: 1 session only—6 weeks; you CAN NOT do any partial summer (even if you are an international family). Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Akiba is back they declared, with a traditional brother/sister setup and a single 6-week session for 2025. Camp details include aiming for 300 campers (capacity 500 campers/200 staff), an all-inclusive $8600 tuition locked for 2025 (and in perpetuity for enrolled families and siblings), and no partial summer. Facilities feature a private lake with a 900ft zipline, two heated pools with instruction, extensive athletic/indoor facilities, and renovated bunks with in-bunk facilities and weekly laundry/towel service. They are culturally Jewish with a Friday night dinner and friendship service, strong health and safety infrastructure, and a vibe aiming to be the nicest camp in the tri-state area.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Traditional brother/sister setup with separate ball fields, pools, and dining hall seating
  - All-inclusive $8600 tuition for 2025; guaranteed in perpetuity for enrolled families and siblings
  - Private lake with 900ft zipline; two heated pools; motorized boating (jet-skis/advanced sailing) at Beltzville State Park
  - Strong facilities: 40,000 sq ft of AC indoor space; extensive athletics; outdoor adventure incl. ATVs and multiple ziplines
  - Health center: 6 nurses, rotating doctor, telehealth; 8 min to St. Luke’s, 10 min to urgent care; can isolate
  - Renovated bunks with in-bunk facilities, cubbies/outlets, real windows; weekly laundry and towel service
  - Programming: 6 periods (2 water, 2 athletic, 2 specialty) with downtime; intercamps; Blue & Gold; big sibling/little sibling
  - Culturally Jewish with Friday night dinner and a friendship service; peanut-free kitchen with allergy prep areas and full-time nutritionist
  - Chaperoned FL flight; prefers Newark for pickups



#### Lowlights

  - CAN NOT do any partial summer (even for international families)
  - Not accepting campers older than rising 8th grade for summer 2025
  - One session only—6 weeks
  - Motorized boating requires 25 min drive to Beltzville State Park
  - Boys and girls separated in the dining hall
  - Parent communication and tech policies: Unknown



#### Similar Camps

  - Pinemere
  - Lindenmere
  - Golden Slipper



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

It will be run in the spirit of the traditional brother/sister setup that it had in the past with separate ball fields, pools, and having boys and girls separated in the dining hall. It will include jet-skis and advanced sailing. They repeated that this is definitely for 2025 and did not discuss future summers. • INDOOR FACILITIES: 40,000 square feet total with air conditioning in the dining hall/rec hall/dance hall/canteen; the weight room will have a specialist for older campers • PROGRAMMING: 6 periods—2 swim/water based, 2 athletic, 2 specialty and it repeats weekly; there is downtime after lunch and dinner; travel by cabin and participate either by cabin or age group pending which activity is scheduled; they participate in intercamps usually with Pinemere/Lindenmere/Golden Slipper; color war is called Blue & Gold; they will also have a big sibling/little sibling program • CABINS: 75 total; all campers will live in renovated bunks with single beds and all facilities inside bunks; boy bunks and girl bunks are very separate on camp; all bathrooms cleaned professionally and daily; there are fans and overhead lighting present in all camper bunks; every bed has its own cubbies and power outlets next to it; all renovated cabins have real windows that open and close; planning on 6-10 campers/2 staff in each bunk • ATHLETIC FACILITIES: 11 tennis courts, 8 pickleball courts, 4 full/6 half basketball courts, 4 beach volleyball courts, 4 softball/kickball fields, 4 multi-sport full size fields for soccer/flag football/etc, 4 gaga pits; gymnastics will have equipment for all elements • OUTDOOR ADVENTURE: includes ATVs, mountain biking, 2 hiking trails (1/2 mi and 1 mi), climbing wall, multiple ziplines, drop zone and others • RELIGION: they are culturally Jewish; will have Friday night dinner with challah/chicken/soup and then a friendship service; bagels on Sunday. At this time NO blessings at meals. • DINING HALL: buffet style and campers sit with their bunk; peanut free and separate prep areas in the kitchen for allergies; there will be a full-time nutritionist on staff as well; salad/pasta bars always available • HEALTH CENTER: can handle Type 1 diabetic; 6 nurses full-time and a doctor that will rotate weekly; access to telehealth for specialists; 8 min from St. Luke’s hospital and 10 min to urgent care; 10-14 private rooms and can isolate at camp if needed • LAUNDRY: every week; same day or next day service; towel service (included) at both the pools/lake and in the bunk • TRAVEL: there will be a chaperoned FL flight for 2025; would consider adding others if enough campers from a specific area; prefer Newark as their airport for pickups

title: WICOSUTA — JODY — 10-25-16
text:
# WICOSUTA



## 1 · Program Snapshot

All girls camp in Hebron, NH. Newfound Lake is known as the 4th cleanest lake in NE. 2 Sessions-each 26 days. There is NO OPTION TO EXTEND. 2017 will be the camp’s 97th summer.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Corey and Justin are the directors. Approximately 50 girls per age group.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Unknown



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Newfound Lake is known as the 4th cleanest lake in NE. Grade 4 is required to take waterfront and swim instruction.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Grades 1-3 have 5 periods of activities with their age group and 1 daily choice period. Grade 4 has 1 choice period, 2-3 periods that are determined in advance, and 1-2 periods with their age Group. This age is required to take tennis, waterfront and swim instruction. Grades 5-9 are a completely choice program.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Optional Horseback Riding program. 9x’s per session.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Unknown



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Great NONJUDGEMENT environment. Their philosophy is Confidence, Community and Competence. On the 1st day of camp each cabin creates a contract to promote this philosophy. When there is a conflict they use the “I message” tactic. “I feel that...when you…” Wico is a Uniform camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

All girls camp in Hebron, NH on Newfound Lake, known as the 4th cleanest lake in NE. Two sessions—each 26 days—and there is NO OPTION TO EXTEND. Great NONJUDGEMENT environment with a philosophy of Confidence, Community and Competence, reinforced by cabin contracts and the “I message.” Optional Horseback Riding program (9x’s per session) and a structured-to-choice progression: Grades 1-3 mostly age-group with one daily choice, Grade 4 mixed with required tennis, waterfront and swim instruction, and Grades 5-9 completely choice.

#### Lowlights

Corey and Justin are the directors 2017 will be the camp’s 97 th summer. All girls camp in Hebron, NH. Newfound Lake is known as the 4 th cleanest lake in NE. 2 Sessions-each 26 days. There is NO OPTION TO EXTEND. Great NONJUDGEMENT environment. Their philosophy is Confidence, Community and Competence. On the 1 st day of camp each cabin creates a contract to promote this philosophy. When there is a conflict they use the “I message” tactic. “I feel that...when you…” Approximately 50 girls per age group. Wico is a Uniform camp. Optional Horseback Riding program. 9x’s per session. Grades 1-3 have 5 periods of activities with their age group and 1 daily choice period. Grade 4 has 1 choice period, 2-3 periods that are determined in advance, and 1-2 periods with their age Group. This age is required to take tennis, waterfront and swim instruction. Grades 5-9 are a completely choice program.

title: WEKEELA — MONICA — 8-3-22
text:
# WEKEELA



## 1 · Program Snapshot

They have 290 campers per session. The kids camp is from ages 7-12 and the teen camp from 13-16. The full session is 6.5 weeks long; kids can go for the 1st or 2nd session. There is a rookie 2-week program with limited availability. Full summer kids are 50% of campers in younger ages and goes up a bit as they age. Kids wear uniform on some days. The camp is 100 years old this summer and special celebrations for it. Facilities are beautiful and everything is in great condition; fields a bit dry, very dry summer.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Kids come from all over the US and the world. 65% of counselors are former campers so they have this strong continuing tradition of camp songs etc. CAMPER PROFILE/CAMP PHILOSOPHY - It is for any kid who wants to be active and try things, says Ephram. We also met several counselors who used to be campers and were there for 10 years or so.



## 3 · Living Quarters

BUNKING – cabins with 10 kids and 3 counselors. Bathrooms in cabins with showers and outside there are porches with shoes and sunscreens outside, making it very organized.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

LAKE/POOL– gorgeous lake front. They have 5 waterski boats, fishing, canoeing, kayaks, paddles, sailboats and all the toys.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMING – They have A, B, C, D and S day in a rotation. S is special day where they have trips and special programming. On all other days kids choose their schedule but each period has to be chosen from each activity area: one land sport, one water sport, one art, one outdoor/adventure, etc. Like that programming is well balanced. They raise flag every morning as a whole group.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Barn with rabbits and goats to pet. Campfires with traditional camp songs etc. Great out door adventure area with climbing wall, low ropes and high ropes, a huge 85 yard zip line; they also have a real climbing wall right on camp. Outdoor campfire cooking program as well as a very good indoor cooking program with excellent facilities (when we were there they were making vegetable dumplings). Environmental classes. In land sports they have a very wide variety which includes basketball, hockey, disc golf, tennis, badminton, pickle ball, yoga and aerobics outside. Huge rec center with hockey, ping pong, arcade, overlooking the lake. Loft with high couches for campers to hang out. There is a general store that sells swag and canteen items.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

COVID protocol: This is the only camp that had us wait outside at the gate until Ephram came in his gold cart and he escorted us close to the office where we remained in the car to do a rapid covid test and only after 15 minutes, with a negative result, they allowed us to get out of the car. This year because of covid they left 2 cabins empty in case they needed to quarantine people; they did not have to use those. DINING HALL – It is nut free. Separate window to accommodate all special diets. There are water bottle refill stations all around camp; Ephram was proud of those.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The camp is 100 years old this summer and special celebrations for it. 65% of counselors are former campers so they have this strong continuing tradition of camp songs etc. SERVICES – There is an optional Friday night shabbat service run by Ephram and he says 100 campers go to it; it is more campy and fun than anything else and campers run prayers together with him. They raise flag every morning as a whole group. Camp Wekeela is a fun, action packed camp with lots of variety in its programming and great facilities. Kids we spoke to were friendly, and happy.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Parent communication and tech – They have 1 optional phone call Home each session that he says many don’t want to do. They do use the photo app with 500 pictures uploaded per day; he did mention helicopter parents who call and ask why my child was not smiling… he says his job was easier before photo app. No packages are allowed. They want to keep things simple. EXTRA COSTS – private tennis and baggage and linens if needed. Travel logistics: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Wekeela is a fun, action packed camp with lots of variety in its programming and great facilities. It is great for a child or teen who wants to try a bit of everything and who wants to try. You don’t have to be a great athlete or artist to go there, you just need to be enthusiastic and want to be a part of the community. Kids we spoke to were friendly, and happy, and we also met several counselors who used to be campers and were there for 10 years or so.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Lots of variety in programming and great facilities.
  - Gorgeous lake front with 5 waterski boats and all the toys.
  - Strong tradition: 65% of counselors are former campers; campfires and songs.
  - Balanced choice-based schedule (A, B, C, D rotation plus S special day for trips and special programming).
  - Excellent indoor and outdoor cooking programs (vegetable dumplings when we were there).
  - Great outdoor adventure area with high ropes and a huge 85 yard zip line, plus a real climbing wall.
  - Nut-free dining with a separate window for special diets; water bottle refill stations all around camp.
  - Optional Friday night shabbat service with 100 campers; campy and fun.



#### Lowlights

  - Rookie 2-week program with limited availability.
  - Only 1 optional phone call home each session.
  - No packages are allowed.
  - Extra costs for private tennis and baggage and linens if needed.
  - Fields a bit dry (very dry summer).
  - Kids wear uniform on some days.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

They have 290 campers per session. The kids camp is from ages 7-12 and the teen camp from 13-16. The full session is 6.5 weeks long. Kids can go for the 1st or 2nd session. There is a rookie 2-week program with limited availability. Full summer kids are 50% of campers in younger ages and goes up a bit as they age. This year because of covid they left 2 cabins empty in case they needed to quarantine people. They did not have to use those. Kids wear uniform on some days. The camp is 100 year old this summer and special celebrations for it. DEMOGRAPHICS/LOGISTICS - 65% of counselors are former campers so they have this strong continuing tradition of camp songs etc. BUNKING – cabins with 10 kids and 3 counselors. Bathrooms in cabins with showers and outside there are porches with shoes and sunscreens outside, making it very organized. DINING HALL – It is nut free. Separate window to accommodate all special diets. There are water bottle refill stations all around camp. Ephram was proud of those. SERVICES – There is an optional Friday night shabbat service run by Ephram and he says 100 campers go to it. It is more campy and fun than anything else. Campers run prayers together with him. CAMPER PROFILE/CAMP PHILOSOPHY - Kids come from all over the US and the world. It is for any kid who wants to be active and try things, skas Ephram. They have 1 optional phone call Home each session that he says many don’t want to do. They do use the photo app with 500 pictures uploaded per day. He did mention helicopter parents who call and ask why my child was not smiling… he says his job was easier before photo app. No packages are allowed. They want to keep things simple. PROGRAMING – They have A,B,C, D and S day in a rotation. S is special day where they have trips and special programming. On all other days kids choose their schedule but each period has to be chosen from each activity area: one land sport, one water sport, one art, one outdoor/adventure, etc. Like that programming is well balanced. They have a barn with rabbits and goats to pet. They raise flag every morning as a whole group. They have campfires with traditional camp songs etc. They have a great out door adventure area with climbing wall, low ropes and high ropes, a huge 85 yard zip line. They also have a real climbing wall right on camp. They have an outdoor campfire cooking program as well as a very good indoor cooking program with excellent facilities. When we were there they were making vegetable dumplings. They also have environmental classes. In land sports they have a very wide variety which includes basketball, hockey, disc golf, tennis, badminton, pickle ball yoga and areobics outside. LAKE/POOL– gorgeous lake front. They have 5 waterski boats, fishing, canoeing, kayaks, paddles, sailboats and all the toys. EXTRA COSTS – private tennis and baggage and linens if needed. FACILITIES - they have beautiful facilities and everything is in great condition. Fields a bit dry, very dry summer. Huge rec center with hockey, ping pong, arcade, overlooking the lake. Loft with hight couches for campers to hang out… There is a general store that sells swag and canteen items. Camp Wekeela is a funny action packed camp with lots of variety in its programming and great facilities. It is great for a child or teen who wants to try a bit of everything and who want to try. You don’t have to be a great athlete or artist to go there, you just need to be enthusiastic and want to be a part of the community. Kids we spoke to were friendly, and happy. title: Merriwood — Michele — 7-30-25
text:
# Merriwood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

New Hampshire, all girls camp. Right now they have 130 girls, 8-15 years old. Brother sister with Mousilauke and Kingswood and some brothers also go to Pemi. Smaller in size and they really pay attention to what the girls need, more personalized approach. Very New Hampshire feeling.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Toured by Director/Owner Susan Miller. She handles the girls very well - and seems to know them all by name. One girl came over to us not feeling well and Susan gave her a huge hug and had a little pep talk with her and she ran back to activity. They really seem to love her! It was only day 2 of the new session so she said its normal.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks were very simple but good - not a lot of “stuff” but very sweet and cute.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

No pool. The dining hall is pretty on the lake.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Typical day is 8a: clean up, Breakfast, Morning activities, 1p: lunch, 2:30-4p electives, Dinner and evening activities. Can make changes to their electives on Sundays for the week, not stuck doing something they don’t enjoy.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Mountain bikes and horses on campus (saw lots of girls are horseback riding very cute). Seems like a very sweet, loving camp without all the bells and whistles.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining room is very cute has fresh cut wild flowers on the tables, dining hall seemed to have nice options hot and buffet for the girls.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall, camp looked very nice and attracts nice low key girls. Uniform. No mention of religion. Smaller in size and they really pay attention to what the girls need, open to working with them and changing electives, more personalized approach. Camp dog Josie “dojo” was playing with all the girls, they love her! Seems like a very sweet, loving camp without all the bells and whistles. No electronics, and very New Hampshire feeling. Note: you can walk to Mousilauke but she said they only see the boys once a session because they’re happy to be in a 'girls world'.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Parents drop off for 2nd session, she said it works well b/c no visiting day for those families. 1st session: bus (Greenwich and NYC). No electronics. Uniform.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Merriwood is a New Hampshire, all girls camp with about 130 girls ages 8-15. It is smaller in size, sweet and loving, and they really pay attention to what the girls need with a more personalized approach and flexible electives. Director/Owner Susan Miller is very hands-on and seems to know them all by name, and the camp attracts nice low key girls. Families should note the uniform, no electronics, no pool, and travel options (bus for 1st session; parent drop-off for 2nd session with no visiting day).



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Smaller in size and more personalized approach; open to working with girls and changing electives.
  - Director/Owner Susan Miller is very hands-on and seems to know them all by name.
  - Mountain bikes and horses on campus; lots of girls horseback riding.
  - All girls, very New Hampshire feeling; no electronics.
  - Brother sister with Mousilauke and Kingswood; only see the boys once a session.



#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - Bunks are very simple (not a lot of 'stuff').
  - No visiting day for 2nd session families (parent drop-off model).
  - Without all the bells and whistles.
  - Uniform required.



#### Similar Camps

  - Wicosutta
  - Robindel
  - Lochearn
  - WaKlo



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

New Hampshire, all girls camp Toured by Director/Owner Susan Miller Right now they have 130 girls, 8-15 years old Typical day is 8a: clean up Breakfast Morning activities 1p: lunch 2:30-4p electives Dinner and evening activities Can make changes to their electives on Sundays for the week, not stuck doing something they don’t enjoy Brother sister with Mousilauke and Kingswood and some brothers also go to Pemi Competitors: Wicosutta, Robindel, Lochearn (as far as sports) *although these are are similar in vibe as well as WaKlo Dining room is very cute has fresh cut wild flowers on the tables, dining hall seemed to have nice options hot and buffet for the girls and is pretty on the lake Mountain bikes and horses on campus (saw lots of girls are horseback riding very cute) Parents drop off for 2nd session, she said it works well b/c no visiting day for those families 1st session: bus (Greenwich and NYC) Camp dog Josie “dojo” was playing with all the girls, they love her! She’s a big golden retriever Bunks were very simple but good - not a lot of “stuff” but very sweet and cute Overall, camp looked very nice and attracts nice low key girls. Uniform. No mention of religion. Smaller in size and they really pay attention to what the girls need, open to working with them and changing electives, more personalized approach. One girl came over to us not feeling well and Susan gave her a huge hug and had a little pep talk with her and she ran back to activity. They really seem to love her! It was only day 2 of the new session so she said its normal. She handles the girls very well - and seems to know them all by name. Seems like a very sweet, loving camp without all the bells and whistles. No electronics, no pool and very New Hampshire feeling. Note: you can walk to Mousilauke but she said they only see the boys once a session because they’r happy to be in a “girls world"

title: Adirondack Camp — Jennifer — 7-31-24
text:
# Adirondack Camp



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp offers 2, 4 or 6 week sessions. Two week session for younger campers only. They have 225 enrolled for first session, 180 for second session; their ideal number is 230. 50% of campers from the tri-state area.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jennifer, Beth, Lisa and Shari toured on 7/11/24 with Shawn, the owner (her parents were the owners but both recently died). Shawn was lovely and camp looked great. No general counselors – all are specialists.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Campers live in open air cabins and sleep with mosquito nets. Sides of cabins can come down in bad weather. Approximately 12 campers and 3 staff per bunk and sleep in bunkbeds. Campers live out of their trunks and they bring their trunks up with them to camp. Bathrooms and shower house are separate from the bunks.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Activities – lake is the highlight of the camp and beautiful. They have 1.5 miles of waterfront. Offer motorized and nonmotorized activities. No pool. According to Shawn the most popular activities at camp are water skiing and fishing. Some activities like sailing are double periods.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Scheduling: campers get 4 choice activities plus mandatory swim; they choose their activities weekly and do Mon- Fri, different programming on weekends.



## 6 · Program Highlights

According to Shawn the most popular activities at camp are culinary. Some activities like rock climbing are double periods. They do have a big fencing program. No golf. They do have a new pottery pavilion outside. Also offer glass blowing and glass fusing. They have guest artists come up and spend a week at camp and that will impact what they are offering in the arts. This summer they did have a cheese making program as well as a magician come spend a week at camp. No big performing arts program but they do an art showcase where campers show off what they have made during the summer. They also offer many out of camp adventure trips, most are optional and first come first served.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining hall – rustic but lovely. Campers sit with their bunks at rectangular table. They also get snacks/cookie break – NO canteen.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Overall Impression: Camp was rustic but lovely with an amazing waterfront. Definitely more of a non Jewish vibe. Shawn was lovely and camp looked great.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp was rustic but lovely with an amazing waterfront. Definitely more of a non Jewish vibe. Would give it to families looking for a more rustic experience, possibly with Adirondack Woodcraft but this camp has much nicer lake and facilities. Camp offers 2, 4 or 6 week sessions with 4 choice activities plus mandatory swim and different programming on weekends.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Amazing waterfront; lake is the highlight and beautiful.
  - 1.5 miles of waterfront.
  - Offer motorized and nonmotorized activities.
  - Water skiing and fishing are popular.
  - Big fencing program.
  - New pottery pavilion outside.
  - Glass blowing and glass fusing.
  - Guest artists come up and spend a week at camp.
  - Cheese making program.
  - A magician came to spend a week at camp.
  - Many out of camp adventure trips; most are optional and first come first served.
  - No general counselors – all are specialists.
  - Open air cabins with mosquito nets; sides can come down in bad weather.
  - Dining hall – rustic but lovely.
  - Shawn was lovely and camp looked great.



#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - No golf.
  - No big performing arts program.
  - NO canteen.



#### Similar Camps

  - Adirondack Woodcraft



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

ADIRONDACK CAMP Jennifer, Beth, Lisa and Shari toured on 7/11/24 with Shawn,the owner ( her parents were the owners but both recently died). Shawn was lovely and camp looked great Camp offers 2,4 or 6 week sessions . They have 225 enrolled for first session, 180 for second session; their ideal number is 230. Two week session for younger campers only. 50% of campers from the tri-state area Scheduling: campers get 4 choice activities plus mandatory swim; they choose their activities weekly and do Mon- Fri, different programming on weekends Campers live in open air cabins and sleep with mosquito nets. Sides of cabins can come down in bad weather. Approximately 12 campers and 3 staff per bunk and sleep in bunkbeds. Campers live out of their trunks and they bring their trunks up with them to camp. Bathrooms and shower house are separate from the bunks. No general counselors – all are specialists. Dining hall- rustic but lovely. Campers sit with their bunks at rectangular table. They also get snacks/cookie break – NO canteen. Activities – lake is the highlight of the camp and beautiful. They have 1.5 miles of waterfront. Offer motorized and nonmotorized activities. No pool. According to Shawn the most popular activities at camp are culinary, water skiing and fishing. Some activities like sailing and rock climbing are double periods. They do have a big fencing program. No golf. They do have a new pottery pavilion outside. Also offer glass blowing and glass fusing. They have guest artists come up and spend a week at camp and that will impact what they are offering in the arts. This summer they did have a cheese making program as well as a magician come spend a week at camp. No big performing arts program but they do an art showcase where campers show off what they have made during the summer. They also offer many out of camp adventure trips, most are optional and first come first served. Overall Impression: Camp was rustic but lovely with an amazing waterfront. Definitely more of a non Jewish vibe. Would give it to families looking for a more rustic experience, possibly with Adirondack Woodcraft but this camp has much nicer lake and facilities.
