title: Ramah Darom — Tami — 6-21-24
text:
# Ramah Darom



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Ramah Darom is a conservative Jewish camp located in Clayton, Georgia.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

June 17, 2024 Ramah Darom tour given by Julia Weiss, Director. Julia Weiss, Camp Director, took me on a tour of the grounds. The difference between Ramah Darom and the other Jewish camps is that the children come from Texas east and Tennessee south. There are always 4 counselors and 10-16 kids depending on the age group. Two bunk counselors go around with the kids and plan the activities and two are at an activity. The ages the counselors can start are rising college freshman. 80% were campers and are now staff. Camp Expert: Tami Feldman, Atlanta, Georgia.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The cabins were very basic. They were small with a lot of bunk beds, 2 sinks and 2 toilets. There was not a lot of room. The bunks are not AC. There is a common area in the middle and they have a dryer for the bathing suits.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

The kids go to the pool or lake once a day. There are no motorized watersports. There is a blob.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Activities for the regular campers are chosen individually but by grade specific. They don’t go around with their cabin all day but they do the activities by their grade. Boys and girls do activities together. Before the campers arrive, they pick their activities and they rank the activities they want. Night activities are by age group unless it is a whole camp activity like a movie.



## 6 · Program Highlights

11th graders stay for the full summer. They spend the first half of camp planning color war and being a CIT. The second half of camp they take a summer road trip up the east coast. They tour colleges and Washington, DC and the Berkshires. Rising 5th and 6th graders have an inclusion program (Tikvah) for high functioning Autism/ADHD. They need to be able to take care of themselves. It is a shorter session. The 18-24 year old Tikvah program consists of them working in the office, the mailroom, etc. They learn life skills and go to the pool and have sports just like being a camper. When I toured, they were playing tennis and being observed by the assistant director. Rising 10th graders go to Birmingham for 3 days. They do educational and fun things there. Each age group, 3rd grade and up, go on a field trip. One example is a tubing trip to Helen, Georgia. They have softball and baseball but it depends on the summer. Sometimes they don’t have a counselor to teach it. This year they do not have those sports but summer 2023 they did. They have a woodworking studio, art, clay, ceramics and fabric painting. When I was touring the art room, they were making Challah covers.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Upon approaching the camp, there was a security guard who was the local sheriff. He checked my ID and made sure I was on the list to visit. Once approved of my tour, I drove down another 1/2 mile to a gate with another security guard where I was able to enter the camp. On campus, they have 2 doctors, one being pediatric and 4 nurses. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They have morning prayers by cabin. Most cabins do it on their screened in porch in the cabin. Fridays for Shabbat they are not required to wear anything special. Every week a different age group leads the Shabbat service, sets up for shabbat, etc. They do observe Shabbat and leave lights on Friday night at sundown until Saturday at sundown. Most cabins leave their front porch light on and their bathroom light on. Many of the boys were walking around with Kippahs on their head.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Ramah Darom is a conservative Jewish camp located in Clayton, Georgia. Activities for the regular campers are chosen individually but by grade specific, and boys and girls do activities together. They do observe Shabbat, with morning prayers by cabin and weekly age-group leadership of services, and the kids go to the pool or lake once a day with no motorized watersports. Overall, I feel this camp would be good for a non sporty, Jewish child.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Security guard who was the local sheriff checked ID at the entrance, then another gate with another security guard.
  - On campus, they have 2 doctors, one being pediatric and 4 nurses.
  - 11th graders stay for the full summer; first half planning color war and being a CIT; second half a summer road trip up the east coast (tour colleges, Washington, DC and the Berkshires).
  - Rising 5th and 6th graders have an inclusion program (Tikvah) for high functioning Autism/ADHD.
  - The 18-24 year old Tikvah program works in the office and mailroom, learns life skills, and goes to the pool and has sports just like being a camper.
  - Activities are chosen individually but by grade; boys and girls do activities together.
  - The kids go to the pool or lake once a day; there is a blob.
  - Each age group, 3rd grade and up, goes on a field trip (e.g., tubing trip to Helen, Georgia).
  - They have a woodworking studio, art, clay, ceramics and fabric painting; they were making Challah covers.
  - 80% were campers and are now staff.



#### Lowlights

  - The bunks are not AC.
  - Cabins were very basic, small, and there was not a lot of room.
  - They have softball and baseball but it depends on the summer; sometimes they don’t have a counselor to teach it; this year they do not have those sports.
  - There are no motorized watersports.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

June 17 th , 2024 Ramah Darom Tour given by Julia Weiss, Director Camp Expert: Tami Feldman, Atlanta, Georgia Ramah Darom is a conservative Jewish camp located in Clayton, Georgia. Upon approaching the camp, there was a security guard who was the local sheriff. He checked my ID and made sure I was on the list to visit. Once approved of my tour, I drove down another ½ mile to a gate with another security guard where I was able to enter the camp. Julia Weiss, Camp Director, took me on a tour of the grounds. On campus, they have 2 doctors, one being pediatric and 4 nurses. 11 th graders stay for the full summer. They spend the first half of camp planning color war and being a CIT. The second half of camp they take a summer road trip up the east coast. They tour colleges and Washington, DC and the Berkshires. Rising 5 th and 6 th graders have an inclusion program (Tikvah) for high functioning Autism/ ADHD. They need to be able to take care of themselves. It is a shorter session. The 18-24 year old Tikvah program consists of them working in the office, the mailroom, etc. They learn life skills and go to the pool and have sports just like being a camper. When I toured, they were playing tennis and being observed by the assistant director. Activities for the regular campers are chosen individually but by grade specific. They don’t go around with their cabin all day but they do the activities by their grade. Boys and girls do activities together. Before the campers arrive, they pick their activities and they rank the activities they want. The kids go to the pool or lake once a day. There are no motorized watersports. There is a blob. Rising 10 th graders go to Birmingham for 3 days. They do educational and fun things there. Each age group, 3 rd grade and up, go on a field trip. One example is a tubing trip to Helen, Georgia. Night activities are by age group unless it is a whole camp activity like a movie. The difference between Ramah Darom and the other Jewish camps is that the children come from Texas east and Tennessee south. They have softball and baseball but it depends on the summer. Sometimes they don’t have a counselor to teach it. This year they do not have those sports but summer 2023 they did. They have morning prayers by cabin. Most cabins do it on their screened in porch in the cabin. Fridays for Shabbat they are not required to wear anything special. Every week a different age group leads the Shabbat service, sets up for shabbat, etc. They do observe Shabbat and leave lights on Friday night at sundown until Saturday at sundown. Most cabins leave their front porch light on and their bathroom light on. Many of the boys were walking around with Kippahs on their head. They have a woodworking studio, art, clay, ceramics and fabric painting. When I was touring the art room, they were making Challah covers. There are always 4 counselors and 10-16 kids depending on the age group. Two bunk counselors go around with the kids and plan the activities and two are at an activity. The ages the counselors can start are rising college freshman. 80% were campers and are now staff. The cabins were very basic. They were small with a lot of bunk beds, 2 sinks and 2 toilets. There was not a lot of room. The bunks are not AC. There is a common area in the middle and they have a dryer for the bathing suits. Overall, I feel this camp would be good for a non sporty, Jewish child.

title: Pinewood — Karen — 7-8-24
text:
# Pinewood



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Pinewood is a co-ed, family owned (70 years) camp located in Hendersonville, North Carolina. Camp Pinewood July 3, 2024 Tour given by Sue Lyons- Director. Camp Expert: Tami Feldman, Atlanta, Georgia. It was a little difficult to find because their address listed isn’t where the entrance to the camp is. They also do not have a call box at the gate so you have to call the camp to have someone let you in. Sue took me around on a golf cart for a very quick tour. We didn’t go into many buildings and she showed me them from the outside.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Most campers are from South Florida. They have a lot of alumni kids. There are 300 campers and 150 staff. Majority are returning campers which is about 85-90%. I was impressed that Sue knew every child’s name at camp. She seemed to be well liked by the kids and was super friendly with the kids she saw, always saying their names. The campers seemed to be happy and having fun.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The cabins were really nice. They had laminate wood floors, lots of shelving, nice bunk beds that have lots of shelving, shelves for their clothes, and full bathroom facilities. The largest cabin holds 16 but there are typically 8-12 kids in a cabin with 3 counselors. They have someone that comes in every day to spray a sanitizer in the cabin. Girls and boy cabins are at opposite ends of camp.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They have a large lake with fishing, waterskiing with a jet ski pulling, a blob, water blow up for climbing, and tubing with a waterski pulling. There is a pool at camp and they do have free swim. There is no instructional swim. Sue mentioned since most campers are from South Florida, they know how to swim.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They do go around the camp with their cabin for activities. Every night they have an activity. They do camp socials with a DJ every Saturday night.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Other activities include riflery, archery, arts and crafts, drama (but no plays), soccer, basketball, lacrosse, kickball, volleyball, tennis, four square, dance, gymnastics, yoga, fitness. They do have horseback riding. It is grouped by cabin but nothing high level. It doesn’t seem like horseback is a big thing at the camp. They have fun trips that they go on. They go to Carowinds, Frankie’s Fun Park, Tree Tops rafting, etc. They are required to wear camp shirts on field trips and for cabin photos.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

At night they have a police officer that patrols camp. The dining hall has two seatings. The first seating is for the younger ages and the late seating is for the older campers. The boys sit on one side and the girls sit on the other. They have separate dining rooms. The camp is nut free and they can usually accommodate allergies. If someone is Gluten Free, there is an extra charge because they have to buy special food. They always have nut butter alternatives at lunch and dinner for sandwiches. They serve kid friendly food. They have ice cream 3 times a session. There is no canteen and they provide all snacks which is included in tuition.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The campers seemed to be happy and having fun. Co-ed community with weekly socials with a DJ every Saturday night.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

It was a little difficult to find because their address listed isn’t where the entrance to the camp is. They also do not have a call box at the gate so you have to call the camp to have someone let you in. They do allow packages but no food. Parents do pay for the field trips and they also supply spending money. They are required to wear camp shirts on field trips and for cabin photos.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Pinewood would be a good camp for someone that loves water sports and is wanting a co-ed camp. I would recommend it for someone in South Florida since that is where the majority of the campers come from. They have a large lake with fishing, waterskiing with a jet ski pulling, a blob, water blow up for climbing, and tubing with a waterski pulling. The cabins were really nice, with laminate wood floors, lots of shelving, nice bunk beds that have lots of shelving, shelves for their clothes, and full bathroom facilities.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Co-ed, family owned (70 years) camp located in Hendersonville, North Carolina.
  - Large lake and water sports (fishing, waterskiing with a jet ski pulling, blob, water blow up for climbing, tubing).
  - Cabins were really nice; laminate wood floors, lots of shelving, full bathroom facilities.
  - Director knew every child’s name and seemed well liked; campers seemed happy and having fun.
  - Strong community with about 85-90% returning campers and many alumni kids.
  - Nut free; snacks included in tuition; ice cream 3 times a session.
  - Police officer patrols camp at night.



#### Lowlights

  - There is no instructional swim.
  - Horseback riding is grouped by cabin but nothing high level; doesn’t seem like horseback is a big thing.
  - It was a little difficult to find; no call box at the gate.
  - Parents do pay for the field trips and they also supply spending money.
  - If someone is Gluten Free, there is an extra charge.
  - Sue took me around on a golf cart for a very quick tour; we didn’t go into many buildings.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

We didn’t go into many buildings and she showed me them from the outside. I was impressed that Sue knew every child’s name at camp. She seemed to be well liked by the kids and was super friendly with the kids she saw, always saying their names. The campers seemed to be happy and having fun. title: WESTCOAST CONNECTION — SHARI — 2-10-21
text:
# WESTCOAST CONNECTION



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Summer 2021 - Confident they’ll run programs but will look a little different. In addition to traditional Teen Tours and Global Adventures (described below) they will have: two career experiences in NYC • Wall Street NY Institute of Finance (NYIF) - finish grades 9-12 only, up to 100 students, 7 or 14 days, dorms at NYU and back-up plan hotel they’ve used in the past; if not able to run in person, can do online format. • Fashion Week Internships (FWI) - finish grades 8-12, 40 students, 14 days, dorms at NYU; will not run if can’t be in person. Online Academy Two choices • Mini: 5 days, 75 min/day (6.25 hrs/week) (website for course options & pricing not updated yet; this program had 540 students in 2020). • Extensive: 5 days, 2hrs/day x 10 sessions (20 hrs over 2 wks) (website for course & pricing not updated yet). Active Teen Tours: mostly domestic including travel & service both East & West Coasts; traditionally 40-48 students on trips, for 2021 they’re looking at 30-35 to meet regulation requirements, and if the Canadian border is not opened up they’ll pivot with a backup plan. Global Adventures: most people are focused on these trips, 12-14 students, more National Parks rather than cities with outdoor activities and recreation, comfortable lodges & hotels (not camping); if other trips can’t run they are prepared to combine adventure programs which are 29-34 days; added two East Coast trips: 14-day New England and 17-day N\&S Carolina (meaningful adventure, not super rustic and no flights). Volunteer Programs: New Orleans, Hawaii, Alaska, Florida will run easily he feels; also including Northeast in Maine, Boston, D.C. (living mostly on college campuses; maybe suites-style hotel in D.C. since G-town not allowing programs on campus this summer) and several adventure/service opportunities in Costa Rica. Pre-College Enrichment: A big question??? American U & G-town not inviting summer programs on campus; UCLA they’ll know more mid-March (working with a private college in CA as substitute if needed); McGill-border still an issue, more info in March/April.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Traditionally they have 40-48 students on trips; for 2021 they’re looking at 30-35 to meet regulation requirements from various States and bus companies. Global Adventures run with 12-14 students. Wall Street NYIF is finish grades 9-12 only (up to 100 students; 7 or 14 days). Fashion Week Internships is finish grades 8-12 (40 students; 14 days). On all programs they will have extra staff to deal with any issues that may arise and will be able to deal with a student (particularly covid) while the rest of the trip runs as planned. A lot of trip leaders will be vaccinated but likely won’t be 100%. They’re advising them to get them whenever possible. They have someone on their team dedicated to decisions and guidelines related to Covid-19 and they have a committee that meets weekly for updates, etc.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Dorms at NYU (with a back-up plan hotel they’ve used in the past) for the NYC career experiences. Comfortable lodges & hotels (not camping) for Global Adventures. Living mostly on college campuses for Northeast service (maybe suites-style hotel in D.C. since G-town not allowing programs on campus this summer). They feel they will operate in ‘real-world’ structure and will not have pod structure. They’ll continue to have rotated room assignments as of now (unless their consultant advises otherwise) so as to not sacrifice who they are and to provide students with what they do best: making everyone feel comfortable and the group is well-connected all around.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Wall Street NYIF: 7 or 14 days. Fashion Week Internships: 14 days. Online Academy Mini: 5 days, 75 min/day (6.25 hrs/week). Online Academy Extensive: 5 days, 2hrs/day x 10 sessions (20 hrs over 2 wks). Global Adventures may combine adventure programs which are 29-34 days. Added two East Coast trips: 14-day New England and 17-day N\&S Carolina. Some decisions will not be made until March/April.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Meaningful adventure, not super rustic and no flights. More National Parks rather than cities with outdoor activities and recreation. Active Teen Tours include travel & service both East & West Coasts. Volunteer programs work on environmental, construction, wildlife projects & charitable organizations, with no work with community members or children. Online Academies are equivalent to what they would have done in Barcelona or other in-person locations previously.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Covid Protocols: Very top of website click Flexibility & Peace of Mind During Covid-19 - all protocols depicted there and this is constantly changing so they are updating regularly. WCC is making decisions along the way & will keep us informed as they know more from ACA, CDC, etc. On all programs they will have extra staff to deal with any issues that may arise and will be able to deal with a student (particularly covid) while the rest of the trip runs as planned. If quarantine is necessary, parents will have to step in (staff can’t leave trip for 14 days). A lot of trip leaders will be vaccinated but likely won’t be 100%; they’re advising them to get them whenever possible. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They feel they will operate in ‘real-world’ structure and will not have pod structure. They’ll continue to have rotated room assignments so as to not sacrifice who they are and to provide students with what they do best: making everyone feel comfortable and the group is well-connected all around. Mitch wholeheartedly feels integrity in all they do and values partnership tremendously with The Camp Experts team & will work with us on all aspects with families. Confident they’ll run programs but will look a little different.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Active Teen Tours are mostly domestic including travel & service both East & West Coasts. If the Canadian border is not opened up for trips that include a CA component, they’ll pivot with a backup plan (spend additional time in locations already planned on trip or add other intriguing locations). Western Canada probably won’t happen for Americans but perhaps good for Int’l and Canadian students. For both teen tours and global adventures Mitch felt multi-city European destinations would likely not happen but single location/city/country may work. He felt confident about: N.O., AL, HI, FL, CO, UT, AZ, Carolinas, New England & Costa Rica. Will California be a viable option given current severity of Covid-19? They don’t know but want to create alternatives & make adjustments accordingly. For several Global Adventures and the added East Coast trips there are no flights. Parent communication and policies: Very top of website click Flexibility & Peace of Mind During Covid-19. Tech/Online: Online Academy (website for course options & pricing not updated yet). Flexibility: Until April 1, trips are fully refundable. Families can switch trips until 5/1 w/ no penalties (would just pay difference if more $$). If WCC decides they can’t run a program they would give options to change trip or refund. CFAR (Cancel For Any Reason) insurance can be purchased by families and is reasonable but NOT ELIGIBLE TO NY RESIDENTS. If quarantine is necessary, parents will have to step in.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Confident they’ll run programs but will look a little different for Summer 2021, with traditional Teen Tours and Global Adventures plus NYC career experiences and an Online Academy. Active Teen Tours are mostly domestic including travel & service both East & West Coasts, while Global Adventures focus on more National Parks rather than cities with outdoor activities and recreation, comfortable lodges & hotels (not camping), and meaningful adventure, not super rustic and no flights. Volunteer Programs include New Orleans, Hawaii, Alaska, Florida and the Northeast (Maine, Boston, D.C.) plus several adventure/service opportunities in Costa Rica, working on environmental, construction, wildlife projects & charitable organizations, with no work with community members or children. Families who value flexibility (fully refundable until April 1; switch trips until 5/1; options to change trip or refund; CFAR available though NOT ELIGIBLE TO NY RESIDENTS) and clear Covid Protocols (dedicated team/committee, extra staff, quarantine support with parents stepping in) will appreciate WCC.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Two career experiences in NYC (Wall Street NYIF; Fashion Week Internships) with NYU dorms.
  - Online Academy (Mini and Extensive) with 540 students in 2020; equivalent to Barcelona or other in-person locations previously.
  - Active Teen Tours: mostly domestic including travel & service both East & West Coasts; 30-35 students in 2021 (vs 40-48 traditionally).
  - Global Adventures: 12-14 students, more National Parks, comfortable lodges & hotels (not camping); added 14-day New England and 17-day N\&S Carolina.
  - Flexibility: Until April 1, trips are fully refundable; families can switch trips until 5/1; change trip or refund if WCC decides they can’t run.
  - Covid Protocols: dedicated person and weekly committee, extra staff, ability to deal with a student while the rest of the trip runs.



#### Lowlights

  - Pre-College Enrichment: A big question??? American U & G-town not inviting summer programs on campus.
  - California viability is unknown; alternatives & adjustments may be needed.
  - Western Canada probably won’t happen for Americans.
  - Multi-city European destinations would likely not happen.
  - If quarantine is necessary, parents will have to step in (staff can’t leave trip for 14 days).
  - CFAR (Cancel For Any Reason) insurance is NOT ELIGIBLE TO NY RESIDENTS.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Summer 2021 - Confident they’ll run programs but will look a little different. In addition to traditional Teen Tours and Global Adventures (described below) they will have: 1. Two career experiences in NYC • Wall Street NY Institute of Finance (NYIF) - finish grades 9-12 only, up to 100 students, 7 or 14 days, dorms at NYU and back-up plan hotel they’ve used in the past. If not able to run in person, can do online format. • Fashion Week Internships (FWI) - finish grades 8-12, 40 students, 14 days, dorms at NYU. Program will likely fill by end of March. Will not run if can’t be in person due to internship structure. 2. Online Academy Two choices • Mini: 5 days, 75 min/day (6.25 hrs/week) Website for course options & pricing not updated yet. This program had 540 students in 2020. • Extensive: 5 days, 2hrs/day x 10 sessions (20 hrs over 2 wks). Website for course & pricing not updated yet. These academies are equivalent to what they would have done in Barcelona or other in-person locations previously. Active Teen Tours: mostly domestic including travel & service both East & West Coasts. Some decisions will not be made until March/April. Traditionally they have 40-48 students on trips, for 2021 they’re looking at 30-35 to meet regulation requirements from various States and bus companies. If Canadian border not opened up for trips that include CA component, they’ll pivot w/ backup plan (spend additional time in locations already planned on trip or add other intriguing locations). As for living arrangements, they feel they will operate in ‘real-world’ structure and will not have pod structure. They’ll continue to have rotated room assignments as of now (unless their consultant advises otherwise) so as to not sacrifice who they are and to provide students with what they do best: making everyone feel comfortable and the group is well-connected all around. Will California be a viable option given current severity of Covid-19? They don’t know but want to create alternatives & make adjustments accordingly, again, they’ll know more next month or two. Global Adventures: they’re finding most people are focused on these trips, 12-14 students, more National Parks rather than cities with outdoor activities and recreation, comfortable lodges & hotels (not camping) and they don’t have to modify much for these trips. If other trips can’t run then they are prepared to combine adventure programs which are 29-34 days. Also added two East Coast trips: 14-day New England and 17-day N\&S Carolina which include active adventure and no big cities. They’re meaningful adventure, not super rustic and no flights. Western Canada probably won’t happen for Americans but perhaps good for Int’l and Canadian students. Volunteer Programs: New Orleans, Hawaii, Alaska, Florida will run easily he feels. They’re also including Northeast in Maine, Boston, D.C. living mostly on college campuses (maybe suites-style hotel in D.C since G-town not allowing programs on campus this summer). Also several adventure/service opportunities in Costa Rica. On all service programs they will work on environmental, construction, wildlife projects & charitable organizations, no work with community members or children. For both teen tours and global adventures Mitch felt multi-city European destinations would likely not happen but single location/city/country may work. Decisions in next two months. He felt confident about: N.O., AL, HI, FL, CO, UT, AZ, Carolinas, New England & Costa Rica. Pre-College Enrichment: A big question??? American U & G-town not inviting summer programs on campus. UCLA they’ll know more mid-March. If not able to be at UCLA, they’re working with a private college in CA as substitute. McGill-border still an issue, more info in March/April. Covid Protocols: Very top of website click Flexibility & Peace of Mind During Covid-19 - all protocols depicted there and this is constantly changing so they are updating regularly. WCC is making decisions along the way & will keep up informed as they know more from ACA, CDC, etc. They have someone on their team dedicated to decisions and guidelines related to Covid- 19 and they have a committee that meets weekly for updates, etc. On all programs they will have extra staff to deal with any issues that may arise and will be able to deal with student (particularly covid) while rest of trip runs as planned. If quarantine is necessary, parents will have to step in (staff can’t leave trip for 14 days). A lot of trip leaders will be vaccinated but likely won’t be 100%. They’re advising them to get them whenever possible. Flexibility: Until April 1, trips are fully refundable. Families can switch trips until 5/1 w/ no penalties, would just pay difference if more $$. If WCC decides they can’t run a program they would give options to change trip or refund. CFAR (Cancel For Any Reason) insurance can be purchased by families and is reasonable but NOT ELIGIBLE TO NY RESIDENTS. title: Camp Micah — Monica — 8-25-22
text:
# Camp Micah



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Micah is a Jewish co-ed private camp with stunning facilities, top-notch programming, equipment and activity choices. They have 290 kids per session and they sell-out in September. Sessions are 3.5 weeks long. Younger kids that stay 1 session vs all summer is 50/50 but as they grow older more and more stay full summer. FACILITIES - they have amazing facilities and everything is in impeccable condition. The fields are very well taken care of.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

CAMP MICAH Monica, Karen, Pilar, Aline visited on August 2nd 2022. We were toured by Jody, one of the directors. We met Mark as well. Jody was so nice. DEMOGRAPHICS/LOGISTICS - 35% of campers come from New England, 35% from NY/NJ and the remaining from other US urban and suburban locations. They almost get no internationals because they sell-out so early in the fall. CAMPER PROFILE/CAMP PHILOSOPHY - there is very good balance between the arts and athletics. Camp suits equally both types of child. Kids seemed very nice, sociable, happy and proud about their camp. We talked to some really lovely kids.



## 3 · Living Quarters

BUNKING – Campers typically live in a bunk with 8-9 campers and 2-3 counselors. Bunks are equipped with 2 hot-water showers, 2 sinks, and 2 toilets. Kids clean but professionals sanitize bathrooms. Cabins look great and well organized. The girls village they now refer to as SABAGO rather than girls as they do have non binary kids and are catering to them.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

LAKE/POOL– Lake is beautiful and there is a great heated pool as well. They offer both motorized and non motorized water sports. They have instructional swim until they pass out test in \&th grade.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMING – camp has guided choice in younger years and the programming becomes more full-choice based as kids grow older. Everyone goes on an overnight with their bunk which becomes more challenging and a longer trip out as they grow older. CANTEEN - they have canteen and a rec room with ping pong, air hockey etc for free time/evenings. On Saturday mornings there is a buffet brunch and kids sleep in.



## 6 · Program Highlights

They have a great gymnastics program, dance and fitness for older campers. They offer hiking which is very popular. They have a great ninja course, two gaga pits in addition to most land sports. 9th grade boys build a sukkah as part of programming. Their last year of camp they travel to Israel and Poland. (When Campers are 16). SERVICES – They do a communal shabbat service on Friday nights run by the camp grandma who is 80 years old and is a rabbi. They do community service program called "Tikunamatata". The whole camp sings Oseh Shalom every night and Modeh Ani together every morning. They raise the US and Israel flag every day and lower flag every evening as a camp activity.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There was armed security at the gate of camp. Jody mentioned not being happy about that but feeling that it was best. DINING HALL – Dining hall is huge, beautiful and very clean. There was a wonderful smell of homemade cookies when we went in. Meals are served family style by bunk. They can handle all food allergies. They do serve peanut butter in a separate area. Food is kosher style (no pork, no shell fish) but meat is not kosher. They can accommodate a real kosher camper. We also saw the chef barbecuing outside and it looked and smelled amazing. The last night of camp is a banquet and kids dress up for that.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp Micah is a warm, caring environment. Strong emphasis on communal activity: shabbat, prayers at meals (jamotzi and abbreviated Birkat Hamazon), meal times. Great balance between sports, arts and outdoor skills and using the great outdoors of Maine.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Camp Micah is a Jewish co-ed private camp with stunning facilities, top-notch programming, and a great balance between arts, athletics, and outdoor skills in Maine. Sessions are 3.5 weeks long with 290 kids per session, they sell-out in September, and younger kids that stay 1 session vs all summer is 50/50 but as they grow older more and more stay full summer. The culture is warm, caring, and communal (shabbat, songs, prayers at meals, flag-raising), with guided choice evolving to more full-choice as campers age and a beautiful lake plus a great heated pool. Food is kosher style with strong allergy support, there is armed security at the gate, and program highlights range from ninja course, gymnastics, dance and hiking to overnights, a sukkah build and a culminating Israel/Poland trip when campers are 16.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Stunning facilities, top-notch programming, equipment and activity choices.
  - Warm, caring environment with strong emphasis on communal activity (shabbat, songs, prayers at meals, flag-raising).
  - Great balance between sports, arts and outdoor skills and using the great outdoors of Maine.
  - Beautiful lake and a great heated pool; both motorized and non motorized water sports.
  - Great gymnastics program, dance and fitness for older campers; ninja course; two gaga pits.
  - Hiking is very popular; everyone goes on an overnight with their bunk which becomes more challenging and a longer trip out as they grow older.
  - Dining hall is huge, beautiful and very clean; chef barbecuing; last-night banquet; can handle all food allergies.
  - Travel to Israel and Poland their last year of camp (when campers are 16).
  - Demographics: 35% New England, 35% NY/NJ, remaining other US; almost no internationals.



#### Lowlights

  - There was armed security at the gate of camp (Jody mentioned not being happy about that but feeling that it was best).
  - They almost get no internationals because they sell-out so early in the fall.
  - They sell-out in September.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Alpengirl — Jaymee — 10-9-23
text:
# Alpengirl



## 1 · Program Snapshot

ACA accredited. Been in business for 27 years. All Girls, Adventure, outdoor, rustic camp. Great transition from camp to adventure trip. Teach outdoor skills in a fun and campy/silly way. Prioritize fun and safety. PLACES OF TRAVEL: Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. Coastal and mountain back expeditions. Beautiful and scenic trips. TYPES OF EXPERIENCES include hiking, backpacking, cooking all meals daily, rock climbing, learning survival skills and about hygiene in the wilderness. PREREQUISITES: Girls must have some camping experience. Recommended for girls with little experience are 3–4-day trips. Must be experienced campers to do a 6 day + most challenging trips.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Gabby - main contact. ALL WOMAN staff is highly trained; all guides and drivers are in their 20s and HIGHLY experienced. Staff are Wilderness first aid trained or wilderness responders, lifeguards, and leaders in the backcountry. Their goal is to prioritize fun and safety. Girls come from all over the US and world. Mainly from Seattle and Bozeman and Jackson hole. You CAN go with friends. Most do not go with friends. They don’t let friends who come together stay in the first tent round so that everyone gets to know each other. 10–12 girls on a trip; 2–3 staff members. References are checked thoroughly\! If a girl has ADD or depression, Alpengirl calls the DR. or therapist to see if they are a good candidate. Alpengirl wants girls who want to be there and are excited to be there.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Live in tents, 4 per tent. Girls learn to set up tents. Most are campsites and have toilets and showers. Girls do laundry once or twice.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Some trips include water activities such as kayaking, tubing, hot springs, surfing, stand up paddle boarding, canoeing, and whitewater rafting.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Fun, feminine take on outdoors, backpacking, go to the bathroom in woods. Yoga every morning. Leader of day prepares tea, build leadership skills, earn skill beads on necklace (first aid, yoga, how to set up fire). Camp cooking—camp gourmet. Fresh organic; girls learn how to cook. Two-burner stoves. Good quality healthy food prepared by girls. Practice Leave No Trace—learn to go to the bathroom in the woods. USE TOILET PAPER. FOSTER LOVE OF OUTDOORS—learn outdoor living skills and teach about hygiene and cleanliness without daily hot showers. Explore nature while challenging yourself. Nail painting—girly but not too girly.



## 6 · Program Highlights

LAND: Rock climbing one day adventure. Do not need experience in any area except camping. Horseback riding. Included in most trips.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

ACA accredited. Their goal is to prioritize fun and safety. Staff are Wilderness first aid trained or wilderness responders and lifeguards. Staff holds money and meds—any meds are collected, distributed privately, and is written down. ASPENGIRL WILL accommodate SOME dietary issues. Accept vegetarian and gluten free. WILL NOT ACCEPT Vegan, strict kosher, or celiacs. Simply too tricky to navigate. Teach about hygiene and cleanliness without daily hot showers. Camp cooking—camp gourmet; fresh organic; good quality healthy food prepared by girls on two-burner stoves. References are checked thoroughly. If a girl has ADD or depression, Alpengirl calls the DR. or therapist to see if they are a good candidate.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

All Girls, Adventure, outdoor, rustic camp with a fun and campy/silly style. Fun, feminine take on the outdoors; nail painting—girly but not too girly. Alpengirl wants girls who want to be there are excited to be there. NOT A CANDIDATE: a girl whose parents want her to be there, but she does not want to be there. FOSTER LOVE OF OUTDOORS—explore nature while challenging yourself. Beautiful and scenic trips.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

WHERE TO FLY TO: Airport fly into Bozeman, Portland, or Seattle. Alpengirl makes arrangements for unaccompanied minors. Ride in ONE 15-passenger van. NO ACCESS TO cell PHONE. Girls can bring a camera. Communication with parents: 2–3 blog and pics taken by staff. Not a good fit for nervous parents that need communication. ½ through trip, staff calls parents to update. They let parents know when they arrive safely.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Alpengirl is an all-girls, adventure, outdoor, rustic camp and a great transition from camp to adventure trip. The all-woman staff is highly trained (WFA/WFR, lifeguards) and prioritizes fun and safety on beautiful and scenic trips in the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. Girls live in tents, practice Leave No Trace, do yoga every morning, cook fresh organic meals, and build leadership with daily roles and skill beads. Not a good fit for nervous parents who need frequent communication or for girls who do not want to be there.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - ACA accredited; been in business for 27 years.
  - All-woman, highly trained staff in their 20s (Wilderness First Aid/Responder, lifeguards, backcountry leaders).
  - Small group size: 10–12 girls on a trip with 2–3 staff.
  - Great transition from camp to adventure trip; teach outdoor skills in a fun and campy/silly way.
  - Beautiful and scenic coastal and mountain back expeditions in the Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies.
  - Daily yoga, leadership rotation (leader of the day prepares tea), and skill beads (first aid, yoga, how to set up fire).
  - Camp gourmet: fresh organic, good quality healthy food prepared by girls on two-burner stoves.
  - Strong safety and meds management; staff holds money and meds and documents distribution.
  - Arrangements for unaccompanied minors; airports: Bozeman, Portland, or Seattle.
  - Clear prerequisites: some camping experience; 3–4-day trips recommended for beginners; 6+ day trips for experienced campers.



#### Lowlights

  - No access to cell phone; limited parent communication (2–3 blogs plus a mid-trip call).
  - Not a good fit for nervous parents that need communication.
  - Dietary limitations: will not accept vegan, strict kosher, or celiacs.
  - Girls must want to be there; not a candidate if only parents want her to attend.
  - No daily hot showers; learning to go to the bathroom in the woods.
  - Friends who come together do not stay in the first tent round.
  - Must have prior camping experience for most trips.
  - Travel by one 15-passenger van.
  - Costs: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Staff are Wilderness first aid trained or wilderness responders, life guards, and Leaders in the back country. Their goal is to prioritize fun and safety. OVERVIEW: All Girls, Adventure, outdoor, rustic camp. Great transition from camp to adventure trip. Teach outdoor skills in a fun and campy/silly way. Prioritize fun. Alpengirl wants girls who want to be there are excited to be there. NOT A CANDIDATE: a girl whose parents want her to be there, but she does not want to be there.) References are checked thoroughly\! If a girl has ADD or depression, Alpen calls DR. or therapist to see if they are a good candidate. Girls come from all over the US and world. Mainly from Seattle and Bozeman and Jackson hole. You CAN go with friends. Most do not go with friends. Don’t let friends who come together stay in first tent round so that everyone gets to know each other 10-12 girls on a trip 2-3 Staff members WHERE TO FLY TO: Airport fly into Bozeman, Portland, or Seattle. Alpengirl makes arrangements for unaccompanied minors. PLACES OF TRAVEL: Pacific Northwest and Northern Rockies. Coastal and mountain back expeditions. Beautiful and scenic trips. PREREQUISITES: Girls must have some camping experience. Recommended for Girls with little experience are 3–4-day trips. Must be experienced campers to do a 6 day + most challenging trips. TYPES OF EXPERIENCES Hiking, backpacking, cooking all meals daily, rock climbing, learning survival skills and about hygiene in the wilderness. WATER: Some trips include water activities such as kayaking, tubing, hot springs, surfing, stand up paddle boarding, canoeing, White water rafting LAND: Rock climbing one day adventure. Do not need experience in any area except camping. Horseback riding. Included in most trips. DAILY RPUTINES: Fun, feminine take on outdoors, backpacking, go to bathroom in woods, yoga every morning. Leader of day prepares tea, build leadership skills, earn skill beads on necklace (fist aid, yoga, how to set up fire) Camp cooking-camp gourmet. Fresh organic, girls learn how to cook. Two burner stoves. Good quality healthy food prepared by girls. Practice leave no trace- learn to go to the bathroom in the woods. USE TOILET PAPER. FOSTER LOVE OF OUTDOORS- learn outdoor living skills and teach about hygiene and cleanliness without daily hot showers. Explore nature while challenging yourself. Nail painting- girly but not too girly ACCOMODATIONS Live in tents, 4 per tent. Ride in ONE 15 passenger van. Girls Learn to set up tents. Most are campsites and have toilets and showers. Girls do Laundry one or twice. IMPORTANT THINGS TO KNOW: ASPENGIRL WILL Accommodate SOME dietary issues. Accept vegetarian and gluten free. WILL NOT ACCEPT Vegan, strict kosher, or celiacs. Simply too tricky to navigate. NO ACCESS TO cell PHONE Girls Can bring a camera. Communication with parents 2-3 blog and pics taken by staff. title: Camp Arcadia — Melissa — 2-18-23
text:
# Camp Arcadia



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Arcadia is an all girls camp in Casco, Maine. They have about 160-200 campers per session. Their mission is to make the world more beautiful and better, to foster personal growth, build lifelong skills, and grow respect and love for the outdoors. Campers represent 26 states and 3 countries and they provide approximately 30 scholarships each year.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Met with Sasha, who is a former camper and current staff member. The camper to counselor ratio is 3:1.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bathrooms: 1st-3rd grade: Bathrooms are in the cabin. 4th-7th: Sinks and toilets are in the cabin but showers are in the shower house. CTG: Have an area called “lower con” that is a bath facility and shower house.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

All campers are required to reach certain ability levels in Boating, Swimming, Campcraft, and Canoeing.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers are grouped according to experience level in activities and are required to master certain levels. Once they reach certain levels in the required activities, their assigned activities lighten up. There are 22 electives to choose from.



## 6 · Program Highlights

For activities, they do not consider themselves a sports focused camp however they are building a pickleball center and they have 4 new tennis courts. EXTRA’s: Horseback Riding, Lifeguarding, Piano Lessons, and tutoring.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Medical: They have just established a “chill zone” for girls who need a breather if they get triggered. They have a social worker on staff as well as 3 nurses. Closest hospital is 40 mins away. Religion: They do have grace at meals and God is mentioned but not Jesus.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They have a diverse camp culture with campers from the entire east coast and across the US. This is a no frills, rustic camp where they are looking for girls who are willing to try new things and are motivated. They like down to earth girls.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Arcadia is an all girls camp in Casco, Maine with about 160-200 campers per session. Their mission is to make the world more beautiful and better, to foster personal growth, build lifelong skills, and grow respect and love for the outdoors. This is a no frills, rustic camp where they are looking for girls who are willing to try new things and are motivated; they like down to earth girls. All campers are required to reach certain ability levels in Boating, Swimming, Campcraft, and Canoeing, there are 22 electives to choose from, and they do not consider themselves a sports focused camp however they are building a pickleball center and they have 4 new tennis courts.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - They have a diverse camp culture with campers from the entire east coast and across the US.
  - Campers represent 26 states and 3 countries and they provide approximately 30 scholarships each year.
  - The camper to counselor ratio is 3:1.
  - They are building a pickleball center and they have 4 new tennis courts.
  - There are 22 electives to choose from.
  - They have just established a “chill zone” for girls who need a breather if they get triggered.
  - They have a social worker on staff as well as 3 nurses.



#### Lowlights

  - Closest hospital is 40 mins away.
  - 4th-7th: Sinks and toilets are in the cabin but showers are in the shower house.
  - They do not consider themselves a sports focused camp.
  - Religion: They do have grace at meals and God is mentioned but not Jesus.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Eisner — Michelle — 7-17-24
text:
# Eisner



## 1 · Program Snapshot

2 sessions offered, first is 4 weeks second is 3. 50% of campers stay full summer. 2nd and 3rd graders are offered a 2 week session. 496 kids at camp at a time. Coming from NY area, Massachusetts, NJ, some PA/CT/RI and "a couple Florida." Schedule is full elective style. All choice programming even for the youngest age groups. All activities are co Ed. Religion is a huge part of camp. Day camp runs parallel to camp from 8am til 4pm; parents drop off and pick up children. Stays separated.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Tour given by Adam Kohane 7/15. Toured with Heather Pasquale, Lana Ast and Emily Rothenberg. Many alumni send their kids; rate of alumni campers is 15-25%. Campers have a 90% retention rate and are described as "spirited, outgoing and genuinely want to be at camp." Song leaders for every age group to lead prayers. Licensed child psychologists and social workers on staff.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Boys and girls bunks kept separate. Oldest boys and girls bunks have communal bathrooms. Transgender kids bunk where they feel they fit and where their family feels they fit. Laundry service once a week, campers bring towels.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake had inflatables; nothing motorized offered. Kayaks and canoes offered. Day trips to Crane Lake are offered for more lake. 2 pools; one lap and one general. Jr lifeguarding program done in the lap pool and has been popular. Instructional swim mandated for younger ages.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Schedule is full elective style. All choice programming even for the youngest age groups. Campers choose schedules in the beginning of the week and it has a 5 day duration until they switch it up. Though elective style, kids are mandated to attend Jewish learning. Shabbat services are held Friday night with kaballat into dinner and a 1 hour service afterward. There are song leaders for every age group to lead prayers. There are songs and Israeli dance. Saturday is a relaxed schedule with Torah service (arks are located in the outdoor amphitheater type facility) which is longer than 1 hour and there are 2 rest periods without activities. S’licha is held where campers talk amongst bunkmates about the week and what was good or bad and how to resolve any issues that arose. Havdalah is held at the conclusion of Shabbat for 15/20 minutes. Ma Ariv is every evening for 20-30 min. Blessings done at meals.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Street hockey. Music studio. Dance (NO GYMNASTICS OFFERED). Ropes (high and low). Fitness (weights, aerobics). Archery. Yoga. Updated tennis and pickle ball. Fine arts (pottery facility newly redone). Maker space - has AC and offers digital arts, basic level 3D printing and engineering type stuff. Farm program with animals (goats, donkeys, puppies, cow). Garden. Basketball. Baseball. Flag football. Theater: 4 plays first session. Oldest campers write their own play. Self defense. Wood Shop. Soccer. Gaga. Compete in intercamp competition. Tournaments and teams are not co Ed. Trips: oldest kids go to Israel. All of upper camp does an overnight to places like Cape Cod, Boston, Lake George, Vermont. Second session only has color war; 4 teams not 2.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

2 security guards at gate everyday. Health Center has 1 doctor on 1 week shifts all summer, always 3 or 4 nurses. Licensed child psychologists and social workers on staff. Meals are kosher style with options for fully kosher kids. Dining Hall has AC. Kids sit by bunk with family style meals; younger ages eat breakfast first. Can handle any food allergy. Vegetarian, vegan, dairy free, gluten free, etc all offered. Canteen - "mo adon" is twice a week and is a slushy and a piece of candy or a jibbit for your croc. Blessings done at meals.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religion is a huge part of camp. Welcoming and accepting environment for kids who may not practice Judaism at home or have parents in an interfaith marriage. All are made to feel comfortable at Eisner. Welcoming and accepting environment for LGBTQ+ children. Camp is decorated with handprints of the oldest campers to leave their mark. Campers are described as spirited, outgoing and genuinely want to be at camp. There are songs and Israeli dance.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Day camp (We do not get a commission on the day camp) runs parallel to camp from 8am til 4pm; parents drop off and pick up children. Stays separated. Tuition for 2025 not determined yet. Tuition varies by age and whether or not families are members of URJ. 2024 mid range campers (entering grade 6) was $7500 for members and $7800 for non members session 1; $6100 for members and $6400 for non members session 2; $12,900 for members and $13,200 for non members full summer. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Eisner offers two sessions (4 weeks and 3 weeks) with a full-summer option, a fully elective program even for the youngest campers, and a strong Jewish life woven throughout daily and Shabbat experiences. Aquatics include a lake with inflatables (no motorized boats), kayaks and canoes, two pools with instructional swim for younger campers, and a popular Jr lifeguarding program. Program breadth spans sports, arts, ropes, maker space (AC), farm and garden, theater (four plays first session), trips and overnights, and color war second session (four teams). The community is welcoming and accepting (including interfaith and LGBTQ+ families), with robust health supports, kosher style meals with extensive dietary accommodations, and campers described as spirited, outgoing and genuinely wanting to be at camp.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Full elective style; all choice programming even for the youngest age groups.
  - Religion is a huge part of camp; mandated Jewish learning, Shabbat services, Ma Ariv every evening, and songs and Israeli dance.
  - Two pools (one lap, one general) plus a lake with inflatables; kayaks and canoes; day trips to Crane Lake; Jr lifeguarding popular; instructional swim for younger ages.
  - Inclusive environment for interfaith and LGBTQ+ children; transgender kids bunk where they and their family feel they fit.
  - Strong health staffing: Health Center has 1 doctor on 1 week shifts all summer, always 3 or 4 nurses; licensed child psychologists and social workers on staff.
  - Dining Hall has AC; maker space has AC; meals are kosher style with extensive dietary accommodations.
  - High camper retention (about 90%) and alumni campers 15-25%.
  - Wide geographic draw (NY area, Massachusetts, NJ, some PA/CT/RI and a couple Florida) and 496 kids at camp at a time.
  - Trips include oldest kids go to Israel; upper camp overnights to Cape Cod, Boston, Lake George, Vermont.
  - Second session only has color war; 4 teams not 2.



#### Lowlights

  - NO GYMNASTICS OFFERED.
  - Tournaments and teams are not co Ed.
  - Oldest boys and girls bunks have communal bathrooms.
  - Tuition for 2025 not determined yet; tuition varies by age and URJ membership.
  - We do not get a commission on the day camp.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Tour given by Adam Kohane 7/15 Toured with Heather Pasquale, Lana Ast and Emily Rothenberg 2 security guards at gate everyday Day camp (We do not get a commission on the day camp ) runs parallel to camp from 8am til 4pm; parents drop off and pick up children. Stays separated. 2 sessions offered, first is 4 weeks second is 3. Tuition for 2025 not determined yet. 50% of campers stay full summer. 2nd and 3rd graders are offered a 2 week session. Many alumni send their kids; rate of alumni campers is 15-25%. Tuition varies by age and whether or not families are members of URJ. 2024 mid range campers (entering grade 6) was $7500 for members and $7800 for non members session 1; $6100 for members and $6400 for non members session 2; $12,900 for members and $13,200 for non members full summer. 496 kids at camp at a time. Coming from NY area, Massachusetts, NJ, some PA/CT/RI and “a couple Florida.” Schedule is full elective style. All choice programming even for the youngest age groups. Campers choose schedules in the beginning of the week and it has a 5 day duration until they switch it up. All activities are co Ed. Lake had inflatables nothing motorized offered. Kayaks and canoes offered. Day trips to crane lake are offered for more lake. 2 pools; one lap and one general. Jr lifeguarding program done in the lap pool and has been popular. Instructional swim mandated for younger ages. Religion is a huge part of camp. Though elective style, kids are mandated to attend Jewish learning. Shabbat services are held Friday night with kaballat into dinner and a 1 hour service afterward. There are song leaders for every age group to lead prayers. There are songs and Israeli dance. Saturday is a relaxed schedule with Torah service (arks are located in the outdoor amphitheater type facility) which is longer than 1 hour and there are 2 rest periods without activities. S’licha is held where campers talk amongst bunkmates about the week and what was good or bad and how to resolve any issues that arose. Havdalah is held at the conclusion of Shabbat for 15/20 minutes. Ma Ariv is every evening for 20-30 min. Blessings done at meals. Meals are kosher style with options for fully kosher kids. Dining Hall has AC. Kids sit by bunk with family style meals; younger ages eat breakfast first. Can handle any food allergy. Vegetarian, vegan, dairy free, gluten free, etc all offered. Canteen- “mo adon” is twice a week and is a slushy and a piece of candy or a jibbit for your croc. Compete in intercamp competition. Tournaments and teams are not co Ed. Welcoming and accepting environment for kids who may not practice Judaism at home or have parents in an interfaith marriage. All are made to feel comfortable at Eisner. Welcoming and accepting environment for LGBTQ+ children. Transgender kids bunk where they feel they fit and where their family feels they fit. Licensed child psychologists and social workers on staff. Will take campers with ADHD and autism “if it makes sense.” Health Center has 1 doctor on 1 week shifts all summer, always 3 or 4 nurses. Trips: oldest kids go to Israel. All of upper camp does an overnight to places like cape cod, Boston, lake George, Vermont. Street hockey Music studio Dance (NO GYMNASTICS OFFERED) Ropes (high and low) Fitness (weights, aerobics) Archery Yoga Updated tennis and pickle ball Fine arts (pottery facility newly redone) maker space- has AC and offers digital arts, basic level 3D printing and engineering type stuff Farm program with animals (goats, donkeys, puppies,cow) Garden Basketball Baseball Flag football Theater: 4 plays first session. Oldest campers write their own play. Self defense Wood Shop Soccer Gaga Laundry service once a week, campers bring towels. Boys and girls bunks kept separate. Oldest boys and girls bunks have communal bathrooms. Campers have a 90% retention rate and are described as “spirited, outgoing and genuinely want to be at camp.” Second session only has color war; 4 teams not 2. Camp is decorated with handprints of the oldest campers to leave their mark.

title: WOLFEBORO CAMP-SCHOOL — DARA — 9-17-24
text:
# WOLFEBORO CAMP/SCHOOL



## 1 · Program Snapshot

110 students for 5 weeks - 60% male – 40% female. Students come mostly from private & boarding schools. 11 year old going into 6 th grade (will take a 10 year old if older sibling is there) through 17/18 years old. 40% international. Domestic students are primarily from the Northeast. Looks like a camp, but functions like a school. Mission specific for academic preparedness. All about process and routine. Teaches students how to do things and get them done.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Students come mostly from private & boarding schools. 60% male – 40% female. 11 year old going into 6 th grade (will take a 10 year old if older sibling is there) through 17/18 years old. 40% international; domestic students are primarily from the Northeast. Teachers only teach academic classes. Residential faculty manage camp activities (must have minimum 2 years college, some are young teachers). Director: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

2 students/tent. Tent on raised platform, no electricity. Very small. Students live out of their trunk/suitcase. Small nightstand with shelves. Battery operated fans. Tents in circle around upper field area. Residential faculty scattered in their cabins throughout. Teachers in buildings around campus. Bathroom/showers around tents.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

2:00 – 5:00pm – camp activities/sports/waterfront (H20 2-4pm structured classes, 4-5pm open swim/boat). Waterfront with non-motorized boating, swimming & fishing. Rowing program – $1500 extra. Train every day but Sunday. Arrive 5:50am, row 6-7am before breakfast. Students must get themselves up. Coached by Dale Hurly – Coach @ Andover.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Monday – Friday 7:15am – Wake; 7:45am – Breakfast; 8:30am-12:30 pm – Academic hours (5 periods with 3 classes and 2 unscheduled periods for daytime study hall, summer reading or start homework); 1:00 – 1:40 pm – Lunch; 1:40 – 2:00 pm – Rest; 2:00 – 5:00pm – camp activities/sports/waterfront (H20 2-4pm structured classes, 4-5pm open swim/boat); 5:00 – 6:00pm meetings or intramurals; 6:15 – 6:40pm dinner; 6:45pm - downtime; 7:30 – 9:15pm Study Hall (assigned seat in assigned building. Boys have AC in their buildings, girls have newer buildings with fans); 9:30pm – Free time; 9:45pm – Lights out Middle school; 10:00pm – Lights out Upper school. Saturday - regular schedule through 4:00 pm, 4-10pm – special events/trips, 10:30 lights out. Sunday - sleep in, late breakfast, 11am-4:30 special trips, 5:00 intramurals, regular schedule starting 6:15 with dinner. Youngest kids have mandatory activity schedule; older kids can choose electives – they pick for a week at a time. SCHOOL: Homework done every day to students' ability and teachers' quality. Earn a grade in each class daily. Students aren’t done with their class until the teacher knows they can do their homework on their own. Every class starts with students writing down their homework assignment. 3-6 students/class. Outdoor above ground open air tents for Humanities classes. Indoor classrooms with more white board space for math & science. Each student has a goals document created with home teachers & family – where they are, where they need to be at end of summer, and then Wolfeboro creates their individual curriculum and schedule.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Art center with ceramics, kiln, beading, painting/drawing. Sports – tennis, lax, basketball, soccer, volleyball, kickball, games. Formal tennis lessons with an outside instructor for an additional fee. Mountain Day – every two weeks they take everyone out for a mid week afternoon hike.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

3 structured meals a day with assigned seats. Students take turns being waiter. Nut free and can accommodate most allergies. Nurse onsite 24 hours/day. Will take a diabetic.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Looks like a camp, but functions like a school. Mission specific for academic preparedness. All about process and routine. Teaches students how to do things and get them done.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Parents can drop off at camp, or they fly to Boston and Wolfeboro provides a van. No computers/screens – all work is done by hand. Cell phone checked in office at start of summer. If students have free time if all work is done and reading up to date, they can use the phone for an hour. Extra costs: Rowing program – $1500 extra. Formal tennis lessons with an outside instructor for an additional fee. Parent communication: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Wolfeboro looks like a camp, but functions like a school that is mission specific for academic preparedness. 110 students for 5 weeks with 60% male – 40% female, ages 11 (will take a 10 year old if older sibling is there) through 17/18, with 40% international and domestic students primarily from the Northeast. The program is all about process and routine: small classes (3-6 students), daily homework and grades, no computers/screens, and a structured weekday schedule with evening study hall. Afternoons offer camp activities, a waterfront with non-motorized boating (including a rowing program), arts, and sports, with youngest kids on mandatory activities and older kids choosing weekly electives.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Mission specific for academic preparedness.
  - Looks like a camp, but functions like a school.
  - 3-6 students/class.
  - No computers/screens – all work is done by hand.
  - Each student has a goals document created with home teachers & family, and Wolfeboro creates their individual curriculum and schedule.
  - H20 2-4pm structured classes, 4-5pm open swim/boat.
  - Nurse onsite 24 hours/day.



#### Lowlights

  - 2 students/tent. Tent on raised platform, no electricity. Very small.
  - Students live out of their trunk/suitcase.
  - Rowing program – $1500 extra.
  - Formal tennis lessons with an outside instructor for an additional fee.
  - Cell phone checked in office at start of summer; limited access (one hour if all work is done and reading up to date).



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

110 students for 5 weeks - 60% male – 40% female. Students come mostly from private & boarding schools. - 11 year old going into 6 th grade (will take a 10 year old if older sibling is there) through 17/18 years old - 40% international. Teaches students how to do things and get them done. - Parents can drop off at camp, or they fly to Boston and Wolfeboro provides a van - Teachers only teach academic classes. - Residential faculty manage camp activities (must have minimum 2 years college, some are young teachers) SCHEDULE Monday – Friday 7:15am – Wake 7:45am – Breakfast 8:30am-12:30 pm – Academic hours (5 periods with 3 classes and 2 unscheduled periods for daytime study hall, summer reading or start homework) 1:00 – 1:40 pm – Lunch 1:40 – 2:00 pm – Rest 2:00 – 5:00pm – camp activities/sports/waterfront (H20 2-4pm structured classes, 4-5pm open swim/boat) 5:00 – 6:00pm meetings or intermurals 6:15 – 6:40pm dinner 6:45pm - downtime 7:30 – 9:15pm Study Hall (assigned seat in assigned building. Boys have AC in their buildings, girls have newer buildings with fans) 9:30pm – Free time 9:45pm – Lights out Middle school 10:00pm – Lights out Upper school Saturday - regular schedule through 4:00 pm, 4-10pm – special events/trips, 10:30 lights out Sunday - sleep in, late breakfast, 11am-4:30 special trips, 5:00 intermurals, regular schedule starting 6:15 with dinner. title: Cardigan Mountain — Michele — 7-30-25
text:
# Cardigan Mountain



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Visited by Michele Gershwin and Emily Rothenberg July 21, 2025. Toured by Matt Rinkin. Right now they have 155 kids ages 8-15 (in the winter, they are a boarding school for middle schoolers with 235 students). Very pretty campus on a lake with private area for watersports like rowing/sailing/dock for swimming. The academic quad has residential buildings, some newer buildings with STEM (classes looked fun and engaging, small amount of students maybe less than 10 in each). Kids can do 2 weeks or 4 weeks.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

During the year this is an all boys school but in the summer it is co-ed (during the year they have a big Hockey program and have an affiliation with Dartmouth). Kids were very sweet and friendly. Classes looked fun and engaging, small amount of students maybe less than 10 in each. Toured by Matt Rinkin.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The academic quad has residential buildings. Dorms were nice, but no A/C because it's New Hampshire and he said it doesn’t get very hot. The rooms were neat and very comfortable. Some kids brought fans but these are low-rise buildings, airy and spacious. It was chilly the day we went and he said that the typical weather there is never too hot.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Very pretty campus on a lake with private area for watersports like rowing/sailing/dock for swimming. The outdoor sports/waterfront feels more camp-like.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Kids can do 2 weeks or 4 weeks. 70 different classes to choose from (we saw them in robotics and ceramics- looked fun). All of them are “enrichment classes” — the goal is to make education fun. Classes looked fun and engaging, small amount of students maybe less than 10 in each.



## 6 · Program Highlights

The academic quad has residential buildings, some newer buildings with STEM. The student store is very cute, has camp swag and kids can buy anything they forgot. The student center has a nice rec room for indoor play like ping pong and games.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining room very nice with A/C (Matt had us stay for lunch and the food was very good/healthy options, good salad bar plus hot food like a private school cafeteria). Health/safety details beyond food and facilities: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Kids were very sweet and friendly. All of them are “enrichment classes” — the goal is to make education fun. During the year this is an all boys school but in the summer it is co-ed (during the year they have a big Hockey program and have an affiliation with Dartmouth). We would give this with Wolfeboro but it’s important to note they are very different. Also Wolfeboro (see our notes for more details) is MUCH more expensive and for a student on a specific mission; this is more for enrichment and making learning fun plus the outdoor sports/waterfront feels more camp-like. Could also give it with the Tilton School (similar).



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Bus from NYC and Boston. They do have photos on the website for parents and they can email. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Cardigan Mountain has a very pretty campus on a lake with a private area for watersports like rowing/sailing and a dock for swimming. In the summer it is co-ed with 155 kids ages 8-15, offering 2-week or 4-week sessions and 70 different “enrichment classes” that make education fun with small classes (maybe less than 10). Dorms were nice and comfortable low-rise buildings (no A/C), while the dining room is very nice with A/C and the food was very good with healthy options, a good salad bar, and hot food like a private school cafeteria. Kids were very sweet and friendly, there’s a cute student store and a student center with a nice rec room, and the outdoor sports/waterfront feels more camp-like.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Very pretty campus on a lake with private area for watersports like rowing/sailing/dock for swimming
  - 70 different classes to choose from (we saw them in robotics and ceramics- looked fun)
  - All of them are “enrichment classes” the goal is to make education fun
  - Classes looked fun and engaging, small amount of students maybe less than 10 in each
  - Dining room very nice with A/C (Matt had us stay for lunch and the food was very good/healthy options, good salad bar plus hot food like a private school cafeteria)
  - The student store is very cute, has camp swag and kids can buy anything they forgot
  - The student center has a nice rec room for indoor play like ping pong and games
  - Bus from NYC and Boston
  - Kids were very sweet and friendly



#### Lowlights

  - Dorms were nice, but no A/C because it's New Hampshire and he said it doesn’t get very hot.
  - Wolfeboro is much more intense academically.



#### Similar Camps

  - Wolfeboro
  - Tilton School



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Visited by Michele Gershwin and Emily Rothenberg July 21, 2025 Toured by Matt Rinkin Right now they have 155 kids ages 8-15 (in the winter, they are a boarding school for middle schoolers with 235 students) Very pretty campus on a lake with private area for waterspouts like rowing/sailing/dock for swimming The academic quad has residential buildings, some newer buildings with STEM (classes looked fun and engaging, small amount of students maybe less than 10 in each) Kids can do 2 weeks or 4 weeks, they were very sweet and friendly Bus from NYC and Boston 70 different classes to choose from (we saw them in robotics and ceramics- looked fun) All of them are “enrichment classes” the goal is to make education fun The student store is very cute, has camp swag and kids can buy anything they forgot, also the student center has a nice rec room for indoor play like ping pong and games Dining room very nice with A/C (Matt had us stay for lunch and the food was very good/healthy options, good salad bar plus hot food like a private school cafeteria) Dorms were nice, but no A/C because its New Hampshire and he said it doesn’t get very hot. The rooms were neat and very comfortable. Some kids brought fans but these are low-rise buildings, airy and spacious. It was chilly the day we went and he said that the typical weather there never too hot. They do have photos on the website for parents and they can email During the year this is an all boys school but in the summer it is co-ed (during the year they have a big Hockey program and have an affiliation with Dartmouth) We would give this with Wolfeboro but it’s important to note they are very different. Wolfeboro is very rustic (kids live in tents without a bathroom inside) and Wolfeboro is much more intense academically. Also worlfeboro \*see our notes for more details is MUCH more expensive and for a student on a specific mission this is more for enrichment and making learning fun plus the outdoor sports/waterfront feels more camp-like. Could also give it with the Tilton School (similar)
