title: Rustic Pathways — Allison — 1-27-23
text:
# Rustic Pathways



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Their mission is to take middle, high school and college age students to explore the world through meaningful community service, cultural immersion and leave a positive impact on places they visit. They have both US and International staff. One leader for every six to seven students with a minimum of two leaders on every trip (one male, one female). Trips average about 15 students per trip with 6-8 being on the more rustic and adventurous trips and 25 on the trip closer to home. They have 40 programs, with about 70-80% of programs including community service; most programs average about 15-20 hours (some as little as 8 or up to 50). Language is NOT required. Vaccinations (covid or otherwise) are NOT required UNLESS country requires them. NO Academic credit is awarded.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

What types of students travel with RP:
• Kids who went to sleepaway and are ready for next step/adventure
• Kids ready to travel apart from their family
• Those looking for a new and bigger adventure
• Those preparing for college and want to have fun but also do meaningful service
• 70-80% of students are from the US, others are from Europe, South America, Mexico, and Asian population is growing
• Boy/Girl Ratio is 40/60
Staffing: They have both US and International staff. One leader for every six to seven students with a minimum of two leaders on every trip (one male, one female). International Staff have connection to the community.
Director/POC: Rustic Pathways Call January 25, 2023 Presented by Nicholas Pompa. Nick’s contact info: 650-526-8172, nicholas@rusticpathways.com. Nick joined last year and will be our point of contact.
Admission Counselors: Wyatt Strombom (based in the US); Emily Green (based in Costa Rica).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations: Some are nicer than others: hostels, hotels, eco resorts, huts, tents, home stays and base houses. Talk to them about what your student is comfortable with.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Hawaii: star gazing at observatory on top of volcano (non-aquatic but notable). Australia: snorkel Great Barrier Reef. France (new for this year): surfing in Bordeaux. Fiji: study environmental impact, sleep in huts on the beach. Dominican Republic: stunning beaches. Costa Rica: rain forest and turtles.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Daily timetable: Unknown. Trips average about 15 students per trip with 6-8 being on the more rustic and adventurous trips and 25 on the trip closer to home. Most programs average about 15-20 hours community service (some as little as 8 or up to 50). Will allow a group that knows each other to travel together.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Where do they go (have 40 programs):
• Hawaii - do star gazing at observatory on top of volcano
• Alaska
• Central and South America:
  o Costa Rica - very popular, run multiple programs, 2 wks community service with ecological impact, soccer and service, rain forest, turtles etc
  o Peru - currently scheduled but keeping an eye on situation
• France - new for this year. Cooking classes in Paris, fine art museum, surfing in Bordeaux, hike Pyrenes and do cheesemaking
• South Korea - history, culture and food
• Italy
• Croatia - new program. Lighter on service, visit UNESCO world heritage sites, emphasis on history
• Australia - snorkel Great Barrier Reef, volunteer at Sydney Zoo
• Dominican Republic - stunning beaches and do work with sugar cane population
• Nepal
• India
• Thailand - more high-end. Stay in eco-resorts and work with elephant conservation
• Vietnam
• Fiji - study environmental impact, sleep in huts on the beach
• Morocco - is more rustic program, immersion in local culture



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Safety is biggest concern. Do medical evaluations before students travel. If student takes medication, they bring it themselves, but leader will carry back-up meds. Partner with international safety company to ensure security and provide med evacs etc if necessary. Vaccinations (covid or otherwise) are NOT required UNLESS country requires them (ie Fiji requires COVID vaccination). Language is NOT required. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Mission-driven toward meaningful community service and cultural immersion with a positive impact on places they visit. Mix of more high-end (e.g., Thailand eco-resorts) and more rustic programs (e.g., Morocco; smaller 6-8 student groups on the most adventurous trips). Student profile includes kids ready for the next step/adventure apart from family, those seeking a new and bigger adventure, and those preparing for college who want to have fun but also do meaningful service. Diverse participant mix with 70-80% from the US and growing international representation; Boy/Girl Ratio is 40/60.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Provide chaperoned flights from LAX, Miami, JFK or EWR or students can meet at airport upon arrival with team. Vaccinations are NOT required UNLESS the destination country requires them. Language is NOT required. NO Academic credit is awarded. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs and pricing: Unknown. Travel insurance/med evacs supported via partner with international safety company.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Their mission is to take middle, high school and college age students to explore the world through meaningful community service, cultural immersion and leave a positive impact on places they visit. Trips average about 15 students per trip, with one leader for every six to seven students (minimum two leaders on every trip, one male, one female), and about 70-80% of programs include 15-20 hours of community service (some as little as 8 or up to 50). They run about 40 programs across Hawaii, Alaska, Central and South America (Costa Rica, Peru), Europe (France, Italy, Croatia), Asia (South Korea, Thailand, Vietnam, India), Australia, Fiji, Dominican Republic, Nepal, and Morocco, with accommodations ranging from hostels, hotels, eco-resorts and base houses to huts, tents and home stays. Safety is the biggest concern (medical evaluations, leader carries back-up meds, partner with an international safety company), language and vaccinations are NOT required unless the destination country requires them, and they provide chaperoned flights from LAX, Miami, JFK or EWR or students can meet upon arrival.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - One leader for every six to seven students with a minimum of two leaders on every trip (one male, one female).
  - International Staff have connection to the community.
  - Safety is biggest concern; medical evaluations before travel; leader carries back-up meds; partner with international safety company for security and med evacs.
  - Have 40 programs across a wide range of destinations with clear thematic activities.
  - About 70-80% of programs include community service; they will award service certificates.
  - Provide chaperoned flights from LAX, Miami, JFK or EWR or students can meet at airport upon arrival with team.
  - Will allow a group that knows each other to travel together.
  - Language is NOT required; vaccinations NOT required unless country requires them.
  - Admission Counselors: Wyatt Strombom (US) and Emily Green (Costa Rica).



#### Lowlights

  - NO Academic credit is awarded.
  - Daily timetable: Unknown.
  - Food details: Unknown.
  - Tech policy: Unknown.
  - Parent communication details: Unknown.
  - Some programs are more rustic and adventurous (smaller 6-8 student groups), which may not fit every student.
  - Peru is currently scheduled but they are keeping an eye on the situation.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Rustic Pathways Call January 25, 2023 Presented by Nicholas Pompa Nick’s contact info: 650-526-8172 nicholas@rusticpathways.com Nick joined last year and will be our point of contact. Their mission is to take middle, high school and college age students to explore the world through meaningful community service, cultural immersion and leave a positive impact on places they visit. They have both US and International staff. One leader for every six to seven students with a minimum of two leaders on every trip (one male, one female). International Staff have connection to the community. Safety is biggest concern. Do medical evaluations before students travel. If student takes medication, they bring it themselves, but leader will carry back-up meds. Partner with international safety company to ensure security and provide med evacs etc if necessary. Trips average about 15 students per trip with 6-8 being on the more rustic and adventurous trips and 25 on the trip closer to home. What types of students travel with RP: • Kids who went to sleepaway and are ready for next step/adventure • Kids ready to travel apart from their family • Those looking for a new and bigger adventure • Those preparing for college and want to have fun but also do meaningful service • 70-80% of students are from the US, others are from Europe, South America, Mexico, and Asian population is growing • Boy/Girl Ratio is 40/60 Where do they go (have 40 programs): • Hawaii-do star gazing at observatory on top of volcano • Alaska • Central and South America o Costa Rica-very popular, run multiple programs, 2 wks community service with ecological impact, soccer and service, rain forest, turtles etc o Peru-currently scheduled but keeping an eye on situation • France-new for this year. Cooking classes in Paris, fine art museum, surfing in Bordeaux, hike Pyrenes and do cheesemaking • South Korea-history, culture and food • Italy • Croatia-new program. Lighter on service, visit UNESCO world heritage sites, emphasis on history • Australia-snorkel Great Barrier Reef, volunteer at Sydney Zoo • Dominican Republic-stunning beaches and do work with sugar cane population • Nepal • India • Thailand-more high-end. Stay in eco-resorts and work with elephant conservation • Vietnam • Fiji-study environmental impact, sleep in huts on the beach • Morocco-is more rustic program, immersion in local culture Accommodations: Some are nicer than others: hostels, hotels, eco resorts, huts, tents, home stays and base houses. Talk to them about what your student is comfortable with. Service: Most programs average about 15-20 hours community service (some as little as 8 or up to 50). About 70-80% of programs have some service. They will award service certificates Language is NOT required Vaccinations (covid or otherwise) are NOT required UNLESS country requires them (ie Fiji requires COVID vaccination) NO Academic credit is awarded Will allow a group that knows each other to travel together Provide chaperoned flights from LAX, Miami, JFK or EWR or students can meet at airport upon arrival with team Admission Counselors: Wyatt Strombom (based in the US) Emily Green (based in Costa Rica)

title: Seneca Lake — Amanda — 8-22-22
text:
# Seneca Lake



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Camp Seneca Lake Tour: July 13, 2022 Guide: Andrew Leibowitz, Special Activities Coordinator & Mini Camp Director. About the Camp Overview: Coed Modern Orthodox Jewish Camp. Sessions: Offers 2 – 3.5 week sessions; Full summer (most kids stay full); 1 week mini- camp the first week of camp. Activities Overview: THIS IS A BASKETBALL & DANCE Camp.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Director/Owner: - Irv Bader, Founder & Director - Ross Zuckerman, Assistant Director. Campers: - 550 campers (unclear if number changes by session). This is full capacity. - Roughly 200 girls & 350 boys - Ages 6 – incoming 10 th graders who work as waiters - Majority of campers seemed to come from Long Island & New Jersey, about 25 kids from FL (Andrew is based in FL) - Also have 1 bunk of boys with special needs that is full so do not recommend for this. Counselors/Staff: - 70 counselors in bunks. Uniform: - Boys wear a kippah - For girls – no tanks or crop tops (but I do think that I saw tank tops).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Lodging Cabins: - 18 to 20 campers - 3 staff - Amenities: A/C & Refrigerators (parents can have water & beverages delivered to bunk for extra charge) - They are cleaned professionally. Beds & Storage: - Single bed, lofts & bunk beds - Beds are wood - Plastic Individual Closets. Facilities: - 4 showers, 4 sinks, 3-4 toilets - All in the bunk. Laundry: - It is done off-site once a week (3 campers per laundry bag).



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Aquatics: - Instruction 2x/week - Boys & girls swim at different times (though female instructor can work with boys) - Campers can wear whatever they want - Can swim in pool (2) or Lake - Pool: is heated and has waterslides - Has hot tub in big house - Lake offers water sports off a jet ski - Have a great Aquapark - Boating: kayaks, paddleboats & banana boat.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Program: - 7 structured periods - Campers travel by bunk - 3 periods in the am and 4 periods in the pm - Note: activities are NOT coed but evening activities can be - Coed dance party every other week. Younger and older campers are assigned to separate sides of the pavilion. Religious Life: - Friday night services – 1 hour; boys wear khakis and polos; girls wear dresses - Saturday services (no activities and most of day involves prayer); 45 minute morning service; breakfast; 90 minute Torah service; free time; lunch; afternoon praters; free time; dinner; service and Havdalah - Blessings at meals (partial Birkat after meals) - Boys and girls can take a Shabbat walk together.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Performing Arts: - Not currently offering Drama, but have gym mats set-up in playhouse for tumbling - HAVE VERY STRONG DANCE PROGRAM. Instructor runs ROIC studio and has been at camp for years. Offers: tap, ballet, hip hop & contemporary. https://www.isischinnerydance.com/about. Fitness Center and spin studio. Sports: - HAVE VERY STRONG BASKETBALL PROGRAM. Have Seneca Park - basketball park with 4 courts in boys camp. - Competition is big at camp. Camp participates in Wayne County Leagues. Counselors draft teams for intramural leagues - Fields/courts: 8 tennis courts, basketball, lacrosse fields, football fields, 2 baseball fields, batting cage, volleyball & beach volleyball - Hockey: 2 rinks for roller or street, 1 rink is covered - Golf: take kids off camp for golf at no extra cost - Horseback Riding: off camp about 15 minutes away. Creative: - General Arts & Crafts facility include clay, beads, art projects that can air dry - Cooking: have 2 facilities, one for baking & top chef cooking room for dairy. Other: - Have high & low ropes, zipline, a climbing wall, bungee jumping and obstacle course - Seneca Skirmish: paintball park - Mountain & Regular Bikes - In non-Covid summers, they take trips to amusement parks and water parks.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall Food: - ALL FOOD IS KOSHER - Meals: dairy is served for breakfast & lunch, meat is served for dinner every night but Thursday (dairy). - There is a salad bar, pasta bar and grilled chicken w/veg offered every night. - Allergies: there is a separate chef, can accommodate nut free, celiac, etc…. Dining Hall: - Dinner and lunch are served in 1 seating, and breakfast is served in 2 seatings - Amenities: has a disco ball, AC. Canteen: - 2 items from the canteen are included. - Candy & Hot Food are offered - The building is newly renovated to include indoor & outdoor lounge areas w/tv & modern jukebox - Boys and girls go to Canteen at same time but line up separately for ease of administration. Other Food/Drink: - Snack Shack: snack area offered in both the girls & boys camps - Seneca Sprinkles: ice cream parlor in center of camp. Can be rented out for camper’s birthdays. (Ice cream after Thursday dairy dinner most weeks) - Boys Camp: has pizza bar, grills and fire pits - There are soda vending machines throughout the camp - Dessert served at every dinner as well. Medical: - 1 doctor & 5 nurses on site - Infirmary has AC - Nearest Hospital: Wayne Memorial.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Coed Modern Orthodox Jewish camp with structured, gender-separated activities (activities are NOT coed but evening activities can be). Religious life is central: Friday night services; Saturday services with no activities and most of day involves prayer; blessings at meals (partial Birkat after meals); boys and girls can take a Shabbat walk together. Competition is big at camp. Coed dance party every other week with younger and older campers on separate sides of the pavilion. Boys wear a kippah; for girls – no tanks or crop tops.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Communication: - 1 phone per week - Letter Writing is required - Post pictures daily on Instagram. Tech policy beyond this: Unknown. Extra costs and inclusions: - 2 items from the canteen are included - Parents can have water & beverages delivered to bunk for extra charge - Seneca Sprinkles can be rented out for camper’s birthdays - Golf is taken off camp at no extra cost. Other costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

I think the ideal camper goes to a religious day school and is very interested in sports. Throughout the day there is a decent amount of prayer and there are no activities for Shabbat. The camp also stressed the importance of the campers being interested in basketball or dancing. Both programs seemed very strong and the campers I saw doing both looked happy.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - THIS IS A BASKETBALL & DANCE Camp.
  - HAVE VERY STRONG DANCE PROGRAM.
  - HAVE VERY STRONG BASKETBALL PROGRAM.
  - Competition is big at camp. Camp participates in Wayne County Leagues. Counselors draft teams for intramural leagues.
  - ALL FOOD IS KOSHER with salad bar, pasta bar and grilled chicken w/veg offered every night.
  - Have a great Aquapark; 2 heated pools with waterslides; lake water sports off a jet ski.
  - AC in bunks, dining hall, and infirmary.
  - Golf: take kids off camp for golf at no extra cost.
  - Wayne Memorial is the nearest hospital; 1 doctor & 5 nurses on site.



#### Lowlights

  - Saturday services (no activities and most of day involves prayer).
  - Activities are NOT coed (evening activities can be).
  - If not, the activities seemed limited.
  - Not currently offering Drama.
  - Also have 1 bunk of boys with special needs that is full so do not recommend for this.
  - Communication is limited: 1 phone per week; Letter Writing is required.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

This is full capacity. - Roughly 200 girls & 350 boys - Ages 6 – incoming 10 th graders who work as waiters - Majority of campers seemed to come from Long Island & New Jersey, about 25 kids from FL (Andrew is based in FL) - Also have 1 bunk of boys with special needs that is full so do not recommend for this Counselors/Staff: - 70 counselors in bunks Uniform: - Boys wear a kippah - For girls – no tanks or crop tops (but I do think that I saw tank tops) Religious Life: - Friday night services – 1 hour; boys wear khakis and polos; girls wear dresses - Saturday services (no activities and most of day involves prayer); 45 minute morning service; breakfast; 90 minute Torah service; free time; lunch; afternoon praters; free time; dinner; service and Havdalah - Blessings at meals (partial Birkat after meals) - Boys and girls can take a Shabbat walk together Program: - 7 structured periods - Campers travel by bunk - 3 periods in the am and 4 periods in the pm - Note: activities are NOT coed but evening activities can be - Coed dance party every other week. Younger and older campers are assigned to separate sides of the pavilion Lodging Cabins: - 18 to 20 campers - 3 staff - Amenities: A/C & Refrigerators (parents can have water & beverages delivered to bunk for extra charge) - They are cleaned professionally Beds & Storage: - Single bed, lofts & bunk beds - Beds are wood - Plastic Individual Closets Facilities: - 4 showers, 4 sinks, 3-4 toilets - All in the bunk Laundry: - It is done off-site once a week (3 campers per laundry bag) Dining Hall & Canteen Dining Hall Food: - ALL FOOD IS KOSHER - Meals: dairy is served for breakfast & lunch, meat is served for dinner every night but Thursday (dairy). - There is a salad bar, pasta bar and grilled chicken w/veg offered every night. - Allergies: there is a separate chef, can accommodate nut free, celiac, etc…. Dining Hall: - Dinner and lunch are served in 1 seating, and breakfast is served in 2 seatings - Amenities: has a disco ball, AC Canteen: - 2 items from the canteen are included. - Candy & Hot Food are offered - The building is newly renovated to include indoor & outdoor lounge areas w/tv & modern jukebox - Boys and girls go to Canteen at same time but line up separately for ease of administration Other: - Snack Shack: snack area offered in both the girls & boys camps - Seneca Sprinkles: ice cream parlor in center of camp. Can be rented out for camper’s birthdays. (Ice cream after Thursday dairy dinner most weeks) - Boys Camp: has pizza bar, grills and fire pits - There are soda vending machines throughout the camp - Dessert served at every dinner as well Medical - 1 doctor & 5 nurses on site - Infirmary has AC - Nearest Hospital: Wayne Memorial Communication - 1 phone per week - Letter Writing is required - Post pictures daily on Instagram Activities Overview: THIS IS A BASKETBALL & DANCE Camp Aquatics: - Instruction 2x/week - Boys & girls swim at different times (though female instructor can work with boys) - Campers can wear whatever they want - Can swim in pool (2) or Lake - Pool: is heated and has waterslides - Has hot tub in big house - Lake offers water sports off a jet ski - Have a great Aquapark - Boating: kayaks, paddleboats & banana boat Creative: - General Arts & Crafts facility include clay, beads, art projects that can air dry - Cooking: have 2 facilities, one for baking & top chef cooking room for dairy Stem: - Has game room with x-box Performing Arts: - Not currently offering Drama, but have gym mats set-up in playhouse for tumbling - HAVE VERY STRONG DANCE PROGRAM. Instructor runs ROIC studio and has been at camp for years. Offers: tap, ballet, hip hop & contemporary. o https://www.isischinnerydance.com/about - Fitness Center and spin studio Sports: - HAVE VERY STRONG BASKTELL PROGRAM. Have Seneca Park - basketball park with 4 courts in boys camp. - Competition is big at camp. Camp participates in Wayne County Leagues. Counselors draft teams for intramural leagues - Fields/courts: 8 tennis courts, basketball, lacrosse fields, football fields, 2 baseball fields, batting cage, volleyball & beach volleyball - Hockey: 2 rinks for roller or street, 1 rink is covered - Golf: take kids off camp for golf at no extra cost - Horseback Riding: off camp about 15 minutes away Other: - Have high & low ropes, zipline, a climbing wall, bungee jumping and obstacle course - Seneca Skirmish: paintball park - Mountain & Regular Bikes - In non-Covid summers, they take trips to amusement parks and water parks. title: 6 Points Sports — Lisa — 7-30-24
text:
# 6 Points Sports



## 1 · Program Snapshot

6 POINTS SPORTS ACADEMY seen on 7-21-24. They have been at this location for 5 years…in West Asheville at the Asheville School. The campus is gorgeous with beautiful fields and beautiful mountains in the background and the facilities are very nice as well. There is a security gate and a 24/7 Rover. They offer 3 12-day sessions; when I was there it was the 3rd session and there were 255 boys and girls attending. Their sessions vary from 275-300 campers. Typically, a split of 65% boys/45% girls. Campers are rising 4th - rising 11th grades. Some of the sports are coed. They choose a major and are instructed by skill level so there may be age differences as well, depending on the sport. Coaches are college level coaches; they may change from session to session but are at a high level of instruction and there are multiple coaches at once. There is a Maccabi every session.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jackie Gordon gave me the tour as she is director. When I visited there were 255 campers; sessions vary from 275-300 campers. Typically, a split of 65% boys/45% girls. Campers are rising 4th - rising 11th grades. Kids fly in from Raleigh NC, FL, MD, DC and their largest draw is from Atlanta (25%). 23 Israeli Staff. Coaches are college level coaches; they may change from session to session but are at a high level of instruction and there are multiple coaches at once. The coaches evaluate at the end of the session, so the kids go home with an idea of what to work on.



## 3 · Living Quarters

They live in family units in the dorms which were very nice. 2 beds/room with bathroom/showers in the hall. All carpeted with bunk beds. G/B sep bldgs. AC window units. Rocking chairs on the porch. Counselors are on the floor in their own rooms. They carry backpacks around but can keep their athletic gear in the locker room.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is an indoor pool for the swimming Major and elective.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

10am-12pm Sports major. Elective: 2:30pm- 4:30pm. Evening: 6:30pm – 8:45pm Sports major. They choose a major and are instructed by skill level so there may be age differences as well, depending on the sport. Electives can be: Alpine Tower, Gaga, Frisbee, yoga, Pilates, music, challah baking, and some majors can be electives.



## 6 · Program Highlights

There are 2 soccer fields, 7 tennis courts, a baseball field, lacrosse, volleyball is inside the gym as is basketball (separate gym). Off campus: gymnastics is 15 min away, softball is off camp as well, they rent a field and golf is 15-20 min away at Broadmoor Golf Links. I saw cheer/dance going on in the theater, very nice facility. Modern/ hip hop mostly and some tumbling. The dance coach has their own dance studio at home. Many of the gyms and facilities have AC. Nice fitness room, HS age is elective. There is a Maccabi every session.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

There is a security gate and a 24/7 Rover. 4 RN’s, an athletic trainer, DR is at urgent care down the street. They are not in a remote area. Cafeteria was very nice, Kosher style, fruit bowl, nut free, sit by dorm, pm and evening snack. Nice round tables.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The campus is gorgeous with beautiful fields and beautiful mountains in the background and the facilities are very nice as well. They will get Jewish values woven into the activities. Jewish life: 2 Rabbis at camp. They wear white on Shabbat, typical Friday night dinner and blessings, 1 hour service with Israeli dancing afterwards. Saturday, late wake-up, 1.5 hr Torah service (they have an ark), then havdallah. There is a Maccabi every session.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Kids fly in from Raleigh NC, FL, MD, DC and their largest draw is from Atlanta (25%). Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Located in West Asheville at the Asheville School, 6 Points Sports Academy offers 3 12-day sessions for rising 4th–11th graders with college-level coaching and a choice of sports majors and electives. The campus is gorgeous with beautiful fields, mountains in the background, many air-conditioned facilities, and an indoor pool. Jewish life is woven into daily activities with 2 Rabbis on site, Shabbat traditions, Israeli dancing, and a Maccabi every session. This program caters to kids from the east coast (and mainly southeast to mid-Atlantic), with strong instruction, evaluations, and a safe, well-run campus.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Gorgeous Asheville School campus with beautiful fields and mountain backdrop; facilities are very nice.
  - College-level coaches with multiple coaches at once and end-of-session evaluations.
  - Clear daily structure with sports major blocks and varied electives (including Alpine Tower, yoga, Pilates, music, challah baking).
  - Many gyms and facilities have AC; nice fitness room (HS age is elective).
  - Indoor pool for swimming major and elective.
  - Strong Jewish life (2 Rabbis, Shabbat, Israeli dancing) and Maccabi every session.
  - Security gate with a 24/7 Rover; 4 RNs, an athletic trainer, and nearby urgent care.
  - Cafeteria is Kosher style, nut free, with fruit bowl and snacks; sit by dorm.
  - Draws campers broadly (largest from Atlanta at 25%; flyers from NC, FL, MD, DC).
  - 23 Israeli Staff.



#### Lowlights

  - Some sports are off campus (gymnastics ~15 min, golf 15–20 min, softball on rented field).
  - Dorm bathrooms/showers are in the hall.
  - AC via window units in dorms.
  - Campers carry backpacks around.
  - Parent communication, tech policy, and extra costs are Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Maccabi in CA



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

6 POINTS SPORTS ACADEMY seen on 7-21-24 They have been at this location for 5 years…in West Asheville at the Asheville School. Jackie Gordon gave me the tour as she is director. The campus is gorgeous with beautiful fields and beautiful mountains in the background and the facilities are very nice as well. There is a security gate and a 24/7 Rover. They offer 3 12-day sessions, when I was there it was the 3 rd session and there were 255 boys and girls attending. Their sessions vary from 275-300 campers. Typically, a split of 65% boys/45% girls. Only 7 campers stayed for 6 weeks straight. 120 kids stay for 2 sessions (4 weeks, any combo) and the majority stay for the 12 days. Kids fly in from Raleigh NC, FL, MD, DC and their largest draw is from Atlanta (25%). Campers are rising 4 th - rising 11 th grades. There are 2 soccer fields, 7 tennis courts, a baseball field, lacrosse, volleyball is inside the gym as is basketball (separate gym). There is an indoor pool for the swimming Major and elective. Off campus: gymnastics is 15 min away, softball is off camp as well, they rent a field and golf is 15-20 min away at Broadmoor Golf Links. They carry backpacks around but can keep their athletic gear in the locker room. 23 Israeli Staff. There is a Maccabi every session. Coaches are college level coaches. They may change from session to session but are at a high level of instruction and there are multiple coaches at once. Some of the sports are coed…I saw lots of boys in the gym warming up with basketball and there were a handful of girls in with them. They choose a major and are instructed by skill level so there may be age differences as well, depending on the sport. The coaches evaluate at the end of the session, so the kids go home with an idea of what to work on. Many of the gyms and facilities have AC. Nice fitness room, HS age is elective. 10am-12pm Sports major Elective: 2:30pm- 4:30pm Evening: 6:30pm – 8:45pm Sports major They live in family units in the dorms which were very nice. 2 beds/room with bathroom/showers in the hall. All carpeted with bunk beds. G/B sep bldgs.. AC window units. Rocking chairs on the porch. Counselors are on the floor in their own rooms. Cafeteria was very nice, Kosher style, fruit bowl, nut free, sit by dorm, pm and evening snack. Nice round tables. I saw cheer/dance going on in the theater, very nice facility. Modern/ hip hop mostly and some tumbling. The dance coach has their own dance studio at home. They will get Jewish values woven into the activities. Jewish life: 2 Rabbis at camp. They wear white on Shabbat, typical Friday night dinner and blessings, 1 hour service with Israeli dancing afterwards. Saturday, late wake-up, 1.5 hr Torah service (they have an ark), then havdallah. 4 RN’s, an athletic trainer, DR is at urgent care down the street. They are not in a remote area. Electives can be: Alpine Tower, Gaga, Frisbee, yoga, Pilates, music, challah baking, and some majors can be electives. There are not many other Jewish sports-focused camps …maybe Maccabi in CA, but this caters to kids on the east coast, and mainly southeast to mid-Atlantic.

title: WAYNE FOR GIRLS — JENNIFER — 7-14-23
text:
# WAYNE FOR GIRLS



## 1 · Program Snapshot

All girls 7 week traditional camp; does offer a 1 time 4 week option but very few campers do it and most end up extending. Camp has 300 campers and is full for this summer AND already full for summer 2024.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Approximately 90% of campers are Jewish. Cabins typically have 14 campers with 3-4 staff. Director profile: Unknown.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: typically 14 campers with 3-4 staff, have 3 bunks per age group. No bunk beds for younger campers. Bathrooms are in the bunk and showers have doors instead of curtains.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Aquatics: no traditional pool, but there is a freshwater pool in the lake (lake water but has a regular pool bottom). Lake is private and offers motorized and nonmotorized water sports.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming is 6 periods – 3 am and 3 pm. Younger campers get 1 daily elective and older campers get 2 electives.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Creative – really nice arts and crafts area; offers a MAD workshop, Mad stands for music, art and digital media. Nice crafts area overall. Performing Arts: put on 3 shows daily. Has a nice gymnastics facility – both indoors and outdoors. Tennis – campers play 5x per week, can also take private lessons for no extra cost. NO horses at camp.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: cozy, campers eat in 1 seating family style. Camp is nut free.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religious Life: Approximately 90% of campers are Jewish. On Friday nights there is an optional 30 minute Shabbat service with challah and grape juice – approximately 60-70 girls attend. Overall Impression: Camp looked great and saw lots of happy campers. Nice traditional all girls camp with nice facilities.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Unknown. Private tennis lessons for no extra cost.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

All girls 7 week traditional camp, with a 1 time 4 week option but very few campers do it and most end up extending. Camp has 300 campers and is full for this summer and already full for summer 2024. Programming is 6 periods – 3 am and 3 pm, with 1 daily elective for younger campers and 2 electives for older campers. Camp looked great and saw lots of happy campers; a nice traditional all girls camp with nice facilities.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Nice traditional all girls camp with nice facilities.
  - Private lake with motorized and nonmotorized water sports and a freshwater pool in the lake (lake water but has a regular pool bottom).

  - Bathrooms in the bunk and showers have doors instead of curtains.
  - No bunk beds for younger campers.



#### Lowlights

  - No traditional pool.
  - NO horses at camp.
  - Very few campers do the 1 time 4 week option.
  - Full for this summer and already full for summer 2024.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Point O' Pines — Jennifer — 8-4-24
text:
# Point O' Pines



## 1 · Program Snapshot

All girls camp in Adirondacks. They will allow younger girls (up to rising 4th) to do a 1 time 4 week session but otherwise session length is 7 weeks. 15 girls did the 4 week option and all ended up extending. They will be adding a CIT program for next summer.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jennifer, Lisa, Shari and Beth toured with director, Nikki Grossman on 7/13/24. Nikki and her husband Max are new directors – they have crafted a letter about their long term commitment to Point o Pines which Beth sent a copy of in a separate email. Has 200 girls for this summer; ideal size is around 250.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins: younger girls – 6 campers with 3 staff; older girls have 10-12 girls with 2/3 staff. Facilities in the bunk. Shower towels are provided. We toured a younger girls bunk which was very spacious, beds spread out with a beautiful view of the lake.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Very pretty and large lake - offers motorized and nonmotorized water sports. Younger girls take swim lessons in the lake 4 x week. Girls must wear swim caps if not wearing a life jacket. No pool.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming: 6 periods in a day. Younger girls get 2 electives/ 4 assigned, interdivision gets 3 electives/3 assigned and older girls get 4 electives/ 2 assigned. Tennis and waterfront are built into their schedule. They have spin bikes which can be done as an elective.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Arts & Crafts: 8 sewing machines, 4 pottery wheels, cooking with iron chef type competition. They also do digital photography and videography - they put on a film festival at the end of camp. Sports: According to Nikki, tennis is most popular. They have 12 courts and 6 pickleball courts. Nice gymnastics center with great view of the lake. New gaga pit this year. Riding: Horses are off site, riding is an additional fee. Riding is down at a 500 acre farm which is an 8 minute drive and girls are taken by bus. They also do adventure fitness, organic gardening, wilderness and farm to table cooking at the farm.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall – has beautiful views of the lake. Whole camp eats together, food is served family style. Canteen is 2 x per week and on extra charge. Health and safety details: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religion: Majority of girls are Jewish. There is an optional Friday night Shabbat done in the library; about 25-30 girls attend. Brothers usually attend Brant Lake. They see their brothers/cousins at Brant Lake 1x per week and the older girls have socials with boys weekly. Overall Impression: Beautiful camp with great lake, manicured grounds. Nikki was great with the girls and is committed to Point o Pines.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra Costs for horseback riding, golf and glass blowing. Canteen is 2 x per week and on extra charge.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Point O' Pines is an all girls camp in the Adirondacks with a very pretty and large lake and manicured grounds. Sessions are 7 weeks, with a one-time 4-week option for younger girls (up to rising 4th) that saw 15 girls extend this summer. New directors Nikki Grossman and her husband Max have shared a letter about their long term commitment, and the program features 6 periods a day with tennis and waterfront built in plus strong arts and sports. The community is majority Jewish with an optional Friday night Shabbat, and there are extra costs for horseback riding, golf, glass blowing, and canteen.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Beautiful camp with great lake, manicured grounds.
  - Very pretty and large lake; offers motorized and nonmotorized water sports.
  - Tennis is most popular; 12 courts and 6 pickleball courts.
  - Nice gymnastics center with great view of the lake; new gaga pit; spin bikes as an elective.
  - Digital photography and videography with a film festival at the end of camp.
  - Nikki was great with the girls and is committed to Point o Pines.



#### Lowlights

  - No pool.
  - Horses are off site at a 500 acre farm (8 minute drive by bus); riding is an additional fee.
  - Extra Costs for horseback riding, golf and glass blowing; canteen is an extra charge.
  - Directors are new.



#### Similar Camps

  - Raquette Lake
  - Bryn Mawr



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Point o Pines Jennifer, Lisa, Shari and Beth toured with director, Nikki Grossman on 7/13/24. Nikki and her husband Max are new directors – they have crafted a letter about their long term commitment to Point o Pines which Beth sent a copy of in a separate email. All girls camp in Adirondacks, has 200 girls for this summer, ideal size is around 250. They will allow younger girls ( up to rising 4 th ) to do a 1 time 4 week session but otherwise session length is 7 weeks. 15 girls did the 4 week option and all ended up extending. Brothers usually attend Brant Lake. They see their brothers/cousins at Brant Lake 1x per week and the older girls have socials with boys weekly. They will be adding a CIT program for next summer. Extra Costs for horseback riding, golf and glass blowing. Programming: 6 periods in a day. Younger girls get 2 electives/ 4 assigned, interdivision gets 3 electives/3 assigned and older girls get 4 electives/ 2 assigned. Tennis and waterfront are built into their schedule. Cabins: younger girls – 6 campers with 3 staff; older girls have 10-12 girls with 2/3 staff. Facilities in the bunk. Shower towels are provided. We toured a younger girls bunk which was very spacious, beds spread out with a beautiful view of the lake. Dining Hall – has beautiful views of the lake. Whole camp eats together, food is served family style. Canteen is 2 x per week and on extra charge. Religion: Majority of girls are Jewish. There is an optional Friday night Shabbat done in the library; about 25-30 girls attend Waterfront: Very pretty and large lake - offers motorized and nonmotorized water sports. Younger girls take swim lessons in the lake 4 x week. Girls must wear swim camps if not wearing a life jacket. No pool. Arts& Crafts: 8 sewing machines, 4 pottery wheels, cooking with iron chef type competition. They also do digital photography and videography- they put on a film festival at the end of camp. Sports: According to Nikki, tennis is most popular. They have 12 courts and 6 pickleball courts. Nice gymnastics center with great view of the lake. They have spin bikes which can be done as an elective. New gaga pit this year. Riding: Horses are off site, riding is an additional fee. Riding is down at a 500 acre farm which is an 8 minute drive and girls are taken by bus. They also do adventure fitness, organic gardening, wilderness and farm to table cooking at the farm. Overall Impression: Beautiful camp with great lake, manicured grounds. Nikki was great with the girls and is committed to Point o Pines. Would give with other 7 week girls camps with nicer facilities such as Raquette Lake and Bryn Mawr.

title: Academy Camps — Pilar — 7-24-24
text:
# Academy Camps



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Located at Suffield Academy (grounds staff remain all summer). Sports Academy. Soccer, Basketball, Tennis, Lacrosse. Sessions 1 or 2 weeks. This year three 2-week sessions available. Some kids stay for 3 or 4 weeks. There is an excursion during bridge weekend. Plans for 2026: 2 new locations in New Jersey. Golf, tennis and swimming at The Peddie School. Baseball, softball, volleyball (boys and girls) and field hockey at Blair Academy. Suffield will have soccer, lacrosse, basketball. Maybe one session of squash and one session of flag football (including girls).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Academy Camps toured July 15, 2024 by Karen, Alyssa, Monica, Carolina, Tami and Pilar Vidal. Toured by Courtney Grady and Mike Murillo, Executive Director. Alison Heisler is the camp mom and Director of Camp Life. Dan Fabi, currently pursuing a Master’s degree in clinical mental health counseling, takes care of kids and staff at an emotional level. Demographics: Max number is 200. 20% leave after first week. They are looking into this for next year because they find it disruptive. Kids come from New England, NY Area, 15% International. 9 to 15 years old. Most kids are 7th and 8th grade. 70% boys. Lots of first-time campers. Counselors are 18+. All current or former athletes.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Dorms: 2 kids per room same age and grade (could do 3 upon request). 10 to 15 kids per floor. 2 counselors per floor. Only counselors have keys to the dorms. Campers are not allowed in dorms without a counselor. Campers don’t have to participate in the same sport to room together. Roommate request and matching available. AC only in lounge area and counselor rooms. Fans in every room. Linens (twin XL) and towels for internationals and upon request. Laundry service on Fridays for 2 weeks+ stays. Athletes lounge available for rest, and other activities.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Swimming – Competitive will be added at new location. Waterfront details: Unknown.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

AM and PM training sessions for their sport are 1 ½ hours. Rest and recovery 1 hour. They can do gym time and use the rec center. One speaker per session. Electives: Most electives are sports related. Sports Conditioning includes workout and speed; done by age. Wellness - Yoga, Meditation, Nature Hikes, Ropes, Rock Wall. Arts & Crafts electives inside during hot days.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Gear bag provided at arrival. They must wear the uniform during sports sessions and during AC Cup. PM Activities: AC Cup competitions, Bingo, Lawn Games, spike ball, volleyball, movie screen with sports games. Prizes for winning can be Dunking Donuts for breakfast, being driven on a golf cart for a day, being the DJ at lunch. Saturday night movie and dance party each session. There is an excursion during bridge weekend (Amusement Park, Basketball Hall of Fame). Older kids have one field trip and one evening program for team building.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining: 3 meals a day. Buffet style. Salad bar. Lunch sandwich bar. Miso soup. Assigned seats with counselors. Each meal has a sign with what it is and what allergens might be in it. Nut aware. Individual peanut butter canisters. Gluten delivered separately. Gluten free pasta available. Meals cooked by year-round chefs. They eat with dorm rooms. No food from home. Health Center: Head nurse is at Suffield year-round. Nurses on duty from 7 am to 10 pm and on duty after. Doctor on call if needed. Pediatric Children’s Hospital is 10 mins away. Athletic trainers during the day and during AC Cup activities.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Kids have a play level between 6 and 8, not elite but quite competitive. Lots of first-time campers. Team-building elements for older kids and friendly competition through AC Cup.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Transportation: Chaperoned buses to and from New York and Boston areas. Airport pickup is from Bradley, Westchester, and Logan. Communication: Mid-week call available. Tech free. Counselors are asked to leave phones in rooms unless needed for camp activities. Had campers who were sent home for inappropriate behavior (keeping their phones). Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Located at Suffield Academy, Academy Camps is a Sports Academy with Soccer, Basketball, Tennis, and Lacrosse. Kids have a play level between 6 and 8, not elite but quite competitive. Sessions 1 or 2 weeks (this year three 2-week sessions available), with AM and PM training sessions (1 ½ hours), rest and recovery, electives, and AC Cup competitions. A good fit for an athletic kid who wants to improve the skill level of their sport and have camp activities as well.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Sports Academy: Soccer, Basketball, Tennis, Lacrosse.
  - AM and PM training sessions are 1 ½ hours with a 1-hour rest and recovery.
  - Most electives are sports related; Wellness (Yoga, Meditation, Nature Hikes, Ropes, Rock Wall).
  - Tech free; mid-week call available.
  - Chaperoned buses (NY and Boston) and airport pickups (Bradley, Westchester, Logan).
  - Dining is nut aware with gluten-free options; meals cooked by year-round chefs.
  - Athletic trainers during the day and during AC Cup activities.
  - Gear bag provided; uniforms required during sports sessions and AC Cup.
  - Older kids have one field trip and one evening program for team building.
  - Athletes lounge available for rest and activities.
  - Plans for 2026 include new NJ locations (Peddie, Blair) and expanded sports.



#### Lowlights

  - AC only in lounge area and counselor rooms; fans in every room.
  - 20% leave after first week; they find it disruptive.
  - No food from home.
  - Strict tech policy; campers have been sent home for keeping phones.



#### Similar Camps

  - Berkshire United

Dan Fabi, currently pursuing a Master’s degree in clinical mental health counseling, takes care of kids and staff at an emotional level. Sports Academy. Soccer, Basketball, Tennis, Lacrosse. title: Pocono Springs — Riley — 7-7-25
text:
# Pocono Springs



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Pocono Springs was purchased in 2017 and re-launched by Dani / Scott as a 5-week single session camp, which is unique. The shorter duration aligns well with various school calendars and vacation schedules (i.e., great option for families from regions like Florida who need a camp that ends earlier). Co-ed. 3-week intro Camper program (Rising 2nd through 5th Grade only. First time campers (2nd–5th grade) are eligible to enroll in the first three weeks of the five week Pocono Springs program, with an option to extend for the full season. Space is limited and campers will be enrolled on a first come first served basis). Size: The camp currently accommodates up to 300 campers, with plans to expand next year to 330. The entire camp was rebuilt seven years ago. Cabins are split by gender (i.e., boys side, girls side) with 3 age groups.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Tour: Scott and Dani Leonard (Directors, Husband and Wife Team). CEs: Heather Pasquale, Michelle Burger, Riley McDonough. Demographic: View core demographic as “down to earth” families with mix of interests. While there are a few international campers, the majority are from the US. The camp is not religiously affiliated but is ~85% Jewish campers. The camp is not widely represented on Long Island but has a new bus route from Livingston this year.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Cabins are split by gender (i.e., boys side, girls side) with 3 age groups. Several buildings have A/C. Cabins are newer. Top bunks are only used by counselors, and laundry is done once a week. The entire camp was rebuilt seven years ago.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Pocono Springs has a large private lake that is central to the campus. Pool. Activities include wake skiing (a highlight).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Pocono Springs operates on a hybrid model, with half the day dedicated to bunk-based activities like singing and league sports, and the other half to elective activities. Afternoons include two elective activities, with one activity being an afternoon choice for flexibility with friends. There are a few trip days, with canteen every third day. The camp structure is split into lower, middle and upper divisions, with field trips divided accordingly and upper camps embarking on trips.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Camp Facilities: Major sports facilities include centers for tennis, hockey, and flag football. Gymnastics building built four years ago. The largest groups of campers participate in tennis, baseball, soccer, and gymnastics. Activities include golf, wake skiing (a highlight), and theater, which is an elective activity enjoyed by the whole camp. Additional activities include pottery, woodshop, spray paint art, bracelet building, and “Sunday night live.”



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Safety: The camp adheres to the rule of three for supervision. There are four nurses and one doctor on-site, with a social psychologist available. Meals: Both breakfast and lunch are served buffet-style, with lunch offering open seating. Dinner is a sit-down, family-style meal. Food: The camp offers vegan and vegetarian options for entrees and is nut-free.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

View core demographic as “down to earth” families with mix of interests. Balanced mix of sports and arts. “Sunday night live.”



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Parental Contact: Parents can expect weekly calls and Zoom sessions by cabin, with a designated visiting day. Travel: The camp is not widely represented on Long Island but has a new bus route from Livingston this year. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown. Additional travel logistics: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Pocono Springs was purchased in 2017 and re-launched by Dani / Scott as a 5-week single session camp, which is unique. The shorter duration aligns well with various school calendars and vacation schedules (i.e., great option for families from regions like Florida who need a camp that ends earlier). Co-ed with a balanced mix of sports and arts, it operates on a hybrid model, with half the day dedicated to bunk-based activities like singing and league sports, and the other half to elective activities. Good mid-budget option with shorter “full-summer” length; 3-week intro Camper program for first time campers (Rising 2nd through 5th Grade) with an option to extend for the full season.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 5-week / full-session only program.
  - Good mid-budget option with shorter “full-summer” length and hybrid daily activity structure.
  - Balanced mix of sports and arts.
  - Wake skiing (a highlight).
  - The camp currently accommodates up to 300 campers, with plans to expand next year to 330.



#### Lowlights

  - Space is limited and campers will be enrolled on a first come first served basis.
  - The camp is not widely represented on Long Island.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

The shorter duration aligns well with various school calendars and vacation schedules (i.e., great option for families from regions like Florida who need a camp that ends earlier). Co-ed. • 3-week intro Camper program (Rising 2 nd through 5 th Grade only. First time campers (2 nd – 5 th grade) are eligible to enroll in the first three weeks of the five week Pocono Springs program, with an option to extend for the full season.Space is limited and camper swill be enrolled on a fist come first served basis. • Size : The camp currently accommodates up to 300 campers, with plans to expand next year to 330. The entire camp was rebuilt seven years ago. Cabins are split by gender (i.e., boys side, girls side) with 3 age groups. • Camp Facilities : Pocono Springs has a large private lake that is central to the campus. Major sports facilities include centers for tennis, hockey, and flag football. Gymnastics building built four years ago. The largest groups of campers participate in tennis, baseball, soccer, and gymnastics. Several buildings have A/C. Cabins are newer. Pool. • Camp Model / Daily Structure : Pocono Springs operates on a hybrid model, with half the day dedicated to bunk-based activities like singing and league sports, and the other half to elective activities. Afternoons include two elective activities, with one activity being an afternoon choice for flexibility with friends. There are a few trip days, with canteen every third day. The camp structure is split into lower, middle and upper divisions, with field trips divided accordingly and upper camps embarking on trips. • Meals : Both breakfast and lunch are served buffet-style, with lunch offering open seating. Dinner is a sit-down, family-style meal. • Parental Contact : Parents can expect weekly calls and Zoom sessions by cabin, with a designated visiting day. • Demographic : View core demographic as “down to earth” families with mix of interests. The camp is not widely represented on Long Island but has a new bus route from Livingston this year. While there are a few international campers, the majority are from the US. The camp is not religiously affiliated but is ~85% Jewish campers. • Safety : The camp adheres to the rule of three for supervision. There are four nurses and one doctor on-site, with a social psychologist available. Top bunks are only used by counselors, and laundry is done once a week. • Programs : Activities include golf, wake skiing (a highlight), and theater, which is an elective activity enjoyed by the whole camp. Additional activities include pottery, woodshop, spray paint art, bracelet building, and “Sunday night live” • Food : The camp offers vegan and vegetarian options for entrees and is nut-free. CE Perspective : 5-week / full-session only program. Good mid-budget option with shorter “full-summer” length and hybrid daily activity structure. Balanced mix of sports and arts.

title: Reach Cambridge — Jaymee — 11-3-22
text:
# Reach Cambridge



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Founded in 2005. Mission: To prepare the students for what comes next - future university, how to choose a career, what subjects interest you, what are your passions? Reach Cambridge provides an international experience on a beautiful college campus in nurturing environment. It is very diverse and have students from abroad. Learn about various cultures. Students participate in a World fair-students do a presentation on their own countries. Courses are offered over a 6 week period in summer 2-4 or 6 options. Structured Day- Academic subjects run in 2 week intervals. 25 academic subjects. VERY STRUCTURED DAILY SCHEDULE. Age 14-18 diverse group of students. 120-150 students in each 2 week session. Inspiring surrounding, Cambridge is beautiful. Over 800 years old.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jenny Evans (managing director) presented. Age 14-18 diverse group of students; very diverse and have students from abroad. 120-150 students in each 2 week session. 3 staff teams: Supervisors, counselors who live in corridors (dorms), and experienced leadership. Staff is very experienced. Professors and doctors and lawyers. The kids meet a lot of experts in the field. Academic excellence no exam or grade, but vigorous.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Accommodations- residential. In the center of Cambridge. Inspiring surrounding, Cambridge is beautiful. Over 800 years old. Living in dorm (corridors) 8-12 students together in a corridor supervised. Eat in dining hall Harry Potter style. Can request a single room or request a specific roommate.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront and aquatics focus is limited, but after classes activities include punting (see colleges from river). Sample excursions include a river tour.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Structured Day- Academic subjects run in 2 week intervals. Lessons take place in morning. Classes are in Morning and are 4 hours. Classes are a mix of lecture style and group work. Hands on experiences experiments, experiential learning. Classes take place all over campus (indoors and outdoors). Example: Debating chamber- huge historical significance. Students get the chance to go in and soak up history and debate there. You can do add on courses instead of activities if you want more academics. Evening workshops and lectures. Come together and get inspired by a variety of speakers. Interesting topics. Some activities are mandatory in the beginning. Then, after icebreakers, the activities are highly encouraged, but not mandatory. After classes activities are inclusive for all. A few choices are: sports, music, arts, exploring Cambridge, punting (see colleges from river).



## 6 · Program Highlights

Reach Cambridge provides an international experience on a beautiful college campus in nurturing environment. It is very diverse and have students from abroad. Students participate in a World fair-students do a presentation on their own countries. Staff is very experienced. Professors and doctors and lawyers. The kids meet a lot of experts in the field. Academic excellence no exam or grade, but vigorous. Bridge the gap between high school and university. Hands on experiences experiments, experiential learning. Classes take place all over campus (indoors and outdoors), including the debating chamber with huge historical significance. Huge range of courses to choose from. Popular Medicine, sciences, English literature and creative writing, architecture. If you are unsure of what class to take: Stem-choose a variety. Engineering, math, good for students who are not sure. Political sciences and liberal arts.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health and safety Protocols in place yet students have independence. BAC British accreditation- they have inspections and meet criteria. What’s included in price-accommodations, two meals a day (LUNCHES ARE NOT INCLUDED), all academic teaching, excursions in classes and 2 excursions, extra curriculars, 24/7 on site staff and support.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Inspiring surrounding, Cambridge is beautiful. Over 800 years old. Very diverse and have students from abroad. Learn about various cultures. Students participate in a World fair-students do a presentation on their own countries. After classes activities are inclusive for all. Eat in dining hall Harry Potter style. Nurturing environment. Students have independence.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

How to apply: Short application form. 500 pound deposit for two week program. Create an online account with personal statement. Medical Travel and flight Personal info. Can request a single room or request a specific roommate. Watch student video reviews will be helpful. Testimonials can help perspective student choose an academic course. Parents are kept up to date via blog with notes and photos. Airport transportation is additional. What’s included in price-accommodations, two meals a day (LUNCHES ARE NOT INCLUDED), all academic teaching, excursions in classes and 2 excursions, extra curriculars, 24/7 on site staff and support. Tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Reach Cambridge is a summer program for ages 14-18 founded in 2005 on a beautiful, historic Cambridge college campus. It offers a VERY STRUCTURED DAILY SCHEDULE with morning classes (4 hours), evening workshops, inclusive activities, and a huge range of 25 academic subjects run in two-week intervals. The community is diverse and international with a World fair, experienced staff (professors, doctors, lawyers), and BAC accreditation with protocols in place yet students have independence. What’s included: accommodations, two meals a day (lunches not included), academics, excursions, and 24/7 on-site support; airport transportation is additional and parents are kept up to date via a blog.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - VERY STRUCTURED DAILY SCHEDULE with morning classes (4 hours) and evening workshops.
  - Diverse, international community with a World fair.
  - Experienced staff (professors, doctors, lawyers); students meet experts in the field.
  - Hands on experiences, experiments, experiential learning; classes across campus, including the debating chamber.
  - Cambridge setting is beautiful and inspiring; eat in dining hall Harry Potter style.
  - BAC British accreditation; 24/7 on site staff and support.
  - Wide range of 25 academic subjects; popular Medicine, sciences, English literature and creative writing, architecture.



#### Lowlights

  - LUNCHES ARE NOT INCLUDED.
  - Airport transportation is additional.
  - Some activities only mandatory at the beginning; later highly encouraged but not mandatory.
  - Short application form requires a 500 pound deposit for two week program.
  - Tech policy: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: Chateaugay — Shari — 8-8-24
text:
# Chateaugay



## 1 · Program Snapshot

We toured camp in the rain, thus our detailed info slightly sparce, our impressions were very positive in that Mitch has really stepped things up since taking ownership (40 extra staff members, progressive learning systems/programs (referred to below as PLS) and very happy campers)! All adults in activity areas, not counselors. Offer 2, 3.5, 7 wks. 40% stay full summer. 260 1st session, 220 2nd session. Capacity is 300. Ages 7-15/Wilderness Program (described later). CIT are 16 y/o. Offering Rookie day in 2024.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Chateaugay – Shari and Lisa B - 10 July 2024 - toured by Mitch Goldman – owner since 2022. Attended as kid. Joined back on staff in 2017. Blane is new Ass’t Dir. Staff are about 70% int’l for now but looking to decrease that if he can. Very hard to get U.S. staff as we’ve heard from most camps. Previous owner Hal was there, he spends time at camp, has a house on property by lake, flies his seaplane and hangs out…was nice to see him.



## 3 · Living Quarters

8-12 campers per bunk with 3 staff members. Boys and girls bunks in close proximity. Wood bunk beds, cubbies and shelves provided. Counselors unpack for campers at all ages and parents can help if they drop off. All bunks have facilities inside except Wilderness Program which has wash houses. Laundry is done on-site 1x/week in group bags which campers sort. They keep the bunks the same each year to the extent needed, will change if there’s an issue. Partial and full summer campers mixed together in bunks. Campers of all ages are easily welcomed. On duty is inside bunk at bedtime.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Instruction in heated pool daily for 10 & under. Optional for older campers. Bathing suit policy: no thongs and will tell them if too revealing. Gorgeous, vast lake (they are in the cove so very private) but utilize various sections for motorized sports and sailing (separate area where bigger sailboat is docked to take out on bigger section of the lake). Offer waterskiing, wake boarding, knee boarding, banana boating, wake surfing, slalom, windsurf, fishing, sailing (extensive w/ 20 boats overall including lasers, catamarans & more), kayaking, canoeing, SUP, aqua park (blob, trampoline, bungee).



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Six periods/day. 2 scheduled with group, 4 are choice. Northern lights (a precursor to Wilderness) and Wilderness have different scheduling based on trips. Activities that are not co-ed are swim instruction, soccer, basketball and lacrosse. Color War at end of summer and Olympics at end of 1st session. Campfire and closure for 1st session with slideshow and ice cream party. Banquet and carnival at end of summer so session special events are not repeated. Clinic Day on Sundays (sleep-ins) 2hrs am, 2hrs pm offering various options.



## 6 · Program Highlights

SPORTS- PLS for tennis and several other sports. Try outs for inter-camps but everyone makes teams. They play Walden, Echo Lake, Southwoods, etc. Not competitive but spirited, low-level competition. Offer soccer, lacrosse, basketball, flag football, baseball, kickball, volleyball, pickleball. Golf off premises in Malone can go 2x/wk, driving range on premises. Horses (8-12 depending on year) on premises, English style, stable mgmt., trail rides, ring rides; bring own riding equipment, but they have some to use as well. CREATIVE- PLS – lots of outdoor spaces in grass and on covered porch. Ceramics w/ lots of wheels and hand building. Jewelry making, sewing, shrinky dinks, glass blowing (INCREDIBLE!), woodworking (also INCREDIBLE!) w/ variety of projects, hand tools and power tools depending on age and tool but with supervision. See website for more creative activities. Performing arts: One show per session, auditions but anyone can participate. Matilda was show this session. Circus arts: Spanish silk & rings. Dance room w/ wood floor, mirrors and bars for tap, jazz, ballet, hiphop, modern, yoga, salsa and meditation w/ Mitch. Gymnastics is still in the back area of the gym facility with the stage w/ basic equipment (uneven bars, vault, balance beam, spring floor-seemed a little more extensive than previous years). Fitness center w/ weights for counselors and age appropriate activities. STEAM/HIGH TECH- digital photography. ORIENTEERING – nature bldg., lots of animals (goats, bunnies, sheep, calves), dogs on property on leash. Extensive high ropes in wooded area (12 elements), climbing wall. TRIPS- O/A is big at Chateaugay. Off camp trips to rock climbing center, canoeing, hiking, nature trails. Wilderness Program for 15y/o is all no frills: about 36 campers in program now, no electricity, live in separate area on camp, cook their own breakfast and lunch. They do 5-day trips including bike 200 miles, hike high peaks and a canoe trip (these are all separate trips).



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – A/C in health ctr, 3 RN’s, 1 MD, hospital CVPH in Plattsburgh (30 mins), Alice Hyde Med ctr in Malone (less used), can take a diabetic camper. Two mental health directors on staff. DINING- traditional campy dining hall with knotty pine, overhead fans, lots of spirit (Lions vs. Bears traditions continue even though previous owners, Dov/Hal not on leadership any longer). Campers sit by bunk, two seatings (older camp/younger camp), buffet style, rectangular tables, kosher style, fruit out all day, nut-free, can accommodate all allergies. Snacks and parties (their version of canteen) at the Ice Creamery/Cooking studio above the boathouse overlooking the lake. Porch outside dining hall for games, creative writing and other activities throughout the summer.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Very accepting community with a handful of neurodivergent campers. This is a case-by-case scenario, but he is open to the discussion as his families are very down-to-earth. Also open to the discussion of an LGTBQ camper and where they would bunk them. Mitch describes camp as a physically and emotionally safe, enriching environment where the whole community benefits. It’s where you can be your best self, have fun, make friends and learn skills. RELIGIOUS LIFE – Optional gathering (doesn’t call it a service) where handful of campers attend and light candles. Population is probably 80% jewish he thinks. No prayers before meals, just camp cheers. No shabbat dinner. Can take to church on Sundays if requested.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

NYC bus stops in Albany for pick-ups. Also buses from Westchester, Palisades, Philly and Montreal. Chaperoned flights from Houston, L.A., D.C., & FL. COMMUNICATION- One call home/session. Mitch is pretty relaxed about it though in general unless it triggers homesickness then he will suggest less or none. No packages in 2024 and has been amazing. Post photos daily. Extra costs: Bikes & backpacks for Wilderness Program, golf, HB riding, WW rafting.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Owner since 2022, Mitch has really stepped things up since taking ownership (40 extra staff members, progressive learning systems/programs (PLS) and very happy campers). The program runs six periods/day with 2 scheduled with group and 4 choice, extensive aquatics on a gorgeous, vast lake, and an O/A Wilderness Program for 15 y/o that is all no frills. Facilities are traditional and comfortable with 8–12 campers per bunk, strong medical support, and a campy dining hall with lots of spirit. Very accepting community, physically and emotionally safe, with buses and chaperoned flights, one call home/session, no packages, and daily photos.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Mitch Goldman – owner since 2022. Attended as kid. Joined back on staff in 2017.
  - 40 extra staff members, progressive learning systems/programs (PLS) and very happy campers.
  - All adults in activity areas, not counselors.
  - Gorgeous, vast lake (they are in the cove so very private) with extensive sailing (20 boats overall including lasers, catamarans & more) and a big aqua park.
  - Wilderness Program for 15y/o is all no frills with 5-day trips (bike 200 miles, hike high peaks, canoe trip).
  - Glass blowing (INCREDIBLE!) and woodworking (also INCREDIBLE!).
  - A/C in health ctr, 3 RN’s, 1 MD, and two mental health directors on staff.
  - Not competitive but spirited, low-level competition.
  - Very accepting community with a handful of neurodivergent campers; open to the discussion of an LGTBQ camper.



#### Lowlights

  - We toured camp in the rain, thus our detailed info slightly sparce.
  - Staff are about 70% int’l for now but looking to decrease that if he can. Very hard to get U.S. staff.
  - Activities that are not co-ed are swim instruction, soccer, basketball and lacrosse.
  - Gymnastics is still in the back area of the gym facility with the stage w/ basic equipment.
  - Wilderness Program for 15y/o is all no frills: no electricity; wash houses.



#### Similar Camps

  - Walden-NY
  - Echo Lake
  - Walt Whitman
  - Schodack
  - Watitoh

title: Pocono Springs — Emily — 8-4-22
text:
# Pocono Springs



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Pocono Springs opened in 2017. 250 kids were at camp this summer which was their max, about half boys and half girls. Many kids from NYC. Kids divided into lower (grades 3-5), middle (grades 6-8) and upper camp (grades 9-11). Next summer they will go up to 300 kids because they are converting a bunk and will have 2 extra girl cabins. 5 week model is what they love and are sticking with it. They will allow 3 weeks for kids entering 3rd, 4th and 5th grade. 5 weeks is June 25-July 29th. Allows kids/families time to do other things in August. Return rate for campers is 90%. This camp is not religious. Kids mostly stayed at camp, no intercamps, brought programming to camp.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Tour Guide: Director Scott Leonard. Scott grew up going to camp at Iroquois Spring and then worked at Woodmont Day Camp for years. Counselors were from US and International this summer. Allowed to go off campus. Counselors need to finish 1st year of college to be eligible. Bunks have about 10 kids and 3 counselors. Have a CIT program going into 11th grade. Many kids from NYC. Few kids from California.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks have about 10 kids and 3 counselors. Bunk beds. Air conditioned, use at night when it’s hot, doesn’t run all day. Bunks have 2 showers, 2 toilets and 2 sinks in the bunk. Laundry is once a week, returned to campers in 24 hours.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Activities include lake (motorized and non) and pool. The lake is pretty and is right in the middle of camp.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming includes cabin activities in the morning and elective activities in the afternoon.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Activities include, Gaga, pickleball, outdoor adventure, driving range, zip line, tennis, jogging trails, climbing tower, street art, arts and crafts, sports (baseball, basketball, lacrosse etc), archery, theater, Discovery/STEM, gymnastics, Rock Band, cooking etc. No horseback riding. Put on one play per 5 weeks, everyone can get a part or do behind the scenes. The gymnastics and dance studio are brand new. Building a welcome center. Canteen once a night. Candy and games. Have color war toward end of summer.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining hall is not A/C but fans. One seating for whole camp. Family style for breakfast and dinner, buffet style for lunch. Can handle all allergies, have a specialist in kitchen. Kids can sit with anyone at lunch, sit with bunk at breakfast and dinner. Health center has 4 Nurses, access to a doctor if needed. Is air conditioned. Close by to an URGI-care and hospital. Lehigh Valley Hospital is 7 miles away. Hired a social/emotional behavioralist and a social worker to work with kids.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Not a uniform camp. Counselors always wear Staff t shirts. Kids seemed very happy at camp. The campers are diverse and down to earth. Facilities are good but not fancy. They have made many improvements to the camp and will continue to.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses leave from Manhattan, Philly/Cherry Hill, Westchester and chaperoned flight from Florida. No visiting day this summer, had zoom call. 2 phone calls during 5 weeks. Parents can email campers, campers write letters. Thinking of running a rookie day next summer, this summer just did private tours. Tuition for 2023: $8950 for 5 weeks Dates June 24th to July 28. Visiting Day July 16. $6300 for 3 weeks Dates June 24 to July 15th.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Pocono Springs opened in 2017 and runs a 5 week model (with a 3 week option for grades 3-5), allowing kids/families time to do other things in August. This camp is for the all-around kid who wants to do a bit of everything, not for the extremely sporty/competitive kid. Kids seemed very happy at camp; the campers are diverse and down to earth, and facilities are good but not fancy. This is a great option for a kid whose family wants to travel in August and wants their kid to experience a session from beginning to end all together without kids coming and going.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - 5 week model is what they love and are sticking with it (3 weeks for kids entering 3rd, 4th and 5th grade).
  - Return rate for campers is 90%.
  - The lake is pretty and is right in the middle of camp.
  - The gymnastics and dance studio are brand new.
  - Can handle all allergies, have a specialist in kitchen.
  - Health center has 4 Nurses and is air conditioned; Lehigh Valley Hospital is 7 miles away.
  - Hired a social/emotional behavioralist and a social worker to work with kids.
  - Canteen once a night; color war toward end of summer.
  - Programming includes cabin activities in the morning and elective activities in the afternoon.
  - Buses leave from Manhattan, Philly/Cherry Hill, Westchester and chaperoned flight from Florida.



#### Lowlights

  - Facilities are good but not fancy.
  - Dining hall is not A/C (fans).
  - Not for the extremely sporty/competitive kid.
  - No horseback riding.
  - Kids mostly stayed at camp, no intercamps.
  - No visiting day this summer; had zoom call.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown
