title: Academy Camps — Amanda — 7-17-23
text:
# Academy Camps



## 1 · Program Snapshot

ACADEMY CAMPS Write Up @ Suffield Academy. 4 Academy Sports are: basketball, tennis, soccer and lacrosse. Big focus on performance, leadership and wellness. Co-ed campers are 10-16yrs old. They can do one or two weeks. They also allow campers to do multiple sessions. However, they feel that Academy Camps is truly meant to be a two-week session camp and are rethinking only allowing one session next summer. Each day campers spend 2 sessions a day practicing their chosen sport.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jem Sollinger - Co-founder; Jon Deren - Co-founder; Josh Hahn - Co-founder; Mike Maurilio - Executive Director. Emily and I were toured by Allison Heisler (program and residential director) and Mike Maurillo (executive director). We also met Mike Evans, the director of basketball (nice guy from Weston, CT). Many counselors are assistant coaches, so they are very hands on with the day to day. Sports are divided by skill level. Lax and basketball (although we did see mixed genders) are not co-ed. Tennis and soccer are. Campers come from NYC, Westchester, CT, LI, NJ, FL, Spain (11 Spanish campers), France & DR. They had 4 sessions this summer and each session exceeded the number of campers they thought they would have (1st - approx 83 campers, 2nd - 85 campers, 3rd - 94 campers & 4th - 92 campers).



## 3 · Living Quarters

Nice clean dorms - 2 campers per room (can make the rooms into 4). Each floor has 2 counselors. Dorms roomed together by age and gender. Can request a roommate. Dorm rooms DO NOT have AC but common spaces do. Campers have fans. We were there on a very hot day and the dorms were surprisingly cool. Laundry done 1x week. Athletic Longue with AC where campers can come for snacks during the day, night chill space with ping pong tables, Pop-A-Shot, chess, music, movie night. Have BBQ’s 1x session on the patio of the Athletic Longue.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Unknown



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Each day campers spend 2 sessions a day practicing their chosen sport. Morning session is 2 hours and afternoon session is 2 hours. Get lots of skill-based instruction. We arrived when campers were in their afternoon sport session. Campers have Quad Chill time 2x a day in a nice grassy area outside of dorms with lawn games, shade, etc. One seating for all campers. Get canteen 2x a day.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Campus is beautiful and well-manicured. Fields are in great shape. New turf on lax field. We saw some campers playing basketball in Tisch Hall (a beautiful indoor facility that can also host tennis, pickleball, volleyball, etc.). 4 Academy Sports are: basketball, tennis, soccer and lacrosse. Sports are divided by skill level. Lax and basketball (although we did see mixed genders) are not co-ed. Tennis and soccer are. Big focus on performance, leadership and wellness. Have different activities in each area. Overall, we were very impressed with Academy Camps. Seems to have top level instruction, organized programming and the grounds/facilities were beautiful.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Full air-conditioned health center with lots of private rooms. 24/7 hr. nurse (male and female). Doc on call if needed, Pediatric Children’s Hospital is 10 mins away. Have an athletic trainer Mon-Fri to help with strengthening, stretching, injuries, etc. 2 strength/conditioning coaches (male & female). Security from 4pm-7am patrolling campus. Staff must wear staff shirts. Dining Hall- very large and clean. Nice round tables. One seating for all campers. Buffet style can accommodate all dietary restrictions (GF, veggie, dairy free, nuts, etc.). Lots of healthy options. Counselors sit at tables with campers. All food is cooked nut free, but they DO have individual packets of peanut butter.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Campus is beautiful and well-manicured. Athletic Longue and Quad Chill spaces create day and night chill time with lawn games, ping pong, Pop-A-Shot, chess, music, movie night, and occasional BBQ’s. Overall, we were very impressed with Academy Camps. Seems to have top level instruction, organized programming and the grounds/facilities were beautiful. They also seem to know and have a pulse on each camper.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Provide transportation for 2-week session campers (buses in Fairfield, New Rochelle, Newton, MA and pick up Bradley and Logan airports). Need extra money for canteen (most campers have btwn $50-100). Get canteen 2x a day. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Overall, we were very impressed with Academy Camps. Seems to have top level instruction, organized programming and the grounds/facilities were beautiful. This camp is great for a sporty kid looking to improve their skills and have a shorter camp experience. They also seem to know and have a pulse on each camper.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Top level instruction.
  - Organized programming.
  - Grounds/facilities were beautiful.
  - Campus is beautiful and well-manicured.
  - Fields are in great shape. New turf on lax field.
  - Big focus on performance, leadership and wellness.
  - Many counselors are assistant coaches, so they are very hands on with the day to day.
  - Full air-conditioned health center with lots of private rooms. 24/7 hr. nurse (male and female). Doc on call if needed, Pediatric Children’s Hospital is 10 mins away.
  - Provide transportation for 2-week session campers.
  - Each session exceeded the number of campers they thought they would have.



#### Lowlights

  - Dorm rooms DO NOT have AC but common spaces do.
  - Need extra money for canteen (most campers have btwn $50-100).
  - Lax and basketball are not co-ed.
  - However, they feel that Academy Camps is truly meant to be a two-week session camp and are rethinking only allowing one session next summer.



#### Similar Camps

  - Next Level
  - Bridgeton
  - Kent Hills
  - Berkshire Soccer
  - IMG
  - US Nike



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

We saw some campers playing basketball in Tisch Hall (a beautiful indoor facility that can also host tennis, pickleball, volleyball, etc.) Campus is beautiful and well-manicured. Get lots of skill-based instruction. 4 Academy Sports are: basketball, tennis soccer and lacrosse. Sports are divided by skill level. Lax and basketball (although we did see mixed genders) are not co-ed. Tennis and soccer are. Big focus on performance, leadership and wellness. Have different activities in each area. Co-ed campers are 10-16yrs old. They can do one or two weeks. They also allow campers to do multiple sessions. However, they feel that Academy Camps is truly meant to be a two-week session camp and are rethinking only allowing one session next summer. They had 4 sessions this summer and each session exceeded the number of campers they thought they would have (1 st -approx 83 campers, 2 nd - 85 campers, 3 rd - 94 campers & 4 th - 92 campers) Campers come from NYC, Westchester, CT, LI, NJ, FL, Spain (11 Spanish campers), France & DR. Provide transportation for 2-week session campers (buses in Fairfield, New Rochelle, Newton, MA and pick up Bradley and Logan airports. Nice clean dorms - 2 campers per room (can make the rooms into 4) Each floor has 2 counselors Dorms roomed together by age and gender. Can request a roommate. Dorm rooms DO NOT have AC but common spaces do. Campers have fans. We were there on a very hot day and the dorms were surprisingly cool. Many counselors are assistant coaches, so they are very hands on with the day to day. Campers have Quad Chill time 2x a day in a nice grassy area outside of dorms with lawn games, shade, etc. Laundry done 1x week. Athletic Longue with AC where campers can come for snacks during the day, night chill space with ping pong tables, Pop-A-Shot, chess, music, movie night. Have BBQ’s 1x session on the patio of the Athletic Longue. Dining Hall- very large and clean. Nice round tables. One seating for all campers. Buffet style can accommodate all dietary restrictions (GF, veggie, dairy free, nuts, etc.). Lots of healthy options. Counselors sit at tables with campers. All food is cooked nut free, but they DO have individual packets of peanut butter. Full air-conditioned health center with lots of private rooms. 24/7 hr. nurse (male and female). Doc on call if needed, Pediatric Children’s Hospital is 10 mins away. Have an athletic trainer Mon-Fri to help with strengthening, stretching, injuries, etc. 2 strength/conditioning coaches (male & female). Security from 4pm-7am patrolling campus. Staff must wear staff shirts. Need extra money for canteen (most campers have btwn $50-100) Get canteen 2x a day. Overall, we were very impressed with Academy Camps. Seems to have top level instruction, organized programming and the grounds/facilities were beautiful. They also seem to know and have a pulse on each camper. This camp is great for a sporty kid looking to improve their skills and have a shorter camp experience. You can give with Next Level, Bridgeton, Kent Hills, Berkshire Soccer, IMG, US Nike.

title: Jeanne D'Arc — Shari — 8-21-24
text:
# Jeanne D'Arc



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Jeanne D’Arc – Shari and Lisa B - 10 July 2024 - toured by Sandy Abbott – 9th summer at JDA. We toured rather quickly in anticipation of major storms (they tried to cancel us the evening prior but we persisted due to their distance) thus our data sparce but enough to share. The founders in the 20’s intended to bring girls together from all over the world who are open-minded, nice girls, building strong character with faith, endurance, courage and confidence and the Story of Joan of Arc (JOA) as their guided principles. Sandy and Randy have embodied that tradition. BASIC DATA – Offer 2wks (any age), 3.5 & 7wks. 88 girls for 1st session, 65 girls signed up for 2nd session. Capacity is 120. 20% stay full summer. Most popular session length is 3.5 wks.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Ages 7-15 and 16y/o are CIT’s. About 65% girls of alum. We met 4 teen girls from NY, MI, PR & CT who couldn’t have been more friendly and expressed their love of their camp community. Brothers attend Cody and Chateaugay. 60 staff members, many prev campers, 12 countries represented for int’l counselors, about 50% of staff members return from year-to-year. Director contact: toured by Sandy Abbott – 9th summer at JDA.



## 3 · Living Quarters

ACCOMODATIONS – Swiss Chalet style cabins/houses. Each one is different, we saw a 14y/o cabin by lake w/ screen porch. Some are multi floor, all have common or living room type space, 10-12 campers, 2-3 staff members. Facilities in bunk, electricity, live out of trunks, wood bunk beds, counselor in separated area inside bunk, OD inside bunk at bedtime. Laundry done on site, 1x/week, older campers are done individually, younger is done w/ a buddy to sort.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

AQUATICS - swim lessons in lake every other day for all ages. Private beach area w/ 2 boats for wake board & knee board, sailing, kayak, canoe, SUP, bathtub boats. Extra cost options include waterskiing and a motorboat trip over Lake Champlain to Burlington.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

PROGRAMMING – Five periods/day, mornings are assigned, afternoons are choice, moving w/ a buddy & roll is called at activities. Encouraged to try everything. Lots of intra-camp games, same team for life, all summer long, awards and celebrations throughout. Big dance at end of summer. Girls pack a bag for the day not going back to cabins in-between activities.



## 6 · Program Highlights

SPORTS – soccer, basketball, softball, volleyball, badminton, kickball, field hockey. Some tournaments and inter-camps. Golf off-site in Malone. Hb riding (5 horses), English style, on property, trail rides, ring for advanced riders, can do stable management, bring own riding equipment and can also provide. Program is always full, 19 riders. CREATIVE – basic camp craft activities, outdoor grilling, dance (jazz, hip hop, modern), basic guitar lessons. One show per session, auditions but all who want to participate get a part. Not a lot here in the creative department. ORIENTEERING/NATURE – chickens, bunnies, organic garden which Sandy loves and wants to expand in the future. OLS big at JDA, archery, riflery w/ bb’s for 12 & younger campers and rifles for 13+, hikes offered regularly, some of which campers plan. Overnights on site for younger campers.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

MEDICAL – NP on site full-time and a 2nd nurse during the day. Plattsburgh Hospital, 40mins away, can take diabetic. Very strict about tick checks reminding girls to check themselves regularly. DINING – The Hearth – traditional, cozy w/ round tables, rafters/plaques/flags, Grace at meals, and gratitude. Family style, tables mixed weekly, no allergy chef or separate area in kitchen but can accommodate all except celiac. Sun butter used. No canteen. Granola bars and fruit available anytime, candy cart on Fridays.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

The founders in the 20’s intended to bring girls together from all over the world who are open-minded, nice girls, building strong character with faith, endurance, courage and confidence and the Story of Joan of Arc (JOA) as their guided principles. Sandy and Randy have embodied that tradition. RELIGIOUS LIFE – Priest at Chapel (beautiful w/ stained glass windows made by alum telling the story of JOA) on Sundays, The Lords Prayer at campfire, always other options available but Bible stories, Mass, mindfulness, a big part of camp. She says it’s non-denominational but we didn’t get that impression based on the religious emphases mentioned throughout. No official big sister program but have a name exchange among older and younger girls. JDA is a bit of a throwback camp, back to basics and other than community and friendship, nothing stands out as a ‘favorite’ activity or part of camp. At any age a new girl would fit in, very accepting community.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Buses/vans from NYC (Lincoln Ctr), Westchester (Bloomingdales in WP), Albany. Campers also from CA, GA, PA, NJ, CT, PR, Mexico, Peru, Panema (about 50% of int’l campers are from latin based demographic). COMMUNICATION – No phone calls home, email 2way, packages allowed 1x/week but no food, letter writing required, pics posted daily, also use Instagram throughout the summer for parents to view happenings at camp. Extra costs: hb riding, golf, waterskiing, motorboat trip over Lake Champlain to Burlington.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Jeanne D’Arc is a bit of a throwback camp, back to basics, with the Story of Joan of Arc as guiding principles and religion woven in throughout the experience. The founders in the 20’s intended to bring girls together from all over the world who are open-minded, nice girls, building strong character with faith, endurance, courage and confidence, and Sandy and Randy have embodied that tradition. It’s a place where girls can go to make great friends while having fun and enjoying the outdoors; at any age a new girl would fit in, a very accepting community. When asked what type of camper would NOT do well at JDA, Sandy said NOT an atheist\!



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Five periods/day, mornings are assigned, afternoons are choice; encouraged to try everything.
  - Lots of intra-camp games, same team for life, awards and celebrations; big dance at end of summer.
  - Swim lessons in lake every other day for all ages; robust waterfront with sailing, kayak, canoe, SUP, and wake/kneeboarding.
  - Hb riding program is always full (5 horses, 19 riders), English style with trail rides, ring work, and stable management.
  - OLS big at JDA; archery and riflery (bb’s for 12 & younger; rifles for 13+); regular hikes and on-site overnights.
  - NP on site full-time plus a 2nd nurse during the day; strong tick-check culture.
  - Dining in The Hearth is traditional and cozy; family style with tables mixed weekly.
  - Two-way email, daily photos, and Instagram for parents; letter writing required.
  - Buses/vans from NYC, Westchester, and Albany expand access.
  - We met 4 teen girls from NY, MI, PR & CT who were very friendly and love their camp community.



#### Lowlights

  - Tour was rushed due to impending storms; data sparse.
  - No allergy chef or separate area in kitchen; cannot accommodate celiac.
  - Not a lot here in the creative department.
  - No official big sister program (uses a name exchange instead).
  - No phone calls home.
  - She says it’s non-denominational but our impression was more religious based on emphases.
  - When asked who would NOT do well: NOT an atheist.
  - Girls pack a bag for the day and do not return to cabins between activities.
  - Nearest hospital (Plattsburgh) is 40 minutes away.



#### Similar Camps

  - North Country Whippoorwhil
  - Runoia
  - Wyonogonic
  - Arcadia

Plattsburgh Hospital, 40mins away, can take diabetic. Very strict about tick checks reminding girls to check themselves regularly. No official big sister program but have a name exchange among older and younger girls. RELIGIOUS LIFE – Priest at Chapel (beautiful w/ stained glass windows made by alum telling the story of JOA) on Sundays, The Lords Prayer at campfire, always other options available but Bible stories, Mass, mindfulness, a big part of camp. She says it’s non-denominational but we didn’t get that impression based on the religious emphases mentioned throughout. Granola bars and fruit available anytime, candy cart on Fridays. ACCOMODATIONS – Swiss Chalet style cabins/houses. Each one is different, we saw a 14y/o cabin by lake w/ screen porch. Some are multi floor, all have common or living room type space, 10-12 campers, 2-3 staff members. Facilities in bunk, electricity, live out of trunks, wood bunk beds, counselor in separated area inside bunk, OD inside bunk at bedtime, laundry done on site, 1x/week, older campers are done individually, younger is done w/ a buddy to sort. SPORTS- soccer, basketball, softball, volleyball, badminton, kickball, field hockey. Some tournaments and inter-camps. Golf off-site in Malone. Hb riding (5 horses), English style, on property, trail rides, ring for advanced riders, can do stable management, bring own riding equipment and can also provide. Program is always full, 19 riders. CREATIVE- basic camp craft activities, outdoor grilling, dance (jazz, hip hop, modern), basic guitar lessons. One show per session, auditions but all who want to participate get a part. Not a lot here in the creative department. ORIENTEERING/NATURE – chickens, bunnies, organic garden which Sandy loves and wants to expand in the future. OLS big at JDA, archery, riflery w/ bb’s for 12 & younger campers and rifles for 13+, hikes offered regularly, some of which campers plan. Overnights on site for younger campers. AQUATICS- swim lessons in lake every other day for all ages. Private beach area w/ 2 boats for wake board & knee board, sailing, kayak, canoe, SUP, bathtub boats. Additional Notes: When asked what type of camper would NOT do well at JDA, Sandy said NOT an atheist\! At any age a new girl would fit in, very accepting community. JDA is a bit of a throwback camp, back to basics and other than community and friendship, nothing stands out as a ‘favorite’ activity or part of camp. It’s a place where girls can go to make great friends while having fun and enjoying the outdoors with religion being woven in throughout their experience. I would give this w/ North Country Whippoorwhil, Runoia, Wyonogonic, Arcadia, and others depending on the family’s additional parameters.




### Author: Shari

title: Creative Arts — Jennifer — 7-3-23
text:
# Creative Arts



## 1 · Program Snapshot

6 POINTS CREATIVE ARTS CAMP. Located on the campus of Westtown school – a Quaker boarding school in West Chester, PA (about 45 min from Philadelphia). It is a newer camp, only open for 6 years and camp has low numbers right now which Rachel partially attributes to COVID. She is hoping to grow the camp to be large and successful like their STEM and sports camps. They offer three 12 day sessions. Sessions run from a Tuesday to a Sunday. Currently have 67 campers enrolled for session 1, 90 for session 2 and 70 for session 3. They have capacity for 200 campers. Age of Campers: Rising 5th to Rising 12th grade. There is currently no bussing offered but willing to add when numbers grow. Will pick up and drop off at Philly airport. Offer intersessions for campers staying for more than session. There is a cost for intersession and will take the campers off site to an amusement park. Otherwise, there are no trips during camp. Campus is open to the public as a Quaker school and there is a day camp there during the week.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Jennifer toured with executive director, Rachel Handloff on 7/2/23. Campers do not need to have prior arts experience but should be passionate about the arts.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Campers are bunked based on the gender they identify as. There are 2-3 typically in a dorm room. Campers each get their own dresser and they share a closet. There are bathrooms down the hall and campers can use based on their gender identity. All dorm rooms have window a/c units.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

There is an indoor pool on campus.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers choose an art major before arriving to camp. Campers spend 2.5 hrs daily in their major. They also get 1 elective and 1 movement arts – which is some type of movement but there are no organized sports on campus. On Thursday – campers can try or sample a different major. Musical theater is their most popular major. They do just a small part of a show as session is only 12 days. Campers do not participate in their majors on Saturday.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Musical theater is their most popular major. Tennis courts on campus but Rachel says her campers have expressed no interest in using them. Campers do not need to have prior arts experience but should be passionate about the arts.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Campus is open to the public as a Quaker school and there is a day camp there during the week. However, 6 points uses separate buildings and staff wear swipes that get them in/out of their buildings. Campers must travel with a staff person at all times. Campers also wear a bracelet identifying them as 6 points campers. They do have a camper care specialist on staff to handle all social/emotional issues as well as 2 nurses on staff for medical issues. Dining Hall – is air conditioned. Campers sit with their bunks. Food is served buffet style except for Fri nights which is served family style. They are nut free and can accommodate all allergies. Food is kosher style. There is no canteen or vending machines on campus. Snacks are served around 3:30 in the afternoon.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Religion: blessings done at every meal, Fri night service and everyone wears a white shirt. There is also a Saturday morning service followed by a choice of Shabbat electives. Campers do not participate in their majors on Saturday. Overall Impression: I saw a lot of campers with purple, green hair, nose rings, black goth makeup etc. I believe they do get a lot of nonbinary campers. Rachel said this camp attracts campers who were unsuccessful at a traditional camp and feel like "they have finally found their people when they come to camp".



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

There is currently no bussing offered but willing to add when numbers grow. Will pick up and drop off at Philly airport. Communication: Campers do NOT call home during their 12 day session. If they stay for intersession, they will call home then. Rachel is implementing for next summer that a director will call the parents of all new campers within the first few days. There is a cost for intersession. Tech policy: Unknown. Additional costs beyond tuition: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Located on the campus of Westtown School in West Chester, PA (about 45 min from Philadelphia), this newer Jewish arts camp offers three 12-day sessions for rising 5th to rising 12th graders. Campers choose an art major, spend 2.5 hours daily in their major with one elective and one movement arts, and can sample a different major on Thursday; musical theater is their most popular major. Safety and care include separate buildings with staff swipes, campers traveling with a staff person at all times and wearing bracelets, a camper care specialist, and 2 nurses; food is nut free, kosher style, and can accommodate all allergies. Best fit is a quirkier Jewish kid with a strong interest in the arts who may not have thrived at a traditional camp and wants to feel like they have finally found their people.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Located on the campus of Westtown school – a Quaker boarding school in West Chester, PA (about 45 min from Philadelphia).
  - They offer three 12 day sessions (Tuesday to Sunday).
  - Campers choose an art major before arriving to camp; 2.5 hrs daily in their major plus 1 elective and 1 movement arts.
  - On Thursday – campers can try or sample a different major.
  - All dorm rooms have window a/c units; Dining Hall – is air conditioned.
  - They are nut free and can accommodate all allergies; Food is kosher style.
  - They do have a camper care specialist on staff to handle all social/emotional issues as well as 2 nurses on staff for medical issues.
  - Will pick up and drop off at Philly airport.
  - Bunked based on the gender they identify as; bathrooms can be used based on their gender identity.
  - There is an indoor pool on campus.



#### Lowlights

  - It is a newer camp, only open for 6 years and camp has low numbers right now which Rachel partially attributes to COVID.
  - From what I observed in their drawing and musical theater programs, the offerings seemed fairly basic.
  - There is currently no bussing offered.
  - Otherwise- there are no trips during camp.
  - Campus is open to the public as a Quaker school and there is a day camp there during the week.
  - Campers do NOT call home during their 12 day session.
  - There is a cost for intersession.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

She is hoping to grow the camp to be large and successful like their STEM and sports camps. They offer three 12 day sessions. Currently have 67 campers enrolled for session 1, 90 for session 2 and 70 for session 3. They have capacity for 200 campers. There is currently no bussing offered but willing to add when numbers grow. Will pick up and drop off at Philly airport. Sessions run from a Tuesday to a Sunday. Offer intersessions for campers staying for more than session. There is a cost for intersession and will take the campers off site to an amusement park. Otherwise- there are no trips during camp. Age of Campers : Rising 5th to Rising 12th grade Campus is open to the public as a Quaker school and there is a day camp there during the week. However, 6 points uses separate buildings and staff wear swipes that get them in/out of their buildings. Campers must travel with a staff person at all times. Campers also wear a bracelet identifying them as 6 points campers. Campers choose an art major before arriving to camp. Campers spend 2.5 hrs daily in their major. They also get 1 elective and 1 movement arts – which is some type of movement but there are no organized sports on campus. On Thursday – campers can try or sample a different major. There is an indoor pool on campus. Tennis courts on campus but Rachel says her campers have expressed no interest in using them. Musical theater is their most popular major. They do just a small part of a show as session is only 12 days. Campers are bunked based on the gender they identify as. There are 2-3 typically in a dorm room. Campers each get their own dresser and they share a closet. There are bathrooms down the hall and campers can use based on their gender identity. All dorm rooms have window a/c units. Dining Hall – is air conditioned. Campers sit with their bunks. Food is served buffet style except for Fri nights which is served family style. They are nut free and can accommodate all allergies. Food is kosher style. There is no canteen or vending machines on campus. Snacks are served around 3:30 in the afternoon. Communication: Campers do NOT call home during their 12 day session. If they stay for intersession, they will call home then. Rachel is implementing for next summer that a director will call the parents of all new campers within the first few days. They do have a camper care specialist on staff to handle all social/emotional issues as well as 2 nurses on staff for medical issues. Religion: blessings done at every meal, Fri night service and everyone wears a white shirt. There is also a Saturday morning service followed by a choice of Shabbat electives. Campers do not participate in their majors on Saturday. Overall Impression: I saw a lot of campers with purple, green hair, nose rings, black goth makeup etc. I believe they do get a lot of nonbinary campers. Rachel said this camp attracts campers who were unsuccessful at a traditional camp and feel like “they have finally found their people when they come to camp” Campers do not need to have prior arts experience but should be passionate about the arts. From what I observed in their drawing and musical theater programs, the offerings seemed fairly basic. I think this camp would be best for a quirkier Jewish kid with a strong interest in the arts.

title: Takajo — Lana — 7-23-23
text:
# Takajo



## 1 · Program Snapshot

400 boys 7 weeks. 4 weeks first summer only. Camp since 1947. Uniform. Bedding not required to be uniform but a lot have it. They provide uniform laundry bags. Camp split 3 camps 8/9/10, 11/12, 13,14/15 year olds. Facilities for the 3 camps are different. Separate basketball courts etc. Lower hoops for little kids etc. Baseball field smaller for smaller kids etc. All-camp facilities are the playhouse, campfire area, lake.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Takajo Rayna (bunk mom for 10 year olds) toured us. She went to Mataponi and Mataoka. Senior at Emory and from Boca. Helps make beds, sunscreen for kids. She tours families. We met owner Jeff at end of tour. 3/4 of his kids are counselors at camp. College athletes as counselors. Most campers from CT NYC Scarsdale NJ Florida. No LI no PA.



## 3 · Living Quarters

8 kids/3 counselors per bunk. 1 is always female till 10 years old. Separate room for their cubbies and sports equipment in bunk. Toilets and sink in bunk. 4 shower houses to share on camp. Towel service for bunk and lake.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake. 2 periods swim one of which is instructional.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6 periods day/ 1 elective (pioneering rock wall or cooking, nature goats chickens ducks take care of them, art center ceramics crafts and art, radio), 2 periods swim one of which is instructional/2 sports/1 skills (tennis golf archery) for 4 days. Travel by division by bunk. 1x/week social and 1x week see sisters.



## 6 · Program Highlights

They say it’s not competitive at sports and that they have a mix. Hockey used to be big. Basketball and soccer biggest sports. Sports teams not ability based. 3 shows a summer.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining hall separate sessions for youngest campers and older kids eat later. AC in dining hall. Nutfree can handle all allergies and kosher. Do not sit by bunk - split amongst age and switch tables every week. Have snack shack with vegetables and prepackaged snacks 3 snacks/day. No canteen no candy. 2 doctors 7 nurses. Orthopedic surgeon 15 mins. Urgent care 25 mins.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Sisters at Tripp Lake Mataponi Vega. Some Mataoka. Impressions - We only saw a few campers as they were in their bunks due to thunder and lightning when we were there. It was clear that Jeff runs a great business. It started to rain just as we started tour so we were quick.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Campers from tristate take Westchester bus. Chaperoned flights from Boston Miami LA. Uniform. Bedding not required to be uniform but a lot have it. They provide uniform laundry bags. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Takajo is a boys camp with about 400 campers and a 7-week season (4 weeks first summer only), founded in 1947. Camp is split into three age-based camps (8/9/10, 11/12, 13/14/15) with separate facilities; most campers are from CT, NYC/Scarsdale, NJ, and Florida. The daily program runs 6 periods with 1 elective (pioneering/rock wall or cooking, nature with goats/chickens/ducks, art center with ceramics/crafts/art, radio), 2 swims (one instructional), 2 sports, and 1 skills block (tennis, golf, archery), plus weekly socials with sister camps and 3 shows a summer. They say it’s not competitive at sports (basketball and soccer biggest), dining is nut-free and can handle all allergies and kosher, there are 2 doctors and 7 nurses, and travel options include a Westchester bus for tristate and chaperoned flights from Boston, Miami, and LA.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - It was clear that Jeff runs a great business.
  - College athletes as counselors.
  - AC in dining hall.
  - Nutfree can handle all allergies and kosher.
  - 2 doctors 7 nurses; orthopedic surgeon 15 mins; urgent care 25 mins.
  - 1 is always female till 10 years old (counselor in bunk).
  - Separate facilities scaled for each age group.
  - Sports culture is a mix and not competitive; basketball and soccer biggest.



#### Lowlights

  - It started to rain just as we started tour so we were quick.
  - We only saw a few campers as they were in their bunks due to thunder and lightning.
  - Sports teams not ability based.
  - No LI no PA.
  - No canteen no candy.



#### Similar Camps

  - Cedar
  - Andro
  - Greylock
  - Winadu
  - Raquette Lake



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Takajo Rayna (bunk mom for 10 year olds) toured us. She went to Mataponi and Mataoka. Senior at Emory and from Boca. Helps make beds sunscreen for kids. She tours families. We met owner Jeff at end of tour. 3/4 of his kids are counselors at camp. It started to rain just as we started tour so we were quick. 400 boys 7 weeks. 4 weeks first summer only. Camp since 1947 Most campers from CT NYC Scarsdale NJ Florida. no LI no PA. Campers from tristate take Westchester bus. Chaperoned flights from boston Miami la Uniform. bedding not required to be uniform but a lot have it. They provide uniform laundry bags 8 kids/3 counselors per bunk. 1 is always female till 10 years old. Separate room for their cubbies and sports equipment in bunk. Toilets and sink in bunk. 4 shower houses to share on camp. Towel service for bunk and lake. Camp split 3 camps 8/9/10, 11/12, 13,14/15 year olds. Facilities for the 3 camps are different. Separate basketball courts etc. Lower hoops for little kids etc. baseball field smaller for smaller kids etc. All camp facilities are playhouse or campfire area lake. Sisters at Tripp lake Mataponi Vega. Some mataoka. 1x/week social and 1x week see sisters 6 periods day/ 1 elective (pioneering rock wall or cooking, nature goats chickens ducks take care of them, art center ceramics crafts and art, radio), 2 periods swim one of which is instructional/2 sports/1 skills (tennis golf archery) for 4 days. Travel by division by bunk. Colleague athletes as counselors. They say it’s not competitive at sports and that they have a mix. Hockey used to be big. Basketball and soccer biggest sports. Sports teams not ability based. 3 shows a summer. Dining hall separate sessions for youngest campers and older kids eat later. Ac in dining hall. Nutfree can handle all allergies and kosher. Do not sit by bunk -split amongst age and switch tables every week. Have snack shack with vegetables and prepackaged snacks 3 snacks/day. No canteen no candy. 2 doctors 7 nurses. Orthopedic surgeon 15 mins. Urgent care 25 mins. Impressions-We only saw a few campers as they were in their bunks due to thunder and lightning when we were there. It was clear that Jeff runs a great business. I’d give along with Cedar, Andro, and some of the Adirondacks and Berkshires brother sister camps like Greylock, Winadu, Raquette Lake

title: Harlam — Lisa — 8-13-23
text:
# Harlam



## 1 · Program Snapshot

6/28/23 Shari and Lisa were toured by Rachel Steinberg. They have 405 campers for their first 3.5 weeks, 400 for the second session with 3 days in between. They offer a 12-day session for current 2nd/3rd graders for the first time only. Only a few stay for the full summer. AC facilities: dining hall, health center, rec hall, digital arts, music, cooking, multi-purpose room. For 2024 they are planting a new garden, continuing to rebuild on the boys side and working on sports fields.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

75–80% of their campers are from the Mid-Atlantic area. They have a few trans/non-binary campers and are bunked with the gender they identify as. They tried a non-gender bunk but no interest. 75% of campers are Reform.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Large bunks, 10–16 campers with 4 counselors. 10th graders are across the street in their own facilities.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

2 pools not heated. Small lake with fishing and boating but no motorized. One free swim.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

2 AM activities, 3 PM activities plus one free swim. Arts and sports are elective, seemed pretty coed.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Color War for 3 days in each session. Friday Shabbat services are 1 hour long; they use the beautiful chapel for services, both English and Hebrew. The campers wear white; Saturday morning is 75 min long. Campers lead the services. Large A\&C area, ceramics, cooking as well. No woodworking this summer. Some STEM activities were being offered. In the amphitheater and rec hall, musicals are performed one per session. Dance, music (not huge), recording studio, and gymnastics are small programs. The indoor sports facility was nice, but the tennis courts were in bad shape. No golf, no horses, one hockey rink. There was an active outdoor adventure area with an indoor and outdoor climbing wall. Mountain biking and archery, with high and low ropes.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining hall with AC was a bit institutional looking, sit by bunk, 2 seatings. They have packets of peanut butter, so not nut free but can accommodate most other allergies. They have 5 RNs and 1 doctor. Camper Care: educators and social workers who support the campers well-being.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They are all about equity, diversity, inclusion. Their website says it all under their mission statement and Tikkun Middot. For a URJ camp it was better than expected.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No phone calls are permitted. 25% of their campers use some form of scholarship money. Travel logistics: Unknown. Tech/devices policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Harlam runs two sessions (405 for the first 3.5 weeks, 400 for the second, with 3 days in between) and offers a 12-day session for current 2nd/3rd graders for the first time only. Large bunks (10–16 campers with 4 counselors), arts and sports electives that seemed pretty coed, 2 AM and 3 PM activities, and one free swim create a balanced daily rhythm. Jewish life is visible with Color War in each session and Shabbat services in a beautiful chapel (Friday 1 hour; Saturday 75 minutes; campers lead; both English and Hebrew; campers wear white). Facilities include many AC spaces and strong camper care (5 RNs, 1 doctor, educators and social workers), though the dining hall felt institutional and the tennis courts were in bad shape.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Active outdoor adventure area with indoor and outdoor climbing wall; mountain biking and archery; high and low ropes.
  - Large A\&C area; ceramics; cooking; some STEM.
  - AC facilities: dining hall, health center, rec hall, digital arts, music, cooking, multi-purpose room.
  - Beautiful chapel; campers lead services; both English and Hebrew; campers wear white.
  - Camper Care: educators and social workers; 5 RNs and 1 doctor.
  - Equity, diversity, inclusion focus.
  - Color War for 3 days in each session.
  - For a URJ camp it was better than expected.



#### Lowlights

  - Dining hall with AC was a bit institutional looking.
  - Tennis courts were in bad shape.
  - No woodworking this summer.
  - 2 pools not heated.
  - No golf, no horses.
  - No phone calls are permitted.
  - Dance, music (not huge), and gymnastics are small programs.



#### Similar Camps

  - NJY Camps
  - Kingswood in Maine
  - Modin (but Modin much nicer, more for the religious aspect)



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

HARLAM 6-28-23 SHARI AND LISA WERE TOURED BY RACHEL STEINBERG THEY HAVE 405 CAMPERS FOR THEIR FIRST 3.5 WEEKS, 400 FOR THE SECOND SESSION WITH 3 DAYS IN BETWEEN. THEY OFFER A 12-DAY SESSION FOR CURRENT 2 ND /3 RD GRADERS FOR THE FIRST TIME ONLY. ONLY A FEW STAY FOR THE FULL SUMMER. THEY HAVE A FEW TRANS/NON-BINARY CAMPERS AND ARE BUNKED WITH THE GENDER THEY IDENTIFY AS. THEY TRIED A NON-GENDER BUNK BUT NO INTEREST. 75-80% OF THEIR CAMPERS ARE FROM THE MID-ATLANTIC AREA. 2 AM ACTIVITIES, 3 PM ACTIVITIES PLUS ONE FREE SWIM. COLOR WAR FOR 3 DAYS IN EACH SESSION. ARTS AND SPORTS ARE ELECTIVE, SEEMED PRETTY COED. LARGE BUNKS, 10-16 CAMPERS WITH 4 COUSELORS. 10 TH GRADERS ARE ACROSS THE STREET IN THEIR OWN FACILITES. DINING HALL WITH AC WAS A BIT INSTITUTIONAL LOOKING, SIT BY BUNK, 2 SEATINGS. THEY HAVE PACKETS OF PEANUT BUTTER, SO NOT NUT FREE BUT CAN ACCOMMODATE MOST OTHER ALLERGIES. AC FACILITES: DH, HEALTH CENTER, REC HALL, DIGITAL ARTS, MUSIC, COOKING, MULTI-PURPOSE ROOM. THEY ARE ALL ABOUT EQUITY, DIVERSITY, INCLUSION. THEIR WEBSITE SAYS IT ALL UNDER THEIR MISSION STATEMENT AND TIKKUN MIDDOT. THEY HAVE 5 RNS AND 1 DOCTOR. NO PHONE CALLS ARE PERMITTED. FRIDAY SHABBAT SERVICES ARE 1 HOUR LONG, THEY USE THE BEAUTIFUL CHAPEL FOR SERVICES, BOTH ENGLISH AND HEBREW. THE CAMPERS WEAR WHITE, SATURDAY MORNING IS 75 MIN LONG. CAMPERS LEAD THE SERVICES. 75% CAMPERS ARE REFORMED. 2 POOLS NOT HEATED. SMALL LAKE WITH FISHING AND BOATING BUT NO MOTORIZED. CAMPER CARE: EDUCATORS AND SOCIAL WORKERS WHO SUPPORT THE CAMPERS WELL-BEING. LARGE A\&C AREA, CERAMICS, COOKING AS WELL. NO WOODWORKING THIS SUMMER. SOME STEM ACTIVITIES WERE BEING OFFERED. IN THE AMPITHEATER AND REC HALL MUSICALS ARE PERFORMED ONE PER SESSION. DANCE, MUSIC (NOT HUGE) RECORDING STUDIO AND GYMNASTICS IS A SMALL PROGRAM. THE INDOOR SPORTS FACILITY WAS NICE, BUT THE TENNIS COURTS WERE IN BAD SHAPE. NO GOLF, NO HORSES, ONE HOCKEY RINK. THERE WAS AN ACTIVE OUTDOOR ADVENTURE AREA WITH AN INDOOR AND OUTDOOR CLIMBING WALL. MOUNTAIN BIKING AND ARCHERY, WITH HIGH AND LOW ROPES. FOR 2024 THEY ARE PLANTING A NEW GARDEN, CONTINUING TO REBUILD ON THE BOYS SIDE AND WORKING ON SPORTS FIELDS. 25% OF THEIR CAMPERS USE SOME FORM OF SCHOLARSHIP MONEY. GIVE WITH: NJY CAMPS, KINGSWOOD IN MAINE, MODIN (BUT MODIN MUCH NICER, MORE FOR THE RELIGIOUS ASPECT). FOR A URJ CAMP IT WAS BETTER THAN EXPECTED.

title: Crescent Cove — Emily — 7-6-23
text:
# Crescent Cove



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Crescent Cove July 6th: Emily R, Michele G and Liz M. Nicole and Dan Feig are the new Directors of Crescent Cove. They are from Chappaqua, NY. Their girls attend Mataponi. Half of the kids are from the old camp, Nashoba North, the other half are new. Right now, they have about 70 kids per session but have the capacity for 225. Starting next summer, CC will be two 3.5 week sessions. Kids cannot come for both. Nashoba North was a very different camp so they are slowly making the transition. There is no longer a day camp portion.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Nicole and Dan Feig are the new Directors of Crescent Cove. They are from Chappaqua, NY. Their girls attend Mataponi. Half of the kids are from the old camp, Nashoba North, the other half are new. They have 5 kids from Greece. Counselors are 50% US and 50% International. They come from Australia, Europe, South Africa.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Girls and boys bunks are separate sides of camp. Bunks nice and clean. In good shape. They have showers and bathrooms in the bunk. Set up as two bunks that are connected by a common space. 12-14 kids in a bunk with 3 counselors. Bunks have a nice storage area. Provide linens to international campers. Disinfect showers and bathrooms once a day. Plan to move to full towel service for all campers next summer.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Waterfront has a beach, Nicole and Jason plan to organize the beach better next summer with a changing room for the kids and to create more beach space. Camp is on Crescent Lake, around the corner is Laurel South. They have fishing, waterskiing, sailboats, paddleboards, etc. instructional swim.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Programming will have core activities with your bunk and then opportunity for electives, choosing for week A and B. There will be 5 periods. 2 with your bunk, then lunch, then 3 electives. They will also have free swim. As the kids get older, they will have more electives.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Because Nashoba North was more of a horseback camp, CC has great riding. They have their own barn on property with 6 horses and riding rings. They also have animal care with calves, sheep, chickens. They plan to build a large indoor center to be used for gymnastics and indoor basketball. They plan to fix up the greens with 2 golf holes. They have a full-length soccer and lacrosse field, volley ball courts and new tennis courts. They are adding another platform to the climbing wall and will add ropes. They have a dance studio. There is an outdoor amphitheater. Kids do showcase night where they perform what they have learned. Theater, the kids performing Mary Poppins. Woodworking is popular, a carpenter from Ireland runs it. They have special events for July 4th etc, archery, disc golf, rock shop band. They have tether ball for free time and gaga. Have pickleball courts and basketball courts. There will be intercamps against Laurel South, Mataponi, North Star.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health center is staffed with Nurses with a Doctor on call, the Urgicare is 10 minutes away. Next summer, they will have a Doctor on staff as well. Nurse gives out camp meds. The dining hall is brand new and really nice. Camp eats together cafeteria style. Have a midday snack and canteen at night. Camp is nut free. There is a screened in porch with a beautiful view of the lake. Can handle gluten, vegetarian and allergies; there is an allergy chef. They offer a lot of healthy options like salad, fruit bowls, etc. Underneath the dining hall, there is indoor space they can use when it rains.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Campers are mixed religions. Don’t plan to have any religious services at camp. They do all camp firesides where they honor a bunk or camper. The previous owner has been very uncooperative and unfriendly which has made the transition difficult. The new camp logo is AWESOME and they are putting it around camp and on t-shirts etc. Overall impression is that Nicole and Jason are very dedicated to camp. They know they have work to do and many projects to get it up to speed but have very specific plans in mind and the grounds have a lot of potential. By next summer, they will have many more kids per session. They seem well connected and great with kids.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Nicole and Dan are happy to do a tour with prospective families or a home visit or zoom. They do not plan to hold a rookie day. There will be no visiting day with the sessions. This year parents all dropped and picked up kids because that is what they were used to. Next summer, may offer bus transportation. Use CampMinder, parents can email kids and they print it. Kids write letters. Photographer posts photos on CampMinder and Instagram. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Nicole and Dan Feig are the new Directors of Crescent Cove. Nashoba North was a very different camp so they are slowly making the transition. Starting next summer, CC will be two 3.5 week sessions and kids cannot come for both. Kids who would be a good fit are well rounded campers that want a little of everything including sports but not competitive.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Because Nashoba North was more of a horseback camp, CC has great riding; their own barn on property with 6 horses and riding rings.
  - Brand new and really nice dining hall; camp eats together cafeteria style.
  - Grounds have a lot of potential; by next summer, they will have many more kids per session.
  - They seem well connected and great with kids.
  - The new camp logo is AWESOME.
  - Full-length soccer and lacrosse field, volley ball courts and new tennis courts; pickleball and basketball courts.
  - Use CampMinder; parents can email kids and they print it; photographer posts photos on CampMinder and Instagram.
  - Health center staffed with Nurses with a Doctor on call; Urgicare is 10 minutes away.



#### Lowlights

  - The previous owner has been very uncooperative and unfriendly which has made the transition difficult.
  - Right now, they have about 70 kids per session (capacity 225).
  - There will be no visiting day with the sessions.
  - Do not plan to hold a rookie day.
  - This year parents all dropped and picked up kids; bus transportation only may be offered next summer.
  - They know they have work to do and many projects to get it up to speed.
  - Waterfront beach needs better organization; plan for a changing room and more beach space next summer.
  - Plan to redo all bathroom floors.



#### Similar Camps

  - Kohut
  - Laurel South
  - Kenmont/Kenwood

title: Belvoir Terrace — Monica — 7-29-24
text:
# Belvoir Terrace



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Belvoir Terrace Toured July 14, 2024 by Karen, Alyssa, Monica, Carolina, Tami and Pilar Vidal. Toured by three generation owner Diane Marcus. She grew up there and camp used to be her grandmother’s. This is a performing and fine arts all girls 5 or 6.5 week (as well as a limited availability 3 week) program located close to Lenox, MA in the Berkshires. Setting is in an old mansion and not a traditional camp setting. Trips off campus Kids have the opportunity of using the lake and having weekly activities at Greylock Camp for boys owned by Diane’s husband.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Demographics 160 campers and 100 staff. Camp fills up by January. Most kids come from NY suburbs, Texas, Georgia, FL and California. A few come from other countries. Three generation owner/director: Diane Marcus; she grew up there and camp used to be her grandmother’s. Unique proposal Diane zooms with each family twice. Once before accepting them and a second time to create their unique schedule with them.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Dorms There are 3 counselors and 9 kids on average. The dorms are rooms in the old mansion converted into a camp. Bathrooms are en suite but small. One bathroom had a piano in it.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They have swimming (pool only, no lake) at camp. Swimming is required for younger campers. Kids have the opportunity of using the lake and having weekly activities at Greylock Camp for boys owned by Diane’s husband, and they also have beach days at Greylock on lake.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Diane zooms with each family a second time to create their unique schedule with them. Lots of girls take private instruction; 100 girls take private voice lessons. Besides the clearly artistic girls they attract, they do also have the opportunity to play sports if they want and compete with other Berkshire camps (tennis, swimming, volleyball, archery, fitness, pilates). Swimming is required for younger campers. Evening programming includes all the silly camp games.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Dance Their ballet program is very advanced and intensive as they house the kids Lincoln Center ballet program during the summer. Every other dance form such as jazz, hiphop, modern is at a range of level and ability. Same with visual arts, theatre etc. Anyone who is interested in learning will do well there. Activities They have 5 theaters and put on 18 shows per summer. They have improv. Their seating in the theatre is assigned, with names on chairs. They have a digital photography program where they learn editing with photoshop. Their fine arts and ceramics department are great. Besides the clearly artistic girls they attract, they do also have the opportunity to play sports if they want and compete with other Berkshire camps.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Diet and Health They have 4 nurses on staff. They do not have a doctor and do not feel they can accommodate diabetes. Food: Unknown.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Setting is in an old mansion and not a traditional camp setting. My feeling is that this is the artist camp for the prim and proper girl. The girls look very well put together in general. All girls environment with assigned seating in the theatre with names on chairs. Girls have camp sisters in camp.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Trips off campus Kids have the opportunity of using the lake and having weekly activities at Greylock Camp for boys owned by Diane’s husband. Brother/sister Once a week they go to Greylock for social. They also have beach days at Greylock on lake. Communication Tech free. They have camp photos on app. Parent communication: Diane zooms with each family twice (once before accepting them and a second time to create their unique schedule). Travel logistics: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Belvoir Terrace is a performing and fine arts all girls 5 or 6.5 week (as well as a limited availability 3 week) program located close to Lenox, MA in the Berkshires, set in an old mansion and not a traditional camp setting. Their ballet program is very advanced and intensive as they house the kids Lincoln Center ballet program during the summer, with 5 theaters and 18 shows per summer plus visual arts, theatre, improv, and digital photography. Pool only (no lake) at camp with swimming required for younger campers, and they have weekly activities, socials, and beach days at Greylock on the lake. My feeling is that this is the artist camp for the prim and proper girl; the girls look very well put together in general.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Performing and fine arts all girls program in the Berkshires set in an old mansion.
  - Ballet program is very advanced and intensive; they house the kids Lincoln Center ballet program during the summer.
  - 5 theaters and 18 shows per summer; strong visual arts, theatre, improv, fine arts and ceramics.
  - Lots of girls take private instruction; 100 girls take private voice lessons.
  - Diane zooms with each family twice to personalize schedules and admissions.
  - Tech free with camp photos on app.
  - Access to Greylock Camp for weekly activities, socials, and beach days on the lake.
  - Sports options and inter-camp competition available.



#### Lowlights

  - Pool only, no lake at camp.
  - Bathrooms are en suite but small.
  - They do not have a doctor and do not feel they can accommodate diabetes.
  - Setting is not a traditional camp setting.
  - Travel logistics and extra costs: Unknown.



#### Similar Camps

  - Stagedoor
  - French Woods Festival
  - Putney School in VT
  - Danbee arts and dance



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Belvoir Terrace Toured July 14, 2024 by Karen, Alyssa, Monica, Carolina, Tami and Pilar Vidal. Toured by three generation owner Diane Marcus. She grew up there and camp used to be her grandmother’s. Sessions This is a performing and fine arts all girls 5 or 6.5 week (as well as a limited availability 3 week) program located close to Lenox, MA in the Berkshires. Setting is in an old mansion and not a traditional camp setting. Trips off campus Kids have the opportunity of using the lake and having weekly activities at Greylock Camp for boys owned by Diane’s husband. Dance Their ballet program is very advanced and intensive as they house the kids Lincoln Center ballet program during the summer. Every other dance form such as jazz, hiphop, modern is at a range of level and ability. Same with visual arts, theatre etc. Anyone who is interested in learning will do well there. Demographics 160 campers and 100 staff. Camp fills up by January. Most kids come from NY suburbs, Texas, Georgia, FL and California. A few come from other countries. Private lessons Lots of girls take private instruction. 100 girls take private voice lessons. Unique proposal Diane zooms with each family twice. Once before accepting them and a second time to create their unique schedule with them. Communication Tech free. They have camp photos on app. Dorms There are 3 counselors and 9 kids on average. The dorms are rooms in the old mansion converted into a camp. Bathrooms are en suite but small. One bathroom had a piano in it. Activities They have 5 theaters and put on 18 shows per summer. They have improv. Their seating in the theatre is assigned, with names on chairs. Besides the clearly artistic girls they attract, they do also have the opportunity to play sports if they want and compete with other Berkshire camps. They have tennis, swimming (pool only, no lake), volleyball, archery, fitness, pilates. They have a digital photography program where they learn editing with photoshop. Their fine arts and ceramics department are great. Swimming is required for younger campers. Evening programming includes all the silly camp games. Brother/sister Once a week they go to Greylock for social. They also have beach days at Greylock on lake. Girls have camp sisters in camp. Diet and Health They have 4 nurses on staff. They do not have a doctor and do not feel they can accommodate diabetes. My feeling is that this is the artist camp for the prim and proper girl. The girls look very well put together in general. I would give this program with Stagedoor, French Woods Festival, Putney School in VT although the unique proposition of this arts camp is that it is all girls and the others are coed. Potentially Danbee arts and dance will be similar although it is still too early to tell as this was their first year.

title: WINGATE KIRKLAND — AMANDA — 8-5-17
text:
# WINGATE KIRKLAND



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Has been a daily choice camp for 60 years and claims they are the original choice camp. Offer 2, 3.5 or 7 weeks (50% do 3.5 & 50% do 7 weeks). Approximately 175 campers depending on session. Ages 7–15. They also offer a day camp for ages 3.9 years–5th grade. Campers come from Baltimore to Boston and everything in between (SF and 1 dozen campers from China).



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Joanne, Wendy, Laurie and myself were toured by Will Rubinstein (owner/director). Most popular age group is finishing 6th & 7th grades.



## 3 · Living Quarters

Bunks are mixed in by sessions, approx. 11 campers with 3 counselors, a boys bunk can be right across from a girls bunk (encourage community). 2 sinks, toilets, showers (staff cleans bathrooms every day). Some rooms have AC but can put in a window unit if necessary. Will provide linens/towels for Int’l campers and staff.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Private pond—no motor boats, log rolling, water trampoline, sailboats, kayaks, paddle boards, fishing, skim boards, etc. 3 swim areas—have swim instruction 4x a week; offer lifeguard training.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

Campers choose each morning. Have league teams—posted on sheets outside of office. Social hall—shows (1 show for each session), evening activities, gaga, hockey.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Art Barn—small space but extended with a big tent; pottery, painting, lanyards, etc. Do a lot of team building/challenge activities (low ropes course, balancing, trust). Challenge Program for campers 7th–9th grade (go on trips—hiking/biking, wilderness skills, etc.). Offer many unique electives; nature you can eat, ethical dilemmas, zombie hunters and how to train a dragon as well as archery, 3D design, music lessons, etc. Trips included 13 yrs and up—NH trip, rafting in ME, RI, Cape Cod whale watching, deep sea fishing. For younger campers do an overnight on camp.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Health Center—3 nurses, husband/wife doc team there daily, give meds before meals and bedtime. Says they are 100% peanut, tree nut, sesame free and can accommodate all allergies. Dining room is tiny and was very hot. Have small outdoor seating area and extra seating downstairs for rainy days. Sunday afternoons kitchen staff is off so Sandy & Will run a BBQ.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Camp is very co-ed, they believe in creating a community. They are big on promoting kindness and random acts of kindness.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

No electronics, Nano for music is ok. Lice treatment is extra $. Parent communication: Unknown. Travel logistics: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Has been a daily choice camp for 60 years and claims they are the original choice camp, where campers choose each morning. It is very co-ed, they believe in creating a community, and they are big on promoting kindness and random acts of kindness. Overall we thought this is a good camp with an interesting selection of activities for a kid who is not very sporty and more low-key, with a private pond, 3 swim areas, and many unique electives. However, we did find it very pricey for what it is.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Has been a daily choice camp for 60 years and claims they are the original choice camp.
  - Overall we thought this is a good camp with an interesting selection of activities for a kid who is not very sporty and more low-key.
  - Private pond—no motor boats, log rolling, water trampoline, sailboats, kayaks, paddle boards, fishing, skim boards, etc.
  - Offer many unique electives; nature you can eat, ethical dilemmas, zombie hunters and how to train a dragon as well as archery, 3D design, music lessons, etc.
  - Health Center—3 nurses, husband/wife doc team there daily.



#### Lowlights

  - Dining room is tiny and was very hot.
  - However, we did find it very pricey for what it is.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

Says they are100% peanut, tree nut, sesame free and can accommodate all allergies Sunday afternoons kitchen staff is off so Sandy & will run a BBQ Bunks are mixed in by sessions, approx. 11 campers with 3 counselors, a boys bunk can be right across from a girls bunk (encourage community) 2 sinks, toilets, showers (staff cleans bathrooms everyday) Art Barn-small space but extended with a big tent pottery, painting, lanyards, etc. Do a lot of team building/challenge activities (low ropes course, balancing, trust, Challenge Program for campers 7 th -9 th grade( go on trips-hiking/biking, wilderness skills, etc.) Social hall-shows (1 show for each session), evening activities, gaga, hockey Offer many unique electives; nature you can eat, ethical dilemmas, zombie hunters and how to train a dragon as well as archery, 3D design, music lessons, etc. Have league teams-posted on sheets outside of office Trips Included 13 yrs and up-NH trip, rafting in ME, RI, Cape Cod whale watching, deep sea fishing, For younger campers do an overnight on camp Lice treatment is extra $ Overall we thought this is a good camp with an interesting selection of activities for a kid who is not very sporty and more low-key. However, we did find it very pricey for what it is




### Author: AMANDA

title: Emerson — Tami — 7-24-24
text:
# Emerson



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Emerson is a coed camp and has 161 campers for summer 2024. They can have up to 200-230. The youngest campers are 7 and the oldest is 15. Half of the campers are 13 and older. They have done some improvements to the camp like new landscaping and painting.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Emerson Date: July 17, 2024. Camp Experts: Tami Feldman, Alyssa Greenberger, Karen Meister, Monica Hirsch, Pilar Vidal, Carolina Lautenberg. Tour: Zach, Director- 2nd summer. The counselors are also specialists. 55% of the campers are boys. There are a mix of Jewish kids and a range of religions.



## 3 · Living Quarters

The girls cabins have been renovated and the boys are newer so they prioritized the girls first. They have a toilet and sink in the cabin and a shower house outside of the cabin. With transgender children, they will have an open conversation with the family and discuss the situation. They will do a bunk by gender identification and it is not communicated with the other families. They currently have a transgender camper and the other campers are not aware.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

They have water skiing and tubing which is the most popular since it is on the lake. They take a quick van ride from camp to get there. Even though there is no lake, they do have a pool. They have competitive swim for kids that swim well and they have free swim and instructional swim for the ones that are beginners and didn’t pass the swim check.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

They do A and B days for activities and have two periods in the AM and two in the PM. There is choice time and you sign up the day before. Some examples are pool, arts, lake, basketball, and upper camp hangout time.



## 6 · Program Highlights

They offer cooking and do a lot of baking. They also have photography, videography, street hockey, woodworking, rocketry, science experiments, pottery, sewing, painting, crocheting- the kids made shorts to wear. Gymnastics has bars and a tumble track and they use the facility for indoor soccer on rainy days. Their climbing wall is new this year and they have renovated the low ropes and they do team building there. There are also non-motorized go carts. The counselors are also specialists. They offer a play and 60 kids sign up. Theater is the 2nd most popular activity after the lake. The dining hall is where their large activities happen since it is the largest building on campus. There is color war.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

The dining hall has a double buffet, can accommodate allergies- celiac, egg (12 kids this session have an egg allergy), gluten free, dairy free, no sesame or soy in the kitchen, no shellfish. They have a special allergy chef and a special window for the kids to get their food. It is a nut free camp.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

They do have competitive kids but they like having a break from their sports during the summer.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

Most of the kids are from the Boston area, Connecticut, New York, New Jersey, some from Florida and some international. They do not offer airport pickups yet but have buses from Boston, West Chester and he is open if the kids are coming from an airplane to do an airport pickup. They have a no phone call policy for 2 weeks. Extra costs: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Emerson is a coed camp with 161 campers in 2024 (capacity 200-230) serving ages 7-15, with half 13+ and 55% boys. The schedule runs A/B days with two morning and two afternoon periods, daily choice sign-ups, and popular activities led by specialist counselors, with theater second only to the lake. Aquatics include a pool on campus plus water skiing and tubing at a nearby lake accessed by a quick van ride, and swim is offered as competitive, free, or instructional. Food service is nut free with a double buffet, a dedicated allergy chef/window, and accommodations for celiac, egg, gluten-free, dairy-free, and no sesame, soy, or shellfish.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Water skiing and tubing is the most popular since it is on the lake (quick van ride from camp).
  - Theater is the 2nd most popular activity after the lake.
  - They do A and B days for activities and have two periods in the AM and two in the PM; choice time sign up the day before.
  - Dining hall has a double buffet with a special allergy chef and a special window; nut free; no sesame or soy; no shellfish; accommodates celiac, egg (12 this session), gluten free, dairy free.
  - Climbing wall is new this year; low ropes renovated and used for team building.
  - Counselors are also specialists.
  - Girls cabins have been renovated; boys are newer; toilet and sink in cabin; shower house outside.
  - There are also non-motorized go carts.
  - They offer a play and 60 kids sign up.



#### Lowlights

  - They do not offer airport pickups yet (director is open to pickups when kids are arriving by airplane).
  - Even though there is no lake on campus, they go off-site for lake activities via a quick van ride.
  - They have a no phone call policy for 2 weeks.
  - A child that wouldn’t fit in would be a kid who only wants to do sports.



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown

title: PINE FOREST — HEATHER — 7-8-24
text:
# PINE FOREST



## 1 · Program Snapshot

Co-Ed camp with separate flagpole lineups, staggered dining. Same activity programming and see each other in passing. 7 week camp—one time partial option. 400 campers even split boys/girls. 5th generation, 96th year. Bunked by grade, have an age group. Eat and sleep with cabin; leagues by division have a chance to meet a mix of campers. Campers from all over including NYC, Westchester, Connecticut, NJ, DC, California MD, PA, FLA—not many from Long Island. Explorers introductory program like Rookie night—3.5 weeks first summer with option to stay.



## 2 · Camper, Staff and Director Profile

Tour Given by Lisa Fayne and Eric Morin (Taking over as Director, along with his wife, Anna Morin). Eric Morin background: Training Pine Forest staff based on experience working at Wharton business Human Resources, and creates programming using background in staffing and orientation. 2 psychologists during counselor training with continuous education. Staff return rate very high 90%. 11th grade program considered interns who have to go through this program in order to return as staff, all Red Cross certified as lifeguards, partial campers and interact with staff as shadows. 11th graders go to Costa Rica for 10 days, do service based program. Very spirited, energetic campers, cheering, singing. Campers are inclusive, athletic. Friendly campers, down to earth, patient kind. Cohesive feel between ages.



## 3 · Living Quarters

8-10 campers each bunk with up to 4 staff per cabin. Each bunk has 2 showers, 2 sinks, 2 toilets. Counselors on top bunks but almost all single beds. No Air Conditioning except in Dining Hall and Health Center, fans in bunks.



## 4 · Waterfront & Aquatics

Lake: Man made lake. Lake is down a very long path, and then across a public road, shared with Lake Greely and Shohola. Strict procedure to cross road; Lake staff first and last to lock gates. Along the way there are Kindness rocks, love notes rocks pay it forward—values based. Campers go to the lake as a division learning canoeing, kayaking, sailing and hangout time for a few periods since it’s quite a walk from main camp 2-3 times/week. Inflatables, wibbits. Coed luaus. Survivor activity. Coed lake block once a week. Boys and Girls heated pools. Tennis and swim on schedule, instructional 3 or 4 times per week, leveled instructions. Towel service for pool and lake.



## 5 · Daily Timetable & Choice

6th grade and below structured. More choice as camper gets older. Some Evening Activities are coed. Daily team athletic changes every day. League program consistent all summer; 4-6 sports. Activities: by age group not bunk. Some late sleep days; morning open electives once per week. Color days. Spirit days like wacky Wednesdays. Trips—Campers go off camp with their age groups. Intercamps—Join teams, and try out based one day tournament.



## 6 · Program Highlights

Traditional sports—always have at least one sport each day. Lit fields. Boys and Girls heated pools. 12 tennis courts. 7 pickleball. Clinics—most popular: basketball number one, flag football, youth soccer, lacrosse as choice boys and girls. Campfire site. Driving range and putting surface. Arts and Crafts loved here—outdoor area including jewelry making, ceramics, STEM, woodworking, general arts, candle making. Theater; Video broadcasting; Theater with screens, karaoke. Gymnastics, martial arts. Dance pavilion. Music: trumpet, electric guitar, like garage band. Mountain biking and trails. Yoga. Making compasses. Leaf coasters. Ropes course—learn how to do independent outdoor adventure, harness themselves in, learn how to be climbing focused vs adventure focused. Rope course and lake. Archery. Gaga. Ninja warrior. Zip lines. Outdoor rec/ nature fire building. Extras: Top basketball. Top ranch—horseback riding off site. Top golf—off camp. Private cooking lessons. Private tennis lessons. 1:1 Fitness Training.



## 7 · Health, Safety, and Food

Dining Hall: Lisa described food as very important, an extension of home—Camp Experts truly enjoyed lunch at Pine Forest\! Girls and boys staggered eating 15 minutes, separate sides. All campers eat with their bunks in the dining room. Kosher style and Nut free, special diet accommodating, including strict Kosher. Bagels and lox on Sundays. Salad bars, lots of choices. Low Key Canteen every other day. Teen canteen coed. Health Center: Mom or dad doctors all summer. 4 nurses live at PF all the time. Encourage emotional support. Outside security team daytime and night time. No Air Conditioning except in Dining Hall and Health Center.



## 8 · Vibe and Culture

Lisa described camp as having a New England, rustic, traditional, down to earth feeling, feels like a village. Very spirited, energetic campers, cheering, singing. Friendly campers, down to earth, patient kind. Campers are inclusive, athletic. Combination of athletic and non athletic. Jewish family owned, 90% Jewish campers. Friday evening Shabbat service, not optional, prayer over challah, candles, wine. Say prayer over dinner each night. Kindness rocks, love notes rocks pay it forward—values based.



## 9 · Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs

$2700 deposit fully refundable until February 1st. Explorers introductory program like Rookie night 3.5 weeks first summer with option to stay. Travel logistics: Unknown. Parent communication: Unknown. Tech policy: Unknown. Extra costs: Unknown.



## 10 · Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary

Pine Forest is a 5th generation, 96th year, co-ed, traditional, down to earth camp that feels like a village. 400 campers (even split boys/girls) live in rustic bunks by grade, with very spirited, inclusive, athletic campers and a cohesive feel between ages. The program blends structure and choice with leagues, daily sports, heated pools, and a man made lake reached via a strict procedure to cross a public road for canoeing, kayaking, sailing, inflatables, and coed luaus. Food is Kosher style and nut free with lots of choices, and the camp is Jewish family owned with Friday evening Shabbat services; note there is no Air Conditioning except in the Dining Hall and Health Center.



### Internal Insights

#### Highlights

  - Staff return rate very high 90%
  - 2 psychologists during counselor training with continuous education
  - Very spirited, energetic campers, cheering, singing
  - Kosher style and Nut free, special diet accommodating, including strict Kosher
  - Boys and Girls heated pools
  - Man made lake with inflatables and wibbits
  - League program consistent all summer; 4-6 sports with daily team athletic changes
  - 12 tennis courts; 7 pickleball; clinics (basketball number one)
  - Outside security team daytime and night time
  - Towel service for pool and lake
  - 5th generation, 96th year
  - Explorers introductory program (3.5 weeks) with option to stay



#### Lowlights

  - No Air Conditioning except in Dining Hall and Health Center
  - Lake is down a very long path and across a public road (strict procedure to cross road)
  - Not many from Long Island
  - Lake periods 2-3 times/week since it’s quite a walk from main camp
  - 7 week camp—one time partial option only
  - Co-Ed but separate flagpole lineups and staggered dining



#### Similar Camps

  - Unknown



## 11 · Original Write-Up (Verbatim)

Tour Given by Lisa Fayne and Eric Morin (Taking over as Director, along with his wife, Anna Morin) CE’s: Heather Pasquale, Michele Gershwin, Michelle Burger, Emily Rothenberg Visited: July 2, 2024
